# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle One

### The First Division: The First Pārājika Rules

The Buddha was in the country of Surāṣṭra together with a great assembly of five hundred bhikṣus. He traveled to the town of Verañjā and stayed beneath trees in a grove. In that town there was a brahmin named Verañjā. King Prasenajit had granted him this town as a fief. He heard that the Buddha, of the Śākya clan, had gone forth from home to study the Way, and had attained the titles of Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyak-Saṃbuddha, Vidyācaraṇa-saṃpanna, Sugata, Lokavid, Anuttara, Puruṣa-damya-sārathi, Śāstā-deva-manuṣyāṇāṃ, Buddha, and Bhagavat; that he universally knows all the mental states of beings in the world; that he teaches the true Dharma, good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, with good meaning and good flavor; that he perfectly demonstrates the marks of pure and clean brahmacarya; and that he had come traveling to this town together with his disciples. He exclaimed with praise: "Excellent! I wish to see the Buddha!" Then, surrounded by five hundred attendants in front and behind, he went out to where the Buddha was. From afar he saw the World-Honored One beneath the trees in the grove, his faculties tranquil and settled, his radiance extraordinary. Filled with joy and elation, he dismounted from his carriage and proceeded on foot, stood and greeted him, then sat to one side. At that time the World-Honored One taught him the wondrous Dharma, instructing, benefiting, and gladdening him. Having heard the Dharma with delight, he immediately said to the Buddha: "May the Buddha and the Sangha accept my offering of support for the three months of the rains retreat!" The Buddha said: "My assembly is large, and your faith, views, preferences, and objects of worship are different from ours." He again said to the Buddha: "Although my faith, views, preferences, and objects of worship differ, I do not regard the World-Honored One's assembly as too large." He asked thus up to three times, and the Buddha then accepted. He rose from his seat, circumambulated to the right, and departed, returning home to prepare provisions for the rains retreat offering.

At that time Māra Pāpīyān had this thought: "Now this brahmin has invited the Buddha and the Sangha for the three-month rains retreat. I shall bewitch and confuse his mind." Having thought this, he came and bewitched him. That brahmin, bewitched by Māra, then entered the inner palace to enjoy the five sensual pleasures. He ordered the gatekeepers: "I am now enjoying pleasures for three months within. Whether external affairs are good or bad, you must not report anything to me!" He completely forgot about his invitation to the Buddha and the Sangha.

At that time, the people of that country were devoted to heterodox ways. The town had no monasteries or lecture halls. North of the city there was a mountain with clean and pure forests and streams, and the Buddha and the great assembly settled there for the rains retreat. At that time there was famine in the world, and begging for alms was difficult to obtain. When they entered the town to beg for food, they obtained nothing at all. At that time, in the country of Pāri, there was a horse dealer who had driven five hundred horses and arrived at the beginning of summer. When the hot season had arrived, he saw that this town was cool, with abundant water and grass, so they all stopped there to feed and pasture the horses. The bhikṣus went to where the horse dealer was and stood silently. The horse dealer, having faith in the Buddha and a pure mind, felt compassion for the bhikṣus who had obtained nothing from begging, and said: "I have only horse grain. If you can eat it, I will reduce half the portion and give you one measure each, enough to sustain your bodies so you can practice the Way." The bhikṣus said: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to eat from the horses' share." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha, praised in various ways fewness of desires and contentment, and told the bhikṣus: "From today onward, I permit you to eat from the horses' share." Then the Venerable Ānanda took the Buddha's portion, asked someone to prepare it into parched grain, and offered it to the World-Honored One. The bhikṣu assembly pounded and cooked theirs before eating.

At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, in a quiet place, had this thought: "Now in this country it is difficult to obtain alms food. I should now take those who have attained supernatural powers to Uttarakuru to eat the naturally growing rice." Having thought this, he rose from his seat, went to the Buddha, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, stood to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Just now I had this thought: 'In this country it is difficult to obtain alms food. I should take those who have attained supernatural powers to Uttarakuru to eat the naturally growing rice.'"

The Buddha told Maudgalyāyana: "You and others could do this, but what about the ordinary bhikṣus?"

Maudgalyāyana said to the Buddha: "I would carry them there by supernatural power."

The Buddha said: "Stop, stop! Although you have such supernatural power, what of the karmic debts from past causes and conditions; where would you place those? And what about the ordinary bhikṣus of the future?" Maudgalyāyana accepted the teaching and remained silent.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra, in a quiet place, had this thought: "Among the Buddhas of the past, which Buddhas' brahmacarya did not long endure? Which Buddhas' brahmacarya endured long?" Having thought this, he rose from his seat, went to the Buddha, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, stood to one side, and said to the Buddha: "Just now I had this thought: 'Among the Buddhas of the past, which Buddhas' brahmacarya did not long endure? Which Buddhas' brahmacarya endured long?'"

At that time, the Buddha praised Śāriputra saying: "Excellent, excellent! What you have contemplated is good, and what you have asked is also good. Śāriputra! The brahmacarya of Vipaśyin Buddha, Śikhin Buddha, and Viśvabhū Buddha did not long endure. The brahmacarya of Krakucchanda Buddha, Kanakamuni Buddha, and Kāśyapa Buddha endured long."

Śāriputra said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! For what reason did the brahmacarya of three Buddhas not long endure, while the brahmacarya of three Buddhas endured long?"

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "The three Buddhas did not extensively teach the Dharma to their disciples, did not establish precepts, and did not recite the Prātimokṣa. After those Buddhas and their disciples had entered parinirvāṇa, their disciples, of various names and clans, who had gone forth, quickly destroyed the brahmacarya. It is like a tray filled with loose flowers placed at a crossroads; when winds blow from the four directions, the flowers are scattered and fall. Why? Because there is no thread to hold them together. So it is, Śāriputra: those three Buddhas did not extensively teach the Dharma to their disciples, did not establish precepts, and did not recite the Prātimokṣa; therefore the brahmacarya could not long endure.

"Furthermore, Śāriputra, Viśvabhū Buddha, together with a thousand disciples, traveled through the Forest of Terror. The reason it was called the Forest of Terror was that any person who had not yet abandoned desire, upon entering this forest, would have all their body hair stand on end; therefore it was called the Forest of Terror. That Buddha taught the Dharma to his disciples through mental transmission, without speaking words aloud: 'Bhikṣus! You should think of this; do not think of that. You should be mindful of this; do not be mindful of that. You should abandon this; you should cultivate this; you should rely on this in practice!' When the bhikṣus mentally understood this, their outflows were exhausted, their minds were liberated, and they attained the path of arhatship.

"Śāriputra! Krakucchanda Buddha, Kanakamuni Buddha, and Kāśyapa Buddha extensively taught the Dharma to their disciples without weariness or fatigue, namely: sūtra, geya, vyākaraṇa, gāthā, udāna, nidāna, avadāna, jātaka, vaipulya, adbhutadharma, avadāna, and upadeśa. They established precepts and recited the Prātimokṣa. After those Buddhas and their disciples had entered parinirvāṇa, their disciples, though of various names and clans who had gone forth, did not quickly destroy the brahmacarya. It is like assorted flowers strung together with thread, placed at a crossroads; when winds blow from the four directions, they cannot be scattered. Why? Because the thread holds them together. So it is, Śāriputra: those three Buddhas extensively taught the Dharma as described above to their disciples; therefore the brahmacarya endured long."

Śāriputra said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! If it is the case that not extensively teaching the Dharma, not establishing precepts, and not reciting the Prātimokṣa causes the brahmacarya not to endure long, I humbly request that the World-Honored One extensively teach the Dharma to his disciples, establish precepts, and recite the Prātimokṣa! Now is the proper time."

The Buddha said: "Desist for now; I myself know the right time. Śāriputra! My assembly is pure and has not yet produced any unprecedented unwholesome dharma. The lowest among my assembly has attained the fruit of stream-entry. All Tathāgatas do not establish precepts for disciples before unwholesome dharmas with outflows have arisen. In my assembly, there is not yet anyone who, relying on extensive learning, gives rise to outflows; there is not yet anyone who, due to material gain and fame, or due to having many desires, or due to displaying supernatural powers and being known to gods and humans, gives rise to outflows."

At that time, the World-Honored One, having completed the three-month rains retreat, told Ānanda: "Come, Ānanda! Let us go together to the place of the brahmin Verañjā." Ānanda accepted the instruction, adjusted his robes, and followed the Buddha to the brahmin's gate. At that time the brahmin was on a high balcony enjoying the five sensual pleasures. From afar he saw the World-Honored One and immediately recalled and awoke to his former invitation. He hurried down, wiped the seat to welcome the Buddha, prostrated with his five limbs touching the ground, paid reverence to the Buddha, and confessed his fault, reproaching himself: "I am a foolish and ignorant person! I invited the Buddha for the rains retreat but completely failed to provide offerings. It was not that my intentions changed, nor that I lacked provisions; I was simply confused and forgetful, and could not remember. I humbly pray that the World-Honored One accept my confession!"

The Buddha said: "You were indeed foolish! You invited the Buddha and the Sangha but completely failed to provide offerings. It is proper that you confess! I shall now, together with the assembly, accept your confession." He further told the brahmin: "In my holy Dharma, one who knows how to confess grows in wholesome dharmas."

The brahmin again said to the Buddha: "May the Buddha and the Sangha remain for one month and accept my offerings!" The Buddha did not accept, telling him: "You, brahmin, have different faith and different views. That you were even able to invite the Buddha is already a great matter." He asked thus up to three times, and the Buddha again did not accept, telling him: "I have already stayed here for the three-month rains retreat. Now I should travel on and cannot remain further." The brahmin again said to the Buddha: "I humbly pray that the World-Honored One accept my farewell offering tomorrow!" The Buddha silently consented.

The brahmin prepared throughout the night various kinds of fine foods. When the mealtime arrived the next morning, he spread out seats. When the appointed time had come, the World-Honored One and his disciples together went and took their seats. The brahmin then set forth the offerings and personally served and poured for them. After the meal, when water had been passed around, he presented four pieces of cotton cloth and one pair of leather sandals to the World-Honored One; to each member of the Sangha, two pieces of cloth and one pair of leather sandals, as a rains retreat gift. The bhikṣus said: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to accept rains retreat gifts." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha praised in various ways fewness of desires and contentment, praised the precepts, and praised the keeping of precepts, and then told the bhikṣus: "On account of the brahmin Verañjā, from today onward, I permit the acceptance of rains retreat gifts." They then all accepted. Thereupon the brahmin's heart was filled with great joy. He took a small seat and sat before the Buddha. The Buddha then spoke a verse of rejoicing:

> "Among all celestial shrines, the worship of fire is foremost;
> Among all non-Buddhist schools, Sāvitrī is foremost;
> Among all people, the wheel-turning king is foremost;
> Among all flowing waters, the great ocean is foremost;
> Among all that give light, the sun and moon are foremost;
> Above and below among gods, the Buddha is the foremost field of merit."

At that time, the World-Honored One, having spoken this verse, further taught the Dharma, instructing, benefiting, and gladdening them. Then he rose from his seat and headed toward the country of Saṃkāśya. Traveling from place to place, he later arrived at Vaiśālī and stayed at the Kūṭāgāraśālā lecture hall by the Markaṭa-hradā (Monkey Pond). There the four assemblies of bhikṣus, bhikṣunīs, upāsakas, upāsikās, kings, great ministers, śramaṇas, and brahmins made offerings, paid reverence, honored, and praised him.

At that time, the elders of the town of Kalandaka had business that took them to the city. They heard that the Buddha, the World-Honored One, was at the Kūṭāgāraśālā lecture hall, and all went to where the Buddha was. They saw the Buddha, the World-Honored One, surrounded by a measureless assembly, teaching the Dharma. In that assembly there was an elder's son of Kalandaka named Sudinna. Having heard the Dharma with joy, he then had this thought: "As I understand what the Buddha has taught, one who lives as a householder is bound by love and attachment, and cannot for one's entire life extensively cultivate brahmacarya. One who has gone forth is unattached, like empty space. Would it not be better for me to go forth from the household life based on faith and cultivate the Way?" When the assembly had each departed, he went before the Buddha, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Just now, hearing the Buddha teach the Dharma, I had this thought: 'As I understand what the Buddha has taught, one who lives as a householder is bound by love and attachment, and cannot for one's entire life extensively cultivate brahmacarya. One who has gone forth is unattached, like empty space. Would it not be better for me to go forth from the household life based on faith and cultivate the Way?' World-Honored One! I have had this thought and now wish to go forth. I humbly pray that you grant me the going forth and ordination!"

The Buddha said: "Very good! Have your father and mother given permission?" He answered: "They have not yet given permission." The Buddha said: "In the Dharma of all Buddhas, if father and mother do not give permission, one may not go forth into the Way." He then said to the Buddha: "I shall now return and inform my parents." The Buddha said: "Now is the proper time."

Thereupon Sudinna rose from his seat, circumambulated three times to the right, returned home, and said to his parents: "I have heard the Buddha's Dharma. The household life is binding and attached. I now wish to go forth and extensively cultivate brahmacarya." His parents replied: "Stop, Sudinna! Do not say such things! We were previously without a child, and prayed to the gods, barely obtaining you. The love for an only child is truly deep in feeling. Even in death we would not be far from you; how can you leave us in life? Your household is wealthy, with gold, silver, and treasures; you may use them freely to cultivate merit and enjoy happiness in this present life. What use is going forth, robbing us of our heart's desire?" He earnestly requested up to three times, but his parents would not permit it. He then rose from his seat, went to a separate place, and made this vow: "If I cannot go forth, I will never eat again. I shall die here; what use is living in vain!" He then ceased eating, for up to six days.

His relatives heard of this and all came to console and persuade him, saying: "Your parents have only you as their child. Their love and affection are deep. Even death would not be distant, let alone hearing of a living separation. Your family is very wealthy and you can use this to plant merit. The Way lies in the mind, not in outward appearance and garments. Why must you injure your life and painfully go against your parents?" They said this up to three times, but he silently refused to accept their words. Then his friends also came to admonish him, speaking earnestly as above, and it was the same. They each left him and went to his parents, all saying: "As we see it, his resolve cannot be turned. If you permit him to go forth, you may still see him from time to time. If he does not enjoy the Way, his return has a set time. He has abstained from food for six days; his remaining life hangs by a thread. In a matter of days, he will be cast into the open field, swallowed and pecked by kites and crows, fought over and eaten by tigers and wolves. As his father and mother, how can you bear this!" Hearing this, his parents answered with tears: "We permit our son to go forth and cultivate brahmacarya; but we wish to make an agreement with him that he return to see us from time to time." When the relatives heard this, they all greatly rejoiced and went again to his place, saying: "Your parents have already permitted you to go forth. Do not forget to return from time to time, and you may go!" Sudinna was greatly overjoyed. He went to his parents and said: "I now go to the Buddha to go forth and cultivate the Way." His parents wept and answered: "We permit you to go forth and extensively cultivate brahmacarya; but do not forget the agreement, and return to see us from time to time."

Thereupon Sudinna bowed in farewell to his parents, circumambulated them three times, and departed. He returned to where the Buddha was, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! My parents have given permission. I humbly pray that you grant me the going forth and ordination!" The Buddha said: "Welcome, bhikṣu! Cultivate the brahmacarya. I have well taught the Dharma that severs all suffering." When the Buddha had spoken this, Sudinna's hair and beard spontaneously fell away, the kāṣāya robe appeared upon his body, and the almsbowl was in his hand; he immediately became a śramaṇa and obtained full ordination.

Not long after going forth, there was famine in the world. When the bhikṣus entered the city to beg for food, they obtained nothing at all. Sudinna, in a quiet place, had this thought: "Now there is this famine, and begging for alms is difficult. The place where I was born has abundant food and drink. I should lead the bhikṣus back to my native town so they can receive offerings, and I can also bring blessings to those people." He then rose from his seat and, together with the bhikṣus, returned to his native town and stayed beneath trees in a grove. When his parents heard of this, they ordered his wife: "You should adorn yourself with the garments and ornaments that our son favored when he was at home." When the adornment was complete, his parents took her with them and went together to that grove.

At that time Sudinna saw his parents coming, rose to greet them, and inquired after their well-being. His parents said: "Why do you disfigure your appearance and live among the trees in a grove? You should return, abandon the Way, and cultivate goodness at home." He said to his parents: "I cannot abandon the Way and return to that lowly life." They repeated this up to three times, but his resolve grew ever firmer. His parents wept bitterly and left, returning home. After several days, Sudinna's wife had her monthly period. She reported this to her mother-in-law. The mother-in-law said with delight: "This is a sign that she can bear a child." She then ordered her to adorn herself with the same garments and ornaments as before, and the parents again took her with them to that grove.

At that time Sudinna greeted them as before. His parents again said: "Why do you disfigure your appearance and live among the trees in a grove, enduring the hardship of wind, dew, hunger, and cold? Your family's wealth is known throughout the world. Just my personal treasures alone are piled above a person's head; how much more the family property, which is beyond measure! You can return home, cultivate goodness as you please, enjoy happiness in this present life, and receive blessed reward hereafter." He said to his parents: "As I see it, the five sensual desires harm virtue; their pleasures are like lightning, while sorrow and suffering extend long. I will never on account of these abandon the cultivation of brahmacarya." He said this up to three times, and his earnest replies grew ever more resolute. His parents then said: "Although you are our son, you now belong to the Śākya clan. You oppose us for the sake of the Way; what more can we say? But the ancestral sacrifices will cease, and the bonds of human kinship are weighty. By royal law, when the line of succession is severed, the family property is confiscated by the government. I have foreseen all of this! Do you not know? Our remaining hope rests on you to continue the family line. Consider this well; our words are exhausted!"

At that time Sudinna, hearing this admonishment, wept sadly. He silently complied with their command and went together with his wife back to their former room, and engaged in sexual intercourse three times. Then a deity descended: at that time, a great and powerful god from the Tuṣita Heaven died and entered the womb.

At that time, the earth deities told the space deities: "The son of Kalandaka has, within this unprecedented Sangha, done an unprecedented thing." The space deities told the Four Heavenly Kings, the Four Heavenly Kings told the Trāyastriṃśa Heaven, and it was reported from one to another up to the Brahmā Heaven.

His wife, when her months were full, gave birth to a son who was intelligent and accomplished. He was named Sīlavaddha (Continuation of the Line). When he grew up, he went forth and attained arhatship.

At that time Sudinna, having committed this evil act, immediately reproached himself: "I have lost my gain! How is it that I have gone forth in the Buddha's true Dharma yet have not perfected the cultivation of brahmacarya?" He became emaciated and haggard, barely having breath left. The bhikṣus saw him and asked: "You previously had a good complexion. Why are you now so haggard? Could it be that you are not happy with the brahmacarya and have committed an evil offense?" He answered: "I have committed an evil offense; that is why I am like this!" They then asked: "What offense have you committed?" He answered: "I engaged in impure conduct with my former wife." The bhikṣus said: "What you have done is unwholesome, is not pure conduct, is not the practice of a śramaṇa, and does not accord with the Way!" They reproached him in various ways as the Buddha had taught them to reproach, and then brought him to the Buddha and reported the matter. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha. It is the constant practice of all Buddhas: they know, yet deliberately ask; they know, yet sometimes do not ask. They ask at the right time, and do not ask at the wrong time. They ask when it is beneficial, and do not ask when it is not beneficial. It is the constant practice of all Buddhas: five hundred vajra spirits attend at their sides. If the Buddha asks three times and one does not answer truthfully, one's head will split into seven pieces. The Buddha asked the bhikṣus: "What do you wish to report?" The bhikṣus again reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha asked Sudinna: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "Truly so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha said: "You foolish man! What you have done is unwholesome, is not pure conduct, is not the practice of a śramaṇa, and does not accord with the Way! This cannot cause those without faith to have faith, and causes those with faith to retreat. Have you not heard me censure in various ways desire, the perception of desire, the thought of desire, and the fever of desire; and praise the severing of desire, the abandoning of the perception of desire, the elimination of the thought of desire, and the extinguishing of the fever of desire? I have constantly taught that desire is like a pile of red-hot bones, like a great fire pit, like a sharp knife, like a sharp arrow, like a poisonous snake, like poison, like an illusion, like a dream that deceives people. How then could you commit this great evil? Have you not heard the Dharma I have taught: that those who have not abandoned desire can be made to abandon desire; that those who are already heedless can be made heedful; that it severs craving and departs from conditioned dharmas; that one without further training abandons desire and proceeds toward the unconditioned Way; that it shows people the true essential path leading to final nirvāṇa? Do you not fear the sufferings of the three evil destinies? If you had not committed this great evil, you would certainly have attained immeasurable wholesome merits in the Buddha's true Dharma. You have been the first to open the gate of outflows and commit this great evil. Māra Pāpīyān constantly watches for the faults of bhikṣus. You have now opened the path for Māra, shattered the banner of Dharma, and raised the flag of Māra. Sudinna! Better to insert one's body into a great fire pit, or into the mouth of a poisonous snake, than to touch a woman's body with this! The evil you have committed will cause you to sink forever in birth and death, and you will never again be able to nurture wholesome dharmas!" After the Buddha had reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For ten benefits I establish precepts for the bhikṣus. What are these ten? Namely: for the harmony of the Sangha, for the governance of the Sangha, for the restraint of evil persons, for those with a sense of shame to dwell in comfort, for severing outflows in this present life, for extinguishing outflows in future lives, for causing those without faith to have faith, for causing those with faith to grow and increase, for the Dharma to endure long, and for the analysis of Vinaya so that brahmacarya endures long. From today, this precept should be recited thus:

"'If a bhikṣu engages in sexual intercourse, he incurs pārājika and is not to be lived with.'

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. There was an āraṇyaka bhikṣu dwelling in a secluded place. A troop of monkeys lived nearby. One bhikṣu developed desire for a female monkey. He lured her with food and then engaged in sexual intercourse with her. Later, many bhikṣus came to inspect the lodgings and arrived at his dwelling place. At that time, that bhikṣu had entered the city to beg for food. The female monkey came and displayed signs of sexual desire. The bhikṣus said to one another: "Observe this monkey; there must be a reason for this. Let us watch together; the evidence will surely appear." The bhikṣu who lived there returned shortly afterward. The monkey immediately went to him and displayed signs of being receptive to intercourse. That bhikṣu then engaged in sexual intercourse with her. The bhikṣus saw this and said: "Have you not heard that the Buddha has established the precept that a bhikṣu who engages in sexual intercourse incurs pārājika?" He answered: "The Buddha's rule applies to human women, not to animals." The bhikṣus said: "Between human women and animals, what difference is there? What you have done is unwholesome, is not pure conduct, is not the practice of a śramaṇa, and does not accord with the Way! This cannot cause those without faith to have faith, and causes those with faith to retreat. Have you not heard the World-Honored One censure in various ways desire, the perception of desire, the thought of desire, and the fever of desire?" They spoke in full as above. After reproaching him, they brought him to the Buddha and reported the matter. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "Truly so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha said: "You foolish man! What you have done is against the Dharma." He reproached him in various ways as stated above. After reproaching him, he told the bhikṣus: "From today, this precept should be recited thus:

"'If a bhikṣu engages in sexual intercourse, even with an animal, he incurs pārājika and is not to be lived with.'"

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, many bhikṣus were not happy cultivating brahmacarya and said to one another: "Going forth in the Buddha's Dharma is extremely painful. We should all together practice the customs of laypeople and the customs of non-Buddhist practitioners; perform the deeds of laypeople and the deeds of non-Buddhist practitioners; enter villages at proper times and also at improper times; engage in killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, drinking alcohol, and eating meat; watch entertainment day and night; and amuse ourselves with songs and chants." They repeatedly spoke in this way without any sense of shame. At that time, bhikṣus who upheld the precepts, who had few desires and were content, reproached them in various ways, then brought them to the Buddha and reported the matter. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "Truly so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways: "You should not say such things as practicing the customs of non-Buddhist practitioners and the customs of laypeople. If one speaks of practicing the customs of non-Buddhist practitioners, each statement is a sthūlātyaya. If of the customs of laypeople, it is a duṣkṛta."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, there was a bhikṣu of the Vṛji town named Sundarananda, who was well-known and received offerings and respect. He was not happy cultivating brahmacarya and practiced the customs of non-Buddhist practitioners and the customs of laypeople; he engaged in killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, and various evil deeds. Those householders who did not believe in or take joy in the Buddha-Dharma reproached him, saying: "How can a śramaṇa of the Śākya clan do such evil?" Everywhere people said: "The bhikṣu Sundara also indulges in the five sensual pleasures. These bhikṣus have no śramaṇa conduct, no brahmin conduct; they do not accept the śramaṇa Dharma, do not accept the brahmin Dharma. Wherever these bhikṣus do not go, there is good fortune!" This evil reputation spread throughout the world.

Then Sundara returned to the assembly and said: "Give me ordination again." The bhikṣus said: "We must report this to the World-Honored One." They then reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "Sundara is not a bhikṣu. If he has already received ordination, a formal motion followed by three proclamations should be made to expel him."

Whether it is the senior monk or one equal to the senior monk who knows the Dharma and Vinaya, they should make the announcement as follows: "May the venerable Sangha hear! The bhikṣu Sundara, whose precepts were weakened yet not renounced, has engaged in sexual intercourse. The Sangha now expels the bhikṣu Sundara. If the time is appropriate and the Sangha approves, let it be so. The announcement is thus."

"May the venerable Sangha hear! The bhikṣu Sundara, whose precepts were weakened yet not renounced, has engaged in sexual intercourse. The Sangha now expels the bhikṣu Sundara. Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak." The second and third proclamations are likewise.

"The Sangha has completed the expulsion of the bhikṣu Sundara. The Sangha has approved by its silence. The matter is thus upheld."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu speaks of practicing the customs of laypeople or the customs of non-Buddhist practitioners, this does not constitute renouncing the precepts. If one verbally says 'I renounce the precepts,' that constitutes renouncing the precepts. From today, this precept should be recited thus:

"'If a bhikṣu, having taken the training rules in common with other bhikṣus, with weakened precepts and without having renounced them, engages in sexual intercourse, even with an animal, that bhikṣu incurs pārājika and is not to be lived with.'"

"Bhikṣu" means: a bhikṣu who begs, a bhikṣu who wears patched and cut dull-colored robes, a bhikṣu who destroys evil, a true bhikṣu, a steadfast bhikṣu, a bhikṣu who sees faults, a bhikṣu ordained by one statement, a bhikṣu ordained by two statements, a bhikṣu ordained by three statements, a bhikṣu ordained by "Welcome," a bhikṣu ordained by a proper formal motion followed by three proclamations. These are called bhikṣus.

"In common training" means: as the Buddha has taught, "For one's entire life not transgressing; training in common in this training." This is called in common training.

"Training rules" means: the precepts one has received that are unbroken, precepts that do not give rise to evil dharmas, precepts that accomplish wholesome dharmas, and precepts associated with concentration.

"Weakened precepts yet not renounced" means: renouncing the precepts while asleep, renouncing the precepts to a sleeping person; these do not constitute renouncing the precepts. Renouncing the precepts while intoxicated, renouncing the precepts to an intoxicated person; renouncing the precepts while insane, renouncing the precepts to an insane person; renouncing the precepts with a scattered mind, renouncing the precepts to a person with a scattered mind; renouncing the precepts when the mind is deranged by illness, renouncing the precepts to a person whose mind is deranged by illness; renouncing the precepts to a non-sentient being, to a non-human, or to an animal; renouncing the precepts by sending a messenger, by sending a letter; renouncing the precepts by making signs, renouncing the precepts by waving the hand; renouncing the precepts by ambiguous words; being alone and perceiving oneself as alone, being alone but perceiving oneself as not alone, not being alone but perceiving oneself as alone, renouncing the precepts in such cases; speaking the language of the central region to a person from a border region, or speaking a border-region language to a person from the central region, renouncing the precepts; renouncing the precepts in jest, renouncing the precepts with indefinite words, renouncing the precepts in anger, renouncing the precepts under coercion; renouncing the precepts to someone to whom one should not address the renunciation: all of these do not constitute renouncing the precepts. Not uttering words to renounce the precepts does not constitute renouncing the precepts. This is called "not renouncing the precepts." The opposite of the above is called renouncing the precepts.

There may be weakened precepts without renouncing the precepts; there may be renouncing the precepts without weakened precepts; there may be both weakened precepts and renouncing the precepts; and there may be neither weakened precepts nor renouncing the precepts.

How is it weakened precepts without renouncing? If a bhikṣu is not happy cultivating brahmacarya, and having become unhappy with brahmacarya, still respects the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha; respects the precepts; respects the śramaṇa Dharma; respects the bhikṣu Dharma; respects the Vinaya; respects the Prātimokṣa; respects the upādhyāya, the ācārya, the upādhyāya's companions, and the ācārya's companions; respects fellow practitioners of brahmacarya; and does not slander the Three Honored Ones; yet reminisces about one's hometown, gardens, bathhouses, mountains, forests, trees, father, mother, brothers, sisters, sons, and daughters, down to servants and bondspeople. Reminiscing in this way, being sorrowful and unhappy, yet for one's entire life not transgressing brahmacarya: this is called weakened precepts without renouncing.

How is it renouncing the precepts without weakened precepts? If a bhikṣu is not happy cultivating brahmacarya, and having become unhappy with brahmacarya, still respects the Buddha, down to respecting fellow practitioners of brahmacarya. He then has this thought: "I wish to become a śrāmaṇera, or an upāsaka, down to wishing to become a non-Buddhist practitioner; not a śramaṇa of the Śākya clan." He further thinks: "I now wish to renounce the Buddha, the Dharma, the Sangha, the precepts, the Vinaya, the Prātimokṣa, the upādhyāya, the ācārya, the upādhyāya's companions, the ācārya's companions, and the fellow practitioners of brahmacarya." He then says: "I now renounce the Buddha. What use is the Buddha to me? What meaning does the Buddha have? I am now free from the Buddha." Down to saying: "I am now free from the fellow practitioners of brahmacarya." He further says: "I am no longer to be reckoned as a śramaṇa of the Śākya clan. I thus slander the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, down to slandering the fellow practitioners of brahmacarya." Making such mental determinations and verbal statements, and declaring them to another person: this is called renouncing the precepts without weakened precepts.

How is it both weakened precepts and renouncing the precepts? If a bhikṣu is not happy cultivating brahmacarya, has little respect for the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, down to having little respect for fellow practitioners of brahmacarya; reminisces about one's hometown, down to servants and bondspeople; and has this thought: "I now wish to renounce the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, down to renouncing the fellow practitioners of brahmacarya." He then says: "I now renounce the Buddha. What use is the Buddha to me? What meaning does the Buddha have? I am now free from the Buddha, down to free from the fellow practitioners of brahmacarya." He further says: "I am no longer to be reckoned as a śramaṇa of the Śākya clan. I thus slander the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, down to slandering the fellow practitioners of brahmacarya." Making such mental determinations and verbal statements, and declaring them to another person: this is called both weakened precepts and renouncing the precepts.

How is it neither weakened precepts nor renouncing the precepts? If a bhikṣu firmly maintains the precepts he has received without abandoning them, unmoved and unchanged: this is called neither weakened precepts nor renouncing the precepts.

"Engages in sexual intercourse" means: sexual intercourse is called the act contrary to brahmacarya, the act of indolence, the dog-like act, the detestable act; the joining of two bodies and the emission of impurity. This is called engaging in sexual intercourse.

"Pārājika" means: it is called a fallen dharma, an evil dharma, a dharma of having one's head cut off, a dharma contrary to the śramaṇa life.

"Not to be lived with" means: as when one was formerly a layperson, one cannot share with bhikṣus in a single training, equal training, unequal training, or any other training; one cannot share with bhikṣus in a single karman, equal karman, unequal karman, or any other karman; one cannot share with bhikṣus in a single Prātimokṣa recitation, equal Prātimokṣa recitation, unequal Prātimokṣa recitation, or any other Prātimokṣa recitation. This is called "not to be lived with."

All Buddha World-Honored Ones have well taught the true Dharma and have also well taught similes. They say that one who commits sexual misconduct is like a needle with a broken eye that can no longer be used; like a person whose life has ended and cannot be revived; like a stone that is broken and cannot be joined again; like the heart of a tāla tree that has been cut and cannot grow again.

In this regard, if a bhikṣu engages in sexual intercourse with three kinds of beings, he incurs pārājika: human, non-human, and animal. If a bhikṣu engages in sexual intercourse with three kinds of females, he incurs pārājika: human female, non-human female, and animal female. With three kinds of males: human male, non-human male, and animal male. With three kinds of paṇḍakas: human paṇḍaka, non-human paṇḍaka, and animal paṇḍaka. With three kinds of genderless beings: human genderless, non-human genderless, and animal genderless. With three kinds of beings with dual sex organs: human with dual sex organs, non-human with dual sex organs, and animal with dual sex organs; engaging in sexual intercourse is likewise.

If a bhikṣu engages in sexual intercourse with a human female, non-human female, or animal female in three orifices: the anus, the vagina, or the mouth; while she is asleep, intoxicated, insane, with scattered mind, with mind deranged by illness, dead, or when less than half consumed: it is pārājika. When more than half consumed, or when only bones remain: if there is emission of impurity, it is saṃghāvaśeṣa; if there is no emission of impurity, it is sthūlātyaya. For genderless females and those with dual sex organs, it is likewise.

If a bhikṣu engages in sexual intercourse with a human male, non-human male, or animal male in two orifices: the anus or the mouth; while he is asleep, down to when less than half consumed: it is pārājika. When more than half consumed, or when only bones remain: if there is emission of impurity, it is saṃghāvaśeṣa; if there is no emission of impurity, it is sthūlātyaya. For genderless males and paṇḍakas, it is likewise.

In the above orifices, if with external preliminary efforts there is internal emission of impurity, or with internal preliminary efforts there is external emission of impurity; while asleep, down to when less than half consumed: it is pārājika. When more than half consumed, or when only bones remain: if there is emission of impurity, it is saṃghāvaśeṣa; if there is no emission of impurity, it is sthūlātyaya.

If a bhikṣu is compelled by force and, in the above orifices, engages in sexual intercourse: if upon entry he experiences pleasure, but not upon withdrawal or while remaining; if upon withdrawal he experiences pleasure, but not upon entry or while remaining; if while remaining he experiences pleasure, but not upon withdrawal or entry; if upon entry and withdrawal he experiences pleasure, but not while remaining; if upon entry and while remaining he experiences pleasure, but not upon withdrawal; if upon withdrawal and while remaining he experiences pleasure, but not upon entry; if upon entry, withdrawal, and while remaining he experiences pleasure; while asleep, down to when less than half consumed: it is pārājika. When more than half consumed, or when only bones remain: if there is emission of impurity, it is saṃghāvaśeṣa; if there is no emission of impurity, it is sthūlātyaya. If upon entry, withdrawal, and while remaining he experiences no pleasure at all: there is no offense.

If a bhikṣu, with a mind of sexual desire, inserts his male organ even partially into any of the above orifices: it is pārājika in every case. If he uses a finger or any external object and inserts it into any of the above orifices: it is sthūlātyaya in every case.

For a bhikṣunī, it is also pārājika. For a śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, or śrāmaṇerī, it is duṣkṛta, and they are expelled.

Those who are not in offense: one with an insane mind, one with a disordered mind, one whose mind is deranged by illness, and one who does so for the first time; these four are not in offense. For all precepts below, it is the same, and will not be repeated.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time there was a bhikṣu named Dhaniya, a potter's son. On Mount Iṣigili he built a grass hut to live in. At the proper time, he took his bowl and entered the city to beg for food. Woodcutters would come after him and destroy his hut, taking the materials away. After eating, when he returned, he would repair it again. This happened up to three times, and his heart grew ever more resentful. He then had this thought: "I am fortunate that I am skilled in working with clay. Why do I not make a solid, complete tile-and-brick dwelling to be free of this trouble?" He then made one. The ridge-beam, main beam, bracket-blocks, crossbeams, rafters, pillars, purlins, girders, lattice-work windows, and doors were as wonderfully crafted as if by a deity. He piled up firewood and fired it until it was a brilliant red, fine and beautiful. When a strong wind blew, it produced the sound of a harp. The Buddha, on Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain, saw the dwelling from afar, variously carved and decorated, red and fine and beautiful. He asked Ānanda: "What dwelling is that?" Ānanda said to the Buddha: "It was made by Dhaniya with his own labor." The Buddha told Ānanda: "What Dhaniya has done is against the Dharma! How can one who has gone forth engage in such harmful work, killing and injuring living creatures without compassion? I have previously taught in various ways the Dharma of loving-kindness and patience. How can a bhikṣu lack such compassion?" After the World-Honored One had reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "Go there and destroy what he has made." The bhikṣus accepted the instruction and went to the dwelling. At that time, Dhaniya came out from inside the dwelling and asked the bhikṣus: "I have not offended you. Why do you come as a group to destroy my dwelling?" The bhikṣus said: "We follow the command of the World-Honored One; it is not our own wish." Dhaniya said: "If the Dharma King has ordered its destruction, what more can I say!" The bhikṣus then together destroyed it and brought Dhaniya to the Buddha, reporting the matter. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Dhaniya: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "I truly did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha had reproached him in various ways as above, he told the bhikṣus: "From today, if a bhikṣu makes a fired brick dwelling, it is sthūlātyaya. If he displays his craftsman skills, it is duṣkṛta."

Then Dhaniya had another thought: "I first built a grass hut, but the woodcutters always destroyed it. Then I made a brick dwelling, but it violated the Dharma King's rules for those who have gone forth. Now I should seek good timber and build a large dwelling. Surely I can then live long in peace without further trouble." He further thought: "The timber superintendent of Rājagṛha is my acquaintance. I shall go and ask him." Having thought this, he went and said: "I need timber. Can you give it to me?" The timber superintendent said: "I do not have the authority over the timber." He asked: "Who does?" He answered: "The king." Dhaniya said: "The king has already given it to me." The timber superintendent said: "If the king has already given it to you, take what you wish." Dhaniya then took the large timbers used for the city's defenses, cut them down, and carried them away.

At that time, the great minister Varṣakāra was inspecting various places and encountered them on the road. He immediately asked the timber superintendent: "Why have you given these large city-defense timbers to that bhikṣu?" He answered: "It was not I who gave them." He asked further: "Then who?" He answered: "It was the king." Varṣakāra then reported to the king: "I am uncertain, great king, why you have given the city-defense timbers to Dhaniya?" The king said: "Who said I gave them?" Varṣakāra said: "The timber superintendent." The king immediately ordered his attendants to arrest the timber superintendent. They obeyed and arrested him, bringing him before the king. At that time, Dhaniya had entered the city to beg for food and saw him on the road. He asked: "What offense have you committed to be bound like this?" He answered: "It is because of the venerable one that I have incurred this great crime. I beg you to save me and spare my life!" Dhaniya said: "Go ahead first; I will come shortly after."

When the timber superintendent was brought before the king, the king asked: "Why did you give the city-defense timbers to Dhaniya?" He replied: "Great king! I did not presume to do so on my own. Dhaniya said the king had ordered that they be given." The king then ordered Dhaniya to be summoned. At that time, Dhaniya was already outside the gate. The king ordered him to come forward, and he came before the king. The king asked: "When did I give timber to a bhikṣu?" Dhaniya said: "Does the king not remember? When you first ascended the throne, you bestowed all the grass, timber, and water within your realm upon śramaṇas and brahmins." The king said: "What I originally bestowed did not include things that had an owner. How extraordinary! A bhikṣu has used such a stratagem to take others' property." He further said: "I am an anointed king. How could I imprison or execute a śramaṇa? You may now quickly go to the Buddha; the Dharma King will deal with you according to the Dharma."

Those who heard of this all said in alarm: "Dhaniya committed a crime deserving death! How is it that he was merely reproached and released? If one can escape so easily, who would not steal?" They further criticized, saying: "The śramaṇas of the Śākya clan personally receive the king's offerings, yet steal the king's timber! How much more should we fear for ourselves, that we will not be safe? The śramaṇas of the Śākya clan constantly praise not stealing and teach people to give. How is it that now they personally practice the ways of a thief? These have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" This evil reputation spread and was heard throughout the land. The elders, householders, and brahmins in the country who did not believe in or take joy in the Buddha-Dharma, upon seeing śramaṇas from afar, reviled them in various ways. The bhikṣus heard this and asked one another: "Who stole the king's timber and brought about this evil reputation?" Dhaniya said: "It was my doing." Then the bhikṣus reproached him in various ways: "What you have done is against the Dharma and does not accord with the Way! The World-Honored One has in various ways criticized taking what is not given and praised not stealing. How is it that you have personally practiced the ways of a thief?" After the bhikṣus had thus reproached him, they brought him to the Buddha and reported the matter. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Dhaniya: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "Truly so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached him in various ways, just as he had reproached Sudinna.

At that time, a great minister of Magadha had gone forth to cultivate the Way and was attending the Buddha at his side. The Buddha asked the bhikṣus: "Under King Ajātaśatru, for stealing what amount does one receive the death penalty?" The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "For five coins or more, one receives the death penalty." After the Buddha had further reproached him using this standard, he told the bhikṣus: "For ten benefits I establish precepts for the bhikṣus. From today, this precept should be recited thus:

"'If a bhikṣu steals five coins or more, he incurs pārājika and is not to be lived with.'"

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, many bhikṣus said: "The precept the Buddha established pertains to goods in a settlement, not to things in an uninhabited place." Other bhikṣus also said: "Whether an offense or not, whether regulated or not, taking things involves no hardship." They each took, with a mind of theft, owned and unowned goods from uninhabited places. Having taken them, each developed doubt and remorse. They went to Ānanda and asked him. Ānanda then reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "Truly so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways: "What difference is there between a settlement and an uninhabited place?" After reproaching them, he told the bhikṣus: "From today, this precept should be recited thus:

"'If a bhikṣu, whether in a settlement or in an uninhabited place, with a mind of theft takes what is not given: if a king or a great minister would seize him, bind him, kill him, or banish him, saying, "You are a thief! You are a fool! You are deluded!": that bhikṣu incurs pārājika and is not to be lived with.'"

"Settlement" means: if a moat, or a hedge or palisade, surrounds an area of up to three yojanas, down to even a single dwelling; this is called a settlement.

Outside the settlement, excluding the area traversed by the settlement's inhabitants, is called an uninhabited place. Outside the settlement, within one arrow-shot, is the area where persons with a sense of propriety relieve themselves; this is called the area traversed by the settlement's inhabitants.

When property belongs to another and is guarded by another, and one takes it without it being given: this is called "with a mind of theft." Also, taking another's property by a deceitful mind, a crooked mind, an angry mind, or an intimidating mind is also called "with a mind of theft."

Whether one takes it oneself or causes another to take it, and the property is moved from its original place: this is called "taking what is not given."

The ruler of a country, the chief of a settlement, an anointed king, or a wheel-turning king: these are called "king."

One who administers the affairs of the country is called a "great minister."

Seizing one by the hand or hair is called "seizing."

Cangues, fetters, pillories, and chains are called "binding."

Taking one's life with a blade or weapon is called "killing."

Driving one out of a single dwelling-place, or out of an entire country, is called "banishing."

Departing from wholesome dharmas and neutral dharmas and falling into unwholesome states is called "thief."

Having no discernment is called "fool."

Entering into darkness is called "deluded."

The offenses in this regard are: items underground, items on the ground, items in the air, a settlement, items of a settlement, a shop, items of a shop, a field, items of a field, a garden, items of a garden, a building, items of a building, a conveyance, items of a conveyance, a load being carried, items of a load, a boat, items of a boat, a pond, items of a pond, things deposited for safekeeping, things given back from deposit, blocking a road, lying in wait on a road, pointing out a location, guiding along a path, instructing in taking, taking together, and not paying taxes.

"Items underground" means: if an item is underground, and a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this item." The arousal of the mental intention and the preparatory acts are each duṣkṛta. Digging the ground is pāyantikā. Grasping the item is duṣkṛta. Moving the item is sthūlātyaya. If the item is moved from its original place and is worth five coins, it is pārājika. If less than five coins, it is sthūlātyaya.

"Items on the ground" means: items on the ground, whether beds, frames, stools, benches, door lintels, beams, ridgepoles, down to items on rooftops or in trees: all such things are called items on the ground. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this item!" The arousal of the mental intention and the preparatory acts, down to grasping the item, are each duṣkṛta. Moving the item is sthūlātyaya. If the item is moved from its original place and is worth five coins, it is pārājika. If less than five coins, it is sthūlātyaya.

"Items in the air" means: if by supernatural power an item is placed in the air, or a bird with an owner carries it, or it is blown by the wind. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this item." The arousal of the mental intention and the preparatory acts are each duṣkṛta. Moving the item is sthūlātyaya. If the item is moved from its original place and is worth five coins, it is pārājika. If less than five coins, it is sthūlātyaya.

"Settlement" means: a perimeter of up to three yojanas, down to even one dwelling-place. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this settlement." The arousal of the mental intention and the preparatory acts are each duṣkṛta. Driving in stakes, each hammer blow is pāyantikā. If by rope-measurement the disputed area gained is worth five coins, it is pārājika. If less than five coins, it is sthūlātyaya.

"Items of a settlement" means: whatever items are within the settlement. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this item." If obtained, it is pārājika.

"Shop" means: if a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this shop." The arousal of the mental intention and the preparatory acts are each duṣkṛta. Driving in stakes, each hammer blow is pāyantikā. If by rope-measurement the disputed area gained is obtained, it is pārājika.

"Items of a shop" means: whatever items are within the shop. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this item." If obtained, it is pārājika.

"Field" means: irrigated and dry fields. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this field." The arousal of the mental intention and the preparatory acts are each duṣkṛta. Driving in stakes, each hammer blow is pāyantikā. If by rope-measurement the disputed area gained is obtained, it is pārājika.

"Items of a field" means: whatever grains and goods produced in the field. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this item." If obtained, it is pārājika.

"Garden" means: fruit gardens, vegetable gardens, and other gardens. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this garden." The arousal of the mental intention and the preparatory acts are each duṣkṛta. Driving in stakes, each hammer blow is pāyantikā. If by rope-measurement the disputed area gained is obtained, it is pārājika.

"Items of a garden" means: whatever products of the garden. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this item." If obtained, it is pārājika.

"Building" means: a building inhabited by householders or renunciants, whether multi-storied. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this building." The arousal of the mental intention and the preparatory acts are each duṣkṛta. Driving in stakes, each hammer blow is pāyantikā. If by rope-measurement the disputed area gained is obtained, it is pārājika.

"Items of a building" means: whatever items are within the building. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this item." If obtained, it is pārājika.

"Conveyance" means: elephants, horses, carriages, palanquins, and all conveyances. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this conveyance." The arousal of the mental intention and the preparatory acts, down to when grasping it, are each duṣkṛta. Moving it is sthūlātyaya. If it is moved from its original place and is worth five coins, it is pārājika. If less than five coins, it is sthūlātyaya.

"Items of a conveyance" means: whatever items are upon the conveyance. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this item." If obtained, it is pārājika.

"Load" means: carried on the head, carried on the shoulder, carried on the back, or carried by hand: all of these are called a load. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this load." The arousal of the mental intention and the preparatory acts are each duṣkṛta. When it is moved, it is sthūlātyaya. If it is moved from its original place and is worth five coins, it is pārājika. If less than five coins, it is sthūlātyaya.

"Items of a load" means: whatever items are in the load. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this item." If obtained, it is pārājika.

"Boat" means: a leather boat, a gourd-raft, a wooden boat, or a bamboo raft: all of these are called a boat. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this boat." The arousal of the mental intention and the preparatory acts are each duṣkṛta. When it is moved, it is sthūlātyaya. If it is moved from its original place and is worth five coins, it is pārājika. If less than five coins, it is sthūlātyaya.

"Items of a boat" means: whatever items are upon the boat. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this item." If obtained, it is pārājika.

"Pond" means: embankments, lakes, and all bodies of water are called a pond. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this pond." The arousal of the mental intention and the preparatory acts are each duṣkṛta. Driving in stakes, each hammer blow is pāyantikā. If by rope-measurement the disputed area gained is worth five coins, it is pārājika. If less than five coins, it is sthūlātyaya.

"Items of a pond" means: whatever products of the pond. If a bhikṣu thinks: "I shall steal this item." If obtained, it is pārājika.

"Deposited for safekeeping" means: if a person deposits an item with a bhikṣu, and with a mind of theft he does not return it. When the owner gives up hope, and its value is five coins, it is pārājika. If less than five coins, it is sthūlātyaya.

"Given back from deposit" means: if a bhikṣu accepts another's deposited item and with a mind of theft does not return it to that person. If its value is five coins, it is pārājika. If less than five coins, it is sthūlātyaya.

"Blocking a road" means: a bhikṣu acts as a lookout for bandits, blocking the road and not allowing other people to come.
