# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Two

### First Division, Initial Section, Third Matter

The Buddha was in Vaiśālī. At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus to cultivate the contemplation of impurity, saying it would yield great fruits and benefits. The bhikṣus thereupon all cultivated and practiced it, deeply entering into loathing and disgust, feeling shame and revulsion toward their own bodies. It was like a young man who delights in cleanliness and purity, who bathes, anoints his body, and puts on fresh, clean clothes, and then suddenly has three corpses hung around his neck, with pus and blood pressing against his body, and worms streaming and covering his entire form. That person, afflicted by this torment, has no other thought but this: "When can I be freed from this disgrace?" The bhikṣus' loathing and disgust for their bodies was just like this. Among them, some killed themselves, some killed one another in succession, and some sought knives, ropes, or consumed poison.

One bhikṣu, having developed loathing for his body, went to the caṇḍāla named Mīrin and said: "End my life for me, and I will give you my robes and bowl." The caṇḍāla, for the sake of the robes and bowl, then took a sharp knife and ended his life. There was blood staining the knife, and he took it to the Vaksu River to wash it. He then gave rise to a mind of regret and had this thought: "What I have done is unwholesome. How could I, for the sake of a small gain, end the life of a morality-keeping śramaṇa and incur immeasurable offenses?"

At that time, Māra, the lord of the heaven of freely transforming others' pleasures, knew his thoughts. Just as a strong man might bend and extend his arm, Māra came before him, rising up from the water and standing upon its surface, and praised him, saying: "Excellent! You have gained great benefit by ending the life of a morality-keeping śramaṇa. Those not yet crossed over, you have crossed over. Your merit and blessings are immeasurable. The heavenly spirits have recorded this, and so I have come to tell you."

The caṇḍāla then gave rise to evil, wrong views, and his heart was filled with great joy: "I should now cross over more of those not yet crossed over." That caṇḍāla well understood the marks of those who loathed their bodies and those who did not: "If an ordinary bhikṣu has not yet separated from desire, when I raise my knife toward him, his heart will be frightened; this is one who has not loathed his body. If I kill such a one, I will gain very little merit. I should now seek those who have already attained the fruits of the path and are without fear." Thereupon, he took a long knife in hand and went from cell to cell, from walking path to walking path, loudly proclaiming: "Those who wish to attain parinirvāṇa, I shall cross them over." At that time, all the bhikṣus who loathed their bodies came out to him, and he immediately ended their lives. In a single day, he killed ten, twenty, and up to sixty. Because of this, the number of the Sangha decreased, and the great virtuous śrāvakas were all no longer to be seen.

At that time, the World-Honored One arose from samādhi and sat in an open place, surrounded by a great assembly. Surveying the Sangha, he said to Ānanda: "Why has the Sangha diminished today?" Ānanda said to the Buddha: "The World-Honored One once taught the bhikṣus the contemplation of impurity. The bhikṣus, cultivating and practicing it, loathed and were anguished by their bodies, and they killed one another in succession. Even Mīrin, in a single day, took the lives of sixty practitioners of the holy life. This is why today the Sangha has diminished. It would be excellent, World-Honored One, if you would teach another wholesome path of Dharma, so that the bhikṣus may dwell in peace and happiness." The Buddha told Ānanda: "You should now announce that all bhikṣus dwelling in dependence on Vaiśālī should come and assemble at the universal assembly hall." Ānanda received the instruction and summoned them to assemble. When they had assembled, he said: "May the Sage know the time." The World-Honored One rose from his seat, went to the lecture hall, took his seat, and asked the bhikṣus: "Did these events truly occur?" They answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha rebuked them in various ways: "You are foolish! What you have done is contrary to the Dharma! Have you not heard my teaching on loving-kindness and patience, on protecting and being mindful of sentient beings? How is it that you did not remember this Dharma?" After rebuking them, he told the bhikṣus: "If one kills oneself, one commits the offense of sthūlātyaya." He further said: "From now on, you should cultivate ānāpāna-smṛti, take delight in the contemplation of purity, and take delight in the contemplation of joy. Having contemplated thus, if unwholesome mental states arise, you will be able to eliminate them. For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, with his own hand, kills a person and ends that person's life, that bhikṣu commits a pārājika offense and is expelled from communal life."

At that time, many bhikṣus became seriously ill. Other bhikṣus came to inquire after them, saying: "Venerable one, has your illness improved at all? Can you bear the suffering?" The sick bhikṣu said: "The illness has not yet improved, and the suffering is unbearable." He then said to the bhikṣus: "Give me a knife, a rope, give me poison, give me food that will worsen my illness, or take me to the edge of a high cliff." The bhikṣus in each case complied with his requests. The sick bhikṣu either stabbed himself with a knife, strangled himself with a rope, consumed poison, ate food that worsened his illness, or threw himself from a high cliff, ending his own life. When the bhikṣus saw that he had died, they gave rise to regret and reported this to Ānanda. Ānanda brought them to the Buddha's presence and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha rebuked them in various ways: "You are foolish! What difference is there between killing a person with one's own hand and giving him a knife so that he dies? From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu kills a person with his own hand, or provides a knife so that another dies, he commits a pārājika offense and is expelled from communal life."

There was also a bhikṣu who became seriously ill. Other bhikṣus came to inquire after him and, as described above, he said to the bhikṣus: "Give me a knife, a rope, or poison." The bhikṣus said: "The Buddha does not allow us to provide implements for someone to kill himself. However, I have an acquaintance who is a hunter. I will summon him for you and have him end your life." The sick bhikṣu said: "Summon him quickly for me!" That bhikṣu ran and told the hunter: "There is a bhikṣu here who is seriously ill and no longer wishes to live. If you end his life, you can gain great merit." The hunter said: "If killing living beings yields great merit, then do butchers and slaughtermen gain great merit? You bhikṣus claim to have minds of loving-kindness and compassion, yet now you instruct others to kill. What difference is there between instructing others to kill and killing oneself?" The bhikṣus all gave rise to regret and went to report to Ānanda. Ānanda brought them to the Buddha's presence and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha rebuked them in various ways and said to the bhikṣus: "What difference is there between killing oneself and instructing others to kill? From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu kills himself or instructs others to kill, he commits a pārājika offense and is expelled from communal life."

There was also a bhikṣu who became seriously ill. Other bhikṣus inquired after him as before and said to the sick one: "Your observance of the precepts is complete, and you deserve to receive heavenly rewards. If you kill yourself, you will certainly be reborn in heaven. Why endure such prolonged suffering?" The sick bhikṣu said: "Even if that is so, though I have this suffering, I cannot kill myself. Why? Because if one kills oneself, one commits the offense of sthūlātyaya; moreover, one would lose the opportunity to widely cultivate the holy life." They further rebuked them, saying: "What difference is there between killing a person with one's own hand and instructing someone to kill himself? Yet you bhikṣus do this evil deed!" The senior bhikṣus heard this and rebuked them in various ways, then brought them to the Buddha's presence and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha rebuked them in various ways: "You are foolish! What difference is there between killing a person with one's own hand and instructing someone to kill himself? From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu kills a person with his own hand, or instructs someone to kill himself, he commits a pārājika offense and is expelled from communal life."

There was also a bhikṣu who became seriously ill. Other bhikṣus inquired after him as before and also said to the sick one: "Your holy life is already established; when you die, you will enjoy heavenly bliss. Why endure such prolonged illness and suffering without killing yourself?" The sick bhikṣu said: "Although that may be so for us, I cannot kill myself. Why? The Buddha has prohibited suicide, making it the offense of sthūlātyaya. Moreover, if my illness is cured, I can cultivate the holy life."

At that time, that country also experienced danger from bandits. The lay people were separated from their flesh and blood and endured all kinds of affliction and sorrow. The bhikṣus said to them: "You have already cultivated the meritorious deeds for rebirth in heaven. Why endure this suffering of separation from your flesh and blood, this grief and sorrow, without killing yourselves?" They answered: "Although we are grieved and sorrowful, we cannot kill ourselves. Why? While we are in the world and endure suffering, we know to cultivate the practice of the path." They further rebuked the bhikṣus, saying: "The way of the śramaṇa is loving-kindness and patience toward sentient beings. How can you praise death and wish for people to kill themselves? What difference is there between killing oneself and praising death?" The senior bhikṣus heard this and rebuked them in various ways, then brought them to the Buddha's presence and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha rebuked them in various ways: "What you have done is contrary to the Dharma. What difference is there between killing oneself and praising death? From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, whether toward a person or a being resembling a person, kills with his own hand, provides a knife or poison to kill, instructs another to kill, or instructs someone to kill themselves, or extols death, or praises death, saying: 'Bah! What use is this wretched life? Death is better than life,' and making such intention, kills in accordance with that intention; through such various causes and conditions, if that person dies because of this, that bhikṣu commits a pārājika offense and is expelled from communal life."

From after entry into the mother's womb up to forty-nine days, this is called "a being resembling a person." After this period, all are called "a person."

Killing with one's own hands, feet, knife, club, poison, and so forth is called "killing with one's own hand."

When another wishes to kill himself and seeks implements for killing, providing them is called "providing a knife or poison to kill."

Dispatching another person to kill is called "instructing another to kill."

Instructing another to seek death is called "instructing someone to kill themselves."

Saying that death is better than life is called "extolling death" and "praising death."

Killing by directing various ghosts and spirits in accordance with one's intention is called "making such intention, killing in accordance with that intention."

Among these, the modes of offense are: killing with one's own hand, sending a messenger, sending a messenger through intermediaries, sending a messenger again, pointing out and indicating, verbal instruction, speaking while asleep, speaking to one who is asleep, speaking while intoxicated, speaking to one who is intoxicated, speaking while insane, speaking to one who is insane, speaking while one's mind is disordered, speaking to one whose mind is disordered, speaking while one's mind is impaired by illness, speaking to one whose mind is impaired by illness, sending a letter, making a sign, hand gestures, ambiguous speech; alone with the perception of being alone, not alone with the perception of being alone, alone with the perception of not being alone; jesting speech, form, sound, smell, taste, touch, upavadha (pit-trap), upaśaya (concealed trap), upahāra (sorcery).

Killing with one's own hand: killing with one's own hands, feet, knife, club, and so forth; if that person dies, it is a pārājika.

Sending a messenger: dispatching a messenger to kill that person; if that person dies, it is a pārājika.

Sending a messenger through intermediaries: dispatching person A to kill, but person A does not kill himself and instead sends person B to kill; if the person dies, it is a pārājika.

Sending a messenger again: the initially dispatched messenger is unable to carry out the killing and returns to report to the bhikṣu, and the bhikṣu dispatches the messenger again to kill; if the person dies, it is a pārājika.

Pointing out and indicating: pointing to the sun, moon, or constellations and saying to someone: "Your merit warrants rebirth there. You may kill yourself." If the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Verbal instruction: speaking of the faults and evils of life, and praising the goodness of death; if the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Speaking while asleep: a bhikṣu, while sleeping, speaks what was previously contemplated, saying: "Your meritorious virtues are already accomplished. You should kill yourself." That person, hearing this, waits until the bhikṣu awakens and asks: "Why did you say this?" He answers: "While I was asleep, wishing to benefit you, I spoke thus to you. Now that I am awake, I also speak thus. You may die according to my words." If the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Speaking to one who is asleep: speaking thus to a sleeping person: "Your meritorious virtues are already accomplished. You may kill yourself with a knife and so forth." Ghosts and spirits cause the sleeper to hear it. Upon waking, the person asks: "Why did you say this?" He answers: "While you were asleep, wishing to benefit you, I spoke thus. Now that you are awake, I also speak thus. You may die according to my words." If the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Speaking while intoxicated: while intoxicated, speaking what was previously contemplated, saying: "Your meritorious virtues are already accomplished. You should kill yourself with a knife and so forth." That person, hearing this, waits until the bhikṣu is sober and asks: "Why did you say this?" He answers: "While I was intoxicated, wishing to benefit you, I spoke thus. Now that I am sober, I also speak thus. You may die according to my words." If the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Speaking to one who is intoxicated: speaking thus: "Your meritorious virtues are already accomplished. You may kill yourself with a knife and so forth." After the intoxicated person becomes sober, the person asks: "Why did you say this?" He answers: "Wishing to benefit you, while you were intoxicated, I spoke thus. Now that you are sober, I also speak thus. You may die according to my words." If the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Speaking while insane, speaking to one who is insane, speaking while one's mind is disordered, speaking to one whose mind is disordered, speaking while one's mind is impaired by illness, speaking to one whose mind is impaired by illness: all are likewise.

Sending a letter: a bhikṣu sends a letter ordering a killing. In composing the letter, each character constitutes a sthūlātyaya. When the letter reaches that person, if that person kills as a result, and the person dies, it is a pārājika.

Making a sign: a bhikṣu says to someone: "When you see me sit down, stand up, raise my hand, lower my hand, or say it is cold or hot, then kill that person." When that person sees the sign and kills, if the person dies, it is a pārājika.

Hand gestures: making hand gestures to instruct someone to kill, and that person kills accordingly; if the person dies, it is a pārājika.

Ambiguous speech: a bhikṣu uses ambiguous speech to instruct someone to kill, and that person kills accordingly; if the person dies, it is a pārājika.

Being alone with the perception of being alone: duṣkṛta. Not being alone with the perception of being alone, or being alone with the perception of not being alone: sthūlātyaya.

Jesting speech: a bhikṣu, laughing and jesting, says: "Your meritorious virtues are already accomplished. You should kill yourself." That person asks: "Why do you say this?" The bhikṣu answers: "Although what I said before was in jest, now my intention is genuine. You may kill yourself." If the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Form: if a bhikṣu performs incantations to summon terrifying ghosts and spirits of frightful appearance to terrorize a person, and the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Sound: if a bhikṣu says: "Your father, mother, sons, and daughters have died; your possessions are destroyed and scattered," making such statements with the intention of causing the person grief and anguish so they kill themselves, and the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Smell: mixing poison with various fragrances and causing the person to inhale them, resulting in death; if the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Taste: placing poison in food and causing the person to eat it; if the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Touch: smearing the body with kapila poison to kill; if the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Upavadha (pit-trap): making a pit-trap to kill all sentient beings; if a person falls in and dies, it is a pārājika; if a non-human falls in and dies, it is a sthūlātyaya; if an animal falls in and dies, it is a pāyattika.

Upaśaya (concealed trap): making a weak bed, covering it thinly on top, placing killing implements underneath, and causing a person to sit on it; if the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

Upahāra (sorcery): killing by means of gu-poison sorcery; if the person dies as a result, it is a pārājika.

If a bhikṣu has this thought: "I shall kill that person," at the moment of forming the intention, it is a duṣkṛta. When making preparations, it is a sthūlātyaya. If the person dies, it is a pārājika. If one kills a non-human, it is a sthūlātyaya. If one kills an animal, it is a pāyattika.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

There is no offense for one acting with a mind of loving-kindness and compassion, without intention to kill.

---

The Buddha was in Vaiśālī. At that time, there was a famine and alms-food was hard to obtain. The bhikṣus entered the city to beg for food but received nothing at all. At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Each of you should go to where you have acquaintances and spend the rains retreat there. Do not remain here suffering from famine." The bhikṣus accepted the instruction. Some went to the land of Magadha, and some went to villages along the Vaksu River. The bhikṣus who went to the riverside gathered and discussed together, saying: "Now alms-food is hard to obtain. In this village, there are those with faith and devotion. We should mutually praise one another, saying: 'So-and-so has attained the first dhyāna, and I too have attained it. So-and-so has attained the second dhyāna, the third dhyāna, the fourth dhyāna, the four immeasurable states, and the four formless absorptions, and I too have likewise. So-and-so has attained the four foundations of mindfulness, up to the eight components of the noble path and the three gates of liberation, and I too have likewise. So-and-so has attained the eight liberations, the nine successive absorptions, the ten totalities, and the ten direct paths, and I too have likewise. So-and-so has attained firm faith, firm Dharma, the four fruits of the śramaṇa, the three knowledges, and the six supernatural powers, and I too have likewise.' When the householders hear this, they will surely give rise to a sense of wonder and say: 'We have gained great benefit, that such holy beings who have attained the path are spending the rains retreat in our village.' They will then prepare various fine foods to make offerings to us, and we shall lack nothing and dwell in peace and happiness." Having deliberated, they entered the town and went to the homes of the wealthy, mutually praising one another as described above, saying: "You have gained great benefit; the holy Sangha, a field of merit, is dwelling in your village." When the householders heard this, they gave rise to a sense of wonder and exclaimed that they had never before encountered such a thing. They all reduced their own portions, ceased making sacrificial offerings, stopped giving to others, and devoted everything to making offerings to the bhikṣus.

It is the constant practice of all Buddhas that there are two great assemblies each year: at the end of spring and the end of summer, bhikṣus from all directions come to pay their respects. The bhikṣus from Magadha, having completed the rains retreat, came emaciated and haggard to the Buddha's presence, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and stood to one side. It is the constant practice of all Buddhas to welcome and inquire after visiting bhikṣus, asking: "Was your rains retreat harmonious? Was alms-food easy to obtain? Were you not wearied by the journey?" The bhikṣus said: "The rains retreat was harmonious, and we were not wearied by the journey, but alms-food was hard to obtain." The Buddha then taught them various wonderful Dharma teachings, instructing, benefiting, and gladdening them, and told them to stay wherever they were dwelling. The bhikṣus from the Vaksu River came with bodies well-nourished and radiant to the Buddha's presence, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and stood to one side. The Buddha likewise made the same inquiries as above. The bhikṣus said: "The rains retreat was harmonious, alms-food was easy to obtain, and we were not wearied by the journey." The Buddha then asked: "In these times of famine, when begging is difficult, how is it that you alone say it was easy?" The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "While we were there, because alms-food was hard to obtain, we mutually praised one another." They related everything in full as described above. The Buddha then asked: "When you praised one another, was it true or false?" The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "Some was true and some was false." The Buddha rebuked those who spoke falsely in various ways: "What you did is contrary to the Dharma, does not accord with the path, and is something that one who has gone forth should not do! It would be better to eat burning stones and swallow molten copper than to use falsehood to consume the faithful offerings of others. Have you not heard me condemn the offense of false speech and praise in various ways the virtue of not speaking falsely? How then could you, for the sake of material support, deceive others by falsely claiming to have attained superhuman states?" He further rebuked them, saying: "Bhikṣus! In the world, there are five great thieves: First, those who become leaders of a hundred to a thousand men, who destroy cities and villages, harm people, and take their possessions. Second, evil bhikṣus who lead other bhikṣus wandering among people, teaching the Dharma by wrong livelihood. Third, evil bhikṣus who claim that Dharma taught by the Buddha was composed by themselves. Fourth, evil bhikṣus who do not cultivate the holy life yet claim they do. Fifth, evil bhikṣus who, for the sake of material support, when they have no superhuman states whatsoever, falsely claim to have attained them. This fifth thief is called the greatest thief among all beings in the world: gods, humans, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, and brāhmaṇas. How could you, for the sake of small material gain, become the greatest of thieves?" Having rebuked them thus, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, not knowing and not seeing superhuman states, and not having fulfilled the noble and beneficent attainments, claims: 'I know thus, I see thus'; and afterward, whether questioned or not questioned, seeking to purge the offense and purify himself, says: 'I said I knew when I did not know, I said I saw when I did not see; I spoke empty, deceitful, and false words': that bhikṣu commits a pārājika offense and is expelled from communal life."

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. There were many bhikṣus of little learning who did not study and did not inquire, who had no superhuman states, yet who considered: "I know, I see, I have realized." Later, they heard other bhikṣus discussing the marks of having attained the path and not having attained the path, and they then understood that they had not attained it. Giving rise to shame and remorse, they thought: "We previously considered that we had attained what we had not attained. Have we not committed a pārājika offense?"

There were also bhikṣus of little learning who did not study and did not inquire, who had no superhuman states, yet who considered: "I know, I see, I have realized." Later, they broadly studied the sūtras and gave rise to shame and remorse, thinking: "According to how I now understand the Dharma taught by the Buddha, I previously considered that I had attained what I had not attained. This is overestimation. Have I not committed a pārājika offense?"

There were also bhikṣus of little learning who did not study and did not inquire, who had no superhuman states, yet who considered: "I know, I see, I have realized." Later, they broadly cultivated the holy life and attained entry into the fruits of the path. Giving rise to shame and remorse, they thought: "I previously considered that I had attained what I had not attained. This is overestimation. Have I not committed a pārājika offense?"

After having these thoughts, each went to Ānanda and asked him about it. Ānanda brought them to the Buddha's presence and fully reported everything to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of these matters, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and, according to each case, asked the bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "There are five kinds of manifesting superhuman states: first, out of foolishness; second, with a disordered mind; third, following evil; fourth, through overestimation; fifth, genuinely possessing them. If one who acts out of foolishness, with a disordered mind, through overestimation, or genuinely possessing them claims to have attained, to say that this constitutes a pārājika has no basis. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, not knowing and not seeing superhuman states, and not having fulfilled the noble and beneficent attainments, claims: 'I know thus, I see thus'; and that bhikṣu afterward, whether questioned or not questioned, seeking to purge the offense and purify himself, says: 'I said I knew when I did not know, I said I saw when I did not see; I spoke empty, deceitful, and false words,' except in the case of overestimation, that bhikṣu commits a pārājika offense and is expelled from communal life."

"Not knowing, not seeing" means: not knowing, not seeing superhuman states.

All Dharmas leading to liberation, namely the various dhyānas, liberations, samādhis, samāpattis, the fruits of the noble path: these are called "superhuman states."

Regarding the suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path as taught by the Buddha, having completed and fulfilled them, with nothing more to seek: this is called "having fulfilled the noble and beneficent attainments."

Saying of oneself: "I know and see the Dharma thus, and the Dharma also knows and sees me": this is called "claiming 'I know thus, I see thus.'"

Whether after one month or up to one year, if questioned: "How did you attain this? Where did you attain this? From whom did you attain this? By what Dharma did you attain this?"

Or if not questioned, but voluntarily confessing one's offense, seeking purity of precepts, purity of mind, purity of views, and purity from doubt, saying: "I did not know and did not see suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path, yet I said I knew and saw; I spoke empty, deceitful, and false words." Although making such a confession, one still commits a pārājika.

Among these, the modes of offense are: there are two ways of committing a pārājika: first, previously having this thought: "I shall falsely claim to have attained superhuman states." Second, at the time of speaking, having this thought: "I am now falsely claiming to have attained superhuman states." There are also three ways of committing a pārājika: two as described above; third, having this thought: "I have already falsely claimed to have attained superhuman states." There are also four ways of committing a pārājika: three as described above; fourth, claiming superhuman states based on a different view. There are also five ways of committing a pārājika: four as described above; fifth, claiming superhuman states based on a different perception. There are also six ways of committing a pārājika: five as described above; sixth, claiming superhuman states based on a different conviction. There are also seven ways of committing a pārājika: six as described above; seventh, claiming superhuman states based on a different inclination. There are also eight ways of committing a pārājika: seven as described above; eighth, claiming superhuman states without answering in accordance with the question asked.

There are four kinds of ignoble speech and four kinds of noble speech. Ignoble speech: saying one has seen when one has not seen, saying one has heard when one has not heard, saying one has sensed when one has not sensed, saying one has known when one has not known. Noble speech: saying one has seen when one has seen, saying one has heard when one has heard, saying one has sensed when one has sensed, saying one has known when one has known. There are also eight kinds of ignoble speech and eight kinds of noble speech. Ignoble speech: saying one has seen when one has not seen, saying one has heard when one has not heard, saying one has sensed when one has not sensed, saying one has known when one has not known, saying one has not seen when one has seen, saying one has not heard when one has heard, saying one has not sensed when one has sensed, saying one has not known when one has known; the reverse of these is called the eight kinds of noble speech. There are also sixteen kinds of ignoble speech and sixteen kinds of noble speech. Ignoble speech: saying one has seen when one has not seen, saying one has heard when one has not heard, saying one has sensed when one has not sensed, saying one has known when one has not known, saying one has not seen when one has seen, saying one has not heard when one has heard, saying one has not sensed when one has sensed, saying one has not known when one has known, saying one does not doubt what one has seen when one doubts, saying one does not doubt what one has heard when one doubts, saying one does not doubt what one has sensed when one doubts, saying one does not doubt what one has known when one doubts, saying one doubts what one has seen when one does not doubt, saying one doubts what one has heard when one does not doubt, saying one doubts what one has sensed when one does not doubt, saying one doubts what one has known when one does not doubt; the reverse of these is called the sixteen kinds of noble speech.

If a bhikṣu, in the presence of a person, claims to have attained superhuman states, and the person understands, it is a pārājika. If the person does not understand, it is a sthūlātyaya. If he speaks to a non-human, it is a sthūlātyaya. If he speaks to an animal, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

There is no offense for one who speaks the truth.

---

## Five-Part Vinaya, First Division, Part Two: Saṅghāvaśeṣa Rules

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the elder Udāyin, burned by the fire of desire, became emaciated and could barely draw breath. He ejaculated semen with his hand and thereby dwelt at ease.

There was another bhikṣu who was also emaciated. Udāyin asked him: "Why are you like this?" He answered: "Elder, I am burned by the fire of desire, and that is why I am like this." Udāyin said: "I was previously the same, but by ejaculating semen with my hand, I came to dwell at ease. If you follow my example, you will also be like this." That bhikṣu said: "What you have done is contrary to the Dharma, is not a pure practice, destroys the śramaṇa Dharma, and does not accord with the path! The World-Honored One has in various ways condemned desire, thoughts of desire, intentions of desire, and the heat of desire; he has taught the cutting off of thoughts of desire, the removal of intentions of desire, and the extinguishing of the heat of desire. He has said that desire is like a bleached bone, like poison. How can you, with this hand, ejaculate semen, receive the faithful offerings of others, and furthermore teach this to others?" After rebuking him, he brought him to the Buddha's presence and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Udāyin: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha likewise rebuked him in various ways as above, then told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu intentionally ejaculates semen, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa."

At that time, bhikṣus who did not unify their minds lost semen during dreams. Upon waking, they had this thought: "In my dream, I also had intention and also moved my body, and I lost semen. Have I not committed a saṅghāvaśeṣa?" Some confessed the offense, some performed mānatta, some underwent restoration, and some directly reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha rebuked them in various ways: "You should not sleep with a scattered mind. If you sleep with a scattered mind, you commit a duṣkṛta."

Sleeping with a scattered mind has five disadvantages: first, bad dreams; second, good spirits do not protect one; third, one does not attain the perception of light; fourth, one has no mind for contemplating the Dharma; fifth, loss of semen. Sleeping without a scattered mind has five merits: no bad dreams, good spirits protect one, one attains the perception of light, one has a mind for contemplating the Dharma, one does not lose semen.

There are five causes for the sexual organ to become erect during sleep: first, the bowels are full; second, the bladder is full; third, wind is prevalent; fourth, insects bite; fifth, desire is prevalent.

He further told the bhikṣus: "If one has not yet separated from desire, aversion, and delusion, and sleeps with a scattered mind, one will certainly lose semen. Although one may not yet be able to separate from them, if one sleeps with a mindful and collected mind, this fault will not occur. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu intentionally ejaculates semen, except during a dream, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa."

"Intentionally ejaculating semen" means: giving rise to the intention, moving the body, and ejaculating semen.

"Saṅghāvaśeṣa" means: for this offense there is a remainder; there are still causes and conditions by which it can be treated; there is still something to rely upon, and one can seek its elimination within the Sangha.

There are ten kinds of semen: first, blue-colored; second, yellow-colored; third, pink-colored; fourth, black-colored; fifth, red-colored; sixth, white-colored; seventh, milk-colored; eighth, butter-colored; ninth, oil-colored; tenth, honey-colored.

If one gives rise to the intention, moves the body, and intends to ejaculate blue-colored semen, but yellow-colored or any other color up to honey-colored comes out, all are saṅghāvaśeṣa. If one gives rise to the intention, moves the body, and intends to ejaculate yellow-colored semen or any color up to honey-colored, but another color comes out, it is likewise.

There are ten ways of giving rise to the intention, moving the body, and ejaculating semen, all of which are saṅghāvaśeṣa: first, for testing oneself; second, to cure illness; third, for the sake of complexion; fourth, for the sake of strength; fifth, for the sake of pleasure; sixth, for the sake of giving; seventh, for the sake of rebirth in heaven; eighth, for an outer-path sacrifice to heaven; ninth, for the sake of seed; tenth, for fire sacrifice.

There are five ways of giving rise to the intention, moving the body, and ejaculating semen, all of which are saṅghāvaśeṣa: internal form, external form, empty space, wind, and water. Internal form means one's own body. External form means another's body. Empty space means moving the body in empty space. Wind means walking facing the wind. Water means walking against a current of water.

There are also five ways of giving rise to the intention, moving the body, and ejaculating semen, all saṅghāvaśeṣa: the bowels are full, the bladder is full, wind is prevalent, insects bite, desire is prevalent.

If one gives rise to the intention but the body does not move and semen is not ejaculated; or if one gives rise to the intention, the body does not move, but semen is ejaculated: both are duṣkṛta.

If one gives rise to the intention, the body moves, but semen is not ejaculated: sthūlātyaya.

If one does not give rise to the intention, the body moves, and semen is not ejaculated; or if one does not give rise to the intention, the body moves, and semen is ejaculated; or if one does not give rise to the intention, the body does not move, and semen is ejaculated: all are not offenses.

If semen is ejaculated during sleep, and upon waking one gives rise to intention and moves the body: sthūlātyaya. If the body moves during sleep and upon waking one gives rise to intention and semen is ejaculated: duṣkṛta. If one gives rise to intention during sleep, but upon waking the body does not move and semen is ejaculated: no offense.

For a śrāmaṇera: duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the elder Udāyin, burned by the fire of desire, had this thought: "Intentionally ejaculating semen has already been prohibited by the World-Honored One. I should now find a way to touch women's bodies and take pleasure in their smoothness." He then swept and sprinkled his room, spread out fine bedding, and took a small seat to sit in the open. Some women came together to sightsee and said to Udāyin: "We have come especially to see the dwelling." He answered: "Sisters, look as you please." He then led them into the room, closed the door, opened the window, and touched them in various ways: grasping, embracing, pressing, stroking, lifting up, pushing down, straddling, or stepping over them. Those among the women who were pleased said to him: "Why not do it properly? What use is this?" Udāyin said: "The Buddha does not allow me to do the fundamental act." Those who were displeased said angrily: "We thought this was a safe place, but now it has become a place of terror. Saying fire burns in water would not be an adequate comparison. Even householders in their homes would be ashamed of such conduct. How can a bhikṣu commit such evil?" They returned to their homes and told everyone. Those who had no faith in the Buddha-Dharma cursed and reviled in various ways, saying: "We householders touch women's bodies, and the śramaṇas of the Śākya clan do just the same. They shave their heads for nothing; how are they different from us? They have no śramaṇa practice and have destroyed the śramaṇa Dharma!" In this way, evil reputation spread throughout the land.

There was also a brāhmaṇa who brought his wife sightseeing and came to Udāyin's room, saying: "I wish to view the dwelling together with my wife." Udāyin said: "You cannot enter at the same time; you must enter one after the other." The brāhmaṇa said: "If we cannot go together, let my wife enter first." After the wife entered the room, Udāyin did as before, touching her in various ways, and she was a long time coming out. The husband said to his wife: "Why were you so long? Do you not wish to see the other rooms?" The wife said: "Stop! Stop! Do not say such things. I entered just one room and nearly died before getting out. How could I possibly look at other rooms?" The husband asked why, and the wife told him everything. The brāhmaṇa thereupon cursed, saying: "How can the śramaṇas of the Śākya clan commit such evil deeds?" He entered Śrāvastī and at the crossroads, in the streets, alleys, and marketplaces, proclaimed everywhere: "The śramaṇas of the Śākya clan touched my wife!" Those who had no faith in the Buddha-Dharma cursed and reviled in various ways: "The śramaṇas of the Śākya clan act this evilly, yet how can they claim to purely cultivate the holy life?" The senior bhikṣus heard this and rebuked them in various ways, then fully reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Udāyin: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha, after rebuking him in various ways, told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, with desire-filled and altered mind, touches a woman's body, whether grasping her hand, grasping her hair, or grasping any part of her body, stroking and enjoying the smoothness, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa."

"Desire-filled and altered mind" means: the mind inclines toward desire, deeply gives rise to intention, the act is nearly accomplished; the good and indeterminate mental states are altered and one falls into an unwholesome state.

"Woman" means: a human female, from the moment of birth onward.

"Touching" means: stroking and touching various parts of the body in various ways, down to even a single hair.

A bhikṣu who touches a woman under five conditions commits a saṅghāvaśeṣa: it is a woman, he perceives her as a woman, she is a human female, she is alive, his mind is defiled, and he touches with intimate intention, being aware of and accepting the contact; even touching her hair is likewise.

A bhikṣu who touches a woman under five conditions commits a sthūlātyaya: it is a woman, he perceives her as a woman, she is a human female, she is alive, his mind is defiled, but he does not touch with intimate intention, yet is aware of and accepts the contact; even touching her hair is likewise.

If a woman touches a bhikṣu, it is likewise.

Five conditions under which touching a woman is not an offense: it is a woman, he perceives her as a woman, she is a human female, she is alive, his mind is defiled, but he does not touch with intimate intention, and the woman grasps the bhikṣu, and the bhikṣu makes efforts to free himself. Although aware of the touch, he does not accept it. Even touching her hair is likewise.

Also: if she is a woman, with the perception of a woman, with doubt about whether she is a woman, with the perception that she has no sexual organs, or with the perception that she has both sexual organs, and he touches her: saṅghāvaśeṣa.

If she is a woman but he perceives her as a man, or perceives her as a eunuch, and he touches her: sthūlātyaya.

If it is a man, with the perception of a man, with doubt about whether he is a man, or with the perception that he is a eunuch, and he touches him: duṣkṛta.

If it is a man but he perceives him as a woman, with the perception that he has no sexual organs, or with the perception that he has both sexual organs, and he touches him: sthūlātyaya.

For a eunuch, it is likewise.

If the person has no sexual organs, with the perception of having no sexual organs, with doubt, with the perception of having both sexual organs, or with the perception of being female, and he touches: saṅghāvaśeṣa. With the perception of being male, or with the perception of being a eunuch, and he touches: sthūlātyaya. For a person with both sexual organs, it is likewise.

If a bhikṣu touches an unclothed woman: saṅghāvaśeṣa. If he touches a clothed woman: sthūlātyaya.

If a woman grasps an unclothed bhikṣu: saṅghāvaśeṣa. If she grasps a clothed bhikṣu: sthūlātyaya.

If a bhikṣu and a woman are both clothed and touch each other: duṣkṛta.

If a bhikṣu grasps a woman's garment and the woman releases the garment to the bhikṣu: sthūlātyaya.

If a woman grasps a bhikṣu's garment and the bhikṣu does not release the garment to the woman: duṣkṛta.

If a bhikṣu touches the body of a dead human female or a non-human female: sthūlātyaya. If he grasps a female animal: duṣkṛta.

For a śrāmaṇera: duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī.

At that time, the elder Udāyin, burned by the fire of desire, had this thought: "Intentionally ejaculating semen and touching women's bodies have already been prohibited by the World-Honored One. I should now find another way to speak lewd and coarse words to women and take pleasure in gratifying desire." He again swept and sprinkled his room, sat in the open, and when women came to sightsee, he led them in, closed the door, and did everything as described above. Then, inside the room, he spoke various kinds of lewd and coarse words to the women, asking questions such as: "What are your hands, feet, thighs, arms, waist, belly, neck, breasts, head, face, nails, hair, and private parts like?" He also said: "Sisters, your hands, feet, and so on down to your private parts are ugly." He also said: "Sisters, your hands, feet, and so on down to your private parts are beautiful." He also asked: "When your husband approaches you, how is it?" He also instructed: "If you comply with my wishes, I will give you precious jewels." He also made this entreaty: "Engage with me, and all the heavenly spirits will witness my heart." The women who heard this, both those who were pleased and those who were not, reacted as described above.

The senior bhikṣus heard this and rebuked him in various ways, then reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Udāyin: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha, after rebuking him in various ways, told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, with desire-filled and altered mind, speaks lewd and coarse words to a woman, addressing her in accordance with sexual matters, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa."

Among these, the modes of offense are: disparagement, praise, solicitation, entreaty, questioning, counter-questioning, and instruction.

A bhikṣu who speaks lewd and coarse words to a woman under five conditions: it is a woman, he perceives her as a woman, she is a human female, she is alive, his mind is defiled, and with intimate intention, from disparagement and praise up to instruction, if she understands: saṅghāvaśeṣa. If she does not understand: sthūlātyaya.

Disparagement means: disparaging a woman's three parts, saying they are small, large, or ugly in form and color.

Praise means: praising a woman's three parts as not small, not large, and beautiful in form and color.

Solicitation means: soliciting a woman's three parts, saying: "If you can give them to me, I can comply with your wishes."

Entreaty means: "May I obtain your three parts; one who obtains your three parts is a blessed and happy person."

Questioning means: asking: "How many ways does your husband have intercourse with you through your three parts? How often does he do it?"

Counter-questioning means: asking: "Doesn't your husband do it this way through your three parts?"

Instruction means: instructing her: "If you use your three parts to comply with a man's wishes, then the man will respect and love you."

The analysis of perception regarding woman, perception of woman, and so on up to both sexual organs with the perception of both sexual organs: all are as described above.

There are also five modes: sending a messenger, a letter, making a sign, gesturing with the hand, and ambiguous speech. If she understands: sthūlātyaya. If she does not understand: duṣkṛta.

If a bhikṣu speaks lewd and coarse words face-to-face to a human female, and she understands: saṅghāvaśeṣa. If she does not understand: sthūlātyaya.

If he speaks lewd and coarse words to a non-human female: sthūlātyaya. If he speaks lewd and coarse words to a female animal: duṣkṛta.

For a śrāmaṇera: duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the elder Udāyin, burned by the fire of desire, had this thought: "Intentionally ejaculating semen, touching women's bodies, and speaking lewd and coarse words to women have all already been prohibited by the Buddha. I should now praise to women the offering of one's own body, and take pleasure in gratifying desire." He again swept and sprinkled his room and did everything as described above. Then, inside the room, he said to the women: "Sisters, if you make offerings to śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, and even if they enter dhyāna absorptions and attain the four fruits of the path, it is not as good as offering sexual intercourse to one who upholds the precepts." The women who heard this, both those who were pleased and those who were not, reacted as described above, up to the Buddha rebuking him in various ways, all as described above. After rebuking him, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, with desire-filled and altered mind, praises to a woman the offering of his own body, saying: 'Sister, offering sexual intercourse is the highest offering,' it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa."

If one uses various words to praise offering the body through desire, each statement is a duṣkṛta. If one says: "It is not as good as offering sexual intercourse," each statement is a sthūlātyaya. If one says: "Offering sexual intercourse is the highest offering," it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa.

A bhikṣu who praises the offering of his own body under five conditions: it is a woman, he perceives her as a woman, she is a human female, she is alive, his mind is defiled, and with intimate intention, he says: "Offering sexual intercourse is the highest offering." If she understands: saṅghāvaśeṣa. If she does not understand: sthūlātyaya.

The analysis of perception regarding woman, perception of woman, and so on up to both sexual organs with the perception of both sexual organs; sending a messenger and so on up to ambiguous speech; speaking face-to-face to a woman; speaking to a non-human female or female animal: all are as described above.

For a śrāmaṇera: duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, there was a householder named Kāru who was intelligent and sharp-witted, skilled at resolving people's doubts. The people of Śrāvastī, in all their undertakings, even matters of marriage, invariably consulted him. If he said to give in marriage, they gave; if he said not to, they did not. Those who obtained good outcomes said: "Because of Kāru, I obtained this good result. I should cause Kāru also to enjoy this happiness." Those who obtained bad outcomes said: "Because of Kāru, I obtained this bad result. I should cause Kāru also to suffer this hardship." In this way, both ill repute and good repute filled the entire country. Kāru later went forth out of faith, and those who came to consult him grew even more numerous month by month, until even King Prasenajit himself personally visited to consult on matters of state. The voices of delight and anger multiplied even more than before.

At that time, there was a widow whose daughter was the most beautiful in the village. Many sought her hand in marriage, but the widow refused them all, answering: "Only if he will come to live in my household and follow the rules of a son will I give her."

At that time, there was a brāhmaṇa of immeasurable wealth who said to the widow: "Give your daughter in marriage to my son, and your daughter will enjoy lasting peace and happiness." The answer was as before. Thereupon, the brāhmaṇa inquired among the people about who frequently visited this family. Someone told him: "The śramaṇa Kāru is intimate with this family." He then invited Kāru and lavishly supported him for a long time. Once they had become familiar, he told him: "I wish to seek the daughter of that certain family for my son. I beseech the venerable one to speak to her on my behalf." Kāru then put on his robes and took his bowl, went to that household, and the widow came out, prostrated, and made inquiries. Kāru said: "You should give your daughter in marriage to that certain family. Their household is wealthy, and she will certainly be happy." The answer was as before. Kāru further said: "If you do not give her, when this girl grows older, she will certainly run away. Why lose your daughter and also lose a good son-in-law?" The widow, hearing this, reluctantly consented. Kāru returned and reported, and the marriage was arranged.

Afterward, the husband's family treated the wife very harshly. She sent a message to her mother: "Please tell the husband's family to allow me a little ease." The mother replied to her daughter: "Wait until Kāru comes, and I will have him speak to them." When Kāru later came to the widow's house, the widow fully told him of her daughter's hardships: "This marriage was originally due to your arrangement. Please speak to them on her behalf." Kāru answered: "This girl lacks good fortune, which is why she suffers so. If she had good fortune, how would she have come to this? It is not in keeping with our śramaṇa Dharma to involve ourselves in such worldly affairs." The widow cursed him, saying: "Previously you knew how to meddle in people's affairs, and now you say it is inappropriate! You are an evil person who will surely come to no good end!" She cursed and reviled him in various ways, with bitter and cutting words. The neighbors heard and came to remonstrate, saying: "Your daughter's unfortunate destiny has caused this hardship. What has it to do with the śramaṇa, that you curse him so bitterly?" The widow answered: "Do you not know? It is because of this śramaṇa that my young daughter has been brought to such hardship." Those who had no faith in the Buddha-Dharma all said: "You trusted a śramaṇa, and your daughter suffers thus. If you follow his words again, it will only be worse." They further cursed and reviled, saying: "We householders act as matchmakers and marriage arrangers, and the śramaṇas of the Śākya clan do just the same. They shave their heads for nothing and wear dyed robes! Their conduct is such; how are they different from us?" Thus, evil reputation spread far and near. The senior bhikṣus heard this and rebuked them in various ways, brought him to the Buddha's presence, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Kāru: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha, after rebuking him in various ways, told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu acts as a matchmaker, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa."

At that time, in Śrāvastī, various powerful men wished to have illicit affairs with young maidens who had come of age. Being ashamed to convey their intentions themselves and having no one else to send, they spoke to the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Can you convey my intentions on my behalf? If something is needed, for one day and night or even just one encounter, how much would be required?" The group-of-six bhikṣus then went to the women and conveyed the full proposition. Someone saw this, and everyone criticized and discussed it: "The śramaṇas of the Śākya clan purely cultivate the holy life, yet how can they commit such evil deeds? Arranging illicit liaisons is something householders would be ashamed of! These śramaṇas have no shame!" The senior bhikṣus heard this and rebuked them in various ways, brought them to the Buddha's presence, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha, after rebuking them in various ways, told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu acts as a matchmaker, whether for illicit affairs, carrying a man's intentions to a woman, or carrying a woman's intentions to a man, even for a single encounter, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa."

There are ten kinds of women and ten kinds of men. The ten kinds of women are: those guarded by father and mother, those guarded by brother or elder sister, those guarded by relatives, those who guard themselves, those guarded by the Dharma, those who act on their own, those bound by gifts of clothing and goods, those bound by mutual vow, those who have a master, and those taken on trust.

Those guarded by father and mother: a woman who has father and mother, and her father and mother can give or withhold her. Guarded by brother, elder sister, or relatives is likewise.

Self-guarded: she has autonomy over herself and can give or withhold herself.

Guarded by the Dharma: one who has gone forth according to the true Dharma and cultivates the holy life.

Acting on her own: she follows her own pleasure.

Bound by gifts of clothing and goods: she has received clothing and goods from another.

Bound by mutual vow: she has made a vow with someone.

Having a master: a woman who belongs to a husband.

Taken on trust: she has received a token from another, agreed upon for one day, one month, or even one encounter.

The ten kinds of men are likewise.

If a bhikṣu receives a message from a man guarded by father and mother: duṣkṛta. If he conveys it to a woman guarded by father and mother, up to a woman taken on trust: sthūlātyaya. If she does not agree, and he reports back: sthūlātyaya. If she agrees, and he reports back: saṅghāvaśeṣa.

If a bhikṣu receives a message from a man guarded by father and mother and conveys it to a woman guarded by father and mother, and the woman says: "You may speak to my father and mother," and the bhikṣu reports these words back: sthūlātyaya. If the man guarded by father and mother further instructs the bhikṣu to speak to that woman's father and mother, and he accepts this message: duṣkṛta. If he speaks to the woman's father and mother and they do not agree, and he reports back: sthūlātyaya. If they agree, and he reports back: saṅghāvaśeṣa.

If he receives a message from a man guarded by father and mother and conveys it to a woman guarded by brother, sister, or relatives, it is likewise. And so on, up to receiving a message from a man taken on trust and conveying it to a woman guarded by father and mother, and so on up to a woman taken on trust: all are likewise. If a bhikṣu receives a message from a woman guarded by father and mother up to a woman taken on trust and conveys it to a man guarded by father and mother up to a man taken on trust: all are likewise.

There are six kinds of communication: personally, by proxy, by letter, through a chain of messengers, by ambiguous speech, and by sign.

If a bhikṣu receives the message personally, delivers it personally, and reports back personally: saṅghāvaśeṣa.

If a bhikṣu receives the message personally, sends someone to deliver it, and reports back personally; or receives the

I notice that while the header indicates Fascicle 卷第二 (5 of 61), the text body references "Fascicle 003: 五分律" and the actual Chinese text content between the delimiter lines is completely empty.

There is no Chinese text provided for me to translate. The space between the delimiter lines (`========================================`) contains no content.
