# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Three

### First Division, Part Two: The Seventh Matter

The Buddha was in the country of Kauśāmbī. At that time, the bhikṣu Chanda constantly went in and out of various households, teaching the Dharma for them, managing official affairs, and treating various illnesses. Kings, great ministers, elders, and householders all treated him with intimate respect. Various people came together to pay their respects, and encountering him at his walking meditation area, they bowed their heads to his feet. He taught them the wondrous Dharma, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and delighting them, after which each returned to their homes. Chanda then went back, but senior monks had already occupied his room. And so it went in succession, until even the small rooms were likewise taken. Since he could not find a place to stay, he went wandering among the people. Later, those people came again to pay their respects. Seeing the bhikṣus walking in meditation in the open, they asked: "Where is our teacher Chanda now?" The bhikṣus said: "We do not know." They searched everywhere but could not find him, so each returned home. When Chanda came back from his travels, he put on his robes, took his bowl, and went to their homes. They all came out to greet him and said: "Venerable! We recently went to the monastery but could not see you. Where have you come from?" He answered: "I am the most junior monk. All the rooms are full with senior monks. Therefore I was wandering, which caused this missed meeting." The people said: "Please find a site for a dwelling. We shall build one for you. This way we gain merit, the Venerable can dwell in peace, and we will not miss our visits." Chanda replied: "I cannot build one myself, as it would interfere with my practice of the Way. When I am more senior, I will naturally obtain one in due course." The people said again: "Fortunately we have the means and a good heart. Wealth is impermanent, and a good heart is hard to maintain. Please find a site; we truly wish to build it." Chanda, seeing their earnestness and finding it difficult to refuse, went about looking for a building site. He saw a place with a sacred tree that was most suitable for construction, and he cut it down. This tree had a spirit, and the people of the country venerated it. Many who made prayers there had their wishes fulfilled. When they suddenly saw it being felled, they were all shocked and alarmed. Those who did not have faith in the Buddha-Dharma all reviled and cursed him, saying: "These śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, are utterly without principle! Selfishly seeking their own benefit, they harm heavenly beings and humans!" Those who had faith in the Buddha-Dharma said: "This tree has a spirit, and the people fear and revere it, worshipping it devoutly day and night, not daring to be disrespectful. Yet the bhikṣus cut it down without hesitation, and all forms and minds remain calm and undisturbed as before. Truly they are of great spiritual power, great nobility, and worthy of reverence." Voices of both praise and blame filled the entire country. The elder bhikṣus heard of this, reproved him in various ways, brought him to the Buddha's presence, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked Chanda: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproved him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu, having a patron, builds a dwelling for himself, he should bring bhikṣus to seek a building site. The bhikṣus should indicate a site that is free from obstructions and has access paths. If he does not bring bhikṣus to seek a building site, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa."

'Having a patron' means: having a donor. The rest is as stated above in the section on having no patron.

---

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, King Bimbisāra daily invited five hundred monks for a meal in rotation, and the ministers and citizens within the city likewise did the same. At that time, the bhikṣus each practiced the Way individually, and there was no one specifically assigned to arrange the rotation of invitations. The group-of-six bhikṣus always went to the good places. People asked them: "We provide meals for the Sangha in rotation. Why do you, Venerables, always come, and we never see the others?" They reproved them thus, but the group-of-six still did not stop.

At that time, Dharmadinna (Dabba), the son of a Licchavi (Mallian), had left home at age fourteen to practice the Way. In a quiet place, he reflected: "Now King Bimbisāra daily invites five hundred monks for a meal in rotation, and the ministers and citizens within the city likewise do the same. But the Sangha has no one to arrange the rotation of assemblies, causing the group-of-six to pick the good places, losing the respect of the multitude and dampening people's intention to give. If I, at age twenty, receive full ordination and attain arhatship, obtaining the six supernatural powers, I shall serve the Sangha as the arranger of meal invitations and distributor of lodgings." At age sixteen, he attained arhatship and obtained the six supernatural powers. At age twenty, he received full ordination and then reflected: "I previously vowed to serve the Sangha as the arranger of meal invitations and distributor of lodgings. The time has now come; I should do so." He then went to the bhikṣus in Rājagṛha and explained his former vow. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked Dabba: "Do you truly wish to serve the Sangha as the arranger of meal invitations and distributor of lodgings?" He answered: "I truly do, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha praised contentment with little and knowing sufficiency, praised the precepts, and praised the upholding of precepts in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now permit Dabba to serve the Sangha as the arranger of meal invitations and distributor of lodgings."

The Sangha should perform a one-motion-one-proclamation karman. A bhikṣu appointed to speak should announce: "May the noble Sangha listen! This bhikṣu Dabba wishes to serve the Sangha as the arranger of meal invitations and distributor of lodgings. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha approve. The motion is thus."

"May the noble Sangha listen! This bhikṣu Dabba wishes to serve the Sangha as the arranger of meal invitations and distributor of lodgings. Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has approved bhikṣu Dabba to serve as the arranger of meal invitations and distributor of lodgings. The Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. The matter is thus held."

Thereupon Dabba served the Sangha as the arranger of meal invitations and distributor of lodgings. When distributing lodgings, he placed those of few desires and contentment together with those of few desires and contentment; those who delighted in quietude together with those who delighted in quietude; those who recited the sūtras together with those who recited the sūtras; those who upheld the Vinaya together with those who upheld the Vinaya; Dharma teachers together with Dharma teachers; chanters together with chanters; forest dwellers together with forest dwellers; almsfood practitioners together with almsfood practitioners; meditation practitioners together with meditation practitioners. Thus those of various different practices each found their appropriate companions, and he guided each according to suitability. In all the rooms and dwelling places, every bhikṣu found peaceful accommodation. When bhikṣus from other regions arrived at dusk, they would go to Dabba to request a lodging place. Dabba would then enter the fire-light samādhi, emitting light from his left hand and indicating the lodging places with his right hand, and all were perfectly satisfied.

At that time, those in distant regions heard that the bhikṣu Dabba was serving the Sangha of Rājagṛha as the arranger of meal invitations and distributor of lodgings and had such virtue. They all thought: "I should go there to pay respects to the World-Honored One, and also see Dabba and witness his supernatural powers." So they set out and arrived at the city at dusk, going to Dabba to request lodging places. Dabba settled them all properly according to the Dharma. The rotation of meal assignments was likewise handled in this way.

At that time, in Rājagṛha there was an elder named Subhadra ("Good-Rice"), who had seen the Dharma and attained the fruit. He daily prepared excellent and fine food for two bhikṣus and came personally to invite them. The Cīraka brothers, who were of meager merit, always received coarse and lowly assignments when lodgings were distributed and meal invitations were arranged. By chance, they happened to be assigned to his household. When Subhadra learned of this, he thought: "These are wicked men without pure conduct. How can they receive my fine offerings?" He returned home and told his wife: "You should prepare coarse and poor food. When the Cīraka brothers come, set out seats outside the gate and have the maidservants serve them." His wife obeyed and prepared the coarse food. When the Cīraka brothers arrived at mealtime with their bowls at Subhadra's house and took their seats, the maidservants brought out the coarse food. Seeing this, the Cīraka brothers asked: "Sisters! Your household always prepares fine food. Why is it coarse today?" The maidservants said: "We are lowly people; we do not know the reason."

After finishing the meal, they returned, cursing along the road: "Dabba, son of the Licchavi! We shall make you suffer worse than us!"

When they reached their dwelling, they said to the senior bhikṣus: "Dabba, son of the Licchavi, acts according to favoritism, anger, delusion, and fear. He gives the good to those he favors, and the bad to those he does not." The bhikṣus said: "You should not speak thus: 'Bhikṣu Dabba acts according to favoritism, anger, delusion, and fear.' Why? Bhikṣu Dabba has attained arhatship and possesses the six supernatural powers. That he would act according to favoritism, anger, delusion, or fear is impossible." The Cīraka brothers said: "It is precisely because he has supernatural powers that he can see which households have good food and which have bad. He gives the good to others and always assigns us the bad. Therefore we say: 'He acts according to favoritism, anger, delusion, and fear.'" Having said this, they went elsewhere, first creating an evil reputation for Dabba, and then went to Rājagṛha, to their sister, the bhikṣuṇī Mettā (Mithilā). When Mettā saw her two brothers come, she went out to greet them with reverence and inquiries. The Cīraka brothers would not speak with her. Mettā said: "I do not recall any offense. Why do my brothers act like this?" The Cīraka brothers answered: "Because you do not help us, Dabba has been tormenting us like this." Mettā said: "What do my brothers wish me to do to help?" They answered: "If you will help us, go to the Buddha and say: 'World-Honored One! In a place where I should have had no fear, I was instead terrified. I have no place of safety. I thought Dabba was a practitioner of pure conduct, but he suddenly came and defiled me, committing a pārājika offense.'" Mettā said: "Dabba is pure. If I slander him, the Sangha will certainly expel me by self-admission. Once I am expelled from the community, who will I depend on?" The Cīraka brothers said: "We will testify against him, and Dabba will be expelled. How could you receive a self-admission expulsion?" Mettā said: "If the Sangha expels Dabba, how could the outcome be different for me?" The Cīraka brothers said: "If only the World-Honored One expels Dabba, what hardship is it for us if you are expelled? We will properly take care of you." The sister, out of respect for her brothers, did not dare disobey their command, and went to the Buddha and spoke to him as described above.

At that time, Dabba and Rāhula were at the Buddha's left and right. The Buddha asked Dabba: "Did you hear what Mettā said?" He answered: "I have heard. The Buddha himself knows." Thus he was asked three times, and his answer was the same each time. Then Rāhula said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Why do you need to ask Dabba three times? You should simply expel this bhikṣuṇī." The Buddha said: "If Mettā had slandered you with this, what would you say?" He answered: "I would say: 'The Buddha himself knows this matter.'" The Buddha said: "You could say that; should Dabba do the same?" The Buddha said to Dabba: "Rise and defend yourself. Now is not the time for silence. You should recall: if something happened, say it happened; if nothing happened, say nothing happened. You must not simply say 'The Buddha himself knows'!" Dabba then rose from his seat, adjusted his robes, knelt with joined palms, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! From the time of my birth, I have never even in dreams had such a thought. How could I now have any recollection of such a thing?" The Buddha praised him: "Excellent, excellent! You have defended yourself well. One who wishes to defend himself should do it like this!" The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Dabba should be given a remembrance proceeding; the bhikṣu should not have charges brought against him. Mettā should be given a white-four karman of expulsion by self-admission."

A bhikṣu announced: "May the noble Sangha listen! This bhikṣuṇī Mettā has stated herself: 'Dabba defiled me.' The Sangha now gives her expulsion by self-admission. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha approve. The motion is thus."

"May the noble Sangha listen! This bhikṣuṇī Mettā has stated herself: 'Dabba defiled me.' The Sangha now gives her expulsion by self-admission. Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak." Thus the second and third times.

"The Sangha has given bhikṣuṇī Mettā expulsion by self-admission. The Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. The matter is thus held."

After bhikṣuṇī Mettā was expelled, she went wandering among the people. The Cīraka brothers still told the bhikṣus: "Dabba, son of the Licchavi, destroyed our sister's pure conduct, and that is why things turned out like this." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "You should examine and question the Cīraka brothers: 'You say Dabba destroyed your sister's pure conduct. Is this true or false?'" The bhikṣus obeyed and questioned the Cīraka brothers. They answered: "What we say is true." The Sangha further asked: "Where did you see this? When did you see it? How did you see it?" They answered: "We saw it at such-and-such a place, at such-and-such a time, in such-and-such a manner." The Sangha then asked Dabba: "Where were you at that time?" He answered: "I was at such-and-such a place." The Sangha further said to the Cīraka brothers: "The place does not correspond; the time does not correspond. How can you say you saw it at such-and-such a place, at such-and-such a time, in such-and-such a manner?" They further said to the Cīraka brothers: "If one tells a lie before a single bhikṣu of firm faith, the offense is heavier than killing countless sentient beings. Before one of firm Dharma, the offense incurred exceeds that of a hundred of firm faith. Thus, by extension, telling a lie before the Sangha, the offense is heavier than that of a hundred arhats." They further said to the Cīraka brothers: "The Sangha is now assembled and does not act according to favoritism, anger, delusion, or fear. You may speak again: is it true or false?" The Cīraka brothers said: "Because Dabba acts according to favoritism, anger, delusion, and fear, we said this." The bhikṣus reproved them in various ways, brought them to the Buddha's presence, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the Cīraka brothers: "Did you really slander Dabba with a baseless pārājika charge?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproved them in various ways: "You foolish men! How could you slander a pure, celibate bhikṣu with a baseless pārājika charge? Have you not heard of the three kinds of people who fall into hell? First, those who violate the precepts, lacking the qualities of a śramaṇa, yet claim to possess them; who do not cultivate pure conduct yet claim to have cultivated it; who within the Buddha-Dharma are like rotten seeds. Second, those who hold such views and make such statements as: 'Sexual desire is not evil,' and thereby act indulgently. Third, those who slander a pure, celibate bhikṣu with a baseless pārājika charge. These three kinds of people will certainly fall into hell. How could you commit such an evil act?" After the Buddha further reproved them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu, himself not acting in accordance with the Dharma, out of malicious anger, slanders a bhikṣu who is without pārājika offenses with a baseless pārājika charge, wishing to destroy that one's pure conduct: if that bhikṣu later, whether questioned or not questioned, says: 'This matter of mine was baseless; I slandered out of anger,' it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa."

'Himself not acting in accordance with the Dharma' means: his own conduct in every respect is not in accordance with the Dharma.

'Malicious anger' means: the nine provocations.

'Baseless' means: not seen, not heard, not suspected.

'Without pārājika offenses' means: having committed none of the four pārājika offenses.

'Wishing to destroy that one's pure conduct' means: wishing to make him return to lay life or become a non-Buddhist practitioner.

'Later, whether questioned or not questioned' means: upon later examination, asking where, when, and how it was seen.

There are four kinds of matters: disputes concerning statements, disputes concerning admonitions, disputes concerning offenses, and disputes concerning proceedings.

If a bhikṣu, without seeing, hearing, or suspecting another of committing a pārājika, slanders with this, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa. If he sees and doubts, hears and doubts, suspects and doubts, sees and forgets, hears and forgets, or suspects and forgets, and slanders with a baseless charge, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa.

If he slanders face to face and the other understands, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa. If the other does not understand, it is a sthūlātyaya.

If he slanders by writing, by messenger, by signs, by similar words, or by hand gestures, and the other understands, it is a sthūlātyaya. If the other does not understand, it is a duṣkṛta.

If he slanders a bhikṣuṇī, a śikṣamāṇā, a śrāmaṇera, or a śrāmaṇerī, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a bhikṣuṇī slanders a bhikṣuṇī, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa. If she slanders a bhikṣu, it is a pāyattika. If she slanders a śikṣamāṇā, a śrāmaṇera, or a śrāmaṇerī, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a śikṣamāṇā, a śrāmaṇera, or a śrāmaṇerī slanders any of the five classes, it is in each case a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Sthūlanandā, because of the bhikṣu Dabba's great virtue in supernatural powers, frequently came to pay him respects, sitting together in one place listening to and receiving the Dharma teachings. The Cīraka brothers saw this and again wished to slander him. Later, coming down from Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain, they saw two monkeys copulating. They then thought: "I shall now give these two monkeys assumed names: the male named Dabba, and the female named Sthūlanandā." Having thought this, they said to the elder bhikṣus: "We previously slandered Dabba with baseless charges. Now we have personally seen him engaging in impure conduct with Sthūlanandā." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "You should assemble the Sangha and examine the Cīraka brothers: 'You say that you previously slandered Dabba with baseless charges, and now you have personally seen him engaging in impure conduct with Sthūlanandā. Is this true or false?'" The bhikṣus obeyed, assembled the Sangha, and questioned the Cīraka brothers, up to "You may speak again: is it true or false?" all as stated above. After questioning in this way, the Cīraka brothers said: "We truly did not see Dabba engaging in impure conduct. We saw that Sthūlanandā frequently visited Dabba and intended to slander him. Coming down from Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain, we saw a male and female monkey copulating. We then gave assumed names: the male as Dabba, the female as Sthūlanandā. Therefore we said we personally saw them engaging in impure conduct." The bhikṣus reproved them in various ways: "How could you take a fragment, or something resembling a fragment, from a different category and use it to slander a bhikṣu without pārājika offenses with a pārājika charge?" They brought them to the Buddha's presence and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the Cīraka brothers: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproved them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu, himself not acting in accordance with the Dharma, out of malicious anger, takes a fragment or something resembling a fragment from a different category to make a pārājika charge against a bhikṣu who is without pārājika offenses, wishing to destroy that one's pure conduct: if that bhikṣu later, whether questioned or not questioned, says: 'This matter of mine was taken from a different category, a fragment or something resembling a fragment; I slandered out of anger,' it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa."

'Matters' means: disputes concerning statements, disputes concerning admonitions, disputes concerning offenses, and disputes concerning proceedings.

If a bhikṣu sees another committing a saṅghāvaśeṣa and is certain with a saṅghāvaśeṣa perception, and out of anger takes a fragment or something resembling a fragment from a different category to slander a bhikṣu without pārājika offenses, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa. By hearing or by suspicion, it is likewise. If he sees, hears, or suspects another of committing a sthūlātyaya, a pāyattika, a prātideśanīya, or a duṣkṛta, and slanders with a pārājika charge, it is likewise. The rest is as stated above.

---

The Buddha was in the town of Mṛgāra, beneath the Añjana grove. At that time, many sons of the noble Śākya clan had left home to practice the Way under the Buddha. Then Śākya Mahānāma said to Anuruddha: "Now all the noble clan members have left home to cultivate pure conduct. How is it that among us brothers, we alone have not? If I leave home, you can manage the household affairs. If you renounce the household, I shall manage them." Anuruddha said: "I wish my elder brother to leave home; I will manage the household affairs." Śākya Mahānāma said: "Previously, because I was at home, you enjoyed pleasures without knowing the hardships. But leaving home to practice the Way is also toilsome. If you stay home, I shall tell you the methods of managing the household." He then instructed him in various ways: what should be done during the day, what during the night. The methods of farming, commerce, trade, and managing workers: he told him all of these. Anuruddha said: "If managing a household is like this, only then is it established. I could not do it for even a single day. I wish my elder brother to stay home; I shall practice the Way." Śākya Mahānāma said: "All Buddha World-Honored Ones require that without one's parents' permission, one cannot pursue the Way. You should now go and inform your mother." Anuruddha went and informed her: "I wish to leave home and practice the Way in the Buddha-Dharma." His mother said: "I have only you two brothers. My love and affection are deep. How can I bear a living separation? Your family is wealthy. Joyfully cultivate merit. Why must you leave home and rob me of this wish?" He earnestly requested up to three times. His mother then answered: "If King Bhadrika leaves home, I will also permit you."

At that time, King Bhadrika, along with Anuruddha, Ānanda, Nandika, Devadatta, Bhāva (Bhāvika), and Kimbila, were all very close to one another, and whatever they undertook, they had vowed never to go against one another's wishes. Then Anuruddha went to King Bhadrika and said: "I now have a small wish. I ask that you certainly comply." The King said: "Our original pact was that we would never refuse one another. If we refuse, may our heads split into seven pieces. Whatever your wish, I will certainly comply!" Anuruddha then told the King of his mother's words. The King said: "As for your wish, I cannot yet comply. Why? My wish to be king has only just been fulfilled today. My relatives are prosperous and noble; there are no external worries. How can I give this up and leave home to practice the Way?" Anuruddha said: "If the King leaves home, my wish will be fulfilled. If you cling to favor and glory, I will be forever lost. I beg the King to reconsider three times and not violate the former vow!" The King said: "I shall comply with your wish. But grant me seven years, and after that, I shall leave home with you to practice the Way." Anuruddha said: "After seven years, the Buddha may not still be present. Moreover, I am fragile by nature and my life is hard to preserve. How can the King make this the deadline?" The King then said: "If seven years is too long, how about six years?" The answer was as above. Five, four, three, two, down to one year; seven months down to one month; seven days down to one day; the replies were all the same. The King said: "We are men of standing. How can we simply depart without preparation? We should devise a stratagem: prepare carriages and go out for an excursion. Under this cover, we can slip away; only then can it be done. You should now go and inform Ānanda and the others of this intention." Anuruddha promptly conveyed the message to the five. The five were all delighted and in complete accord. That very night they prepared the four kinds of troops with the utmost worldly display, and at dawn they set out for an excursion. After touring to their fill, they secretly took with them the barber Upāli, dismissed all their attendants, went to a secluded place, gave their jeweled garments to him, had him shave their hair, changed their attire, and departed. Not long after they left, Upāli thought: "The Śākyas are fierce and powerful. If they learn that I shaved those men's hair, they will certainly kill me. Since even such noble men can renounce their homes, why should I not give up my barber's tools and these jeweled garments and follow them?" He then shaved his own head, hung all the jeweled garments on a tree, and thought: "Whoever needs them may take them." He then hurried along and soon caught up with the seven, saying to them: "I too wish to follow you and leave home." The seven accepted him, and they all went together to the Buddha's presence, bowed their heads to his feet, and said: "World-Honored One! We now wish to leave home and purely cultivate the holy life. Upāli is our servant. We ask that the Buddha first give him full ordination, and then ordain us afterward. This will enable us and the Śākya clan to destroy our great pride toward him." The Buddha first ordained Upāli, and the seven were ordained afterward.

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "Kapilavastu is not far from here. If the Śākyas learn of this, there may be interference." He then took the eight men to the city of Bhadrikā and stayed beneath the Net-Grove trees. He taught them the wondrous Dharma: "The eye is impermanent, forms are impermanent, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from the conditions of eye-contact are impermanent. Up to: the mind is impermanent, dharmas are impermanent, mind-consciousness, mind-contact, and the feelings born from the conditions of mind-contact are impermanent. You, noble disciples, should contemplate thus, give rise to a mind of revulsion, and attain the wisdom of liberation. What needed to be done has been done, the holy life has been established, one does not receive a subsequent body." When this Dharma was taught, six of them exhausted their defilements and attained arhatship. Ānanda, who attended the Buddha, did not exhaust all defilements. Devadatta alone gained nothing.

After King Bhadrika attained arhatship, his mind was purified and without fear. Whether sitting beneath a tree, in the open, or walking in meditation, he would spontaneously exclaim: "How wonderful! How wonderful!" A certain bhikṣu, hearing this voice, thought: "Bhikṣu Bhadrika must be recalling the pleasures of the world and is not enjoying the holy life." He went and told the Buddha: "Just now I heard Bhadrika say: 'How wonderful! How wonderful!' He must be recalling the pleasures of when he was king and is not enjoying the holy life." The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "You may call him here." He went and said: "The Teacher summons you." Bhadrika went at once to the Buddha's presence, bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, and stood to one side. The Buddha asked Bhadrika: "Did you really say 'How wonderful'?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." The Buddha further asked Bhadrika: "What meaning did you see that made you say 'How wonderful'?" Bhadrika replied: "In the past, when I was a layperson, I lived within seven layers of city walls and moats, surrounded by seven rows of elephants, seven rows of horses, seven rows of chariots, and seven rows of infantry; the four kinds of troops encircled me. If I suddenly heard an unusual sound, my heart would tremble and my hair would stand on end. Now, on open ground beneath a tree, I am calm and without worry. Therefore I exclaimed 'How wonderful!'" The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Bhadrika has already attained arhatship. That he would not enjoy the holy life is impossible."

At that time, the World-Honored One, on account of Bhadrika, spoke this verse:

> "How wonderful, the arhat!
> No longer bound by love and affection.
> Having destroyed desire, anger, and delusion,
> No more entangled in the net of fetters.
> Having arrived at nirvāṇa,
> The mind is free from defilement.
> Not clinging to the world,
> Liberated, without any outflows.
> Fully comprehending the five aggregates,
> Roaming in the forest of the seven factors,
> The great dragon's path:
> All fears have been subdued.
> Accomplished in ten kinds of qualities,
> With the dragon's virtue, in samādhi and dhyāna,
> All outflows exhausted,
> The foremost in the world.
> Unmoved and fearless,
> No longer receiving a subsequent body,
> Having arrived at the place of cessation and extinction,
> Forever free from the retribution of suffering and pleasure.
> Abiding in the wisdom of no-more-learning,
> This body is the final limit.
> The holy life is firmly established,
> Nothing is untrustworthy.
> Among the heavens above and below,
> No more pleasures of desire remain.
> This is called the lion's roar:
> None can surpass the Buddha."

Thereupon, the World-Honored One, together with the great and virtuous śrāvakas, accepted the invitation of the Nāga King Anavatapta. Devadatta had not yet attained supernatural powers and was unable to go. His shame deepened, and he thought: "I should now ask how to cultivate the path of supernatural powers." He went and said to the Buddha: "I wish the Buddha would teach me the method for cultivating supernatural powers." The Buddha taught him accordingly. Devadatta practiced and during the rain retreat he obtained supernatural powers. Having obtained them, he considered: "Whom should I convert first?" He then thought: "Prince Ajātaśatru, the crown prince of King Bimbisāra, whose name is 'Joy of the Multitude'; I should first convert him, and afterward others will follow my teaching." Having thought this, he disappeared from beneath the Net-Grove and appeared on the prince's bed, manifesting as a small child, sucking his finger and lying on his back. The prince saw him and was greatly frightened, asking: "Are you a deva? Or a spirit or demon?" He answered: "I am Devadatta. Do not be afraid, do not be frightened!" The prince said: "If you are Devadatta, return to your original form." He immediately transformed back, with his bearing and deportment as before. The prince was delighted and took him as his teacher. He came daily to pay his respects, riding in five hundred carriages. Devadatta also manifested as five hundred small children on the carriages, lying on their backs and sucking their fingers. Additionally, five hundred carriages carried excellent food and various delicacies to offer to him. At that time, the people of the country were amazed and said: "Devadatta has great supernatural power, performing these transformations, so that the prince comes daily to pay respects, offering various delicacies and provisions." Thereupon Devadatta, unable to measure his own capacity, wished to attract and maintain a following.

At that time, the World-Honored One departed from the Net-Grove and traveled among the people, arriving in the country of Kauśāmbī, where he stayed at the Ghoṣila Park. At that time, Maudgalyāyana (Moggallāna) was staying in a separate place. In this country, there had previously been a son of Kauṇḍinya named Kakusandha (Kahxi), who had purely cultivated the holy life and attained the fruit of anāgāmin (non-returner), and was reborn in the Brahmā Heaven. In the still of the middle of the night, he descended from heaven, emitting a great light, and went to Maudgalyāyana. Bowing his head to his feet, he said: "Devadatta is now converting Prince Ajātaśatru, manifesting various supernatural transformations. I fear he will certainly seek to attract a following and split the harmonious Sangha." Having said this, he suddenly vanished. Then Maudgalyāyana, at dawn, adjusted his robes and went to the Buddha's presence, reporting Kakusandha's words in full to the Buddha. The Buddha asked Maudgalyāyana: "What do you think? Do you believe what Kakusandha said is true?" He answered: "I believe so." The Buddha told Maudgalyāyana: "Do not speak these words! Why? In all the heavens above and below, I see no śramaṇa, brāhmaṇa, deva, Māra, or Brahmā who could lead the Buddha's assembly." He further told Maudgalyāyana: "There are five kinds of teachers in the world, all of whom exist now. First, one whose morality is impure but who claims it is pure; his disciples know the truth but conceal his faults, hoping for material gain. Second, one whose livelihood is wrong and deceitful but who claims to be upright; his disciples likewise conceal this. Third, one whose teachings are unwholesome but who claims they are well-spoken; his disciples praise them as good. Fourth, one whose views are impure but who claims they are pure; his disciples proclaim his views are pure. Fifth, one who teaches what is not Dharma-Vinaya but claims it is Dharma-Vinaya; his disciples also say it is the Dharma. Yet none of these can make the wise accept and believe. Maudgalyāyana! The Tathāgata's morality is pure, without deceit, with no unwholesome speech, with pure knowledge and views, and what he teaches is the Dharma. The wise accept and believe; he does not need disciples to cover and praise for him."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu, having completed the rain retreat in Rājagṛha, put on his robes, took his bowl, and came to the Buddha's presence. He said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Devadatta has converted Prince Ajātaśatru, manifesting as a small child, and so forth, and various delicacies are offered to him." The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Do not envy Devadatta's transformations and the material offerings he thereby obtains. If there are those who respectfully make offerings to him, it increases his long night of suffering. It is like beating a vicious dog with a stick, which only increases its ferocity. Devadatta is like this: the more offerings he receives, the more his afflictions increase."

At that time, the World-Honored One, wishing to further expound this meaning, spoke this verse:

> "The fool increases his evil,
> Born from material gain.
> The deluded one severs pure and white dharmas,
> Like the severing of head from body.
> Not cultivating pure conduct,
> Yet aspiring to attract followers,
> Wishing to stand above all others,
> Hoping all will follow him as their authority.
> Some people seek material gain,
> Some seek nirvāṇa.
> Material gain harms what is pure and white;
> Cessation and extinction remove miserliness and greed."

He further told the bhikṣus: "Banana trees, bamboo, and reeds die when they bear fruit. A mule dies when it conceives. Devadatta's craving for material gain is just like this."

At that time, the World-Honored One, wishing to further expound this meaning, spoke this verse:

> "The banana tree dies when it fruits,
> Bamboo and reeds likewise die when they fruit.
> The mule dies from pregnancy,
> The warrior perishes through greed."

Thereupon, the World-Honored One gradually traveled from the country of Kauśāmbī toward Rājagṛha and stayed at Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain. He was offered provisions by bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, upāsikās, kings, great ministers, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, ascetics, and householders, who respectfully honored, revered, and praised him. Robes, food, bedding, and medicines: he was unattached to all, like a lotus flower.

At that time, the World-Honored One was surrounded by an immeasurable great assembly, teaching the Dharma. Devadatta rose from his seat, adjusted his robes, bared his right shoulder, bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, knelt with joined palms, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I pray you rest at ease! I shall now lead and manage the Sangha." The Buddha said to Devadatta: "Even Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana cannot lead my assembly; how much less can you, a fool who feeds on saliva?" Thereupon Devadatta gave rise to resentful anger: "How can the World-Honored One, before the great assembly, reprove and humiliate me in such a lowly manner?" Because he generated an evil mind toward the Buddha, his supernatural powers were first diminished. He then thought: "The Buddha praises Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana but disparages me!" He further generated an evil mind toward Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana; this was the second diminishment of his supernatural powers. He returned to his dwelling, where he was surrounded by kings and the great assembly and taught the Dharma. A certain bhikṣu in the assembly came and said to the Buddha: "Now Devadatta is surrounded by kings and the great assembly, teaching the Dharma." The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Devadatta not only in this life has obtained such a great assembly; in past lives as well, he once obtained such. Bhikṣus:

"Long ago in ancient times, there was a young brāhmaṇa student in a mountain cave reciting the book of the kṣatriya class. A wild fox living nearby listened intently to his recitations. The fox understood something in his mind and thought: 'With my understanding of this book's language, it is enough to make me the king of all beasts.' Having thought this, he set out wandering and met a thin and weak fox, which he wished to kill. That fox said: 'Why do you want to kill me?' He answered: 'I am the king of beasts. You do not submit to me, so I must kill you.' That fox said: 'Please do not kill me; I will follow you.' So the two foxes traveled together. They encountered another fox and again wished to kill it. The questions and answers were as above, and that fox also said: 'I will follow you.' Thus, in succession, he subjugated all foxes. With the pack of foxes, he then subjugated all elephants. With the multitude of elephants, he subjugated all tigers. With the multitude of tigers, he subjugated all lions, and so he temporarily gained the position of king of beasts. Having become king, he further thought: 'Now that I am king of beasts, I should not take a beast as my wife.' He then mounted a white elephant, leading the hordes of beasts in uncountable numbers, and surrounded the city of Kāśī with many hundreds of thousands of circuits. The king sent a messenger to ask: 'Why do all your beasts act this way?' The fox answered: 'I am the king of beasts. You should give me your daughter. If you give her, that is well! If you do not, I will destroy your kingdom!' The messenger returned with this report. The king assembled his ministers to deliberate. All except one minister said: 'We should give her! Why? Our kingdom's strength depends entirely on elephants and horses. We have elephants and horses, but they have lions. When our elephants and horses smell them, they will be terrified and collapse to the ground. In battle we would certainly lose and be destroyed by the beasts. Why begrudge one daughter and lose the entire kingdom?' Then one great minister, wise and farsighted, said to the king: 'In all my study of past and present, I have never heard or seen a human king's daughter given to a lowly beast. Though I am weak and dull, I shall certainly kill this fox and scatter all the beasts.' The king asked: 'What plan do you propose?' The great minister answered: 'Your Majesty need only send a messenger to fix a date for battle. First, we should request one wish from them: that the lions roar first and fight afterward. They will think we are afraid and will have the lions roar first and fight afterward. On the day of battle, Your Majesty should order everyone in the city to block their ears.' The king followed his advice, sent a messenger to fix the date, and also made the above request. On the day of battle, he again sent word to confirm, and then dispatched his army. As the battle lines were about to clash, the fox indeed ordered the lions to roar first. When the fox heard the roar, his heart burst into seven pieces, and he fell from the elephant to the ground. Thereupon all the beasts scattered and fled at once."

The Buddha spoke a verse regarding this matter:

> "The wild fox, pride inflated,
> Sought to gather kin around him.
> He went to the city of Kāśī,
> Proclaiming himself king of beasts.
> Human pride is also like this:
> Scheming to lead a following,
> In the land of Magadha,
> Calling himself the Lord of Dharma."

He told the bhikṣus: "The King of Kāśī at that time was myself. The wise and farsighted great minister was Śāriputra. The fox king was Devadatta. Bhikṣus! Devadatta in the past deceptively gained a following; now it is the same.

"Śāriputra! Go to the midst of Devadatta's assembly and make this announcement: 'Those who accept Devadatta's teaching of the five points thereby fail to see the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha.'" Śāriputra said: "I once praised Devadatta. How can I now go and denounce him?" The Buddha said: "When you praised him before, was that truthful?" He answered: "It was truthful!" The Buddha said: "Now it is proper to denounce him, and the denunciation is also truthful." He told the bhikṣus: "A one-motion-one-proclamation karman should now be performed to appoint Śāriputra to go to the midst of Devadatta's assembly and denounce Devadatta."

A bhikṣu announced: "May the noble Sangha listen! Śāriputra is now appointed to go to the midst of Devadatta's assembly and make this statement: 'Those who accept Devadatta's teaching of the five points thereby fail to see the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha.' If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha approve. The motion is thus.

"May the noble Sangha listen! Śāriputra is now appointed to go to the midst of Devadatta's assembly and make this statement: 'Those who accept Devadatta's teaching of the five points thereby fail to see the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha.' Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has appointed Śāriputra. The Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. The matter is thus held."

Thereupon Śāriputra went to the midst of Devadatta's assembly and loudly announced: "Those who accept Devadatta's teaching of the five points thereby fail to see the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha." At that time, everyone in that assembly proclaimed: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, are jealous of one another. Seeing that Devadatta receives many offerings, they make such statements." At that time, King Bimbisāra, who was in that assembly, then proclaimed an order: "Do not say such things! Why? The Buddha's assembly is pure, being without jealousy."

Thereupon Devadatta said to the crowd: "Do you wish to see the heavenly mandārava flowers?" All said: "We wish to see them." Devadatta then disappeared before the assembly, went to the flower pond, and was about to pick the flowers when he immediately lost his supernatural powers and was back in his original seat. Having lost his supernatural powers, Devadatta generated an evil mind wishing to harm the Buddha. He said to the Prince: "Now your father the king rules the world righteously. As I see it, there is no end to his decline. Human life is impermanent; one cannot be certain of the next breath. Why wait to eventually inherit the throne? You may take action yourself and soon possess the four seas. I shall kill the Buddha and take his place as the Lord of Dharma. A new king and a new Buddha in the land of Magadha, jointly promoting the Way; would that not be good?" The Prince answered: "The grace of father and mother surpasses that of heaven and earth. They have nurtured and raised me, and I wish to repay this beyond all measure. How can you guide me toward such treason?" Devadatta, hearing this, felt no shame and continued to entice and mislead him with clever words. The Prince was finally confused and swayed, accepting his words with pleasure.

Later, the Prince secretly carried a sharp sword toward the palace gate. Harboring treasonous intent within, he could not help but tremble with fear, and at the palace gate he fell to the ground and rose again. The gate officials saw this and thought: "The Prince always comes with dignified composure. Today he is like this; there must be a reason." They went and questioned him. The Prince answered: "I wish to kill the King; therefore I am like this." They also asked: "Prince, whose instruction do you follow?" He answered: "Devadatta's." The gate officials conferred among themselves: "What should we do?" The first opinion said: "All the śramaṇas and Prince Ajātaśatru should all be killed." The second opinion said: "The Buddha had already sent Śāriputra beforehand to announce his evil treachery. How can we indiscriminately kill the śramaṇas? The punishment should only be upon the Prince and Devadatta, these two alone!" The third opinion said: "We should not presume to judge this matter ourselves. We should report it to the King. Whatever the King orders, we shall carry it out." Having deliberated, they reported it to the King. The King asked: "What is the opinion of you ministers?" They reported the above in full. The King then dismissed the one who held the first opinion, stripped the one who held the second opinion of his office, commended the one who held the third opinion and promoted his rank. He further commanded the assembled ministers to deliberate the matter. The ministers all said: "The second opinion above was considered most appropriate, yet the King has stripped that minister of his office. Observing the King's sagely mind, he cannot bear to cause harm. Since strict punishment has been relaxed, we should follow the lesser plan. The King established the Crown Prince originally as heir to the kingdom. His eagerness to become king caused him to harbor this treachery. If you abdicate and give him the throne, his evil will certainly cease." This deliberation suited the King's mind. He immediately abdicated and installed the Prince as King, titled Ajātaśatru. When he first ascended to the throne and enjoyed the five sensual pleasures, the murderous treacherous mind was temporarily pacified. After a short time, however, he killed his father without cause.

At that time, King Ajātaśatru had a great ferocious elephant. Devadatta secretly went to the elephant trainer and said: "Tomorrow Gautama will walk along this road. You should make the elephant drunk with liquor and release it onto the road. The Buddha has much pride and will certainly not avoid it. Thus he will be trampled to death. I will pay you handsomely." The next morning, the World-Honored One, at mealtime, put on his robes, took his bowl, and entered the city with five hundred disciples to beg for food. The elephant trainer had already made the elephant drunk. Seeing the Buddha coming from afar, he released it. Those who had faith in the Buddha-Dharma, seeing the drunken elephant released, all went to the Buddha and said: "We pray the World-Honored One to take another road!" The five hundred disciples and Ānanda also spoke thus. The Buddha answered all of them three times: "Do not worry! The dragon will not harm me!" All the disciples, without realizing it, left the Buddha and went by other roads. Only Ānanda followed behind alone.

At that time, spectators packed the streets on all four sides. Each deliberated among themselves: "Now two dragons fight; let us see who prevails." The non-Buddhist groups said: "The elephant dragon is mighty; it will certainly triumph over the human." The Buddha's disciples said: "The human dragon is noble in the Way; the elephant will certainly submit." Since empty debate proved nothing, they pooled together gold coins and wagered on the outcome.

Then the drunken elephant, seeing the Buddha coming from afar, flared its ears, trumpeted, and charged at the Buddha. Ānanda, in terror, was dazed and unknowingly pressed himself under the Buddha's arm. The Buddha said to Ānanda: "You heard three times earlier: 'Do not worry.' Why do you not believe, but still harbor such fear?" The Buddha, seeing the elephant coming, entered the samādhi of loving-kindness and spoke this verse:

> "Do not harm the great dragon!
> The great dragon's appearance in the world is rare.
> If you harm the great dragon,
> In later lives you will fall into evil realms."

After hearing the verse, the elephant spread its trunk upon the ground and embraced the World-Honored One's feet. It looked up and down at the Buddha three times in quick succession, circumambulated him three times to the right, and backed away. From then on, it became a gentle elephant. All marveled at this extraordinary event and exclaimed in unison: "The śramaṇa Gautama, without using blade or staff, subdued this ferocious elephant! The people of the kingdom are now freed from fear. How wonderful!" All the non-Buddhist groups were completely ashamed, while the Buddha's disciples leaped for joy and collected over seven hundred thousand gold coins.

After the Buddha subdued the elephant, he further spoke this verse:

> "The elephant, drunk and full of angry fury,
> Charged toward the Deva among Devas.
> All the people watched,
> Pooling coins to wager on the outcome.
> Its form was like a great mountain,
> Its strength surpassed that of sixty elephants.
> Its trumpeting shook the hearts of men;
> A single roar could shatter enemy formations.
> The one of great power, Deva among Devas,
> In compassion for beings, appeared in the world.
> To subdue the ferocious elephant,
> He stood still before it.
> The elephant was subdued, and the multitude witnessed;
> Monastics and laity all leaped with joy,
> Praising the Buddha's subduing of the ferocious elephant,
> Like the lion king."

Then Devadatta, seeing this, thought: "Since this scheme could not harm the Buddha, I must find another fierce man who does not recognize the Buddha, and bribe him handsomely to go and kill him." He searched in all four directions and found a strong man, to whom he said: "Kill the Buddha for me, and I will richly reward you." That man, greedy for the reward, accepted the assignment and went. At that time, the World-Honored One was walking in meditation in the open. Seeing the man from afar, he filled his entire being with the samādhi of loving-kindness and raised his hand to beckon him. Thereupon the man, without realizing it, dropped his knife, hurried to the Buddha, bowed his head to his feet, and said: "I was mad and foolish, wishing to harm the World-Honored One. Knowing my crime is grave, I beg to be allowed to repent!" The Buddha said: "You were truly foolish! How could you, for the sake of money, wish to harm the Tathāgata? In my Dharma, if one recognizes one's transgression and repents, one's roots of merit increase." He then taught him the Dharma: namely, the discourse on giving, the discourse on morality, the discourse on rebirth in heaven, the defilements of household life, and the joy of renunciation. That man was inwardly delighted. The Buddha, knowing his mind, further taught him the Dharma: namely, suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. Hearing the Dharma, he gained understanding. Among all dharmas, he became distant from dust and free from defilements, attaining the purity of the Dharma-eye. Having seen the Dharma and attained the fruit, he took refuge in the Three Treasures and received the five precepts. The World-Honored One sent him away, and he returned by a different road.

Devadatta then recruited two more men to kill the first man and silence the evil report. He further sent four men; and so on in succession, up to thirty-two men, all of whom first went to the Buddha's presence. The Buddha likewise taught them the Dharma in order as before, and all attained the fruit of stream-entry (srotāpanna).

At that time, the bhikṣus, hearing that Devadatta was sending men to harm the Buddha, all carried weapons and guarded the World-Honored One, dividing into groups, each stationed on one side. It is the constant practice of all Buddhas to come out of their rooms twice daily. Coming out at dawn, the Buddha saw the bhikṣus all around him on every side and asked: "Why are you all standing here with weapons?" The bhikṣus said: "We heard that Devadatta sent men to harm the World-Honored One. We could not rest easy, so we stationed ourselves here." The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "If the Tathāgata were to die by violence, that is impossible. There are five kinds of teachers in the world who need protection. I do not need you. Each of you should go to your own quarters and guard your own minds!"

When Devadatta learned of this, he thought again: "I still could not harm the Buddha by this means. I should find another person, lead him there myself, and then surely succeed." He found one man and together they climbed Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain. At that time, the World-Honored One was walking in meditation on a rock below the mountain. Devadatta had the man push a rock down to harm the Buddha. When the man made up his mind to push the rock, his four limbs could not lift it. He then reflected on the Buddha's great merit, and his hands and feet were restored. Devadatta, seeing this, grew even more furious and said: "How incompetent you are! Get out of here quickly!" He then seized a large rock himself and pushed it down to harm the Buddha. Below the mountain there was a spirit named Kimbila, who caught the rock and threw it far away. A fragment flew and struck the Buddha, wounding the big toe of his foot. The World-Honored One, seeing this, said to Devadatta: "You have now incurred the offense of uninterrupted retribution. If with an evil mind you draw blood from a Buddha's body, you will certainly fall into the uninterrupted Avīci Hell."

Devadatta further thought: "Since I cannot harm the Buddha, I should simply split his harmonious Sangha! The Buddha has great supernatural power. If I can split his Sangha, my fame will surely spread far and wide." The Buddha, knowing his intent, said to Devadatta: "Do not split the harmonious Sangha! If the Sangha has been split and one can harmonize it, that person will be reborn in heaven and enjoy bliss for an eon. If the Sangha is harmonious and one splits it, that person will fall into hell and suffer for an eon." Devadatta, hearing this, temporarily abandoned this intention, but soon after, the same thought arose again as before. The Buddha admonished him as at first, and then spoke this verse:

> "The joy of communal harmony,
> Harmony is always peaceful.
> If one splits the harmonious Sangha,
> One suffers in hell for an eon.
> The joy of communal harmony,
> Harmony is always peaceful.
> If one harmonizes a split Sangha,
> One enjoys heaven for an eon.
> If one makes divisions and distinctions,
> Always speaking unwholesome words,
> To split the harmonious Sangha:
> One suffers in hell for an eon.
> If one does not make divisions and distinctions,
> Always able to speak the good Dharma,
> To harmonize a split Sangha:
> One enjoys heaven for an eon."

Devadatta, hearing this, again temporarily abandoned the intention, but soon after it arose again, and his scheming exceeded what it had been before.

At that time, the bhikṣus heard that Devadatta wished to split the harmonious Sangha and went to report to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and, after remotely reproving Devadatta in various ways, told the bhikṣus: "You should appoint a bhikṣu who is on friendly terms with Devadatta to go and admonish him: 'Do not split the harmonious Sangha. Do not work toward splitting the Sangha. You should be in harmony with the Sangha. Because the Sangha is harmonious, it is joyful and free from contention, of one mind and one training, like water and milk blended together, jointly promoting the teacher's instruction, dwelling in peace and happiness.' If he accepts, good. If he does not accept, numerous bhikṣus should be sent. If he still does not accept, the Sangha should go to admonish him." The bhikṣus obeyed. After three such rounds of admonishment, Devadatta refused each time. The bhikṣus reproved him in various ways and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus. After further remotely reproving Devadatta in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu makes diligent effort to split the harmonious Sangha, and other bhikṣus say to that bhikṣu: 'Do not make diligent effort to split the harmonious Sangha. You should be in harmony with the Sangha. Because the Sangha is harmonious, it is joyful and free from contention, of one mind and one training, like water and milk blended together, jointly promoting the teacher's instruction, dwelling in peace and happiness.' If thus admonished, he stubbornly persists and does not desist, he should be admonished a second and third time. If at the second and third admonition he desists from this matter, good. If he does not desist, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa."

'Making effort to split' means: seeking the causes and conditions for splitting the Sangha.

'Harmonious' means: jointly performing the upoṣadha, pravāraṇā, karman, and regular communal acts.

'Sangha' means: from four persons and above.

When that bhikṣu wishes to split the Sangha, the other Sangha members, having seen, heard, or known of it, should appoint one bhikṣu who is on friendly terms with him to go admonish him. If he desists, he should repent of one duṣkṛta. If he does not desist, numerous bhikṣus should be sent to admonish him. If he desists, he should repent of two duṣkṛtas. If he still does not desist, the Sangha should admonish him. If he desists, he should repent of three duṣkṛtas. If he does not desist, a white-four karman of admonition should further be performed. A bhikṣu should announce:

"May the noble Sangha listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so is making diligent effort to split the harmonious Sangha. The Sangha has already admonished him: 'Do not make diligent effort to split the harmonious Sangha.' Thus admonished, he stubbornly persists and does not desist. The Sangha now admonishes him by karman. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha approve. The motion is thus." After the motion, one should say to that bhikṣu: "The Sangha has completed the motion. The remaining three proclamations are yet to come. You should desist from this matter. Do not commit a saṅghāvaśeṣa." If he desists, he should repent of three duṣkṛtas and one sthūlātyaya.

If he does not desist, one should further announce: "May the noble Sangha listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so is making diligent effort to split the harmonious Sangha, and so forth, the Sangha now admonishes him by karman. Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak." One should further say to that bhikṣu: "The Sangha has completed the first proclamation. The remaining two proclamations are yet to come. You should desist from this matter. Do not commit a sa

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese during the Liu Song dynasty by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.
