# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Four

### First Division, Part Three: Indefinite Rules

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda constantly visited the home of a certain householder. One morning, he put on his robes, took his bowl, and went to that house. He spread out his sitting cloth and sat alone in a secluded place with the householder's wife, speaking coarse and evil words about sexual matters.

At that time, Viśākhā Mṛgāramātā heard that Upananda was sitting alone in a secluded place with the householder's wife, speaking coarse and evil words about sexual matters. She thought: "If the householder returns and sees this, he will certainly develop ill will toward other bhikṣus, causing them to suffer long nights of pain. I should send someone to report this to the World-Honored One." She then told the brahmin Nāliṅga, who regularly received her offerings: "Go to the Buddha, bow your head at his feet, and report this matter in full." The brahmin went and reported to the Buddha. The Buddha taught him various wonderful dharmas, then sent him on his way back. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked Upananda: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." The Buddha rebuked him in various ways, as stated in the section on sexual offenses, and then told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish an indefinite rule for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu sits alone in a secluded place, a place suitable for sexual intercourse, with a single woman, and a trustworthy upāsikā sees this and speaks regarding one of three categories of offenses: either pārājika, or saṃghāvaśeṣa, or pāyattika; if the bhikṣu says: 'It is as the upāsikā states,' he should be dealt with according to whichever of the three categories she states. This is called an indefinite rule."

"Alone" means: one bhikṣu and one woman, with no third person present.

"Secluded place" means: a place not visible to the eye.

"Trustworthy" means: one who has seen the Four Noble Truths and would not speak falsehood for the sake of herself, for the sake of others, or for the sake of gain.

"Upāsikā" means: one who has taken the Three Refuges and abandoned wrong paths.

"Indefinite" means: if she states one matter among the three categories, the senior bhikṣus should ask the bhikṣu: "Did you go to that house?" If he says: "I went," he should not yet be dealt with. They should further ask him gently: "Did you sit alone in a secluded place with a woman, speak coarse evil words, or engage in sexual intercourse?" If he says: "No!" the senior and junior bhikṣus should question him sharply: "Speak truthfully, do not speak falsely! Is it as the upāsikā says or not?" If he says: "It is as the upāsikā says," then and only then should he be dealt with according to whichever offense she stated.

For a śrāmaṇera, it is a duṣkṛta.

The second indefinite rule concerns sitting with a woman in an open place. Except for pārājika, everything else is as stated above.

"Open place" means: a place visible to the eye.

### First Division, Part Four: Niḥsargika-Pāyattika Rules

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the World-Honored One had instructed the bhikṣus to keep only three robes, but the group-of-six bhikṣus wore different robes before the meal, after the meal, and in the afternoon. Other bhikṣus saw this and asked: "The World-Honored One does not permit keeping extra robes. Have you not heard this?" They answered: "We have also heard it, but these robes of ours were obtained from the Sangha, or obtained from householders, or are rag-robes that others gave us because they had worn them. We were not originally told to store them for the five types of loss."

The bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways, brought them to the Buddha, and reported the matter. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." The Buddha rebuked them in various ways: "Have you not heard that I previously praised few desires and contentment, that robes should merely cover the body, and food should merely sustain life? Just as birds fly with their feathers always accompanying them, so bhikṣus should always keep their three robes with them. How can you now accumulate things unlawfully?" After rebuking them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu keeps extra robes past one night, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

At that time, when bhikṣus needed any particular robe, the Sangha would perform a karman and give them items that should be distributed. At that time, Aniruddha's robes were coarse, shabby, and worn out. The bhikṣus said to him: "Your robes are shabby and worn out. Why don't you obtain one from the Sangha and have it made, completing it in one day?" Aniruddha said: "I dare not take one, fearing that if it cannot be completed in one day, I would commit a niḥsargika-pāyattika offense."

At that time, bhikṣus from the town of Pāri came to Śrāvastī, intending to undertake the later rain-retreat. When the time arrived they could not make it, so they settled in the town of Sāvittha for their retreat. After the retreat was completed, on the sixteenth day they set out for the Buddha's place. On the road they encountered mud and water, and their three robes were coarse and heavy, causing them extreme exhaustion. They arrived, bowed at the Buddha's feet, and sat to one side. The Buddha asked the bhikṣus: "Was your retreat harmonious? Was begging for food not scarce? Was the road not tiring?" They answered: "The retreat was harmonious, and begging for food was not scarce. We were previously staying in Pāri and wished to come here for the retreat. Because of many acquaintances we could not depart early. We wished to make the later retreat but could not make it in time, so we stayed in Sāvittha for the retreat. After the retreat was completed, on the sixteenth day we set out. On the road we encountered mud and water, and our three robes were coarse and heavy, causing us extreme exhaustion." The bhikṣus then also reported Aniruddha's matter to the Buddha in full. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus, praised few desires and contentment, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit the acceptance of the kaṭhina robe, and with it, five things shall not be offenses: first, eating separately from the assembly; second, eating more than once; third, going to other houses before or after meals without informing other bhikṣus; fourth, keeping extra robes; fifth, sleeping apart without losing the three robes."

At that time, the bhikṣus thought: "The Buddha has permitted keeping extra robes through accepting the kaṭhina robe. For how long is this permitted?" Having thought this, they reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted during the time the kaṭhina robe is in effect. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu, after completing his three robes and after the kaṭhina robe has been relinquished, keeps an extra robe past one night, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

At that time, Ānanda received two pieces of cotton cloth, accepting them on behalf of Śāriputra. Śāriputra was staying in another place. Ānanda thought: "The World-Honored One does not permit keeping extra robes past one night. Śāriputra is not here now. What should be done about this?" Having thought this, he reported to the Buddha. The Buddha asked Ānanda: "How many days until Śāriputra returns?" He answered: "Perhaps ten days, or perhaps less than ten days." The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus, praised few desires and contentment in various ways, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu, after completing his three robes and after the kaṭhina robe has been relinquished, keeps an extra robe for up to ten days; if beyond that, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Completing the three robes" means: having finished washing, dyeing, and sewing them.

"Relinquishing the kaṭhina robe" means: relinquishing it through a motion-and-one-proclamation karman.

"Extra" means: anything beyond the three robes is called "extra." "Robes" means: cotton robes, wool robes, wild silkworm silk robes, hemp-cloth robes, and linen robes.

"Ten days" means: if one obtains a robe on one day, one should on that same day dispose of it, either by formally designating it, giving it to someone, or making a determination-gift of it. If one does not dispose of it on that same day, and on the second day one obtains more robes, one should dispose of all of them on that day. If one does not dispose of them on that day, and from the third day up to the tenth day one obtains more robes, one should likewise dispose of all of them on that day. If one does not dispose of them on that day, then when dawn breaks on the eleventh day, all robes obtained during those ten days become niḥsargika-pāyattika.

If there are robes held past ten days, they should be forfeited to the Sangha of bhikṣus, or given to one, two, or three bhikṣus; they must not be forfeited to other persons or non-humans. After forfeiting them, one should then confess the offense. If one confesses without first forfeiting, the offense deepens.

Apart from the three robes being extra, if any other cloth items, down to a hand-cloth, are kept past ten days, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If it is a determination-gift, there is no offense.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus, after completing the rain-retreat, wished to travel. They thought: "We shall soon return here. One robe is sufficient; why take many?" Having thought this, they bundled up their remaining robes, placed them on shelves, entrusted them to the resident bhikṣus, and departed.

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus returned from another place and said to the resident bhikṣus: "Assign us rooms." The resident bhikṣus then assigned them the room where the group-of-seventeen had stored their robes. The group-of-six bhikṣus saw the robes on the shelves and asked: "Why are you keeping these extra robes?" They answered: "These belong to the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus. After completing the rain-retreat, they went traveling and could not carry them, so they left them in our keeping." The group-of-six bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." The Buddha rebuked them in various ways: "You foolish ones! Have you not heard me say that bhikṣus should keep their three robes and bowl with them, just as birds fly with their feathers always accompanying them?" After rebuking them, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu, after completing his three robes and after the kaṭhina robe has been relinquished, is separated from any one of the three robes for more than one night, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

At that time, there was a rag-robe bhikṣu who wished to go to the town of Sāketa, but his robes were heavy and he could not carry them. He wished to leave them behind but did not know what to do. He reported this to the bhikṣus, and they brought him to the Buddha and reported the matter. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu who keeps rag-robes has heavy robes and wishes to travel to another place but cannot carry them, that bhikṣu should request from the Sangha a karman for not losing his robes."

He should remove his sandals, bow his head at the feet, kneel with palms joined, and speak thus: "Venerable Sangha, listen! I, bhikṣu so-and-so, wish to travel to such-and-such a place. My rag-robes are heavy and I cannot carry them. I wish to leave them behind. I now request from the Sangha a karman for not losing my robes." After requesting thus a second and third time, a bhikṣu in the Sangha proclaims: "Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so wishes to travel to such-and-such a place. His rag-robes are heavy and he cannot carry them. He wishes to leave them behind. He requests from the Sangha a karman for not losing his robes. Now the Sangha will perform for him a karman for not losing his robes. If the time is right for the Sangha and the Sangha approves, listen. The motion is thus."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so wishes to travel to such-and-such a place. His rag-robes are heavy and he cannot carry them. He wishes to leave them behind. He requests from the Sangha a karman for not losing his robes. Now the Sangha will perform for him a karman for not losing his robes. Will the elders who approve remain silent; those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has performed the karman for not losing his robes for bhikṣu so-and-so. The Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. The matter is thus held."

At that time, the bhikṣus, seeing that the World-Honored One permitted the karman for separation from robes, then constantly performed the karman for separation from robes for sleeping apart, and also performed the karman for complete separation from all three robes, wearing only shabby and worn-out robes while traveling. Senior bhikṣus saw them and asked: "Why are you wearing shabby and worn-out robes while traveling?" They answered: "The Buddha permits the karman for separation from robes. Therefore we constantly perform the karman for sleeping apart from our robes, and also perform the karman for separation from all three robes." The bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways, brought them to the Buddha, and reported the matter. The Buddha said: "You should not constantly perform the karman for sleeping apart from robes, nor perform the karman for complete separation from all three robes. These two are unlawful karmans. Both the bhikṣus and the Sangha incur a duṣkṛta for each. Through this karman of sleeping apart from robes, for each robe and each night of separation, all incur the offense of losing the robe. I now permit the bhikṣus the karman for leaving robes behind: for those who entered the earlier retreat, nine months; for those who entered the later retreat, eight months. One may not perform the karman for leaving behind the saṃghāṭī or the antaravāsa. I permit the karman for the uttarāsaṅga. In places where there is danger of thieves, among the three robes, whichever cut-and-sewn robe is the most valuable, that one may be left behind as appropriate. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu, after completing his three robes and after the kaṭhina robe has been relinquished, is separated from any one of the three robes for more than one night, except by Sangha karman, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Separation from robes" refers to: a park, same boundary or different boundary; a building, same boundary or different boundary; a bhikṣuṇī monastery, same boundary or different boundary; a village, same boundary or different boundary; a multi-storied building, same boundary or different boundary; a vehicle, same boundary or different boundary; a boat, same boundary or different boundary; a threshing floor, same boundary or different boundary; under a tree, same boundary or different boundary; open ground, same boundary or different boundary; a road, same boundary or different boundary.

"Park, same boundary" means: the Sangha has performed a karman establishing a boundary for not losing robes, and within it one can freely come and go. "Different boundary" means: the Sangha has not performed a karman establishing a boundary for not losing robes; or even though performed, within it one cannot freely come and go. For buildings, bhikṣuṇī monasteries, villages, and multi-storied buildings, it is likewise.

"Vehicle, same boundary" means: within it one can freely take up or store things. "Different boundary" means: within it one cannot freely take up or store things. For boats, it is likewise.

"Threshing floor, same boundary" means: the place where grain is threshed, where one can freely take things. "Different boundary" means: where one cannot freely take things.

"Under a tree, same boundary" means: the area covered by the tree's shade. "Different boundary" means: the area not covered by the tree's shade.

"Open ground, same boundary" means: sitting cross-legged, seven feet from one's face. "Different boundary" means: beyond seven feet.

"Road, same boundary" means: seven bow-lengths from one's face. "Different boundary" means: beyond seven bow-lengths.

When dawn breaks, if the bhikṣu returns to the boundary, or even if just one foot enters the boundary, the robe is not lost. If one verbally says: "I relinquish this robe," the robe is still not lost. If one does not say "relinquish" and dawn breaks, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika.

For a bhikṣu, any robes designated for use beyond the three robes that are separated overnight incur a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the bhikṣus, after completing their three robes and after the kaṭhina robe had been relinquished, received out-of-season robes. The bhikṣus were embarrassed and said: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to accept out-of-season robes." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the bhikṣus: "Is it true that you received out-of-season robes and were embarrassed, saying: 'The Buddha has not yet permitted us to accept out-of-season robes'?" They answered: "It is true, World-Honored One." The Buddha praised few desires and contentment in various ways, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit the acceptance of out-of-season robes."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus thought: "The World-Honored One permits us to accept out-of-season robes." So they accepted many without formally designating them, giving them to others, or making determination-gifts of them. Other bhikṣus saw this and asked: "Have you not heard the World-Honored One's rule that one may not keep extra robes?" They answered: "Although the Buddha restricted keeping extra robes, he permits accepting out-of-season robes." They were further asked: "Do you keep out-of-season robes at all times, without formally designating them, giving them to others, or making determination-gifts of them?" They answered: "That is so!" The senior bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." The Buddha rebuked them in various ways: "You foolish people! You should not be greedy and full of desires. In the ways of non-Buddhist practitioners, receivers are insatiable while donors deliberate carefully. In my true Dharma, there is contentment with few desires; though donors may be without limit, receivers should take little." After rebuking them, he told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu obtains out-of-season robes and does not formally designate them, give them to others, or make determination-gifts of them, even for one night, it is a duṣkṛta."

At that time, bhikṣus at a certain dwelling place obtained many robes. After formally designating them, giving them to others, and making determination-gifts, the remaining pieces of cloth they offered to other bhikṣus. Those bhikṣus would not accept them, saying: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to accept incomplete robes." They were told: "Accept them for now; they will eventually be completed."

At that time, the elder Kapī obtained a narrow and short robe. Day after day he stretched and pulled it, trying to make it wider and longer. The Buddha, who inspected the rooms every five days, saw Kapī pulling at the robe and asked: "What are you doing?" He answered: "I obtained this small robe and cannot formally designate it." The Buddha further asked: "Do you have a prospect of obtaining more cloth?" He answered: "Yes." He asked again: "How long until you can get it?" He answered: "Perhaps one month, or less than one month." The Buddha praised few desires and contentment in various ways, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit keeping out-of-season robes that are insufficient, hoping to complete them, for up to one month."

After the Buddha permitted keeping insufficient out-of-season robes, the bhikṣus carried these robes while traveling for more than one month. Other bhikṣus saw this and asked: "The Buddha does not permit keeping insufficient out-of-season robes for more than one month. How can you carry these robes while traveling for more than one month?" They rebuked them in various ways, brought them to the Buddha, and reported the matter. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu, after completing his three robes and after the kaṭhina robe has been relinquished, obtains out-of-season robes and needs them, he should accept them and quickly make them into designated robes. If they are sufficient, good. If insufficient, hoping that there is another place to obtain more to complete them, he may keep them for up to one month. If beyond that, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Out-of-season robes" means: after the kaṭhina robe has been relinquished, whatever robes are obtained are all called out-of-season robes.

"Needs" means: among the three robes, one has become old and damaged and needs to be repaired or replaced.

"Hoping that there is another place to obtain more" means: there should be another place from which one expects to obtain cloth, hoping to get it within one day up to one month.

If a bhikṣu obtains an incomplete robe on one day and on that same day has a prospect of obtaining more, if he obtains it he should complete the robe, formally designate it, give it to someone, or make a determination-gift of it. If he does not formally designate it, give it to someone, or make a determination-gift of it, and dawn breaks on the eleventh day, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika. For the second day up to the tenth day, it is likewise.

If on the eleventh day there is a prospect and one obtains cloth, on that same day one should complete it, formally designate it, give it to someone, or make a determination-gift of it. If one does not formally designate it, give it to someone, or make a determination-gift of it, and dawn breaks on the twelfth day, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika. Up to the thirtieth day, it is likewise.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time in the town of Uṣeṇa, there was a young householder who went out on an excursion and saw a woman named Lotus-Color, whose complexion was like peach blossoms and who possessed all the marks of feminine beauty. They felt mutual affection and esteem, and he took her as his wife. After a short time, the wife became pregnant, and he sent her back to her parents' home. When the month was full, she bore a daughter. Because his wife was in confinement, he could not be close to her, and so he secretly had an affair with her mother. Lotus-Color discovered this and wished to leave him, severing the bond of husband and wife. But fearing to bring trouble upon her parents, and moved by compassion for her infant, she swallowed her shame and endured. She returned to her husband's home and raised the daughter until she was eight years old, and then she departed.

She arrived at Vārāṇasī, exhausted from hunger and thirst, and sat by the water's edge. At that time, a certain elder was out on a pleasure excursion. Seeing her, he greatly admired her. He asked about her native place, her parents, her clan, to whom she now belonged, and why she was alone here. Lotus-Color said: "I am the daughter of such-and-such a family. At present I belong to no one." The elder further asked: "If you belong to no one, can you become my chief wife?" She answered: "For a woman to have a husband, why should it not be possible?" He then took her home in his carriage and installed her as his chief wife. Lotus-Color managed his household, harmonizing the senior and junior members, and husband and wife cherished each other for up to eight years.

At that time, the elder said to his wife: "I have debts outstanding in the town of Uṣeṇa that have not been collected for eight years. Calculating interest, they have grown to a hundred million. I now wish to go collect them, and we must part for a while." The wife said: "In that town, the customs are such that women are loose. If you go, you may lose your manly virtue. Wealth and possessions are like dirt; why should they be worth the reckoning?" He answered: "Though I am dull and ignorant, I would not descend to such disorder." The wife further said: "If you must go, I wish to hear an oath." He answered: "Very well!" He then said: "If, from the time I set forth until I return through the door, I harbor a single improper thought, may I perish along with that thought." Thereupon he took leave and went to that town. The debts to be collected were in many places, and he stayed for years. Being away from home so long, his longing for a mate grew ever deeper. He thought: "How can I avoid violating my former oath while fulfilling my present desire?" He then thought further: "If I commit adultery, that would violate my oath. But if I take a different wife, it would not break my vow." So he searched and happened to see a girl of elegant and beautiful appearance whose gaze was modest and not wayward; he felt great affection and went to seek her hand in marriage. The father, considering the elder's talent, intelligence, and great wealth, happily gave her to him.

After the debt collection was completed, he took her back to his home country and settled her in a separate house before returning to his own home. He went out in the morning and returned in the evening, different from how it had been before. Lotus-Color was puzzled and secretly asked the servants. The servants answered: "There is a young wife outside; that is why it is like this." When her husband returned that evening, Lotus-Color asked: "You have a new wife; why do you hide her and not let me see?" He answered: "I feared you would be resentful if you saw, so I kept her outside." The wife said: "I have no jealousy; the spirits are my witnesses. You may bring her home and she can help you manage the household." He then brought her home, and it turned out to be Lotus-Color's own daughter. Mother and daughter met face to face without recognizing each other. Later, while bathing and washing her hair, Lotus-Color carefully examined her features and began to suspect it was her daughter. She asked about her native place, parents, and clan, and the girl answered everything in detail. Then she knew it was truly her daughter.

The mother cried out in shock: "Before, I shared a husband with my mother; now I share a husband with my daughter! In the confusion of birth and death, it has come to this! If one does not sever desire and go forth to study the Way, how will such delusions and inversions ever cease?" She then left and went to the gate of Jetavana, exhausted from hunger and thirst, and sat beneath a tree.

At that time, the World-Honored One was expounding the Dharma, surrounded by an innumerable assembly. Lotus-Color saw the many people coming and going, and thinking it was a festival where there would be food and drink, she entered the monastery. She saw the Buddha, the World-Honored One, expounding the Dharma for the assembly. Hearing the Dharma, her understanding opened, and her hunger and thirst vanished. Then the World-Honored One surveyed the entire assembly to see who should be liberated. Only Lotus-Color was ready to attain the fruit of the path. He then taught her the Dharma of the Four Noble Truths: suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. Right there on her seat, she was freed from dust and defilement and attained the purity of the Dharma-eye. Having attained the fruit, with single-minded devotion and palms joined, she stood facing the Buddha. After the Buddha had finished teaching the Dharma and the assembly had each departed, Lotus-Color came before the Buddha, bowed at his feet, knelt with palms joined, and said to the Buddha: "In the Buddha's Dharma, I wish to be permitted to go forth." The Buddha immediately assented and told Mahāprajāpatī Bhikṣuṇī: "You should now ordain this woman into the path." Receiving his instruction, she ordained her and gave her the precepts. Diligently practicing with vigor, Lotus-Color then attained arhatship. Having attained arhatship, she sported in the various dhyānas and liberations, and her countenance shone with radiance, surpassing what it had been before.

When mealtime came, she took her bowl and entered the city to beg for food. A certain brahmin saw her and became infatuated. He thought: "This bhikṣuṇī cannot be obtained now; I should follow her to her dwelling place and find a way to accomplish my aim." After Lotus-Color finished begging for food, she returned to the Andhavana park and entered her dwelling room. The brahmin secretly followed her to learn where she lived. The next day, when mealtime came and she again went out to beg for food, the brahmin slipped in behind her and hid under her bed. That day, the bhikṣuṇīs discussed the Dharma throughout the night and, exhausted, returned to their rooms and fell deeply asleep lying on their backs. Then the brahmin came out from under the bed and committed the impure act. The bhikṣuṇī immediately awoke and rose up into the sky. At that moment, the brahmin, right there on the bed, was swallowed alive into the earth and entered hell. Lotus-Color, from the sky, went to the Buddha, bowed her head at his feet, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha asked: "At that time, what was your mind like?" She answered: "Like burning iron pressing against the body." The Buddha said: "In such a case, there is no offense." She further asked the Buddha: "Is there any offense in sleeping alone?" The Buddha said: "For one who has attained the path, there is no offense."

At that time, a band of thieves gathered and deliberated: "Where should we go to divide our loot, obtain fine food, and also find beautiful women?" They all said: "This Andhavana park where the bhikṣuṇīs dwell must have beautiful women and also much excellent food and offerings. If we go there to divide our loot, we will surely get what we desire."

At that time, the thief chief was a devotee of the Buddha's teaching. Hearing this, he was displeased and thought: "These people will certainly harass the bhikṣuṇīs. I should secretly send someone ahead to warn them." He immediately dispatched a person who told the bhikṣuṇīs: "This evening, evil men will come. They will certainly harass you. You had better leave." When the bhikṣuṇīs heard this, they entered the city of Śrāvastī. A great minister of that city had previously donated a residence to the Sangha of bhikṣus, but no monks were living there. The bhikṣuṇīs arrived in the evening and stayed there for the night.

At that time, the band of thieves entered the Andhavana park at night but found no one there at all. The thief chief was delighted and thought: "The bhikṣuṇīs have escaped this peril; how wonderful!" He then filled the finest robe with cooked and uncooked delicious food and drink, and hung it from a tree branch, thinking: "If there is a bhikṣuṇī who has attained the path and possesses supernatural powers, let her take this robe and food." Then the bhikṣuṇī Lotus-Color, as quickly as a strong man bends and extends his arm, went from Śrāvastī to the Andhavana park and took it from the tree.

The next day at mealtime, she used the food she had obtained to prepare an offering for the elders Upasena and Upananda. When the time came, they both went. She set out various foods; after the meal, she served water. Then she took a small seat, sat before the assembly, and requested the teaching of the wonderful Dharma. After Upasena had taught the Dharma, he rose from his seat and left. Upananda stayed behind and asked Lotus-Color: "Sister, where did you get this fine food?" Lotus-Color told him the entire story. Upananda said: "Can you show me the robe?" She showed it to him. When Upananda saw it, he became covetous and immediately asked her for it. Lotus-Color said: "This cannot be given! Why? Women have little merit, and they are required to keep five robes." Upananda said: "It is like a person who gives an elephant or horse but does not include the saddle and bridle. You are the same; how can you provide such an elaborate food offering yet begrudge this one robe and not give it to me?" Repeatedly and insistently he pressed her with countless arguments to demand the robe. In the end, unable to resist, she gave it to him.

Upananda took the robe and returned to his dwelling. The bhikṣus saw it and said: "You are a fortunate person to obtain such a fine robe." He answered: "I have no fortune. I pressured the bhikṣuṇī and barely managed to obtain it." When the bhikṣus heard this, they rebuked him in various ways: "How can you pressure a bhikṣuṇī and take her robe?"

At that time, the World-Honored One was troubled by the noise and disturbance of the four assemblies coming and going. He told the bhikṣus: "I now wish to enter quiet seclusion for three months. No one is permitted to come to my place, except for one bhikṣu who brings food. You also should establish a rule among yourselves." They obediently established: "From now on, no one is permitted to go to the Buddha's place without authorization, except for the one bhikṣu who brings food. Those who violate this incur a pāyattika."

At that time, the elder Upasena had not heard about the Sangha's rule. He later arrived in Śrāvastī and asked another bhikṣu: "In which room is the Buddha?" The bhikṣu pointed it out, and he went to the front of the room, knocked on the door with his hand, and the Buddha himself opened it. He bowed at the Buddha's feet and sat to one side. The Buddha asked Upasena: "Your community is pure and your deportment is complete. How do you teach and train them to achieve this?" He answered: "Whoever seeks ordination from me, I instruct them to practice the twelve dhūta austerities for their whole life: You shall be a forest-dweller for life, a beggar for food, eat once a day, eat only one kind of food, accept food only once, beg in sequential order, dwell among graves, wear rag-robes, keep only three robes, sit on whatever bedding is available, sit under trees, and sit in the open. World-Honored One, if a person can practice these ways for their whole life, they may enter my community, and I will serve as their teacher." The Buddha praised him: "Excellent, excellent! You may truly be called one who teaches disciples well." He then asked: "Do you know that this Sangha has established a rule?" He answered: "I do not know. Why? Because I have heard from the Buddha: 'What the Buddha has not established, one may not arbitrarily establish; what has been established, one should follow and practice.'" The Buddha then told him the full account of the above matter. He answered: "I am unable to confess a pāyattika according to the Sangha's rule." The Buddha said: "Excellent! What you say is right." At that time, the resident bhikṣus stood outside the room waiting for Upasena to come out. They told him: "You have violated the Sangha's rule and should confess a pāyattika." He answered: "What pāyattika have I committed?" The bhikṣus explained the above matter in full. He answered: "I will not confess according to the Sangha's rule. Why? I have personally heard from the Buddha: 'If the Buddha has not established it, the Sangha may not establish it. If the Buddha has already established it, the Sangha may not violate it.'"

Then the Buddha himself came out and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, if there is a forest-dwelling bhikṣu like Upasena, he is permitted to come to my place." When the bhikṣus heard this, they thought: "We too should practice these dhūta austerities, and then we can come to the Buddha's place freely." So they all began to practice them.

At that time, the houses and offerings that the householders had provided had no one to accept them. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "I now permit the four assemblies to see me freely."

At that time, Mahāprajāpatī Bhikṣuṇī, hearing this instruction from the Buddha, came with five hundred bhikṣuṇīs toward the Buddha's place. On the way, they met Upasena. In Upasena's assembly, one bhikṣu had coarse, shabby, and worn-out robes. She asked: "Elder, why are you wearing this?" He answered: "I have no other robes." The bhikṣuṇī then pointed to her own robe and said: "Can you wear this?" He answered: "I can." She also asked: "Can you give me the one you are wearing?" He answered: "I can." So they exchanged robes.

They went on to the Buddha's place, bowed their heads at his feet, and sat to one side. The Buddha asked Gautamī: "Why are you wearing this shabby, worn-out robe?" She answered with the above account. The Buddha taught her the Dharma and sent her back to her dwelling. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked that bhikṣu: "Did you really exchange your coarse, shabby robe with a bhikṣuṇī for a fine robe?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." The Buddha rebuked him in various ways: "You foolish person! You should not be greedy!" The bhikṣus then also reported Upananda's matter to the Buddha. The Buddha asked Upananda: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu takes a robe from a bhikṣuṇī, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

There were bhikṣus who had bhikṣuṇī relatives with many acquaintances who could obtain cloth and garments. These bhikṣus were wearing coarse, shabby robes. The bhikṣuṇīs asked: "Why are you wearing such poor robes?" They answered: "There is no place to obtain them." They said: "Why don't you get them from me?" They answered: "The Buddha has ruled that one may not take robes from bhikṣuṇīs." The bhikṣuṇīs said: "Only among relatives can one know what may be given and what may be taken. We wish you would report this to the Buddha." The bhikṣus then reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus, praised few desires and contentment in various ways, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu takes a robe from a non-relative bhikṣuṇī, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

At that time, bhikṣus and bhikṣuṇīs in Śrāvastī jointly received a gift of robes and divided them together. Sometimes a robe suitable for bhikṣus to wear was received by bhikṣuṇīs, and sometimes a robe suitable for bhikṣuṇīs to wear was received by bhikṣus. The bhikṣuṇīs said to the bhikṣus: "Exchange robes with us." They answered: "The Buddha does not permit us to take robes from non-relative bhikṣuṇīs." The bhikṣuṇīs said: "Exchanging robe for robe, how can this be called 'taking'?" They went and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus, praised few desires and contentment in various ways, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu takes a robe from a non-relative bhikṣuṇī, except in exchange, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Non-relative" means: from one's parents up to seven generations, there is no kinship.

"Exchange" means: it is mutually beneficial, and each takes what is suitable for them.

Taking a robe from a śikṣamāṇā or śrāmaṇerī is a duṣkṛta.

If a relative is one who violates the precepts or holds wrong views, taking a robe from her is a duṣkṛta.

A śrāmaṇera who takes a robe from a bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, or śrāmaṇerī incurs a duṣkṛta.

If one does not seek it but she gives of her own accord, and one knows she has surplus and then accepts, there is no offense.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda, one morning, put on his robes, took his bowl, and went to the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā's place. He sat down and stood up carelessly and inadvertently exposed his body. Upananda saw this and ejaculated impure matter. The bhikṣuṇī noticed and said: "Elder, give me your robe to wash." He took it off and gave it to her. After she obtained the robe, she then placed the impure matter inside her own body.

Also, other bhikṣus gave their robes to bhikṣuṇīs, ordering them to wash, dye, and beat them. At that time, the bhikṣuṇīs, burdened with these tasks, were hindered from recitation, meditation, and practice of the Way. Lay people saw this and rebuked them in various ways, saying: "The bhikṣuṇīs are constantly engaged in washing, dyeing, and beating robes. How are they different from householders?"

At that time, Mahāprajāpatī Bhikṣuṇī, together with five hundred bhikṣuṇīs, went to the Buddha. They bowed their heads at his feet and sat to one side. The Buddha asked Gautamī: "Why do the bhikṣuṇīs' hands and feet all have dye stains?" She answered with the full account. After the Buddha taught the wonderful Dharma for the bhikṣuṇīs, they each returned to their dwellings. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really have bhikṣuṇīs wash, dye, and beat your robes?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." The bhikṣus then also reported Upananda's matter to the Buddha. After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu has a bhikṣuṇī wash an old robe, or dye it, or beat it, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

There were elderly and sick bhikṣus who could not wash, dye, or beat their own robes. Bhikṣuṇī relatives who were able to wash, dye, and beat came and asked to do it for them. The bhikṣus said: "The Buddha does not permit us to have bhikṣuṇīs wash, dye, or beat our robes." The bhikṣuṇīs said: "Only among relatives can one know what is appropriate and what is not. We wish you would report this to the Buddha." The bhikṣus then reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus, praised few desires and contentment, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu has a non-relative bhikṣuṇī wash an old robe, or dye it, or beat it, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Old robe" means: one that has been worn and has become soiled.

If he orders her to wash, and she washes but does not dye or beat; if he orders her to dye, and she dyes but does not wash or beat; if he orders her to beat, and she beats but does not wash or dye; if he orders her to wash and dye, and she washes and dyes but does not beat; if he orders her to wash and beat, and she washes and beats but does not dye; if he orders her to dye and beat, and she dyes and beats but does not wash; if he orders her to wash, dye, and beat, and she washes, dyes, and beats: all are niḥsargika-pāyattika.

If he orders her to wash, but she does not wash and instead dyes; if he orders her to wash, but she does not wash and instead beats; if he orders her to wash, but she does not wash and instead dyes and beats: all are duṣkṛta.

If he orders her to dye, but she does not dye and instead washes, or beats, or washes and beats; if he orders her to beat, but she does not beat and instead washes, or dyes, or washes and dyes; if he orders her to wash and dye, but she does not wash and dye and instead beats; if he orders her to wash and beat, but she does not wash and beat and instead dyes; if he orders her to dye and beat, but she does not dye and beat and instead washes: it is likewise.

If he orders a non-relative to wash, dye, and beat, but a relative washes, dyes, and beats; if he orders a non-relative to wash, dye, and beat, but a relative and a non-relative together wash, dye, and beat; if he orders a relative and a non-relative together to wash, dye, and beat, but a relative washes, dyes, and beats; if he orders a relative and a non-relative together to wash, dye, and beat, but a non-relative washes, dyes, and beats; if he orders a relative and a non-relative together to wash, dye, and beat, and a relative and a non-relative together wash, dye, and beat: all are niḥsargika-pāyattika.

If the robe is not yet ready to be washed, dyed, or beaten, and he orders a non-relative to wash, dye, or beat it, it is a duṣkṛta.

If he orders a relative to wash, dye, and beat, but a non-relative washes, dyes, and beats, there is no offense. Everything else is as stated in the section on taking robes.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, in the city there was a householder who was fond of fine clothing, who believed in and delighted in the Buddha's Dharma, and who constantly went to hear the teachings. At that time, the householder, wearing his finest clothes and accompanied by his retinue, went out from the city to pay respects to the World-Honored One and the bhikṣus. After the Buddha taught him the Dharma, showing, instructing, benefiting, and gladdening him, the householder bowed and took his leave. On the way he met Upananda. Upananda also taught him the Dharma. As they were about to part, the householder said: "Elder, tomorrow please come to my home for a simple meal." Upananda answered: "I do not lack food; what I suffer from is lack of robes. Can you give me one of the robes you are wearing?" The householder said: "I will give you one, but let me return home and consider it. I cannot simply give it to you right now." Upananda said: "I have heard that you are fond of generosity. How is it that toward me alone you are so distant and stingy?" He also said: "I teach the Dharma, enabling liberation from birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, suffering, and affliction. For the sake of liberating people like you, I neglect my own livelihood. How can you now begrudge this one robe?" Thereupon the householder took it off and gave it to him.

When the householder reached the city gate, the gatekeeper asked: "You went out wearing heavy garments, but now you return lightly dressed. Did you give them to a woman? Were you robbed?" He answered: "I did not give them to a woman, nor was I robbed. They were forcibly begged from me by a śramaṇa follower of the Śākya clan." The gatekeeper said: "Do not say such things! I have heard that the śramaṇas who are followers of the Śākya clan have few desires and are content. Even if someone gives them something, they may not accept it. How could they now forcibly beg for people's possessions?" The householder repeated as before. Those who did not believe in or delight in the Buddha's Dharma, upon hearing this, proclaimed: "Good, it serves him right to be robbed! If you continue to associate with them, it will be even worse! Those śramaṇas who are followers of the Śākya clan constantly praise generosity and condemn taking what is not given. Yet now they forcibly take people's clothing and possessions; how are they different from robbers?" When the householder returned home, the questions and answers among the household members were all as above.

When the senior bhikṣus heard of this, they rebuked Upananda in various ways, brought him to the Buddha, and reported the matter. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked Upananda: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu begs for a robe from a householder or a householder's wife, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

At that time, the bhikṣus were wearing coarse, shabby robes. Their relatives saw them and said: "Why are you wearing such worn-out robes? Why don't you take some from us?" They answered: "The Buddha does not permit us to beg for robes from householders or householders' wives. You may give to the Sangha, and we will take from the Sangha." The relatives said: "We specifically wish to give to the bhikṣus; we do not wish to give to the Sangha so that others may receive them." The bhikṣus said: "If the Buddha permitted us to beg for robes from relative householders and householders' wives, we would not be wearing such shabby robes." The relatives said: "Only among relatives can one know what may be given and what may be taken. We wish you would report this to the Buddha." The bhikṣus then reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu begs for a robe from a non-relative householder or householder's wife, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

At that time, many bhikṣus were traveling with a merchant caravan. They lost their way and were robbed and stripped. Naked, with bare flesh, they returned toward the city of Śrāvastī. People on the road asked: "Who are you?" They answered: "We are śramaṇas, followers of the Śākya clan." They were further asked: "Where are your robes and bowls?" They answered: "They were taken by robbers." When they reached Jetavana, the bhikṣus questioned them, and the answers were the same. They were further asked: "If you are bhikṣus, how did you receive ordination, observe the poṣadha, and perform the pravāraṇā?" They answered: "We received ordination, observed the poṣadha, and performed the pravāraṇā in such-and-such a manner." Yet the bhikṣus still did not give them robes. They then went to the Buddha. The Buddha rebuked them: "How can you come naked before the Buddha? Could you not have obtained tree leaves or grass to cover your bodies?" He told the bhikṣus: "From now on, those who come naked before the Buddha incur a duṣkṛta." The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "The Buddha does not permit us to beg for robes from non-relative householders or householders' wives. Our relatives are very far from here. How can we obtain robes?" The Buddha said: "Have you already been to the resident bhikṣus?" They answered: "We have." He asked: "Why did they not give you robes?" They answered: "The bhikṣus were interrogating us: 'How did you receive ordination, observe the poṣadha, and perform the pravāraṇā?' Although we answered properly, they still did not give us any." The Buddha rebuked the resident bhikṣus from afar: "How can you see bhikṣus naked and not show concern for them?" He then praised few desires and contentment for the sake of the bhikṣus who had lost their robes, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu begs for a robe from a non-relative householder or householder's wife, except when there is a valid reason, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika. Valid reasons are: robes stolen, robes lost, robes burned, robes swept away by water, or robes destroyed. These are the valid reasons."

If robes are stolen or even destroyed, but one still has other robes, or has robes stored in another place, one may not beg.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, many bhikṣus came from the town of Pāri toward the Buddha, were robbed, and lost their robes. They said to each other: "Although the Buddha permits begging for robes from non-relative householders and householders' wives under the five valid circumstances, we do not know from whom to beg."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus thought: "These bhikṣus have lost their robes and do not know from whom to beg. We should solicit for them, and if there is any surplus, we will take it for ourselves." Having thought this, they told the bhikṣus who had lost their robes. The bhikṣus who had lost their robes said: "Excellent!"

Then the group-of-six bhikṣus went throughout the city telling all the householders and householders' wives: "Some bhikṣus have come from the town of Pāri, wishing to see the World-Honored One, and were robbed and lost their robes. You should all contribute and give to them."

When the householders and householders' wives heard this, each contributed generously, and a great quantity of robes was obtained, more than enough for each person. The bhikṣus who had lost their robes said: "We already have enough; there is no need to beg further." The group-of-six bhikṣus said: "You have a valid reason for begging for robes, but we do not. Allow us to use your reason to beg for more." The bhikṣus who had lost their robes said: "As the elders wish."

Then the group-of-six bhikṣus went around begging again and obtained a great many robes. At that time, the householders gathered and deliberated: "The bhikṣus who lost their robes are not so many. All the men, women, and children of our city contributed generously, which should have been more than enough. Why are they still asking for more? Could it be that they wish to hoard, trade, or barter, and are not cultivating the pure practice?" When the senior bhikṣus heard of this, they rebuked them in various ways and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a bhikṣu whose robes have been stolen, lost, burned, swept away by water, or destroyed begs for robes from non-relative householders or householders' wives, and the householders or householders' wives wish to give many robes, that bhikṣu should accept at most two robes. If he accepts more than this, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda constantly visited a certain householder's home and taught the Dharma, and also helped manage matters of illness and legal affairs. A certain bhikṣu, one morning, put on his robes, took his bowl, and entered the city to beg for food. He happened to pass by that household and overheard the husband and wife discussing: "Upananda has been kind to us. We should use such-and-such an amount of robe-cloth to make a robe and give it to him." After begging for food, that bhikṣu returned and told Upananda: "You are fortunate!" Upananda said: "What fortune do I have?" He answered: "Today when I was begging for food, I went to a certain householder's home and heard the husband and wife discussing: 'Upananda has been kind to us. We should use such-and-such an amount of robe-cloth to make a robe and give it to him.' If you go there now, you will certainly receive it without doubt."

The next morning at mealtime, Upananda put on his robes, took his bowl, and went to that house. The householder came out and greeted him. Upananda said: "Are you making a robe for me with such-and-such an amount of robe-cloth?" He answered: "That is so!" Upananda said: "You know that I do not wear poor robes. If you make a good robe, I shall wear it myself and always remember you; for matters of illness and legal affairs, I shall take care of you. But if it is not good, I shall give it to my disciple, or put it in a container. You will merely lose this material, with no merit from the giving." At that time, the householder said to those around him: "This person is insatiable, hard to support, and hard to satisfy. What I intended to give of my own accord, even five or six times more, would still not please him. Though we were on good terms before, from now on it will be otherwise." In the end, he did not give it. When the senior bhikṣus heard of this, they rebuked Upananda in various ways, brought him to the Buddha, and reported the matter. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked Upananda: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited thus:

"If a non-relative householder and householder's wife discuss together: 'We shall use such-and-such an amount of robe-cloth to make a robe and give it to a certain bhikṣu,' and that bhikṣu, without having first been invited to make requests, goes and asks the householder or householder's wife: 'Are you making a robe for me with such-and-such an amount of robe-cloth?' They answer: 'That is so.' He then says: 'Excellent, householder or householder's wife! Please make such a robe and give it to me; make it fine.' It is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Without having first been invited to make requests" means: they had not previously asked the bhikṣu what kind of robe he needed.

"Make it fine" means: requesting that it be made extremely fine, superior to what was originally planned.

If one requests a fine robe from a relative, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. Upananda also had another household he regularly visited, and the husband and
