# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Five

### First Division: Niḥsargika-Pāyattika Rules, Continued

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, many householders together invited the Buddha and the Sangha. Among them, some were splitting firewood, some were fetching water, some were sweeping and sprinkling the ground, some were laying out sitting mats, some were arranging flowers, some were setting up the high seat, and some were preparing the food.

At that time, Upananda, early in the morning, put on his robes and took his bowl, and went first to the household that had made the invitation. Going to each person, according to whatever they were doing, he praised them, and further said: "What you are doing now is joyful and excellent." The people said: "We are truly joyful, making these offerings! We want to make the food and drink various kinds of delicacies, and we shall also distribute robes to the Sangha." Upananda said: "Many people give robes and goods to the Sangha; if you also give, they will just pile up and become useless things. Why waste your merit on useless giving, rather than giving to me? If you give to me, I will personally wear them and always think of you. In sickness or legal matters, I will take care of things for you." When those people heard this, they gathered together to discuss. Among them, some said: "If the Sangha does not need them, we may as well give to him, so that we obtain the merit of giving something that will be used." Others said: "Originally it was intended as an offering to the Sangha; how can we redirect it to one person?" Those in favor of giving were the majority, so they gave them to him.

At that time, Upananda carried a heavy load of robes and returned to the monastery. The bhikṣus exclaimed: "You are a person of great merit! How is it that you went out briefly and obtained these robes?" Upananda said: "It was obtained through clever eloquence, not through merit." He then gave a complete account of how he had obtained the robes. When the senior bhikṣus heard this, they rebuked him in various ways: "You foolish person! How can you redirect offerings meant for the Sangha and take them for yourself?"

At that time, those householders, having finished preparing the food, sent a messenger to inform the Buddha. Thereupon the World-Honored One put on his robes and took his bowl, surrounded by the bhikṣu Sangha in front and behind, went to their home, and sat down on the seat. The householders personally served the food with their own hands. After the meal, water was offered, but there was no gift-offering. Those who had originally not wanted to give to Upananda whispered among themselves: "Today our food lacked nothing. Yet certain people, without good reason, took what was meant as an offering to the Sangha and gave it to one person alone, so we are short of the dakṣiṇā. Are they not ashamed?" The bhikṣus asked: "What are you whispering about?" They answered with the full account. The bhikṣus rebuked Upananda in various ways: "You foolish person! How can you redirect offerings meant for the Sangha and take them for yourself?"

At that time, the World-Honored One, having taught the wonderful Dharma for the householders, rose from his seat and departed. The senior bhikṣus reported this matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Upananda: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu redirects offerings intended for the Sangha to himself, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

There were bhikṣus who did not know that certain offerings were intended for the Sangha, and redirected them to themselves. When they later learned, they felt ashamed, or had already confessed. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha, praised fewness of desires and contentment, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and then told the bhikṣus: "If one does not know that an offering is intended for the Sangha and redirects it to oneself, there is no grounds for an offense of niḥsargika-pāyattika. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, knowing that a donor intends to give something to the Sangha, redirects it to himself, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Knowing" means: either knowing oneself or hearing from another.

"Intending to give to the Sangha" means: if a person has made up their mind and says: "I shall take this thing and give it to that Sangha."

If one redirects what was intended as an offering to the Sangha and gives it to another person, it is pāyattika. If one redirects it to another Sangha, to the bhikṣuṇī Sangha, to the combined Sangha of both orders, to the Sangha of the four directions, or to a stūpa, in all cases it is duṣkṛta. If one redirects what was intended for the bhikṣuṇī Sangha, the combined Sangha of both orders, or the Sangha of the four directions, it is likewise.

If one redirects offerings intended for a stūpa to oneself, or gives them to the bhikṣu Sangha, the bhikṣuṇī Sangha, the combined Sangha of both orders, the Sangha of the four directions, to another person, or to another stūpa, in all cases it is duṣkṛta. If one redirects what was intended as an offering to a person, it is likewise. Even redirecting a morsel of rice intended for one animal to another animal is also duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is duṣkṛta.

If one informs the Sangha and the Sangha consents; or if the donor themselves redirects what was intended for the Sangha and gives it to oneself, there is no offense.

---

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, Pilindavatsa was dwelling on Mount Laṅkura. He was flying through the air, plastering and sprinkling the room where he lived. At that time, King Bimbisāra went to that mountain. Pilindavatsa, seeing the king coming, suddenly returned to the ground and said: "Welcome, great king! Please sit here." After the king sat down, he asked: "Why are you doing this yourself? Do you have no park-keeper?" He answered: "I do not." The king then told one of his ministers: "Provide this bhikṣu with a park-keeper." Pilindavatsa said: "The Buddha does not permit me to keep a park-keeper." The king said: "You may report this to the Buddha." After the king left, he reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha, praised fewness of desires and contentment, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit bhikṣus to keep park-keepers." The minister whom the king had ordered had no faith in or fondness for the Buddha-Dharma and ultimately did not provide one, and Pilindavatsa also did not ask for one. Later, he put on his robes and took his bowl, entered the city to beg for food. The king and his ministers saw him from afar on a high building, and the king had this thought: "I previously promised that bhikṣu a park-keeper; I wonder whether he has received one or not?" He then asked the minister he had previously ordered. The minister said: "I have not yet given one." The king then asked: "How many days has it been since my order?" The minister said: "It has been five hundred days." The king said: "Give him that number of people according to the number of days." The great minister followed the order and assigned five hundred households, one person from each household. The five hundred households each day assigned one person in turn to sweep and clean the dwelling and attend to whatever was needed.

At that time, the people of that village, on a festival day, had men and women adorned with splendid clothing, going out to enjoy themselves. There was one poor girl who went about crying loudly. At that time, Pilindavatsa entered the village to beg for food and saw the girl crying. He asked her mother: "Why is your daughter crying like this?" She answered: "Today everyone is dressed in fine attire, going out to enjoy themselves. Our family is poor, unable to match others; that is why she weeps." At that time, Pilindavatsa saw a cow eating grass. He said to the mother: "Bring me a little grass." She fetched some and gave it to him. Pilindavatsa then wove the grass, transforming it into two golden flower garlands, and gave them to the girl's mother, saying: "In this world, there are two kinds of gold that are supreme: Jambudvīpa gold and that created by supernatural power. You may take these and give them to your daughter to wear." The girl, having received them, was extremely joyful. She wore them and went about, and everyone admired her. At that time, there was a person who, seeing this, became envious and jealous, and reported to King Bimbisāra: "In such-and-such village, such-and-such household has found a fine hidden treasure. The flower garland that the girl wears is unmatched in the world, and your majesty's inner palace has nothing like it." The king immediately summoned the family and said: "You have found a hidden treasure; show it to me." They answered: "We truly did not find one." The king then asked: "Where did your daughter get what she wears?" They answered: "It was Pilindavatsa who wove grass and transformed it." When the king heard this, he became extremely angry and skeptical: "How can weaving grass produce golden garlands?" He then ordered the authorities to arrest them and throw them in prison.

Later, Pilindavatsa again went to that village and saw the same woman weeping loudly. He asked: "Why are you crying loudly again today?" She answered: "My family member is in prison." He asked: "For what offense?" She answered: "Because of the golden flower garland that the venerable one gave us." He said: "Do not weep! I will arrange for your family to be released immediately."

Pilindavatsa then went first to the prison warden's office. When the prison wardens saw him, they all greeted him respectfully, saying: "Venerable one! Why have you troubled yourself to come here?" He answered: "My park-keeper is imprisoned; that is why I have come. Can you release this person for me?" They answered: "This person found a fine hidden treasure and did not show it to the king. If they show it to the king, they may be released." Pilindavatsa said: "I wove it from grass; it is not a hidden treasure." Those people said: "To weave gold from grass is impossible." Pilindavatsa then transformed all the seats where they sat into golden beds, and said: "Now see for yourselves what you are sitting on." They all saw themselves sitting on golden beds and became greatly terrified. They got down from the beds and prostrated, saying: "Please forgive us! Quickly undo this! If the king hears that we sat on golden beds, he will surely punish us severely." Pilindavatsa said: "Release my park-keeper, and then I will undo it for you." They said: "This is not within our authority." He asked: "Whose authority is it?" They answered: "The king's." Pilindavatsa then undid the transformation and flew to the king's presence, hovering in the air. At that time, the king was on the upper story of a high building. Seeing him, the king made obeisance and asked: "Venerable one! What brings you here?" He answered: "My park-keeper is imprisoned; that is why I have come. Please release them!" The king said: "That person found a fine hidden treasure. If they show it to me, they may be released." Pilindavatsa said: "I wove it from grass; it is not a hidden treasure." The king said: "To weave gold from grass is impossible." At that time, Pilindavatsa struck a pillar of the king's building with his staff, and it instantly transformed into a golden building. He asked: "King, what is this tall building made of?" The king was delighted and immediately ordered the release.

In this way, Pilindavatsa successively manifested the four kinds of supernatural powers on four occasions. At that time, the people, hearing of and seeing the miraculous transformations, gave rise to faith and delight in the Buddha-Dharma. They offered the Sangha morning meals and afternoon meals, drinks, non-time beverages, bathing supplies, body ointments, foot ointments, and lamp oil.

At that time, the Sangha received large quantities of raw and cooked ghee, oil, honey, and rock sugar. They could not finish consuming them, and they accumulated on the ground, flowing everywhere, staining robes, bedding, and sleeping materials with mud. When householders saw this, they asked: "Whose things are these?" Some people answered: "These are accumulated by the śramaṇas of the Śākya clan." The householders said: "The śramaṇas of the Śākya clan claim to eat sparingly, yet they accumulate like this and eat as they please. They claim to seek liberation from birth, old age, and death, but now they only seek such fine flavors. They have no śramaṇa conduct; they destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" When the senior bhikṣus heard this, they rebuked them in various ways and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I do not permit consuming ghee, oil, honey, or rock sugar that has been kept overnight. Offenders commit duṣkṛta!"

At that time, many bhikṣus were ill and could not obtain a lay attendant to receive these daily on their behalf; they also had no money to buy them, and there was nowhere to purchase them. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha, praised fewness of desires and contentment, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit sick bhikṣus to consume ghee, oil, honey, and rock sugar kept overnight for up to six nights."

At that time, bhikṣus exceeded six nights. The senior bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu is sick, he may take the four kinds of tonics: ghee, oil, honey, and rock sugar. Once received, they may be kept for up to seven days. If they exceed this, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

If one receives something on the first day, receives more on the second day, and receives more up to the seventh day, and any of it is kept until the dawn of the eighth day, it is all niḥsargika-pāyattika. One should formally relinquish it to the Sangha. The Sangha should give it to laypeople or śrāmaṇeras. It may be used for lighting lamps or applying to the feet; only the bhikṣu who relinquished the medicine may not use it, and no bhikṣu may consume it.

For bhikṣuṇīs it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, there were bandits of the eighth month who constantly lay in wait to capture people and kill them as sacrifices to heaven. All people and bhikṣus were terrified. The day of sacrifice was approaching, yet they had not captured anyone. The bandits discussed among themselves: "In forest-dwelling places there must be bhikṣus; they would be easy to capture." They went to one such place. The bhikṣus, upon hearing of this, each fled. The bandits, having failed to capture anyone, further discussed: "We should go to another place and not be lazy, lest we miss the day of sacrifice." At that time, among that group there was one who had formerly been a monk. He said to the group: "I have heard that the Buddha's teaching does not permit bhikṣus to be separated from their robes for even one night. If we simply guard the place, they will surely return before dawn." The others said: "If they do not return, we will kill you instead. If you do not object, we will stay." He answered: "Very well!" Thereupon the bandits stayed.

At that time, the bhikṣus, fearing the offense of sleeping separated from their robes, all returned in the latter part of the night. The bandits asked: "Did you think we had already left?" They answered: "We knew you were here, but the Buddha does not permit us to sleep separated from our robes; that is why we returned." The bandits then killed them, taking blood where they needed blood and cutting flesh where they needed flesh. Those who did not die had this thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us, before the eighth month is over, to leave one of our robes deposited at a layperson's home within the boundary, we would not have suffered this disaster." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha, praised fewness of desires and contentment, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit bhikṣus dwelling in forest places, during the three months of the rains retreat, before the eighth month is over, to deposit one robe at a layperson's home within the boundary and sleep separated from it without offense."

There were bhikṣus dwelling near a village who also deposited one robe at a layperson's home within the boundary and slept separated from it. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I do not permit those dwelling near a village to sleep separated from their robes."

Further, there were bhikṣus in forest-dwelling places where there was no danger who slept separated from their robes. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I also do not permit sleeping separated from robes in forest-dwelling places where there is no danger. Only when there is doubt and fear do I permit it."

At that time, bhikṣus who had deposited their robes at others' homes did not go to check on them at all. Over time, they became damp and soiled, gnawed by insects and decayed. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should go check on them and air them out."

At that time, the bhikṣus went frequently, and the householders became annoyed. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit checking once every ten days."

At that time, bhikṣus had Sangha business, stūpa business, preceptor or teacher's business, or other business requiring them to go outside the boundary, but because of their robes they did not dare leave. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If there is business that necessarily requires one to go outside the boundary, I permit being separated from robes for one night."

Bhikṣus went outside the boundary for one night, but their business was not finished. They reported this to the Buddha again. The Buddha said: "I permit up to six nights."

Having permitted six nights, bhikṣus then wore coarse, shabby robes and traveled, exceeding six nights. The senior bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu dwells in a forest place during the three months of the rains retreat, before the eighth month is over, in a place where there is danger, he is permitted to deposit one robe at a layperson's home within the boundary. If there is a reason to go outside the boundary, he may sleep separated from this robe for up to six nights. If he exceeds this, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Three months of the rains retreat" means: the early rains retreat.

"Before the eighth month is over" means: the later rains retreat.

"One robe" means: either the saṅghāṭī or the uttarāsaṅga; whichever is more valued, one robe should be deposited. One may not deposit the antaravāsaka, because it is worn on the body; for prostrations, entering the Sangha assembly, and begging for food, one cannot wear only a single robe; and one may not deposit two robes.

"Having a reason to go outside the boundary for six nights" means: if there is stūpa business, preceptor or teacher's business, or other business, one may leave one robe at a layperson's home and go outside the boundary for a maximum of six nights. If the business is concluded in one night, two nights, up to five nights, and one does not return, it is duṣkṛta.

For śrāmaṇeras, it is duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Viśākhā Mṛgāramātā invited the Buddha and the Sangha to a meal the next day. That day there happened to be a terrifying rainstorm from heaven. The rain was such that in the time it takes a strong man to flex and extend his arm, a single bowl would be filled. The earth received this water like a drop of oil falling on a pile of hot sand; had it not been so, it would have formed a great ocean. The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Just as it now rains within Jetavana, so too it rains throughout Jambudvīpa. You may all go out and bathe in it, for this is the last equal rain." The bhikṣus then went out and bathed naked in the rain.

At that time, Viśākhā sent a maidservant to tell the Buddha: "The food is ready." The maidservant arrived at Jetavana and saw all the bhikṣus bathing naked. She thought: "These are non-Buddhist practitioners, not bhikṣus." She returned and reported thus. Viśākhā thought: "They must be bhikṣus bathing outdoors; this foolish maidservant does not know and mistakes them for non-Buddhist practitioners." She sent her again, saying: "Go to the gate of Jetavana and make this announcement: 'The food is ready; may the holy ones know the time.'" The maidservant went again to the gate of Jetavana, intending to make the announcement as instructed. But the bhikṣus had finished bathing and returned to their rooms; she did not see a single person. She thought: "Earlier this place was full of non-Buddhist practitioners; now they are gone." She returned home and reported likewise. Viśākhā thought again: "The bhikṣus must have finished bathing and are resting." She sent her again, saying: "You may enter the gate and make the announcement in the courtyard." The maidservant again followed the instruction, entered the gate of Jetavana, and made the announcement in the courtyard. The Buddha, hearing the announcement, told the bhikṣus: "Viśākhā has announced that the time has come. You should all put on your robes and take your bowls, and together accept her invitation." The bhikṣus obeyed the instruction and all gathered in the universal assembly hall. The maidservant then came forward and again addressed the Buddha: "The food is ready; may the holy ones know the time." The Buddha said: "You may go ahead; we will arrive shortly." Thereupon the World-Honored One, in the time it takes a strong man to flex and extend his arm, together with the bhikṣus vanished from the universal assembly hall and appeared on the seats that Viśākhā had arranged, with their robes not wet. Viśākhā, seeing the Buddha and the Sangha suddenly seated there with dry robes, thought: "I have gained a great benefit, making offerings to such a holy teacher and holy disciples. Though the heavenly rain poured down torrentially, their robes are not wet." Filled with joy and elation, she served various fine foods with her own hands. After the meal, when water had been offered, she stood to one side with palms joined, and addressed the Buddha: "May the World-Honored One grant me my wishes!" The Buddha told Viśākhā: "The Buddha, in this world, has forever departed from all wishes." Viśākhā again said: "May the Buddha grant me pure and attainable wishes." The Buddha said: "Very good!" Viśākhā addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! This morning I sent my maidservant to announce that the food was ready. She saw all the bhikṣus bathing naked and returned to tell me that Jetavana was full of non-Buddhist practitioners and there were no bhikṣus. World-Honored One! How can bhikṣus bathe naked before their preceptors and teachers? I wish the Buddha would permit all bhikṣus to keep rain-bathing robes. I will, for the rest of my life, provide rain-bathing robes to all the bhikṣus of Śrāvastī." She further said: "Recently, on a small errand, I went to the Aciravatī River and saw bhikṣuṇīs bathing naked in the river. People who saw them all laughed and mocked, saying: 'Even when a woman wears clothing, she still may not look attractive; how much less so when a renunciant bares her body.' I wish the Buddha would also permit all bhikṣuṇīs to keep water-bathing robes. I will also, for the rest of my life, provide water-bathing robes to all the bhikṣuṇīs of Śrāvastī." She further said: "The Buddha has spoken of three kinds of illness: the first kind, one who dies whether or not medicine is obtained; the second kind, one who recovers whether or not medicine is obtained; the third kind, one who recovers if medicine is obtained but dies if medicine is not obtained. I wish the Buddha would permit all bhikṣus to take medicines. I will also, for the rest of my life, provide medicines to all the bhikṣus of Śrāvastī." She further said: "The Buddha has spoken of three kinds of illness: the first kind, one who dies whether or not appropriate food is obtained; the second kind, one who lives whether or not appropriate food is obtained; the third kind, one who lives if appropriate food is obtained but dies if it is not obtained. I wish the Buddha would permit all bhikṣus to eat food appropriate to their illness. I will also, for the rest of my life, provide food appropriate to illness to all the bhikṣus of Śrāvastī." She further said: "If a caretaker of the sick goes out to beg for food, the sick one's care suffers. I wish the Buddha would permit all bhikṣus to accept food for those who tend the sick. I will also, for the rest of my life, provide food for those tending the sick among all the bhikṣus of Śrāvastī." She further said: "When guest bhikṣus arrive, weary from the road, they do not know where to beg for food upon first arriving. I wish the Buddha would permit all bhikṣus to accept my food for guest bhikṣus, so they may rest from fatigue and learn where to beg for food. I will also, for the rest of my life, provide food for guest bhikṣus in Śrāvastī." She further said: "If bhikṣus who are about to depart on a journey enter the village to beg for food, they may miss their companions, and on a desolate road they may encounter the bandits of the eighth month or lose their way. I wish the Buddha would permit departing bhikṣus to accept my food for departing bhikṣus. I will also, for the rest of my life, provide food for departing bhikṣus in Śrāvastī." She further said: "I have heard that the World-Honored One permitted the bhikṣus of the Ānapriya country to eat congee. I wish the Buddha would permit all bhikṣus to accept my congee. I will also, for the rest of my life, provide congee to all the bhikṣus of Śrāvastī." She further addressed the Buddha: "I wish the World-Honored One would accept from me, for the rest of my life, robes, food, and medicines."

The Buddha asked Viśākhā: "Seeing what benefit do you ask for these nine wishes?" She answered: "Bhikṣus from various regions will come to this country to pay respects to the World-Honored One. If someone says that a certain bhikṣu in such-and-such a place has passed away and attained the fruit of stream-entry, once-returning, non-returning, or arhatship, I will ask them: 'Did that bhikṣu ever come here?' If they answer: 'He once came,' I will think: 'That bhikṣu must have once received from me at least one kind of offering,' and I will be filled with joy and my roots of merit will increase." Thereupon the World-Honored One told Viśākhā: "I grant you eight wishes; one wish cannot be granted."

At that time, Viśākhā took a small seat and sat before the Buddha. The Buddha spoke a verse of rejoicing for her:

> "Joyfully giving food and drink
> To the Buddha and holy disciples,
> Performing merit that destroys miserliness,
> One receives rewards of constant joy and happiness.
> Born in heaven with long life,
> Returning here, free from defilement and dust,
> The great fruit of practicing the Dharma
> Is dwelling long in the pure heavenly bliss."

At that time, the World-Honored One further taught various wonderful Dharma, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and delighting her. He then returned to Jetavana, assembled the bhikṣus, praised fewness of desires and contentment, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit all bhikṣus to receive rain-bathing robes; all bhikṣuṇīs to receive water-bathing robes; to accept medicines appropriate to illness, food appropriate to illness, food for those tending the sick, food for guest bhikṣus, food for departing bhikṣus, and congee."

At that time, the bhikṣus had this thought: "The Buddha permits us to keep rain-bathing robes." They then constantly begged for and kept them, neither formally designating them, nor giving them to others, nor making them determined as surplus. They carried heavy loads of robes as they traveled. When other bhikṣus saw them, they asked: "Have you not heard the Buddha's rule against keeping extra robes?" They answered: "Although the Buddha has such a rule, he also permits keeping rain-bathing robes." The bhikṣus further asked: "Do you constantly keep rain-bathing robes without formally designating them, without giving them to others, and without determining them as surplus?" They answered: "That is so!" The senior bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, when one month of spring remains, should seek a rain-bathing robe, and when half a month remains, should wear it. If before one month remains he seeks it, or before half a month remains he wears it, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Rain-bathing robe" means: used during the rainy season for bathing; it is also used during the summer bathing season.

When one month of spring remains, if there is someone who has previously promised to give a rain-bathing robe, a friendly bhikṣu should go on his behalf and say: "Now is the time for sewing, dyeing, and making rain-bathing robes." If he obtains one, good. If not, he should again speak on his behalf: "Everywhere, people have already sewn, dyed, and made rain-bathing robes." If he obtains one, good. If not, he should further speak on his behalf: "You previously promised to give a rain-bathing robe to such-and-such a bhikṣu; now is the proper time." If he obtains one, good. If not, that bhikṣu should seek one elsewhere and may keep it until the fifteenth of the eighth month, a total of one hundred and thirty-five days of keeping it. If after this time he has not made it into another robe, has not formally designated it, has not given it to others, and has not determined it as surplus, it is duṣkṛta.

For śrāmaṇeras, it is duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus arrived at a merchant village. The merchants said: "Venerable ones, stay here for the rains retreat. When we return from our journey, we will give you rains-retreat offerings." The group-of-six bhikṣus said: "If you wish us to stay, then give to us now. During the rains retreat, we will make robes; when the retreat is over, we will wear them and go to pay respects to the Buddha." The merchants discussed among themselves: "If we give the rains-retreat offerings first, the bhikṣus will stay. Our family members will be able to hear the Dharma, receive the eight precepts, and purify their body, speech, and mind." So they collected the goods and gave them, and then departed on their journey.

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus, having received the rains-retreat offering goods, left for another place after the merchants had departed. When those merchants returned having made a profit, they told their family: "Although we have already given the bhikṣus the rains-retreat offerings, now that we have returned safely with profit, we should make further offerings. You all may relax and listen to the Dharma." The family members answered: "The bhikṣus left shortly after you departed." The merchants then invited other bhikṣus from the vicinity. Those bhikṣus answered: "You should make offerings to those you originally invited; we cannot accept your offerings." The merchants then said angrily: "We originally gave those offerings for them to stay here for the rains retreat. They took our goods and left; how is this different from theft?" When the senior bhikṣus heard this, they rebuked them in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I do not permit receiving rains-retreat offerings during the rains retreat. Offenders commit duṣkṛta!"

At that time, King Prasenajit had bandits on his border, and he dispatched Iṣidatta and Pūrṇa to go and suppress them. The two discussed: "We are going now; perhaps we will lose our lives. Let us contribute goods to make offerings to the bhikṣus." They brought their goods to the bhikṣus and said: "We are going to suppress bandits and fear we may not return. We offer these goods; please accept them." The bhikṣus thought: "The World-Honored One does not permit us to receive rains-retreat offerings during the rains retreat." Not knowing what to do, they reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Ānanda: "How many days until the pravāraṇā?" He answered: "Ten days." The Buddha praised fewness of desires and contentment in various ways, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit bhikṣus, whether in the early or later rains retreat, to receive urgent offering robes within ten days before the pravāraṇā."

Having permitted the acceptance of urgent offering robes, the bhikṣus then constantly kept them, neither formally designating them, nor giving them to others, nor determining them as surplus, carrying heavy loads of robes and traveling about. When the senior bhikṣus saw them, they asked: "Have you not heard the Buddha's rule against keeping extra robes?" They answered: "Although the Buddha has such a rule, he also permits accepting urgent offering robes." The bhikṣus further asked: "Do you constantly keep urgent offering robes without formally designating them, without giving them to others, and without determining them as surplus?" They answered: "That is so!" The senior bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I do not permit constantly keeping urgent offering robes without formally designating them, without giving them to others, and without determining them as surplus. I permit keeping them until the robe season."

Having permitted keeping them until the robe season, the bhikṣus still kept them beyond the robe season. The senior bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, whether in the early or later rains retreat, within ten days before the pravāraṇā, receives an urgent offering robe, and if it is needed, he should accept it and keep it until the robe season. If he exceeds this, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Urgent offering robe" means: when someone is about to go on a military campaign, or a woman is about to give birth, and at such urgent times they make an offering; if the time passes, they would no longer make the offering.

"Robe season" means: the time when the kaṭhina robe has been received. After the kaṭhina robe is relinquished, it is called the non-robe season.

For bhikṣuṇīs it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda begged a bowl from a merchant at an improper time, saying: "I need a bowl now; please give me one." The merchant answered: "Venerable one, please wait a moment. There is a meeting of merchants now; if I miss it, I will be fined five hundred gold coins." Upananda said: "I have heard that you are diligent in supporting the practice of the Way. How can you now abandon meritorious deeds and put worldly affairs first?" The merchant, hearing this, thought: "Even if I am fined, I must first make this offering." He then bought a bowl and gave it to him before going, but he arrived too late. Everyone who saw him said: "He should be fined!" The merchant said: "It was not for personal reasons that I violated the assembly's rule. A śramaṇa asked me for a bowl, and I could not refuse him, so I was late." Those who had no faith in or fondness for the Buddha-Dharma said: "For the sake of one śramaṇa, he publicly violated the assembly's rule. He should be severely fined!" So they fined him.

After being fined, the merchant said angrily: "The śramaṇas of the Śākya clan have no sense of propriety. He would not wait even a moment, causing me to be fined." People criticized them in various ways: "These śramaṇas always talk about knowing the right time and having few desires and contentment, yet at an improper time they forcibly beg from people. They have no śramaṇa conduct; they destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" When the senior bhikṣus heard this, they rebuked him in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Upananda: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked him in various ways, he asked the bhikṣus: "What do you think: is a bowl without repairs still a bowl?" They answered: "It is." He further asked: "With one repair, up to five repairs, is it still a bowl?" They answered: "It is." He told the bhikṣus: "With no repairs or one repair, up to four repairs, it is a bowl. With five repairs, it is not a bowl. For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, whose bowl does not yet have five repairs, begs for a new bowl for the sake of a better one, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

This bowl should be relinquished in the midst of the Sangha. The Sangha should take the worst bowl among the assembly and give it to him, saying: "You shall take this bowl and use it until it breaks. This is the proper procedure."

Bowls are of three kinds: iron bowls, soapstone bowls, and earthenware bowls. They are further of three kinds: large, medium, and small. A large one holds three pāthas of rice, excluding soup and vegetables. A small one holds one pātha of rice, excluding soup and vegetables. A medium one is between the large and small.

"For the sake of a better one" means: seeking a sturdier or superior one.

If one already has a bowl with no repairs, up to four repairs, and begs for another bowl with no repairs, up to four repairs, and obtains it, in all cases it is niḥsargika-pāyattika.

If one already has a bowl with no repairs, up to four repairs, and begs for a bowl with five repairs and obtains it, in all cases it is duṣkṛta.

"Should be relinquished in the midst of the Sangha" means: the new bowl that was obtained should be relinquished to the entire Sangha; one may not relinquish it to one, two, or three persons.

The procedure for relinquishment: one should go to the midst of the Sangha and say: "Venerable Sangha, please listen! I, bhikṣu so-and-so, had a bowl not yet having five repairs, and begged for a new bowl, committing a niḥsargika-pāyattika offense. I now relinquish it to the Sangha. This is the announcement."

The Sangha should perform a motion with one approval-act to appoint a bhikṣu who knows the procedure to distribute it in the Sangha. One bhikṣu should announce: "Venerable Sangha, please listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so had a bowl not yet having five repairs and begged for a new bowl, which he now relinquishes to the Sangha. The Sangha now appoints bhikṣu so-and-so as the bowl-distributor. If the time is right, may the Sangha approve. This is the announcement."

"Venerable Sangha, please listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so had a bowl not yet having five repairs and begged for a new bowl, which he now relinquishes to the Sangha. The Sangha now appoints bhikṣu so-and-so as the bowl-distributor. Let those elder bhikṣus who approve remain silent; let those who do not approve speak. The Sangha has appointed bhikṣu so-and-so as the bowl-distributor; the Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. Let this matter be thus upheld."

That bhikṣu should then make an announcement directing all bhikṣus to each bring out their bowls. He should then take the relinquished bowl to the most senior monk and ask: "Do you need this bowl?" If he says he does, the distributor should examine the senior monk's existing bowl; if he has no bowl, or if it is too large, too small, cracked, chipped, or crooked, he should not make the exchange. If it has none of these five defects, he should exchange it. After the exchange, he should take the senior monk's bowl and proceed to the second in seniority, and so on down to the most newly ordained bhikṣu, following the same procedure. The Sangha should take the very last bowl and give it to the bhikṣu who relinquished his bowl. If during the bowl distribution no one takes it, he should be allowed to have it returned to him.

The Sangha should instruct him: "This is your bowl. Take good care of it. Do not place it on the ground. Do not use it for removing refuse. Do not use it to store leftover overnight food. Do not use it to warm water. Do not use it to store incense. Do not use it to store medicine. Take care of it in this way. If it breaks, you are permitted to beg for a new one."

For bhikṣuṇīs it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda obtained many bowls. He used them for five or six days and then set them aside, so that old bowls were everywhere. When householders saw them, they asked: "Who accumulated all these?" Someone said: "It is Upananda." The householders said: "The śramaṇas of the Śākya clan always talk about few desires and contentment, yet now they are insatiable, collecting and accumulating like bowl merchants. They have no śramaṇa conduct; they destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" When the senior bhikṣus heard this, they rebuked him in various ways, brought him before the Buddha, and reported this. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Upananda: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu keeps an extra bowl for even one night, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu obtained two bowls on his own. He thought: "The Buddha does not permit me to keep an extra bowl for even one night." He immediately gave one bowl to another bhikṣu. After giving it away, his remaining bowl broke, and he had no bowl to use while traveling. The bhikṣus asked him: "You previously had two bowls; why do you now have none?" He answered with the above account. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked that bhikṣu: "How many days after you gave away the bowl did your other bowl break?" He answered: "Ten days." The Buddha praised fewness of desires and contentment, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu keeps an extra bowl for up to ten days, and if he exceeds this, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

If one obtains two bowls, one should ask one's preceptor or teacher: "Of these two bowls, which is better?" If the preceptor or teacher cannot discern well, one should use each for five days to determine for oneself which is better, formally designate the better one, and give the inferior one to another.

For śrāmaṇeras, it is duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in the city of Aṭavī. At that time, bhikṣus made kauśeya silk bedding for themselves, making it themselves and also having others make it; carrying cocoons themselves and also having others carry them; boiling cocoons themselves and also having others boil them. When householders saw this, they said: "We boil cocoons, and the bhikṣus do the same. How are the śramaṇas of the Śākya clan any different from us? They always talk about compassion and forbearance toward living beings, yet they personally boil cocoons. They have no śramaṇa conduct; they destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!"

There was one bhikṣu who, using already-processed wild silkworm cotton, asked other bhikṣus to help make bedding. The cotton was not enough, so he went to a cotton seller and said: "My bedding cotton is too little; please give me a little." That person answered: "I do not have processed cotton yet." The bhikṣu then said: "Can you make some for me?" That person then boiled the cocoons in front of the bhikṣu, and the pupae wriggled and made sounds. The bhikṣu instructed: "Press them down into the hot water!" That person then scolded and cursed him: "You always preach the Dharma of not killing living beings, yet now you teach people to kill. You have no śramaṇa conduct; you destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" When the senior bhikṣus heard this, they rebuked them in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu makes bedding from new kauśeya silk, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Kauśeya" means: cotton produced by silkworms.

"Bedding" means: a mattress or anything that, from the time it is first completed, can withstand three shakings without falling apart; this is called bedding.

It should be relinquished to the Sangha and may not be relinquished to others. The Sangha should spread it on the ground, or on rope-beds and wooden beds. Except for the bhikṣu who relinquished the mattress, all members of the Sangha may sit or lie on it in order of seniority.

The intention to make it and the preparatory acts are all duṣkṛta. When it is completed, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika. Even if one does not make it oneself or have others make it, if another person gives it and one accepts it, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika.

For śrāmaṇeras, it is duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in the city of Kauśāmbī. At that time, many Vṛji people used pure black wool felt, which was glossy and attractive, all for clothing and bedding. The Vṛji bhikṣus also imitated them in making such things. When householders entered their rooms and saw it, they were greatly alarmed, thinking that Vṛji noblemen had gathered there. They asked passersby: "What kind of distinguished people's clothing is this?" They answered: "These are not noblemen's possessions; they belong to the Vṛji bhikṣus!" The householders then criticized, saying: "These bhikṣus are like kings, like great ministers, like nobles when riding in carriages and on horses, wearing such attire. I have heard that bhikṣus wear cut-piece robes and seek the unconditioned path. Yet now they are like this. They have no śramaṇa conduct; they destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" When the senior bhikṣus heard this, they rebuked them in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked those bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu makes new bedding from pure black ewe's wool, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Pure black" means: naturally black or dyed black.

It should be relinquished to the Sangha. The Sangha should spread it on rope-beds and wooden beds, and it should not be spread on the ground. The rest is as stated in the section on kauśeya silk bedding.

---

The Buddha was in the city of Kauśāmbī. At that time, the Vṛji bhikṣus made black ewe's wool bedding with a little white wool and inferior wool mixed in, and then claimed: "It is now compliant."

At that time, the senior bhikṣus saw this and asked: "Have you not heard the Buddha's rule against making bedding from pure black ewe's wool?" They answered: "We have heard it! But we have already mixed in white and inferior wool, so it is no longer pure black." The bhikṣus said: "Pure black with a little mixture, what difference does it make?" They rebuked them in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked those bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit bhikṣus, when making bedding, to use two parts pure black ewe's wool, a third part white, and a fourth part inferior. For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu makes new bedding, he should use two parts pure black ewe's wool, a third part white, and a fourth part inferior. If he exceeds this proportion, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

If a bhikṣu makes bedding of forty palas, he should use twenty palas of pure black, ten palas of white, and ten palas of inferior. If the black exceeds by even one pala, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika. The rest is as stated in the section on pure black wool bedding.

---

The Buddha was in the city of Kauśāmbī. At that time, the Vṛji bhikṣus thought: "The Buddha permits us to use two parts pure black ewe's wool, a third part white, and a fourth part inferior to make bedding." They then begged for large quantities of the three colors of wool, making the bedding themselves and having others make it. In every place they stayed, it was everywhere. When householders came to visit and saw it, they asked: "Whose things are these?" The answer was: "The Vṛji bhikṣus'." The householders criticized, as stated in the section on extra bowls. When the senior bhikṣus heard this, they rebuked them in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked those bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu makes new bedding, he should keep it for six years. If before six years have elapsed, whether he has relinquished it or not, he makes another new bedding, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu kept rag-heap bedding, which he found useful. He wished to travel from Śrāvastī to Sāketa, but the bedding was too heavy to carry. He did not know what to do and reported this to the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus brought him before the Buddha and reported this. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "This bhikṣu wishes to go to Sāketa, but his bedding is heavy, and he finds it useful and cannot relinquish it, yet cannot carry it. The Sangha should perform a motion with one approval-act to exchange it for a lighter one."

That bhikṣu should request from the Sangha, saying: "I, bhikṣu so-and-so, have my own bedding, which I find useful. Now I wish to travel to such-and-such a place, but it is too heavy to carry. I request the Sangha to exchange it for a lighter one belonging to the Sangha." He should make this request a second and third time.

In the midst of the Sangha, one bhikṣu should announce: "Venerable Sangha, please listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so has his own bedding, which he finds useful. Now he wishes to travel to such-and-such a place, but it is too heavy to carry. He requests the Sangha to exchange it for a lighter one. The Sangha will now make the exchange. If the time is right, may the Sangha approve. This is the announcement."

"Venerable Sangha, please listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so has his own bedding, which he finds useful. He wishes to travel to such-and-such a place, but it is too heavy to carry. He requests the Sangha to exchange it for a lighter one. The Sangha will now make the exchange. Let those elder bhikṣus who approve remain silent; let those who do not approve speak. The Sangha has exchanged a lighter Sangha bedding for bhikṣu so-and-so; the Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. Let this matter be thus upheld." From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu makes new bedding, he should keep it for six years. If before six years have elapsed, whether he has relinquished it or not, he makes another new bedding, unless by Sangha act, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"Six years" means: counting the days to complete six years. The rest is as stated in the section on pure black wool bedding.

---

The Buddha was in the city of Kauśāmbī. At that time, the Vṛji laypeople made sitting cloths from pure black ewe's wool. The Vṛji bhikṣus also begged for materials and made them. The householders became annoyed by the begging. Later, when they came to the monastery to visit, they saw that the bhikṣus had accumulated many pure black ewe's wool sitting mats. They criticized, as stated in the section on pure black bedding. When the senior bhikṣus heard this, they reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked those bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu makes a new sitting cloth from pure black ewe's wool, he should use a piece of his old sitting cloth, one sugata hand-span, to spoil the good appearance. If he does not do so, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"One sugata hand-span" means: a square of two feet.

"To spoil the good appearance" means: to cover the new one with a piece of old one, as one wishes. The rest is as stated in the section on pure black wool bedding.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, bhikṣus carried loads of sheep's wool as they traveled along the road. People on the road who saw them all criticized: "We have family burdens and carry sheep's wool; the bhikṣus do the same. They wear damaged-color cut-piece robes in vain, shave their heads, and beg for food; how are they different from us? They have no śramaṇa conduct; they destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!"

There was one bhikṣu who lived in the mountains and was accustomed to carrying sheep's wool. Exhausted from the road, when he reached the monastery courtyard, he collapsed on the ground. When the bhikṣus saw him, they thought he was possessed by a ghost and sprinkled urine on him. He said: "Venerable ones! Why are you sprinkling me?" They answered: "We were afraid you were possessed by a ghost; that is why we sprinkled you." He said: "I am not possessed by a ghost. The load of wool was heavy, and the road was tiring. I was overcome by heat and exhaustion." The bhikṣus said: "The Buddha has established that bhikṣus should keep three robes and a bowl, like birds that fly together with their feathers. Have you not heard this, yet still carry such loads?" They rebuked him in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked that bhikṣu: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha had rebuked him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu carries sheep's wool while traveling on the road, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

At that time, there was a householder who built a room for the Sangha. He thought: "If bhikṣus come to stay in this room, I will make offerings and also provide robes." A bhikṣu came to stay in his room, and the householder offered him sheep's wool. The bhikṣu did not accept it. The householder said: "I gathered this sheep's wool originally for the bhikṣus, not for myself." The bhikṣu answered: "The Buddha does not permit me to carry sheep's wool myself; how can I accept it?"

There was also a bhikṣu who needed sheep's wool to make bedding but did not know how to make it himself and wanted to ask another bhikṣu, but did not dare accept it. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked that bhikṣu: "How far away is the person you wish to ask?" He answered: "Three yojanas from here." Thereupon the World-Honored One praised fewness of desires and contentment, praised the precepts, praised upholding the precepts, and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu obtains sheep's wool and needs to carry it somewhere, he may carry it himself for up to three yojanas. If he exceeds this, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

If a bhikṣu obtains sheep's wool and needs to carry it somewhere, he should have a lay attendant carry it. Only if there is no lay attendant may he carry it himself, but he may not carry it on a shoulder pole, on his head, or on his back. An offender commits duṣkṛta.

For śrāmaṇeras, it is duṣkṛta.

No offense is committed in the following cases: within three yojanas;

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from Sanskrit into Chinese during the Liu Song dynasty by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Six

### First Division, Part Five: Pāyattika (Pācittiya), First Section

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time there was a Dharma-teacher bhikṣu named Śāraṇa, who was intelligent and eloquent in debate. None among the four assemblies, non-Buddhist practitioners, śramaṇas, or brāhmaṇas could match him. He would assert what was wrong to be right and what was right to be wrong, claim to know what he did not know and claim not to know what he did know, always using clever argumentation to defeat others in speech. The bhikṣus who saw this all sighed in admiration and asked him: "When you debate with others and assert what is wrong to be right, do you actually think it is right, or do you know it is wrong?" He answered: "I truly know it is wrong, but I am ashamed of being defeated, so I speak falsely!" The elder bhikṣus reproached him in various ways: "The Buddha constantly praises not speaking falsely and also teaches others not to speak falsely. How can you now speak falsely for the sake of winning and losing?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Śāraṇa: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu speaks falsely, it is a pācittiya.'

At that time, bhikṣus would see a bhikṣuṇī and call her a bhikṣu; see a bhikṣu and call him a bhikṣuṇī; or see a man and say it was a woman; or see a woman and say it was a man; or see a non-Buddhist practitioner and say he was a son of the Śākyas; or see a son of the Śākyas and say he was a non-Buddhist practitioner. In these various cases of seeing one thing and saying another, they felt ashamed: "Have we perhaps committed a pācittiya?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu speaks according to his mental perception, that he commits a pācittiya is impossible. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu intentionally speaks falsely, it is a pācittiya.'

"Intentionally speaking falsely" is as explained in the section on claiming to have attained superhuman dharmas.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, bhikṣus together with their upādhyāyas and ācāryas, and those sharing the same upādhyāyas and ācāryas, were diligently studying together, not sleeping during the first or last watch of the night.

The group-of-six bhikṣus thought: "Now these bhikṣus are teaching each other day and night without ceasing. Before long they will surpass us, will see our faults, and will seek out our transgressions. We should disparage them together and vex them so they abandon their studies." They went and said to them: "You are of low and base birth, artisan commoners! You have committed many great evils and have no benevolent or good conduct!" The bhikṣus, hearing this, became distressed and abandoned their studies. The group-of-six bhikṣus said to others: "We have already ruined their recitation, meditation, and practice of the path." The elder bhikṣus reproached them in various ways: "How can you disparage the bhikṣus and cause them to abandon their studies?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "In the past, there was a city called Takṣaśilā. At that time, a brāhmaṇa in that city had a single bull that was swift and powerful. There was also a householder who likewise had a bull, no different from the other. The two men competed to test the strength of the two bulls, wagering fifty gold coins that the loser would pay. The brāhmaṇa's bull won. The householder, ashamed at losing the gold, obtained another bull, twice as strong as the former, and proposed doubling the wager. The brāhmaṇa then said to his bull: 'That householder has obtained another bull of extraordinary strength and wants to double the wager. Can you do it?' The bull answered: 'I can!' They then gathered in one place to test the two bulls' strength.

"The brāhmaṇa, fearing his bull would lose, disparaged and goaded it: 'Crooked-horns! Pull harder, thin-neck, put your back into it! Why are your steps so crooked?' The bull, hearing these words, greatly lost its strength and was defeated by the other bull. After the brāhmaṇa had lost double, he asked the bull: 'You said you could do it. Why did you lose?' The bull answered: 'I truly was capable, but hearing the disparaging words, my strength was completely drained. You may arrange another wager, doubled again, requiring the pulling of a hundred carts up a steep slope. When the contest comes, entice me with kind words, saying: "Curved-horns! Your steps are steady and proper, your form is beautiful and fine; leisurely pull the hundred carts up the steep slope."' So they wagered again, and the bull indeed won."

The Buddha, on account of this matter, then spoke a verse:

> "One should speak pleasing words, and not speak unpleasant ones;
> Even animals hearing them are delighted, pulling heavy loads up steep slopes,
> And thus have no rival, gaining double with joy.
> How much more for humankind, where praise and blame bring neither gain nor loss?

"Bhikṣus! Even animals hearing disparaging words lose their strength, how much more so for humans! I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu disparages a bhikṣu, it is a pācittiya.'

"Disparaging" means saying one is of low birth, an artisan class.

And so forth; even if one speaks the truth but with the intention to disparage, if the other person hears and understands, each statement is a pācittiya. If the other does not hear or does not understand, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one says: "You are of low birth," and the other denies it, yet one still insists it is so, each statement is a pācittiya.

If a bhikṣu disparages a bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, or śrāmaṇerī, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a bhikṣuṇī disparages a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī, it is a pācittiya; if she disparages a śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, or śrāmaṇerī, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, or śrāmaṇerī disparages any of the five assemblies, it is a duṣkṛta.

Those who are not at fault: speaking for the purpose of benefiting, speaking in admonishment, or speaking with mutual consent.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, bhikṣus were diligently studying, as described in the section on disparagement. The group-of-six bhikṣus again feared being surpassed, so they sowed discord among them. Going to one bhikṣu, they would say: "You are on close terms with me, yet that one speaks of you as being of low and base birth, an artisan commoner, who has committed great evils and has no benevolent or good conduct. When I heard his words, it was as if he were speaking about me." Going to the other bhikṣu, they would say the same. Both sides, hearing this, became mentally scattered, abandoned their studies, grew resentful toward each other, and would no longer speak to one another.

One bhikṣu asked the other bhikṣus: "Why will you not speak with me?" They answered: "Someone said that you were speaking ill of us!" He said: "Who said so?" They answered: "The group-of-six bhikṣus." He said: "The group-of-six bhikṣus also said that you were speaking ill of me." The elder bhikṣus, hearing this, reproached the group-of-six bhikṣus in various ways: "How can you engage in double-tongued discord!" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways: "You foolish people! How can those dwelling in the same Dharma engage in double-tongued discord?" He told the bhikṣus: "In the past, there was a lion named Good-Tusk, and a tiger named Good-Claw, who were close friends. There was a wild fox that always followed them seeking food, but the lion and tiger would not speak with it. Later, the fox thought: 'These two beasts are very loving and devoted to each other. I should sow discord between them so that each will seek food separately; what they leave behind will surely be plentiful, and I shall obtain it.' The fox went to the tiger and spoke a verse:

> "'Good-Claw, you are brave and mighty, of excellent birth, color, and strength;
> Good-Tusk speaks ill of you, and hearing it, my heart is displeased.'

"Then it went to the lion and also spoke a verse:

> "'Good-Tusk, you are brave and mighty, of excellent birth, color, and strength;
> Good-Claw speaks ill of you, and hearing it, my heart is displeased.'

"The two beasts, hearing the verses, were each displeased with the other. Good-Tusk, being wise, then thought: 'Good-Claw is not speaking with me; this must be caused by the fox's discord-sowing.' Later, he obtained a calf and offered it to the tiger, but the tiger refused to eat. Thereupon Good-Tusk asked in verse:

> "'I have ceased my own meal to offer it to you; why do you not eat?
> Between close friends there should be no fault, yet instead we are displeased with each other.
> Could it be that you believed the fox's words, which have come between us?
> If you persist in harboring resentment, it will eventually become a lasting grudge.
> Examining this, there is no other cause; it must be the fox's slander.
> This base creature has divided our friendship; now we should kill and be rid of it!'"

He told the bhikṣus: "Even animals consider discord-sowing to be wrong; how much more so for humans! I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu engages in double-tongued discord toward a bhikṣu, it is a pācittiya.'

The remainder is as stated in the section on disparagement.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda constantly frequented the home of a certain householder, teaching him the Dharma, settling official disputes, and relieving various ailments. Later, the household declined until there remained only a mother-in-law and daughter-in-law. Upananda, out of familiar affection, would teach the Dharma to the mother-in-law but would stop when the daughter-in-law came; and when teaching the Dharma to the daughter-in-law, he would likewise stop when the mother-in-law came. Each developed suspicions, thinking he surely intended to commit impure acts. They then told others, and this became known far and near. Those who lacked faith in the Buddha-Dharma criticized and reproached, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, practice what is unlawful, exceeding even the licentious people of the world! They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" The elder bhikṣus, hearing this, reproached him in various ways, and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Upananda: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu teaches Dharma to a woman, it is a pācittiya.'

At that time, there was a bhikṣu of great spiritual power who, at mealtime, put on his robe, took his bowl, and entered the city to beg for food. He came in turn to a certain house. The woman of the house came out, spread a seat for him, and offered fine food and drink. After he had eaten, she placed a small bench before him, sat down, and said: "Venerable one! Please teach me the Dharma." The bhikṣu observed her and knew that within a short time, a fatal wind would arise in this woman, she would die and fall into hell; but if he taught her the Dharma, she could, right there on her seat, become free from dust and defilement. Although he perceived this clearly, he thought: "The Buddha has decreed that one may not teach Dharma to a woman. Even at the cost of my life, I should not violate this." So he answered: "Sister, be at ease; I am not permitted to teach." Having spoken, he departed. Not long after he left, the woman indeed had a wind-attack and died. The bhikṣu, pitying her, returned to the monastery and told others about it. The bhikṣus brought him to the Buddha and reported this. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked that bhikṣu: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One!" The Buddha also asked the bhikṣu: "If you had taught her the Dharma, how many statements would it have taken for her to understand?" He answered: "Five or six statements." Thereupon the Buddha praised fewness of desires and contentment, praised the precepts and praised upholding the precepts, and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit bhikṣus to teach Dharma to a woman up to five or six statements. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu teaches Dharma to a woman beyond five or six statements, it is a pācittiya.'

At that time, bhikṣus entered others' homes, and women requested the teaching of Dharma. After teaching five or six statements, they remained silent. The women said: "We do not yet understand; please explain further." The bhikṣus said: "Sisters! The Buddha does not permit us to teach Dharma to women beyond five or six statements." The women said: "You may teach the other bhikṣus, and we will thereby gain understanding." The bhikṣus said: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to teach Dharma to women by means of teaching a bhikṣu." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit teaching Dharma to women by means of a bhikṣu being present."

At that time, a bhikṣu entered the home of a minister without a bhikṣu companion. The women requested the teaching of Dharma. He answered: "The Buddha does not permit me to teach Dharma to women beyond five or six statements." The women then called a small boy to stand before them and said: "Venerable one! You may teach this boy the Dharma, and we will thereby gain understanding." He answered: "The Buddha has not yet permitted me to teach Dharma to women by means of a small boy." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit teaching Dharma to women when a knowledgeable man is present."

At that time, bhikṣus thought: "Although the Buddha has permitted us to teach Dharma to women when a knowledgeable man is present, such knowledgeable men, though knowing, are hard to encounter." So they again did not teach. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit teaching Dharma to women when a man who can separately discern good and evil speech is present. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu teaches Dharma to a woman beyond five or six statements, unless a man who can separately discern good and evil speech is present, it is a pācittiya.'

At that time, there was an upāsaka who had taken a wife from a family that did not observe the Dharma. He said to the bhikṣus: "Venerable ones! Please teach my wife the Dharma so that she may have faith and joy in the Three Treasures; administer the Three Refuges, the Five Precepts, and the Eight-Part Precepts to her; and teach her the ten wholesome and ten unwholesome paths of action." The bhikṣus all declined to teach. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit administering the Three Refuges, the Five Precepts, and the Eight-Part Precepts to women, and teaching them the ten wholesome and ten unwholesome paths of action."

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

"Five statements" means: form is impermanent, feeling, perception, mental formations, and consciousness are impermanent.

"Six statements" means: the eye is without self, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are without self.

If a bhikṣu finishes teaching five or six statements to a woman and says: "Sister! The Dharma extends just to this point," and rises from his seat and leaves, then if there is another reason and he returns and sits again to teach, he does not commit an offense.

If, after teaching five or six statements, another woman arrives, he may teach the latter woman. In this way, by succession, he may teach limitless women without offense. If he is reciting sūtras and a woman comes to listen; or if a woman asks about the meaning, and he must ensure she understands, even beyond five or six statements, none of these are offenses.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus had power, while the other virtuous bhikṣus lacked power. The group-of-six bhikṣus constantly obstructed the five kinds of karman: the karman of reproach, the karman of banishment, the karman of requiring dependence, the karman of accusation, and the karman of requiring submission. If a bhikṣu had been subjected to the five kinds of karman and the Sangha wished to lift them, they would also obstruct it and not permit it.

Later, the group-of-six bhikṣus lost their power, and the virtuous bhikṣus gained power. The Sangha had karman matters to attend to. The group-of-six bhikṣus' robe-making season had arrived. The bhikṣus said: "We should now summon the group-of-six bhikṣus to conduct the Sangha's business together. If they are unwilling to set aside their robe-making and come, they should delegate their consent, and we can then lawfully conduct the business." They assembled the Sangha and sent someone to tell the group-of-six bhikṣus: "You should come; the Sangha is now assembling." The group-of-six bhikṣus said: "We have things to do; we now delegate our consent." They then delegated one bhikṣu to come to the great assembly. For those to whom the Sangha should apply the five kinds of karman, they applied the five kinds of karman; for those from whom the Sangha should lift the five kinds of karman, they lifted the five kinds of karman. After the karman proceedings, the delegated bhikṣu returned to the group-of-six bhikṣus. The group-of-six asked: "What did the Sangha do?" He answered: "All the karman we wished for, the Sangha did not perform; what we did not wish for, the Sangha instead performed." The group-of-six bhikṣus then went to the bhikṣus who had been subjected to karman and said: "Do not worry; I will exert my power for you. I did not know earlier that they were performing karman against you, so I delegated my consent! Had I known, I would have abandoned my business and gone. How could they have completed this karman?" They also went to the bhikṣus whose karman had been lifted and said: "I do not accept the lifting of your karman, and I do not accept your confession!" The elder bhikṣus, hearing this, reproached the group-of-six bhikṣus: "How can you reopen matters after the Sangha has lawfully concluded them?" They brought them to the Buddha and reported this.
