The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu reopens a matter after the Sangha has concluded it, it is a pācittiya.'

At that time, bhikṣus did not know that the Sangha had concluded a matter and reopened it. Later, upon learning, they felt ashamed; some confessed and repented. The elder bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If one does not know that the Sangha has concluded a matter and reopens it, that this constitutes a pācittiya is impossible. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu, knowing the Sangha has concluded a matter, reopens it, it is a pācittiya.'

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus whose matters had been unlawfully concluded, who thought: "It would be good if the Buddha permitted us to reopen matters that were unlawfully concluded." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit reopening matters that the Sangha has unlawfully concluded. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu, knowing the Sangha has lawfully concluded a matter, reopens it, it is a pācittiya.'

If the Sangha has lawfully concluded a matter and one reopens it, it is a pācittiya. If the Sangha concluded a matter without a karman procedure and one reopens it, it is a duṣkṛta. If one reopens a private matter, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī and went with five hundred bhikṣus to the village of Aṭavī. At that time, the householders there thought: "The Buddha has been long in coming here and will soon depart again. We should become close to the bhikṣus, learn to recite sūtra verses, and ask about what we do not understand. After the World-Honored One departs, we will have something to rely upon." They went to the bhikṣus and said: "Venerable ones! Please teach us to recite and read sūtra verses." The bhikṣus said: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to teach laypeople to recite sūtras." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit teaching laypeople to recite sūtras."

At that time, bhikṣus who had gone forth from various countries recited sūtra verses with incorrect pronunciation and phrasing. The householders criticized and reproached them, saying: "How can bhikṣus who attend upon the Buddha day and night not know the grammar of masculine, feminine, neuter, hermaphrodite speech, and singular and plural forms?" The bhikṣus, hearing this, were each ashamed. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" The Buddha then remotely reproached the householders: "You foolish people! How can you criticize those from other countries for incorrect pronunciation and phrasing when reciting sūtras?" He told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu teaches an unordained person to recite sūtras, it is a pācittiya.'

Afterward, there were householders who again sought to receive instruction in reciting sūtras. The bhikṣus said: "People like you criticized our pronunciation; do not seek to receive instruction from us! Why do you come now, only to trouble yourselves in vain!" They answered: "Venerable ones! We do not disparage the Buddha-Dharma, nor do we seek another field of merit. How can you refuse to teach us because those other people were at fault?"

There were also śrāmaṇeras who wished to receive instruction in sūtras. The bhikṣus said: "Wait until you receive full ordination, and then we shall instruct you." The śrāmaṇeras said: "We who have gone forth should recite sūtra verses; how can you say we must wait for full ordination before being instructed?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit teaching unordained persons to recite sūtras, but they may not recite in unison. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu teaches an unordained person sūtras by reciting in unison, it is a pācittiya.'

"Reciting in unison" means: reciting simultaneously; or if the teacher's voice has not yet ceased and the student is already reciting; or if the student has not finished reciting and the teacher again prompts. Sentence by sentence, each instance is a pācittiya.

"One should first instruct, saying: 'Wait until I finish speaking, then recite.'"

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in the village of Aṭavī. The householders there, because the Buddha was about to depart, all came to the bhikṣus and together with the bhikṣus sat in meditation in the same room, or practiced walking meditation together, not sleeping during the first or last watch of the night.

At that time, every five days the bhikṣus would teach the Dharma throughout the entire night, and then, exhausted, they would lie down. One bhikṣu did not keep his mind focused and fell into deep sleep. He kicked off his robe, exposing his body, and his member became erect and visible. A householder saw this and covered him again with the robe. This happened up to three times. The householder then angrily reproached: "These people constantly hear various teachings against desire, yet now they lie exposed with erections. If you do not delight in the path, why not return to lay life?" That bhikṣu, hearing this, was deeply ashamed. The elder bhikṣus, hearing of it, were also greatly embarrassed, and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked that bhikṣu: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached that bhikṣu in various ways: "I constantly praise sleeping with a focused mind, which is free from five evils. Why did you not keep your mind focused? If a bhikṣu, whether in walking meditation, sitting meditation, sitting, standing, or lying down, acts without proper deportment, people who see this are not pleased, do not develop faith, and those who already had faith lose it. This is not serving as a great light for the world." He told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu lodges together with an unordained person, it is a pācittiya."

Later, the householders again wished to come to the bhikṣus to sit in meditation and practice the path. The bhikṣus drove them out and would not permit it. The householders said: "Venerable ones! Do not drive us away. We do not seek another field of merit; we take refuge only in the venerable ones. How can you reject us all because of one person's fault?" Among the householders, those with power forced their way into the rooms. The bhikṣus, unable to stop them, went out to sleep in the open, troubled by mosquitoes, gadflies, wind, rain, and dust.

At that time, the Buddha traveled from the village of Aṭavī to the Ghoṣira Garden in the country of Kauśāmbī. Rāhula went separately to the Vāgīra monastery, swept and cleaned one room, laid out the bedding, fetched water, and then closed the door and went to the Buddha's quarters. After he left, the bhikṣu who distributed lodging assigned the room to someone else, and that bhikṣu entered and settled in the room. After Rāhula listened to the Dharma during the first watch, he returned to the room he had been assigned. The bhikṣu inside heard him and asked: "Who is it?" He answered: "It is Rāhula!" The bhikṣu said: "Why have you come?" He answered: "This is my room." The bhikṣu said: "The lodging distributor has given this room to me." Rāhula said: "I first swept and cleaned it and laid out the bedding, then went briefly to the Buddha's quarters to listen to and receive the Dharma teaching. How can you now refuse to return it to me?" The bhikṣu said: "Although you prepared it, I am the senior monk and should reside here." Rāhula said: "May I share the room with you?" The bhikṣu said: "No!" He asked to enter to sit or stand, or to stay under the eaves, but all were refused. Thereupon Rāhula thought: "If I go to another room, it will be the same. Only in the privy can I find peace!" So he went to the privy. At that time, there was a black snake in the privy. The Buddha, with his divine eye, saw this and thought: "If I do not go there, Rāhula will shortly be killed by the snake." He went to the privy entrance, snapped his fingers, and cleared his throat. Rāhula also made a sound in response. The Buddha asked: "Who are you?" He answered: "It is Rāhula!" The Buddha asked: "Why are you here?" He answered with the full account of what had happened. Thereupon the World-Honored One took Rāhula back to his own quarters. When the night had passed, he assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked that bhikṣu: "Did you really refuse to accommodate Rāhula?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached him in various ways: "You foolish person! How can a fox drive out a lion?" At that time, the bhikṣus also reported to the Buddha the matter of the householders forcing their way into rooms and their having to sleep in the open. The Buddha praised fewness of desires and contentment in various ways, praised the precepts and praised upholding the precepts, and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to lodge together with an unordained person for two nights. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu lodges with an unordained person beyond two nights, it is a pācittiya."

"Lodging together" means: sharing one room for the night.

If there is a roof overhead and four walls; or a roof overhead with one wall missing, or two walls missing, all are pācittiya. With three walls missing, there is no offense.

If there are four walls and the roof has been covered halfway, less than half, or more than half, all are pācittiya. If only a little is covered, there is no offense.

In any of these places, if one exceeds two nights, at the time of the last watch, pressing one's side against the bed or turning over, each instance is a pācittiya.

No offense in the following cases: sharing a roof but with a partition between them; if one is ill and cannot get up; if there are various dangers; if one sits throughout the night without lying down; if the other person lies down and the bhikṣu sits; or if the other sits and the bhikṣu lies down.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise.

---

The Buddha was in Vaiśālī. At that time, there was famine and alms were hard to obtain. He told the bhikṣus: "Each of you should settle for the rains retreat with acquaintances as you see fit." There were bhikṣus who settled for the retreat along the Vaggumudā River. Through various circumstances, as described in the section on claiming to have attained superhuman dharmas, until the Buddha asked: "Did you praise one another; was it true or false?" They answered: "Some was true and some was false." The Buddha said: "Those who spoke falsely have committed a pārājika." He reproached the bhikṣus who truly had attainments in various ways: "How can you declare to unordained persons that you have attained superhuman dharmas?" After the reproach, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu declares to an unordained person that he has attained superhuman dharmas, saying: 'I know thus, I see thus,' and it is true, it is a pācittiya."

"Superhuman dharmas" are as explained above. If one declares to an unordained person that one has attained superhuman dharmas, each statement is a pācittiya.

If one declares to an ordained person without being asked, each statement is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

No offense in the following cases: speaking at the time of attaining nirvāṇa; being asked by an ordained person and then speaking.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, bhikṣus committed saṅghāvaśeṣa offenses, such as intentional emission of semen, bodily contact with a woman, speaking lewd words to a woman, or praising the offering of one's own body to a woman. Some had requested probation (parivāsa) from the Sangha and been granted it; some were undergoing penance (mānāpya); some were undergoing the original period; some were being restored. The group-of-six bhikṣus would ridicule all of them before the Sangha, causing the bhikṣus to be doubly ashamed.

Furthermore, one bhikṣu had committed the offense of intentional emission of semen, and the Sangha had given him probation. At that time, that bhikṣu's patron invited the Sangha for a meal and had a fine seat specially prepared for that bhikṣu. The group-of-six bhikṣus went to the patron's home first and sat on the fine seat. The householder said: "Do not sit there! I have prepared that for the bhikṣu to sit in the place of honor." The group-of-six bhikṣus said: "That bhikṣu should not sit here!" The householder said: "Whether he should or not, I know for myself!" The group-of-six bhikṣus said: "He formerly should have sat here, but now he should be in the lowest seat." The householder asked further: "Why is this?" They answered: "That bhikṣu has committed an offense." The householder asked again: "What offense did he commit?" They answered: "He committed the offense of intentional emission of semen." The householder then criticized and reproached: "These śramaṇas constantly speak of removing lustful thoughts, extinguishing lustful passions, and severing lustful inclinations; yet now they do this, defiling the path. They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" The elder bhikṣus, hearing this, reproached the group-of-six bhikṣus in various ways: "How can you disclose to unordained persons the serious offenses of others?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu discloses the serious offenses of others to an unordained person, it is a pācittiya."

There were bhikṣus who, not knowing these were serious offenses, disclosed them to unordained persons. Upon later learning, they developed doubt: "Have I perhaps committed a pācittiya?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu does not know another's offense is a serious one and discloses it to an unordained person, that this constitutes a pācittiya is impossible. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, knowing another's serious offense, discloses it to an unordained person, it is a pācittiya."

At that time, the World-Honored One commanded the Sangha to perform a karman appointing Śāriputra to go to Devadatta's group and proclaim: "If anyone accepts Devadatta's five practices, such a person does not see the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha." The bhikṣus then wondered: "If one discloses another's serious offenses to an unordained person, is it necessarily required that there be a karman authorization, or not?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "Without a Sangha karman, one may not disclose another's serious offenses to unordained persons. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, knowing the serious offenses of another bhikṣu, discloses them to an unordained person, except by Sangha karman, it is a pācittiya."

"Serious offenses" means: pārājika or saṅghāvaśeṣa offenses.

The person appointed by the Sangha's karman should follow the Sangha's instructions. If instructed to disclose to person A but discloses to person B; or if instructed to disclose this offense but discloses that offense, each instance is a pācittiya.

If a bhikṣu discloses the serious offenses of a bhikṣuṇī, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a bhikṣuṇī discloses the serious offenses of a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī, it is a pācittiya.

If a śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, or śrāmaṇerī discloses the serious offenses of a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī, it is a duṣkṛta.

If an unordained person has already heard that a bhikṣu committed a serious offense and asks a bhikṣu about it, the bhikṣu may ask in return: "What did you hear?" The person says: "I heard such and such!" Then the bhikṣu says: "What I heard is the same!" This is no offense.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the World-Honored One praised the Vinaya in various ways, praised reciting the Vinaya, praised upholding the Vinaya, and praised Upāli, saying that a Vinaya-holding bhikṣu has five meritorious qualities: first, he firmly protects his own precept conduct; second, he can resolve the doubts of those who have a sense of shame; third, he himself abides in the true Dharma; fourth, he speaks without fear in the Sangha; fifth, he subdues enemies.

At that time, the bhikṣus thought: "The Buddha has spoken thus for our sake. How can we not diligently study and recite the Vinaya?" They then applied themselves to rigorous study and recitation, day and night without slacking.

The group-of-six bhikṣus thought: "Now these bhikṣus are diligently receiving, reciting, and studying the Vinaya day and night. They will surely become very wise, understand the characteristics of offenses, see our faults, and ultimately cause us harm. We should now jointly disparage the Vinaya and those who study the Vinaya, causing them to abandon their studies and cease recitation." They went to the bhikṣus and asked: "What are you studying?" They answered: "The Vinaya." The group-of-six bhikṣus said: "What use is it to study these petty precepts? Why not study the sūtras on the meaning of the five aggregates, the six sense-bases, and so forth? As for reciting the Vinaya, it amounts to no more than the four matters, the thirteen matters, and the two undetermined rules. What use is knowing more? Knowing more and seeing more only increases people's doubts." The bhikṣus said: "More knowledge, more doubts; we also think so!" And they ceased their study and recitation. The group-of-six bhikṣus said among themselves: "Those bhikṣus are no longer studying the Vinaya; we are at peace and can enjoy comfort!" The bhikṣus heard this and asked: "What are you saying?" They answered truthfully. The elder bhikṣus then reproached them in various ways, and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu says: "What use are these petty precepts? When these precepts are recited, they cause people distress!" One who thus disparages the precepts, it is a pācittiya.'

"Precepts" means: the Prātimokṣa, the precept text recited at the fortnightly upoṣadha.

If a bhikṣu forms the intention, wishing to cause people to turn away from the Vinaya, to not recite or read it, and disparages the precepts, it is a pācittiya.

If a bhikṣu forms the intention: "I shall disparage so that the Prātimokṣa will not endure long," and disparages the precepts, it is a sthūlātyaya.

If one causes people to turn away from other sūtras spoken by the Buddha and disparages them, it is a pācittiya. If one wishes the Dharma not to endure long and disparages, it is a sthūlātyaya.

If a bhikṣu disparages the precepts of bhikṣus, it is a pācittiya. If he disparages the precepts of bhikṣuṇīs, śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, or śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a bhikṣuṇī disparages the precepts of bhikṣus or bhikṣuṇīs, it is a pācittiya. If she disparages the precepts of śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, or śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, or śrāmaṇerī disparages the precepts of the five assemblies, all are duṣkṛta.

If any of the five assemblies disparages the precepts of upāsakas or upāsikās, all are duṣkṛta.

If one, fearing that a newly ordained person will develop doubt and abandon the practice or lose heart, teaches that one should not yet recite the precepts, this is no offense.

---

The Buddha traveled from the country of Kośala with five hundred bhikṣus toward the village of Aṭavī. At that time, the bhikṣus there, hearing that the Buddha was coming, thought: "The householders here have no faith in the Buddha-Dharma; there is no large assembly hall. Where will the Buddha and the great assembly stay?" They gathered to discuss, and then themselves cut down grass and trees to prepare a place. The householders then criticized and reproached: "We laypeople cut down grass and trees; why do renunciants also do this? These people constantly speak of compassion, forbearance, and protecting living beings, yet now they cut and fell, harming without principle. They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" After the Buddha arrived, he went to the new assembly hall and sat upon his seat. He asked the bhikṣus: "Who built this hall?" They answered: "We did." He asked further: "Who cut and felled the grass and trees?" They answered: "We also did." The Buddha reproached them in various ways, saying: "You foolish people, you should not have done this! Among grass and trees, people perceive living beings. By doing this, you have caused people to harbor ill-will!" After the reproach, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu kills living grass and trees, it is a pācittiya."

At that time, bhikṣus had park-keepers or śrāmaṇeras cut down grass and trees. The elder bhikṣus asked: "Has the Buddha not prohibited killing living grass and trees?" They answered: "We had others do it, so we do not violate the Buddha's prohibition." The elder bhikṣus said: "What difference is there between killing oneself and having others kill?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu kills living grass and trees himself, or has others kill them, it is a pācittiya."

At that time, bhikṣus were building new dwellings. Some householders came to inspect the dwellings and said to the bhikṣus: "Excellent, venerable ones! All the materials for these dwellings were donated by us. Complete the construction quickly so that we may gain the merit of our offering being put to use." The bhikṣus said: "The Buddha does not permit us to kill grass and trees ourselves or have others kill them. How can we complete it?" As a result, the bhikṣus had no dwellings to live in, the courtyard grass grew over their heads, and they also lacked tooth-cleaning sticks, not knowing what to do. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of seeds: root-seeds, stem-seeds, node-seeds, and fruit-seeds. All grass and trees grow from these four kinds of seeds. If a bhikṣu needs any of them, he should tell a pure person, saying: 'You know about this!' If the person does not understand, say further: 'Look at this!' If still not understood, say: 'I need this!' If still not understood, say: 'Give me this!' From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu cuts down a spirit-village himself, or instructs another saying: 'Cut this down!' it is a pācittiya."

If it is living grass, with the perception of living grass or with doubt about living grass, both are pācittiya. If it is dry grass but with the perception of living grass or doubt about dry grass, it is a duṣkṛta. If it is dry grass with the perception of dry grass, there is no offense.

If one chops with a knife or axe, each chop is a pācittiya.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, killing living grass and trees without cause is a duṣkṛta.

If, being burned by fire, one splits or chops, knowing it will certainly not grow again, there is no offense.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus repeatedly committed offenses: getting on and off beds improperly, eating repeatedly, eating separately from the assembly, and entering the village at improper times without informing a virtuous bhikṣu. The bhikṣus who saw this said: "You should not repeatedly commit these offenses. You should recognize your own offenses and confess to others. Do not betray the faith offerings and suffer in the long night!" The group-of-six bhikṣus said: "What offenses have we committed?" The bhikṣus said: "You have committed such and such offenses." The group-of-six bhikṣus did not respond about whether they had offended or not, but instead spoke of other matters. The bhikṣus said: "We are not asking you about that! Why do you not answer about whether you committed offenses or not, and instead speak of other matters?" The group-of-six bhikṣus said: "We know you are not asking about these matters; we are speaking of them on our own!" The bhikṣus reproached them in various ways and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu does not answer in accordance with the question but speaks of other matters, it is a pācittiya.'

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus continued their former misdeeds. The bhikṣus again admonished them as above. The group-of-six bhikṣus remained silent and did not respond. The bhikṣus asked: "The Buddha has established a precept prohibiting not answering in accordance with the question. Why are you silent?" The group-of-six bhikṣus said: "The Buddha prohibited not answering in accordance with the question. If I now do not speak at all, what offense is there?" The bhikṣus said: "What difference is there between speaking of other matters and not speaking at all?" They reproached them in various ways and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways: "What difference is there between speaking of other matters and not speaking at all?" After the reproach, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu intentionally does not answer in accordance with the question, it is a pācittiya.'

If one does not answer in accordance with the question, each question carries a pācittiya.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one mistakenly takes another's words as the question and answers accordingly, or if due to a prior grudge one does not speak to the other and therefore does not answer, there is no offense.

---

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, the bhikṣu Mettiya said: "The bhikṣu Dabbaka acts out of favoritism, hatred, delusion, and fear!" The bhikṣus, hearing this, reproached him: "How can you falsely accuse one whom the Sangha has appointed, saying he acts out of favoritism, hatred, delusion, and fear?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣu Mettiya: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu falsely accuses one whom the Sangha has appointed in front of others, it is a pācittiya.'

Thereupon Mettiya, no longer able to falsely accuse in front of others, falsely accused the bhikṣu Dabbaka in private, saying he acted out of favoritism, hatred, delusion, and fear. The elder bhikṣus, hearing this, reproached him: "The Buddha has already established this prohibition. Why do you still falsely accuse one appointed by the Sangha?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Mettiya: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached him in various ways: "What difference is there between accusing in front of others and accusing in private?" After the reproach, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu falsely accuses one whom the Sangha has appointed, it is a pācittiya.'

If one falsely accuses a person appointed by the Sangha through a motion-karman, a motion-with-one-proclamation karman, or a motion-with-three-proclamations karman, saying this person acts out of favoritism, anger, delusion, or fear, each statement is a pācittiya. If one falsely accuses a person appointed by the Sangha without a karman, or any other person, each statement is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If the person truly acts out of favoritism, hatred, delusion, or fear, and one says: "I shall speak about that person, listen," there is no offense.

---

The Buddha was in Vaiśālī. At a certain dwelling place in a low, damp area, a bhikṣu was assigned a room in the low, damp part. He took the bedding outside and spread it in the open to air and dry it. At mealtime, he put on his robe, took his bowl, and entered the city to beg for food. After he left, a great rain came, the water rose, and the bedding was flooded and swept away. When he returned from his meal, he could not find them. He searched urgently and recovered some items: a large bed here, a small bed there, a mattress here, a quilt there. The bhikṣus who saw asked: "Where did you get all this bedding and these items?" He explained as above. The elder bhikṣus reproached him: "What you have done is improper! People who make bedding for the Sangha are hard to find, and once spread out, there is no one to collect them. How could you leave without putting them away, causing them to be swept away? Had they been lost entirely, this room would have been left empty!" After reproaching him, they reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked that bhikṣu: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached him in various ways as above, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu spreads Sangha bedding in the open and leaves without putting it away, it is a pācittiya.'

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus had park-keepers and śrāmaṇeras spread Sangha bedding in the open and left without instructing them to put it away, allowing birds to peck and gnaw at it and mud and rain to rot and damage it. The elder bhikṣus who saw said: "Have you not heard the Buddha's prohibition against spreading Sangha bedding in the open and leaving without putting it away?" They answered: "We had others spread it; we do not violate the Buddha's prohibition." The bhikṣus said: "What difference is there between spreading it oneself and having others spread it?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu spreads Sangha bedding in the open himself or has others spread it, and when leaving does not put it away himself or instruct others to put it away, it is a pācittiya.'

There were bhikṣus who spread Sangha bedding in the open. The group-of-six bhikṣus came afterward and sat or lay on the bedding, and when leaving did not put it away. The first bhikṣus assumed the group-of-six bhikṣus should put it away; the group-of-six bhikṣus assumed the first bhikṣus should put it away. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "The first bhikṣus should entrust the matter to the later bhikṣus, and the later bhikṣus should put it away. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"'If a bhikṣu spreads Sangha bedding in the open himself, or has others spread it, or if others have spread it and he sits or lies on it, and when leaving does not put it away himself, does not instruct others to put it away, and does not entrust its putting away, it is a pācittiya.'

There were bhikṣus who, seeing Sangha bedding soiled with mud and dirt, placed ashes, soil, or cow dung on it and dried it in the sun, and did not dare leave the boundary. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If it rains, you may collect it, and are permitted to leave."

There were bhikṣus who, while drying Sangha bedding, did not dare go outside the boundary. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one is certain that it will not rain upon return, going outside the boundary is permitted."

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who, while drying Sangha bedding, sat in meditation beside it or fell sound asleep and talked in their sleep. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to sit in meditation or fall sound asleep beside bedding that is being dried. One who does so commits a duṣkṛta!"

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who, after drying Sangha bedding, did not promptly collect it, and it was damaged by sun exposure. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one does not collect and put away in a timely manner, it is a duṣkṛta."

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who saw Sangha bedding spread in the open but, because they had not spread it themselves, had not had others spread it, and had not sat or lain on it, did not collect and put it away. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one sees Sangha bedding spread in the open and does not put it away, it is a pācittiya."

There were laypeople who came into the monastery and requested Sangha bedding to spread in the open. The bhikṣus refused, and the laypeople became greatly displeased. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to give it!" After giving it, they wanted to put it away promptly and told the laypeople to leave quickly, and the laypeople became angry. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not urge them to leave quickly. One should watch for when they leave and then put it away. If one does not put it away, it is a pācittiya."

Furthermore, laypeople invited the Sangha and borrowed Sangha bedding, which they spread at their homes. The bhikṣus sat on it and left without putting it away. The Buddha said: "One should put it away. If one does not, it is a pācittiya."

If bhikṣus go to a bhikṣuṇī monastery and there is Sangha bedding of the bhikṣuṇī Sangha spread in the open, and thinking it is not Sangha bedding, one does not put it away, it is also a pācittiya.

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who carried beds and mats themselves. The householders criticized and reproached: "These śramaṇas are like performers, like illusionists!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One is not permitted to carry them oneself. One who does so commits a duṣkṛta!"

Also, at the time of a great assembly, Sangha bedding was spread in the open. The bhikṣus would put it away every time one person sat and rose, and because of this, it wore out quickly. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If it is not raining, one is permitted to wait until all the business is completely finished, and then put it away." After the Buddha permitted entrusting, the bhikṣus who came later to sit or lie on the bedding would entrust the putting away to their upādhyāyas, ācāryas, those sharing the same upādhyāya or ācārya, and other senior monks, and also to sick bhikṣus. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not entrust this to upādhyāyas, ācāryas, those sharing the same upādhyāya or ācārya, other senior monks, or sick bhikṣus. One who does so commits a duṣkṛta!" The bhikṣus entrusted a single bhikṣu, and the single bhikṣu, putting it all away alone, became exhausted. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "According to the amount of bedding, if there is little, entrust to few bhikṣus; if there is much, entrust to many

I notice that the instruction says to translate Fascicle 卷第六 (Fascicle 6), but then at the bottom it says "Fascicle 007: 五分律." There also appears to be no Chinese text provided between the delimiter lines. The space between the two "========" lines is empty.

Without the actual Chinese source text to translate, I am unable to produce a translation. Could you please provide the Chinese text of the fascicle you would like translated? Once you paste the full Chinese text, I will translate it completely and in full according to all the rules specified.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Seven

### First Division, Part Five: The Twenty-Second Matter

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the bhikṣus were taking turns instructing the bhikṣuṇīs in sequential order. They told the bhikṣuṇīs: "Tomorrow it is Bhikṣu Panthaka's turn to instruct you. You should go to him and receive the Dharma teaching." Having said this, they returned to their dwelling place and told Panthaka: "Tomorrow you should instruct the bhikṣuṇīs." The next day, at mealtime, Elder Panthaka put on his robes and took his bowl, entered the city to beg for food. After eating, he returned, swept inside and outside, fetched clean water, prepared hand and foot towels, laid out a seat in an open area, took a rope-bed for himself, and sat in meditation beside it.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇīs came to Panthaka's place, bowed their heads at his feet, and sat down in their seats. Thereupon Panthaka asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Have you previously heard the Eight Rules of Respect?" They answered: "We have heard them." He further said: "Sisters, listen again: First, the bhikṣuṇī Sangha should, every half-month, request from the bhikṣu Sangha a person to give instruction. Second, when the bhikṣuṇī Sangha takes the rains retreat, they must always depend upon the bhikṣu Sangha. Third, when the bhikṣuṇīs hold the pravāraṇā ceremony, they should, by a motion-and-one-proclamation act, send three bhikṣuṇīs to request from the bhikṣu Sangha instruction regarding offenses seen, heard, or suspected. Fourth, after a śikṣamāṇā has trained for two years in the six precepts, she should seek full ordination from both assemblies. Fifth, a bhikṣuṇī must not revile a bhikṣu, nor speak of a bhikṣu in lay households, saying he has violated precepts, violated deportment, holds wrong views, or practices wrong livelihood. Sixth, a bhikṣuṇī must not charge a bhikṣu with an offense, but a bhikṣu may reprove a bhikṣuṇī. Seventh, if a bhikṣuṇī commits a gross offense, she should seek to perform the mānāpya penance for half a month in both Sanghas; after performing the mānāpya, then for the abhyudhāna, she should have her offense cleared in an assembly of twenty bhikṣus and twenty bhikṣuṇīs. Eighth, even if a bhikṣuṇī received full ordination a hundred years ago, she should still bow to a newly ordained bhikṣu." Having explained these Eight Rules of Respect, he then spoke a verse:

> "If you wish for a good mind, do not be heedless;
> The noble ones' good Dharma should be diligently studied.
> If one is a person of wisdom and single-minded focus,
> Then one can be free from worry and affliction."

Having spoken this verse, he closed his eyes and sat upright. At that time, the bhikṣuṇīs whispered to each other: "This bhikṣu only knows this one verse. How could he be able to instruct us?" Panthaka heard this and thought: "These bhikṣuṇīs look down upon me." Thereupon he leaped into the sky and manifested dividing one body into countless bodies, then merged them back into one. He passed through stone walls, walked on water as on ground, and entered the earth as if it were water. Sometimes he showed half his body, sometimes his whole body. Sometimes smoke arose from the upper part of his body while fire blazed below; sometimes fire blazed above while smoke arose below. Sometimes water came from above his body while fire came from below; sometimes fire came from above while water came from below. Or he sat and reclined in the air, like a bird in flight. Or he touched the sun and moon with his hands, or his body stood level, reaching up to the Brahmā heaven. Having displayed these miraculous transformations, he returned to his original seat and spoke the verse as before. The bhikṣuṇīs, seeing these miraculous transformations, were greatly delighted and said: "Venerable one! Please instruct us further with your supernatural powers!" Thereupon Panthaka rose up in the east and disappeared in the west, rose up in the west and disappeared in the east, and likewise in all other directions. He performed various such miraculous transformations, then sat down again and repeated the above verse, continuing until sunset, and only then departed.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇīs arrived at the city gate in the evening, but the city gate was already closed. They knocked on the gate and asked to enter. The gatekeeper asked: "Who are you?" They answered: "We are bhikṣuṇīs." The gatekeeper asked: "Where are you coming from at night?" They answered: "The Venerable Panthaka was instructing us, which is why we are returning late." The gatekeeper said: "Go back to where you came from. Even if the king came, I would not dare to open the gate!" Since the bhikṣuṇīs could not enter, some stayed under the gate, some at the edge of the moat, some under trees, and at night they were afflicted by mosquitoes, gadflies, wind, and dust. The next morning when the gate opened, they were the first to enter. At that time, the householders asked each other: "These bhikṣuṇīs entered first thing at dawn. Where did they come from?" Some people said: "They must have been out looking for men!" Those who had no faith or delight in the Buddha-Dharma reproached them in various ways, saying: "We laypeople do not practice the pure conduct, and you bhikṣuṇīs are just the same. You shave your heads for nothing and wear robes of muted colors!" The senior bhikṣus heard this and reproached Panthaka: "How could you instruct the bhikṣuṇīs until sunset?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Panthaka: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, having been assigned by the Sangha to instruct the bhikṣuṇīs, continues until sunset, it is a pācittika."

'Assigned by the Sangha' means: assigned by a motion-and-one-proclamation act.

If a bhikṣu has been assigned by the Sangha, he should tell the bhikṣuṇīs: "Sisters, if there is no difficulty, I will instruct you; if there is a difficulty, I cannot instruct you."

If the instruction is given at the bhikṣuṇīs' dwelling place, he should tell them: "You should lay out seats, and I will come." If he cannot go, he should sweep and prepare his own dwelling place as before. He should bring a senior bhikṣu as a companion; only if it is truly impossible should he go alone. After explaining the Eight Rules of Respect, if it is still early in the day and he can further explain other Dharma teachings, that is also good. He should estimate the early or late hour of the day, making sure to return to his dwelling place before sunset. When the Dharma talk is finished, if those who should depart first may depart. If there are places of danger, the bhikṣu should escort the bhikṣuṇīs to their destination.

If a bhikṣu instructs the bhikṣuṇīs until sunset, for each utterance, it is a pācittika.

For a śrāmaṇera, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the bhikṣus were taking turns instructing the bhikṣuṇīs in sequential order, and the bhikṣuṇīs all attained various dhyānas, liberations, samādhis, and correct attainments. At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus, without being assigned by the Sangha, also went to instruct, but they only spoke coarse, unwholesome, and evil words. The senior bhikṣuṇīs remained silent and did not listen, but the group-of-six bhikṣuṇīs praised them saying: "Excellent! None surpasses this!" Thereupon Bhikṣuṇī Mahāprajāpatī, together with five hundred bhikṣuṇīs, went to the Buddha and reported this to him. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu enters the dwelling place of bhikṣuṇīs, it is a pācittika."

At that time, bhikṣus who had matters to attend to, stūpa affairs, Sangha affairs, or private affairs, and needed to enter the bhikṣuṇīs' dwelling place, were ashamed and did not dare, not knowing what to do. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "Unless it is for the purpose of instruction, entry is not permitted. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, without being assigned by the Sangha, enters the dwelling place of bhikṣuṇīs for the purpose of instruction, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣus who, although assigned by the Sangha, were still ashamed and did not dare to enter. Because the bhikṣuṇīs received no instruction, they gained nothing. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "Bhikṣus assigned by the Sangha are permitted to enter. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, without being assigned by the Sangha, enters the dwelling place of bhikṣuṇīs for the purpose of instruction, it is a pācittika."

At that time, Bhikṣuṇī Bhadrā was ill and sent a message to Śāriputra: "May the Venerable One come and give me a final Dharma talk!" Śāriputra said: "The Buddha does not permit one who is not assigned by the Sangha to enter the bhikṣuṇīs' dwelling place for the purpose of instruction." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I permit teaching the Dharma to a sick bhikṣuṇī without being assigned by the Sangha. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, without being assigned by the Sangha, enters the dwelling place of bhikṣuṇīs for the purpose of instruction, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pācittika. The valid reason is: when a bhikṣuṇī is ill. This is called the valid reason."

If a bhikṣu, without being assigned by the Sangha, enters the dwelling place of bhikṣuṇīs for the purpose of instruction, for each step taken, step by step it is a pācittika. If one foot enters the doorway, it is a duṣkṛta.

For a śrāmaṇera, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the senior bhikṣus were taking turns instructing the bhikṣuṇīs, and the bhikṣuṇīs would separately invite them and make offerings, giving them bowl-bags, waist cords, lamp oil, robes, food, medicines, and the like as gifts. When the group-of-six bhikṣus saw this, they told the bhikṣus: "You should assign us as instructors." The bhikṣus said: "According to what the Buddha has taught, one must be endowed with ten qualities. You do not possess them. How can you seek to be assigned?" The group-of-six bhikṣus then said: "The bhikṣus instruct the bhikṣuṇīs for the sake of offerings and gain." The bhikṣus reproached them in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu says: 'The bhikṣus instruct the bhikṣuṇīs for the sake of offerings and gain,' it is a pācittika."

If one says it is for the sake of offerings and gain that bhikṣuṇīs, śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, or śrāmaṇerīs are instructed, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one says that bhikṣus practice the twelve dhūtaguṇas, sit in meditation, recite sūtras, or perform various meritorious deeds, all for the sake of offerings and gain, for each utterance, it is a duṣkṛta.

For a śrāmaṇera, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, bhikṣus sat alone in a secluded place with bhikṣuṇīs, and as a result developed attachment, lost delight in the pure conduct, and some returned to lay life or joined other religious groups. Householders who saw this all criticized and reproached them, saying: "These śramaṇas sit alone in a secluded place with bhikṣuṇīs, just like laypeople with prostitutes! They consume the faithful offerings of others and do such things. They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard this and reproached them in various ways, then reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked those bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu sits alone in a secluded place with a bhikṣuṇī, it is a pācittika."

Sitting alone in a secluded place with a śikṣamāṇā or śrāmaṇerī is likewise.

For a śrāmaṇera, it is a duṣkṛta.

If many bhikṣus and bhikṣuṇīs sit together; or if dangers arise and one must sit alone in a secluded place with them; these are all not offenses.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, there was an āraṇyaka bhikṣu who dwelled in a forest hermitage and never associated with any monastic or lay persons. One morning, that bhikṣu put on his robes, took his bowl, and entered a village to beg for food. On the road he met two bhikṣuṇīs. One bhikṣuṇī said to the other: "I now wish to become acquainted with this bhikṣu. Will you join me?" She answered: "Very well!" When the bhikṣu arrived, they bowed to him, but the bhikṣu remained silent and did not speak with them. The two bhikṣuṇīs then discussed together: "This bhikṣu does not wish to become acquainted on the road. Let us go to his dwelling place and pay our respects." The next morning, the two bhikṣuṇīs went early to the bhikṣu's place and paid their respects. At the first bow and the second bow, he did not speak with them. At the third bow, he finally said: "Long life to you!" The two bhikṣuṇīs finished their bows and departed.

Later, that bhikṣu again entered the village to beg for food. The two bhikṣuṇīs saw him in an alleyway, bowed and greeted him, and then spoke with him. The two bhikṣuṇīs then offered the bhikṣu a piece of cloth and some dyed thread. The bhikṣu accepted them. They said: "Venerable one, you must be weary. Come to our dwelling place and rest a little, then go beg for food." He went and rested there. When he had rested and was about to go beg for food, the bhikṣuṇī further said: "We have vegetable sauce here. If you obtain food, you can come back and eat it here." The bhikṣu again accepted her words. When he obtained food, he brought it back. This happened not just once. Sometimes the bhikṣu returned from begging food first and waited for the bhikṣuṇīs; sometimes the bhikṣuṇīs returned first and waited for the bhikṣu. They even went to other families and praised each other.

Later, that bhikṣu obtained a fine robe and, with a deceitful mind, thought: "I shall now give this robe to that bhikṣuṇī. She surely will not accept it, and I will gain the credit of generous giving." Having thought this, he first praised the bhikṣuṇī before the other bhikṣus, saying: "Such-and-such bhikṣuṇī is of good family, has gone forth with faith, is firm and correct, with few desires and contentment." The bhikṣus said: "One who has gone forth should be like this!" After praising her, he took the robe and gave it to the bhikṣuṇī, and the bhikṣuṇī immediately accepted it. Having failed in his original scheme, he was vexed and resentful in his heart. He went back and told the bhikṣus: "Such-and-such bhikṣuṇī has shallow faith, is full of desires and insatiable!" The bhikṣus said: "Just now you said she had few desires and was content. Why do you now speak like this?" He gave the full account as above. The bhikṣus reproached him in various ways: "How could you, without genuinely relinquishing the object in your heart, falsely give it to someone, estimating that they would not accept it, and vainly hope for their gratitude?" They reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked that bhikṣu: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." The Buddha reproached him in various ways: "Have you not heard me praise the relinquishing of possessions when giving to others, after which one obtains great merit? How could you, without relinquishing the object in your heart, falsely give it to someone?" After reproaching him, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu gives a robe to a bhikṣuṇī, it is a pācittika."

At that time, bhikṣus who had bhikṣuṇīs who were relatives found that their robes and bedding were all coarse and worn, and they could not obtain medicine for illnesses. The bhikṣus thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us to give robes and other items to our bhikṣuṇī relatives, they would not suffer like this." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to give robes and other items to bhikṣuṇīs who are relatives. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu gives a robe to a bhikṣuṇī who is not a relative, it is a pācittika."

At that time, in Śrāvastī, both Sanghas received already-made robes and divided them together. Sometimes a bhikṣu received a robe suitable for a bhikṣuṇī to wear, and sometimes a bhikṣuṇī received one suitable for a bhikṣu. They wished to exchange but did not dare. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to exchange robes with bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu gives a robe to a bhikṣuṇī who is not a relative, except in exchange, it is a pācittika."

If one gives a robe to a bhikṣuṇī who is a relative but who is of broken precepts or wrong views, it is a duṣkṛta. If one gives a robe to a śikṣamāṇā or śrāmaṇerī who is not a relative, it is a duṣkṛta.

For a śrāmaṇera, it is a duṣkṛta.

If it is done for managing work or affairs, for skillfully expounding the sūtras and Dharma, or for one who recites many sūtras and precepts, giving a robe is not an offense.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, there was a bhikṣuṇī with few acquaintances who obtained an unmade robe. Not knowing how to make it herself, she said to the bhikṣuṇīs: "I do not know how to make a robe. Please make it for me." The bhikṣuṇīs said: "Sister, we are very busy and cannot make it. You should go to the bhikṣu Sangha and ask; one who has a compassionate heart will surely make it for you." She then went to the bhikṣu Sangha and said: "I have few acquaintances and have obtained this unmade robe. I do not know how to make it myself. Please make it for me." The bhikṣus also answered as above. She then went to Elder Udāyin and addressed him as above. Udāyin said: "I can make it! But do not come repeatedly to urge me. Let me do it at my own pace, and I will make it for you." She answered: "As the Elder wishes." Thereupon Udāyin took the robe, cut and sewed it, but after a long time it was not finished. The bhikṣuṇī came to ask: "Venerable one, is the robe finished yet?" Udāyin said: "We had an agreement beforehand. Why do you come to urge me?" She answered: "I have come to inquire, not daring to urge you!" She then returned to her dwelling.

Afterwards, Udāyin, using threads of various colors, embroidered on the central panel images of men and women in sexual union. When the images were completed, he called the bhikṣuṇī to come and collect it. She came to take it, and Udāyin told her: "Do not yet unfold and look at it, nor show it to anyone. When Bhikṣuṇī Mahāprajāpatī goes to visit the Buddha, at that time, at the main crossroads, then unfold and wear it." The bhikṣuṇī took the robe and departed, and indeed she did not unfold it to look at it, nor did she show it to anyone. When Bhikṣuṇī Mahāprajāpatī went to visit the Buddha, she unfolded it at the main crossroads. People on the road who saw it clapped their hands and laughed loudly, saying to each other: "This bhikṣuṇī is indeed so skillful at embroidery, making such images as she desires, and wears them on her robe!" That bhikṣuṇī was extremely ashamed and immediately returned to her dwelling. Bhikṣuṇī Mahāprajāpatī folded up this robe and brought it to the Buddha, unfolded it and showed the Buddha: "May the World-Honored One please look at what was made!" The Buddha spoke various Dharma teachings for Gautamī, then sent her back to her dwelling. Because of this matter, he assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Udāyin: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." The Buddha reproached him in various ways: "You foolish person! How could you make such a defiling robe for someone?" After reproaching him, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu makes a robe for a bhikṣuṇī, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣus who had bhikṣuṇīs who were relatives, whose robes were worn out and tattered. Although they begged for cloth, they did not know how to make robes. The bhikṣus thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us to make robes for our bhikṣuṇī relatives, they would not suffer like this." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to make robes for bhikṣuṇīs who are relatives. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu makes a robe for a bhikṣuṇī who is not a relative, it is a pācittika."

When a bhikṣu takes the robe of a bhikṣuṇī who is not a relative, it is a duṣkṛta. When cutting it, when dyeing it, it is each a pācittika. When sewing it, for each stitch, it is a pācittika. The rest is as stated in the section on giving robes.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, bhikṣus in the Magadha country were traveling through the countryside with bhikṣuṇīs. Sometimes one bhikṣu traveled with one bhikṣuṇī, and so on up to many. When crossing deep water or climbing steep hills, they saw each other's bodies, developed attachment, no longer delighted in practicing the pure conduct, and went so far as to return to lay life or join other religious groups. Laypeople who saw this criticized them saying: "These śramaṇas are like people traveling with their wives or prostitutes!" They made various criticisms, as stated above in the section on sitting alone in a secluded place. The senior bhikṣus heard this and reproached them in various ways, then reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu travels on the road together with a bhikṣuṇī, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣus traveling on the road with many companions who saw bhikṣuṇīs also traveling on the same road. They thought: "Have we not committed a pācittika?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If they did not previously make an agreement together, committing a pācittika is not possible. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, having previously agreed with a bhikṣuṇī, travels on the road together, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣus who had previously agreed with bhikṣuṇīs to travel on the road together, but later did not dare to go, or the two avoided each other. Because of the prior agreement, they had doubts. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If one previously agreed with a bhikṣuṇī to travel together on the road, but then does not go, or if the two avoid each other, committing a pācittika is not possible. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, having previously agreed with a bhikṣuṇī, travels on the road together from one village to another village, it is a pācittika."

At that time, a bhikṣuṇī on a dangerous road saw a bhikṣu and called out: "Venerable one, come quickly! Let us travel together on the road!" That bhikṣu went to her and said: "Sister, the Buddha has established a rule that does not permit traveling on the road together with a bhikṣuṇī." The bhikṣuṇī said: "This road is dangerous and difficult, and I am a weak woman. Only by relying on you, Venerable one, can I get through." He answered as before, and the bhikṣu departed. Afterwards, the bhikṣuṇī was robbed and stripped by bandits. Naked, she cried out loudly: "Robbers have stripped me! Robbers have stripped me!" That bhikṣu heard her cries from afar. When he reached his dwelling place, he told the other bhikṣus. The bhikṣus brought him to the Buddha and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to travel on the road together with bhikṣuṇīs when the road is dangerous and there are places of fear and suspicion. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, having previously agreed with a bhikṣuṇī, travels on the road together from one village to another village, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pācittika. The valid reasons are: when traveling with many companions, or when there are places of fear and suspicion. These are called valid reasons."

If a bhikṣu, having previously agreed with a bhikṣuṇī, travels together on the road, in a place without villages, for half a yojana, it is a pācittika. If one previously agrees with a śikṣamāṇā or śrāmaṇerī to travel together on the road, it is likewise.

For a śrāmaṇera, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, bhikṣus in the Magadha country were traveling by boat with bhikṣuṇīs. Sometimes one bhikṣu traveled in one boat with one bhikṣuṇī, and so on up to many. When boarding and leaving the boat, they saw each other's bodies. Laypeople criticized them. The senior bhikṣus heard this, and so on up to the Buddha telling the bhikṣus, all as stated above in the section on traveling on the road together. "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, having previously agreed with a bhikṣuṇī, travels together by boat, it is a pācittika."

There was a bhikṣuṇī at the bank of the Aciravatī River, waiting for a boat to cross. Afterwards, a bhikṣu came. The bhikṣuṇī said: "Venerable one! It is dangerous here. Let us cross together." The bhikṣu answered: "The Buddha has established a rule that does not permit us to ride in the same boat with a bhikṣuṇī." The boatman also said: "Just get on the boat together, each sitting at one end." The bhikṣu would not agree. The bhikṣuṇī said: "If that is not possible, then Venerable one, please cross first." The bhikṣu then crossed first. Before the boat reached the far bank, the bhikṣuṇī was stripped, down to her bare flesh. The boatman, seeing this, criticized and reproached them: "You have gone forth together in the religious life, yet you cannot protect each other. How much less would you protect others! You have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!"

That bhikṣu returned to the monastery and told the other bhikṣus. The bhikṣus brought him to the Buddha and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked that bhikṣu: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." The Buddha reproached him in various ways: "You foolish person! How could you abandon the bhikṣuṇī and let her be robbed and stripped?" After reproaching him, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, having previously agreed with a bhikṣuṇī, travels together by boat, whether going upstream or downstream, except for a direct crossing, it is a pācittika." The rest is as stated in the section on traveling on the road together.

---

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, Nanda and Bhadra ate food that had been praised by bhikṣuṇīs. The bhikṣus, seeing this, reproached them in various ways.

At that time, Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana were traveling through the countryside and arrived at Rājagṛha. A certain householder, hearing that the two had come, went out to welcome them, bowed his head at their feet, and sat to one side. They taught him the wonderful Dharma, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening him. The householder then invited them for a guest-bhikṣu meal the next day, and they silently accepted. The householder returned to his home and prepared various foods and drinks, and laid out fine seats. Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana, at the proper time, put on their robes, took their bowls, and went to his house. Śāriputra was about to enter first when Bhikṣuṇī Sthūlanandā, who was already in that household, heard his voice. He then stopped and did not enter. The bhikṣuṇī, seeing the various foods and drinks prepared and fine seats laid out, asked: "Are you expecting to host the king? Or is this a wedding?" The householder answered: "We are not hosting the king, nor is it a wedding. We wish to make offerings to the Venerable Śāriputra and Mahā-Maudgalyāyana." The bhikṣuṇī said: "Why don't you invite the great dragons, instead of making offerings to these bhikṣus of little virtue?" The householder asked: "Who are the great dragons?" She answered: "The group-of-six bhikṣus!" She further said: "If you wish to invite those of good family who have gone forth, who practice the dhūtaguṇas, the four foundations of mindfulness up through the noble Eightfold Path, stream-enterers up through arhats; if you wish for a good son-in-law, good children, rebirth in heaven, liberation, or prosperity in this present life, you should make offerings to the group-of-six bhikṣus." Having praised them thus, she sat in silence. Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana then entered. The bhikṣuṇī greeted them first, saying: "Excellent, Venerable Ones! Please take these seats." She then told the householder: "Excellent, householder! Today you have obtained great merit and benefit by inviting such great dragon bhikṣus." The householder then reproached her, saying: "Just now you called them of little virtue, and now you call them great dragons. With such inconsistency, how are you not ashamed? From now on, do not enter my house! I will no longer make offerings to you!" Thereupon the householder personally served the food. After the meal, he brought water, took a low seat, and sat before the two bhikṣus. After they had taught the wonderful Dharma, they rose from their seats, departed, and returned to the Buddha. The Buddha, seeing them from afar, said: "Welcome, Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana! Where have you eaten a guest-bhikṣu meal?" They answered: "World-Honored One, we went to a certain householder's home, and there was a bhikṣuṇī who at one point called us of little virtue and at another point called us great dragons." The Buddha asked: "What did she say?" They gave the full account. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really eat food that was praised by bhikṣuṇīs?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu eats food that was praised by a bhikṣuṇī, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who praised food for bhikṣus in a hidden place, and the bhikṣus only found out afterwards. They felt ashamed and thought: "Have I not committed a pācittika?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If one does not know that the food was obtained through a bhikṣuṇī's praise, eating it and committing a pācittika is not possible. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu knowingly eats food obtained through a bhikṣuṇī's praise, it is a pācittika."

At that time, a certain household invited five hundred bhikṣus for a meal. A bhikṣuṇī who previously received offerings from that household said: "Give the bhikṣus their food. Do not let them miss the proper time!" The bhikṣus heard this and felt ashamed, not daring to eat anymore. They went back and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣuṇī did not previously praise, but at the time of the meal says: 'Give the bhikṣus good food; bhikṣus, eat this food,' committing a pācittika is not possible. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu knowingly eats food obtained through a bhikṣuṇī's praise, except when the donor had already made up their mind beforehand, it is a pācittika."

'Praise' means: praising one as having attained superhuman qualities.

If a śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, or śrāmaṇerī praises, and a bhikṣu eats food obtained through this praise, it is a duṣkṛta.

For a śrāmaṇera, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, there was a great minister who constantly made offerings to the Buddha and the bhikṣu Sangha. A certain poor man saw this and thought: "This great minister has obtained great merit, being able to make offerings to the Buddha and Sangha like this. If I had possessions, I too would do likewise." He further thought: "I now have nothing. I can only hire myself out as a laborer and use the wages for offerings." He then worked as a hired laborer, eating only one meal a day and saving the other meal's portion. His employer asked: "Why do you save one meal portion?" He answered: "I will save it for now and collect it all later." After a time, when he knew he had accumulated enough, he told his employer: "Please pay me all my wages at once, and prepare various food provisions for me." The employer asked: "You are poor. Why do you want all your wages at once to suddenly prepare various foods and drinks?" He answered: "I have seen the great minister of Rājagṛha constantly inviting the Buddha and the Sangha, making various offerings. I made this vow that I too would do the same. That is why I have been working as a laborer, wishing to fulfill this intention. Now I calculate that my wages are sufficient for one offering, which is why I now wish to prepare the food provisions all at once." The employer, hearing this, was filled with a rare feeling: "Such a poor person toils with his body as a laborer, and even with the little wealth he has, he still uses it for offerings. How much more should I, who have wealth!" At the moment this thought arose, the food provisions were already prepared. Yet the employer was unable to do it himself, so he hired at several times the cost, allowing the laborer to prepare all manner of delicacies as he wished. At that time, the laborer took the funds and went to the Buddha's place, preparing an offering of the most exquisite foods in the world. Because of the sincerity of his intention, spirits and deities came to assist, and in an instant, everything was spontaneously prepared. It happened to be a festival day with many offerings, and many people came competing to invite the bhikṣus to eat at their homes. The bhikṣus said to each other: "Today a poor man has exhausted his strength to prepare this feast. Everyone should eat sparingly for his sake." Although they admonished each other, what they ate was very little, yet the invitations were many and they soon became full.

At that time, the poor man's food provisions were ready, and he announced: "The time has come." Thereupon all the bhikṣus gathered and took their seats; only the Buddha remained in his room. The poor man personally served and ladled the food, happily offering it. Although the bhikṣus accepted it, they ate very little. The poor man thought: "Do the bhikṣus pity me for being poor, fearing the food will not be enough? Or is the food bad and not fit to eat?" He asked them about it. Among the bhikṣus, those of less modesty answered: "We feared your food would be insufficient, so we ate at other homes beforehand." The poor man said resentfully: "Why did you first accept my invitation and then eat at other homes? I originally exerted all my strength, intending to provide the full offering! Now, Venerable Ones, even if you cannot eat, please take it away as you wish. Do not let any remain." When the bhikṣus heard this, they forced themselves to eat more, yet still could not finish even half of what was provided. When the Sangha had finished eating, the poor man further thought: "I strongly urged the Sangha to eat. Have I not committed an offense?" He reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Excellent, poor man! You were able to see others making merit and, through your labor, aspired to emulate them. Although you have received a human body, the causes and conditions for rebirth in heaven are already fully complete. From the time you first gave rise to the thought of making offerings to the Buddha and Sangha, in accordance with each matter, you have obtained immeasurable merit. Even if the entire Sangha did not eat a single grain, your merit would be fully complete. You shall now also receive rewards in this present life." The poor man, hearing this, was overjoyed. The Buddha further taught him various wonderful Dharma teachings, and right there on his seat, he became free from dust, free from defilement, and obtained the pure Dharma eye. Having seen the Dharma, he took the three refuges and upheld the five precepts. He rose from his seat, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, circumambulated to the right, and departed.

At that time, there were five hundred merchants coming from the land of Avanti. The road was long and desolate, and they had been without provisions for three days. They sent a mounted messenger ahead to seek cooked food. He told everyone: "We are five hundred merchants coming from Avanti. We have been without provisions for three days, so I was sent ahead to seek cooked food. If there is any cooked food, the price does not matter!" The people of the city said: "There is nothing available here. Only a poor man at the monastery grounds is holding a feast, and there is much left over. Go there and ask; you will surely obtain some without doubt." Thereupon the messenger quickly rode there and fully explained the situation. The poor man answered: "The food I have prepared today is not for monetary gain. Just come quickly and do not discuss the price." The messenger, hearing this, was overjoyed beyond expectation, and happily returned to report. In a short while, they all arrived, saying: "Give us food quickly, and we will repay you handsomely!" He answered as before. He then served the food. After they were fully satisfied, they inquired of others: "What occupation does this person have, that he is able to prepare such various provisions?" Someone gave the full account. The merchants, hearing this, were doubly amazed. They immediately gathered one hundred thousand gold pieces to repay his generosity. They further inquired: "Is a certain person of this city still alive?" The answer was: "He has died." They asked again: "Does he have children or descendants?" The answer was: "The host who just gave you the food is his son." The merchants, hearing this, were deeply moved and told the poor man: "Your father was our master." They gave him another one hundred thousand gold pieces to honor the old connection. The great minister of Rājagṛha and the employer, hearing and seeing these events, were even more delighted, and each also sent one hundred thousand gold pieces to forge a new friendship. That very day, King Bimbisāra further appointed him as a great minister. In a single day, he became suddenly wealthy and eminent, and the people of the country called him "Elder Suddenly Arisen."

The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really accept someone's invitation and then eat food at other places first?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." The Buddha reproached them in various ways: "You foolish people! How could you, having already accepted someone's invitation, eat food at other places first?" After reproaching them, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus.
