From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu eats food repeatedly, it is a pācittika."

At that time, Pilindavatsa and eighty other bhikṣus all had serious illnesses and could not eat a full meal at once. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit sick bhikṣus to eat food repeatedly. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu eats food repeatedly, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pācittika. The valid reason is: when ill. This is called the valid reason."

At that time, the World-Honored One permitted the bhikṣus to accept the kaṭhina robe, with five exemptions from offenses. The bhikṣus thought: "Is it that during the robe season, eating repeatedly is not an offense? And after the robe season is over, is it also not an offense?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "During the robe season, it is not an offense. After the robe season, it is an offense. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu eats food repeatedly, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pācittika. The valid reasons are: when ill, during the robe season. These are called the valid reasons."

There were laypeople who knew that bhikṣus were not permitted to eat food repeatedly. They thought: "We shall devise a means to make robes for the bhikṣus. When the bhikṣus come to accept them, we can also make offerings of food." They then made robes and invited the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus said: "We cannot eat the food. Just give us the robes." The laypeople said: "Venerable Ones, if you accept our food, only then will we give the robes." The bhikṣus thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us to eat food repeatedly when robes are being given, we would not lack robes." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to eat food repeatedly when robes are being given. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu eats food repeatedly, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pācittika. The valid reasons are: when ill, during the robe season, when robes are being given. These are called the valid reasons."

At that time, Ānanda went to a householder's home. The householder's family had prepared various foods and drinks. Ānanda, forgetting he had previously accepted an invitation, accepted their food. Just as he was about to eat, he remembered and told the host: "Please take back the food. I have previously accepted an invitation and cannot eat again." The householder said resentfully: "How can you accept my food and then suddenly change your mind?" Thereupon Ānanda quickly returned and reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If such circumstances arise, one should first mentally transfer one's share, thinking: 'I give my share of the invitation to such-and-such bhikṣu.' After that, one may eat." If one eats without mentally transferring to someone, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, sometimes the earlier invitation had a later meal set up, and sometimes the later invitation had an earlier meal set up. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "At the time of accepting the invitation, one should say: 'I will eat whichever meal is served first.'"

'Eating food repeatedly' means: having already accepted another's invitation, one eats food at another place. This is called eating food repeatedly.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one is assigned by the Sangha; or eats in a separate room; or laypeople who have taken the eight precepts set up an offering; or it is the regular communal meal; these are not offenses.

---

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, Devadatta, seeking to gather support, taught various householders, having one household invite four Sangha members, or five, or ten. The senior bhikṣus reproached the bhikṣus who accepted the invitations, saying: "How can you accept separate invitations for a group meal in order to support Devadatta?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really accept Devadatta's separate invitations for group meals?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu accepts a separate invitation for a group meal, it is a pācittika."

There were sick bhikṣus who dragged themselves while ill to beg for food, and their condition worsened. Householders told them: "Do not drag yourself to beg while ill. You can eat at our home!" They answered: "The World-Honored One has established a precept that does not permit us to accept separate invitations for group meals. If you make offerings to the whole Sangha, we will naturally receive our share." The householders said: "Our families are poor and cannot afford to provide widely. We can only manage to provide for the sick. Venerable Ones, if you need it, please come and take it." The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit sick bhikṣus to accept separate invitations for group meals." The robe season and when robes are being given are as stated in the section on eating food repeatedly. "From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu accepts a separate invitation for a group meal, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pācittika. The valid reasons are: when ill, during the robe season, when robes are being given. These are called the valid reasons."

There were bhikṣus who wished to make robes, but because they had to beg for food, the robes could not be finished quickly, obstructing their practice. They thought: "If, during the time of making robes, the Buddha would permit us to accept separate invitations for group meals, the robes could be finished quickly and would not obstruct our practice." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to accept separate invitations for group meals during the time of making robes. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu accepts a separate invitation for a group meal, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pācittika. The valid reasons are: when ill, during the robe season, when robes are being given, during the time of making robes. These are called the valid reasons."

There were bhikṣus traveling with companions who arrived at a village and told their companions: "We will enter the village to beg for food. Please wait for us a little." They answered: "There is no need to beg for food. We will provide for you." The bhikṣus said: "The World-Honored One does not permit us to accept separate invitations for group meals." So they entered the village to beg, and their companions left without them. When the bhikṣus finished begging, they could no longer catch up with their companions, and they were robbed by bandits, stripped to their bare flesh. The bhikṣus thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us to accept separate invitations for group meals when traveling on the road, we would not have suffered this calamity."

There were bhikṣus who had taken passage on someone's boat. At mealtime, they went to beg for food, and the boatman departed, causing them great hardship. The situation was as above. They all reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit bhikṣus to accept separate invitations for group meals when traveling on the road or when traveling by boat. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu accepts a separate invitation for a group meal, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pācittika. The valid reasons are: when ill, during the robe season, when robes are being given, during the time of making robes, when traveling on the road, when traveling by boat. These are called the valid reasons."

It was the constant practice of all Buddhas that there were two great assemblies each year, at the end of the spring and summer months. Bhikṣus from all directions would come to pay their respects. Because the assembly was so large, invitations were very infrequent, and begged food was hard to obtain. The bhikṣus thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us to accept separate invitations for group meals during the time of a great assembly, we would not suffer like this." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to accept separate invitations for group meals during the time of a great assembly. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu accepts a separate invitation for a group meal, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pācittika. The valid reasons are: when ill, during the robe season, when robes are being given, during the time of making robes, when traveling on the road, when traveling by boat, during a great assembly. These are called the valid reasons."

At that time, King Bimbisāra's younger brother, named Kāḷa, followed a certain religious tradition. Every year he would universally invite śramaṇas of all ninety-six traditions for one great assembly. Hearing that the Śākya śramaṇas did not accept separate invitations for group meals, and being unable to provide broadly for the entire Sangha, he was troubled that the assembly would lack śramaṇas of the Buddha's path, and was worried and unhappy. He thought: "How can I get the Śākya śramaṇas to come? I should entrust this to the King, and then it can be accomplished." He reported this to the King. The King, because of this matter, went to see the Buddha and fully explained his brother's wish. After the King departed, the Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to accept separate invitations for group meals during a śramaṇa assembly. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu accepts a separate invitation for a group meal, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pācittika. The valid reasons are: when ill, during the robe season, when robes are being given, during the time of making robes, when traveling on the road, when traveling by boat, during a great assembly, during a śramaṇa assembly. These are called the valid reasons."

'Separate invitation for a group meal' means: from within the Sangha, separately inviting four persons or more. This is called a separate invitation for a group meal.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one initially accepts a separate invitation for a group meal, but upon arriving they split up and say they have accepted different invitations, so that it no longer constitutes a group, this is not an offense.

---

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, there was famine everywhere and begged food was hard to obtain. All the bhikṣus gathered in Rājagṛha. People in distant places said: "In the past we used to see bhikṣus morning and evening. Why have they now all disappeared?" Someone said: "Begged food is hard to get here. They have all gone to Rājagṛha. That is why they are not seen." The people said: "We should build small shelters and provide one meal and one night's lodging for one bhikṣu per day. If no one comes to eat, we should accumulate it and wait for later arrivals." They then did so. At that time, there was one household that always prepared excellent food. The group-of-six bhikṣus, while traveling about, constantly stayed at that household, and no other bhikṣus could obtain any. The other bhikṣus told the householder: "You make this one-meal food for the Sangha. How is it that we cannot get even one turn?" The householder answered: "I originally made this place for the Sangha, but the group-of-six bhikṣus stay and will not leave, so I no longer get to see other monks. This is their fault, not mine." The senior bhikṣus heard this and reproached the group-of-six bhikṣus in various ways. They reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, at a place that provides one meal, takes more than one meal, it is a pācittika."

At that time, Śāriputra had a wind disease. He went to a one-meal place and, after eating one meal, was about to go elsewhere. The bhikṣus said: "Elder, you are ill. There is no need to go elsewhere. We will offer you our food portions." He answered: "The World-Honored One does not permit taking more than one meal at a one-meal place." Some householders, hearing that Śāriputra was ill, also invited him to stay, and he answered as before. Thereupon Śāriputra, dragging his illness, departed. The bhikṣus thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit sick bhikṣus to take more than one meal at a one-meal place, there would not be this suffering." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit sick bhikṣus to take more than one meal at a one-meal place. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, without being ill, at a place that provides one meal, takes more than one meal, it is a pācittika."

At a one-meal place, if many bhikṣus arrive together in the evening, and the donor offers non-time drinks or foot-anointing oil, they are permitted to receive them in turn. The next morning, they should receive food in order. If there is none, they should leave after one meal. If the donor asks them to stay, they are permitted to stay. If they have left and need to return due to some matter, they should inform the host. If the host permits them to stay, they may stay; if not, they should leave. If a later-arriving bhikṣu receives one meal and there is food remaining, it should be given to the bhikṣu whom the host has asked to stay.

If many bhikṣus come and there are many one-meal places, the bhikṣus should distribute themselves among them.

If one takes more than one meal at a relative's home, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If there are various dangers and one cannot leave, it is not an offense.

---

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, there was a woman about to return to her husband's home. She prepared various cakes as provisions for the journey. A bhikṣu, going door to door begging for food, came to her house. The woman asked: "What do you need?" He answered: "I need food!" She asked again: "Can you eat khādanīya food?" He answered: "I can!" She then filled his bowl with various cakes and gave them to him. After that bhikṣu received them, he told the other bhikṣus: "Such-and-such a household has much excellent food. You should go and beg there." When the bhikṣus heard this, they all went to beg, and all the cooked food was given away completely.

At that time, the woman's husband's family sent someone to summon her. She answered: "My travel provisions are not yet prepared!" She then prepared various foods again. This happened three times: each time bhikṣus came to beg and she gave everything away. The husband's family sent three summons, and each time she answered the same way. They became very angry, thinking she had other intentions, and sent a messenger saying: "I have already sought another wife. I no longer want your blind daughter!" Thereupon the woman's family was all angry and resentful, saying: "It is because of the Śākya śramaṇas that our daughter is widowed!" They further cursed with various foul words, overflowing from their mouths. Neighbors said to them: "The husband treated your daughter poorly. What does that have to do with the śramaṇas?" They then gave the full account. Those who had no faith

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Fascicle Eight

## First Division: Pācittiya Offenses

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, the Buddha had not yet established a rule for bhikṣus against eating at improper times. Bhikṣus went begging for food in the dark of night: some fell into ditches and gullies, some bumped into women, some encountered bandits who robbed them, and some were injured by insects and wild beasts. Eating without any regulation of time, they neglected the cultivation of the holy life.

At that time, Kālodāyin, wearing robes of mixed colors, with a dark face and red eyes, went begging for food in the darkness. A pregnant woman saw him by a flash of lightning and cried out in great alarm: "Piśāca! Piśāca!" Kālodāyin said: "I am a śramaṇa begging for food, not a piśāca." She bitterly scolded him, saying: "Why don't you cut open your belly with a knife, rather than beg for food in the pitch darkness of night! Other śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas are satisfied with one meal. How is it that you eat with no regard for day or night?" The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reproached him in various ways, then reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked Kālodāyin: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "I truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu eats at an improper time, it is a pācittiya."

At that time, there was a bhikṣu who had taken emetic and purgative medicine. He was unable to eat in time, and his stomach felt empty and distressed. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Apply ghee to his body." He still did not recover. The Buddha said: "Apply barley flour to his body." He still did not recover. The Buddha said: "Apply ghee mixed with barley flour to his body." He still did not recover. The Buddha said: "Bathe and wash him with warm water." He still did not recover. The Buddha said: "Give him warm water to drink." He still did not recover. The Buddha said: "Fill a basin with rich meat broth and have him sit in it. With such measures as these, one may persist until dawn, but under no circumstances may one eat past the proper time."

"Improper time" means: from directly after midday until the time when the dawn light has not yet appeared; this is called "improper time."

If a bhikṣu, at an improper time, perceives it as an improper time or has doubt about whether it is an improper time; or at an improper time perceives it as a proper time, all are pācittiya. At a proper time, if he perceives it as an improper time or has doubt about whether it is a proper time, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, the World-Honored One had not yet established a rule for bhikṣus against eating food kept overnight. There was a shrine to a deity that was a place of entertainment, and crowds of people vied in bringing fine foods to observe the festivities there. Sometimes staying one or two nights, the delicacies were abundant and plentiful. The bhikṣus obtained food there but could not finish it all. It accumulated in their rooms, filling every space, attracting insects and rats that gnawed through walls and damaged buildings. Lay people saw this and asked: "Who accumulated all this food?" Some people said: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan!" They all criticized and reproached them: "These bald-headed ones only know how to greedily accept things, without regard for waste and loss! They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!"

There was also an āraṇyaka bhikṣu dwelling in a forest hermitage who thought: "Begging for food every day hinders and obstructs the practice of the Way." So he collected various foods all at once, either storing them as they were or drying them in the sun. When other bhikṣus came traveling and saw this, they said to him: "Has not the World-Honored One said that robes need only cover the body and food need only sustain life? Why do you accumulate so much food and spread it about drying in the sun in such disorder?" That bhikṣu said: "This place is far from the village. Begging for food every day hinders and obstructs the practice of the Way. Therefore I collect it all at once to put an end to the trouble." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked that bhikṣu: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "I truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu eats food kept overnight, it is a pācittiya."

"Food kept overnight" means: food that has already been received and is kept overnight; this is called "food kept overnight." If one eats such food, each mouthful is a pācittiya.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, a famine afflicted this country and food was hard to obtain through begging. The twenty-eight yakṣa generals came to the Buddha's dwelling, prostrated at his feet, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The world now suffers famine. We wish the Buddha would travel among the people. We shall transform the minds of the people and cause them to give rise to wholesome thoughts." The World-Honored One silently consented. Then the Four Heavenly Kings, Śakra Devānām Indra, and Brahmā, lord of the Sahā world, also came to the Buddha, prostrated at his feet, stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha as above. The Buddha also silently consented.

Thereupon the World-Honored One departed from Rājagṛha together with a great Sangha of bhikṣus, twelve hundred and fifty in number. There were also five hundred bhikṣuṇīs, five hundred upāsakas, and five hundred upāsikās, all traveling together through the kingdom of Magadha. Additionally, there were a thousand male and female followers of other paths, as well as five hundred beggars, all following behind the Buddha, seeking leftover food. Wherever the World-Honored One went, people exhausted their means in making offerings. Those who came from the four directions bringing offerings could not even get the chance to present them. Food and drink were abundant and plentiful, with much left over. Each of the bhikṣus gave the remainder of their food to the male and female followers of other paths and to the beggars. Lay people saw this and criticized them, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, receive everything they can get, and what they cannot finish eating, they give to those of wrong views! They are not even as good as those who milk cows, who know to save some for the calves!" Others also said: "If the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, make offerings even to followers of other paths, why should we not serve them as well?"

Thereupon the World-Honored One proceeded to the town of Anāthapiṇḍada. At that time, there was a great brāhmaṇa named Śramaṇa, who with five hundred carts heavily loaded with food and drink followed the Buddha. For over five months he sought his turn to make an offering, but was never able to get one. His household sent a message urging: "The farming season is about to pass. You should return to attend to our livelihood." The brāhmaṇa went to Ānanda and said: "I have five hundred carts loaded with food and drink, wishing to make an offering to the Buddha and the Sangha. I have followed the Buddha for over five months but have never been able to present it. A message from my household urges me to return, and I cannot stay longer. I wish to scatter the food provisions along the road so that the Buddha and the Sangha may tread upon them as they pass, and my long-held wish would thus be fulfilled." Ānanda replied: "I shall report this to the World-Honored One." He then reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha said to Ānanda: "You may take the brāhmaṇa to inspect the household that is providing the food offering. Whatever they lack, instruct him to prepare it." Ānanda received the instruction and took the brāhmaṇa to inspect the household providing the food offering. He saw that there was no congee, nor fried cakes with oil and honey. The brāhmaṇa then made seven kinds of congee and two kinds of cakes. Early the next morning, he reported to the Buddha that the cakes and congee were ready. The Buddha said to Ānanda: "You should help distribute them." Ānanda received the instruction and helped distribute the congee and cakes. At that time, there was a female follower of another path holding a small girl. Ānanda, seeing that she had a child, gave her an extra two cakes. The followers of other paths said: "This bhikṣu is attached to this woman, which is why he gave her an extra two cakes." Others said: "He only did so because she was holding a child; it was not favoritism." They quarreled noisily, and the seating area fell into disorder. When Ānanda saw this, he felt uneasy in his mind, so he went to the Buddha and reported the entire matter, including the previous events, to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked Ānanda: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "I truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu gives food to followers of other paths, whether male or female, it is a pācittiya."

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who gave food to naked ascetics of other paths. One of them wrapped a bundle of cakes and entered Rājagṛha. People asked him: "Where have you come from?" He answered: "I have come from among the bald-headed householders." They asked again: "What is in your bundle?" He answered: "Honey-fried cakes." They asked again: "From whom did you receive them?" He answered: "The householders regard the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, as a field of merit, and the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, in turn regard me as a field of merit. I received them from them." People then criticized and reproached them: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, do not know moderation; they greedily accept without satiety! We reduce the portions meant for our wives, children, and our own sustenance to make these offerings, and they give them to these people of wrong views and evil conduct! If we should make offerings to such people, we would do so ourselves. Why do we need the śramaṇas as intermediaries?" The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reproached them in various ways, then reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "We truly did, World-Honored One." The Buddha reproached them in various ways: "Whether clothed or unclothed, what difference does it make?" After reproaching them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu gives food to naked ascetics of other paths, whether male or female, it is a pācittiya."

There were followers of other paths who came begging at mealtimes. The bhikṣus did not dare give them food, and the followers of other paths angrily cursed them, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, teach people to practice generosity, yet they themselves are stingy! What kind of path is this?" When devout and faithful lay people heard this, they also criticized and reproached them: "How is it that these śramaṇas always beg from others, but never give to others?" There were also bhikṣus whose relatives among the followers of other paths came begging, but they also did not dare give. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to give food to followers of other paths, but they must not give it with their own hands. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu gives food with his own hand to naked ascetics of other paths, whether male or female, it is a pācittiya."

If a follower of another path comes begging, one should take a morsel from one's own portion, place it in a separate spot, and let them take it themselves. One should not give Sangha food. If beggars, stray dogs, or birds come seeking food, one should estimate how much of one's own food to take as a portion, then reduce it to give to them. One must not give beyond one's own portion.

For śrāmaṇeras, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one's parents are followers of other paths and one gives to them with one's own hand, there is no offense.

---

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, some lay people came to the monastery and asked the bhikṣus: "How many people are in the Sangha?" The bhikṣus said: "The Sangha has such-and-such number of people." The lay people said: "We wish to invite the entire Sangha tomorrow. Please come and accept our humble meal." The group-of-six bhikṣus said to them: "If you provide us with milk, curds, ghee, oil, fish, and meat, we will accept your invitation." The people answered: "We will have to borrow or purchase these items to prepare them." After saying this, they each returned home. Some were unable to borrow, and some could not purchase what was needed. The next morning, the food was prepared, and it was announced: "The time has come." The entire Sangha put on their robes, took their bowls, went to their homes, and sat down in their seats. Water was served and food was distributed. The group-of-six bhikṣus said: "Why is there no milk, curds, ghee, oil, fish, or meat?" They answered: "We could not borrow them, and could not buy them." The group-of-six bhikṣus then overturned their bowls and left. The lay people all said: "These monks, not getting fine food, overturn their bowls and leave! Are they kings? Are they great ministers? Those who leave the household life do so to seek liberation; begging food should be enough. How is it that they instead crave fine flavors? They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reproached them in various ways, then reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "We truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu goes to a lay person's home and requests milk, curds, ghee, oil, fish, or meat, it is a pācittiya."

Afterward, bhikṣus who obtained fine food did not dare eat it, or after eating it, confessed and repented the offense. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and told the bhikṣus: "If one does not request fine food but obtains it on one's own and eats it, committing a pācittiya is impossible. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu goes to lay people's homes and requests such fine foods as milk, curds, ghee, oil, fish, or meat, and obtains and eats them, it is a pācittiya."

There were sick bhikṣus whom doctors instructed to eat fine food. The bhikṣus said: "The Buddha does not permit us to request such food. How can we obtain it?" They thought: "If the Buddha would permit us to request this food, our illness would be cured." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit sick bhikṣus to request fine food. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If in households there are such fine foods as milk, curds, ghee, oil, fish, and meat, and a bhikṣu who is not sick requests them for himself, and obtains and eats them, it is a pācittiya."

If one requests for a sick bhikṣu, or requests from the home of relatives, or from the home of acquaintances, there is no offense.

For śrāmaṇeras, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda regularly received offerings from a brāhmaṇa. Later, putting on his robe and taking his bowl, he went to the brāhmaṇa's home. The brāhmaṇa was not there, and he sat alone with the brāhmaṇa's wife, talking together. At that time, the brāhmaṇa's desire arose, and he returned home midway. Seeing Upananda talking with his wife, he thought: "This bhikṣu is now teaching the Dharma; this is not a time for sensual activity." So he went back outside. But his desire grew stronger, and after a short while he entered again. Upananda still had not left. Again he thought as before, suppressed himself and went out. After another short while, he entered again and said to his wife: "Quickly give the bhikṣu his food and send him on his way. Do not obstruct his practice of the Way!" The wife understood his meaning and, not wanting her husband to abandon the path and follow desire, answered: "Let the bhikṣu eat at his leisure. What is so urgent?" This happened three times, yet Upananda still refused to leave. The brāhmaṇa then said to him: "We lay people have many concerns, and during free moments we should attend to marital matters. You do not know the appropriate time and stubbornly refuse to leave. Who would believe that your kind practices the holy life?" The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reproached him in various ways, then reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked Upananda: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "I truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu sits with a woman in a home where a couple wishes to eat together, it is a pācittiya."

"Eating" means: a man and woman who affectionately wish to dine together.

"Sitting" means: knowing that one is obstructing their affair, yet deliberately sitting.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If many people are sitting together, if there is a partition or barrier, or if it is a day when the husband and wife have taken the eight precepts, there is no offense.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda regularly received offerings from a brāhmaṇa. Later, he went to his home. The brāhmaṇa was not there, and he sat with the brāhmaṇa's wife in a secluded place. The brāhmaṇa returned and said to his wife: "You sat with the bhikṣu in a secluded place. I truly have no suspicion, but if people see this, they will certainly say you have done something wrong, bringing disgrace to the śramaṇa and shame to our household!" He also said to Upananda: "I truly do not doubt the Venerable One, but the Venerable One should not be careless about such an evil reputation!" The wife, relying on the power of her beauty, the power of having many children, and the power of her skill in managing wealth, scolded her husband: "If I sit with a bhikṣu, it certainly will not bring trouble upon you! You need not speak such excessive words!" The brāhmaṇa became greatly enraged and rebuked Upananda: "Even we lay people do not sit alone in seclusion with another man's wife. Yet you śramaṇas do such things? You have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reproached him in various ways, then reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked Upananda: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "I truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu sits alone with a woman in a secluded place, it is a pācittiya."

For śrāmaṇeras, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda regularly received offerings from a brāhmaṇa. Later, he went to his home. The brāhmaṇa was not there, and he sat with the brāhmaṇa's wife alone in an open place. And so forth, up to the Buddha reproaching him in various ways, all as stated in the section on sitting in a secluded place. He told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu sits alone with a woman in an open place, it is a pācittiya."

For śrāmaṇeras, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, there was a matter at the border, and King Prasenajit marshaled the four kinds of troops, intending to go and suppress the rebellion. The group-of-six bhikṣus said to one another: "I have heard that when a consecrated king goes on a military campaign, the military array and adornments are magnificent. We have never seen this; let us go and watch." So they went to the roadside. The soldiers of the vanguard saw them and all said angrily: "How is it that today we see these inauspicious people? We dislike seeing such people even at home, and now on a military march we cannot avoid them! If the king did not revere and have faith in them, we would cut off their heads!" When the king saw the group-of-six bhikṣus from afar, he too was displeased. He sent a person to ask: "Venerable Ones, why are you here?" They answered: "We heard that when a consecrated king sends out his army, the military display is magnificent. We have never seen it, so we came to watch." The king thought: "Who can report this to the Buddha?" He then thought again: "Whether or not it is reported, the Buddha will know by himself." He then took rock sugar and dried ginger and entrusted them to the group-of-six bhikṣus, saying: "Venerable Ones, please carry these and present them to the World-Honored One, with my boundless respect." After the entire army had passed, they each returned to their dwellings. They presented the king's gifts to the Buddha and said: "The king sends his boundless respect." The Buddha asked the group-of-six: "How did you come to see the king?" They answered with the full account. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and reproached the group-of-six bhikṣus in various ways: "You are foolish! What you did was unlawful! When an army sets out on the march, seeing śramaṇas is considered inauspicious. The soldiers must have been angry and resentful, and the king was displeased with you, which is why he sent the gifts to me." After reproaching them, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu watches an army set out on the march, it is a pācittiya."

If one gives rise to the intention to watch and makes preparations, with each step taken on the way, if one sees but does not hear, or hears but does not see, all are duṣkṛta. If one both hears and sees, and distinguishes the military array and adornments, whether the front or rear, all are pācittiya.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If while traveling on the road or at a walking-meditation place one happens to see the army and does not stop to watch, there is no offense.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, followers of other paths made this plan: "Now King Prasenajit and the great ministers who have faith in the Dharma are all away. Taking advantage of the fact that no one can obstruct us, let us combine our strength and dig a channel through the Jetavana to divert water through it." The bhikṣus heard of this and said to the upāsakas and upāsikās: "You should go and report to the king! This is beyond our power to prevent." It happened that the king and the great ministers Ilāśvadatta, Pūrṇa, Sudatta, and others, having not seen the bhikṣus for a long time, sent messengers to invite them. The bhikṣus discussed: "If the Buddha permits us to go to the military camp, they will surely not allow the followers of other paths to dig a channel through the Jetavana." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now permit the bhikṣus to go to the military camp." The bhikṣus obeyed and accepted the king's invitation. After arriving, they reported the entire matter to the king. The king immediately issued a decree: "Anyone who digs a channel through the Jetavana shall be executed along with their entire clan." Afterward, he provided various kinds of offerings for the bhikṣus. Having obtained fine food and provisions, the bhikṣus did not wish to return. The soldiers criticized and reproached them: "This is not a forest hermitage. We lay people cannot avoid being here; why should the śramaṇas also remain?" Others also said: "These bhikṣus do not truly have faith in and delight in the Buddha's Dharma; wherever they get fine food, they stay! They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reproached them in various ways, then reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "We truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, having a reason, goes to the military camp, he may stay at most two or three nights. If he exceeds this, it is a pācittiya."

Even though there is a reason, if the matter can be settled by sending a letter or message, one should send a letter or message. Only if one must go in person may one go. When the matter is concluded, one should return immediately and must not stay overnight. If the matter is not concluded, one may stay one night. If not concluded after one night, one may stay a second night. If still not concluded, one may stay a third night. Whether the matter is concluded or not, if one exceeds three nights, it is a pācittiya.

If the matter is concluded immediately, one should not stay overnight. If one stays overnight, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, bhikṣus went to the military camp and stayed two or three nights, watching the troops take up arms and form battle formations. When the battle commenced, the warriors saw them and said with great anger: "These inauspicious people have come here again! If the king did not have faith and delight in the Buddha's Dharma, we would first cut off their heads and then attack the enemy!" In the chaos of battle, they shot arrows at the bhikṣus, injuring many of them. The bhikṣus carried and bore the wounded back to their dwellings. People on the road saw them and also said with great anger: "Such śramaṇas deserve to be shot! Why did they go to watch the battle formation for no reason? They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reproached them in various ways, then reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "We truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, having a reason, goes to the military camp and stays two or three nights, and watches the military formations and battles, it is a pācittiya."

If one watches birds or beasts fighting, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Ariṣṭa who gave rise to an evil, wrong view, saying: "As I understand the Buddha's teaching, the things the Buddha calls obstructions to the path do not actually obstruct the path." The bhikṣus asked him: "Did you truly say, 'As I understand the Buddha's teaching, the things the Buddha calls obstructions to the path do not actually obstruct the path'?" He answered: "Yes!" The bhikṣus asked again: "How do you come to such an understanding?" He answered: "Here there are the two elders Citta and Sudatta, as well as various upāsakas, who all dwell amid the five sensual pleasures, swallowed by desires, burned by desires, and yet they have attained the fruits of stream-entry, once-returning, and non-returning. Furthermore, followers of other paths who have not abandoned their original views but have gone forth in the true Dharma also attain the four fruits of the śramaṇa. Therefore I hold this understanding." The bhikṣus, wishing to make him abandon this evil, wrong view, admonished him: "Do not say such things! Do not slander the Buddha! Do not misrepresent the Buddha! The Buddha has not said such things. You should abandon this evil, wrong view! The Buddha has, through various skillful means, reproached desires, saying that desires are like a pile of red-hot bones, and so forth, up to saying they are like poison. If one indulges in the five sensual pleasures, being burned by desires, confused by thoughts of desire; or if followers of other paths who have not abandoned their original views attain the four fruits of the śramaṇa; such things are impossible!" The bhikṣus admonished him in this way, but he only increased in his wrong view and stubbornly refused to abandon it. The bhikṣus then went to Śāriputra and said: "Venerable One, Ariṣṭa holds such a wrong view." They explained everything as above. "Out of compassion, please admonish and persuade him to abandon it!" Śāriputra silently consented and went to Ariṣṭa's place. They exchanged greetings, and he sat to one side. He asked: "Did you truly say such things?" He answered: "I truly did." He explained further in detail as before. Śāriputra admonished him: "Do not say such things! What you have said is contrary to the Dharma and contrary to the Vinaya!" He reproached him in various ways, but that bhikṣu stubbornly refused to abandon his view. Śāriputra rose from his seat, went to the Buddha, and reported everything in detail to the Buddha: "We humbly beg the World-Honored One, out of compassion, to instruct this bhikṣu to abandon his evil, wrong view!" The Buddha then ordered a bhikṣu: "Go and summon Ariṣṭa!" The bhikṣu obeyed and went to tell him: "The Teacher summons you." Ariṣṭa immediately came, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and stood to one side. The Buddha asked Ariṣṭa: "Did you truly say such things?" He answered: "I truly did, World-Honored One." The Buddha asked again: "How do you understand my teaching?" He answered just as before. The Buddha said: "You foolish person! Do not slander the Tathāgata! Do not misrepresent the Tathāgata! Why? I have said that things that obstruct the path truly do obstruct the path! If one indulges in the five sensual pleasures, burned by the fire of desires, confused by thoughts of desire; or if followers of other paths who have not abandoned their original views attain the four fruits of the śramaṇa; such things are impossible." The World-Honored One spoke to him, but he still would not abandon his view. The Buddha further said: "I have already seen that you hold this evil, wrong view! This constitutes slandering me, this constitutes misrepresenting me! Return to your dwelling; I shall separately question the other bhikṣus." After Ariṣṭa left, the Buddha, wishing to make clear that bhikṣu's evil, wrong view, asked the bhikṣus: "Do you understand my teaching the way Ariṣṭa does?" The bhikṣus said: "We do not understand it that way." He asked again: "How do you understand it?" They answered: "The Buddha has said that things that obstruct the path truly do obstruct the path." They explained further in detail as above. The Buddha said: "Excellent! You have well understood my intention." He told the bhikṣus: "You should send one bhikṣu who is on friendly terms with Ariṣṭa to admonish him in private with gentle words: 'Do not say such things! Do not slander the Buddha! Do not misrepresent the Buddha! The Buddha has said that things that obstruct the path truly do obstruct the path. You should abandon this evil, wrong view!' If he abandons it, good. If he does not abandon it, several bhikṣus should go and admonish him as above. If he abandons it, good. If he does not abandon it, the Sangha should go and admonish him likewise. If he abandons it, good." The bhikṣus received the instruction and, after these three rounds, he stubbornly refused to abandon his view. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account, reproached Ariṣṭa from afar, and told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu says: 'As I understand the Buddha's teaching, the things the Buddha calls obstructions to the path cannot obstruct the path,' the bhikṣus should say to this bhikṣu: 'Do not say such things! Do not slander the Buddha! Do not misrepresent the Buddha! The Buddha has said that things that obstruct the path truly do obstruct the path. You should abandon this evil, wrong view!' If admonished thus and he stubbornly refuses to abandon it, he should be admonished a second and third time. If after the second and third admonition he abandons the matter, good. If he does not abandon it, it is a pācittiya."

If that bhikṣu does not abandon the evil, wrong view, and the bhikṣus, having either seen or heard of it, should send one bhikṣu to admonish him in private. If he abandons it, he should confess one duṣkṛta in repentance. If he does not abandon it, several bhikṣus should go and admonish him. If he abandons it, he should confess two duṣkṛtas in repentance. If he does not abandon it, the Sangha should go and admonish him. If he abandons it, he should confess three duṣkṛtas in repentance. If he does not abandon it, the Sangha should admonish him with a motion-and-three-proclamations karman.

One bhikṣu proclaims: "Let the Sangha listen! The bhikṣu so-and-so has given rise to an evil, wrong view and says: 'As I understand the Buddha's teaching, things that obstruct the path cannot obstruct the path.' The Sangha has admonished him: 'Do not say such things! Do not slander the Buddha! Do not misrepresent the Buddha! The Buddha has said that things that obstruct the path truly do obstruct the path!' The Sangha has admonished him thus, but he stubbornly refuses to abandon it. The Sangha now admonishes him by karman. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

After the motion, he should be told: "The Sangha has completed the motion. You may now abandon this evil, wrong view." If he abandons it, he should confess four duṣkṛtas in repentance.

If he does not abandon it, it should further be proclaimed: "Let the Sangha listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so says: 'As I understand the Buddha's teaching, things that obstruct the path cannot obstruct the path.' The Sangha has admonished him: 'Do not say such things! Do not slander the Buddha! Do not misrepresent the Buddha! The Buddha has said that things that obstruct the path truly do obstruct the path!' The Sangha has admonished him thus, but he stubbornly refuses to abandon it. The Sangha now admonishes him by karman. Let those senior ones who consent remain silent. Let those who do not consent speak."

He should again be told: "The Sangha has completed the first proclamation of the karman. You may now abandon this evil, wrong view." If he abandons it, he should confess five duṣkṛtas in repentance.

If he does not abandon it, the second proclamation should be made as above. After the second proclamation is completed, he should again be told: "The Sangha has completed the second proclamation of the karman. You may now abandon this evil, wrong view." If he abandons it, he should confess six duṣkṛtas in repentance.

If he does not abandon it, the third proclamation should be made. If he abandons it before the third proclamation is completed, he should also confess six duṣkṛtas in repentance. When the third proclamation of the karman is completed, whether he abandons it or not, it is a pācittiya.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, there were bhikṣus who knew that Ariṣṭa had not abandoned his evil, wrong view, yet sat with him, spoke with him, lodged with him, and shared undertakings with him. Other bhikṣus reproached them, saying: "Ariṣṭa has not abandoned his evil, wrong view, and the Sangha has already performed the karman. How can you sit with him, speak with him, lodge with him, and share undertakings with him?" The bhikṣus who heard this paid no heed. The senior bhikṣus reproached them in various ways and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "We truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If bhikṣus know that a bhikṣu has not made a proper confession and has not abandoned his evil, wrong view, and yet sit with him, speak with him, lodge with him, and share undertakings with him, it is a pācittiya."

For each period of extended conversation, each utterance is a pācittiya. For each sitting together, each instance of sitting is a pācittiya. For each lodging together, each night of lodging is a pācittiya. For each shared undertaking, each instance of shared undertaking is a pācittiya.

Even if he has abandoned the evil, wrong view, as long as the Sangha has not dissolved the karman, it is also a pācittiya. If he holds an evil, wrong view but the Sangha has not yet performed the karman, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one does not know, or if the karman was not properly performed, there is no offense.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda had two śrāmaṇeras who gave rise to evil, wrong views, saying: "As I understand the Buddha's teaching, indulging in the five sensual pleasures cannot obstruct the path." And so forth, up to Śāriputra reporting it to the Buddha, as stated in the section on Ariṣṭa's evil, wrong view. The Buddha then ordered a bhikṣu: "Go and summon those two śrāmaṇeras." The bhikṣu obeyed and went to tell them: "The Teacher summons you." The two śrāmaṇeras immediately came, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and stood to one side. The Buddha asked the two śrāmaṇeras: "Did you truly say such things?" They answered: "We truly did, World-Honored One." He further asked the śrāmaṇeras: "How do you understand my teaching?" The śrāmaṇeras answered just as Ariṣṭa had said. The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "These śrāmaṇeras should be summoned before the Sangha and instructed to abandon their view. They should be instructed a second and third time. If they abandon it, good. If they do not abandon it, the Sangha should perform a motion-and-three-proclamations karman to expel them."

One bhikṣu proclaims: "Let the Sangha listen! The śrāmaṇera so-and-so does not abandon his evil, wrong view. The Sangha now performs an expulsion. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

Further proclaim: "Let the Sangha listen! The śrāmaṇera so-and-so does not abandon his evil, wrong view. The Sangha now performs an expulsion. Let those senior ones who consent remain silent. Let those who do not consent speak." Likewise for the second and third time.

"The Sangha has performed the expulsion of śrāmaṇera so-and-so. The Sangha consents, as shown by its silence. The matter is thus held."

After the Sangha had expelled those two śrāmaṇeras, they went wandering among the people. At that time, Upananda kept those śrāmaṇeras, speaking with them and lodging with them. The bhikṣus told him: "The Sangha has already performed the karman to expel these śrāmaṇeras. You must not keep them, speak with them, or lodge with them!" Upananda said: "These are my elder śrāmaṇeras. If I do not look after them, who should? Who can protect the orphaned and suffering and bring them to peace and happiness?" The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reproached him in various ways, then reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked Upananda: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "I truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a śrāmaṇera says: 'As I understand the Buddha's teaching, indulging in the five sensual pleasures cannot obstruct the path,' the bhikṣus should say to this śrāmaṇera: 'Do not say such things! Do not slander the Buddha! Do not misrepresent the Buddha! The Buddha has said that the five sensual pleasures obstruct the path and truly do obstruct the path. You, śrāmaṇera, should abandon this evil, wrong view!' If instructed thus and he stubbornly refuses to abandon it, he should be instructed a second and third time. If after the second and third instruction he abandons the matter, good. If he does not abandon it, the bhikṣus should say to this śrāmaṇera: 'Get out! From now on, do not say the Buddha is my teacher! Do not walk behind the bhikṣus! As other śrāmaṇeras are permitted to share lodging with bhikṣus for two nights, you also no longer have this privilege. Foolish one, get out! Vanish! Do not dwell here!' If a bhikṣu knows that a śrāmaṇera has been lawfully expelled, and yet keeps him, employs him, dwells with him, or speaks with him, it is a pācittiya."

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the elder Kālodāyin, because he did not like seeing bad bhikṣus, also did not like seeing crows. Some lay people came with slingshots to visit. At that time, a flock of crows was gathered on the rooftop. He said to them: "These crows possess the ten qualities of a bad bhikṣu: first, stinginess; second, coveting what belongs to others; third, shamelessness; fourth, endurance of insults; fifth, filthiness; sixth, lack of compassion; seventh, hoping and expecting; eighth, insatiability; ninth, hoarding; tenth, forgetfulness. These crows possess these ten qualities. Do you want to kill them?" Those who did not believe in karmic retribution answered: "Yes!" He then took their slingshots and asked the people: "Where shall I aim?" Some said: "Aim at the left eye!" He hit the left eye and the crow died. Others said: "Aim at the right eye!" He hit the right eye and the crow died. In this manner, in a short time, he killed up to several dozen. Those who did not have faith in or delight in the Buddha's Dharma criticized and reproached him: "These śramaṇas always speak of compassion and caring for all living beings, yet now they slaughter them without conscience. They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reproached him in various ways, then reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked Kālodāyin: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "I truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu takes the life of an animal, it is a pācittiya."

There were bhikṣus who, while traveling on the road, or pulling loads, or smoking their bowls, accidentally killed various insects. They all felt ashamed, and some confessed and repented the offense. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and told the bhikṣus: "If one accidentally kills a living being, committing a pācittiya is impossible. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu intentionally takes the life of an animal, it is a pācittiya."

"Animal" means: excluding nāgas, all other animals.

"Intentionally taking life" means: having the prior intention to kill, and then ending its life.

If one takes the life of an animal, for each one killed, it is a separate pācittiya.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus came to the dwelling place of the group-of-six bhikṣus. The group-of-six bhikṣus thought: "These bhikṣus have a sense of shame, are of few desires, and are content. Now that they have come here, they will surely see our faults. We should devise a means to make them have doubt and remorse. Once they have doubt and remorse, they will surely return to their teachers." Having thought this, they said to them: "Did you properly receive the full ordination? Was there a proper boundary for the ordination? Was the karman performed properly? Have you not committed any pārājika, saṃghāvaśeṣa, pācittiya, prātideśanīya, or duṣkṛta offenses? Do you well guard your body and speech? Did your upādhyāya and ācārya first properly receive the full ordination, and so forth, up to well guarding body and speech?" They answered: "We do not know about ourselves, nor do we know about our upādhyāya and ācārya. We now ask the Venerable Ones; please tell us. When we received the full ordination, and when our upādhyāya and ācārya did, was everything properly done?" They answered: "If we speak truthfully, you surely will not believe us. You should ask those whom you trust." The group-of-seventeen bhikṣus then went to their teachers and asked about these matters. Their teachers answered: "Everything about your ordination was proper, and when I received ordination in the past, it was likewise. Who caused you to have such doubt and remorse?" They answered: "The group-of-six bhikṣus." The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reproached them in various ways, then reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "We truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu causes another bhikṣu to have doubt and remorse, it is a pācittiya."

There were bhikṣus who had committed offenses and, feeling doubt and remorse in their minds, asked other bhikṣus: "Having committed such-and-such a matter, what kind of offense does one incur?" The bhikṣus thought: "The Buddha has established a precept that one must not cause another bhikṣu to have doubt and remorse." So they answered: "We do not know." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and told the bhikṣus: "If, not intending to cause doubt and remorse, one tells someone about the offense they committed, committing a pācittiya is impossible. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu intentionally causes a bhikṣu to have doubt and remorse, thinking: 'Let this bhikṣu be troubled even for a short time,' it is a pācittiya."

"Doubt and remorse" means: doubt about one's birth, doubt about one's ordination, doubt about having transgressed the precepts, doubt about one's robes.

If one causes a bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, or śrāmaṇerī to have doubt and remorse, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a bhikṣuṇī causes a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī to have doubt and remorse, it is a pācittiya. If she causes a śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, or śrāmaṇerī to have doubt and remorse, it is a duṣkṛta.

For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, causing any of the five assemblies to have doubt and remorse, all are duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus, having influence, obstructed the Sangha's karmans and the dissolution of karmans. When the Sangha did not comply, they rose and left. Then they went to the bhikṣu who had been subjected to the karman and said: "Do not worry! I have already obstructed the Sangha's karman on your behalf. Since the Sangha did not comply with me, I rose and left; therefore the karman is invalid." They then went to the bhikṣu whose karman was being dissolved and said: "Do not think the Sangha has dissolved your karman! When the Sangha was dissolving the karman, I had already obstructed it. Since the Sangha did not comply with me, I rose and left; therefore the dissolution of the karman is invalid. You should now seek the Sangha to dissolve it again." The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reproached them in various ways, then reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "We truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, while the Sangha is deliberating on a matter, rises and leaves, it is a pācittiya."

At that time, some bhikṣus had urgent matters and needed to leave but did not dare. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus who have urgent matters to rise and leave after giving their consent. To give consent, one should say to one person: 'Venerable One, bear this single-mindedly in mind! The Sangha is now deliberating on a matter. I, bhikṣu so-and-so, give my consent in this proper Sangha matter.' From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, while the Sangha is deliberating on a matter, rises and leaves without giving his consent, it is a pācittiya."

"The Sangha deliberating on a matter" means: a motion karman, a motion-and-one-proclamation karman, or a motion-and-three-proclamations karman.

If the karman is performed under a roof, each time one goes outside, each departure is a pācittiya.

If the karman is performed in the open air, and one departs beyond one fathom from the face of the Sangha, it is a pācittiya.

If a person with supernatural powers departs, rising four finger-breadths above the ground, it is a pācittiya.

If the Sangha deliberates on a matter without performing a karman and one leaves, it is a duṣkṛta. If one comes and leaves during a private room deliberation, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise.

If the Sangha deliberates without performing a karman, or deliberates in a private room, a śrāmaṇera may be present. If he rises and leaves, it is a duṣkṛta. For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is likewise.

If the Sangha's karman is not properly performed and one leaves without giving consent, there is no offense.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus came to the dwelling place of the group-of-six bhikṣus, and they tickled one another in play. One bhikṣu was tickled by the group and, unable to stop laughing, lost his breath and died. The group-of-seventeen bhikṣus wept over him. The senior bhikṣus asked: "Why are you weeping?" They answered: "A bhikṣu died from playing and laughing; that is why we weep." They asked again: "How did this happen?" They answered: "We were tickling one another, and he could not stop laughing, and his breath ceased." The bhikṣus reproached them in various ways and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus on this account and asked the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "We truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu tickles another bhikṣu, it is a pācittiya."

If a bhikṣu tickles a śrāmaṇera, or even an animal, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus went to the Aciravatī River to fetch water. While there, they bathed and played in the water, diving, splashing, and dousing one another. At that time, King Prasenajit and Queen Mallikā ascended a tower and saw them from afar. The king said to the queen: "Look at your field of merit!" The queen said to the king: "The Buddha has not yet established a rule about this. They are young and have gone forth without yet understanding the Dharma. May the king not, on seeing this, lose faith and reverence toward the other bhikṣus, and thereby suffer for a long night!" After the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus finished their various games, they stood up in the water and put on their robes. The queen said to the king: "Your Majesty, please watch my field of merit!" After they put on their robes, one of them threw a water vessel into the air, flew after it, passed over the tower, like a king of geese. The queen again said to the king: "Look further at my field of merit." The king was greatly delighted, and his faith and reverence increased further. Then the queen instructed the brāhmaṇa Nāriṅga: "Go to the Buddha and report this matter to the Buddha." He obeyed and went. The Buddha taught him the Dharma, instructing, benefiting, and gladdening him, and then sent him back. On this account the Buddha gathered the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "We truly did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu plays in the water, it is a pācittiya."

If one plays in the water, or even with water held in a vessel, dousing one another, all are pācittiya.

If one makes snowballs, or plays with dewdrops on grass tips, all are duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one does not do it for play, there is no offense in any case.

---

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the World-Honored One had not yet established a rule for bhikṣus against sharing a room overnight with a woman. Sometimes one bhikṣu and one woman; sometimes many bhikṣus and few women; sometimes few bhikṣus and many women shared a room overnight. They developed attachment, and some returned to lay life, while others joined other paths. Lay people saw this and criticized them: "These śramaṇas share a room overnight with women. How are they different from lay people? They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!"

At that time, there was a young widow whose husband had died. She thought: "Where shall I now find another good match?" She then thought: "I cannot go door to door searching. I should build a guesthouse and let both householders and renunciants stay freely. Among them I shall choose." She then built it, and announced on the roads that those needing lodging could stay there.

At that time, Aniruddha arrived at that village in the evening and asked where he might find lodging. Someone told him: "At such-and-such a house." He went and requested lodging. Aniruddha had always been handsome, and after attaining the path, his complexion was even more radiant than before. The widow saw him and thought: "I have already found a fine husband!" She pointed out a room, saying: "You may stay in there." Aniruddha entered the room and sat in the cross-legged posture. Shortly after he sat down, some merchants came seeking lodging. The widow answered: "Although I normally receive guests, I have already given the room to the bhikṣu, and it is no longer up to me." The merchants then, following the owner's words, asked Aniruddha for permission to lodge. Aniruddha said to the widow: "If it is up to me, let them all stay." The merchants entered. The widow then thought: "I should invite the bhikṣu into the inner room. If I do not do so now, there may be no future opportunity." She prepared a fine bed inside, lit a lamp, and said to Aniruddha: "Please come inside." Aniruddha entered and sat in the cross-legged posture, with his mind focused and attentive. After everyone had fallen asleep, the widow said: "Venerable One, do you know my intention in inviting you?" He answered: "Sister, your intention must surely be directed toward merit and virtue." The widow said: "That was not my original intent." She then told him her full feelings. Aniruddha said: "Sister, we must not commit such evil deeds. The World-Honored One's rules also do not permit it." The widow said: "I am of good family, in the prime of my youth, accomplished in propriety, and abundant in wealth. I wish to serve the Venerable One in all that is needed. Please accept!" Aniruddha answered as before. The widow then thought: "The only thing that seduces

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese during the Liu Song dynasty by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle 9

*Note: The source text provided for this fascicle is empty. There is no Chinese content to translate.*

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Nine

### First Division, Part Five: The Sixty-Fourth Matter

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus repeatedly violated the precepts. The other bhikṣus admonished them, up to saying "Do not suffer through the long night," all as described above. The group-of-six bhikṣus asked: "In what place did the Buddha establish this rule?" The bhikṣus said: "Do you not know?" They answered: "We do not know." The bhikṣus said: "We shall now tell you the place where the rule was established." At the time of reciting the precepts, they then told them: "The Buddha established the rule in this context." The group-of-six bhikṣus said: "Only now do I learn of this rule, recited in the Prātimokṣa at the fortnightly upoṣadha." The bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you actually do this?" They answered: "We actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, at the time of reciting the precepts, speaks thus: 'Only now do I learn of this rule, recited in the Prātimokṣa at the fortnightly upoṣadha,' and the bhikṣus know that this bhikṣu has already sat through the recitation of the precepts two or three times, that bhikṣu shall not be excused on the grounds of not knowing. He shall be dealt with according to the Dharma for whatever offense he has committed. He should be rebuked for his ignorance: 'What you have done is unwholesome! At the time of the recitation of precepts, you did not listen single-mindedly or keep it in your heart.' This is a pācittika."

When a bhikṣu gives a bhikṣu the full ordination, he should immediately teach him and explain in detail.

If one has sat through the recitation of the precepts two or three times, whether knowing or not knowing, and speaks thus, it is a pācittika.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, various bhikṣus frequently entered the palace of King Prasenajit. Seeing the beautiful women, they gave rise to thoughts of attachment, lost delight in cultivating the pure conduct, and some returned to lay life or became non-Buddhist practitioners. The great ministers, seeing this, said: "Why does the king not keep the palace women more securely hidden, allowing various men of different families to see them?"

At that time, Ānanda regularly received offerings from the king. In the early morning, he put on his robe, took his bowl, and entered the inner palace. At that time, the king and Queen Mallikā were sleeping together and had not yet risen. When the queen saw Ānanda coming, she hurriedly threw on a garment and got down from the bed. The garment she threw on was extremely fine and slippery, and without her realizing it, it slipped off, and she crouched on the ground in shame. The king then reproached him, saying: "I am busy with affairs of state and go to sleep late at night, so I cannot rise early. How is it that a bhikṣu comes straight in at early morning?" Ānanda, ashamed, immediately returned to where the Buddha was and reported in full to the Buddha both the matter of the various bhikṣus entering the palace and his own incident. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you actually do this?" They answered: "We actually did, World-Honored One." The Buddha then also related Ānanda's incident himself, rebuked them in various ways, and told the bhikṣus: "Entering the king's inner palace has ten faults: First, if the king, when intoxicated, approaches other palace women and forgets upon sobering, should one of them become pregnant, he will surely suspect a bhikṣu. Second, palace women may jest and laugh upon seeing a bhikṣu, arousing suspicion of romantic feelings. Third, if the king has secret plans and outsiders learn of them, he will suspect it was a bhikṣu who transmitted them. Fourth, if valuables are lost within the king's palace, he will suspect a bhikṣu of taking them. Fifth, if a minister is deposed, outsiders will surely say: 'It was because of a bhikṣu!' Sixth, if someone is punished, outsiders will surely suspect a bhikṣu was behind it. Seventh, if someone who should not yet have received an official post is given one by the king, it will also be suspected to be due to a bhikṣu's influence. Eighth, if the king is fond of going out on excursions, which involves much labor and expense, this too will be attributed to a bhikṣu's doing. Ninth, within the palace there are many beautiful women and precious ornaments and clothing; a bhikṣu seeing these will surely give rise to attachment and may violate the precepts or return to lay life. Tenth, if among the princes there is one who rebels, it will surely be suspected that a bhikṣu instigated it."
