After rebuking them, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu enters the king's palace and crosses the threshold, it is a pācittika."

At that time, after the Buddha had established this precept, the bhikṣus did not dare cross the city gate threshold when begging for food and did not know what to do. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit entering the palace, but one must not cross the threshold of the inner palace. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu enters the king's palace and crosses the threshold of the inner palace, it is a pācittika."

At that time, King Prasenajit would annually travel with the palace women through the kingdom's territories, and everywhere there were detached palaces and separate pavilions. Some bhikṣus arrived at villages in the evening seeking lodging. The householders said: "The king is not presently in this palace. You may enter and stay the night. The king has faith in and delights in the Buddha-Dharma; hearing of this, he will surely be pleased." The bhikṣus did not dare and so had no place to stay. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to enter the king's empty palace. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, when the king has not yet removed the treasures and has not yet concealed the treasures, enters and crosses the threshold of the inner palace, it is a pācittika."

"Treasures" means valued possessions and also the women; all are called "treasures."

"Has not yet removed" means the women are in the palace and have not yet departed.

"Has not yet concealed" means the women are in this palace and have not yet been hidden away.

At that time, if one crosses the threshold of the inner palace with both feet, it is a pācittika. For however far one enters, step by step it is a pācittika. If one foot crosses, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one enters the homes of other great ministers or elders and crosses the inner threshold, it is a duṣkṛta.

For a śrāmaṇera, it is a duṣkṛta.

If the king invites one to enter, there is no offense.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the countries of Kośala and Magadha were raiding each other, and the roads between the two countries were cut off. After the rainy-season retreat, the bhikṣus of Rājagṛha thought: "We can only travel with bandits as companions in order to go and pay respects to the World-Honored One. Even if the border patrols capture us for being with bandits, King Prasenajit has faith in and delights in the Buddha-Dharma, and he will surely not hold us guilty." They then traveled with the bandits to the other country's border, and indeed they were captured and brought before the patrol commander. The soldiers reported: "These are bandits!" The patrol commander said: "Those wearing kāṣāya robes, what are they?" The soldiers answered: "They are also bandits!" The bhikṣus then spoke for themselves: "We are not bandits. We are śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas. Having completed the rainy-season retreat in Rājagṛha, we wished to pay respects to the World-Honored One. The roads were dangerous and difficult, so we traveled with them as companions." The patrol commander said: "You are not śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas! You must be wearing these robes as a disguise to act as spies!" He then sent them to the king. The bhikṣus explained as before, and the king released them. Among the attendant ministers, those who did not believe they were śramaṇas said: "These bandits are disguised in bhikṣu robes. The king, out of faith in and delight in the Buddha-Dharma, has released them." When the senior bhikṣus heard of this, they rebuked them in various ways and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you actually do this?" They answered: "We actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu travels as a companion with bandits, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣus traveling on the road with bandits without knowing they were bandits. Once they realized it, they felt ashamed, thinking they had committed a pācittika. Some left the road to avoid them, or stayed behind. The bandits then asked: "Why are you doing this?" They answered: "The Buddha has prohibited us from traveling with you as companions." Hearing these words, the bandits became greatly enraged, beat the bhikṣus, and stripped them of their robes, leaving them naked. The bhikṣus returned and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "If one does not know they are bandits and travels with them, committing a pācittika is not the case. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, knowing they are bandits, travels with them as companions, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣus traveling on the road who encountered bandits along the way. They felt ashamed, thinking they had committed a pācittika. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "If one does not arrange to travel with bandits and merely encounters them on the road, committing a pācittika is not the case. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu arranges to travel together with bandits as companions, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣus who had arranged to travel with bandits along a nearby road, but some did not go, and some took a different road. All had doubts: "Have we perhaps committed a pācittika?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "Although one arranged to travel with bandits along a nearby road, if one ultimately does not go or takes a different route, committing a pācittika is not the case. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu arranges to travel on the same road with bandits, from one village to another village, it is a pācittika."

If one arranges to travel with an evil bhikṣu, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If various dangers arise and one arranges to travel together, there is no offense.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, various bhikṣus traveled on the road together with women. Sometimes one bhikṣu traveled with one woman or up to many women; or two bhikṣus up to many bhikṣus traveled with one woman or up to many women, going together and crossing water, mutually seeing each other's bodies, giving rise to thoughts of attachment. Some returned to lay life or became non-Buddhist practitioners. The householders, seeing this, reproached them: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, travel on the same road with women. How is this different from taking a wife on a journey? Who knows whether these people practice the pure conduct? They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!"

At that time, there was a householder who often beat his wife. After beating her, he went out. The wife thought: "My husband often beats me and might secretly kill me. I should flee." So she left. Outside the village, she saw a bhikṣu, went up to him, and asked: "Venerable one, where are you going?" He answered: "I wish to go to such-and-such a place." The woman then followed behind him. The husband thought: "I just beat my wife; perhaps she might kill herself!" He immediately returned home and searched for her but could not find her. He asked the neighbors: "Have you seen my wife?" They all said: "We have not." He went outside the village and saw a non-Buddhist female practitioner. He asked her: "Have you perhaps seen such-and-such a woman?" She answered: "I saw a śramaṇa, a son of the Śākyas, take her away." The man immediately chased after them in haste. When he caught up, he said to the bhikṣu: "Why have you taken my wife and run off?" The bhikṣu answered: "I have committed no evil deed. Your wife happened to travel on the same road as I." The wife also said to her husband: "Do not harbor evil thoughts toward this bhikṣu. He and I traveled the same road as intimately as relatives!" Hearing his wife's words, the husband thought: "So they deceive me thus; there must have been some evil between them!" He then beat the bhikṣu nearly to death before stopping. That bhikṣu thought: "I am now weak and exhausted, unable to continue on my way. I should enter the fire-light samādhi to restore myself and give my body strength, and then proceed." Having thought thus, he gathered his robes and bowl, entered the fire-light samādhi, and smoke issued from his body. The wife saw this and said to her husband: "You did not believe my words! Look at what is issuing from that bhikṣu's body!" After a moment, the bhikṣu's entire body blazed with light. The wife said again: "You did not believe my words! Look again at the bhikṣu's body and see how it is!" That bhikṣu then, by the power of this samādhi, traveled to where the Buddha was and reported all the preceding events in full to the World-Honored One. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you actually do this?" They answered: "We actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu travels on the road together with a woman, it is a pācittika."

Some bhikṣus were traveling together and along the way saw women also traveling on this road. They had doubts and remorse: "Have we perhaps committed a pācittika?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu does not arrange to travel with a woman on the same road, committing a pācittika is not the case. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu arranges to travel on the road together with a woman, it is a pācittika."

Some bhikṣus had arranged to travel on the road with women but afterward did not dare go, or took a different road, yet still had doubts and remorse. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu previously arranged to travel on the road with a woman but ultimately does not go, or takes a different route, committing a pācittika is not the case. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu arranges to travel on the road together with a woman, from one village to another village, it is a pācittika."

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

The Buddha was in the country of Kośala, traveling through the countryside with a great Sangha of twelve hundred and fifty bhikṣus. The bhikṣus found lodging either in buildings, under trees, or in the open.

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus together with the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus gathered a large pile of firewood and grass, lit a fire in the open, and sat beside it to warm themselves. A snake came out from a hole in the wood. The bhikṣus, seeing it, threw things at it. The snake retreated back in, but driven out by the heat, it came out again. The bhikṣus threw things at it again, and the snake went back in. Shortly after, it came out again, struck one bhikṣu, and bit him, and he died immediately. The bhikṣus surrounded him weeping. The senior bhikṣus asked: "Why are you weeping?" They answered: "This bhikṣu was bitten by a snake and has died." They recounted the above events in full. The senior bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways: "How could you see the snake come out repeatedly and still not avoid it, causing him to be bitten to death?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you actually do this?" They answered: "We actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu lights a fire, it is a pācittika."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus had park keepers and śrāmaṇeras light fires. The senior bhikṣus saw this and rebuked them: "Have you not heard that the Buddha has prohibited lighting fires?" They answered: "We had the park keepers and śrāmaṇeras light them; we are not at fault!" The bhikṣus said: "What difference is there between lighting a fire yourself and having someone else light it?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you actually do this?" They answered: "We actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu lights a fire himself or has someone else light it, it is a pācittika."

There were sick bhikṣus who consulted a physician. The physician said: "You should take such-and-such medicine, light a fire, and bathe." The sick bhikṣus said: "The Buddha does not permit us to light fires ourselves or have others light them. Please teach us to take other medicine." The physician said: "Venerable one, you should precisely take this medicine, light a fire, and bathe." The bhikṣus thought: "If the Buddha would permit us to light fires ourselves or have others light them, our illness could be cured." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit sick bhikṣus to light fires or have others light them. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, when not sick, lights a fire himself or has someone else light it, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣus who needed to cook soup or porridge but did not dare light a fire. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to cook soup and porridge, but not for the purpose of warming themselves by the fire. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, when not sick, for the purpose of warming himself, lights a fire, it is a pācittika."

The bhikṣus wished to smoke their bowls and light fires; and also to ward off dangerous animals, and to light lamps and candles; they did not know what to do. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus, for such reasons, to light fires themselves or have others light them. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, when not sick, for the purpose of warming himself, lights a fire himself or has someone else light it, it is a pācittika."

If one lights a fire for warming and the flames rise as high as four finger-breadths, it is a pācittika.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, various bhikṣus visited the homes of acquaintances, saw ornamental jewelry, picked it up to look at, and put it back in its original place. Later it was stolen by someone else. The owner returned looking for it but could not find it. Someone said: "I saw a bhikṣu handling it!" The owner then went to ask the bhikṣu: "I lost such-and-such a jewel. Did the bhikṣu see it?" The bhikṣu answered: "I earlier picked it up and put it back in its original place." The owner said: "Since you picked it up, it has not been seen again. Please return it to me!" The bhikṣu answered: "I truly did not take it!" The owner did not believe him and gave the bhikṣu a bad reputation.

At that time, there was a non-Buddhist practitioner who carried five hundred gold coins in a bag. He went to the waterside to drink and forgot to take the bag with him. A bhikṣu came along afterward and saw it, thinking: "Whose belongings are these?" He looked around in all directions and saw a person ahead. He then thought: "This must belong to that person. I should take it and return it." So he picked it up and went after the person. That person had not gone far when he remembered the bag of gold and immediately rushed back. The bhikṣu asked: "Why have you come back?" The person became angry and said: "You inauspicious creature! Why do you ask me?" The bhikṣu said: "Even if I am inauspicious, you should tell me why you returned." The person said: "I forgot a bag at the waterside and have come back to find it." The bhikṣu then showed it to him: "Is this your bag?" Having seen the bag, the person became even more angry and said: "You inauspicious creature! Why did you take my bag? Wait right here; let me count the contents of the bag!" The bhikṣu answered: "I never opened this bag, nor did I look inside. If I wanted to take it, why would I show it to you? Fearing you might lose it, I carried it to return to you." The person then said: "My bag contained one thousand gold coins. Now five hundred are missing. Return them to me!" The bhikṣu answered as before. The person then forcibly dragged the bhikṣu to the judge.

At that time, the judge did not have faith in or delight in the Buddha-Dharma. He judged unreasonably, ordering the bhikṣu bound with arms behind his back, beaten while a donkey-drum was sounded, and paraded to the crossroads to be executed. At that time, King Prasenajit was on a high balcony and saw this from afar. He asked his attendants: "Who is that?" They answered: "A śramaṇa, a son of the Śākyas." The king immediately ordered: "Bring him back to the court of judgment; I shall go out myself!" He then went out and asked the person: "Why do you torment this bhikṣu?" The person reported to the king as above. The king then asked the bhikṣu, who also answered the king as above. The king asked the person: "Is this truly your bag?" He answered: "It is." The king then put five hundred gold coins into the bag, but the bag could not hold them. The king then said: "You said the bag contained one thousand gold coins. Why now can it not hold them?" The person then confessed: "Originally there were truly only five hundred. In my anger, I falsely accused the bhikṣu." The king then asked the judge: "If a person deceives the king to his face, what punishment should be given?" The judge said: "This person should be put to death, and his property confiscated by the state." The king then applied this law, confiscated his property, bound his arms behind his back, beat the donkey-drum, and had him paraded to the crossroads to be executed. Some people said: "You falsely accused a śramaṇa, a son of the Śākyas, and should receive this punishment! If you make such false accusations again, it will be even worse!" Others said: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, may well have taken my jewels; perhaps this was true!" At that time, the bhikṣu said to the king: "May it please the king to spare this man's life, so that the Buddha-Dharma does not acquire a bad reputation." The king then released him and rebuked the judge: "If you ever again render such a judgment, you shall be dealt with as this man was about to be!" Those who did not have faith in or delight in the Buddha-Dharma reproached in various ways: "Even we laypeople are ashamed to handle jewels. Why do the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, do so? They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, rebuked them in various ways, and reported all the preceding and subsequent events in full to the World-Honored One. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you actually do this?" They answered: "We actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu picks up valuables or articles equivalent to valuables, it is a pācittika."

At that time, Viśākhā, the Mother of Mṛgāra, wearing her most exquisite ornamental jewelry, went with her relatives to enjoy a pleasure garden. The garden was near the Jetavana. Before she had finished observing the people's enjoyment, she thought: "It is not fitting for me to share in this indulgence. I should take this opportunity to pay respects to the World-Honored One." She then took her maidservant to the Jetavana. Arriving at the gate, she thought again: "It is not fitting for me to wear such fine adornments when visiting the World-Honored One." She then removed her jeweled garment and placed it beside the moat. At that time, Śāriputra, while walking in meditation, saw this from afar. Viśākhā, the Mother of Mṛgāra, went forward and bowed at the Buddha's feet, then stood to one side. The Buddha taught her the Dharma in various ways, instructing, demonstrating, benefiting, and delighting her. After a while, she withdrew. Absorbed in what she had heard, she forgot the jewels she had placed there and went home. After the city gates had closed, she remembered them, and thought: "If I tell people I lost these jewels, it might harm the Buddha-Dharma." She remained silent until dawn. Śāriputra then reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha told Śāriputra: "Go and fetch them." He received the instruction and went to fetch them. The Buddha told Śāriputra: "Tomorrow morning, personally return them to her." He received the instruction and returned them. Viśākhā, the Mother of Mṛgāra, praised him, saying: "Excellent! I have such a great teacher and fellow practitioners of the pure conduct! If other non-Buddhist practitioners had obtained these items, how would they return them to me? Last night I had already relinquished them in my mind. I should now fulfill that intention." She then offered them to the Sangha of the four directions and said to Śāriputra: "Please use these to build a guest monastery hall." Śāriputra did not dare accept. He reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Accept it."

Furthermore, there were householders who entered the monastery every five days to pay respects. They would sometimes remove finger rings or ear ornaments, and upon leaving would forget them. The bhikṣus saw them but did not dare pick them up, and other people saw them and took them away. When the householders returned to the monastery seeking them, they asked the bhikṣus: "I lost such-and-such valuables. Did the bhikṣus see them?" They answered: "We saw them but did not dare pick them up." The householders said: "Venerable ones, if you saw them, why did you not pick them up and return them to me? What use does a renunciant have for such things?" They answered: "We truly did not take them." The householders did not believe them and gave the bhikṣus a bad reputation.

Furthermore, some bhikṣus traveled with companions and stayed at one place together. Among the companions, some forgot their belongings and left. The bhikṣus saw them but did not dare pick them up, and other people took them. The companions asked the bhikṣus: "I lost such-and-such items. Venerable one, did you see them?" They answered: "We saw them but did not dare pick them up." The companions said: "Venerable one, if you saw them, why did you not pick them up and return them to me? What use does a renunciant have for such things?" They answered: "We truly did not take them." The companions did not believe them and gave the bhikṣus a bad reputation. The bhikṣus thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us, within the monastery and at lodging places, to pick up valuables ourselves or have someone pick them up, the householders would not lose their valuables, and we would not acquire this bad reputation." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus, within the monastery and at lodging places, to pick up valuables themselves or have someone pick them up. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu picks up valuables or articles equivalent to valuables, whether picking them up himself or having someone else pick them up, except within the monastery and at lodging places, it is a pācittika. If within the monastery or at a lodging place one picks up valuables or articles equivalent to valuables, if later an owner claims them, one should return them. This is how it should be done."

"Valuables" means: pearls, maṇi gems, lapis lazuli, conch-shell jade, gold, and silver.

"Articles equivalent to valuables" means: all other possessions.

"Within the monastery" means: the place where the Sangha dwells; land belonging to the Sangha.

"Lodging place" means: outside the monastery, staying at a layperson's home, or traveling with companions and staying in the wilderness.

If a bhikṣu sees an object within the monastery, he should have a lay attendant pick it up. If there is no lay attendant, he should pick it up and store it himself. If someone comes to claim it, he should assemble the Sangha, ask about the characteristics of the lost item, and then return it. If, having picked it up and stored it, he wishes to travel elsewhere, he should entrust it to another person.

If a bhikṣu goes to a layperson's home and sees an object, he should have a lay attendant store it. If there is no lay attendant, he should store it himself. If there is someone present, he should entrust it to that person before leaving. If there is no one present, he should call the owner out and entrust it to them before leaving.

If a bhikṣu is traveling on the road with companions and sees an object, he should have a lay attendant pick it up. If there is no lay attendant, he should pick it up himself and return it. When returning it, he should gather the group of people and first ask: "Have you lost any belongings?" If someone says "Yes," he should ask: "What was it?" If the answer matches, then he should return it.

If one parts ways with companions onto a different road and cannot catch up, upon reaching a village one should entrust it to a faithful and reliable upāsaka to return it.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, the bhikṣus bathed twice or three times a day, using much bathing powder. The householders, seeing this, reproached them: "These bhikṣus bathe so frequently and use so much bathing powder, like kings and great ministers! They originally left the household life seeking liberation. They should be reciting sūtras and contemplating the impurities of the body. Instead, they polish and adorn their bodies day and night! They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!"

At that time, there was an astrologer who told King Bimbisāra: "An inauspicious star is about to appear. The king should bathe in a certain spring to avert this calamity. If not, you may lose your kingdom or face danger to your life!" The king then ordered his attendants to prepare that spring. They received the order and went, but found the bhikṣus filling the spring and bathing in it. They returned and reported to the king. The king said: "Wait until the bhikṣus finish bathing!" In this manner, they were sent to check three times during the day and three times during the night, but each time they went and returned there was never a vacancy. The brahmin told the king again: "This star is about to appear. If you bathe after it appears, there will be no benefit!" Hearing these words, the king immediately prepared his carriages and went to the spring, where he bathed downstream. The ministers reproached on account of this: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, do not know propriety! They are not diligent, do not contemplate the impurities of the body. They only seek to groom and decorate themselves, washing and bathing their bodies! They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, rebuked them in various ways, and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you actually do this?" They answered: "We actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu bathes more than once within a half month, it is a pācittika."

There were sick bhikṣus whose physician said they would recover only by bathing. The bhikṣus said: "The Buddha does not permit us to bathe frequently. Please think of another method." The physician said: "Only bathing will work; there is no other treatment." The bhikṣus thought: "If the Buddha would permit bathing when sick, our illness could be cured."

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who did various work and their bodies became soiled with mud and dirt, and their robes became filthy. This made them even more fatigued. They thought: "If the Buddha would permit bathing when working, our fatigue would be relieved and our robes would be clean."

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who became fatigued from traveling on the road and wished to bathe but did not dare. They thought: "If the Buddha would permit bathing when traveling, our fatigue could be relieved."

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus whose robes were soiled by wind, rain, and dust. They thought: "If the Buddha would permit bathing when soiled by wind and rain, we could avoid being defiled by dust and mud."

During the last month and a half of spring and the first month of summer, the bhikṣus suffered from heat, oppression, and sweating. They thought: "If the Buddha would permit bathing in hot weather, we could be free of this affliction." Each of them reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of these matters, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to bathe frequently when sick, when working, when traveling, during wind and rain, and during hot weather, without offense. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu bathes more than once within a half month, except with proper cause, it is a pācittika. The proper causes are: when sick, when working, when traveling, during wind and rain, and during hot weather. These are called the proper causes."

"When sick" means: one is ill and needs to bathe.

"When working" means: managing various tasks, even including sweeping the floor of one's room.

"When traveling" means: one yojana, two yojanas, or even traveling half a yojana.

"During wind and rain" means: being soiled by wind, rain, dust, and mud.

"During hot weather" means: suffering from oppressive heat and sweating.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a bath attendant or a sick person whose body is already wet bathes incidentally, there is no offense.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus had built a new dwelling. The group-of-six bhikṣus wished to live in it and drove out the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus. The group-of-seventeen bhikṣus refused to leave, so they beat them, and the others cried out loudly. The senior bhikṣus asked: "Why are you crying out?" They answered: "The group-of-six bhikṣus beat us!" The senior bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you actually do this?" They answered: "We actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu strikes a bhikṣu, it is a pācittika."

There was a bhikṣu who was choking on food and asked a bhikṣu to pound his back. The bhikṣu did not dare, and the choking bhikṣu died. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu strikes a bhikṣu without anger, committing a pācittika is not the case. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, out of anger, strikes a bhikṣu, it is a pācittika."

If one strikes a bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, śrāmaṇerī, another person, or an animal, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a bhikṣuṇī strikes a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī, it is a pācittika. If she strikes a śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, śrāmaṇerī, another person, or an animal, it is a duṣkṛta.

For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, if they strike any of the five assemblies, other people, or animals, it is a duṣkṛta.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus again came to the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus' dwelling seeking to stay. They refused to leave, so the group-of-six raised their hands in a threatening gesture as if to strike. The others cried out loudly. The senior bhikṣus heard this, came out, and asked: "Why are you crying out?" They answered: "The group-of-six bhikṣus are about to strike us!" The senior bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you actually do this?" They answered: "We actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu raises a hand threateningly against a bhikṣu, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣus who, when teaching the Dharma, gestured with their hands, or pointed out a place, and they felt doubts and remorse: "Have we perhaps committed a pācittika?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu raises a hand at a bhikṣu without anger, committing a pācittika is not the case. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, out of anger, raises a hand threateningly against a bhikṣu, it is a pācittika."

If the threatening hand reaches the other person, it is a pācittika. If the threatening hand does not reach, it is a duṣkṛta. The rest is as stated in the section on striking a bhikṣu.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus received their assigned dwelling. The group-of-six bhikṣus, by seniority, took turns entering to stay. The group-of-seventeen then moved to the adjacent rooms. The group-of-six bhikṣus conspired together: "The group-of-seventeen bhikṣus are conscientious and cautious. Living so close to us, they will surely see our faults. We should devise a means to make them move far away." They went and told them: "This was previously an empty room with many frightening occurrences. You should not stay in it!" The group-of-seventeen bhikṣus said: "We will firmly close the door. What is there to be afraid of?" That night, in the darkness, the group-of-six created various frightening apparitions. The next morning, they asked the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus: "Did you sleep well last night?" They answered: "We heard frightening sounds, but we closed our door and reflected, and were not afraid at all!" The group-of-six bhikṣus then conspired again: "We cannot frighten them this way. We should wait for them to go out and then hide under their beds." They then secretly entered and hid, and in the darkness of night they pulled at their robes, pulled at their feet, or lifted their beds and moved them to different places. Thereupon, the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus cried out in great alarm. The senior bhikṣus came and asked: "Why are you crying out?" They answered: "There should not be thieves here, yet we do not know who pulled our robes, who pulled our feet, and who lifted our beds and moved them to different places." The senior bhikṣus then brought fire to illuminate the room and saw the group-of-six bhikṣus crouching under their beds. They asked: "What are you doing here?" They answered: "We wanted to frighten the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus." The senior bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you actually do this?" They answered: "We actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu frightens a bhikṣu, it is a pācittika."

There was a visiting bhikṣu who asked a resident bhikṣu: "What should one be wary of in this room?" Some did not dare answer, and others, after answering, had doubts, fearing they had committed a pācittika offense. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu does not intentionally frighten a bhikṣu, committing a pācittika is not the case. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu intentionally frightens a bhikṣu, it is a pācittika." The rest is as stated in the section on striking a bhikṣu.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the bhikṣu Dharma thought: "Upananda previously seized my robe, and the Buddha rebuked me on that account and established a precept for the bhikṣus. I shall now, in the Sangha, declare his offenses of saṃghāvaśeṣa." Having thought this, he went to the senior bhikṣus and said: "Upananda has touched a woman's body, spoken coarse and vulgar words, and praised his own offering of sexual service." The bhikṣus asked: "How do you know?" He answered: "I traveled with him and saw him do these things." The senior bhikṣus rebuked him: "How could you conceal this when you were not angry and reveal it only when angry?" After rebuking him, they reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked Dharma: "Did you actually do this?" He answered: "I actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu conceals the gross offenses of a bhikṣu, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣus who did not know that what another bhikṣu had committed was a gross offense, and only learned of it later. They felt doubts and remorse: "Have we perhaps committed a pācittika?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "If one does not know that what a bhikṣu committed is a gross offense, committing a pācittika is not the case. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, knowing that a bhikṣu has committed a gross offense, conceals it past one night, it is a pācittika."

From dawn until the next dawn-sign has not yet appeared, for each interval of time, it is a duṣkṛta. When the dawn-sign appears, it is a pācittika.

For a śrāmaṇera, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one wishes to speak but there is no one available, or if one fears danger may arise and conceals it, there is no offense.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda thought: "The bhikṣu Dharma promised to travel with me, so I gave him a robe. Since he then refused to go, I took the robe back. The World-Honored One rebuked me for this and established a precept for the bhikṣus. Afterward, he furthermore exposed my offenses. I shall now falsely accuse him of a baseless saṃghāvaśeṣa." Having thought this, he told the senior bhikṣus: "I truly touched a woman's body, spoke coarse and vulgar words, and praised my own offering of sexual service. The bhikṣu Dharma did likewise." The bhikṣus asked: "How do you know?" He answered: "I traveled with him and saw it." The bhikṣus rebuked him: "How can you falsely accuse a bhikṣu of a baseless saṃghāvaśeṣa?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked Upananda: "Did you actually do this?" He answered: "I actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu falsely accuses a bhikṣu of a baseless saṃghāvaśeṣa, it is a pācittika."

If one falsely accuses a bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, or śrāmaṇerī, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a bhikṣuṇī falsely accuses a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī, it is a pācittika. If she falsely accuses a śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, or śrāmaṇerī, it is a duṣkṛta.

For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, if they falsely accuse any of the five assemblies, it is a duṣkṛta.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda thought: "The bhikṣu Dharma promised to travel with me," and so forth up to "and furthermore exposed my offenses," all as stated above. "I falsely accused him of a baseless saṃghāvaśeṣa, but it could not harm him. I shall now deal with him by other means." He went to Dharma and said: "You are my disciple and I am your teacher. You first wronged me, and I also wronged you. Let us now make peace and harbor no more resentment. We can travel together to various families as before and eat much delicious food." Dharma then made peace and followed him. Upananda would always take him to places where there was no food. When people came with invitations, he would wink, gesture, or signal them to leave. He would calculate the time to return to the monastery, knowing it would be too late for the midday meal, and then send Dharma away, saying: "There is no food here today. You should return to the monastery." Once Dharma was gone, Upananda would go to the house of the person who had invited them and eat much delicious food. Dharma returned to the monastery and indeed could not make it in time for the midday meal. After eating, Upananda returned and asked Dharma: "Did you make it in time for the meal?" He answered: "I did not." Upananda then deceitfully consoled him: "Although you missed one meal today, tomorrow I shall make sure you get the finest food." The next day, where they went was just the same as before. This happened three times. Then Upananda said to Dharma: "All the places I have taken you to recently are places where delicious food is obtained, yet you cannot get any. I fear your ancestors are punishing you, or the deities are displeased with you, or perhaps it is due to your own evil karma. Do not blame me! You should hurry back to the monastery in time for the midday meal." Dharma rushed back but again could not make it in time. After many days without food, he became emaciated and could no longer get up. After eating, Upananda returned to where Dharma was staying and again asked: "Did you make it in time for the meal?" He answered: "I did not." Upananda then said: "You deceived your teacher and should be punished thus. If you do it again, I will make it even worse!" Dharma then finally realized his teacher's deception and cried out in anger: "How can a bhikṣu deceive like this, causing me to go without food for three days, nearly dying?" The senior bhikṣus asked: "Why are you crying out?" He answered: "Upananda tormented me for three days, making me go without food!" The senior bhikṣus rebuked Upananda in various ways, and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked Upananda: "Did you actually do this?" He answered: "I actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu says to a bhikṣu: 'Come with me to various families, and I will get you much delicious food and drink,' but does not give it, and sends him away to return, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣus who took other bhikṣus to various families but could not obtain food. They felt ashamed and thought: "Have we perhaps committed a pācittika?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu does not intend to vex the other and fails to obtain food, committing a pācittika is not the case. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu says to another bhikṣu: 'Come with me to various families, and I will get you much delicious food and drink,' and with the intent to vex him does not give it, and sends him away to return, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣus who took a bhikṣu who was caring for the sick to various families to request food for the sick bhikṣu. Fearing the sick person might miss the midday meal, they sent him back quickly. Having sent him away, they felt ashamed: "Have we perhaps committed a pācittika?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu does not send away another bhikṣu for the purpose of eating alone, committing a pācittika is not the case. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu says to another bhikṣu: 'Come with me to various families, and I will get you much delicious food and drink,' and having arrived, does not give it, but says: 'Go! Sitting with you and talking with you is unpleasant. I prefer to sit alone and talk alone,' intending to vex that bhikṣu, it is a pācittika."

If a bhikṣu acts in this manner to vex a bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, śrāmaṇerī, or even an animal, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a bhikṣuṇī acts in this manner to vex a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī, it is a pācittika. If she vexes a śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, śrāmaṇerī, or even an animal, it is a duṣkṛta.

For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, if they act in this manner to vex any of the five assemblies, it is a duṣkṛta.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, many bhikṣus were traveling together as companions through the country of Kośala when they were robbed by bandits and stripped of everything, robes and bowls. They arrived at a patrol station. The patrol commander asked: "Venerable ones, where did you encounter the bandits?" They answered: "At such-and-such a place." The patrol commander then conferred with his men: "If the king hears that bhikṣus encountered bandits in our territory, he will surely punish us." They then said to the bhikṣus: "Venerable ones, please wait a moment. We shall go and pursue these bandits." They went in pursuit and soon caught up with them. They surrounded them tightly and demanded all the stolen goods, recovering everything. Dyed robes and white garments were placed in separate piles. The patrol soldiers conferred: "Whose should we return first?" Someone said: "We should first return the bhikṣus' property. The king has faith in and delights in the Buddha-Dharma, and hearing this he will surely be pleased." They then told the bhikṣus: "You may each take your robes." The bhikṣus had doubts about the robes, some saying "This is my robe" and others saying "This is not my robe," and so they did not dare take them. The patrol soldiers asked: "Why do you not take them?" They answered: "We cannot identify our own robes, so we do not take them." The patrol soldiers said: "Then those who can identify theirs should take them first." Thereupon, the non-Buddhist practitioners took the bhikṣus' fine robes, and the bhikṣus were left with the non-Buddhists' poor ones. The patrol soldiers, knowing that the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, always wore fine robes but now had the non-Buddhists' poor garments, said: "What is remarkable about you śramaṇas? You cannot even recognize your own robes; how can you know the mind? If you knew the characteristics of your robes, how could the non-Buddhists have gotten your fine ones?" The bhikṣus went to where the Buddha was and reported this. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and further asked the bhikṣus: "Is it true that you did not recognize the characteristics of your robes?" They answered: "It is true, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu receives a new robe, he should mark it with one of three colors: either dark blue, black, or mūlān. If he does not mark it with one of the three colors, it is a pācittika."

If one does not make a mark and wears the robe, each time one wears it, it is a pācittika.

If one does not wear it, each night one keeps it, it is a pācittika.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one receives a new robe that has already been marked, it is not an offense to not mark it again.

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus' robes, bowls, and various belongings were scattered about in various places. They had not been put away. The group-of-six bhikṣus then took them and hid them. When the group-of-seventeen realized their things were missing, they asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "My such-and-such items were here and there. Who took them?" They answered: "Many people came by earlier. Did they not perhaps take them?" They then asked: "Where did those people who came earlier go?" They answered: "East, west, south, north; who knows where they went?" The group-of-seventeen bhikṣus then rushed out in all four directions pursuing the visitors, caught up with them, and said: "I have lost robes, bowls, a sitting mat, and a needle case. Please return them to me." The people said: "Venerable one, we came for the Dharma, not to steal." Having received this response, the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus returned in shame. The group-of-six bhikṣus asked: "Did you see the people who came earlier?" They answered: "Yes." "Did you get your robes?" They answered: "No." Thereupon, the group-of-six bhikṣus brought out the robes and bowls and showed them: "Are these your robes and bowls?" They answered: "They are." The bhikṣus who saw this rebuked them in various ways and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you actually do this?" They answered: "We actually did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I shall now establish a precept for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu himself hides a bhikṣu's robe, bowl, sitting mat, needle case, or any such requisite for livelihood, or has someone hide them, it is a pācittika."

Furthermore, there was a bhikṣu who did not put away his robes, bowls, and belongings. The other bhikṣus did not dare put them away for him, and they were lost. Because of this, they came under suspicion and acquired a bad reputation. They thought: "If the Buddha would permit us to put away others' belongings, those bhikṣus would not lose their things, and we would not acquire this bad reputation." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told the bhikṣus: "If one does not intend to hide things but puts away another's belongings for them, committing a pācittika is not the case. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, for amusement, hides a bhikṣu's robe, bowl, sitting mat, needle case, or any such requisite for livelihood, or has someone hide them, it is a pācittika."

If one hides the belongings of a bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, śrāmaṇerī, or even an animal, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a bhikṣuṇī hides the belongings of a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī, it is a pācittika. If she hides others' belongings, it is a duṣkṛta.

For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śr

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Fascicle 10

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

---

## First Division, Part Six: Rules of Confession (Pratideśanīya)

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the laywoman Mother of Vaggalā had faith in and took delight in the Buddha's Dharma. She constantly made offerings to śramaṇas and was a person of noble character. Afterward, she went forth out of faith, was of few desires and content with little, and received many offerings. When she went begging for food and was carrying it back, she met a bhikṣu and asked: "Why are you going about like this?" He answered: "Begging for food!" She then asked: "Can you accept this food from me?" He answered: "I can!" She then gave it to him. She then entered another house to beg for food. That bhikṣu told other bhikṣus: "The bhikṣuṇī Mother of Vaggalā is able to obtain food and drink. You can go get some from her!" When the bhikṣus heard this, they went to her at once. The bhikṣuṇī, whenever she obtained food, would give it to them. She thought: "The last portion I obtain, I shall carry back to eat myself." When she obtained it and was carrying it out, she again encountered a bhikṣu, who greeted her as before, and she again gave it to him, returning with an empty bowl.

After eating, the bhikṣus gathered and discussed: "That bhikṣuṇī is fortunately able to obtain much food and drink. Why should we trouble ourselves begging elsewhere? We should simply follow behind her every day!" So the next morning they entered the village and followed after her. The bhikṣuṇī, whenever she obtained food, gave it away and returned with an empty bowl. On the third day, when she went out begging in the early morning, a householder was riding by in a horse-drawn carriage. The bhikṣuṇī tried to step aside, but she collapsed on the ground. At that time, King Prasenajit had issued a decree: anyone within the kingdom who showed disrespect to śramaṇas of the Śākya clan would be severely punished. The householder was greatly frightened. He got down from his carriage, helped her up, and apologized: "I did not harm you. Why did you suddenly fall?" She answered: "You truly did not harm me. It is because I am faint from hunger!" He asked further: "Were you unable to get food from begging?" She answered: "All the food I obtained I gave to the bhikṣus, and so I have come to this state!" He then said: "Please accept my food!" She silently assented. He then gave her food, and she again gave it to the bhikṣus.

By the time the householder had gotten down from his carriage, a large crowd had gathered. They all criticized and reproached, saying: "This bhikṣuṇī's generosity may be boundless, but those who receive should know their own limits! These people constantly speak of having few desires and being content with little, yet now they greedily take and cruelly exhaust one of their own path!" The senior bhikṣus heard this and reproached them in various ways, then reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a pratideśanīya rule for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu receives food from a bhikṣuṇī, that bhikṣu should confess before the bhikṣus: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy offense. I now confess before the venerable ones!' This is called a rule of confession."

There were bhikṣus who had bhikṣuṇī relatives who were able to obtain food and drink. Seeing that the bhikṣus had difficulty begging for food, they said: "Do not torment yourselves. Take food from us!" The bhikṣus said: "The Buddha does not allow us to receive food from bhikṣuṇīs." The bhikṣuṇīs said: "Surely relatives should give and relatives should accept. Please report this to the Buddha!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to receive food from bhikṣuṇīs who are relatives. From now on, this pratideśanīya rule should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu receives food from a bhikṣuṇī who is not a relative, that bhikṣu should confess before the bhikṣus: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy offense. I now confess before the venerable ones!' This is called a rule of confession."

Furthermore, there were sick bhikṣus who dragged themselves out to beg for food despite their illness, and their illness would invariably worsen. The bhikṣuṇīs told them: "Do not torment yourselves. Accept food from us!" The sick bhikṣus said: "The Buddha does not allow us to receive food from bhikṣuṇīs who are not relatives." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit sick bhikṣus to receive food from bhikṣuṇīs who are not relatives. From now on, this pratideśanīya rule should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, when not sick, receives food from a bhikṣuṇī who is not a relative, that bhikṣu should confess before the bhikṣus: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy offense. I now confess before the venerable ones!' This is called a rule of confession."

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, whether at the monastery, at their own dwelling place, or at the homes of various families, would prepare morning meals, afternoon meals, cakes, and congee for the bhikṣus. They also prepared baths for them and offered various oils and ghee. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit receiving food offered by bhikṣuṇīs, but it must not be received in the streets. From now on, this pratideśanīya rule should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu, when not sick, in the streets, receives food with his own hands from a bhikṣuṇī who is not a relative, that bhikṣu should confess before the bhikṣus: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy offense. I now confess before the venerable ones!' This is called a rule of confession."

If a bhikṣu is outside the village and the bhikṣuṇī is inside the village when he receives food; if a bhikṣu is inside the village and the bhikṣuṇī is outside the village when he receives food; if a bhikṣu is in the air and the bhikṣuṇī is on the ground when he receives food; if a bhikṣu is on the ground and the bhikṣuṇī is in the air when he receives food: all are duṣkṛta offenses.

For a śrāmaṇera: a duṣkṛta offense.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, a householder invited both assemblies of the Sangha to a meal. The group-of-six bhikṣus and the group-of-six bhikṣuṇīs sat facing each other and instructed the servers to give more food to each other, so that the other virtuous bhikṣus could not get enough to eat. They said to the host: "You have invited the Sangha; why do you not add more food?" He answered: "These bhikṣus have confused me. I do not know who should receive more and who should not!" The host then criticized and reproached the group-of-six bhikṣus: "These people urge each other to eat, just as if they had brought their wives to a joint invitation! They have no śramaṇa conduct and violate the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a pratideśanīya rule for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu is invited to eat at a layperson's house, and there is a bhikṣuṇī present who says: 'Give rice to this bhikṣu! Give soup to this bhikṣu!', the bhikṣus should tell that bhikṣuṇī: 'Sister, please step back and wait until the bhikṣus have finished eating!' If among the bhikṣus not even one bhikṣu tells that bhikṣuṇī: 'Sister, please step back and wait until the bhikṣus have finished eating!', those bhikṣus should confess before the bhikṣus: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy offense. I now confess before the venerable ones!' This is called a rule of confession."

At that time, five hundred bhikṣus were eating at the home of a householder. A bhikṣuṇī who was regularly supported by that family came, and all the bhikṣus called out in unison: "Step back! Step back!" The bhikṣuṇī was extremely ashamed and immediately left. The host saw this and asked the bhikṣus: "What offense has this bhikṣuṇī committed that you drive her away in unison?" Some people also said: "These śramaṇas are afraid the bhikṣuṇī will take their food portions, and that is why they act this way! They have gone forth together, yet they are jealous of each other. If they cannot even be good to each other, how much less to others!" The senior bhikṣus heard this and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If, when bhikṣus are eating, a bhikṣuṇī does not instruct the servers to add food out of partiality, anger, delusion, or fear, and merely stands silently, then committing a pratideśanīya offense is impossible. From now on, this pratideśanīya rule should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu is invited to eat at a layperson's house, and there is a bhikṣuṇī who instructs the servers, saying: 'Give rice to this bhikṣu! Give soup to this bhikṣu!', the bhikṣus should tell that bhikṣuṇī: 'Sister, please step back and wait until the bhikṣus have finished eating.' If among the assembly not even one person speaks, that bhikṣu should confess before the bhikṣus: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy offense. I now confess before the venerable ones!' This is called a rule of confession."

If a bhikṣuṇī instructs the servers to add food for the bhikṣus, the first senior seat should speak. If she does not heed the first senior seat, the second senior seat should speak next, and so on down, even to the most newly ordained.

If a śikṣamāṇā or śrāmaṇerī instructs the servers to add food for the bhikṣus and the bhikṣus do not say "Step back": a duṣkṛta offense.

If a bhikṣu instructs the servers to add food for bhikṣus and they eat unequally: a duṣkṛta offense.

For a śrāmaṇera: a duṣkṛta offense.

The Buddha was in the country of Kauśāmbī. At that time, the householder Ghoṣila had faith in and took delight in the Buddha's Dharma, had seen the Dharma and attained its fruit, and constantly made offerings to the Buddha and the bhikṣu Sangha. Afterward, his wealth and possessions became exhausted. His relatives, both near and distant, sent food to him. But the bhikṣus still went to his home and took away full bowls. The members of his household could not endure the hunger and hardship. Neighbors who saw this all criticized and reproached, saying: "Though the donor may have no limit to generosity, those who receive should know contentment! How can they encroach upon and damage this family whose wealth is exhausted? We share our food portions with them, and still these bhikṣus strip them bare! They have no compassion and only seek to satisfy themselves! They have no śramaṇa conduct and violate the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now permit the bhikṣus to perform a 'training family' formal act of two proclamations for the householder Ghoṣila, so that not even one bhikṣu may enter his home."

One bhikṣu should proclaim: "Let the venerable Sangha listen! The householder Ghoṣila: when bhikṣus went to his home, they took various foods, filling their bowls and departing, leaving nothing behind, causing his family's wealth to be exhausted. We now perform the training family formal act, so that not even one bhikṣu may enter his dwelling again. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha approve. The proclamation is thus."

"Let the venerable Sangha listen! The householder Ghoṣila: when bhikṣus went to his home, they took various foods, filling their bowls and departing, leaving nothing behind, causing his family's wealth to be exhausted. We now perform the training family formal act, so that not even one bhikṣu may enter his dwelling again. Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has already performed the training family formal act for Ghoṣila; the Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. The matter is held thus."

At that time, the bhikṣus then went about performing the training family formal act for other families everywhere. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to perform the training family formal act for other families everywhere! If the wife is a noble person and the husband is an ordinary person; or if the wife is an ordinary person and the husband is a noble person: the training family formal act should not be performed for them. Only if both husband and wife are noble persons, without miserly or greedy minds, and their wealth has become exhausted, should the training family formal act then be performed."

At that time, all the bhikṣus dared not go to Ghoṣila's home anymore, and everyone in that household, great and small, longed to see them. Ghoṣila went to the monastery and addressed the bhikṣus: "I have taken refuge in the Three Treasures and seek no other field of merit. I beg the venerable ones to come and go at my home!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You may go." Though the bhikṣus went, they would not eat. The householder said: "I have taken refuge in the Three Treasures and seek no other field of merit. Please accept my food!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You may accept one-third of what fills the bowl." Since the Buddha had permitted accepting one-third, all the bhikṣus went to beg, and the family's wealth became even more exhausted than before. The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a pratideśanīya rule for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If there are training families for which the Sangha has performed the training family formal act, and a bhikṣu receives food at such a training family, that bhikṣu should confess before the bhikṣus: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy offense. I now confess before the venerable ones!' This is called a rule of confession."

When Ghoṣila's wealth had not yet been exhausted, he had separately established a fund to invite sick bhikṣus from the Sangha and provide for them, and he also had a medicine fund in the same way. The sick bhikṣus later felt ashamed and dared not accept. The householder said: "I originally set aside these resources and established the medicine fund for the sick bhikṣus in the Sangha. If they do not accept, I will never take it back!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "These are offerings that were invited and given when his wealth had not yet been exhausted. I now permit bhikṣus to accept them as they wish. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If there are training families for which the Sangha has performed the training family formal act, and a bhikṣu, when not sick and without having previously received an invitation, receives food at such a training family, that bhikṣu should confess before the bhikṣus: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy offense. I now confess before the venerable ones!' This is called a rule of confession."

Furthermore, there was a bhikṣu who, though not sick, took food from a formally designated training family. After receiving it, he had doubts: "Have I perhaps committed a pratideśanīya offense?" He carried it back and gave it to another bhikṣu. After that bhikṣu ate, he asked: "Why did you not eat?" He answered: "I was not sick and took this food from a formally designated training family. I feared committing a pratideśanīya offense." The other bhikṣu said: "According to your doubt, I have now committed it!" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If one takes food from a formally designated training family but does not eat it, and gives it to another to eat, neither commits an offense. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If there are training families for which the Sangha has performed the training family formal act, and a bhikṣu, when not sick and without having previously received an invitation, receives food with his own hands at such a training family, that bhikṣu should confess before the bhikṣus: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy offense. I now confess before the venerable ones!' This is called a rule of confession."

If a training family's wealth is exhausted, and the Sangha has gardens or fields, they should be given to the family to manage, on terms different from the usual allocation, with the remainder for the family's own sustenance. If there are no gardens or fields, and the Sangha has occasions of special offerings, the training family should be employed as workers and given the surplus. If there is none of this either, bhikṣus who have obtained food through begging should go to the family's home to eat and give them what remains. If this is not possible, the family should be brought to the monastery, given rooms and bedding, fed in proper sequence, given non-staple drinks at improper times, and given all such things. If there is cloth to be distributed, they should also receive a share. As for the women of the training family, the bhikṣuṇīs should likewise care for them in this way.

For a śrāmaṇera: a duṣkṛta offense.

The Buddha was in Kapilavastu, in the Nyagrodha Grove. At that time, there were bhikṣus dwelling in a forest hermitage. Laypeople bringing food to them were robbed by bandits. They reproached, saying: "Why did you not warn us? If we had known, we would have carried weapons to defend ourselves, or we simply would not have come!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a pratideśanīya rule for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu dwelling in a forest hermitage, in a place of doubt and fear, does not first keep watch, and receives food within the monastery, that bhikṣu should confess before the bhikṣus: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy offense. I now confess before the venerable ones!' This is called a rule of confession."

At that time, five hundred slaves of the Śākyas revolted and took up residence in the forest hermitage area. The Śākya women wished to go visit and make offerings to the Sangha. The slaves heard this and plotted together: "We shall seize them on the road!" The bhikṣus heard of this and went to tell the Śākya women: "There are bandits here who plan to seize you. You should not come!" The women then stopped. The slaves then said: "The reason the Śākya women did not come must be that the bhikṣus went and told them beforehand!" They questioned the bhikṣus, and the bhikṣus, not speaking falsely, answered truthfully. The slaves then beat the bhikṣus, seized all their robes and bowls, and left them nearly dead. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should not say there are bandits. Just tell them not to come."

At that time, the bhikṣus did not know when outsiders would come. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should always keep watch from afar. If you see someone coming, quickly go and tell them. If they have food, take it from them and swiftly send them back. From now on, this pratideśanīya rule should be recited as follows:

"If a bhikṣu dwelling in a forest hermitage, in a place of doubt and fear, does not first keep watch, and receives food with his own hands within the monastery without going outside to receive it, that bhikṣu should confess before the bhikṣus: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy offense. I now confess before the venerable ones!' This is called a rule of confession."

If someone bringing food arrives suddenly and has already entered the monastery, the bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One person should immediately receive it on behalf of all, setting aside one portion for himself and distributing the rest to the assembly. Then he should exchange his own portion by trading food with one person in the assembly. Send the food-bearer away quickly. If they cannot leave, the food-bearer should be hidden so that the bandits do not see them. If they cannot be hidden, they should be given a kāṣāya robe to drape over themselves and sent away. If this too is not possible, their head should be temporarily shaved, they should be dressed in Dharma robes, and sent away."

For a śrāmaṇera: a duṣkṛta offense.

If an army passes through and gives food, or if bandits themselves bring food and give it, there is no offense.

---

## First Division, Part Seven: Rules to Be Learned (Śaikṣa-dharma)

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus wore their lower garments either too high, or too low, or unevenly, or like a palmyra leaf, or like an elephant's trunk, or like a round fruit, or with fine pleats. Householders saw this and criticized: "These śramaṇas wear their lower garments sometimes like women, sometimes like performers, thinking this is attractive, without any proper deportment! They do not even know how to wear a garment, let alone understand the Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn to wear the lower garment not too high, not too low, not unevenly, not like a palmyra leaf, not like an elephant's trunk, not like a round fruit, and not with fine pleats."

Wearing it too high means: above the mid-shin.

Wearing it too low means: below the ankles.

Wearing it unevenly means: the four corners are not aligned.

Wearing it like a palmyra leaf means: high in front and low in back.

Like an elephant's trunk means: one corner hangs down from above.

Like a round fruit means: gathering it up to make it round and tucking it in front of the belly.

Fine pleats means: making fine pleats around the waist.

If one does not understand and does not ask, yet wears it in these ways: a duṣkṛta offense. If one understands but is not careful and wears it in these ways: a duṣkṛta offense. If one understands but treats the precept lightly or treats people with contempt and wears it in these ways: a pācittika offense.

For bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise. For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs: a duṣkṛta offense.

In times of illness, or in mud and rain, there is no offense.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus draped their upper robes either too high, or too low, or unevenly. Householders criticized them, and so forth, up to establishing a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus, all as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn to drape the upper robe not too high, not too low, and not unevenly."

The meanings of high, low, and uneven are as explained above.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus entered laypeople's houses without properly covering their bodies, or sat in laypeople's houses in this manner; or they threw back their robes over the right shoulder and entered laypeople's houses, or sat in laypeople's houses in this manner; or they threw back their robes over the left shoulder and entered laypeople's houses, or sat in laypeople's houses in this manner; or they threw back their robes over both shoulders and entered laypeople's houses, or sat in laypeople's houses in this manner; or they swayed their bodies, or shook their heads, or shrugged their shoulders, or swung their arms, or leaned on people, or stood with arms akimbo, or rested their chin on their hand, or swung their arms, and entered laypeople's houses, or sat in laypeople's houses in this manner; or they looked upward, or looked around left and right, and entered laypeople's houses, or sat in laypeople's houses in this manner; or they walked on tiptoe into laypeople's houses, or sat in laypeople's houses in this manner; or they walked crouching into laypeople's houses, or sat in laypeople's houses in this manner; or they covered their heads and entered laypeople's houses, or sat in laypeople's houses in this manner; or they entered laypeople's houses laughing and joking, or sat in laypeople's houses in this manner; or they entered laypeople's houses speaking loudly, or sat in laypeople's houses in this manner; or they entered laypeople's houses without composure, or sat in laypeople's houses in this manner. Householders saw this and criticized as before. The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish rules to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, these precepts should be recited as follows:

"One should learn to enter laypeople's houses with the body well covered; and so forth, up to: one should learn to sit in laypeople's houses with composure."

The Buddha traveled to the country of Bharga, accompanied by a great assembly of five hundred bhikṣus, and arrived at Mount Suṃsumāragiri, where he stayed in the Fearsome Forest.
