At that time, Prince Bodhi had newly built a lecture hall on this mountain, and no śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa had yet sat in it. The prince heard that the Buddha had arrived at the mountain and was staying in the Fearsome Forest. He told Sañjikāputta the student: "In my name, inquire of the World-Honored One: 'Are you free from illness and affliction? Is your daily life comfortable? I have newly built a lecture hall on this mountain, and no śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa has yet sat in it. I wish that the World-Honored One and the Sangha would be the first to use this hall, and therein accept a modest offering, so that I may have peace and well-being for a long night! If the Buddha has any instruction, I shall faithfully accept it!' Report this to the Buddha and quickly return with his response." The student received the instruction. When he arrived, he prostrated at the Buddha's feet, stood to one side, and conveyed the prince's message in full. The Buddha silently assented. When Sañjikāputta knew the Buddha had accepted, he returned and reported to the prince. The prince prepared abundant fine food and drink through the night. The next day, he personally brought it to the lecture hall. Both inside and outside his house, cloths of various colors were spread. When the time came, he addressed the Buddha: "May the World-Honored One deign to grace us with his divine presence!" The Buddha, with five hundred bhikṣus in attendance before and behind, arrived at the lecture hall and stood at the bottom of the stairway. The prince bared his right shoulder, placed his right knee on the ground, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha: "May the World-Honored One ascend this humble hall, so that I may receive peace and happiness for a long night!" The Buddha still did not ascend. This was repeated up to three times. At the last request, the Buddha glanced at Ānanda. Ānanda, understanding the Buddha's holy intent, told the prince: "Remove these colorful cloths. The Buddha will not step upon them, out of compassion for future generations!" The prince immediately ordered the cloths removed and repeated his invitation as before. Thereupon the World-Honored One, together with the Sangha, ascended and took their seats. The prince personally served the food. The bhikṣus held their bowls with one finger or two fingers pinching the rim, and when food was placed in them, they all dropped their bowls. Food and drink spilled and flowed, soiling the crystal floor. The householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus are just like spoiled children, or like acrobats." The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn to receive food with focused attention."

Receiving food with focused attention means: with the left hand attentively holding the bowl, the right hand supporting the rim.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus received food overflowing their bowls and cast aside soup and rice. Laypeople criticized: "These bhikṣus receive greedily without limit, like starving people!"

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who, at laypeople's homes, when they got rice, ate it all without waiting for the soup; and when they got soup, ate it all without waiting for the rice. Laypeople criticized: "These bhikṣus eat greedily like dogs!"

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who picked at food from various spots in their bowls. Again, there were bhikṣus who hollowed out the center of their food. Again, there were bhikṣus who curled their fingers to scrape the bowl while eating. Again, there were bhikṣus who sniffed their food before eating. Householders saw all this and criticized. The senior bhikṣus heard, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish rules to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, these precepts should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to receive food overflowing the bowl; to eat soup and rice together; not to pick at food from various spots in the bowl; not to hollow out the center of the food; not to curl fingers to scrape the bowl while eating; and not to sniff food before eating."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus looked around left and right while eating. Laypeople criticized: "These bhikṣus are like dogs or birds, eating their own food while watching other people eat! They do not even know proper eating etiquette, let alone understand deeper principles!" The senior bhikṣus heard, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "One should not look around left and right while eating."

At that time, the bhikṣus then dared not look around at all, and ate with their eyes closed, unable to see when soup and rice were being served. The group-of-six bhikṣus took their desirable food. When they opened their eyes and asked: "Who took my food?" The others answered: "You yourselves do not look, yet you blame others!" The other bhikṣus reproached them in various ways and reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn to eat while looking attentively at the bowl."

Looking attentively at the bowl means: keeping one's gaze fixed on the bowl, watching when food is being served.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus dropped and scattered their rice and food. Householders criticized: "These bhikṣus eat like little children!"

Furthermore, five hundred bhikṣus were eating at the home of a householder. Among the laypeople, some said: "The bhikṣus do not drop any rice at all." Others said they did, and the two made a wager. The bhikṣus happened not to drop any rice that day, but later, at another place, they were seen eating and dropping rice, and were criticized as above. The bhikṣus heard, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to drop rice or food."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus handled clean food vessels with food-soiled hands, greasy and dirty, and the other bhikṣus found it repulsive. Householders saw this and criticized: "How can they handle clean food vessels with food-soiled hands?" The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "When eating, one should not handle clean food vessels with the right hand."

Later, when laypeople served rice, bhikṣus tried to receive it with the left hand, and the laypeople would not give it, saying: "They did not tell us about this." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "One should wash one's hands clean before handling food vessels. I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to handle clean food vessels with food-soiled hands."

Food-soiled hands means: hands dirtied by food, or greasy.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus slurped their food noisily. Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who made sounds while chewing food. Householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus eat like dogs lapping water."

Furthermore, a brāhmaṇa invited the bhikṣus and offered them congee. The bhikṣus sipped the congee with loud sounds. One bhikṣu remarked: "Today the bhikṣus eat as if they are shivering from cold!" After saying this, he felt doubt and remorse: "I have now disparaged the Sangha." He did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked that bhikṣu: "With what mind did you say this?" He answered: "Partly with resentment, partly in jest." The Buddha said: "Reproaching with resentment: no offense. Reproaching in jest: a duṣkṛta offense." He told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish rules to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, these precepts should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to slurp food noisily; and not to make sounds while chewing food."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus licked their food up. Householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus eat like cattle!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to lick up food."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus ate with their hands overly full, and food fell and dropped to the ground. Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who opened their mouths wide while eating. Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who opened their mouths wide waiting before the food arrived, flies entered their mouths, and after eating they vomited copiously. Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who wrinkled their noses while eating. Householders saw all this and criticized. The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish rules to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, these precepts should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to eat with overly full handfuls."

"One should learn not to eat with the mouth wide open."

"One should learn not to open the mouth wide waiting before the food arrives."

"One should learn not to eat while wrinkling the nose."

The bhikṣus, when food came to their mouths, still dared not open them, soiling the edges of their mouths, with food flowing down to the ground. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should open the mouth at a proper distance, neither too far nor too close."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus spoke with food in their mouths, and food either fell to the ground, or fell on their robes, or fell back into the bowl. Householders saw this and all criticized. The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to speak with food in the mouth."

Afterward, when laypeople served food and asked whether more was needed, the bhikṣus dared not answer, and the laypeople criticized: "These bhikṣus are arrogant and will not speak to people." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "When food is being served, it is permitted to say whether one needs more or not."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus ate with their mouths stuffed full, their cheeks bulging out. Householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus eat like monkeys."

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who bit food in half and put the remainder back into the bowl. Householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus eat uncleanly!"

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who stretched out their arms to take food. Householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus are like elephants using their trunks!"

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who shook their hands while eating. Householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus are like elephants flicking their trunks!"

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who stuck out their tongues while eating. Householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus are like dogs with their tongues hanging out!"

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who swallowed food whole. Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who rolled rice into balls and tossed them into their mouths from a distance. Householders saw all this and criticized. The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish rules to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, these precepts should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to eat with bulging cheeks."

"One should learn not to bite food in half."

"One should learn not to stretch out the arm to take food."

"One should learn not to shake the hand while eating."

"One should learn not to eat with the tongue sticking out."

"One should learn not to swallow food whole."

"One should learn not to roll rice into balls and toss them into the mouth from a distance."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus poured the water from rinsing their bowls inside laypeople's houses. Householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus do not even know where to pour the foul water from rinsing their bowls, let alone know about distant matters!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "One should not pour bowl-rinsing water inside laypeople's houses."

There were laypeople who had newly built houses and obtained the water from the bhikṣus' bowls to sprinkle on the ground as an auspicious act. The bhikṣus dared not sprinkle it. The householders said: "These bhikṣus cannot even accept people's respect!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I permit bhikṣus to sprinkle water from the bowl that contains no food, to use for sprinkling the ground. I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to pour water from the bowl containing food inside laypeople's houses."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus covered their soup with rice. The laypeople, thinking they had not yet received soup, gave them more. When they realized what had happened, they criticized: "These bhikṣus cover their soup with rice, like little children!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "One should not cover soup with rice."

There were sick bhikṣus who dared not cover their soup with rice, and insects fell into the soup and they could not remove them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "It is permitted to cover soup with rice, but one should not do so hoping to get more. I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to cover soup with rice hoping to get more."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, there were bhikṣus who went to laypeople's homes and complained about the food. Furthermore, there were bhikṣus who asked for more food themselves. Householders all criticized. The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "One should not complain about food or ask for more food oneself." The bhikṣus then dared not ask for more food on behalf of sick bhikṣus. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "It is permitted to ask for more food on behalf of other bhikṣus, but not for oneself. I will now establish rules to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, these precepts should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to complain about food."

"One should learn not to ask for more food for oneself."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus looked into the bowls of those sitting beside them to see how much they had. Householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus are like little children, looking into others' bowls: 'You got more, I got less; you got less, I got more.'" The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "One should not look into the bowls of those sitting beside you to see how much they have."

At that time, five hundred bhikṣus were eating at a home. After eating, they said to each other: "How rare was that food!" The junior bhikṣus said: "The senior seats got the good food, and we did not!" The bhikṣus thought: "If the Buddha permits us to look into others' bowls, we could know who received food and who did not, and those who did not receive could be given some." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "It is permitted to look into the bowls of those sitting beside you, but one should not generate a mind of resentment. I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to look into the bowls of those sitting beside you with a resentful mind."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus urinated and defecated while standing. Householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus are like donkeys and horses!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to urinate or defecate while standing."

At that time, some bhikṣus were ill and unable to squat. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I permit bhikṣus who are ill to urinate and defecate while standing. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to urinate or defecate while standing, except in case of illness."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus urinated and defecated in water. Householders criticized. The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to urinate or defecate in water."

There were sick bhikṣus whose physician told them: "You should urinate and defecate in water, and I shall examine it to determine whether or not you can be treated." The bhikṣus dared not do so and said: "Please use another method!" The physician said: "Only by examining this can I know." The sick bhikṣus thought: "If the Buddha permits urinating and defecating in water, then I might be cured." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit sick bhikṣus to urinate and defecate in water. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to urinate or defecate in clean water, except in case of illness."

If one urinates or defecates on wood and it flows into the water as a consequence, there is no offense.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus urinated and defecated on living grass and leaves. Householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus are like cattle and sheep!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to urinate or defecate on living grass and leaves, except in case of illness."

If one urinates or defecates on wood and it flows onto living grass and leaves as a consequence, there is no offense.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus taught the Dharma to people wearing wooden clogs or leather sandals. Householders saw this and criticized: "This Dharma is venerable and supremely subtle, yet these bhikṣus teach it to people wearing clogs and leather sandals, showing disrespect for the Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish rules to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, these precepts should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to teach the Dharma to a person wearing wooden clogs."

"One should learn not to teach the Dharma to a person wearing leather sandals."

There were sick people who could not remove their clogs or leather sandals and wished to hear the Dharma, but the bhikṣus dared not teach them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣus to teach the Dharma to sick people wearing clogs or leather sandals. From now on, these precepts should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to teach the Dharma to a person wearing wooden clogs, except in case of illness."

"One should learn not to teach the Dharma to a person wearing leather sandals, except in case of illness."

Dharma means: that which the Buddha has taught, that which hearers have taught, that which sages have taught, that which devas have taught, and all that is taught in accordance with the Dharma.

If many people are wearing clogs or leather sandals and one cannot make them remove them, one may teach on account of those not wearing them, and there is no offense.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus taught the Dharma to people with exposed chests, and so forth, up to people leaning on staffs. Householders saw and criticized as above. The senior bhikṣus heard this, reproached them in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish rules to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, these precepts should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to teach the Dharma to a person with an exposed chest."

There were sick people who wished to hear the Dharma, but the bhikṣus dared not teach them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "It is permitted to teach the Dharma to sick people with exposed chests. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to teach the Dharma to a person with an exposed chest, except in case of illness."

When a person is seated and the bhikṣu is standing; when a person is on a high seat and the bhikṣu is below; when a person is lying down and the bhikṣu is sitting; when a person is in front and the bhikṣu is behind; when a person is on the road and the bhikṣu is off the road; teaching the Dharma to a person with head covered, to a person with robe thrown back, to a person with robe thrown back on left and right, to a person holding an umbrella over themselves, to a person riding a mount, to a person leaning on a staff: all are as stated above.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus taught about the sufferings of hell to people carrying swords or bows and arrows. Those people, upon hearing this, became extremely angry and struck or shot the bhikṣus, killing them. The senior bhikṣus heard this and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish rules to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, these precepts should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to teach the Dharma to a person carrying a sword."

"One should learn not to teach the Dharma to a person carrying a bow and arrows."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus took the food portions at an invitation on behalf of the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus. The group-of-six bhikṣus deliberately delayed and would not let the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus return early. As midday approached, the group-of-seventeen bhikṣus climbed trees to watch for them. The senior bhikṣus saw this and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I will now establish a rule to be learned for the bhikṣus. From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to climb a tree taller than a person."

At that time, there was a bhikṣu traveling toward the country of Kośala who encountered a dangerous animal on the road but dared not climb a tree, and was killed by the animal. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "From now on, this precept should be recited as follows:

"One should learn not to climb a tree taller than a person, except for an important reason."

An important reason means: dangerous animals and other perils; this is called an important reason.

For bhikṣuṇīs, except for urinating and defecating on living grass and vegetables, all the rest is as above.

For śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs: a duṣkṛta offense.

---

## First Division, Part Eight: Seven Methods for Settling Disputes (Sapta Adhikaraṇa-śamatha)

"Where did it arise that the rule of settlement in the presence of the assembly should be applied, and the settlement in the presence of the assembly was applied?" The answer: "In the city of Campā." "On whose account did it arise?" The answer: "The group-of-six bhikṣus."

"Where did it arise that the rule of settlement by recollection should be applied, and the settlement by recollection was applied?" The answer: "In the city of Rājagṛha." "On whose account did it arise?" The answer: "Dharmapāla, son of the Licchavi."

"Where did it arise that the rule of settlement by non-insanity should be applied, and the settlement by non-insanity was applied?" The answer: "In the city of Rājagṛha." "On whose account did it arise?" The answer: "The bhikṣu Gagga."

"Where did it arise that the rule of settlement by self-confession should be applied, and the settlement by self-confession was applied?" The answer: "In the city of Śrāvastī." "On whose account did it arise?" The answer: "On account of a certain bhikṣu."

"Where did it arise that the rule of settlement by majority vote should be applied, and the settlement by majority vote was applied?" The answer: "In the city of Śrāvastī." "On whose account did it arise?" The answer: "On account of many bhikṣus."

"Where did it arise that the rule of settlement by spreading grass should be applied, and the settlement by spreading grass was applied?" The answer: "In the city of Śrāvastī." "On whose account did it arise?" The answer: "On account of many bhikṣus."

"Where did it arise that the rule of settlement by investigation of the original statement should be applied, and the settlement by investigation of the original statement was applied?" The answer: "In the city of Śrāvastī." "On whose account did it arise?" The answer: "Udāyin."

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle 11

*Note: The source text provided for this fascicle is empty. There is no Chinese text to translate.*

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Eleven

### Second Division, First Section: Rules for Bhikṣuṇīs, Pārājika Offenses

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the elder Upāli asked the Buddha: "The World-Honored One has already established a precept for the bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu, having trained together with other bhikṣus in the rules of training, with weakened precepts and without renouncing them, engages in sexual intercourse, even with an animal, that bhikṣu commits a pārājika offense and is not to dwell together with the Sangha.' How should I uphold this precept? Should it be upheld as a single-division Sangha or as a two-division Sangha?" The Buddha said: "It should be upheld as a two-division Sangha. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, having trained together with other bhikṣuṇīs in the rules of training, with weakened precepts and without renouncing them, willfully engages in sexual intercourse, even with an animal, that bhikṣuṇī commits a pārājika offense and is not to dwell together with the Sangha."

At that time, the elder Upāli again asked the Buddha: "The World-Honored One has already established a precept for the bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu, whether in a village or in an uninhabited place, with a thieving mind takes what is not given, and so forth, down to not dwelling together.' How should I uphold this?" The Buddha said: "It should be upheld as a two-division Sangha. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, whether in a village or in an uninhabited place, with a thieving mind takes what is not given, and a king or a great minister seizes, binds, kills, or banishes her, saying: 'You are a thief! You are a fool!' That bhikṣuṇī commits a pārājika offense and is not to dwell together with the Sangha."

At that time, Upāli again asked the Buddha: "The World-Honored One has already established a precept for the bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu, whether a human or a human-like being, kills oneself, and so forth, down to not dwelling together.' How should I uphold this?" The Buddha said: "It should be upheld as a two-division Sangha. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, whether a human or a human-like being, kills oneself, or provides a knife or poison to kill, or instructs someone to kill, or instructs someone to kill themselves, praises death, extols death, saying: 'Bah! What use is living an evil life? Death is better than life,' with such intent, and kills in accordance with that intent; through such various causes and conditions, if that person dies because of this, that bhikṣuṇī commits a pārājika offense and is not to dwell together with the Sangha."

At that time, Upāli again asked the Buddha: "The World-Honored One has already established a precept for the bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu, without knowing or seeing, claims superhuman dharmas, and so forth, down to not dwelling together.' How should I uphold this?" The Buddha said: "It should be upheld as a two-division Sangha. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, without knowing or seeing superhuman dharmas, the noble and excellent fulfillment, claims of herself: 'I know thus, I see thus.' That bhikṣuṇī at a later time, whether questioned or not questioned, seeking to be freed from the offense and seeking purification, says: 'I did not know but said I knew, did not see but said I saw; it was empty, deceitful, and false speech.' Except in the case of overestimation, that bhikṣuṇī commits a pārājika offense and is not to dwell together with the Sangha."

At that time, Viśākhā had a son-in-law named Mṛgāra. Mṛgāra respected Viśākhā just as he would respect his own mother, and people thus came to call her "Viśākhā, Mṛgāra's mother." Her grandson was named Śīlabhadra. Śīlabhadra constantly had his mind fixed upon the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā. Later he invited the bhikṣuṇī Sangha, but Thullanandā claimed illness and did not go, remaining together with a young śrāmaṇerī to guard the monastery. When the time came, the bhikṣuṇī Sangha all went to his house. Śīlabhadra personally served the food and asked: "Why has Thullanandā not come?" The bhikṣuṇīs replied: "Because of her illness, the Sangha assigned her to guard the dwelling. That is why she did not come." After he had served food to the senior seats, he quickly went to ask: "What ailment are you suffering from?" She replied: "All my joints ache!" He then massaged her. The bhikṣuṇī said: "You may massage everywhere, but you must not engage in sexual conduct!" After the massage, he asked: "What do you need?" She replied: "I need dried dates." He went and bought them for her. The bhikṣuṇī held the dates in her hands and asked: "Do you see these dried dates?" He replied: "I see them!" The bhikṣuṇī said: "If a person fixes their mind on a place where sexual conduct cannot be practiced, their spirit shrivels up just like these!" Thereupon, Śīlabhadra engaged in various forms of bodily contact with the bhikṣuṇī and then departed. When the bhikṣuṇīs returned from the meal and entered the gate, they saw him from afar, and all suspected that he had committed impure conduct with Thullanandā. They asked: "Have you broken your celibate conduct?" She replied: "I have not broken my celibate conduct. I only engaged in bodily contact with a man!" The young śrāmaṇerī also confirmed: "It was like this!" The bhikṣuṇīs reproached her in various ways, saying: "The Buddha has in various ways censured bodily contact with men and in various ways praised not touching men's bodies! How could you do such an evil thing?" After reproaching her, they went to the Buddha and reported the matter. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and asked Thullanandā: "Is this true?" She replied: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached her in various ways as above, and then told the bhikṣus: "For the sake of ten benefits, I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, with passionate desire and a changed mind, accepts various forms of touching and rubbing from a man: below the hairline, above the knees, and behind the elbows; that bhikṣuṇī commits a pārājika offense and is not to dwell together with the Sangha."

A bhikṣuṇī means: one who has received full ordination through the procedure of a motion followed by three announcements.

The explanation of 'with passionate desire and a changed mind' and what follows is as stated in the precept concerning a bhikṣu touching a woman's body.

At that time, various bhikṣuṇīs accepted men holding their hands, holding their robes, making appointments, walking alone together, dwelling alone together, speaking alone together, sitting alone together on one seat, and being physically intimate with men. Because of this defiled desire, they no longer delighted in the Way. Some returned to lay life, and some joined non-Buddhist orders.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā put on newly dyed robes, rubbed and adorned her body, painted and decorated her eyebrows and eyes, and went to a crowded place. Various men grasped her hand and grasped her robe, saying: "Your hands are soft and lovely! Your robes are fine and smooth!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and reproached her in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Did you really commit these eight acts?" They replied: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways, and then told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, with passionate desire and a changed mind, accepts a man holding her hand, holding her robe, making an appointment, walking alone together, dwelling alone together, speaking alone together, sitting alone together on one seat, or being physically intimate with a man; if all eight acts are fulfilled, that bhikṣuṇī commits a pārājika offense and is not to dwell together with the Sangha."

'Holding the hand' means: from the elbow forward.

'Holding the robe' means: robes worn on the body.

'Making an appointment' means: making an appointment to go to a certain place for sexual conduct or bodily contact, and going there.

'Walking alone together' means: walking alone with a man on the same road.

'Dwelling alone together' means: dwelling alone with a man in one place.

'Speaking alone together' means: speaking alone with a man in one place.

'Sitting alone together on one seat' means: sitting alone with a man on one bed.

'Being physically intimate with a man' means: when sitting together on one seat, turning one's body toward the man.

If she commits holding hands, up to turning her body toward the man, each one is a thullaccaya. If she commits seven of the acts and has already repented accordingly, but later commits one more act making eight, it also constitutes a pārājika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, the bhikṣu Chanda repeatedly committed offenses: getting on and off his bed improperly, eating repeatedly, eating as a separate group, entering a village at an improper time without informing a virtuous bhikṣu. The bhikṣu Sangha imposed on him the act of suspension for not acknowledging offenses. His sister, the bhikṣuṇī Uccā, came and went, spoke with him, and gave him robes and food. The bhikṣuṇīs saw this and said: "Sister! This bhikṣu has already had the act of suspension for not acknowledging offenses imposed upon him by the harmonious bhikṣu Sangha. The bhikṣus do not dwell with him, do not share activities with him, and do not speak with him. How can you come and go, speak with him, and give him robes and food?" Uccā said: "This is my younger brother! If I do not look after him, who will?" The senior bhikṣuṇīs reproached her in various ways and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and asked Uccā: "Is this true?" She replied: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached her in various ways, and then told the bhikṣus: "A bhikṣuṇī who is on friendly terms with Uccā should be sent to admonish her privately: 'Do not follow a bhikṣu upon whom the Sangha has imposed the act of suspension for not acknowledging offenses!' If she accepts, good. If she does not accept, many bhikṣuṇīs should go and admonish her. If she accepts, good. If she still does not accept, the bhikṣuṇī Sangha should go and admonish her." The bhikṣus received the instruction. The bhikṣuṇīs sent one bhikṣuṇī to admonish her, up to the bhikṣuṇī Sangha admonishing her, but she did not accept any of them. The senior bhikṣuṇīs reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha, reproached Uccā in various ways from afar, and then told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī knows that the Sangha has lawfully imposed the act of suspension for not acknowledging offenses upon a bhikṣu, and the bhikṣus do not dwell with him, do not share activities with him, and do not speak with him, yet she follows him; and the bhikṣuṇīs say to this bhikṣuṇī: 'Sister! This bhikṣu has had the act of suspension for not acknowledging offenses imposed by the bhikṣu Sangha. The bhikṣus do not dwell with him, do not share activities with him, and do not speak with him. Do not follow him!' If, when so admonished, she firmly holds and does not relinquish, she should be admonished a second and third time. If at the second and third admonition she relinquishes the matter, that is good. If she does not relinquish, that bhikṣuṇī commits a pārājika offense and is not to dwell together with the Sangha."

The remainder is all as stated in the section on Devadatta's schism. Non-offenses are also as stated there.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Xiūxiūmó and the bhikṣuṇī Pōpō always traveled together. Later, Pōpō passed away, and Xiūxiūmó wept for her. The bhikṣuṇīs said: "Do not be like this! All conditioned things are subject to destruction. As the Buddha has said, parting from those one loves is inevitable; nothing endures forever. If conditioned dharmas did not decay and disperse, that would be impossible!" Xiūxiūmó said: "I will no longer weep for her! Why? Because when she was alive, she did not practice celibate conduct!" The bhikṣuṇīs asked: "How do you know?" She replied: "She traveled with me, and I personally saw her engage in sexual intercourse with a man." The bhikṣuṇīs reproached her, saying: "How could you conceal her offense when she was alive and only reveal it after her death?" The senior bhikṣuṇīs reproached her in various ways and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and asked Xiūxiūmó: "Is this true?" She replied: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached her in various ways, and then told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī sees another bhikṣuṇī commit a pārājika offense and conceals it; and that bhikṣuṇī at a later time, whether still present, or dead, or gone far away, or expelled, or having abandoned the Way, or having undergone a change of sex, says: 'I previously personally saw her commit a pārājika offense'; that bhikṣuṇī commits a pārājika offense and is not to dwell together with the Sangha."

Concealment: From dawn to the beginning of the first watch of the night, each interval is a duṣkṛta. From the beginning of the first watch of the night until before the appearance of the dawn, each interval is a thullaccaya. When the dawn appears, it is a pārājika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she wishes to speak but there is no bhikṣuṇī available, she has not yet been able to speak; if she enters the great cessation samādhi, or if one of the eight difficulties arises, these are all non-offenses.

The eight difficulties are: first, illness; second, a king; third, bandits; fourth, flood; fifth, fire; sixth, robes and bowl; seventh, life; eighth, celibate conduct.

### Second Division, Second Section: Rules for Bhikṣuṇīs, Saṅghāvaśeṣa Offenses

At that time, the elder Upāli asked the Buddha: "The World-Honored One has already established a precept for the bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu acts as a go-between, and so forth, down to even one act of intercourse, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa.' How should I uphold this?" The Buddha said: "It should be upheld as a two-division Sangha. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī acts as a go-between, whether for the purpose of illicit relations, conveying a man's intention to a woman or conveying a woman's intention to a man, even for one act of intercourse; that bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

The elder Upāli again asked: "The World-Honored One has already established a precept for the bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu, being himself improper, out of malicious anger falsely accuses with a baseless pārājika charge a bhikṣu who has committed no pārājika, and so forth, down to saṅghāvaśeṣa.' How should I uphold this?" The Buddha said: "It should be upheld as a two-division Sangha. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, being herself improper, out of malicious anger falsely accuses with a baseless pārājika charge a bhikṣuṇī who has committed no pārājika, wishing to destroy her celibate conduct; and that bhikṣuṇī at a later time, whether questioned or not questioned, says: 'This matter had no basis; I accused out of anger'; that bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

The elder Upāli again asked the Buddha: "The World-Honored One has already established a precept for the bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu, being himself improper, out of malicious anger takes a fragment or something resembling a fragment from a different matter, and so forth, down to saṅghāvaśeṣa.' How should I uphold this?" The Buddha said: "It should be upheld as a two-division Sangha. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, being herself improper, out of malicious anger takes a fragment or something resembling a fragment from a different matter and makes a pārājika accusation against a bhikṣuṇī who has committed no pārājika, wishing to destroy her celibate conduct; and that bhikṣuṇī at a later time, whether questioned or not questioned, says: 'I took a fragment or something resembling a fragment from a different matter and, abiding in anger, accused that bhikṣuṇī'; this is an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

At that time, the Śākyas made an agreement among themselves: "We shall not intermarry with those of common birth. If anyone violates this, they shall be severely punished!" At that time, a Śākya woman named Kṛṣṇā Licchavī lost her husband. Her husband's younger brother wanted to take her as his wife. The woman refused. He asked three times, but she firmly refused to consent. He then thought: "This woman must be having an affair with another man. I shall kill her!" He arranged a gathering under the pretext of making an offering for his brother, and told her: "A gathering is being made for your husband. Come and offer incense!" The woman went. After she arrived, he made her drink wine until she was intoxicated, then committed impure acts with her. Afterward, he scratched and wounded her flesh with his nails, and reported to the authorities, saying: "The Śākya woman Kṛṣṇā Licchavī is my wife, and she has now committed adultery with an outsider!" The authorities immediately sent officers to arrest her. When the woman sobered up from her intoxication, she saw that her body was injured and wounded everywhere, and she thought: "This man will certainly have me killed!" She then fled to the city of Śrāvastī and went to the bhikṣuṇīs' quarters to leave home and study the Way. The authorities could not arrest her and learned that she had gone to Śrāvastī. They sent a letter of transfer to King Prasenajit, saying: "A woman of our country has committed a crime punishable by death and has fled to your country. Please return her to us! If any criminal from your country flees to our land, we shall likewise return them." At that time, King Prasenajit asked his attendants: "Has this woman entered our country?" They replied: "Yes! The bhikṣuṇīs have already ordained her. Your Majesty previously issued a decree that if anyone within the kingdom commits offenses against bhikṣus or bhikṣuṇīs, they shall be severely punished. Since she has already gone forth, no one dares insult her." The king then replied to the letter, saying: "This woman indeed came to our country, but she has already gone forth. She cannot be pursued and punished. If there are other matters, we shall respectfully follow your instructions!" The Śākyas then censured and reproached, saying: "Whenever there is such a crime, it can no longer be punished. This brings disorder to our country! These bhikṣuṇīs no longer distinguish between those who may be ordained and those who may not. What Way is this? They have no conduct of śramaṇas and destroy the dharma of śramaṇas!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Is this true?" They replied: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways, and then told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī knowingly ordains a woman who has committed a crime as a bhikṣuṇī, that bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

Furthermore, there were women who were thieves, stolen women, and women condemned to death. Laypeople told them: "If you can go forth, I will spare your life." They then sought to go forth and told the bhikṣuṇīs: "Please ordain and save us!" The bhikṣuṇīs said: "The Buddha has not permitted us to ordain people like you." They reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If the master permits, bhikṣuṇīs are allowed to ordain them. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī knowingly ordains a woman who has committed a crime without the permission of the master, that bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

Furthermore, there were women who had committed crimes and could not go forth in the Buddha's dharma, so they entered non-Buddhist orders. Laypeople later saw them. Some said: "These are our criminal women. We should strip them of their non-Buddhist robes!" Others said: "Going forth among non-Buddhists is already a heavy punishment in itself. Let them go!" They were then released. These women later came to the bhikṣuṇīs and said: "Sisters! It was not that we did not revere and have faith in the Buddha-dharma when we went forth among non-Buddhists. Since the sisters would not ordain us, we were compelled and had no choice but to enter the non-Buddhist orders! Those whom we feared have now permitted us to go forth. Please have compassion and ordain us!" The bhikṣuṇīs did not know what to do and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If a woman who committed crimes has previously already gone forth, she is now permitted to be ordained. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī knowingly ordains as a renunciant a woman who has committed a crime without the permission of the master, except for one who has previously gone forth; that bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

'Having committed a crime' means: if she has committed adultery, or if she has committed theft; this is called 'having committed a crime.'

'Master' means: the one who has the authority over life and death; this is called the 'master.'

If a bhikṣuṇī forms the intention to ordain such a woman, and in the process of making preparations up to assembling the Sangha, before the three announcements of the formal act are completed, all are duṣkṛtas. When the three announcements of the formal act are completed, for the preceptress, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa; for the remaining nuns in the assembly, it is a thullaccaya.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Uccā repeatedly committed offenses: getting on and off her bed improperly, eating repeatedly, eating as a separate group, entering others' homes at improper times. The bhikṣuṇī Sangha imposed on her the punishment of suspension for not acknowledging offenses. At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā, knowing that Uccā's mind was not yet subdued and that she was not respectful toward the Sangha, did not follow the assembly but instead, with her own retinue, went outside the boundary and lifted the suspension for her. After that bhikṣuṇī had her suspension lifted, she became even more arrogant and disrespectful toward the Sangha. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and reproached in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and asked Thullanandā: "Is this true?" She replied: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached her in various ways, and then told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī knows that the Sangha has lawfully suspended a bhikṣuṇī, and that the bhikṣuṇī's mind is not yet subdued and she is not compliant with the Sangha, yet without following the assembly, she goes with her own retinue outside the boundary to lift the suspension; that bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

During the intention to lift the suspension and the preparations, up to before the three announcements of the formal act are completed, all are duṣkṛtas. When the three announcements of the formal act are completed, for the one performing the formal act, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa; for the remaining members of the assembly, it is a thullaccaya.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs traveling alone on the road were seen by laypeople, who teased them with crude and lewd speech, or seized and groped them, or attempted to commit impure acts with them.

Furthermore, many bhikṣuṇīs were traveling with a merchant caravan. Thullanandā saw a man and became mentally attached to him, gradually falling behind. The bhikṣuṇīs said to her: "Why don't you hurry and keep up with the group? This place is dangerous. Don't let wicked people rob you!" She replied: "Do you see the person coming behind us?" The bhikṣuṇīs said: "We see him!" Thullanandā said: "When I see this man, my heart is greatly delighted and attached to him!" The bhikṣuṇīs reproached her, saying: "How can you become attached to a man while on the road?"

Furthermore, many bhikṣuṇīs crossed a river to collect cow dung. After they crossed, the water rose and they could not return, and they were plundered by bandits.

Furthermore, some bhikṣuṇīs slept alone and lost their robes and bowls, or had their celibate conduct violated. The senior bhikṣuṇīs who saw and heard of these matters reproached them in various ways and reported everything to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Is this true?" They replied: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways, and then told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī travels alone, sleeps alone, crosses water alone, or lags behind alone on the road out of attachment to a man; that bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

At that time, there were bhikṣuṇīs on the road who felt suspicious and fearful, and so ran toward a village to a relative's house. Furthermore, there were bhikṣuṇīs who were exhausted from traveling, or who were old and sick, and could not keep up with the group; or there was shallow water, or there was a bridge or boat, and they dared not cross alone; or there was a place to sleep, but they feared the men there and dared not sleep alone in another place. The bhikṣuṇīs did not know what to do and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "When there are valid reasons, they are permitted to go alone as they wish. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī travels alone, sleeps alone, crosses water alone, or lags behind alone on the road out of attachment to a man, except when there are valid reasons; that bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented. Valid reasons are: when running in fear, when old, sick, or exhausted and unable to keep up with companions, where the water is narrow and shallow or there is a bridge or boat, and where one fears men. These are called valid reasons."

If she travels alone through a place without villages, for half a yojana; or if there are villages, from one village to the next village; both are saṅghāvaśeṣa offenses.

If she falls behind but can see the bhikṣuṇīs from afar without hearing their voices, or can hear their voices without seeing their forms, both are duṣkṛtas.

If she can neither see nor hear, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa.

If she crosses water alone, and the water is ten cubits wide and half a thigh deep, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa; if less, it is a duṣkṛta.

When sleeping, they should be able to reach each other by hand. If they cannot reach each other, during the first watch and the middle watch, it is a thullaccaya; when the dawn appears, it is a saṅghāvaśeṣa.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, a certain householder donated his residence to the bhikṣu Sangha. The bhikṣu Sangha exchanged it with the bhikṣuṇī community for the Andavana. Queen Mallikā later donated the King's Garden to the bhikṣuṇī Sangha. The bhikṣuṇī Sangha demolished the buildings at the previously exchanged site and built new ones at the King's Garden, and the former site became empty land.

At that time, the son of the householder who had donated the residence thought: "My father formerly donated this to the Sangha, and the Sangha exchanged it with the bhikṣuṇīs. Now the bhikṣuṇīs no longer live there. I can take it back and cultivate it." He then took it. The bhikṣuṇīs said: "Do not take the Sangha's land!" He replied: "Although my father donated it to the Sangha, the Sangha no longer uses it. It should revert to me!" The bhikṣuṇīs said: "We have not relinquished this land. Do not make us go to the authorities about you, which would result in your having to pay a large sum!" He refused to return it. They then went to the authorities and reported him, and he was made to pay a large sum. The laypeople saw this and censured them, saying: "How can these bhikṣuṇīs go to the authorities to accuse people, causing them great financial loss? These women receive his offerings and still accuse him; how much more so would they do to others! They have no conduct of śramaṇas and destroy the dharma of śramaṇas!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Is this true?" They replied: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways, and then told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī goes to the authorities to accuse someone, that bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

If a bhikṣuṇī is treated with contempt, she should inform the person's father or mother. If there is no father or mother, she should inform the person's relatives. If there are no relatives, she should inform bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, or upāsikās. If bhikṣus or bhikṣuṇīs have the ability but do not provide protection, it is a duṣkṛta.

When speaking, she should say: "That person has treated me with contempt."

She should say "Admonish them on my behalf!" but should not bring a legal accusation.

If she goes to the authorities to accuse someone, each round trip constitutes one saṅghāvaśeṣa.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, there was a merchant whose wife had died. He thought: "Where should I now seek a good wife?" At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Caṇḍā Sumanā had a disciple named Sumā, who was of exceptional beauty. When he saw her, he became mentally attached and thought: "If I lure her with food, perhaps I may achieve my aim." He said to her: "If you need ghee, oil, honey, rock sugar, staple food, or non-staple food, you may obtain them all from me." The bhikṣuṇī then went to obtain them. After they became familiar with each other, he asked the bhikṣuṇī: "Do you know the intention behind my giving you food?" She replied: "You give me food in order to gain merit." He said: "That is not the reason! I have lost my wife. Seeing your pure cultivation, I am greatly attracted to you. Can you condescend?" She replied: "I cannot!" The man said again: "Become my wife, and I will give you rare treasures, extremely fine clothing, food at all proper times, and ensure you lack nothing." She again replied as before. Other merchants saw this and helped pressure and threaten her, saying: "If you do not wish to become his wife, why do you accept his food and drink? If you truly refuse, we shall take your robes and bowl!" Others said: "Quickly send her away, lest people hear of this! If the king learns of it, he will certainly punish us severely!" The laypeople heard and censured them, saying: "How can a bhikṣuṇī accept food and drink from a man who is infatuated with her? These women have no conduct of śramaṇas and destroy the dharma of śramaṇas!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached in various ways, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and asked the bhikṣuṇī Sumā: "Is this true?" She replied: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached her in various ways, and then told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī accepts food and drink from a man who has a mind of attachment toward her, that bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

There were men who invited bhikṣuṇīs for the morning meal and the afternoon meal, and developed a mind of attachment during the process. The bhikṣuṇīs knew this but did not dare accept, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣuṇī has no mind of attachment and accepts food and drink from a man who has a mind of attachment, there is no grounds for a saṅghāvaśeṣa offense. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, having a mind of attachment herself, accepts food and drink from a man who has a mind of attachment toward her, that bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

Furthermore, there was a bhikṣuṇī who had a mind of attachment and received food and drink from a man with a mind of attachment. After receiving it, she felt doubtful: "Have I perhaps committed a saṅghāvaśeṣa offense!" She then brought the food back and gave it to other bhikṣuṇīs. The other bhikṣuṇīs asked: "This food and drink are delicious. Why don't you eat them?" She explained the matter in full. The other bhikṣuṇīs said: "What you fear, we should also fear!" They reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣuṇī who has a mind of attachment personally receives food from a man with a mind of attachment but does not eat it and gives it to others, this is not an offense. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, having a mind of attachment herself, personally receives and eats food from a man who has a mind of attachment toward her, that bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, that merchant thought again: "Previously I tried to lure that bhikṣuṇī with food, but too little time had passed, which is why it did not succeed. Now I should again lure her with food; over a longer time, her mind may change!" He went to her and said: "Before I spoke frivolously and became presumptuous. Please accept my apology! From now on, take food from me as before." The bhikṣuṇī said: "Enough, enough, sir! You previously caused me trouble, bringing severe reproach from the Buddha, and a precept was established for the bhikṣuṇīs because of it. How could I today allow myself to be disgraced again?" The bhikṣuṇī's preceptress said to her: "Before you were able to obtain much fine food and drink. Why can you no longer obtain it?" She replied: "That man previously troubled me, and the Buddha established a precept because of it. I dare not accept from him again!" The preceptress said again: "You just should not generate a mind of attachment yourself. What difficulty is there in accepting food? If he generates a mind of attachment, what concern is that of yours?" The bhikṣuṇī reproached her preceptress with displeasure, saying: "The Buddha has in various ways censured intimacy with men and praised keeping distance. How can you instruct me to accept food and drink from a man with a mind of attachment?" The bhikṣuṇīs heard and reproached the teacher, saying: "How can you instruct your disciple to accept food from a man with a mind of attachment?" After reproaching her in various ways, they reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and asked Caṇḍā Sumanā: "Is this true?" She replied: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached her in various ways, and then told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī instructs another bhikṣuṇī with these words: 'You just should not generate a mind of attachment yourself. What difficulty is there in accepting food and drink from a man with a mind of attachment?' That bhikṣuṇī has committed an initial saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

If she makes this instruction, each utterance is a saṅghāvaśeṣa.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

Upāli again asked: "The World-Honored One has already established a precept for the bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu, striving to break up the harmonious Sangha, and so forth, down to saṅghāvaśeṣa.' How should I uphold this?" The Buddha said: "It should be upheld as a two-division Sangha. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī strives to break up the harmonious Sangha, and the bhikṣuṇīs say to this bhikṣuṇī: 'Do not strive to break up the harmonious Sangha. You should be in harmony with the Sangha. When the Sangha is in harmony, there is joy and no disputes; with one mind and one training, like water and milk blended together, they jointly promote the Teacher's instruction and dwell in ease.' If, when so admonished, she firmly holds and does not relinquish, she should be admonished a second and third time. If at the second and third admonition she relinquishes the matter, that is good. If she does not relinquish, that bhikṣuṇī, after the three admonitions, has committed a saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

Upāli again asked: "The World-Honored One has already established a precept for the bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu assists in breaking up the harmonious Sangha, and so forth, down to saṅghāvaśeṣa.' How should I uphold this?" The Buddha said: "It should be upheld as a two-division Sangha. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī assists in breaking up the harmonious Sangha, whether one, two, or many, and says to the bhikṣuṇīs: 'What this bhikṣuṇī says is spoken with knowledge, not without knowledge; she speaks dharma, not non-dharma; she speaks vinaya, not non-vinaya; all of this is agreeable to our minds.' The bhikṣuṇīs say to this bhikṣuṇī: 'Do not say: "What this bhikṣuṇī says is spoken with knowledge, not without knowledge; she speaks dharma, not non-dharma; she speaks vinaya, not non-vinaya; all of this is agreeable to our minds." Why? This bhikṣuṇī does not speak with knowledge, does not speak dharma, and does not speak vinaya. Do not delight in assisting in breaking up the harmonious Sangha. You should delight in assisting in maintaining the harmony of the Sangha. When the Sangha is in harmony, there is joy and no disputes; with one mind and one training, like water and milk blended together, they jointly promote the Teacher's instruction and dwell in ease.' If, when so admonished, she firmly holds and does not relinquish, she should be admonished a second and third time. If at the second and third admonition she relinquishes the matter, that is good. If she does not relinquish, that bhikṣuṇī, after the three admonitions, has committed a saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

Upāli again asked: "The World-Honored One has already established a precept for the bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu is of evil disposition, difficult to speak with, and having trained in the same precepts with other bhikṣus, repeatedly commits offenses, and so forth, down to saṅghāvaśeṣa.' How should I uphold this?" The Buddha said: "It should be upheld as a two-division Sangha. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī is of evil disposition, difficult to speak with, and having trained in the same precepts with other bhikṣuṇīs, repeatedly commits offenses; and when the bhikṣuṇīs admonish her regarding her offenses in accordance with dharma and vinaya, she responds: 'Venerable sisters! Do not speak to me about what is good or bad. I likewise will not speak to you about good or bad.' The bhikṣuṇīs say to her again: 'Do not make yourself impossible to speak with. You should speak words of dharma to the bhikṣuṇīs, and the bhikṣuṇīs should likewise speak words of dharma to you. Thus through mutual instruction, mutually helping to remove offenses, the Tathāgata's assembly is accomplished.' If, when so admonished, she firmly holds and does not relinquish, she should be admonished a second and third time. If at the second and third admonition she relinquishes the matter, that is good. If she does not relinquish, that bhikṣuṇī, after the three admonitions, has committed a saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

Upāli again asked: "The World-Honored One has already established a precept for the bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu, dwelling near a village, engages in evil conduct and corrupts households, and so forth, down to saṅghāvaśeṣa.' How should I uphold this?" The Buddha said: "It should be upheld as a two-division Sangha. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, dwelling near a village, engages in evil conduct and corrupts households; her evil conduct is seen, heard, and known by all; her corrupting of households is also seen, heard, and known by all. The bhikṣuṇīs say to this bhikṣuṇī: 'You engage in evil conduct and corrupt households. Your evil conduct is seen, heard, and known by all; your corrupting of households is also seen, heard, and known by all. Leave! You should not live here!' That bhikṣuṇī says: 'The venerable sisters are acting out of partiality, anger, delusion, and fear. Why? There are other bhikṣuṇīs who have committed the same offenses, yet some are driven out and some are not.' The bhikṣuṇīs say to her again: 'Do not say: "The venerable sisters are acting out of partiality, anger, delusion, and fear. There are other bhikṣuṇīs who have committed the same offenses, yet some are driven out and some are not." You engage in evil conduct and corrupt households. Your evil conduct is seen, heard, and known by all; your corrupting of households is also seen, heard, and known by all. Abandon this speech about acting out of partiality, anger, delusion, and fear. Leave! You should not live here.' If, when so admonished, she firmly holds and does not relinquish, she should be admonished a second and third time. If at the second and third admonition she relinquishes the matter, that is good. If she does not relinquish, that bhikṣuṇī, after the three admonitions, has committed a saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Xiūxiūmó and the bhikṣuṇī Pōpō engaged in evil conduct together, had an evil reputation, mutually concealed each other's offenses, and disturbed the Sangha. The bhikṣuṇīs said to them: "You two engage in evil conduct and have an evil reputation. You should separate from each other, abandon these evil deeds and the matter of disturbing the Sangha, and in the Buddha's dharma grow and dwell in ease!" The two bhikṣuṇīs said: "We do not engage in evil conduct, we have no evil reputation, and we do not disturb the Sangha. There are other two bhikṣuṇīs here who engage in evil conduct, have evil reputations, and disturb the Sangha." The bhikṣuṇīs said: "Do not speak thus! Why? There are no other two bhikṣuṇīs here who commit evil and disturb the Sangha; there are only you two. You should separate from each other, abandon these evil deeds and the matter of disturbing the Sangha, and in the Buddha's dharma grow and dwell in ease!" When so admonished, they firmly held and did not relinquish. The bhikṣuṇīs reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha and asked the two bhikṣuṇīs: "Is this true?" They replied: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways, and then told the bhikṣus: "A bhikṣuṇī who is on friendly terms with those two bhikṣuṇīs should be sent to admonish them privately. If they accept, good. If they do not accept, many bhikṣuṇīs should go and admonish them. If they accept, good. If they do not accept, the bhikṣuṇī Sangha should go and admonish them." The bhikṣus received the instruction and ordered the bhikṣuṇīs to go and admonish them. After three attempts, they still did not accept. The senior bhikṣuṇīs reproached them in various ways and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the two-division Sangha, reproached the two bhikṣuṇīs in various ways from afar, and then told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If two bhikṣuṇīs engage in evil conduct together, have an evil reputation, mutually conceal each other's offenses, and disturb the Sangha; and the bhikṣuṇīs say to them: 'You two bhikṣuṇīs engage in evil conduct, have an evil reputation, mutually conceal each other's offenses, and disturb the Sangha. You should separate from each other, abandon these evil deeds and the matter of disturbing the Sangha, and in the Buddha's dharma grow and dwell in ease!' and the two bhikṣuṇīs say: 'We do not engage in evil conduct, we have no evil reputation, we do not mutually conceal each other's offenses, and we do not disturb the Sangha. There are other two bhikṣuṇīs here who engage in evil conduct together and disturb the Sangha'; and the bhikṣuṇīs say again: 'Do not speak thus! Why? There are no other two bhikṣuṇīs here who commit evil and disturb the Sangha; there are only you two. You should separate from each other, abandon these evil deeds and the matter of disturbing the Sangha, and in the Buddha's dharma grow and dwell in ease!' If, when so admonished, they firmly hold and do not relinquish, they should be admonished a second and third time. If at the second and third admonition they relinquish the matter, that is good. If they do not relinquish, those bhikṣuṇīs, after the three admonitions, have committed a saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented."

'Mutually concealing each other's offenses' means: whether saṅghāvaśeṣa, thullaccaya, pāyattika, pratideśanīya, duṣkṛta, or failure to guard one's speech.

'Disturbing the Sangha' means: whether during poṣadha, pravāraṇā, or various formal acts; all are as stated in the section on Devadatta's schism.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Xiūxiūmó and the bhikṣuṇī Pōpō engaged in evil conduct together, had evil reputations, and so forth, down to "in the Buddha's dharma grow and dwell in ease," as stated above. The two bhikṣuṇīs said: "We do not engage in evil conduct, nor do we disturb the Sangha. The Sangha sees that we are weak and looks down on us, and that is why they speak thus!" The bhikṣuṇīs said again: "Do not speak thus! Why? The Sangha does not see you as weak or look down upon you. You yourselves engage in evil conduct and disturb the Sangha. You should separate from each other, abandon these evil deeds and the matter of disturbing the Sangha, and in the Buddha's dharma grow and dwell in ease!" When so admonished, they firmly held and did not relinquish. The senior bhikṣuṇīs reproached them, and so forth, down to "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs," as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If two bhikṣuṇīs engage in evil conduct together, have evil reputations, mutually conceal each other's offenses, and disturb the Sangha; and the bhikṣuṇīs say to them: 'You two bhikṣuṇīs, do not engage in evil conduct together, having evil reputations, mutually concealing each other's offenses, and disturbing the Sangha.
