You should separate from each other, abandon these evil deeds and the matter of disturbing the Sangha, and in the Buddha's dharma grow and dwell in ease!' and the two bhikṣuṇīs say: 'We do not engage in evil conduct, we have no evil reputation, we do not mutually conceal each other's offenses, and we do not disturb the Sangha. The Sangha sees that we are weak and looks down upon us, and that is why they speak thus!' and the bhikṣuṇīs say again: 'Do not speak thus! Why? The Sangha does not see you as weak or look down upon you! You two should separate from each other, abandon these evil deeds and the matter of disturbing the Sangha, and in the Buddha's dharma grow and dwell in ease!' If, when so admonished, they firmly hold and do not relinquish, they should be admonished a second and third time. If at the second and third admonition they relinquish the matter, that is good. If they do not relinquish, those bhikṣuṇīs, after the three admonitions, have committed a saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented." The remainder is as stated above.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, Chanda's mother was named Sumanā. She was of evil disposition, so people came to call her Caṇḍā Sumanā. She liked to quarrel with others. When the Sangha adjudicated her matters, she would say the Sangha acted out of partiality, anger, delusion, and fear. The bhikṣuṇīs said to her: "Do not like to quarrel with others. Do not say: 'The Sangha acts out of partiality, anger, delusion, and fear!' Why? The Sangha does not act out of partiality, anger, delusion, or fear. Abandon this speech, and in the Buddha's dharma grow and dwell in ease!" When so admonished, she firmly held and did not relinquish. And so forth, down to "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs," as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī likes to quarrel with others, and when the Sangha adjudicates her matters, says the Sangha acts out of partiality, anger, delusion, and fear; and the bhikṣuṇīs say to her: 'Do not like to quarrel with others. Do not say: "The Sangha acts out of partiality, anger, delusion, and fear." Why? The Sangha does not act out of partiality, anger, delusion, or fear. Abandon this speech, and in the Buddha's dharma grow and dwell in ease!' If, when so admonished, she firmly holds and does not relinquish, she should be admonished a second and third time. If at the second and third admonition she relinquishes the matter, that is good. If she does not relinquish, that bhikṣuṇī, after the three admonitions, has committed a saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented." The remainder is as stated above.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Caṇḍā Sumanā liked to quarrel with others. When the Sangha adjudicated her matters, she said in anger: "I renounce the Buddha, renounce the Dharma, renounce the Sangha, renounce the precepts, and will become a non-Buddhist! Other śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas also train in precepts and also have a sense of shame. I shall practice the pure celibate conduct among them!" The bhikṣuṇīs said to her: "Do not like to quarrel with others. Do not say: 'I renounce the Buddha, and so forth, down to practicing the pure celibate conduct among them!' Why? Other śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas have no training in precepts and no sense of shame. How could you practice celibate conduct among them? Abandon this evil view, and in the Buddha's dharma grow and dwell in ease!" When so admonished, she firmly held and did not relinquish. And so forth, down to "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs," as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī likes to quarrel with others, and when the Sangha adjudicates her matters, says: 'I renounce the Buddha, renounce the Dharma, renounce the Sangha, renounce the precepts, and will become a non-Buddhist! Other śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas also train in precepts and also have a sense of shame. I shall practice the pure celibate conduct among them!' and the bhikṣuṇīs say to her: 'Do not like to quarrel with others. Do not say: "I renounce the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha!" Why? Other śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas have no training in precepts and no sense of shame. How could you practice celibate conduct among them? Abandon this evil view, and in the Buddha's dharma grow and dwell in ease!' If, when so admonished, she firmly holds and does not relinquish, she should be admonished a second and third time. If at the second and third admonition she relinquishes the matter, that is good. If she does not relinquish, that bhikṣuṇī, after the three admonitions, has committed a saṅghāvaśeṣa offense, which may be repented." The remainder is as stated above.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from Chinese during the Liu Song dynasty by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle 12

*Note: The source text provided for this fascicle is empty. There is no Chinese text to translate.*

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421, Fascicle 12

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

---

## Second Division, Part Three: Rules for Bhikṣuṇīs on Offenses Requiring Forfeiture (Niḥsargika-Pāyattika)

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upāli asked the Buddha: "The World-Honored One has already established precepts for bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu, after completing his three robes and relinquishing the kaṭhina cloth, keeps an extra robe for up to ten days; if beyond that, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika.' And so forth down to: 'If a bhikṣu himself handles gold, silver, or money, or has someone handle it for him, or accepts it with intent, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika.' How should I uphold these?" The Buddha said: "They should be upheld as rules for the twofold Sangha. From now on, these precepts should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, after completing her three robes and relinquishing the kaṭhina cloth, keeps an extra robe for up to ten days; if beyond that, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, after completing her robes and relinquishing the kaṭhina cloth, sleeps apart from any one of the five robes for even one night, except by Sangha procedural act, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, after completing her robes and relinquishing the kaṭhina cloth, obtains out-of-season cloth, if she needs it she should accept it and quickly make it into a robe to be kept. If it is sufficient, good. If not sufficient, she may wait where she expects to obtain more, so as to make it complete, for up to one month. If beyond that, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī begs for cloth from a householder or a householder's wife who is not a relative, except when there is a valid reason, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika. Valid reasons are: having one's robe stolen, losing one's robe, having it burned, having it carried away by water, or having it destroyed. These are the valid reasons."

"If a bhikṣuṇī whose robe has been stolen, lost, burned, carried away by water, or destroyed begs for cloth from a householder or a householder's wife who is not a relative, and the householder or householder's wife wishes to give many robes, the bhikṣuṇī should accept only two robes. If she accepts more than that, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a householder or a householder's wife who is not a relative discusses together: 'We shall provide such-and-such a value of cloth to make a robe and give it to such-and-such a bhikṣuṇī,' and the bhikṣuṇī, without having been previously given an open invitation, goes and asks the householder or householder's wife: 'Are you making a robe for me with such-and-such a value of cloth?' and they answer: 'Yes!' and she then says: 'Excellent, householder, householder's wife! Please make such-and-such a robe for me, for the sake of quality,' it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If householders or householders' wives who are not relatives each discuss separately: 'We shall each provide such-and-such a value of cloth to make a robe and give it to such-and-such a bhikṣuṇī,' and the bhikṣuṇī, without having been previously given an open invitation, goes and asks the householders or householders' wives: 'Are you each making a robe for me with such-and-such a value of cloth?' and they answer: 'Yes!' and she then says: 'Excellent, householders, householders' wives! Please combine them to make one robe for me, for the sake of quality,' it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a king, a great minister, a brāhmaṇa, or a householder, for the sake of a bhikṣuṇī, dispatches a messenger to deliver robe-money, and the messenger arrives at the bhikṣuṇī's place and says: 'Venerable aunt! That king or great minister sends this robe-money; venerable aunt, please accept it!' and the bhikṣuṇī says: 'I should not accept robe-money. If I receive pure cloth, I shall accept it by hand.' The messenger says: 'Venerable aunt, is there an attendant?' The bhikṣuṇī then points out a person. The messenger goes to the attendant and says: 'Such-and-such king or great minister sends this robe-money and gives it for such-and-such bhikṣuṇī. Please accept it, make a robe, and give it to her.' After the messenger has given it, he returns to the bhikṣuṇī and says: 'Venerable aunt! The attendant you indicated, I have already given it to them. When you need the robe, you may go and get it.' The bhikṣuṇī should go to the attendant two or three times and say: 'I need a robe, I need a robe.' If she obtains it, good. If she does not obtain it, she should go four, five, or six times to stand silently before the attendant. If she obtains it, good. If she obtains it by seeking beyond this, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika. If she does not obtain the robe, she should go to the place the messenger came from, either going herself or sending a message, saying: 'The robe-money you sent for such-and-such bhikṣuṇī, the bhikṣuṇī ultimately did not receive it. You should retrieve it yourself; do not let it be lost.' This is how it should be done."

"If a bhikṣuṇī personally goes begging for thread and hires a weaver to weave it into cloth, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a householder or householder's wife has a weaver weave cloth to make a robe for a bhikṣuṇī, and the bhikṣuṇī, without having been previously given an open invitation, goes to the weaver and says: 'Do you know? This cloth is being made for me. Weave it well for me, make it very fine and wide, and I shall separately reward you.' If afterward she gives even one meal or the value of one meal, and the weaver obtains it, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī gives a robe to another bhikṣuṇī, and afterward in anger and displeasure either personally seizes it back or has someone seize it back, saying: 'Return my robe, I am not giving it to you!' it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, knowing that a donor intends to give something to the Sangha, diverts it for her own use, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī is ill and may take the four kinds of tonic medicines to be kept and consumed: ghee, oil, honey, and rock sugar; once received, they may be kept up to seven days. If beyond that, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, during the first or the last rains retreat, ten days before the pravāraṇā, receives urgently offered robes; if she needs them, she should accept them, up until the robe season. If beyond that, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, when her bowl has not yet five mends, begs for a new bowl for the sake of a better one, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī engages in various kinds of buying and selling for profit, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī uses gold, silver, or money for various kinds of buying and selling, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī personally handles gold, silver, or money, or has someone handle it for her, or accepts it with intent, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

---

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā begged for a bowl from a person at a shop. That person bought a bowl and gave it to her. Having received it, she said: "I no longer need this. Please give me ghee instead." He then gave her ghee and sold the bowl he had purchased. The shop neighbors saw this and said: "You are selling goods from the shop. Can you not make a living? Must you also act as a bowl dealer?" He answered: "Thullanandā begged me for a bowl. I bought one to give her, but she no longer wanted it. Then she begged me for ghee, so I gave her ghee, and that is why I am selling this bowl!" The people said: "The way of a beggar is to accept whatever one begs for. Why, having already received something, does she not take it but instead demand something else? These people always speak of having few desires and being content, yet now they are insatiable. They have no conduct of renunciants! They destroy the Dharma of renunciants!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs, hearing this, reproached her in various ways, and the matter was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the twofold Sangha, and asked the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā: "Did you really do this?" She answered: "I really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached her in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī first begs for one thing and, having obtained it, does not use it and then begs for something else, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

For each additional thing begged for, regardless of amount, each one is niḥsargika-pāyattika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs were accepting out-of-season cloth as if it were in-season cloth, and visiting bhikṣuṇīs were then unable to obtain robes. The senior bhikṣuṇīs, seeing this, reproached them in various ways, and the matter was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the twofold Sangha, and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī accepts out-of-season cloth as if it were in-season cloth, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

This cloth should be forfeited to the Sangha and should not be forfeited to one, two, or three bhikṣuṇīs.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

At that time, there was a junior bhikṣuṇī who had few acquaintances. She obtained unfinished cloth but did not know how to make it into a robe. She brought it to the various bhikṣuṇīs and said: "I am the most junior and have few acquaintances. Please make this for me!" The bhikṣuṇīs said: "We have many duties and cannot make it for you. You should go to Thullanandā and ask her; she has many finished robes and might trade with you." The bhikṣuṇī then brought it to Thullanandā, who traded a finished robe for it. Having obtained it, she returned to her dwelling place. The bhikṣuṇīs asked her: "Did you get a robe?" She answered: "I did." They asked: "From whom?" She answered: "Thullanandā." The bhikṣuṇīs said: "Bring it out so we can see!" She brought the robe out and showed it. The bhikṣuṇīs said: "This robe is several times better than your cloth. From the beginning, no one has ever been able to get such a bargain from her. That you have suddenly gotten this is truly extraordinary!" At that time, Thullanandā overheard from the other side of the wall and said to the bhikṣuṇī: "Return my robe! I will not trade with you!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs, hearing this, reproached her in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī trades a robe with another bhikṣuṇī and afterward regrets it and demands it back, if she gets it back, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If both parties regret the trade, or if the robe obtained does not match the original agreement and it is taken back, there is no offense.

---

At that time, the laywoman Viśākhā invited the bhikṣuṇī Sangha and wished to give them menstrual cloths. She sent a message requesting their body measurements, and all sent theirs. Only Thullanandā did not give hers, and when a second message was sent requesting it, she answered: "I have already left behind desire. I no longer have menstruation and do not need this cloth." When Viśākhā had finished making the cloths, she sent a message to the bhikṣuṇīs: "The cloths are finished; please come and collect them!" The bhikṣuṇīs all went and took their seats. At that time, Thullanandā's menstruation had just come, and she went first to take a cloth. The bhikṣuṇīs took theirs in order of seniority, but the cloths were too few and insufficient; one junior bhikṣuṇī did not receive one. Viśākhā asked: "Did everyone get a cloth?" They answered: "One junior did not receive one." She asked: "Why?" They answered: "Thullanandā previously said she did not need one and did not provide her measurements. But now she took one first, which is why there are not enough." Viśākhā said: "How can someone first say she does not need one, and then at the time take one, causing another not to receive one?" The senior bhikṣuṇīs reproached her in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, when the bhikṣuṇīs tell her to take a menstrual cloth, says she does not want one, and then at the time takes one first, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

This cloth should be forfeited to the Sangha and should not be given to one, two, or three bhikṣuṇīs.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she previously said she did not want one, but at the end there are extra and she takes one, there is no offense.

---

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā frequently went in and out of King Prasenajit's palace. The king said: "Venerable aunt, if you need anything, take it from me." She then said: "I need a heavy robe." The king said: "Take one from the inner palace." She then said: "I want the kambala that the king himself wears." The king gave it to her. The ministers criticized and reproached her, saying: "This bhikṣuṇī has gone forth in the unconditioned Dharma, wearing dyed and cut robes. How can she now demand what the king himself wears? Though the king has no stinginess, the recipient should know moderation! These people always speak of having few desires and being content, yet now they are insatiable. They have no conduct of renunciants! They destroy the Dharma of renunciants!"

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Black Licchavī was honored and supported by the Licchavīs of Vaiśālī. Whenever people had legal troubles, she was able to help resolve them, and they were all delighted, saying: "Thanks to the venerable aunt's kindness, we have been freed from punishment and difficulty. If you need anything, we shall offer it to you!" She then said: "I need a heavy robe." They asked: "How much should the heavy robe cost?" She answered: "I need a robe worth a thousand coins!" The people then criticized and reproached her: "Our legal troubles, even though we spent five or six times the cost, do not amount to this price! These people always speak of having few desires and being content, yet now they are insatiable!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs, hearing this, reproached them in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī begs for a heavy robe, she should accept one of cheap value worth four large coins. If she accepts a robe of expensive value, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

A heavy robe means: a robe for the cold season.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā again went to King Prasenajit and asked for the light robe the king himself wore. The bhikṣuṇī Black Licchavī asked the Licchavīs for a light robe worth five hundred coins. The ministers and the people criticized and reproached them, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī begs for a light robe, she should accept one of cheap value worth two and a half large coins. If she accepts a robe of expensive value, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

A light robe means: a garment worn in the hot season.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Kṣemā arrived in Śrāvastī. They held the upoṣadha in the open air, and were troubled by wind, rain, dust, and mosquitoes. The resident bhikṣuṇīs spoke to a householder: "Now the bhikṣuṇī Sangha holds the upoṣadha in the open, and there are such-and-such troubles. As the Buddha has said, the gift of a hall to the Sangha is foremost. Excellent, householder! Please build an upoṣadha hall for the Sangha!" He answered: "I have many duties and cannot build it myself. I will entrust these materials to you, venerable aunts; please arrange it together. When it is finished, inform me, and I shall go personally to donate it to the Sangha." When the bhikṣuṇīs received the materials, they discussed together: "Our robes are worn and tattered. Let us divide these materials and each use them to make robes, and also deal with the hardship of the open-air upoṣadha." Having discussed, they divided them and made robes. When the robes were finished, they told the householder: "You should rejoice! The bhikṣuṇīs' robes were worn and tattered. The materials you gave were divided and used to make robes; the robes are now finished!" When the householder heard this, he criticized and reproached them: "I do not rejoice! Why? Previously you said building an upoṣadha hall was foremost, yet now you have used the materials to make robes!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs, hearing this, reproached them in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī begs from one householder for the Sangha and for a specific purpose, but uses it for something else, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

If one forms the intention to divide it, or makes preparations to do so, each is a duṣkṛta.

The materials should be forfeited to the Sangha and should not be forfeited to one, two, or three bhikṣuṇīs.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she begged for a specific purpose, but the donor afterward himself directed it to be used differently, there is no offense.

---

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Kṣemā held the upoṣadha in the open, and so forth, being troubled by mosquitoes, as described before. The bhikṣuṇīs solicited donations from many places in order to build an upoṣadha hall, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī begs from many householders for the Sangha and for a specific purpose, but uses it for something else, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika." The rest is as stated above.

---

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Kṣemā had no dwelling place. She went to a householder and said: "I have no dwelling place. Build a dwelling for me!" The householder answered as before and gave her the materials. Having obtained them, she thought: "My robes are coarse and tattered. I should use these to make robes; dwelling places are plentiful anyway." She then used them to make robes. When the robes were finished, she went and told the householder, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī begs from one householder for herself for a specific purpose, but uses it for something else, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika." The rest is as stated above.

---

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Kṣemā had no dwelling place, and begged from many householders, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī begs from many householders for herself for a specific purpose, but uses it for something else, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika." The rest is as stated above.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs accumulated many vessels and utensils. In front rooms and back rooms, everywhere there were such things. When a fire broke out and burned the buildings, they carried out the goods ceaselessly. The laypeople who came to fight the fire saw this and asked: "Whose goods are these?" They answered: "They belong to the bhikṣuṇīs." The laypeople then criticized and reproached: "How inauspicious these are! They have gone forth in the unconditioned Dharma, wearing dyed and cut robes, yet they accumulate goods and utensils like kings and great ministers! They always speak of having few desires and being content, yet now they hoard without end! They have no conduct of renunciants! They destroy the Dharma of renunciants!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs, hearing this, reproached them in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī hoards vessels and utensils, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

Vessels and utensils means: utensils for daily use. They are permitted to keep one container each for ghee, oil, honey, incense, medicine, sauce, and vinegar. They are also permitted to keep one pot, one pan, and one ladle each, as well as one small jar for storing rice and food. Keeping more than this is niḥsargika-pāyattika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs accumulated many bowls, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī accumulates many bowls, it is niḥsargika-pāyattika."

They are permitted to keep seven types of coarse bowls: one for holding food and drink, one for holding incense, one for holding medicine, one for holding leftover food, one for spitting into, one for sweeping, and one for urine.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

## Second Division, Part Four: Bhikṣuṇī Rules on Pāyattika Offenses

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upāli asked the Buddha: "The World-Honored One has already established precepts for bhikṣus: 'If a bhikṣu intentionally speaks falsely, it is a pāyattika.' And so forth down to: 'If a bhikṣu knows that a donor intends to give something to the Sangha and diverts it to give to another person, it is a pāyattika.' How should I uphold these?" The Buddha said: "They should be upheld as rules for the twofold Sangha. From now on, these precepts should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī intentionally speaks falsely, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī insults a bhikṣuṇī, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī engages in divisive speech to create discord among bhikṣuṇīs, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī teaches Dharma to a man beyond five or six sentences, except when a woman who can distinguish good from bad speech is present, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, knowing the Sangha has lawfully decided a matter, again reopens it, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī teaches sūtras to a woman who has not received full ordination, reciting together with her, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī shares a room with a woman who has not received full ordination for more than two nights, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, to a woman who has not received full ordination, personally claims to have attained superhuman states, saying: 'I know thus, I see thus,' and it is true, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, knowing a bhikṣuṇī has committed a grave offense, reveals it to a woman who has not received full ordination, except by Sangha procedural act, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī says: 'What use are these petty precepts? When these precepts are recited, they cause people distress!' Denigrating the precepts in this way is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī personally cuts down a living plant, or has someone cut it down, saying: 'Cut this!' it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī intentionally does not respond when questioned, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī falsely accuses a person appointed by the Sangha, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī in the open air personally spreads out Sangha bedding, or has someone spread it out; or if others have spread it and she sits or lies on it; and when leaving, she does not put it away herself, does not ask someone to put it away, and does not entrust someone to put it away, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī in a Sangha dwelling personally spreads out Sangha bedding, or has someone spread it out; or if others have spread it and she sits or lies on it; and when leaving, she does not put it away herself, does not ask someone to put it away, and does not entrust someone to put it away, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, in anger and displeasure, personally drags a bhikṣuṇī out of a Sangha dwelling, or has someone drag her out, saying: 'Get out! Begone! Do not stay here!' it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, knowing that another has already spread out bedding, comes afterward and forcefully spreads her own, or has someone spread it, thinking: 'If she is unhappy, she will leave on her own,' it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, on the upper story of a Sangha multi-story building, forcefully sits or lies on a rope-bed or wooden bed with pointed legs, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, knowing that water contains living creatures, takes it for watering mud, or for drinking, eating, or other uses, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī eats more than one meal, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pāyattika. Valid reasons are: when ill, during the robe season, and when distributing robes. These are the valid reasons."

"If a bhikṣuṇī accepts a separate invitation to a group meal, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pāyattika. Valid reasons are: when ill, during the robe season, when distributing robes, when making robes, when traveling on a road, when traveling by boat, at a great assembly. These are the valid reasons."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, when not ill, at a place where one meal is offered, exceeds one meal, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī goes to a lay household and is generously offered much food and drink, such as cakes or parched grain; if she does not eat at that house, she should accept two or three bowls. After leaving, she should share the food with other bhikṣuṇīs. If she is not ill and accepts more than this, or does not share with other bhikṣuṇīs, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī has finished eating and eats again without performing the procedure for leftover food, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, knowing another bhikṣuṇī has finished eating and has not performed the procedure for leftover food, forcefully urges her to eat, wishing to cause her to commit an offense, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī puts food in her mouth without having it formally offered, except for tasting food, toothwood, and water, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī eats at the wrong time, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī eats overnight food, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī sits in a secluded place with a man at a house where food is being served, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī goes to watch an army setting out on march, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, having a valid reason, goes to the army and stays for up to two or three nights; if beyond that, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, having a valid reason, goes to the army and stays for two or three nights, and watches the army in battle formation or engaged in combat, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī says: 'As I understand the Buddha's teaching, the dharmas said to obstruct the path are not actually able to obstruct the path,' and the bhikṣuṇīs say to this bhikṣuṇī: 'Do not say this! Do not slander the Buddha! Do not falsely accuse the Buddha! The Buddha has said that obstructive dharmas truly obstruct the path. Abandon this evil wrong view!' If, when thus admonished, she firmly holds to it and does not abandon it, she should be admonished a second and third time. If after the second and third admonition she abandons it, good. If she does not abandon it, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, knowing that a bhikṣuṇī has not properly repented and has not abandoned her evil wrong view, speaks with her, sits with her, sleeps in the same place, or shares in activities, it is a pāyattika."

"If a śrāmaṇerī says: 'As I understand the Buddha's teaching, if one indulges in the five sensual pleasures, they cannot obstruct the path,' and the bhikṣuṇīs say to this śrāmaṇerī: 'Do not say this! Do not slander the Buddha! Do not falsely accuse the Buddha! The Buddha has said that the five sensual pleasures obstruct the path and truly obstruct it. You, śrāmaṇerī, should abandon this evil wrong view!' If, when thus instructed, she firmly holds to it and does not abandon it, she should be instructed a second and third time. If after the second and third instruction she abandons it, good. If she does not abandon it, the bhikṣuṇīs should say to this śrāmaṇerī: 'You, get out! From now on, do not say the Buddha is your teacher! Do not walk behind the bhikṣuṇīs! As other śrāmaṇerīs may share lodging with bhikṣuṇīs for two nights, you also do not have this privilege. Foolish one, get out! Begone! Do not stay here!' If a bhikṣuṇī, knowing a śrāmaṇerī has been lawfully expelled, keeps her, employs her, dwells with her, or speaks with her, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī deliberately causes another bhikṣuṇī to have doubts and remorse, thinking: 'Let this bhikṣuṇī be troubled even for a short time,' it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, when the Sangha is deciding a matter, leaves without giving her consent, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī tickles another bhikṣuṇī, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī plays in water, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī shares a room with a man, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī drinks alcohol, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī is disrespectful to the teacher, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī personally digs the ground, or has someone dig it, saying: 'Dig this!' it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, after a dispute, listens in silence, thinking: 'I shall remember what the bhikṣuṇīs say,' it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī accepts an open invitation for four months; if she accepts beyond this, except for a renewed invitation, a self-delivered invitation, or a standing invitation, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī repeatedly commits offenses and, when the bhikṣuṇīs lawfully admonish her, says: 'I will not study this precept! I shall ask other bhikṣuṇīs who are holders of the Dharma and holders of the Vinaya!' it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, at the time of reciting the precepts, says: 'I only now learn that this rule is recited in the fortnightly upoṣadha from the Prātimokṣa,' and the bhikṣuṇīs know that this bhikṣuṇī has already sat through the recitation of precepts two or three times, then the bhikṣuṇī does not escape on the grounds of not knowing; she should be dealt with according to whatever offense she committed. She should be reproached for her ignorance: 'What you did is unwholesome! During the recitation of precepts, you did not listen with a unified mind and did not take it to heart.' It is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī makes an appointment with bandits and travels with them on the road, from one village to another, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī makes an appointment with a man and travels with him on the road, from one village to another, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, when not ill, for the sake of warming herself, personally lights a fire or has someone light one, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī picks up jewels or valuables equivalent to jewels, either personally or by having someone do so, except within the Sangha grounds or at her lodging, it is a pāyattika. If within the Sangha grounds or at her lodging she picks up jewels or valuables equivalent to jewels, and later the owner requests them, she should return them. This is how it should be done."

"If a bhikṣuṇī bathes more than once within half a month, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pāyattika. Valid reasons are: when ill, when working, when traveling, during wind and rain, during hot weather. These are the valid reasons."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, in anger, strikes a bhikṣuṇī, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, in anger, raises her hand against a bhikṣuṇī, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī intentionally frightens a bhikṣuṇī, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī falsely accuses a bhikṣuṇī of a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense without basis, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī says to another bhikṣuṇī: 'Come with me to visit households, and I will give you much delicious food and drink!' and having arrived, does not give it, but says: 'Go away! Sitting with you or talking with you is unpleasant; I prefer to sit alone and talk alone!' doing so to cause distress, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī obtains a new robe, she should mark it with one of three colors: blue, black, or mūla-colored. If she does not mark it with one of the three colors, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, for the sake of a joke, hides a bhikṣuṇī's robe, bowl, sitting cloth, needle case, or any other such personal article, or has someone hide it, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, when the Sangha is deciding a matter, has lawfully given her consent, and afterward raises objections, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī says: 'The bhikṣuṇīs are diverting Sangha property to give to acquaintances,' it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī forcefully seizes a robe that she has given as a pure gift to a bhikṣu, bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, or śrāmaṇerī, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī, having accepted another's invitation, before or after the meal goes to visit other households without informing a nearby bhikṣuṇī, except when there is a valid reason, it is a pāyattika. A valid reason is: during the robe season. This is the valid reason."

"If a bhikṣuṇī stuffs bedding with tūla cotton, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī makes a rope-bed or wooden bed for sitting or lying, the legs should be eight sugata-finger-breadths high, excluding the part inserted into the frame hole. If beyond that, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī makes a needle case out of bone, ivory, or horn, it is a pāyattika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī makes a robe of the sugata robe measurement, or exceeds it, it is a pāyattika. The sugata robe measurement is: nine sugata hand-spans in length and six hand-spans in width. This is called the sugata robe measurement."

"If a bhikṣuṇī knows that a donor intends to give something to the Sangha and diverts it to give to another person, it is a pāyattika."

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs were eating garlic, raw and cooked, morning and afternoon, eating it plain or mixed with food. Their dwellings stank. When laypeople came to visit and smelled the garlic odor, they criticized: "It is just like the kitchen of a lay household!"

Furthermore, bhikṣuṇīs went to the house of a prominent man. The prominent man smelled the garlic odor from their eating and said: "Venerable aunts, please stay away! Your mouths smell of garlic!" The bhikṣuṇīs were embarrassed.

Furthermore, there was a garlic seller who invited the bhikṣuṇīs and gave them garlic, and because of this became impoverished, unable to maintain his food supply. His family members said: "If you cannot give us food, let us go! You can continue being a servant to the bhikṣuṇīs!" The neighbors heard and reproached him: "Your family has no food. What business is it of the bhikṣuṇīs?" He explained the matter in full. Those who did not have faith in the Buddha-dharma said: "Because you associate with the bhikṣuṇīs, you have come to this hardship. If you continue associating with them, it will become even worse! These people left home originally seeking liberation, yet now they crave after delicious flavors! They have no conduct of renunciants! They destroy the Dharma of renunciants!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs, hearing this, reproached them in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī eats garlic, it is a pāyattika."

If she eats raw garlic, each mouthful swallowed is a pāyattika. If she eats cooked garlic, it is a duṣkṛta.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she eats it when ill, or someone forces her to eat it against her will, there is no offense.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs were slapping their vulvas with their hands, giving rise to lustful desire. Some consequently returned to lay life or became followers of other teachings.

Thullanandā also slapped her vulva with her hand, and her vulva became greatly swollen so that she could no longer walk. Her disciple went to the family that regularly provided offerings and said: "The teacher is ill. Please provide food." They gave it. The women of that household then came to inquire about her illness, saying: "Venerable aunt, what ailment do you suffer from?" She answered: "I am ill." They asked: "What kind of illness? We are all women; why not tell us?" She then explained the matter in full. The women then criticized and reproached her: "These people always denigrate desire, thoughts of desire, the fever of desire, and lustful intentions, yet now they do such things! Why not abandon the religious life and enjoy the five sensual pleasures? They have no conduct of renunciants! They destroy the Dharma of renunciants!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs, hearing this, reproached her in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī slaps her vulva with her hand, it is a pāyattika."

If she slaps with her hand, each slap is a pāyattika. If she discharges impure matter, it is a sthūlātyaya.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs made a phallus from gum and inserted it into their vulvas, giving rise to lustful desire. Some consequently returned to lay life or became followers of other teachings.

Furthermore, there was a bhikṣuṇī who made one, tied it to her heel, and inserted it into her vulva. At that time, a śikṣamāṇā was frying oil when a fire broke out and burned the dwelling. The bhikṣuṇī, panicking, forgot to untie it and rushed outside with it hanging from her foot. The people who came to fight the fire saw it and asked: "Venerable aunt, what is that on your foot?" She explained truthfully. They then criticized and reproached her, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī makes a phallus and inserts it into her vulva, it is a pāyattika."

If she discharges impure matter, it is a sthūlātyaya.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs were inserting one to five fingers into their vulvas to wash, injuring the flesh so that blood came out, and becoming ill from this. The senior bhikṣuṇīs, seeing this, reproached them in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī washes her vulva with water, it is a pāyattika."

After the Buddha prohibited washing, they became foul and unclean, and in the hot season worms grew. The bhikṣuṇī Mahāprajāpatī, together with five hundred bhikṣuṇīs, went to the Buddha and said: "World-Honored One! We women have bodies that are foul. We rely on water for cleansing. We beg that it be permitted!" The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "I now permit the bhikṣuṇīs to cleanse with water." After the Buddha permitted it, they again behaved as before. The bhikṣuṇīs, seeing this, reproached them in various ways, and so forth, down to telling the bhikṣus, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī washes her vulva with water, she should use two fingers, up to the first joint. If she exceeds this, it is a pāyattika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If there is a sore inside the vulva, or a worm has entered, or grass or a stone has entered, using a finger beyond the first joint is not an offense.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs shaved hair in two areas, the armpits and the private parts, giving rise to lustful desire. Some consequently returned to lay life or became followers of other teachings.

At that time, Thullanandā also shaved her private area hair. Her patron family was marrying off their daughter, and the daughter wished to see her, so they sent a message summoning her. The bhikṣuṇī went. The family was preparing a bath for the daughter, and the daughter said: "First let the bhikṣuṇī bathe!" They called her to bathe, but she said: "I do not need to bathe." The women then forcefully removed her robe and made her bathe. They then noticed she had shaved her private area and asked: "Venerable aunt, why did you shave there?" She asked in return: "Why do you shave there?" The women said: "We do it for the sake of men!" The bhikṣuṇī said: "I do the same!" The women then criticized and reproached her: "These people always denigrate desire, yet they do such things and do not practice the holy life! Why not return to lay life and freely indulge in the five sensual pleasures? They have no conduct of renunciants! They destroy the Dharma of renunciants!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs, hearing this, reproached her in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī shaves her armpit or private area hair, it is a pāyattika."

If she shaves, each stroke of the razor is a pāyattika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one must shave because of a sore, or if someone forcefully holds one down and shaves, these are not offenses.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs stood alone in a secluded place with bhikṣus, talking together, and developed feelings of attachment. Some consequently returned to lay life or became followers of other teachings. Laypeople who saw this said: "This bhikṣu and bhikṣuṇī are standing alone together and talking in a secluded place. They must be talking about sexual matters! They have no conduct of renunciants! They destroy the Dharma of renunciants!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs, seeing and hearing this, reproached them in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī stands alone in a secluded place with a bhikṣu, talking together, it is a pāyattika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If there are many bhikṣuṇīs and one bhikṣu, or in times of the eight dangers, there is no offense.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs stood alone in a secluded place with laymen and followers of other teachings, talking together. This led to having their bodies touched, hearing coarse and obscene sexual talk, or being forced into sexual acts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs, seeing this, reproached them in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī stands alone in a secluded place with a layman or a follower of other teachings, talking together, it is a pāyattika." The rest is as stated above.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs stood alone in an open place with bhikṣus, talking together, and developed feelings of attachment, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī stands alone in an open place with a bhikṣu, talking together, it is a pāyattika." The rest is as stated above.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs stood alone in an open place with laymen and followers of other teachings, talking together. This led to having their bodies touched, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī stands alone in an open place with a layman or a follower of other teachings, talking together, it is a pāyattika." The rest is as stated above.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs stood alone in a street or alley with a bhikṣu, whispering in each other's ears, and sent their companion bhikṣuṇī far away. Laypeople criticized them. The senior bhikṣuṇīs, hearing this, reproached them in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī stands alone in a street or alley with a bhikṣu, whispering in each other's ears, and sends her companion bhikṣuṇī far away, it is a pāyattika." The rest is as stated above.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs stood alone in a street or alley with laymen and followers of other teachings, whispering in each other's ears, and sent their companion bhikṣuṇī far away. Laypeople who saw this criticized them, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī stands alone in a street or alley with a layman or a follower of other teachings, whispering in each other's ears, and sends her companion bhikṣuṇī far away, it is a pāyattika." The rest is as stated above.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs bathed naked. Laypeople who saw this surrounded them and mocked them. The senior bhikṣuṇīs, hearing this, reproached them in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī bathes naked, it is a pāyattika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she bars people from coming, or bathes in a secluded place, or is screened by trees or cloth, these are not offenses.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs were going about without their bathing garment and did not know what to wear when bathing. The senior bhikṣuṇīs, seeing this, reproached them in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī goes about without her bathing garment, it is a pāyattika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If it is being dyed, washed, or beaten, or if it has been burned, carried away by water, or destroyed, there is no offense.

---

At that time, the disciple of the bhikṣuṇī Caṇḍa Sumanā obtained five new robes by herself. She said to her preceptor: "Please wear them for me first, so that I may gain merit!" After the preceptor put them on, she did not return them, and the disciple went about in coarse and tattered robes. The bhikṣuṇīs saw this and asked: "You obtained five new robes by yourself. Why are you not wearing them?" She answered: "I first offered them to my preceptor for her to wear, but she has not returned them!" The bhikṣuṇīs reproached Caṇḍa Sumanā: "How can it be that when a disciple obtains new robes and first offers them to you, you then do not return them?" The senior bhikṣuṇīs reported the matter to the Buddha, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī receives a new robe offered to her first and then does not return it, it is a pāyattika."

If a bhikṣuṇī obtains a new robe and offers it to another bhikṣuṇī, that bhikṣuṇī should wear it for one day. If she is asked to wear it longer, she should do so, according to the owner's wishes as to duration. If she exceeds the agreed limit, it is a pāyattika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

At that time, there was a bhikṣuṇī who obstructed the Sangha from distributing robes. The other bhikṣuṇīs waited for her, and this hindered their meditation and practice of the path. The senior bhikṣuṇīs reproached her: "How can a bhikṣuṇī obstruct the Sangha from distributing robes?" They reproached her in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī obstructs the Sangha from distributing robes, it is a pāyattika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she is ill and cannot go, or did not hear about it, there is no offense.

---

At that time, there was a junior bhikṣuṇī who obtained unfinished cloth. She had few acquaintances and did not know how to make it into a robe herself, so she brought it to the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā and asked her to make it. Thullanandā answered: "I have many duties and cannot make it for you." She then brought it to other bhikṣuṇīs, who made it into a robe for her. Thullanandā asked: "Who made it for you? Bring it here so I can see whether it was made properly." She showed it to her. Thullanandā said: "This robe is not properly made. You should quickly tear it apart! I will make another one for you." The bhikṣuṇī tore it apart and brought it to her, but Thullanandā would not make it. The bhikṣuṇī was unable to get it completed and went about in coarse and tattered robes. The torn cloth pieces were spread out drying on the roof. At that time, a fire broke out and the pieces were burned. The wind blew them onto the roofs of lay houses and the fire spread, burning many homes. The laypeople, seeing the fire came from the bhikṣuṇīs' dwelling, angrily reproached: "We provide for these bhikṣuṇīs, and in return they become our enemies!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs, hearing this, reproached her in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī personally tears apart a bhikṣuṇī's robe, or has someone tear it apart, and does not sew it back together, it is a pāyattika."

Furthermore, there was a bhikṣuṇī who tore apart another bhikṣuṇī's robe and could not finish it in one day; it took four or five days. The matter was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the twofold Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣuṇīs four or five days to complete a torn robe. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī tears apart a bhikṣuṇī's robe, or has someone tear it apart, and it is not completed within four or five days, it is a pāyattika."

Furthermore, there was a bhikṣuṇī who tore apart another bhikṣuṇī's robe and then fell ill and could not complete it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the twofold Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit that if a bhikṣuṇī tears apart another's robe and then falls ill and cannot complete it, this is permissible. From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī tears apart a bhikṣuṇī's robe, and without being ill, does not complete it within four or five days, it is a pāyattika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs went about without their five robes, wearing coarse and tattered clothing when visiting households. The senior bhikṣuṇīs, seeing this, reproached them in various ways, and so forth, down to establishing a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, as described above. "From now on, this precept should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī goes about without the five robes, it is a pāyattika."

Going about without the five robes means: from one house to another, or even stepping out of one's dwelling gate, it is a pāyattika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If the robes are being washed, dyed, beaten, or sewn, there is no offense.

---

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs gave bhikṣuṇī robes to laywomen and women of other teachings. Those women then wore them when going about. Other laypeople who saw them bowed to them, and the women said: "I am a laywoman!" or "I am a follower of other teachings!" The laypeople then criticized and reproached: "How can bhikṣuṇīs give bhikṣuṇī robes to

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from Chinese during the Liu Song dynasty by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle 12 (Part 25 of 61)

*Note: The source text provided for this fascicle is empty. There is no Chinese text to translate.*

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Thirteen

### Second Division, Part Four: The Eighty-Ninth Matter

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs did not observe the rains retreat and traveled about among the people. Some encountered eighth-month bandits, and some encountered floods, fires, and various calamities. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways: "How can bhikṣuṇīs not observe the summer rains retreat?" And so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī does not observe the rains retreat, it is a pācittika."

Rains retreat means: the earlier or later rains retreat.

If without a valid reason one waits for the later rains retreat, it is a duṣkṛta.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If the eight difficulties arise, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs observed the rains retreat in places where there was no bhikṣu Sangha. They then had various doubts: whether one may ordain or may not ordain, whether one may confer the precepts or may not confer the precepts, whether robe-making was in accordance with the Dharma or not in accordance with the Dharma. Regarding the precepts, they had various doubts of this kind and did not know whom to ask. Furthermore, they were treated with contempt by wicked people and non-Buddhists. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and the matter was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled both Sanghas and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Is this really so?" They answered: "It is really so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha rebuked them in various ways, saying: "Did I not previously teach the Eight Rules of Respect, that bhikṣuṇīs should observe the rains retreat in dependence on the bhikṣu Sangha?" After rebuking them, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī does not observe the rains retreat in dependence on the bhikṣu Sangha, it is a pācittika."

Although she depends on the bhikṣu Sangha, if there is no one to give instruction, it is a duṣkṛta.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If during the rains retreat the bhikṣu Sangha moves away, dies out, or abandons the path; if one is restrained by a powerful force; or if one is ill, there is no offense in all these cases.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, although observing the rains retreat, traveled about during it and encountered bandits, fires, and various calamities. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī travels about during the rains retreat, it is a pācittika."

Traveling about means: if one goes from one village to another village, or if there is no village, travels half a yojana, it is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If the eight difficulties arise, there is no offense.

At that time, after the rains retreat was completed, bhikṣuṇīs did not request from the bhikṣu Sangha examination of offenses seen, heard, or suspected. Without anyone to give instruction, they were ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs rebuked them in various ways, and the matter was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled both Sanghas and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Is this really so?" They answered: "It is really so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha rebuked them in various ways, saying: "Did I not previously teach the Eight Rules of Respect, that when bhikṣuṇīs complete the rains retreat, they should request from the bhikṣu Sangha examination of offenses seen, heard, or suspected?" After rebuking them, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, after completing the rains retreat, does not request from the bhikṣu Sangha examination of offenses seen, heard, or suspected, it is a pācittika."

If the Sangha is not harmoniously assembled, or at the time of the eight difficulties, there is no offense.

At that time, householders invited the bhikṣuṇī Kṣemā to observe the rains retreat in Śrāvastī, saying: "If you accept our invitation, we will provide supplies according to the season!" She accepted their invitation. She then stayed on permanently and did not go elsewhere afterward. Those householders criticized and rebuked her, saying: "We have other things to attend to. These bhikṣuṇīs do not know how to measure, and do not know when to leave! They always speak of having few desires and knowing contentment, yet now they are insatiable!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, after an invitation for the rains retreat is completed, does not depart within one night, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she is ill, or if there is fear, or if the invitation was not for a fixed period, or if she observes the rains retreat at a place other than where the invitation was made and does not depart, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs traveled in frightening places within the country without anyone to protect them, and were robbed and stripped by wicked people. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī travels alone in frightening places within the country without anyone to rely on for protection, it is a pācittika."

Even if many bhikṣuṇīs travel together, if they have no strong lay companion, it is called traveling alone.

Going from one village to another village, or if there is no village, traveling half a yojana, it is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If there is urgent danger and one flees in rebellion, there is no offense in any case.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs traveled in frightening places outside the country's borders, also without anyone to protect them, and were robbed and stripped by wicked people, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī travels alone in frightening places outside the country's borders without anyone to rely on for protection, it is a pācittika."

The remainder is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs were staying at a monastery built by the mother of Viśākhā. After completing the rains retreat, they departed on a journey without entrusting the empty monastery to anyone. Afterward, a fire broke out. Someone saw it and told the mother of Viśākhā: "The bhikṣuṇī monastery you built is being burned by fire!" She then sent servants to go and extinguish it, and it was saved from being completely burned. When the bhikṣuṇīs later returned, the mother of Viśākhā asked: "Venerable sisters! Did you not lose anything?" They answered: "We lost such-and-such items!" They then went on at great length describing the lost items. The mother of Viśākhā rebuked them, saying: "How can you observe the rains retreat at my monastery, leave without entrusting it to anyone, cause it to catch fire, and then go on at great length about what you lost!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, after completing the rains retreat, departs on a journey without entrusting the monastery to someone, it is a pācittika."

If she does not entrust it and steps one foot outside the gate, it is a duṣkṛta; if both feet are outside the gate, it is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If other bhikṣus or bhikṣuṇīs have come to stay, or if there is no one to whom one can entrust it, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs observed the rains retreat at the monastery of the mother of Viśākhā. After completing the retreat, they departed without returning the monastery to its owner. Afterward, a fire broke out. Someone saw it and told the mother of Viśākhā: "The bhikṣuṇī monastery you built is being burned by fire!" The mother of Viśākhā said: "Let it burn completely! Previously, the bhikṣuṇīs departed without entrusting it, causing the fire. When they returned afterward, they went on at great length about their lost items, bringing me a bad reputation!" When those bhikṣuṇīs later returned again, the mother of Viśākhā asked: "Venerable sisters! When you left, did you leave any belongings in the monastery?" They answered: "No!" She then rebuked them, saying: "How could you leave without returning my monastery to me, causing it to burn completely? If you had told me, I would have guarded it myself and prevented this!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, after completing the rains retreat, departs without returning the monastery to its owner, it is a pācittika."

The remainder is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs went sightseeing at the royal palace and at painted halls, and also went visiting various places of entertainment. They went to flower ponds where many people gathered to watch. The bhikṣuṇīs said to the men: "Please move aside a little; do not press close to us!" The men said: "Unlucky ones! Shaven-headed, wearing patchwork robes, you should not come to such places! That you come here means you are seeking men! Why should we move aside for you?" They then seized and dragged them, speaking coarse and lewd words. The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī goes sightseeing to various places, it is a pācittika."

Forming the intention and making preparations: duṣkṛta. If she sets out, with each step it is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one does not go for the purpose of watching entertainment, there is no offense in any case.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, at the time of the half-monthly poṣadha, did not go to the bhikṣu Sangha to request an instructor. Because there was no one to give instruction, they were ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and the matter was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled both Sanghas and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Is this really so?" They answered: "It is really so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha rebuked the bhikṣuṇīs in various ways: "Did I not previously teach the Eight Rules of Respect, instructing you to request an instructor every half-month?" After rebuking them, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī does not request an instructor from the Sangha every half-month, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, if they do not seek instruction and repentance, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she requests but cannot obtain one, or if she is ill, there is no offense.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Kṣemā passed away. The bhikṣuṇīs erected a bone stūpa for her within the bhikṣu monastery. Her attendants circumambulated it three times daily, weeping and crying, saying: "She who gave us the Dharma! She who gave us robes, food, beds, bedding, and medicine! How could she so suddenly abandon us and depart forever?" The bhikṣus were troubled by this, as it obstructed their meditation and practice of the path.

At that time, Upāli came into the monastery and asked the resident bhikṣus: "What is this sound?" They explained the matter in full. Upāli then had someone demolish the stūpa. When the bhikṣuṇīs heard this, they deliberated together: "We should all take sticks and beat that bhikṣu. Whoever does not join in going, we will no longer live with her!" After deliberating, they all took sticks and went to the monastery. Seeing a bhikṣu, they surrounded him intending to beat him, but upon recognizing he was not Upāli, they stopped. They went further and on a narrow path encountered Upāli. They blocked him from the front and rear, raising their sticks to beat him. Upāli then used his supernatural power to fly to the Buddha, and reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled both Sanghas and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Is this really so?" They answered: "It is really so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha rebuked the bhikṣuṇīs in various ways, saying: "What you have done is against the Dharma! How can bhikṣuṇīs beat a bhikṣu!" After rebuking them, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī enters a bhikṣu's dwelling place, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣuṇīs traveling on the road who saw an empty monastery and wished to enter and pay respects, but did not dare enter. They reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled both Sanghas and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣuṇīs to enter an empty monastery. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī enters a dwelling place where bhikṣus are present, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who had business within a monastery and needed to enter but did not dare enter. They reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled both Sanghas and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣuṇīs who have business requiring them to enter a monastery to inform the bhikṣus. If the bhikṣus permit it, then they may enter. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī enters a dwelling place where bhikṣus are present without informing a bhikṣu, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who had business and wished to enter a monastery, but the bhikṣus were either in meditation or asleep, and they could not inform them. They reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled both Sanghas and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣuṇīs to proceed into the monastery, and upon seeing a bhikṣu, to inform him. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī enters a dwelling place where bhikṣus are present and, upon seeing a bhikṣu, does not inform him, it is a pācittika."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who, at a time of urgent danger, wished to take refuge in a monastery to escape the danger but did not dare enter. Some were robbed by bandits, and some were harmed by wild beasts. They reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled both Sanghas and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣuṇīs, at times of urgent danger, to enter a monastery as they see fit. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī enters a dwelling place where bhikṣus are present and, upon seeing a bhikṣu, does not inform him, except at a time of urgent danger, it is a pācittika."

If she does not see a bhikṣu and enters without being able to inform one, she must inform a bhikṣu as soon as she sees one. That bhikṣu should evaluate the situation: if it is appropriate for her to enter, he should permit entry; if it is not appropriate, he should not permit entry.

If she sees a bhikṣu and does not inform him, or enters without permission, it is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs who had not completed twelve years took on disciples. Unable to give instruction and unable to look after them, the disciples were ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī who has not completed twelve years takes on disciples, it is a pācittika."

Not completed twelve years means: it has not been twelve years since she received the precepts.

Taking on disciples means: acting as preceptor for someone.

If she forms the intention to take on a following, up until the one motion and three proclamations procedure is not yet completed, it is a duṣkṛta. When the one motion and three proclamations procedure is completed, for the preceptor it is a pācittika; for the other bhikṣuṇī teachers and Sangha members, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, although having completed twelve years, were deaf, blind, mute, or had various other illnesses. Being without knowledge, they took on disciples but could not give instruction or look after them. The disciples were ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and the matter was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled both Sanghas and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Is this really so?" They answered: "It is really so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha rebuked them in various ways, saying: "How can bhikṣuṇīs who are blind, deaf, mute, or have various illnesses take on disciples, being unable to give instruction or look after them, causing their disciples to be ignorant and unknowing and unable to study the precepts?" After rebuking them, he told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣuṇīs to take on a following through a one motion and one proclamation procedure."

That bhikṣuṇī should go to the bhikṣuṇī Sangha, remove her leather sandals, bare her right shoulder, kneel with her right knee on the ground, join her palms, and say: "May the noble Sangha of sisters listen! I, bhikṣuṇī so-and-so, have completed twelve years and wish to take on a following. I request from the Sangha the procedure for taking on a following. May the Sangha be so good as to grant me the procedure for taking on a following." She should request thus three times.

The bhikṣuṇīs should evaluate and examine whether this bhikṣuṇī is capable of taking on a following or not. If she is not capable, they should not grant her the procedure for taking on a following. If she is capable, they should grant it.

A single bhikṣuṇī, either the senior or one equal to the senior, who knows the Dharma and knows the Vinaya, should announce: "May the noble Sangha of sisters listen! This bhikṣuṇī so-and-so has completed twelve years and wishes to take on so-and-so as a following. She requests from the Sangha the procedure for taking on a following. The Sangha now grants her the procedure for taking on a following. If the time is appropriate and the Sangha consents, may it be heard. The motion is thus."

"May the noble Sangha of sisters listen! This bhikṣuṇī so-and-so has completed twelve years and wishes to take on so-and-so as a following. She requests from the Sangha the procedure for taking on a following. The Sangha now grants her the procedure for taking on a following. Whichever sisters consent, remain silent; whoever does not consent, speak. The Sangha has granted bhikṣuṇī so-and-so the procedure for taking on a following. The Sangha consents, as indicated by silence. The matter is held thus."

"I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī has completed twelve years but the Sangha has not granted her the procedure for taking on a following, and she takes on a following, it is a pācittika."

The remainder is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs conferred full ordination on previously married women who had not completed twelve years of age. They were ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī confers full ordination on a previously married woman who has not completed twelve years of age, it is a pācittika."

Not completed twelve years means: although she has been married, she has not yet reached twelve years of age.

Married means: she has had relations with a man. The remainder is as stated above in the section on taking on disciples.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, although the previously married women had completed twelve years of age, the women were deaf, mute, or had various other illnesses, and they conferred full ordination on them. They were foolish and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: "I now permit bhikṣuṇīs to confer full ordination on previously married women who have completed twelve years of age through a one motion and three proclamations procedure."

The person wishing to receive full ordination should go to the bhikṣuṇī Sangha and say: "May the noble Sangha of sisters listen! I, so-and-so, have been married and have completed twelve years of age. I seek to receive full ordination from preceptor so-and-so. I now request from the Sangha full ordination. May the Sangha be so good as to grant me full ordination, out of compassion!" After requesting thus three times, the bhikṣuṇīs should evaluate whether she may be given ordination or may not be given ordination. A single bhikṣuṇī should perform the procedure, according to what was stated above. He told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī confers full ordination on a previously married woman who has completed twelve years of age, without the Sangha performing the procedure, it is a pācittika."

The remainder is as stated in the section on taking on disciples.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs conferred the training precepts on unmarried girls who had not completed eighteen years of age. They were ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts.
