The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī confers the training precepts on an unmarried girl who has not completed eighteen years of age, it is a pācittika."

Unmarried girl means: she has not had relations with a man.

Forming the intention and making preparations, up until the one motion and one proclamation procedure is not yet completed, it is a duṣkṛta. When the one motion and one proclamation procedure is completed, it is a pācittika; for the other teachers and the assembly, all are duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, although the unmarried girls had completed eighteen years of age, the girls were deaf, mute, or had various other illnesses, and they conferred the training precepts on them, and so forth, up to: I now permit bhikṣuṇīs to confer two years of training precepts on unmarried girls who have completed eighteen years of age through a one motion and one proclamation procedure.

The person wishing to receive the training precepts should go to the bhikṣuṇī Sangha and say: "May the noble Sangha of sisters listen! I, so-and-so, with preceptor so-and-so, now request from the Sangha the two years of training precepts. May the noble Sangha of sisters be so good as to grant me the two years of training precepts, out of compassion!" After requesting thus three times, the bhikṣuṇīs should carefully evaluate whether she may be given the training precepts or may not be given the training precepts. A single bhikṣuṇī should perform the procedure, according to what was stated above. He told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, although the unmarried girl has completed eighteen years of age, confers the training precepts without the Sangha performing the procedure, it is a pācittika."

The remainder is as stated above.

At that time, the householder's wife wished to go forth and sought to leave home with the bhikṣuṇī Sthūlanandā. Sthūlanandā said: "First give me your robe, and then I will ordain you." The householder's wife rebuked her, saying: "I am the householder! How can you first demand my robe and only afterward ordain me? You do not wish to deliver me from birth, old age, sickness, and death, but instead seek profit from my robe! These ones have no conduct of śramaṇas; they destroy the Dharma of śramaṇas!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked her in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī says to a lay woman, 'First give me your robe, and then I will ordain you,' it is a pācittika."

If a bhikṣuṇī says this to a śikṣamāṇā or śrāmaṇerī, it is a duṣkṛta.

If the person owes a debt, and the debt is repaid before she is ordained, there is no offense.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Sthūlanandā said to the bhikṣuṇīs: "Grant me the procedure for taking on a following." The bhikṣuṇīs said: "As the Buddha has stated, those who meet the criteria should be granted the procedure for taking on a following. You do not meet the criteria, so we cannot grant you the procedure for taking on a following." That bhikṣuṇī then said: "The bhikṣuṇīs are partial due to affection, anger, delusion, and fear! Those they fear, they grant it to; those they do not fear, they do not grant it to." The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked her in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, when the bhikṣuṇīs say, 'As the Buddha has stated, those who meet the criteria should be granted the procedure for taking on a following; you do not meet the criteria,' then reproaches the bhikṣuṇīs, it is a pācittika."

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs did not go to listen at the time of instruction and did not go to listen at the time of formal acts. Thus they were ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī does not go to listen at the time of instruction and formal acts, it is a pācittika."

Instruction means: the teaching of the Eight Rules of Respect and other dharmas.

Formal acts means: a motion procedure, a one motion and one proclamation procedure, or a one motion and three proclamations procedure.

At that time, when bhikṣuṇīs had a śikṣamāṇā who had completed two years of training, they did not confer full ordination on her. Later that person contracted a serious illness, becoming deaf, blind, mute, or developing various other diseases that obstructed the ordination procedure.

The disciple of the bhikṣuṇī Kṣemā had completed two years of training, yet she too was not given full ordination. She was told: "You should continue studying these precepts." Later that person contracted leprosy, and they did not know what to do. The senior bhikṣuṇīs rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, when a śikṣamāṇā has completed two years of training and there is no impediment, does not confer full ordination on her and says, 'You should continue studying these precepts,' it is a pācittika."

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs ordained prostitutes, who would not accept instruction. They were like yoked oxen with crooked necks, unfit to pull a cart; when hitched to a cart, they only wanted to escape from the yoke. The senior bhikṣuṇīs rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī ordains a prostitute, it is a pācittika."

Forming the intention, up until the one motion and three proclamations procedure is not yet completed, it is a duṣkṛta. When the one motion and three proclamations procedure is completed, for the preceptor it is a pācittika; for the other bhikṣuṇī teachers and Sangha members, it is a duṣkṛta.

If the woman is disgusted with the female body and is ordained on that account, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs conferred full ordination on a śikṣamāṇā who had not completed two years of training. She was ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī confers full ordination on a śikṣamāṇā who has not completed two years of training, it is a pācittika."

The remainder is as stated in the section on taking on disciples.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, although the śikṣamāṇā had completed two years of training, the woman was deaf, blind, mute, or had various other illnesses, and they conferred full ordination on her. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above, as stated in the section on previously married women who have completed twelve years. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī confers full ordination on a śikṣamāṇā who has completed two years of training without the Sangha performing the procedure, it is a pācittika."

The remainder is as stated in the section on taking on disciples.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs conferred full ordination on a śikṣamāṇā who had completed two years but had not studied the precepts. She was ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī confers full ordination on a śikṣamāṇā who has completed two years but has not studied the precepts, it is a pācittika."

The remainder is as stated in the section on taking on disciples.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs conferred full ordination on a pregnant woman. When she entered the village to beg for food, the lay people saw her and teased her, saying: "This bhikṣuṇī carries a heavy load; she should be given food quickly!" Some said: "Just look at her belly!" Some said: "These ones do not practice the holy life!" Others said: "She does practice the holy life; this is from before she left home." They then rebuked the bhikṣuṇīs, saying: "Why did you not wait until she gave birth, and then let her go forth? By this you disgrace the holy life! These bhikṣuṇīs do not know who is suitable and who is not suitable for ordination. They have no conduct of śramaṇas! They destroy the Dharma of śramaṇas!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī confers full ordination on a pregnant woman, it is a pācittika."

Forming the intention, up until the one motion and three proclamations procedure is completed, all as stated above.

If one wishes to confer full ordination, one should first examine her breasts. If there are no signs of a child, there is no offense. If ordination has already been completed and only afterward she is found to be pregnant, there is also no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs conferred full ordination on a woman who had recently given birth. Holding a bowl in one hand and cradling a child in the other, she went begging for food. The lay people saw her and teased her, saying: "Quickly give food to both of them!" The lay people criticized and complained. The senior bhikṣuṇīs rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī confers full ordination on a woman who has recently given birth, it is a pācittika."

The remainder is as stated above in the section on taking on disciples.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs conferred full ordination on disciples every year. With so many disciples, they could not instruct each one. The disciples were ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī confers full ordination on a disciple every year, it is a pācittika."

A bhikṣuṇī may confer full ordination on one disciple every other year. The remainder is as stated in the section on taking on disciples.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs conferred full ordination on their disciples in the bhikṣuṇī Sangha, and only on the following day went to the bhikṣu Sangha to receive ordination. During that one night, the ordination candidate developed a condition that obstructed the ordination procedure, and the bhikṣu Sangha would no longer confer ordination. The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī confers full ordination on a disciple on a different day, it is a pācittika."

From forming the intention, up until the dawn has not yet appeared, it is a duṣkṛta. When the dawn appears, for the preceptor it is a pācittika; for the other teachers and Sangha members, it is a duṣkṛta.

If the Sangha is not harmoniously assembled, or the eight difficulties arise, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs who were newly ordained did not rely upon and attend their preceptor. Because there was no one to give instruction, they were ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī who is newly fully ordained does not rely upon and attend her preceptor for six years, or sends someone else to attend in her place, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If the preceptor does not require reliance and attendance, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, after conferring full ordination on disciples, did not look after them, did not instruct them, and did not teach them to recite and study. The disciples were ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, during the six years of having a disciple, does not personally look after her, nor ask others to look after her, it is a pācittika."

If the disciple does not accept instruction, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs conferred full ordination on their disciples without taking them away from their original place. Men who had formerly known them saw them and developed feelings of attachment, then teased them and spoke coarse and lewd words. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, having taken on a disciple, does not personally take her, nor have someone take her, five or six yojanas away from her original place, it is a pācittika."

Original place means: either her birthplace or the place where she was married.

If the disciple does not comply, there is no offense.

At that time, when fellow students of bhikṣuṇīs were ill, they did not look after them. Consequently, some did not recover in time, and some died. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī does not personally look after a fellow student who is ill, nor ask others to look after her, it is a pācittika."

Fellow students means: those with the same preceptor or the same teacher, and also constant companions.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If they do not reside in the same place, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs ordained women who were servants of others. The lay people criticized and complained, saying: "These bhikṣuṇīs make no distinction between those who may be ordained and those who may not!" Some householders said: "Their robes and bowls should be seized and they should be handed over to the authorities." Others said: "King Prasenajit has decreed that anyone who insults a bhikṣuṇī shall be given a heavy penalty. We should release them quickly lest others hear of it!" All said: "These ones have no conduct of śramaṇas; they destroy the Dharma of śramaṇas!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī ordains a woman who is a servant, it is a pācittika."

Servant means: either belonging to the government or to another person.

Forming the intention, up until the one motion and three proclamations procedure is completed, also as stated above.

If the master permits it, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs ordained women with chronic illness. They could not study the precepts, being ignorant and unknowing. The senior bhikṣuṇīs saw this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī ordains a woman with chronic illness, it is a pācittika."

Chronic illness means: suffering from constant chills and fevers that recur at regular intervals.

Forming the intention, up until the one motion and three proclamations procedure is completed, also as stated above.

If the illness is contracted after receiving ordination, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs ordained women who were married to husbands. The lay people criticized and complained, some wanting to seize their robes and some wanting to expel them, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, all as stated in the section on women who are servants. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī ordains a woman who is married to a husband, it is a pācittika."

Forming the intention, up until the one motion and three proclamations procedure is completed, also as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs ordained women who owed debts, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, all as stated above in the section on women who are servants. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī ordains a woman who owes debts, it is a pācittika."

Forming the intention, up until the one motion and three proclamations procedure is completed, also as stated above.

If the woman says, "After I have gone forth, I will repay the debt," and she is ordained on this basis, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs stood and spoke with men in dark places. Developing feelings of attachment, they lost their delight in practicing the holy life, and some reverted to lay life or joined non-Buddhist groups. The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī stands and speaks with a man in a dark place, it is a pācittika."

If she speaks in a dark place, with each utterance it is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If it is a place of uncertainty and fear, or if a lamp suddenly goes out, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, when visiting the homes of acquaintances, would sit on their beds without asking. The lay people criticized and complained, saying: "We do not like to see such unlucky things! They do not know what is appropriate to sit on and what is not, and have no propriety!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī sits on someone's bed without informing the host, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If the host invites her to sit, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs personally gave food to lay men and non-Buddhist men. Those men thought: "This bhikṣuṇī must be giving me food out of feelings of attachment!" They then teased her and spoke coarse and lewd words. The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī personally gives food to lay men or non-Buddhist men, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she does not give it personally, or if she personally gives it to relatives, there is no offense in any case.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs spoke to lay people about the faults of bhikṣus, saying: "That bhikṣu violates the precepts, violates proper deportment, violates right views!" When the bhikṣus heard this, they became angry and would no longer give instruction. The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and the matter was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled both Sanghas and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Is this really so?" They answered: "It is really so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha rebuked them in various ways, saying: "Did I not previously teach you the Eight Rules of Respect?" After rebuking them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī speaks to lay people about the faults of a bhikṣu, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If the lay person has already heard and asks about it, she should ask in return: "How did you hear it?" If the person says: "I heard it in such-and-such a way!" and the bhikṣuṇī has also heard the same, then she may answer truthfully; there is no offense.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Caṇḍa Sumanā, after quarreling with someone, beat herself and struck herself, wailing and crying loudly. The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked her in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, after quarreling with someone, beats herself and cries, it is a pācittika."

If she strikes herself or beats herself, each and every blow is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs pointed from a distance at Caṇḍa Sumanā and discussed her quarrel. She thought they were scolding her and screamed loudly: "The bhikṣuṇīs are scolding me!" The bhikṣuṇīs then went and asked: "What scolding have we done? Don't you even understand what is being said?" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked her in various ways, saying: "How can a bhikṣuṇī not clearly understand what others are saying and falsely claim they are scolding her?" And so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī does not clearly understand what others are saying and falsely becomes angry at them, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, when quarreling, made oaths and imprecations against each other, saying: "If I am like this, may I fall into hell! Like Devadatta and Kokālika who received their punishments, may I receive the same! If I am not like this, may you receive such punishment!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī makes oaths and imprecations against herself, in reality cursing others, it is a pācittika."

Making such oaths and imprecations, with each utterance it is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs threw feces and urine over the fence wall, soiling people and non-humans.

At that time, there was a Brahmin great minister who wore a crown and was ritually purified and clean. Early in the morning he bathed, put on garments scented with fragrance, and wished to go to the celestial shrine to seek restoration of his official position. He wrapped his head as he walked the road, fearing he might see shaven-headed people wearing patchwork robes. When he passed by the bhikṣuṇī's wall, feces that was thrown landed on his head and flowed over his entire body. He was greatly angered and said: "I feared seeing these unlucky people, and now I have been doused with thrown feces! This must be an inauspicious event for my fate. However, I shall go to King Prasenajit and demand the execution of these bald slave women!" Thereupon he turned back and encountered a Brahmin physiognomist. The Brahmin asked: "Why are you in this state?" He explained the matter in full. The physiognomist said: "This is actually very auspicious! You will now obtain a thousand gold coins and be restored to your former official position!" He was still angry and did not stop. He went, still bearing the form soiled by feces, directly to the king's court. The king asked: "Why are you in this state?" He explained the matter in full. The king clapped his hands and laughed greatly, then ordered that he be given a thousand gold coins and restored to his former official position. The king's attendant ministers then criticized and complained, saying: "Throwing feces to soil people, is this the intention of seeking the Way and benefiting others?" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī throws feces or urine over a fence wall, or has someone throw it, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs threw sweepings and leftover food over the fence wall, soiling people and non-humans. The lay people saw this and criticized and complained, saying: "How can bhikṣuṇīs throw sweepings over the wall, soiling people? These ones have no proper conduct!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī throws sweepings and leftover food over a fence wall, or has someone throw it, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, in front of the King's Garden Monastery, the ground was extremely level and flat with soft, beautiful grass growing on it. People often played and amused themselves there, disturbing the bhikṣuṇīs' meditation and practice of the Way. The bhikṣuṇīs, being troubled, all defecated and urinated there to soil it and make it unclean, in order to drive the people away. When the people later came as usual to play, the filth got on their hands, feet, clothing, and utensils. They were greatly angered, saying: "Who has freely relieved themselves in this place?" Someone said: "It was the bhikṣuṇīs!" Everyone criticized and complained, saying: "These renunciants who seek the Way and purity, how can they defile such a fine place and cut off people's enjoyment?" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī defecates or urinates on living grass, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If there is an urgent illness, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs also threw sweepings and leftover food onto the ground in front of the King's Garden Monastery to drive people away. The lay people criticized and complained. The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī throws sweepings or leftover food onto living grass, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If thrown into a pit or a place that is not clean, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs spent the night at a house where a couple was present. Hearing the sounds of the husband and wife having intercourse, they developed feelings of desire, lost their delight in the Way, and some reverted to lay life or joined non-Buddhist groups. The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī spends the night at a house where a couple is present, it is a pācittika."

A house where a couple is present means: where there are a man and woman with mutual feelings of intimacy.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she is ill and needs to stay overnight, or if difficulties arise, there is no offense in any case.

At that time, bhikṣus asked bhikṣuṇīs: "Where is the house of so-and-so?" or "Where does this road lead?" The bhikṣuṇīs, being disrespectful, did not answer. The bhikṣus became resentful and would no longer give instruction, and so the bhikṣuṇīs were ignorant and unknowing and could not study the precepts. The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, when a bhikṣu asks her a question, does not answer, it is a pācittika."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus spoke coarse and vulgar words when questioning bhikṣuṇīs. Although the bhikṣuṇīs answered, they felt shame and humiliation. The matter was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled both Sanghas and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣuṇīs: if a bhikṣu asks in a proper manner, they should answer. From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, when a bhikṣu asks in a proper manner, does not answer, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If there is prior animosity and they are not on speaking terms, not answering is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs rode on conveyances when visiting lay people. The lay people criticized and complained, saying: "These bhikṣuṇīs are like the queen or noblewomen, riding conveyances when they come. They have no proper deportment!" The senior bhikṣuṇīs heard this and rebuked them in various ways, and so forth, up to: the Buddha now established a precept for the bhikṣuṇīs, also as stated above. "From now on, this precept should be stated thus:

"If a bhikṣuṇī travels riding on a conveyance, it is a pācittika."

Conveyance means: a cart, a palanquin, an elephant, a horse, or even wearing wooden clogs; all are called conveyances.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she is old, or ill, or compelled by a powerful force, or traveling on a road, or even if she has an ailment of the toes, there is no offense in any case.

I notice that while the fascicle header says 卷第十三 (Fascicle 13), the actual content indicator at the bottom says "Fascicle 014." However, no Chinese text has been provided between the delimiter lines for me to translate.

Could you please provide the actual Chinese text content of this fascicle? The space between the delimiter lines appears to be empty. I would be happy to translate the full text once it is supplied.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Fourteen

### Second Division, Part Four

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs wore leather sandals and carried parasols when visiting laypeople. The laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "These bhikṣuṇīs come and go like prostitutes!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī wears leather sandals and carries a parasol while walking about, it is a pācittika."

Parasol: this includes even a grass parasol.

Leather sandals: this includes even single-layered ones.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

Exceptions are as stated above.

At that time, there was a married couple who both went forth into the renunciant life. The husband, a bhikṣu, would beg for food, bring it back, and go to the dwelling of his wife, the bhikṣuṇī, to eat. His wife, the bhikṣuṇī, would hold a water pitcher and stand before him, fan him with a fan, give him water, and constantly ask whether he was cold or warm. The husband, the bhikṣu, ate with his head bowed, not looking at her and not speaking with her. Before that bhikṣu had been a layperson, he had had a woman with whom he had an illicit affair, and she also had gone forth and was staying at that place. She came out and saw him, and the bhikṣu smiled at her. His wife, the bhikṣuṇī, was seized with jealousy and struck the bhikṣu on the head with the water pitcher, breaking it open. The bhikṣuṇīs reproached her, saying: "How can a bhikṣuṇī hold a water pitcher and fan, stand before a bhikṣu, and supply water and fanning? And then go so far as to break his head!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached her in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī holds a water pitcher and a fan, stands before a bhikṣu, and either supplies water or fans him, it is a pācittika."

If she wishes to supply water, after finishing she should go far away and should not remain standing in front of him.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs recited medical texts and prescriptions. The laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "These ones only study the art of medicine and have no intention of seeking the Way! Why not use this effort to recite the Buddha's sūtras instead?" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī recites medical texts and prescriptions, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If it is for one's own illness, or out of compassion, or if one is compelled by force to read them, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs taught others to recite medical texts and prescriptions. The laypeople criticized and reproached them. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī teaches others to recite medical texts and prescriptions, it is a pācittika." The rest is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs treated people's illnesses, compounding, boiling, and pounding various medicines, not resting in the first or last watch of the night. Laypeople saw this and criticized and reproached them, saying: "These ones are like physicians, like physicians' apprentices! Why do they not seek the Way and cure the illness of birth and death, instead busying themselves with such worldly affairs?" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī treats people's illnesses as a livelihood, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If it is done out of compassion, or if compelled by force, and not for the sake of gain, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs taught others to treat illnesses. Laypeople came and said: "Teach us the Dharma!" They replied: "For treating heat, do this; for treating cold, do this; for treating wind, do this; for treating various illnesses, do this." The laypeople said: "We came for the Dharma, not for medical treatment!" They further criticized and reproached them, saying: "These ones only study the art of medicine and do not know the Way of the Dharma! If that were not so, why would they not teach us the Dharma?" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī teaches others to treat illnesses as a livelihood, it is a pācittika." The rest is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs did work for families they knew. The householders criticized and reproached them, saying: "These ones abandon their own proper work to do work for other families! They have no śramaṇa conduct and violate śramaṇa principles!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, for the sake of food and drink, does work for a lay household, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If it is done out of compassion, or if compelled by force, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs shared bedding and slept with laywomen and women of non-Buddhist orders. Their bodies came into contact, giving rise to minds of desire and lust; they lost their delight in the holy life, and some returned to lay life or joined non-Buddhist orders. Afterward, the women told their acquaintances, saying: "The body of such-and-such a bhikṣuṇī has such-and-such fine qualities!" Afterward, when the laypeople saw bhikṣuṇīs, they would point and jest, saying: "The one with the fine body is this bhikṣuṇī! That bhikṣuṇī!" The bhikṣuṇīs were shamed by this. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī shares bedding and sleeps with a laywoman or a woman of a non-Buddhist order, it is a pācittika."

If they share the same bed and the same blanket, their undergarments should provide a barrier between them.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs shared bedding and slept with other bhikṣuṇīs, śikṣamāṇās, or śrāmaṇerīs, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī shares bedding and sleeps with another bhikṣuṇī, a śikṣamāṇā, or a śrāmaṇerī, it is a pācittika."

The rest is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs covered one another while sleeping with laywomen and women of non-Buddhist orders, seeing each other's bodies, giving rise to minds of desire and lust, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī mutually covers and sleeps with a laywoman or a woman of a non-Buddhist order, it is a pācittika."

The rest is as stated above.

If there was already a covering and one adds another covering on top, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs mutually covered and slept with other bhikṣuṇīs, śikṣamāṇās, or śrāmaṇerīs, seeing each other's bodies, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī mutually covers and sleeps with another bhikṣuṇī, a śikṣamāṇā, or a śrāmaṇerī, it is a pācittika."

The rest is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs applied fragrant ointments to their bodies, and also had others apply them. This gave rise to minds of desire and lust; they lost their delight in cultivating the holy life, and some returned to lay life or joined non-Buddhist orders. Laypeople who smelled their fragrance criticized and reproached them, saying: "These ones apply fragrant ointments to their bodies, just like prostitutes! They have no śramaṇa conduct and violate śramaṇa principles!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī applies fragrant ointments to her body, it is a pācittika."

Fragrance means: root fragrances, stem fragrances, leaf fragrances, flower fragrances, insect-derived fragrances, and resin fragrances.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If it is for treating illness, or if compelled by force to apply them, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs rubbed their bodies with oils and moisturizers to make them gleam and appear lustrous. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "These ones rub their bodies with oils and moisturizers to make them gleam and appear lustrous, just like prostitutes! They have no śramaṇa conduct and violate śramaṇa principles!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī rubs her body with oils and moisturizers, it is a pācittika."

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Bhaddā Kāpilānī had a body that was insufficiently moistened; her skin was dry and cracked. She consulted a physician. The physician said: "You should use oils and moisturizers to rub your body!" She replied: "The Buddha does not permit me to rub my body with oils and moisturizers. Please think of another treatment!" The physician said: "There is no other treatment!" The bhikṣuṇī thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit rubbing the body with oils and moisturizers when ill, I would no longer have to endure this suffering!" She reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on this account, assembled the twofold Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣuṇīs to rub their bodies with oils and moisturizers when ill. From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, without illness, rubs her body with oils and moisturizers, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs kept flower garlands or wore them. This gave rise to minds of attachment; they lost their delight in cultivating the holy life, and some returned to lay life or joined non-Buddhist orders. The elder bhikṣuṇīs saw this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī keeps flower garlands or wears them, it is a pācittika."

Even tying grass or leaves on the head for adornment is all called wearing a flower garland.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If compelled by force, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs wore jeweled necklaces. This gave rise to minds of desire and lust; they lost their delight in cultivating the holy life, and some returned to lay life or joined non-Buddhist orders. The elder bhikṣuṇīs saw this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī wears a jeweled necklace, it is a pācittika."

Even a necklace made of wood is likewise included.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

Exceptions are as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs wore tight-fitting garments that made their figures appear slender and shapely. This gave rise to minds of desire and lust, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī wears a tight-fitting garment, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

Exceptions are as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs kept various bodily adornments. This gave rise to minds of desire and lust, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī keeps various bodily adornments, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs kept their hair long, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī keeps her hair long, it is a pācittika."

If a bhikṣuṇī's hair grows long, it is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, keeping hair long and not shaving it is a duṣkṛta.

One should shave once every half-month; exceeding this is called having long hair.

If there is no one to shave it, or if compelled by force and unable to shave, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs wore the adornments of laywomen. This gave rise to minds of desire and lust.

At that time, the host of the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā had newly taken a wife. The wife gave her adornments to Thullanandā and told her to wear them. Having put them on, she covered her head and lay down on the bed. The bridegroom returned from outside and, wanting to approach his wife, uncovered her head and only then realized who it was. He rebuked her, saying: "If I had not uncovered the head and had engaged in sexual relations, would that not have resulted in a grave offense? How can a bhikṣuṇī do such a thing? She has no śramaṇa conduct! She violates śramaṇa principles!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached her in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī wears adornments, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

Exceptions are as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs made adornments for others. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "These ones make adornments like prostitutes!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī makes adornments for others, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs spun thread. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "These ones depend on others for food and clothing, yet do not contemplate practicing the Way to repay the offerings of the faithful. What is the purpose of spinning thread? They have no śramaṇa conduct! They violate śramaṇa principles!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī spins thread, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If making waist cords, meditation belts, mesh bags, or thread for sewing robes, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, visiting the homes of lay acquaintances, spread out their bedding and stayed there. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "How can one leave one's own dwelling and stay in another's home? We are not pleased to see such inauspicious behavior!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, without asking the layperson, spreads out bedding and stays in their home, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If staying in the home of relatives, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, at a layperson's home, spread out the host's seating and bedding, or had someone else spread it out. When leaving, they did not put it away themselves or instruct someone else to put it away. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "How can bhikṣuṇīs spread out someone's seating and bedding and then not put it away when they leave! We are constantly acting as servants for these people!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, going to a layperson's home, spreads out the host's seating and bedding, or has someone else spread it out, and when leaving does not put it away herself or instruct someone else to put it away, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she entrusts someone to put it away, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs themselves cooked raw food items. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "How can bhikṣuṇīs cook raw food themselves? If they cook their own food, why do they also go begging from others? They have no śramaṇa conduct; they violate śramaṇa principles!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī herself cooks raw food items, it is a pācittika."

If it is for illness, there is no offense.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Chamā came to Śrāvastī. The bhikṣuṇī Caṇḍa Sumanā lent her a monastery dwelling to stay in. Chamā had attained the samādhi of loving-kindness and possessed great authority and virtue; her retinue was accomplished. Caṇḍa's disciples all revered Chamā and wished to follow her. When Caṇḍa became aware of this, she angrily scolded her, saying: "I lent you a monastery dwelling to stay in, and you repay me by enticing my disciples away!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached her in various ways: "How can one lend a monastery to another and then angrily slander her?" And so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī first permits someone to stay, and afterward angrily slanders her, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If the accusation is true, but spoken out of anger, there is no offense.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Bhaddā Kāpilānī developed an abscess on her inner thigh. Without informing the Sangha, she had a male physician lance it, drain the pus, wash it, and apply medicine. The elder bhikṣuṇīs saw this and reproached her, saying: "You are one who has already abandoned desire, so perhaps this is acceptable for you! But if it were someone who has not yet abandoned desire, would she not commit a grave offense?" And so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, without informing the Sangha, has a male person treat her illness, it is a pācittika."

If she wishes to have a male person treat her illness, she should strike the ghaṇṭā to assemble the Sangha, who should come before the sick person. Then her body should be wrapped in cloth, leaving exposed only the area that needs to be treated.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she has a woman treat her, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs at night would open the main gate and go out without telling another person to close it. At night, thieves came and stole the bhikṣuṇīs' robes and bowls. The elder bhikṣuṇīs reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī at night opens the main gate and goes out without telling another bhikṣuṇī to close it, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

In a place without danger, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs went to lay households at improper times. There was a certain wealthy household that thieves had constantly wanted to rob but had not found the opportunity. They inquired of passersby: "Who comes and goes at this household?" Someone said: "The bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā is on good terms with this household and frequently visits them." The thieves went and told Thullanandā: "Auntie! So-and-so is calling for you." She followed their words and went in the evening. The householder opened the gate for her, and the thieves rushed in and plundered everything. The householder angrily reproached her, saying: "If this bhikṣuṇī had not come at an improper time, I would not have opened the gate and would not have suffered this calamity! I made offerings hoping for merit, but instead incurred disaster. How is this different from supporting an enemy?" The elder bhikṣuṇīs reproached her in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī enters a layperson's home at an improper time, it is a pācittika."

Furthermore, bhikṣuṇīs whom laypeople summoned did not dare to go. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on this account, assembled the twofold Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣuṇīs to go when summoned by laypeople. From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, without being summoned by the layperson, enters their home at an improper time, it is a pācittika."

Improper time means: from after midday until before the appearance of dawn.

If a layperson summons her, she should carefully assess whether the messenger is a trustworthy person or not. She should also inquire carefully to ascertain whether it is true or false. If she still has doubts, upon reaching the door, she should first ask the household: "Did you indeed summon me?" Only then should she enter. She should further consider whether it is a time that could be dangerous or not.

If she goes to a layperson's home at an improper time, with one foot outside the threshold, it is a duṣkṛta; with both feet outside, it is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, a householder invited the bhikṣuṇī Sangha for a meal. The bhikṣuṇīs, before the meal time, put on their robes, took their bowls, and went to his house. They requested drinks from the food preparer, or asked for pot scrapings, or asked for rice. The food preparer thought: "The food being prepared today is precisely for these people. Whether given before or after, what difference does it make?" So he gave them everything, and the rice and drinks were completely exhausted. When the householder, at the proper time, struck the ghaṇṭā to assemble the Sangha and ordered the food to be served, the food preparer explained the situation in full. The householder then criticized and reproached them, saying: "These bhikṣuṇīs are like children, unable to endure even a short wait! So greedy for food; what Way could they possibly have?" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, having accepted an invitation, eats before the host has announced 'Eat as you please,' it is a pācittika."

If she eats before the announcement of "Eat as you please," each mouthful is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, after the bhikṣuṇīs had duly performed a banishment procedure, the banished bhikṣuṇī refused to leave. The elder bhikṣuṇīs reproached her in various ways, saying: "What is the difference between a banishment procedure and no banishment procedure?" And so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, having been subject to a banishment procedure, does not leave, it is a pācittika."

Banishment procedure means: a motion with three readings of the resolution.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she is ill, or if one of the eight dangers arises, or if the procedure was not conducted properly, there is no offense.

At that time, when the bhikṣuṇīs duly assembled the Sangha, certain bhikṣuṇīs did not go promptly. The bhikṣuṇīs waited for them, which hindered their practice of the Way. The elder bhikṣuṇīs reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, when the Sangha duly assembles, does not go promptly, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she is ill, or if she did not hear, or if one of the eight dangers arises, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs went to watch singing, dancing, and performances. This gave rise to minds of attachment; they lost their delight in the Way, and some returned to lay life or joined non-Buddhist orders. Laypeople who saw this criticized and reproached them, saying: "These ones watch singing, dancing, and performances like prostitutes! They have no śramaṇa conduct; they violate śramaṇa principles!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī watches singing, dancing, and performances, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

Exceptions are as stated above regarding viewing the king's palace and viewing paintings.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs went to border regions. The people of the border regions seized them and made them slaves, or stole their robes and bowls, or violated their celibacy. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī goes to a border region, it is a pācittika."

Border region means: a place where there are no bhikṣus or bhikṣuṇīs.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If one flies there, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs ordained persons of dual sex. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "How can bhikṣuṇīs ordain persons of dual sex? They have no sense of who is fit to be ordained and who is not! They have no śramaṇa conduct; they violate śramaṇa principles!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī ordains a person of dual sex, it is a pācittika."

If there is doubt, she should first examine the person.

From the arising of the intention, up until the three readings of the resolution are not yet complete, it is a duṣkṛta. When complete, for the preceptor, it is a pācittika; for the other teacher-nuns and Sangha members, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs ordained women whose two passages were fused into one. The elder bhikṣuṇīs reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī ordains a woman whose two passages are fused into one, it is a pācittika."

If the condition is discovered after ordination, there is no offense. The rest is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs ordained women who constantly had menstrual blood. When going to beg for food, blood flowed and stained their feet. Laypeople who saw this were disgusted and criticized and reproached them, saying: "These bhikṣuṇīs have no sense of who is fit to be ordained and who is not, ordaining such people who soil others' beds and seats! They have no śramaṇa conduct; they violate śramaṇa principles!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī ordains a woman who constantly has menstrual blood, it is a pācittika."

The rest is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs did not bow to bhikṣus, did not welcome them or see them off, and did not invite them to sit. The bhikṣus became angry and no longer gave instruction, and the bhikṣuṇīs remained ignorant and unable to learn the precepts. The elder bhikṣuṇīs saw this and reproached them in various ways, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on this account, assembled the twofold Sangha and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Is this truly so?" They answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways, saying: "Did I not previously state in the eight rules of respect that you should bow to bhikṣus? From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, seeing a bhikṣu, does not rise, does not bow, and does not invite him to sit, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she is ill, or if there was a prior grudge and they do not speak to each other, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs thought: "The Buddha does not permit us to shave the hair of our private parts. We should burn it instead!" So they burned it. At that time, a brahmin who had lost a sheep was searching for it and arrived at the bhikṣuṇīs' quarter. Smelling the odor of burning hair, he thought the bhikṣuṇīs had stolen and slaughtered his sheep. He went to the king and reported the matter. The king then summoned the bhikṣuṇīs and asked: "Aunties, did you kill the brahmin's sheep?" They answered: "We did not kill it!" He asked: "If you did not kill it, how is it that there is the smell of burning sheep's wool?" They then truthfully explained. The king heard this, laughed heartily, and released them. The ministers who heard about it criticized and reproached them, saying: "How can bhikṣuṇīs, instead of practicing the Way, burn the hair of their private parts?" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī burns the hair of her private parts, it is a pācittika."

The rest is as stated regarding shaving the hair of the private parts.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs went to laypersons' homes without wearing their saṃkakṣikā. The wind blew up their upper robes, exposing their bodies. Laypeople who saw this teased them and spoke coarse and vulgar words, causing them shame. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī enters a layperson's home without wearing her saṃkakṣikā, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If it is being washed, dyed, beaten, or sewn, or if she has none, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs sat face-to-face with laypeople, leaning close to their bodies while teaching the Dharma, appearing as if whispering privately. This gave rise to minds of attachment, and some returned to lay life or joined non-Buddhist orders. Laypeople criticized and reproached them. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī sits face-to-face with a layperson, leaning close to their body while teaching the Dharma, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs themselves sang and danced. The householders criticized and reproached them, saying: "These bhikṣuṇīs sing and dance themselves, like prostitutes!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī herself sings and dances, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs obstructed the receiving of the kaṭhina robe. The bhikṣuṇīs waited for a long time without it being received, which hindered their practice of the Way. The elder bhikṣuṇīs reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī obstructs the receiving of the kaṭhina robe, it is a pācittika."

If she is ill, or if she did not hear, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs obstructed the relinquishing of the kaṭhina robe, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī obstructs the relinquishing of the kaṭhina robe, it is a pācittika."

The rest is as stated above.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Chamā, being intelligent and quick-witted in debate, questioned and challenged the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus were unable to answer and were greatly shamed. Afterward, whenever they saw bhikṣuṇīs, they would leave the road to avoid them. As a result, no one would instruct the bhikṣuṇīs anymore, and they remained ignorant and unable to learn the precepts. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī questions and challenges a bhikṣu, it is a pācittika."

Certain bhikṣuṇīs who had doubts did not dare to ask questions, and because of this they remained ignorant and unable to learn the precepts. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on this account, assembled the twofold Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣuṇīs to first address the bhikṣu; if he permits the question, then she may ask. From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, without first addressing the bhikṣu, questions him about doctrine, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, Upananda constantly went in and out of the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā's dwelling. On one occasion, he put on his robe, took his bowl, and went to her place. They sat and moved about in a frivolous manner, exposing their bodies to each other. Upananda then discharged impure substances. Thullanandā took his undergarment and washed it, then inserted the impure substances into her own genitals, and consequently became pregnant. The bhikṣuṇīs saw this and asked: "Are you not practicing the holy life?" She answered: "It is not that I am not practicing the holy life! I took a man's impure substances and inserted them into my own genitals, which caused this pregnancy!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached her in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī inserts a man's impure substances into her own genitals, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs performed the non-Buddhist fire-worship ritual, lighting fires and reciting their incantations. The householders criticized and reproached them, saying: "These ones cannot even purify their own views; how could they attain the Way? They have no śramaṇa conduct; they violate śramaṇa principles!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī performs the non-Buddhist fire-worship ritual and lights a fire, it is a pācittika."

If she does it with wrong views, it is a sthūlātyaya. If she performs any of the various non-Buddhist practices, they are all pācittikas.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs bathed where people were present. People who saw them watched and made sport of them. The elder bhikṣuṇīs saw this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī bathes where people are present, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs recited non-Buddhist spells and incantations. Laypeople criticized and reproached them: "These ones recite non-Buddhist spells and have no intention of seeking the Way!" And so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, all as stated in the section on reciting medical texts and prescriptions. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī recites non-Buddhist spells and incantations, or teaches others to recite them, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs conferred full ordination with only a single assembly. Those so ordained were ignorant and unable to learn the precepts. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī confers full ordination with only a single assembly, it is a pācittika."

From the arising of the intention, up until the motion with three readings of the resolution is not yet complete, it is a duṣkṛta. When complete, for the preceptor, it is a pācittika; for the other teacher-nuns and Sangha members, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs performed the procedure for keeping disciples by themselves, performed the procedure for the two-year training in the precepts by themselves, and conferred the two-year training in the precepts by themselves. They were unable to properly instruct their disciples, who remained ignorant and unable to learn the precepts. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī performs the procedure for keeping disciples by herself, it is a pācittika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī performs the procedure for the two-year training in the precepts by herself, it is a pācittika."

"If a bhikṣuṇī confers the two-year training in the precepts by herself, it is a pācittika."

The rest is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, after performing the procedure confirming that the two-year training in the precepts was completed, waited overnight before conferring full ordination. During the interval, difficulties arose and the candidate was unable to receive full ordination. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, after performing the procedure confirming that the two-year training in the precepts is completed, waits overnight before conferring full ordination, it is a pācittika."

From the arising of the intention, up until before the appearance of dawn, it is a duṣkṛta. After the appearance of dawn, for the preceptor, it is a pācittika; for the other teacher-nuns and assembly, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she is ill, or if difficulties arise, or if the Sangha does not assemble, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, after performing the procedure for the two-year training in the precepts, waited overnight before conferring the training in the precepts. During the interval, difficulties arose and the candidate was unable to receive it. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, after performing the procedure for the two-year training in the precepts, waits overnight before conferring the training in the precepts, it is a pācittika."

From the arising of the intention, up until before the appearance of dawn, it is a duṣkṛta. After the appearance of dawn, for the preceptor, it is a pācittika; for the other teacher-nuns and assembly, it is a duṣkṛta.

Exceptions are as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs wove cloth themselves. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "How can bhikṣuṇīs, instead of contemplating the practice of the Way, weave cloth themselves, like other weavers!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī herself weaves cloth to wear, it is a pācittika."

If she weaves and throws the shuttle, each throw is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If weaving a waist cord or a meditation belt, there is no offense.

At that time, attendants of King Prasenajit and bhikṣuṇīs went sightseeing in dangerous places. They were robbed by bandits; some had their celibacy violated, and some were taken captive. Other bhikṣuṇīs reported this to the king. The king said: "I cannot even control myself; what can I do about the bhikṣuṇīs?" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, saying: "Why did you go traveling in dangerous places?" And so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī travels about in a dangerous place within the country, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she was already traveling on the road and danger subsequently arose, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs made images of themselves, or had others make them.

At that time, Thullanandā also had someone make her image. Seeing her own likeness, her mind gave rise to attachment, and she thought: "My appearance is like this; why should I destroy it by cultivating the holy life?" The elder bhikṣuṇīs saw this and reproached her in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī makes an image of herself, or has someone else make one, it is a pācittika."

Making an image of oneself means: either painting, or with wood, or with clay.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If someone secretly makes it and shows it to her, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs adorned women. This gave rise to minds disenchanted with the Way, and some returned to lay life or joined non-Buddhist orders. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "How can bhikṣuṇīs adorn women, like mothers adorning their daughters? How is that different from adorning oneself? Instead of contemplating the practice of the Way, they merely engage in improper decoration. They have no śramaṇa conduct; they violate śramaṇa principles!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī adorns a woman, it is a pācittika."

Adorning means: combing her hair, and even inserting a single flower or placing a single bracelet; each instance is a pācittika.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, while bathing in water, walked against the current. Being touched by the water, they gave rise to minds of desire and lust, and some returned to lay life or joined non-Buddhist orders. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī walks against the current in water, it is a pācittika."

If she walks against the current, each step is a pācittika; if she discharges impure substances, it is a sthūlātyaya.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she has no lustful intention, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs lay face up beneath the eaves where water dripped down, and the drops entered their genitals. This gave rise to minds of desire and lust, and some returned to lay life or joined non-Buddhist orders. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī lies face up in a place where water falls, it is a pācittika."

The rest is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs wound cord around their waists to make them slender and attractive. This gave rise to minds of desire and lust. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "These ones work on their waists to make them slender, like prostitutes! They have no intention toward the Way, but only engage in improper things!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī works on her waist to make it slender, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs applied various treatments to their bodies to make them attractive. This gave rise to minds of desire and lust, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī applies various treatments to her body, it is a pācittika."

The rest is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs dressed in the manner of courtesans. This gave rise to minds disenchanted with the Way, and some returned to lay life, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī dresses in the manner of a courtesan, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs dressed in the manner of lay women. This gave rise to minds disenchanted with the Way, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī dresses in the manner of a lay woman, it is a pācittika."

The rest is as stated above.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs, with lustful minds, gazed upon their own bodies. This gave rise to minds of desire and lust, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, with a lustful mind, gazes upon her own body, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs looked at themselves in mirrors. This gave rise to minds disenchanted with the Way, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī looks at herself in a mirror, it is a pācittika."

If she looks at her reflection in water, it is a duṣkṛta.

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

If she has a sore on her face and looks at it, there is no offense.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs engaged in various forms of divination for themselves, and also had others divine for them.
