Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "These ones have not abandoned wrong views; how could they attain the Way?" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī divines for herself, or has others divine for her, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs privately discussed among themselves: "Will we who have gone forth attain the ultimate goal? Or will we not attain it? Should we quit the holy life, or should we not? If we quit, will we get a good husband? How many children will we have? What will our fortune and status be?" Because of such discussions, worldly sentiments arose; they lost their delight in the Way, and some returned to lay life or joined non-Buddhist orders. The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, saying: "How can bhikṣuṇīs engage in worldly discussions and forget their resolve for the Way?" And so forth until the present rule was established for the bhikṣuṇīs, as stated above. "From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī engages in worldly discussions, it is a pācittika."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

### Second Division, Part Five: Bhikṣuṇī Rules of Confession (Pratideśanīya)

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs were fond of eating ghee and frequently begged it from others. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "Ghee makes people radiant and lustrous; it is what worldly people eat. How can bhikṣuṇīs not seek the flavor of the Dharma? Greedy for delicacies, seeking a fine complexion; how are they different from prostitutes? They have no śramaṇa conduct! They violate śramaṇa principles!" The elder bhikṣuṇīs heard this and reproached them in various ways, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on this account, assembled the twofold Sangha and asked the bhikṣuṇīs: "Is this truly so?" They answered: "It is truly so, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish the pratideśanīya rules for the bhikṣuṇīs. From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī eats ghee, she should confess before the bhikṣuṇīs: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy act. I now confess before the venerable aunties!' This is called the confession rule."

At that time, when bhikṣuṇīs received ghee during Sangha meals, at invitation meals, or while begging, they did not dare eat it. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on this account, assembled the twofold Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit bhikṣuṇīs to eat ghee that they receive without having begged for it. From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī begs for ghee and eats it, that bhikṣuṇī should confess before the bhikṣuṇīs: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy act. I now confess before the venerable aunties!' This is called the confession rule."

Certain ill bhikṣuṇīs needed ghee but did not dare beg for it. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on this account, assembled the twofold Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit ill bhikṣuṇīs to beg for and eat ghee. From now on, this rule should be stated as follows:

"If a bhikṣuṇī, without illness, begs for ghee and eats it for herself, that bhikṣuṇī should confess before the bhikṣuṇīs: 'I have fallen into a blameworthy act. I now confess before the venerable aunties!' This is called the confession rule."

For śikṣamāṇās and śrāmaṇerīs, it is a duṣkṛta.

For bhikṣuṇīs begging for oil, begging for honey, begging for rock sugar, begging for milk, begging for curds, begging for fish, and begging for meat, all are as stated above.

### Second Division, Part Six: Bhikṣuṇī Rules of Conduct to Be Learned (Śaikṣa-dharma)

At that time, Upāli asked the Buddha: "The

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese during the Liu Song dynasty by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle 15

I notice that the text provided for this fascicle appears to be empty. The source material between the delimiters contains no Chinese text to translate. There is only the fascicle header "五分律" (Five-Part Vinaya) but no actual content.

If you could provide the Chinese text for Fascicle 15 (or Fascicle 14, as referenced in the heading 卷第十四), I would be happy to translate it in full.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle the Fifteenth

### Beginning of the Third Division: The Method of Ordination, Part One

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha and told the bhikṣus: "In the past there was a king named Ulma, who had four sons by concubines: the first named Illuminating-Eye, the second named Keen-Eye, the third named Tamed-Elephant, and the fourth named Nīru. They were intelligent and far-reaching in wisdom, and all possessed majestic virtue. The chief queen had a son named Long-Life, who was dull, unworthy, and ugly, despised by all people. The queen thought: 'Although my son is the eldest, his talent does not match those others. Those four sons all possess majestic virtue; the kingdom's succession will surely fall to them. What plan can I devise to secure my son's inheritance?' She further thought: 'The king trusts and loves me, as well as the other consorts. I should first seek by affection, and then achieve it by reason!' Acting on this thought, she adorned herself and, when the king came to her, doubled her attentiveness and respect. When the king wished to be intimate with her, she suddenly wept. The king asked the reason, and the queen answered: 'A humble wish of mine is unfulfilled; it will be the end of me!' The king said: 'If your wish is at all reasonable, I swear I will not refuse you!' She then addressed the king: 'Your four sons all possess majestic virtue. Although my son is the eldest, his talent does not match others. If they inherit the great enterprise, they will surely seize it away! If the king would banish the four sons, my heart would be at ease!' The king said: 'The four sons are filial and brotherly, and have committed no offense against the kingdom. How can I banish them?' The queen further said: 'My heart labors and toils, truly for the sake of the family and the kingdom! These four sons of yours all possess majestic virtue, and the people have already each attached themselves to them. Once they compete, they will surely destroy one another, and the succession of the great kingdom will not necessarily go to your descendants!' The king said: 'Enough, enough! Say no more!' He then summoned the four sons and ordered them to leave the country. The four sons obeyed the command and immediately prepared their departure. At that time, the mothers of the four sons, as well as their sisters, all sought to go with them. Moreover, warriors, artisans, brāhmaṇas, elders, householders, and all manner of people were glad to follow, and the king permitted them all. Thereupon the four sons bowed farewell and departed, crossing the Pañcāla River and arriving north of the Himālayas, where the land was level and broad, the four directions clear and pure, and there were many fine fruits and various birds and beasts. The four sons, upon seeing this, called the brāhmaṇas, elders, and householders to stop, and together discussed: 'Of all the places we have passed through, none surpasses this one! Shall we dwell here?' All agreed without dissent. They immediately settled there and built a city. Within a few years, people came to join them like going to a marketplace, and gradually it flourished and became a great kingdom.

"Several years after their departure, the father king thought of his sons and asked his ministers: 'Where are my four sons now?' They answered: 'They are north of the Himālayas, near the Śākya forest. They have built a city, the people are prosperous, the land is fertile and the fields rich, and there is no lack of clothing or food.' The king, upon hearing this, sighed three times: 'My sons are capable!' He sighed thus three times. From this they were given the name the Śākya clan.

"Nīru had a son named Siṃhahanu. Siṃhahanu's son was named Godhanu. Godhanu's son was named Niṣūra. Niṣūra had four sons: the first named Śuddhodana, the second named Śuklodana, the third named Droṇodana, and the fourth named Amṛtodana. King Śuddhodana had two sons: the first named Bodhisattva, the second named Nanda. Śuklodana had two sons: the first named Ānanda, the second named Devadatta. Droṇodana had two sons: the first named Mahānāma, the second named Anuruddha. Amṛtodana had two sons: the first named Bāhva, the second named Bhadrika. The Bodhisattva had a son named Rāhula.

"The Bodhisattva from youth had the aspiration to leave home. The father king, fearing he would pursue the Way, constantly entertained him with the five desires. When he reached the age of fourteen, he had a chariot prepared for an excursion. Going out the eastern gate of the city, he encountered an old man, white-haired and stooped-backed, leaning on a staff, walking feebly. He asked the charioteer: 'Who is this person?' The charioteer answered: 'An old man!' He asked further: 'What does it mean to be old?' The charioteer answered: 'When one's years are advanced and faculties mature, the form changes and complexion declines, sitting and rising are extremely painful, and the remaining life is but little; this is what is called old.' The Bodhisattva said: 'Am I free from this?' The charioteer answered: 'Not yet!' He then turned the chariot back to the palace and thought to himself that he had not escaped the law of old age, and was sorrowful and joyless. The king asked the charioteer: 'Was the prince happy on the outing?' The charioteer answered: 'He was not happy!' The king asked: 'Why?' The charioteer answered: 'He encountered an old man; therefore he was not happy.' The king feared the astrologers' words would prove true and that he would leave home before long, so he further increased the five desires to entertain him.

"Long afterward, the Bodhisattva again ordered the charioteer to prepare the chariot for an excursion. Going out the southern gate of the city, he encountered a sick person, thin and emaciated, leaning against a doorway, gasping for breath. He asked the charioteer: 'Who is this person?' The charioteer answered: 'A sick person!' He asked further: 'What does it mean to be sick?' The charioteer answered: 'When the four great elements increase or diminish, one cannot eat or drink, the breath is feeble and faint, and life hangs by a moment; this is what is called sick.' He asked: 'Am I free from this?' The charioteer answered: 'Not yet!' He then turned the chariot back to the palace and thought to himself that he had not escaped old age and sickness, and his sorrow increased further. The king again asked the charioteer: 'Was the prince happy on this outing?' The charioteer answered: 'He was even more unhappy.' The king asked: 'Why?' The charioteer answered: 'He encountered a sick person; therefore he was not happy.' The king feared he would leave home before long, so he further increased the five desires, entertaining him day and night.

"Long afterward, the Bodhisattva again ordered the charioteer to prepare the chariot for an excursion. Going out the western gate of the city, he encountered a dead person, the corpse being carried along in front, with the household's men and women wailing and following behind. He asked the charioteer: 'Who is this person?' The charioteer answered: 'A dead person!' He asked further: 'What does it mean to be dead?' The charioteer answered: 'When the breath ceases and the spirit departs, one has no further awareness. One is abandoned in the empty wilderness, forever separated from kin and relatives; this is what is called dead.' He asked: 'Am I free from this?' The charioteer answered: 'Not yet!' The Bodhisattva thought to himself that he had not escaped the laws of old age, sickness, and death, and his sorrow increased further. He immediately turned the chariot back. On the way he encountered a person who had shaved off his beard and hair, wearing a Dharma robe, holding a bowl, gazing at the ground as he walked. He asked the charioteer: 'Who is this person? His clothing is different from the world.' The charioteer answered: 'A person who has left home!' He asked further: 'What does it mean to leave home?' The charioteer answered: 'One who skillfully subdues himself, possesses all deportment, constantly practices patience, and has compassion for sentient beings; this is what is called leaving home.' The Bodhisattva, upon hearing this, exclaimed three times: 'Excellent! Only this is truly joyful!' He then got down from the chariot, and with reverence asked: 'Why are your appearance and clothing so completely different from the world?' The answer was as above. The Bodhisattva again exclaimed three times: 'Excellent! Only this is truly joyful!' He then mounted the chariot and headed toward the palace. A certain woman saw the Bodhisattva from afar and, her heart filled with desire and love, spoke this verse:

> "A mother with such a son is joyful, and his father too is greatly pleased;
> A woman with such a husband would find joy surpassing nirvāṇa."

"The Bodhisattva, upon hearing the word 'nirvāṇa,' was joyful and elated, and thought to himself: 'When will I attain this supreme nirvāṇa?' Returning to the palace, he pondered that he had not escaped the laws of birth, old age, sickness, and death. The king asked the charioteer: 'Was the prince happy on this outing?' The charioteer answered: 'At first going out he was not pleased, but on returning he was very happy!' The king asked: 'Why?' The charioteer answered: 'Going out he encountered a dead person; therefore he was not pleased. Returning, he saw a bhikṣu; therefore he was happy.' The king then thought: 'The astrologers' words are true; he will certainly leave home!' He further increased the five desires, entertaining him day and night. After the Bodhisattva had been entertained by the courtesans, he briefly fell asleep, and all the courtesans also fell deeply into slumber. The Bodhisattva soon awoke and observed the courtesans, who were leaning on one another as pillows, some with their bodies exposed like wooden mannequins, with nasal mucus, tears from their eyes, and drool flowing from their mouths, while lutes, zithers, pipes, and flutes lay scattered on the ground. He also saw the palace, which appeared like a tomb. Having seen this, the Bodhisattva exclaimed three times: 'Woe! Woe!' He went to look at his father king's dwelling palace, and the palace's appearance had likewise transformed. He again exclaimed: 'Woe!' and deep revulsion arose in him. Thereupon the Bodhisattva ordered his servant Chandaka: 'Rise and saddle the horse! Do not let anyone hear!' Chandaka said: 'Night is not the time for going out. There should be no excursion, and there is no enemy threatening the upper palace. May I ask why you order the horse saddled at night?' The prince answered: 'There is a great enemy. Do you not know? The enemies of old age, sickness, and death are the greatest of enemies! Quickly saddle the horse. Do not delay!' He then saddled the white horse and led it to the central courtyard, reporting: 'The horse has arrived!' The Bodhisattva went to the horse and was about to mount. The horse let out a great, sorrowful neigh. The devas, fearing there would be obstruction, scattered the horse's sound so that people could not hear it. The Bodhisattva mounted the horse and headed toward the gate, and the gate opened of itself. He then headed toward the city gate, and the gate also opened of itself. Having passed through the gate, he headed toward the Anupiya grove, not far from the city. He dismounted, removed his jeweled garments, and said to Chandaka: 'You may lead the horse back and take the jeweled garments to the palace. Tell them that I bow and address my parents: "I now take leave to pursue the Way. I shall return before long. Please do not worry!"' Chandaka wept and knelt for a long time, saying: 'The astrologers of old prophesied that the prince would become a wheel-turning sage king, with the seven treasures and a thousand sons, ruling the four continents, governing the world with the true Dharma, without the use of arms, in natural peace. Why now do you abandon this royal position, remove your jeweled garments, and accept suffering in the mountains and wilderness?' The Bodhisattva asked in return: 'What else did the astrologers prophesy at that time?' Chandaka answered: 'They said that if you did not delight in ruling the world, and left home to pursue the Way, you would achieve the unsurpassed, perfect, right enlightenment.' The Bodhisattva said: 'Having heard these words, why do you now worry? Just quickly return home and inform my parents: "Even if my bones dry out and decay, unless I exhaust the source of birth, old age, sickness, and death, I will never return!"' Thereupon Chandaka, weeping sorrowfully, prostrated himself before him, circumambulated him three times to the right, and led the horse, carrying the jeweled garments, back to the palace.

"The Bodhisattva went forward and saw a hunter wearing a kāṣāya robe. He went to him and exchanged his own garments, worth a hundred thousand, for it, and putting on the kāṣāya, went on his way. The Bodhisattva went further toward a Sumanā tree. Beneath the tree there was a barber, and he asked to have his hair removed. The barber shaved it. Śakra, Lord of the Devas, in the time it takes a strong man to flex and extend his arm, arrived before the Bodhisattva, received the hair in a cloth, and took it back to the heavenly palace. After the shaving, the Bodhisattva thought: 'I have now become one who has left home, and the precepts are naturally complete.' Thereupon he gradually traveled and arrived at Rājagṛha. King Bimbisāra from youth had five wishes: first, that when his father king passed away, he would succeed to the throne; second, that when he was king, he would encounter the appearance of a Buddha in the world; third, that he would see the Buddha in person and attend and make offerings to him; fourth, that he would generate a joyful mind and hear the true Dharma; fifth, that having heard the Dharma, he would immediately attain faith and understanding. The Bodhisattva entered the city to beg for food. His deportment was composed and orderly, gazing at the ground as he walked. At that time there was no bowl, so he held a lotus leaf, passing along the road, and the leaf did not separate from the stem. At that time the king was with his ministers on a high tower, and seeing the Bodhisattva from afar, considered him extraordinary and refined. Looking at his ministers, he said: 'I have never seen or heard of anyone comparable to this person. He must be a divine sage!' They all said: 'We have heard that north of the Himālayas, in Kapilavastu, there is a king named Śuddhodana, who begot a son named Bodhisattva. Astrologers examined him and said that if he remained a householder, he would become a wheel-turning sage king, ruling the four continents, with the seven treasures coming to him naturally: namely the wheel treasure, elephant treasure, horse treasure, jewel treasure, woman treasure, minister treasure, and general treasure. The king would have a thousand sons, brave and strong. He would govern the world with the Dharma, without the use of arms, in natural peace. If he did not delight in worldly things and left home to pursue the Way, upon completing the Way he would be called a Buddha, delivering beings from birth and death. We have heard he has left home; this person must be he!' The king, upon hearing these words, was greatly overjoyed and said: 'Of my five wishes of old, one has already been fulfilled. The remaining four will surely be obtained now!' He immediately ordered two men: 'Go and see where the Bodhisattva is resting. I shall go visit him.' They received the order and hastened. They saw the Bodhisattva, having finished begging for food, return to Mount Pāṇḍava, facing the direction of the Pāpīyas country, sitting in cross-legged meditation. One person remained to observe, while the other returned to report. The king immediately had his chariot prepared, went to where the Bodhisattva was staying, stopped at the foot of the mountain, and walked up the mountain to reach the Bodhisattva. The Bodhisattva said: 'Welcome, great king! Are you not fatigued?' The king bowed his head in homage at his feet, then sat to one side, and addressed the Bodhisattva: 'In what country were you born? Of what clan did you leave home?' The Bodhisattva answered: 'I was born north of the Himālayas, in Kapilavastu of the Śākya country. My father is named Śuddhodana, and my clan name is Gautama.' The king, wishing to test the Bodhisattva, said: 'Bhikṣu, your clan is noble and esteemed. For generations you have been royal lineage, and your saintly virtue is natural. You ought to rule the four seas. All within the four seas look up to you with longing. If you would condescend to lower your aspirations, I too would serve you as a vassal, facing north.' The Bodhisattva answered: 'No position is more exalted than that of a wheel-turning king, and I have already abandoned it. How much more the four seas! The reason I left home to seek the Way is to deliver all beings from the great suffering of birth and death! Why do you not ask me, when I have completed the Way, to deliver you first, instead of pettily trying to entice me with this?' The king said: 'Excellent! These words are most joyful! On the day you complete the Way, I pray you first deliver me and the people of this kingdom!' The Bodhisattva consented, and the king was greatly overjoyed. He bowed at his feet and took his leave.

"After the king departed, the Bodhisattva headed toward the Bodhi tree. Not far from the tree he saw a person cutting grass, named Svastika. He begged some grass from him, took it to the foot of the tree, spread it out, sat in cross-legged meditation, straightened his body, set his mind right, and fixed his attention before him. He then removed the five hindrances, separated from desires and unwholesome dharmas, and attained up to the fourth dhyāna, sported within it, and practiced the thirty-seven factors of the path to enlightenment. With this purified mind, the three kinds of knowledge illuminated thoroughly, namely: knowledge of past lives, knowledge of others' minds, and knowledge of the exhaustion of the outflows, as described in the Sūtra of Auspicious Manifestations and Original Arising. Thereupon he arose and went to the village of Uruvilvā. Having just attained the Buddha Way, he sat beneath a grove of trees. In the first watch of the night, he contemplated the twelve links of dependent origination in forward and reverse order: Because of this condition, this exists; when this condition ceases, this ceases. Namely: ignorance conditions formations; formations condition consciousness; consciousness conditions name-and-form; name-and-form conditions the six sense bases; the six sense bases condition contact; contact conditions feeling; feeling conditions craving; craving conditions grasping; grasping conditions becoming; becoming conditions birth; birth conditions old age, death, sorrow, lamentation, suffering, and affliction. If ignorance ceases, then formations cease; if formations cease, then consciousness ceases; if consciousness ceases, then name-and-form ceases; if name-and-form ceases, then the six sense bases cease; if the six sense bases cease, then contact ceases; if contact ceases, then feeling ceases; if feeling ceases, then craving ceases; if craving ceases, then grasping ceases; if grasping ceases, then becoming ceases; if becoming ceases, then birth ceases; if birth ceases, then old age, death, sorrow, lamentation, suffering, and affliction all cease. Having seen this meaning, he spoke this verse:

> "All dharmas that arise from conditions are like this; the ascetic first begins meditation.
> Having understood this law of conditions, one can remove all doubts.
> All dharmas that arise from conditions are like this; the ascetic first begins meditation.
> Shattering the darkness of Māra, like the sun rising into the sky."

"At that time the World-Honored One had a wind ailment in his body. The mountain spirit of Mount Masūra immediately obtained a harītakī fruit and offered it to the Buddha: 'May the Buddha eat this to cure the wind ailment!' The Buddha accepted and ate it, and the wind ailment was cured. He sat in cross-legged meditation for seven days, experiencing the bliss of liberation. After the seven days had passed, he arose from samādhi and traveled among people.

"At that time there were five hundred merchants traveling with five hundred carts. Among them were two leading men: the first named Trapuṣa, the second named Bhallika. The two men had a former good friend who, after death, had become a benevolent spirit and constantly followed them. The spirit thought: 'The Buddha has just now accomplished the great Way, and no one has yet offered food. I should now cause these two men to feed the Buddha, so that for the long night they may obtain peace!' Immediately, through spiritual power, the cart-oxen all stumbled. The people were frightened and looked in all four directions seeking the spirit. That spirit, from the sky, spoke: 'Do not be afraid! Do not be afraid! The Buddha, the World-Honored One, has just now accomplished the great Way. He sat quietly for seven days, arose from concentration and was traveling, and is now sitting beneath that tree. No one has yet offered him food. If you offer him flour-cakes and honey, you will obtain peace for the long night!' The people were overjoyed and immediately mixed flour-cakes with honey. They all went to the tree. From afar they saw the World-Honored One, his appearance outstanding and extraordinary, his faculties tranquil and settled, possessing the thirty-two marks of a great person, with a circular radiance extending one fathom, like a golden mountain. They prostrated at the Buddha's feet and offered the flour-cakes and honey. The World-Honored One thought: 'Past Buddhas all received offerings with a bowl; future Buddhas will also be the same. I too should now use a bowl to receive offerings.' The Four Heavenly Kings, knowing the Buddha's intention, each took a naturally fragrant, pure stone bowl and offered it to the World-Honored One, saying: 'We humbly pray you will compassionately accept these vessels of ours and receive the merchants' offering!' The Buddha further thought: 'If I take one king's bowl, the other kings will not be pleased.' So he accepted all four bowls, stacked them in his left hand, pressed them with his right hand, and merged them into one bowl, with which he received the offering. Having received it, he said: 'You should take refuge in the Buddha and take refuge in the Dharma!' They then received the twofold refuge. These were the first among humans, the two merchants, to receive the twofold refuge. He then spoke a verse of joyous blessing for them:

> "May you two-footed ones be at peace; may the four-footed ones also be at peace.
> May you be at peace in going; may you also be at peace in returning.
> Just as plowing the field has its hope, and planting seeds also has its hope,
> So too does your voyage into the sea have hope; the fruit you obtain will also be like that."

"At that time, after the World-Honored One had spoken this verse, he further expounded various wonderful Dharma for the merchants, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening them. He then went to another tree and ate the flour-cakes and honey. Having eaten, he again sat in cross-legged meditation, entered concentration for seven days, and experienced the bliss of liberation. After the seven days had passed, he went to where the dragon Mucalinda was and sat beneath a tree. The dragon emerged from the water and offered non-human food to the World-Honored One. The Buddha accepted and ate it, then again entered concentration for seven days, experiencing the bliss of liberation. At that time it rained for seven days. The clouds were very dark, causing one's hair to stand on end. The dragon thought: 'Now the rain is fearsome. I should transform into a great body, coil around the Buddha seven times, and cover the Buddha's head above, so that wind, rain, mosquitoes, and gadflies do not disturb and trouble the World-Honored One!' He then did so. After seven days had passed, the World-Honored One arose from samādhi. The dragon saw that the rain had stopped and the sky was clear. He abandoned his original form, transformed into a young man, bowed his head, and addressed the Buddha: 'I transformed into a great body, coiled around seven times, and covered the Buddha's head above, wishing to shield from wind, rain, mosquitoes, and gadflies, not to cause offense or disturbance!'

"The Buddha, based on this matter, spoke this verse:

> "In quiet places, the joy of solitude; hearing the Dharma, the joy of seeing the Dharma.
> Not harming the world is joy; being compassionate toward beings is joy.
> Being free from desire in the world is joy; equally crossing over attachments is joy.
> One who can subdue the conceit of self: this is the supreme joy."

"After the Buddha had spoken the verse, he arose and went to the village of Uruvilvā Senā. He entered the village to beg for food and in turn came to the house of the brāhmaṇa Senā. At the gate he stood silently. His daughter Sujātā, seeing the Buddha's majestic and extraordinary appearance, came forward, took the Buddha's bowl, filled it with fine food, and offered it to the World-Honored One. The Buddha accepted the food and said: 'You should take refuge in the Buddha and take refuge in the Dharma!' She then received the twofold refuge. This was the first among women: Sujātā was the first to receive the twofold refuge as an upāsikā. After the Buddha had eaten, he returned again to beneath the Bodhi tree, sat in cross-legged meditation, and was in samādhi for seven days, experiencing the bliss of liberation. After the seven days had passed, he arose from samādhi, put on his robe, took his bowl, and again went to that house. Senā offered food and received the twofold refuge, also as described above. Afterward the Buddha went again to that house. His wife, seeing the Buddha, offered food and received the twofold refuge, also as described above. Afterward the Buddha went again to that house. Their four sisters, seeing the Buddha, offered food and received the twofold refuge, also as described above. After eating, the Buddha returned again to beneath the Bodhi tree, arose from samādhi of seven days, and headed toward the Ajapāla Nyagrodha tree. On the way he saw a woman churning curds to make butter, and he begged food from her. That woman took the bowl, filled it with curds, and offered it to the Buddha. She received the twofold refuge, also as described above. After eating, the Buddha went forward to the tree and was in samādhi for seven days. After the seven days had passed, he arose from samādhi and had this thought: 'The Dharma I have attained is extremely profound and subtle, difficult to understand and difficult to see, tranquil and unconditioned, known by the wise, not within the reach of the foolish! Sentient beings delight in and are attached to the den of the three realms, accumulating these actions. How could they awaken to the twelve links of dependent origination, this extremely profound, subtle, and difficult-to-see Dharma? Moreover, to still all formations, sever all streams, exhaust the source of attachments, and achieve nirvāṇa without remainder is even more extremely difficult. If I were to teach, I would only weary myself in vain, wither and suffer for nothing!'

"At that time the World-Honored One, wishing to further clarify the meaning of the inexpressible, spoke this verse:

> "The Way I have accomplished is difficult. If I were to speak it for those in their dens,
> Going against the current to reverse birth and death, the profound subtlety is extremely hard to understand.
> Covered over by the stain of desire, in darkness with nothing to see,
> Those of greed, anger, and delusion cannot enter this Dharma."

"At that time the World-Honored One, for this reason, remained silent and did not teach the Dharma. Then Brahmā, the Heavenly King, from the Brahma heaven above, knew the Buddha's thought from afar and had this thought: 'Now the Buddha, the Perfectly Enlightened One, has appeared in the world but does not expound the Dharma he has realized for sentient beings. The world will long decline and forever remain in blind darkness; upon death, beings will again fall into the three evil destinies!' Having thought this, in the time it takes a strong man to flex and extend his arm, he vanished from the Brahma heaven and appeared before the Buddha. Bowing his head in homage at the Buddha's feet, he stood to one side and addressed the Buddha: 'We humbly pray the World-Honored One will have compassion for sentient beings and teach the Dharma at this time! There are sentient beings who can receive the Buddha's teaching. If they do not hear it, they will fall back!' He repeated this three times. He further, based on this meaning, spoke a verse to request the Buddha:

> "Previously in this land of Magadha, impure and mixed dharmas were always taught.
> Please open the gate of amṛta and expound the pure and unadulterated meaning!
> Since I have been in the Brahma palace, I have seen all the ancient Buddhas teach.
> We humbly pray the present All-Seeing One will also spread the Dharma-hall teaching!
> Sentient beings are submerged in sorrow and affliction, unable to leave birth, old age, and death,
> Yet many among them delight in goodness; please teach the Dharma of victory in battle!"

"At that time the World-Honored One silently accepted this. With the Buddha-eye he universally surveyed the world and saw that the faculties of sentient beings were sharp or dull: some feared the three evil destinies of future lives; some could receive the Dharma, like the great ocean; some were like lotus buds in the mud, some having emerged from the water, some not yet emerged, yet unstained. He then spoke this verse:

> "Previously, fearing only vain weariness, I did not teach the extremely profound meaning.
> Now the amṛta shall be opened; all should hear it."

"At that time Brahmā, upon hearing this verse, was joyful and elated. He prostrated at the Buddha's feet, circumambulated three times to the right, and suddenly vanished, returning to the heavenly palace. The Buddha thought: 'The amṛta shall be opened. Who should hear it first? Udraka Rāmaputra is intelligent and easily awakened; this person should be first!' Having thought this, he was about to set out when a deva in the sky spoke: 'Udraka Rāmaputra passed away seven days ago.' The Buddha said: 'Alas! He has suffered a great loss. How could he not have heard the drum of the amṛta Dharma?' He further reflected: 'The amṛta shall be opened. Who should hear it next? Ārāḍa Kālāma is intelligent and easily awakened; he should hear it next!' Just as he was about to set out, the deva again spoke: 'Ārāḍa Kālāma passed away last night.' The Buddha said: 'Alas! He did not get to hear the drum of the amṛta Dharma. In the coming and going of birth and death, how can rest be obtained?' He further reflected: 'The amṛta shall be opened. Who should hear it next? The father king long ago dispatched five men who followed and attended me in hardship. This merit should be repaid! These five men are now in the country of Vārāṇasī, in the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park.' Having thought this, he set out. Before he arrived, on the way he met a brāhmaṇa named Upajīva. Seeing the World-Honored One from afar, with his outstanding and extraordinary appearance, his faculties tranquil and settled, a circular radiance extending one fathom, like a golden mountain, the brāhmaṇa asked: 'Whose disciple are you? What Way do you practice? That you have attained such dignity!'

"At that time the World-Honored One answered with a verse:

> "Omniscience is supreme; free from bonds, free from defilement.
> I do not practice following a teacher; I have naturally penetrated the holy Way.
> Alone, without equal, able to bring peace to the world;
> At Vārāṇasī I shall beat the drum of the amṛta Dharma."

"The brāhmaṇa asked further: 'You call yourself the supreme one. I wish to hear the meaning of this!'

"The Buddha again answered with a verse:

> "Able to remove all fetters, extinguish all outflows of the three realms,
> And crush all evil dharmas: therefore I am supreme!"

"The brāhmaṇa did not accept this, slapped his thigh, and departed. The brāhmaṇa's guardian spirit from past lives, from the sky, spoke a verse to him:

> "The Buddha has just appeared in the world, honored above and below among devas and humans.
> How is it that having encountered him, you turn and abandon him to go?"

"Although the brāhmaṇa heard this verse, he still went without looking back.

"Thereupon the World-Honored One went to Vārāṇasī, heading toward the five men. The five men saw the Buddha coming from afar and made an agreement among themselves: 'The śramaṇa Gautama formerly ate only one sesame seed and one grain of rice a day, and still could not attain the Way. Now that he has become desirous, he is far from the Way! We will merely spread a small seat for him, but we must not rise to greet him, bow, or inquire after his health!' When the World-Honored One arrived, the five men could not help themselves and rose to bow. They took his robe and bowl, spread a fine seat, and washed his feet with water. Yet they still showed disrespect to the Tathāgata, addressing him by his family name to his face: 'So-and-so, please take this seat!' The Buddha told the five men: 'You foolish ones made an agreement. How is it that you could not hold firm to it? Do not disrespect the Buddha by calling him by his family name to his face, lest you bring upon yourselves great suffering and retribution for the long night! I have now accomplished the unsurpassed, perfect enlightenment. You should with one mind listen to and receive my teaching and instruction. If you comply without opposition or resistance, you will before long attain the noble going forth, purely cultivate the holy life, and directly realize the fruit of the Way! Birth and death are ended; the holy life is established; what was to be done has been done; you will fully understand the five aggregates and abide in nirvāṇa.' The five men replied: 'You previously performed such difficult austerities and still did not attain the superhuman Dharma or the noble and fully satisfying attainment! How much less now that you have strayed from the Way and indulge in desires, can the superhuman Dharma be attained!' The Buddha again told them: 'Do not disrespect the Tathāgata's unsurpassed, perfect enlightenment. The Buddha has not strayed from the Way, nor does he indulge in desires!' The five men, upon hearing this, abandoned their former attitude. The Buddha further told them: 'There are two extremes in the world that should not be approached: the first is attachment to sensual desire, saying there is no fault in desire; the second is the wrong view of tormenting the body, which has no trace of the Way. Abandoning these two extremes, one finds the Middle Way, which produces the eye, knowledge, illumination, and awakening, leading toward nirvāṇa. What is the Middle Way? It is the Eightfold Noble Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. This is the Middle Way. There are further the Four Noble Truths: the Noble Truth of suffering, the Noble Truth of the origin of suffering, the Noble Truth of the cessation of suffering, and the Noble Truth of the path to the cessation of suffering. What is the Noble Truth of suffering? It is the suffering of birth, the suffering of old age, the suffering of sickness, the suffering of death, the suffering of sorrow, lamentation, and affliction, the suffering of meeting those one hates, the suffering of being separated from those one loves, and the suffering of not obtaining what one seeks. In summary, the five grasping aggregates are suffering. This is called the Noble Truth of suffering. What is the Noble Truth of the origin of suffering? It is craving for existence, together with the afflictions that co-arise, delighting and attaching everywhere. This is called the Noble Truth of the origin of suffering. What is the Noble Truth of the cessation of suffering? It is the cutting off of craving without remainder, cessation, and nirvāṇa. This is called the Noble Truth of the cessation of suffering. What is the Noble Truth of the path to the cessation of suffering? It is the Eightfold Noble Path. This is called the Noble Truth of the path to the cessation of suffering. This Dharma I had not previously heard: the eye arose, knowledge arose, illumination arose, awakening arose, penetration arose, wisdom arose. This Dharma should be known; I had not previously heard: the eye arose and so on up to wisdom arose. This Dharma is already known; I had not previously heard: the eye arose and so on up to wisdom arose. This is the Noble Truth of suffering; this Noble Truth of suffering should be known; this Noble Truth of suffering is already known. I had not previously heard: the eye arose and so on up to wisdom arose. This is the Noble Truth of the origin of suffering; this Noble Truth of the origin of suffering should be abandoned; this Noble Truth of the origin of suffering is already abandoned. I had not previously heard: the eye arose and so on up to wisdom arose. This is the Noble Truth of the cessation of suffering; this Noble Truth of the cessation of suffering should be realized; this Noble Truth of the cessation of suffering is already realized. I had not previously heard: the eye arose and so on up to wisdom arose. This is the Noble Truth of the path to the cessation of suffering; this Noble Truth of the path to the cessation of suffering should be cultivated; this Noble Truth of the path to the cessation of suffering is already cultivated. I had not previously heard: the eye arose and so on up to wisdom arose. I have already truly known. This is the three turnings and twelve aspects of the Dharma wheel, by which unsurpassed, perfect enlightenment was accomplished.' When this Dharma was spoken, the earth shook in six ways.

"Kauṇḍinya, far from dust and freed from defilement, attained the purity of the Dharma-eye regarding all dharmas. The Buddha asked: 'Kauṇḍinya! Do you understand? Kauṇḍinya! Do you understand?' Kauṇḍinya answered: 'I understand, World-Honored One!' The earth spirits, upon hearing this, informed the sky spirits. The sky spirits informed the Cāturmahārājika Heaven. The Cāturmahārājika Heaven informed the Trāyastriṃśa Heaven. In this manner it was transmitted successively up to the Brahma Heaven, saying: 'The Buddha has now at Vārāṇasī turned the unsurpassed Dharma wheel, which had never been turned before! Whether by śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, devas, Māra, Brahmā, or anyone in the entire world, it had never been turned!' The devas rejoiced and rained down various flowers, all luminous, like stars falling to the ground. In the sky, celestial music was played. Thereupon Kauṇḍinya rose from his seat, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and addressed the Buddha: 'World-Honored One! I pray you grant me the going forth and the full ordination!' The Buddha said: 'Come, bhikṣu! Receive the full ordination. In my well-proclaimed Dharma and Vinaya, one can bring an end to all suffering and purely cultivate the holy life.' Kauṇḍinya's beard and hair fell away of themselves, a kāṣāya robe appeared on his body, and a bowl appeared in his hands. This was how Kauṇḍinya obtained the going forth and full ordination. From this time onward, he was known as Ājñāta Kauṇḍinya. The Buddha then taught the Dharma to the other four. Bhadrika and Vāṣpa, the two, attained the purity of the Dharma-eye, saw the Dharma and attained the fruit. Having seen the Dharma and attained the fruit, they rose from their seats, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and addressed the Buddha: 'World-Honored One! I pray you grant us the going forth and the full ordination!' The Buddha said: 'Come, bhikṣus!' And so on up to the bowl appearing in their hands, also as described above. He then taught the Dharma to the other two. Aśvajit and Mahānāma attained the purity of the Dharma-eye, saw the Dharma and attained the fruit. Having seen the Dharma and attained the fruit, they rose from their seats, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and addressed the Buddha: 'World-Honored One! I pray you grant us the going forth and the full ordination!' The Buddha said: 'Come, bhikṣus!' And so on up to the bowl appearing in their hands, also as described above.

"The Buddha told the five bhikṣus: 'You should single-mindedly seek the right severance of afflictions. I too formerly single-mindedly sought the right severance of afflictions, and therefore accomplished the unsurpassed, perfect enlightenment! What do you think: is form permanent or impermanent?' They answered: 'Impermanent!' He asked further: 'If it is impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?' They answered: 'Suffering!' He asked further: 'If it is suffering, is it self or not-self?' They answered: 'Not-self!' Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness were likewise, and the questions and answers were also as above. 'Therefore, bhikṣus! Form, whether internal or external, whether past, future, or present, should all be truly seen as not-self. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are also like this! A noble disciple should contemplate thus, become disenchanted, free from attachment, and thereby attain liberation. Attaining the knowledge of liberation: the holy life is established; what was to be done has been done!' When this Dharma was spoken, the five bhikṣus exhausted all outflows and attained the path of arhatship. At that time in the world there were six arhats.

"There was further a householder's son named Yaśas, who was by nature virtuous and good, disenchanted with the world, and delighted in hearing the Dharma. The World-Honored One thought: 'That householder's son Yaśas will leave home through faith!' He then went to the bank of the Varaṇā River, spread grass, and sat through the night. At that time, the householder's son, having amused himself with the five desires, briefly fell asleep, and all the courtesans were also sleeping. The householder's son soon awoke, looked at his dwelling, and it appeared like a tomb. He observed the courtesans, all like wooden mannequins, leaning on one another as pillows, with nasal mucus, tears from their eyes, and drool flowing from their mouths, while lutes, zithers, pipes, flutes, and musical instruments lay scattered about. He was greatly alarmed and a feeling of revulsion arose in him. He went toward where his father was staying and saw it was likewise. His revulsion increased further. He then went toward the gate, and the gate suddenly opened of itself. He went toward the door and the great city gate, and they all opened naturally. He went straight to the bank of the Varaṇā River and cried out loudly: 'I am now in distress and anguish, with nowhere to turn!'

"At that time the World-Honored One extended his golden-colored arm and beckoned, saying: 'Young man, come here! This place is unconditioned; there is no distress or anguish!' Yaśas, upon hearing the Buddha's voice, found all his distress and anguish suddenly vanish. He removed his lapis lazuli sandals and placed them on the bank, crossed the water, and went to the Buddha. From afar he saw the World-Honored One, whose appearance was outstanding and extraordinary, like a golden mountain, and a joyful mind arose in him. Having arrived, he bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and sat to one side. The Buddha expounded various wonderful Dharma for him, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening him. He then taught the Four Noble Truths of suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. Right there on his seat, far from dust and freed from defilement, he attained the purity of the Dharma-eye. Afterward, the courtesans awoke and together searched for Yaśas. Not knowing where he was, they told his father and mother. The parents searched in all four directions, following in succession, and also announced to people: 'If anyone knows where my son is, I will give them the jeweled garments my son was wearing!' His father went to the city gate at night, waited for it to open, and left the city. He saw his son's footprints and followed them. Having arrived at the riverbank, he saw the lapis lazuli sandals on the bank. Now joyful, now sorrowful, he set aside the sandals and crossed the water. The Buddha, seeing him from afar, feared this would disturb the son's wholesome mind, and through transformation created an obstruction so that the son could see the father but the father could not see the son. The father asked the Buddha: 'Śramaṇa, have you seen my son?' The Buddha said: 'Please sit down. If he is here, why worry about not seeing him?' Upon hearing these words, the father thought: 'The śramaṇa surely does not speak falsely!' He came forward, bowed at the Buddha's feet, and sat to one side. The Buddha expounded various wonderful Dharma for him, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening him, namely: a discourse on giving, a discourse on morality, a discourse on being reborn in heaven; the dangers and faults of the five desires, how they generate various outflows; the defilements of household life, and the freedom from attachment in leaving home. After expounding such various aids to enlightenment, he then further taught the Dharma that all Buddhas constantly teach, namely: suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. Right there on his seat, far from dust and freed from defilement, he attained the purity of the Dharma-eye, saw the Dharma and attained the fruit. Having seen the Dharma and attained the fruit, he received the threefold refuge, and then received the five precepts. Among all the upāsakas among humans, Yaśas's father was the first to receive the three refuges and five precepts. Yaśas, hearing the Buddha expound the Four Noble Truths for his father, exhausted all outflows and his mind was liberated. Afterward the Buddha enabled father and son to see each other. The father said to his son: 'Rise and return home! Your mother, having lost you, is sorrowful and nearly dead!' The Buddha said to the father: 'If a person has attained liberation from the outflows, could he return to receive desires?' He answered: 'He could not!' The Buddha said: 'While I was teaching you the Dharma, Yaśas contemplated all dharmas, exhausted his outflows, and his mind attained liberation!' The father addressed the Buddha: 'The Buddha taught me the Dharma, and thereby Yaśas has swiftly obtained a great benefit!' Thereupon Yaśas rose from his seat and addressed the Buddha: 'World-Honored One! I pray you grant me the going forth and the full ordination!' The Buddha said: 'Come, bhikṣu!' And so on up to the bowl appearing in his hands, also as described above. At that time in the world there were seven arhats.

"Then Yaśas's father rose from his seat, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and addressed the Buddha: 'We humbly pray the World-Honored One, together with Yaśas, will accept my food offering tomorrow!' The Buddha silently accepted. The father again prostrated at the Buddha's feet, circumambulated three times, and departed. He returned home and prepared various fine foods and drinks. When the time came, the Buddha, together with Yaśas, put on his robe, took his bowl, and went to his house, where he took his seat. The householder and his wife served the food with their own hands. After eating, when the washing water had been provided, the wife took a small seat and sat before the Buddha. The Buddha said: 'Sister, take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, take refuge in the Bhikṣu Sangha!' She then received the three refuges, and next received the five precepts. This was how Yaśas's mother first received the three refuges and the five precepts. At that time the World-Honored One, for Yaśas's mother and all the great and small members of her household, expounded various wonderful Dharma, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening them. They all became far from dust and freed from defilement, attained the purity of the Dharma-eye, saw the Dharma and attained the fruit. Having seen the Dharma and attained the fruit, they all received the three refuges and next received the five precepts.

"At that time Yaśas had four friends: the first named Pūrṇa, the second named Subāhu, the third named Vimala, the fourth named Gavāmpati. They heard that Yaśas had left home to cultivate the holy life under the śramaṇa Gautama, and discussed among themselves: 'His Way must be superior, to make a man of noble family disregard worldly glory! We should all go to the Great Śramaṇa and purely cultivate the holy life!' The four were delighted, with aspiration for the Way in their hearts. They went to where Yaśas was and asked him: 'Is the holy life you cultivate able to be complete and the most superior?' He answered: 'This Way is immeasurable and is the most superior!' He then took the four to the Buddha's presence. They prostrated at the Buddha's feet and stood to one side. The Buddha expounded various wonderful Dharma for them, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening them. They all, right there on their seats, became far from dust and freed from defilement, attained the purity of the Dharma-eye, saw the Dharma and attained the fruit. Having seen the Dharma and attained the fruit, they prostrated at the Buddha's feet and addressed the Buddha: 'World-Honored One! I pray you grant us the going forth and the full ordination!' The Buddha said: 'Come, bhikṣus!' And so on up to the bowl appearing in their hands, also as described above. Not long after receiving ordination, they diligently practiced without slacking and attained arhatship. At that time in the world there were eleven arhats.

"Fifty more of Yaśas's former friends and associates heard that Yaśas was cultivating the holy life under Gautama. They discussed leaving home, and so on up to attaining arhatship, all as described above. At that time in the world there were sixty-one arhats.

"The astrologer Asita knew that the Bodhisattva would become a Buddha and would turn the Dharma wheel at the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park in the country of Vārāṇasī. He also thought: 'After my life is over, among my disciples, the young brāhmaṇa Nārada will succeed me. All my offerings will belong to him. He will surely become attached to them and will no longer remember that the Buddha is to appear in the world. I should now, near the Deer Park, establish a dwelling for him and instruct him to think three times daily: "The Buddha is to appear in the world! When he appears, you should purely cultivate the holy life under him!"' Having thought this, he established the dwelling and instructed him as planned. Asita soon passed away. Nārada indeed obtained the offerings, and his attachment was deep. He no longer remembered that the Buddha was to appear in the world.

"At that time, the Nāga King Elāpattra had this thought: 'Long ago, Kāśyapa Buddha prophesied for me: "In the future, after hundreds of thousands of millions of billions of years, Śākyamuni Buddha will appear in the world. That Buddha will prophesy when you will escape the nāga body." The time should now have come; I should go to see the Buddha.' That nāga, in order to see the Buddha, on the six fast days sat in the Ganges River, holding a golden bowl filled with silver grain and a silver bowl filled with golden grain, and also adorned two women, and spoke this verse:

> "Who is the highest among kings? In what are the defiled and undefiled equal?
> How does one become stainless? Who is called a fool?
> Who is carried away by the stream? Attaining what is one called wise?
> How is one not carried by the stream and called liberated?"

"After the Nāga King had spoken this verse, he thought: 'If anyone can explain this verse, he is the Buddha! Or if he has heard it from the Buddha, he will surely show me where the Buddha is! I now see no other śramaṇa, brāhmaṇa, deva, Māra, Brahmā, or anyone in the world who can explain this verse.' Having thought this, he proclaimed: 'If anyone can explain this verse, I will give the golden and silver bowls filled with golden and silver grain, and these two women.' At that time, numerous other śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, elders, and householders competed to explain the verse's meaning for the nāga. The Nāga King recited it for them, but none could explain it. At that time, the young brāhmaṇa Nārada was revered by all the people of Magadha, who said: 'This young man has great knowledge and insight and can surely explain it!' They all went together to invite him. The young man thought: 'I am revered by the entire country. If I say I cannot, I will be abandoned by all those people. Although I have not yet understood it, I should devise a means to preserve my reputation!' He then told the people: 'All of you come with me to the nāga, and I shall explain it!' Thereupon the people, together with the young man, surrounded him respectfully and went to the nāga. He said to the nāga: 'Recite your verse, and I shall expound it!' The nāga then recited the verse. The young man said: 'This is very easy to explain! After seven days I shall come and explain it.' He then memorized the verse and first asked other śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, Pūraṇa Kāśyapa, and the six teachers. None could explain it. They all replied with evasive anger and abuse, saying: 'It is meaningless!' Wishing thereby to cover up their inability to explain it. The young man further thought: 'My teacher formerly told me the Buddha would appear in the world, and I should purely cultivate the holy life under him. Now the śramaṇa Gautama is in the Deer Park. He can surely explain it. I should go ask him.' He further thought: 'These six teachers are elderly and widely experienced, yet they cannot explain it! How much less the śramaṇa Gautama, who is young and has only recently left home; could he explain it!' He then thought: 'Light and darkness are natural and cannot be judged by seniority. Though Gautama is young, he should not be taken lightly!' Having thought this, he went to the Buddha, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, stood to one side, and recited the Nāga King's verse to ask the Buddha. The Buddha then explained:

> "The sixth king is the highest; the defiled and the defiled are equal.
> The undefiled is stainless; the defiled one is called a fool.
> The fool is carried away by the stream; one who can extinguish is called wise.
> Abandoning the stream and no longer flowing: this is called liberation."

"After the young man heard this verse explained, he deeply knew this was the Buddha. He memorized and held it, and on the seventh day went to the nāga. At that time, eighty-four thousand people were on both banks of the Ganges, wishing to hear the young man explain the verse's meaning. The young man again said to the nāga: 'Recite your previous verse.' The Nāga King recited it, and the young man then recited the verse the Buddha had spoken and explained it. The Nāga King, upon hearing the verse, was joyful and elated, and thought: 'The Buddha has appeared in the world! I have now already seen the Buddha! Why is this? I see no other śramaṇa, brāhmaṇa, deva, Māra, Brahmā, or anyone in the world who can explain this meaning.' Having thought this, he asked the young man: 'Tell me truly! The verse you spoke, from whom did you hear it? I now see no other śramaṇa, brāhmaṇa, or anyone in the world who can speak this verse. Only the Buddha, or one who has heard it from the Buddha!' The young man answered: 'I tell you truly: the Buddha has appeared in the world, and I heard it from him!' The Nāga King joyfully asked: 'Where is the Buddha now? I wish to see him!' The young man knelt and extended his right hand, pointing in the direction of the Buddha, saying: 'The Buddha is now there!' The Nāga King's joy increased further, and he exclaimed three times: 'Homage to the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One!' He then said to the young man: 'Please escort me to the Buddha's presence to pay respects to the World-Honored One!' The young man answered: 'Very well!' The Nāga King then resumed his own body. His body was large and long, his eyes like great bowls, his breath like thunder, fire light coming from his mouth. He rose up against the current from the water. The eighty-four thousand people all followed along. Having reached the shore, the nāga then transformed into a wheel-turning sage king, went up the bank, and proceeded to the Buddha. From afar he saw the World-Honored One, whose appearance was outstanding and extraordinary, like a golden mountain. The Nāga King was overjoyed and paid infinite reverence. The Buddha, seeing the Nāga King, called him by name: 'Welcome, Nāga King Elāpattra!' The Nāga King, upon hearing this, was further delighted and reverent: 'The World-Honored One knows my name! The Sugata recognizes my name!' He came forward, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, stood to one side, recited his original verse, and asked the Buddha. The Buddha recited for him the verse that the young man had received. The Nāga King, upon hearing it, was first greatly joyful, and then wept. The Buddha asked the Nāga King: 'Why in a moment are you now joyful, now sorrowful?' He answered: 'World-Honored One! I recall that in the past, under Kāśyapa Buddha, I purely cultivated the holy life. At a later time, I took a purple flower stem and went to the Buddha and asked: "World-Honored One! If a bhikṣu kills this grass, what offense does he incur?" The Buddha answered me: "Due to this cause and condition, some may fall into the most painful hells!" Upon hearing these words, I neither believed nor respected them, and deliberately pricked an erāpattra tree leaf, thinking: "Let me see what retribution there will be!" In the end I did not abandon this view, nor did I repent. After my life ended, I was born in this long-lived nāga existence. Because of this karma, I am called Elāpattra Nāga. After receiving this body, I went again to the Buddha and asked: "When will I escape from this nāga body?" The Buddha answered me: "In the future, after hundreds of thousands of billions of millions of years, Śākyamuni Buddha will appear in the world. That Buddha will prophesy when you will attain liberation!" Now having seen the World-Honored One, a rare and extraordinary feeling arises in me. I now know that the words of all Buddhas are without falsehood; therefore I laugh with joy. Yet I also reflect that I formerly disobeyed the Buddha's teaching, and now again I am unable to receive the Buddha's clear precepts; therefore I weep.' He further addressed the Buddha: 'I pray you prophesy when I shall escape from this nāga body!' The Buddha said: 'In the future, after hundreds of thousands of billions of millions of years, Maitreya Buddha will appear in the world. At that time you will escape the nāga body, leave home, receive the precepts, broadly cultivate the holy life, and exhaust the source of suffering!' The Buddha then conferred the three refuges on the nāga as an upāsaka. He further expounded various wonderful Dharma for the eighty-four thousand people, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening them, namely: a discourse on giving, and so on up to renunciation being joy. After they were all gladdened, he further taught the Dharma that all Buddhas constantly teach: suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. The eighty-four thousand people, right there on their seats, became far from dust and freed from defilement, attained the purity of the Dharma-eye, saw the Dharma and attained the fruit. Having seen the Dharma and attained the fruit, they received the three refuges and then received the five precepts. Thereupon the Nāga King said to the young man: 'What do you want with these nāga women? Nāga women are easily angered and may harm each other with poison fire! Whatever gold, silver, or precious things you need, I will give them all to you!' The young man answered: 'Enough, enough, Nāga King! I do not want the nāga women, nor do I want gold or silver! Because I heard the last verse the Buddha spoke, I have become free from desire for the desire realm.' After the Buddha had finished teaching the Dharma, he said to the Nāga King: 'You may return to your abode!' The Nāga King received the instruction, prostrated, and withdrew. After the Nāga King had gone, the young man came forward, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and addressed the Buddha: 'World-Honored One! I pray you grant me the going forth and the full ordination!' The Buddha said: 'Come, bhikṣu!' And so on up to the bowl appearing in his hands, also as described above. Not long after leaving home, he diligently practiced without slacking and attained arhatship. At that time in the world there were sixty-two arhats.

"Thereupon the World-Honored One, from the Deer Park, gradually traveled and arrived at the Sāla grove, where he sat beneath a tree. Not far from the grove there was a park. At that time, thirty friends, each bringing his wife, were amusing themselves in that park. One man had no wife, so he hired a prostitute, dressed her in fine clothes, and together they went to the park. Just as they were about to indulge their desires to the fullest, the prostitute, wearing his fine clothes, suddenly ran away. They helped each other search and came to the Sāla grove. From afar they saw the World-Honored One, whose appearance was outstanding and extraordinary, like a golden mountain. Upon seeing him, a feeling of wonder arose in them. They all went to the Buddha, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and sat to one side. They asked the Buddha: 'Great śramaṇa! Have you seen a woman come this way?' The Buddha said: 'Would you rather seek yourselves, or seek after another?' They answered: 'We would rather seek ourselves than seek after a woman!' The Buddha said: 'Then sit down. I shall teach you the Dharma!' They all accepted the instruction, bowed again, and sat. The Buddha expounded various wonderful Dharma for them, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening them, and so on up to suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. The thirty men all became far from dust and freed from defilement, attained the purity of the Dharma-eye, saw the Dharma and attained the fruit. Having seen the Dharma and attained the fruit, they addressed the Buddha: 'I pray you grant us the going forth and the full ordination!' The Buddha said: 'Come, bhikṣus!' And so on up to their attaining arhatship, also as described above. At that time in the world there were ninety-two arhats.

"At that time there were further sixty men who, traveling for the purpose of a marriage, passed by the Sāla grove. From afar they saw the World-Honored One, whose appearance was outstanding and extraordinary, like a golden mountain. They all came forward to the Buddha, prostrated at the Buddha's feet. The Buddha taught them the Dharma, and so on up to their attaining arhatship, all as described above. At that time in the world there were one hundred and fifty-two arhats."

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese during the Liu Song dynasty by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle 16

I notice that the provided Chinese text for this fascicle is empty. There is no source text between the markers to translate. The fascicle heading indicates Fascicle 16 (卷第十六), though the initial reference mentions Fascicle 15 (卷第十五, described as 31 of 61).

Without the actual Chinese text content, I am unable to provide a translation. If you could supply the Chinese text for this fascicle, I would be happy to translate it in full.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Sixteen

### Beginning of the Third Division: The Method of Receiving the Precepts

"Thereupon the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: 'Each of you should go forth in separate groups to travel through the world. There are many who are virtuous and good, capable of receiving instruction. I shall now go alone to the place of Uruvilvā Kāśyapa in the優為 district to convert him.' The bhikṣus received the instruction and departed in separate groups. The World-Honored One then went to Kāśyapa's place. Kāśyapa served a poisonous nāga, which he kept in a separate, quiet chamber. No one dared enter it except Kāśyapa. The Buddha deliberately arrived at his place in the evening and asked to lodge for the night in the nāga's chamber. Kāśyapa replied: 'That would not be good at all! There is a poisonous nāga inside, and I fear it would harm you!' The Buddha said: 'Do not worry; the nāga will not harm me!' Kāśyapa replied: 'If you are not afraid, then enter and stay as you wish!' The Buddha then took grass, entered the chamber, spread a seat and sat down. He thought: 'I shall gradually transform the nāga's body, making its form like a small snake, and place it inside my bowl, thereby subduing it.' Just after he had sat down for a short while, the nāga became greatly enraged, and smoke issued from its entire body. The Buddha also emitted smoke. The nāga raised its body in flames, and the Buddha also raised his body and emitted fire. Both fires blazed intensely, and the nāga's chamber was brilliantly illuminated.

"At that time Kāśyapa and his disciples came and surrounded the nāga's chamber, lamenting sadly: 'How pitiful! The great śramaṇa did not heed my words and has been harmed by the nāga!' The next morning, the Buddha came out carrying the nāga in his bowl, and said to Kāśyapa: 'This poisonous nāga in the bowl, which everyone feared, has now been subdued!' Kāśyapa thought to himself: 'Although this great śramaṇa has spiritual powers, he is not as good as my own true path!' The World-Honored One then, by his spiritual power, in the time it takes a strong man to flex and extend his arm, took the nāga and placed it in the space between worlds, then returned to Kāśyapa's place. Kāśyapa asked the Buddha: 'Where did you put the nāga?' He replied: 'I placed it in the space between worlds.' Kāśyapa again thought: 'This great śramaṇa has extraordinary spiritual powers! In an instant he took the nāga and placed it in the space between worlds! Even so, he is still not equal to me, who have already attained the path of an arhat!'

"Kāśyapa said to the Buddha: 'I wish the great śramaṇa would stay here; I will provide offerings myself!' The Buddha said: 'If you can come yourself every day to invite me, I will accept your invitation.' He replied: 'Very well!'

"Not far from Kāśyapa's place was a dense grove, and the Buddha stayed within it. At night the Four Heavenly Kings descended to attend upon and guard him, and also wished to hear the Dharma. The radiance of the Four Heavenly Kings was like four fires blazing. Kāśyapa rose in the night and saw what appeared to be four great blazing fires beside the Buddha, but did not know what they were. The next morning, he came to invite the Buddha, saying: 'The food is prepared; please come and eat!' He also asked: 'Last night, nearby there were four luminous masses, like fire but not fire. What were they?' The Buddha said: 'Last night the Four Heavenly Kings came down to make offerings and hear the Dharma; it was their radiance.' Kāśyapa again thought: 'This great śramaṇa has extremely great spiritual power, such that the Four Heavenly Kings themselves come to make offerings! Even so, he is still not equal to me, who have already attained the path of an arhat!' The Buddha said to Kāśyapa: 'You go ahead first; I will follow after.' As soon as Kāśyapa left, the Buddha, in the time it takes to flex and extend one's arm, went to the Jambu tree, took its fruit and returned. Before Kāśyapa had arrived, the Buddha was already in his seat. Kāśyapa arrived afterward and, seeing the Buddha, asked: 'I did not take another road back, nor was there any other path, yet I did not see the great śramaṇa. By what road did the great śramaṇa come?' The Buddha said: 'Just after you left, I went to the Jambu tree, took its fruit and returned. It is fragrant, delicious, and good to eat. I now give it to you; you may try eating it!' Kāśyapa again thought: 'The great śramaṇa has great spiritual power, yet he is still not equal to me, who have already attained the path of an arhat!'

"After the Buddha had eaten, he returned to that grove. At night, Śakra Devānām Indra descended himself to attend upon and guard him, and also wished to hear the Dharma. The radiance of Śakra illuminated the entire grove, surpassing that of the Four Heavenly Kings. Kāśyapa saw it at night but again did not know what kind of light it was. The next morning he again came to invite the Buddha, saying the food was prepared, and also asked about the light. The Buddha said: 'Last night Śakra Devānām Indra came to make offerings and hear the Dharma; it was his radiance.' Kāśyapa again thought: 'This great śramaṇa is indeed powerful, such that Śakra himself comes to make offerings; yet he is still not equal to me, who have already attained the path of an arhat!' The Buddha said to Kāśyapa: 'You go ahead first; I will follow after.' As soon as Kāśyapa left, the Buddha went to a haritaki grove at the edge of Jambudvīpa, took its fruit and returned. Before Kāśyapa had arrived, the Buddha was already in his seat. Kāśyapa arrived afterward and asked as before. The Buddha said: 'Just after you left, I went to a haritaki grove at the edge of Jambudvīpa, took its fruit and returned. It is fragrant, delicious, and good to eat. I now give it to you; you may try eating it!' Kāśyapa again thought as before.

"After the Buddha had eaten, he returned to that grove. At night, Brahmā, the lord of the sahā world, descended himself to attend upon and guard him, and also wished to hear the Dharma. Brahmā's radiance surpassed that of Śakra. Kāśyapa saw it at night but again did not know what kind of light it was. The next morning he again came to invite the Buddha, saying the food was prepared, and also asked about the light. The Buddha said: 'Last night Brahmā descended to make offerings and hear the Dharma; it was his radiance.' Kāśyapa again thought: 'This great śramaṇa is indeed powerful, such that Brahmā himself comes to make offerings; yet he is still not equal to me, who have already attained the path of an arhat!' The Buddha said to Kāśyapa: 'You go ahead first; I will follow after.' As soon as Kāśyapa left, the Buddha again went to an āmalaka grove at the edge of Jambudvīpa, took its fruit and returned. The rest is as stated above.

"After the Buddha had eaten, he returned to that grove. At that time, the World-Honored One needed water for bathing and washing. The Nairañjanā River spontaneously curved its course, flowing past beside the Buddha, so that the Buddha could use it. The next morning, Kāśyapa again came to invite the Buddha, saying the food was prepared. Seeing that the river had curved its course, he asked: 'Who curved this stream?' The Buddha said: 'Yesterday I needed water, and the water curved to come of itself!' Kāśyapa again thought: 'This great śramaṇa is indeed powerful; when his mind wished for water, the water curved its flow for him. Yet he is still not equal to me, who have already attained the path of an arhat!' The Buddha said to Kāśyapa: 'You go ahead first; I will follow after.' As soon as Kāśyapa left, the Buddha went to Godānīya and obtained cow's milk from there. The rest is as stated above.

"After the Buddha had eaten, he returned to that grove. At that time, a female servant of the brahmin Sinā died, and her garments were discarded among the grave mounds. The Buddha took them back and thought: 'Where shall I wash these?' Just as this thought arose, Śakra Devānām Indra descended, pointed to the ground with his finger, and water sprang forth forming a pool. He said to the Buddha: 'You may wash them here!' The mountain spirit Abhiṣeka brought a large stone basin and also said to the Buddha: 'You may use this for washing!' The Buddha then thought: 'What shall I hold onto while washing these garments?' Not far from the pool was a kakubha tree, whose spirit bent a branch down so the Buddha could grasp it. When the Buddha finished washing the garments, he dried them in midair. The next morning, Kāśyapa again came to invite the Buddha, saying the food was prepared. Seeing the matters related to the washing of garments, he asked the Buddha about all of it. The Buddha explained everything in full, and Kāśyapa's thoughts were as before. The Buddha said to Kāśyapa: 'You go ahead first; I will follow after.' As soon as Kāśyapa left, the Buddha went to Uttarakuru and obtained naturally-growing rice. The rest is as stated above.

"After the Buddha had eaten, he returned to that grove. At that time, the next morning was Kāśyapa's festival day. He thought: 'If I do not invite the Buddha today, and the people see him, they will surely abandon me and compete to serve him!' So he refrained from inviting the Buddha. The Buddha knew this from afar, and again went to Uttarakuru, obtained food, and ate. After that day had passed, Kāśyapa again came to invite the Buddha, saying the food was prepared, and also asked: 'The Buddha did not come yesterday; where did you eat?' The Buddha said: 'Yesterday was your festival day, and you thought: "If the Buddha comes and the people all see him, they will surely abandon me and compete to serve him!" Therefore I went to Uttarakuru, obtained food, and ate.' Kāśyapa again thought: 'This great śramaṇa is indeed powerful; he can even know people's thoughts! Yet he is still not equal to me, who have already attained the path of an arhat!' Thereupon the Buddha went together with Kāśyapa to his home. After eating, the Buddha returned to that grove.

"At that time, Kāśyapa's five hundred disciples were all splitting firewood together, but their axes would not lift. They reported this to their teacher. The teacher said: 'I fear this is the doing of the great śramaṇa. Go and ask him!' They went and asked the Buddha. The Buddha asked: 'Do you want the axes to lift?' They replied: 'We do!' The Buddha said: 'Go back; the axes will lift by themselves!' When the axes lifted, they then would not come down. They again reported to their teacher, and the teacher told them to ask the Buddha. The Buddha asked: 'Do you want them to come down?' They replied: 'Yes!' The Buddha said: 'Go back; the axes will come down by themselves!' When they came down, the axes all stuck into the firewood and could not be lifted again. They again reported to their teacher, and the teacher told them to ask the Buddha.
