The Buddha asked: 'Do you want them to lift?' They replied: 'Yes!' The Buddha said: 'Go back; they will lift by themselves and you can use them!' And they were all able to use them. Then they wanted to light a fire, but the fire would not light. They again reported to their teacher, and the teacher told them to ask the Buddha. The Buddha asked: 'Do you want the fire to light?' They replied: 'Yes!' The Buddha said: 'Go back; the fire will light by itself!' The fire then lit by itself. Once lit, it would not go out. They again reported to their teacher, and the teacher told them to ask the Buddha. The Buddha asked: 'Do you want it to go out?' They replied: 'Yes!' The Buddha said: 'Go back; the fire will go out by itself!' The fire then went out by itself. Then they wanted to pour water to extinguish the charcoal, but the water stayed in the pitcher and absolutely would not come out. They again reported to their teacher, and the teacher told them to ask the Buddha. The Buddha said: 'Do you want the water to come out?' They replied: 'Yes!' The Buddha said: 'Go back; the water will come out by itself!' The water then came out by itself. Once out, it would not stop. They again reported to their teacher, and the teacher told them to ask the Buddha. The Buddha said: 'Do you want it to stop?' They replied: 'Yes!' The Buddha said: 'Go back; the water will stop by itself!' The water then stopped by itself.

"At that time, dark clouds brought great rain for seven days. The grove where the Buddha stayed and Kāśyapa's home were all flooded into one body of water. Kāśyapa, fearing that the Buddha had been swept away by the water, came by boat to look. He then saw the World-Honored One walking in meditation on the surface of the Nairañjanā River's waters. Kāśyapa again thought: 'This great śramaṇa is indeed powerful! Though the waters are flooding and surging, he is not swept away or submerged, but is instead walking in meditation on top of them. Yet he is still not equal to me, who have already attained the path of an arhat!'

"Thereupon the World-Honored One flew up into the sky and told Kāśyapa: 'You are not an arhat! Why do you falsely claim to have attained the path?' Kāśyapa said: 'It is truly so, World-Honored One! It is truly so, World-Honored One!' He further said to the Buddha: 'I wish to go forth and receive the complete precepts under the great śramaṇa!' The Buddha said: 'Have you informed your disciples yet?' He replied: 'Not yet.' The Buddha said: 'You should first inform them.' Kāśyapa accepted the instruction and returned to tell his disciples: 'Do you know? I wish to cultivate the pure brahma-conduct under the great śramaṇa. If you wish to follow me, that is good; if not, you are free to do as you please!' The five hundred disciples said in unison: 'We saw the Buddha subdue the nāga and already gave rise to faith. We were only waiting for our teacher! We all wish to follow!' Thereupon the teacher and disciples together went to the Buddha's place and said to the Buddha: 'We, teacher and disciples, all wish to go forth and receive the complete precepts!' The Buddha said: 'Welcome, bhikṣus! Receive the complete precepts. In my well-proclaimed Dharma and Vinaya, you will be able to exhaust all suffering and cultivate the pure brahma-conduct!' The hair of Kāśyapa and his five hundred disciples fell away of its own accord, kāṣāya robes appeared on their bodies, and alms-bowls were in their hands. Having received the precepts, they took their former garments and fire-worship implements and cast them all into the Nairañjanā River. This was the ordination of Kāśyapa and his five hundred disciples receiving the complete precepts.

"Kāśyapa had two younger brothers: the elder was named Nadī Kāśyapa, and the younger was named Gayā Kāśyapa. The elder brother had three hundred disciples, and the younger brother had two hundred disciples. They lived one yojana downstream from their older brother. When they saw their brother's fire-worship implements floating down with the current, they feared their brother had been harmed by evil people or swept away by the great flood. The two brothers immediately took their five hundred disciples and went upstream against the current. They saw their brother and his followers had all become śramaṇas and asked in astonishment: 'Why is this so?' He replied: 'This path is supreme; there is no greater way of liberation!' The two brothers and their five hundred disciples all discussed together: 'Our elder brother is foremost in wisdom, and now he takes delight in this. This path must be supreme! We should all join our brother in going forth.' They then went together to the Buddha, bowed their heads to the Buddha's feet, and said to the Buddha: 'We wish to be given the going-forth and receive the complete precepts!' The Buddha said: 'Welcome, bhikṣus!' Up through the point of having alms-bowls in their hands, it was as stated above.

"Thereupon the World-Honored One thought: 'Where is there an abundance of food, drink, and bedding, where I can instruct these one thousand bhikṣus who were formerly brahmins?' He thought: 'At Mount Gayā there is an abundance of food, drink, and bedding!' Having thought this, he took the thousand bhikṣus and went to that place. He instructed them with three things: first, instruction through supernatural powers; second, instruction through teaching the Dharma; third, instruction through commands. What is instruction through supernatural powers? It is as explained in the section on supernatural penetrations. What is instruction through teaching the Dharma? Saying: 'Bhikṣus! You should contemplate this; you should not contemplate that! You should be mindful of this; you should not be mindful of that! You should cultivate this; you should cut off that; you should practice in accordance with this!' What is instruction through commands? Saying: 'Bhikṣus! All is ablaze! How is all ablaze? The eye is ablaze, forms are ablaze. Eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and feelings born from eye-contact conditions are also ablaze. With what are they ablaze? They are ablaze with the fire of desire, ablaze with the fire of anger and the fire of delusion. Up through the mind and mental objects, it is likewise. When a noble disciple hears this Dharma, revulsion arises, there is no defilement or attachment, and liberation is attained! The knowledge of liberation arises: what was to be done has been done, the brahma-conduct has been established, and there is no further becoming!' When this Dharma was spoken, the thousand bhikṣus exhausted their defilements, and their minds attained liberation!

"At that time, the World-Honored One thought: 'Long ago I made a promise with King Bimbisāra that when I attained the path I would convert him. I should now go to him!' He then, surrounded before and after by the thousand bhikṣus, gradually traveled toward Rājagṛha. King Bimbisāra heard that the Buddha had attained the path, had converted the three Kāśyapa brothers of Uruvilvā and their one thousand disciples, and was now coming to his city. He immediately commanded the forty-two thousand villages in his kingdom, with each village sending two distinguished persons, to go forth together to welcome the Buddha. Eighty-four thousand people, riding elephants, horses, and chariots, accompanied and led the procession. At that time, it was the end of spring, and the heat was already extremely intense. The people all thought: 'If only we could have some shade!' At that time, Śakra Devānām Indra knew their thoughts and conjured a cloud canopy, with a cool breeze gently arising. He transformed himself into a Brahmā god, wearing yellow garments, holding a seven-jeweled staff and a seven-jeweled handled whisk, hovering one cubit above the ground, leading the way before the Buddha. When the people of Magadha tried to go before the Buddha, Śakra drove them away. Everyone was displeased with him and spoke this verse:

> 'In the form of a Brahmā god, holding a staff and treading on air,
> Speaking soft and gentle words: whose attendant is he?'

"At that time, Śakra Devānām Indra answered in verse:

> 'Freed from all bonds, the supreme tamer of men,
> The Worthy One, the Well-Gone One: I am his attendant.'

"At that time, King Bimbisāra thought: 'Wherever the Buddha stops for the night, I shall give that very place to the Buddha and establish a monastery there.' The Buddha knew his intention and stayed the night at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, the great assembly all harbored doubts, thinking: 'We do not know whether the Buddha or Uruvilvā Kāśyapa is the disciple.' The Buddha knew the assembly's thoughts and addressed Uruvilvā Kāśyapa in verse:

> 'Uruvilvā, what did you see
> that you abandoned the practice of fire worship?
> I now ask you personally:
> you may answer truthfully.'

"Uruvilvā Kāśyapa answered in verse:

> 'Ever craving fine flavors,
> with mind racing among sounds and forms,
> I saw that these are defilements,
> and therefore abandoned the practice of fire worship.'

"At that time, the great assembly, although they heard the Buddha and Kāśyapa each speak a verse, had not yet understood the meaning and still harbored doubts. The Buddha knew the assembly's minds and again asked in verse:

> 'The five flavors please the mouth,
> sounds and forms delight the heart.
> You see these as defilements:
> where did you attain their absence?'

"Uruvilvā Kāśyapa again answered in verse:

> 'I saw the path of cessation,
> where all is without attachment,
> neither different nor divisible:
> in this I abandoned the fire sacrifice.'

"At that time, the great assembly, although they heard the verses again, still harbored doubts and uncertainty. The Buddha knew their minds and told Kāśyapa: 'Rise and fan the Buddha!' He received the instruction, rose, and fanned. The Buddha further told Kāśyapa: 'Display your supernatural transformations!' He then manifested various kinds of miraculous transformations: dividing his body into a hundred million forms, then merging them back into one; passing through stone walls; entering the earth as if it were water; walking on water as if it were ground; sitting and lying in the air like a bird in flight; his entire body blazing brilliantly, with smoke rising like clouds; touching the sun and moon with his hands; standing upright to reach the Brahmā heaven, moving freely without obstruction. Or water issued from the upper part of his body while fire blazed from the lower part; or fire blazed from the upper part while water issued from the lower part. Then he descended, bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, circumambulated three times to the right, knelt with palms joined, and said to the Buddha: 'The World-Honored One is my teacher; I am the World-Honored One's disciple!' Having said this three times, he told the great assembly: 'All that I know, down to these supernatural transformations, is entirely due to the grace of my great teacher!' Thereupon the great assembly finally knew that Kāśyapa was the Buddha's disciple, and toward the Buddha they were immeasurably joyful and reverential. It is the constant way of all Buddhas that they do not teach the Dharma until people's minds have been turned. The Buddha, knowing that the great assembly was already joyful and reverential, taught them various wonderful Dharma, instructing, demonstrating, benefiting, and delighting them, and also taught the Dharma that the Buddha always teaches: suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. King Bimbisāra and the eighty-four thousand people, right there in their seats, became free from dust and defilement, and attained the purity of the Dharma eye. Having seen the Dharma and attained its fruit, they took the three refuges and received the five precepts. Thereupon King Bimbisāra bowed his head and invited the Buddha and the Sangha for the midday meal the next day. The Buddha silently accepted. Joyfully the king returned to his palace, ordered the preparation of various fine foods, and the next morning had seats arranged in the Bamboo Grove. He came himself and announced: 'The food is prepared!' The Buddha and the great assembly sat down in order. The king personally served and poured, joyful and untiring. After the meal, water was brought. Standing to one side, the king said to the Buddha: 'I now offer this Bamboo Grove to the World-Honored One!' The Buddha said: 'You may give it to the Sangha; the merit will be even greater!' The king further said to the Buddha: 'I beg you to accept it!' The Buddha said: 'Just give it to the Sangha; I am within the Sangha!' The king then accepted the instruction and donated it to the Sangha of the four directions. Then he took a small seat and sat before the Buddha. The Buddha spoke verses of rejoicing and blessing, as he had spoken for Verañjā. Having spoken, he further taught various wonderful Dharma, and then sent the king back to his residence. The king rose from his seat, bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, circumambulated three times to the right, and withdrew.

"At that time, the World-Honored One was at the Bamboo Grove monastery in Rājagṛha. There was a village called Nālandā, and there was a former brahmin named Sañjaya, who had two hundred and fifty students under his tutelage. Among his disciples, there were two who were foremost: one named Upatissa and the other named Kolita. At that time, Aśvajit, wearing his robe and carrying his bowl, entered the city to beg for food. His countenance was peaceful and pleasant, his faculties were serene and composed, his robes were neat and proper, and he walked with his eyes cast downward. At that time, Upatissa was out walking, and from afar saw Aśvajit's dignified and composed deportment. He exclaimed that it was unprecedented, and when Aśvajit drew near, asked: 'What manner of practice is this, that your garments are different from the usual? Do you have a teacher and lineage that I may hear of?' Aśvajit replied: 'The śramaṇa Gautama is my great teacher. He is the one we revere, and from him we receive instruction.' Upatissa said: 'What Dharma does your great teacher teach?' Aśvajit said: 'I am young and my studies are still shallow. How could I expound the vast and profound meaning of my teacher? I shall now briefly state the essentials for you. My teacher teaches: Dharmas arise from conditions, and also cease from conditions. All dharmas are empty and without a master.' When Upatissa heard this, his mind was awakened and his understanding opened; he attained the purity of the Dharma eye. He then returned to his dwelling and told Kolita the Dharma he had heard. When Kolita heard it, he too was freed from dust and defilement and attained the purity of the Dharma eye. He immediately asked: 'Where is the Tathāgata traveling and dwelling now?' Upatissa replied: 'He is now at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.' Kolita said: 'The Tathāgata is our teacher. We should go together to pay respects and greet him!' Upatissa said: 'Our teacher, on his deathbed, entrusted us with the two hundred and fifty disciples to guide to accomplishment. How could we not inform them and just leave on our own?' The two then went to their disciples and said: 'We wish to cultivate the pure brahma-conduct under the śramaṇa Gautama. Each of you may follow your own inclination.' At that time, all two hundred and fifty disciples were happy to follow. The two then led their disciples together to the Bamboo Grove. The World-Honored One saw them from afar and told the bhikṣus: 'Those two coming are named Upatissa and Kolita. These two shall be the foremost among my disciples: with immeasurable wisdom and foremost in supernatural powers.' In a short while they arrived. The Buddha progressively taught them the Dharma: the discourse on generosity, the discourse on maintaining the precepts, and the discourse on being reborn in heaven. He reproved desire as impure and praised renunciation. Right there in their seats, their defilements were exhausted and their minds were liberated. They all came forward and said to the Buddha: 'We wish to go forth and cultivate the pure brahma-conduct!' The Buddha said: 'Welcome, bhikṣus! In my Dharma, cultivate the brahma-conduct and exhaust the source of suffering!' They were thereby named as having gone forth and received the complete precepts."

At that time, the World-Honored One was traveling in Rājagṛha. The three Kāśyapa brothers of Uruvilvā and their one thousand disciples, Śāriputra, Maudgalyāyana and their two hundred and fifty disciples had all gone forth and studied the path. The prominent, noble clansmen, elders, and householders of Rājagṛha also all went forth. A great assembly surrounded him, gathered in that kingdom, and he taught the Dharma for them.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, the World-Honored One had not yet instructed the bhikṣus to have preceptors or ācāryas. Because there were no preceptors or ācāryas, their deportment was undisciplined. The wearing of their upper and lower robes was all improper. They did not know what was pure and what was impure. They did not keep mindfulness before them and did not properly guard their sense-faculties. When entering villages to beg for food, they received impure food. They took food with their own hands, not receiving it from others. When someone handed food to them, they snatched and picked from the person's hand. They grasped the rim of the bowl rather than properly holding it up to receive. While eating, they talked loudly and in confusion. Those who had no faith in or fondness for the Buddha's Dharma criticized and reproached them, saying: "These śramaṇas are worse than the non-Buddhist practitioners! They have no deportment, even talking loudly and in confusion while eating! They have no conduct of śramaṇas! They destroy the dharma of śramaṇas! Wherever they have not yet passed through, those places have good fortune."

Furthermore, there was a sick bhikṣu whom no one tended, and because of this he died. The elder bhikṣus heard of it and criticized in various ways. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha gathered the bhikṣu Sangha on this account and asked the bhikṣus: "Is it truly so?" They replied: "It is truly so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha criticized in various ways, saying: "How can you let your minds be scattered, with conduct, posture, sitting, and lying all improper?" After the criticism, he told the bhikṣus: "If one improperly wears the upper and lower robes, up through talking loudly and in confusion while eating, all are duṣkṛta offenses. From now on, for the sake of ten benefits, I allow the bhikṣus to have preceptors!"

A preceptor naturally gives rise to loving care for his disciple as for a son. A disciple naturally gives rise to reverent respect for his preceptor as for a father. Through diligent mutual instruction and admonition, and mutual respect and deference, the Buddha-Dharma can be increased, expanded, and caused to endure long.

The method of requesting a preceptor: One should bare the right shoulder, remove one's leather sandals, kneel, and with both hands support the preceptor's feet, speaking these words: "I, so-and-so, now request the venerable one to be my preceptor. May the venerable one be my preceptor. I wish the venerable one to be my preceptor. By depending on the venerable one as preceptor, I may receive the complete precepts." One requests in this manner three times. The preceptor should reply: "Very well! I shall instruct and admonish you. Do not be heedless!"

A disciple should serve and attend to his preceptor. If he enters a village without informing his preceptor, it is a duṣkṛta. If he wishes to go with another bhikṣu, he should also inform his preceptor. If he does not inform, or if he is not given permission and goes anyway, all are duṣkṛta offenses. If another bhikṣu invites him to go together, it is likewise. If he wishes to receive robes, bowls, leather sandals, and such from another bhikṣu, he should also inform his preceptor. If he does not inform, or if he informs but is not given permission and takes them, all are duṣkṛta offenses. If he wishes to give robes and bowls to another bhikṣu, it is likewise. If another bhikṣu wishes to carry his robes and bowls for him or to take things for him, he should also inform his preceptor. If he does not inform, or if he informs but is not given permission and simply does it, all are duṣkṛta offenses. If another bhikṣu requests him to carry robes and bowls or has him take things, it is likewise. For anything that is to be done, up to shaving the head or shaving someone else's head, he should inform his preceptor. The only exceptions are urinating, defecating, and using a tooth-cleaning stick.

If the preceptor commits a serious offense, the disciple should diligently make efforts to have it quickly eliminated. If he does not make efforts, it is a duṣkṛta. If the Sangha should impose parivāsa on the preceptor, or mānāpya, or return to the original date, or perform the abhivāhanā, the disciple should diligently make efforts and request the Sangha to quickly impose the parivāsa and so forth up to abhivāhanā. If he does not diligently act, it is a duṣkṛta. On the day the preceptor's offense is to be cleared, the disciple should sweep and sprinkle, arrange seats, prepare voting sticks, assemble the Sangha, and find a bhikṣu to perform the karman. If he does not do so, it is a duṣkṛta. If the Sangha is to perform a censure-karman, an expulsion-karman, a dependence-karman, a suspension-karman, or a submission-karman against the preceptor, the disciple should diligently request the Sangha not to do so. If he does not request the Sangha, it is a duṣkṛta. If the Sangha must definitely perform these karmans, the disciple should request that they not violate proper procedure. If he does not request, it is a duṣkṛta. If the preceptor is ill, the disciple should attend to him at his side. If the preceptor has possessions, the disciple should, with his knowledge, exchange them for food appropriate for the illness and medicine appropriate for the illness. If the preceptor has no possessions but the disciple has his own, he should exchange them on the preceptor's behalf. If the disciple also has none, he should seek them. Furthermore, morning and evening the disciple should teach the Dharma to the sick preceptor. Until the preceptor's illness has recovered, the disciple should not travel. If he does not act accordingly, it is a duṣkṛta.

If a disciple commits a serious offense, up through not recovering from illness, the preceptor's care and attention should also be likewise.

At that time, some bhikṣus conferred the precepts with one phrase, saying: "You take refuge in the Buddha!" Other bhikṣus conferred the precepts with two phrases, saying: "You take refuge in the Buddha! You take refuge in the Dharma!" Still other bhikṣus conferred the precepts with three phrases, saying: "You take refuge in the Buddha! You take refuge in the Dharma! You take refuge in the Sangha!" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not confer the precepts with one phrase, two phrases, or three phrases!"

Furthermore, some bhikṣus conferred the precepts using the "Welcome, bhikṣu" method. The elder bhikṣus criticized: "How can you confer the precepts using the 'Welcome, bhikṣu' method like the Buddha?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not confer the precepts using the 'Welcome, bhikṣu' method!"

At that time, the bhikṣus thought: "Only the Buddha confers precepts on bhikṣus. Can we also do so? If we can, how should we confer them?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now permit you to confer the precepts on bhikṣus. You should confer them using the announcement-followed-by-three-proclamation karman."

The one who wishes to receive the precepts should bare the right shoulder, remove his leather sandals, bow to the Sangha, place his right knee on the ground, and make this statement: "May the venerable Sangha hear me! I, so-and-so, seek to receive the complete precepts from the preceptor so-and-so. I now request the Sangha to grant me the complete precepts. May the Sangha ferry me across. Out of compassion!" He states this three times.

Among the assembly, a bhikṣu who knows the procedures, whether the senior monk or one equal to the senior monk, should announce to the Sangha: "May the venerable Sangha hear me! This person so-and-so wishes to receive the complete precepts, with so-and-so as preceptor. The Sangha now confers the complete precepts on so-and-so, with so-and-so as preceptor. If the time is right and the Sangha approves, let it be so. This is the announcement."

"May the venerable Sangha hear me! This person so-and-so wishes to receive the complete precepts, with so-and-so as preceptor. The Sangha now confers the complete precepts on so-and-so, with so-and-so as preceptor. Whichever elders approve, let them remain silent. Those who do not approve, speak." The second and third proclamations are likewise.

"The Sangha has approved the conferral of the complete precepts on so-and-so, with so-and-so as preceptor. The Sangha approves, by its silence. Let this matter be so held."

At that time, bhikṣus used as few as four and up to nine persons to confer the complete precepts on one or even many persons. The elder bhikṣus criticized this, and it was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit an assembly of ten to confer the complete precepts." The bhikṣus then counted non-humans, laypeople, expelled persons, suspended persons, self-proclaimed offenders, those of different views, mad persons, persons of confused mind, persons with minds impaired by illness, bhikṣuṇīs, śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs to make up the ten required for conferring the complete precepts. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should be ten properly qualified bhikṣus who confer the complete precepts."

The bhikṣus conferred the complete precepts on sleeping persons, intoxicated persons, mad persons, persons of confused mind, persons with minds impaired by illness, and persons of heterodox views. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not confer the complete precepts on sleeping persons, up through persons of heterodox views! It should be ten properly qualified bhikṣus who confer the complete precepts on a properly qualified person."

The bhikṣus used sleeping persons, intoxicated persons, mad persons, persons of confused mind, and persons with minds impaired by illness as preceptors. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not use such persons as preceptors!"

The bhikṣus further used two persons, up to ten persons, as preceptors. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One person should serve as preceptor; one should not use two up to ten persons!"

There were persons who wished to receive the complete precepts but could not assemble ten properly qualified bhikṣus. They thought: "If the Buddha would permit us to receive the complete precepts on the occasion of the poṣadha, the occasion of the pravāraṇā, or when the Sangha has already assembled of its own accord, we would not have such difficulty!" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit the complete precepts to be received on the occasion of the poṣadha, the occasion of the pravāraṇā, or when the Sangha has already assembled of its own accord."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus, being in disharmony with the preceptor or ācārya of the ordination candidate, created obstacles for the candidate. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the ordination candidate has no actual impediment, one should not create obstacles for him. If one creates obstacles, it is a duṣkṛta."

Furthermore, some bhikṣus, on account of trivial or insignificant matters, forcibly created obstacles for the ordination candidate: saying he seemed blind, or saying he seemed lame; seeing that he was short, saying he had not yet reached twenty years of age; or saying his parents seemed not to have consented to his going forth. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not create obstacles for the ordination candidate on account of trivial or insignificant matters. If one creates obstacles, it is a duṣkṛta."

The bhikṣus still continued to create obstacles. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If it accords with the wishes of the preceptor and ācārya, the precepts should be conferred."

Furthermore, some bhikṣus performed separate-assembly ordinations within the boundary. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should go outside the boundary and, using the announcement-followed-by-one-proclamation karman, establish a smaller boundary for the ordination."

First, one bhikṣu should announce the boundary markers on the four sides. Then one bhikṣu announces: "May the venerable Sangha hear me! According to the boundary markers announced by bhikṣu so-and-so, the Sangha now establishes an ordination platform for shared dwelling, shared poṣadha, and shared receipt of offerings. If the time is right and the Sangha approves, let it be so. This is the announcement."

"May the venerable Sangha hear me! According to the boundary markers announced by bhikṣu so-and-so, the Sangha now establishes an ordination platform for shared dwelling, shared poṣadha, and shared receipt of offerings. Whichever elders approve, let them remain silent. Those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has established the ordination platform according to the boundary markers announced by bhikṣu so-and-so, for shared dwelling, shared poṣadha, and shared receipt of offerings. The Sangha approves, by its silence. Let this matter be so held."

The bhikṣus, having established the ordination platform, departed without dissolving it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should dissolve the boundary using the announcement-followed-by-one-proclamation karman before departing."

One bhikṣu announces: "May the venerable Sangha hear me! This established boundary: the Sangha now dissolves this boundary. If the time is right and the Sangha approves, let it be so. This is the announcement."

"May the venerable Sangha hear me! This established boundary: the Sangha now dissolves this boundary. Whichever elders approve, let them remain silent. Those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has dissolved this boundary. The Sangha approves, by its silence. Let this matter be so held."

At that time, the bhikṣus brought the ordination candidate to the ordination site and said to the senior monk: "Please perform the karman." He replied: "I have not memorized the karman." Down to even the most junior monk, it was the same, and the ordination could not be performed. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All should memorize the karman. If after ten rains one still has not memorized it, it is a duṣkṛta."

The bhikṣus brought two ordination candidates to the ordination site and wished to confer the precepts. The two candidates disputed over who should go first, and the ordination could not be performed. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Whoever arrives first at the ordination site should receive the precepts first. If two persons arrive at the same time, the one who is older in age should receive them first. If they are the same age, the one whose preceptor is more senior should receive them first. If the preceptors are also the same, the karman should be performed simultaneously, with the one whose name is called first receiving first. For three persons, it is likewise."

Upāli asked the Buddha: "Can one perform the karman for three persons in other matters as well?" The Buddha said: "Yes." He further asked: "Can one perform the karman for four persons?" The Buddha said: "Under no circumstances may the karman be performed for four persons!"

The bhikṣus brought ordination candidates to the ordination site and wished to confer the precepts, but encountered bandits who stripped them, and they returned nearly dead. The bhikṣus thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us to establish an ordination platform within the monastery grounds, we would not suffer such calamities." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now permit the establishment of an ordination platform within the monastery grounds using the announcement-followed-by-one-proclamation karman. First, the monastery boundary should be dissolved using the announcement-followed-by-one-proclamation karman."

A bhikṣu who knows the procedures announces: "May the venerable Sangha hear me! This single dwelling place where the Sangha shares dwelling, shares poṣadha, and shares receipt of offerings: the boundary previously established here is now dissolved. If the time is right and the Sangha approves, let it be so. This is the announcement."

"May the venerable Sangha hear me! This single dwelling place where the Sangha shares dwelling, shares poṣadha, and shares receipt of offerings: the boundary previously established here is now dissolved. Whichever elders approve, let them remain silent. Those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has dissolved the previously established boundary. The Sangha approves, by its silence. Let this matter be so held."

After the monastery boundary has been dissolved, one then establishes the ordination platform. One bhikṣu should announce the four boundary markers of the ordination platform, and another bhikṣu should perform the announcement-followed-by-one-proclamation karman, as stated above. After the ordination platform has been established, one then re-establishes the monastery boundary.

One bhikṣu should announce the four boundary markers, and also announce the excluded inner area. Another bhikṣu announces: "May the venerable Sangha hear me! Bhikṣu so-and-so has announced the four boundary markers and the excluded inner area. The Sangha now establishes the greater boundary for shared dwelling, shared poṣadha, and shared receipt of offerings. If the time is right and the Sangha approves, let it be so. This is the announcement."

"May the venerable Sangha hear me! Bhikṣu so-and-so has announced the four boundary markers and the excluded inner area. The Sangha now establishes the greater boundary for shared dwelling, shared poṣadha, and shared receipt of offerings. Whichever elders approve, let them remain silent. Those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has established the greater boundary of the Sangha according to the four boundary markers and the excluded inner area announced by bhikṣu so-and-so, for shared dwelling, shared poṣadha, and shared receipt of offerings. The Sangha approves, by its silence. Let this matter be so held."

At that time, there was a non-Buddhist wanderer, a young brahmin, who wished to go forth and receive the complete precepts in the true Dharma. He went to Śāriputra and said: "Please grant me the going-forth and the complete precepts." Śāriputra would not accept him. He then went to all five hundred bhikṣus in the same manner, and none would accept him. He went away weeping and returned home. The Buddha, observing with his divine eye, asked Śāriputra: "Why did that young brahmin go away weeping?" Śāriputra explained the matter in full. The Buddha further asked: "Has this person ever spoken even one kind word to the bhikṣus?" He replied: "Yes." The Buddha asked: "What kind word?" He replied: "Once when I was begging for food, this person praised me, saying: 'This son of the Śākya śramaṇas is virtuous and good. He should be given food.'" The Buddha said: "This kindness should be repaid. You should ordain him." Śāriputra accepted the instruction and immediately conferred the complete precepts on him.

Furthermore, there was another non-Buddhist wanderer, a young brahmin, who, being of meager merit, could not obtain food by begging. He thought: "The śramaṇa sons of the Śākyas easily obtain food by begging, and people are happy to give medicine to the sick and emaciated among them. I should now go forth and receive the complete precepts among them!" Having thought this, he went to the monastery and said to the bhikṣus: "Please grant me the going-forth and confer the complete precepts." The bhikṣus immediately conferred the complete precepts on him. Because of his meager merit, when it came his turn for the Sangha's invited meals, the invitations ceased. The bhikṣus told him: "You should put on your robe, take your bowl, and go beg for food!" He replied: "Venerable ones! It was precisely because I feared begging for food that I went forth in the Buddha's Dharma. How can you now tell me to beg for food?" The elder bhikṣus criticized: "How could you ordain someone who cannot beg for food?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not ordain such a person; if one does, it is a duṣkṛta. When ordaining someone, one should first ask: 'Why do you wish to go forth?' If he says it is for the sake of food and drink, one should not ordain him. If he says it is to seek the good Dharma and because he is weary of birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, suffering, and affliction, then he should be ordained. When conferring the complete precepts, one should first explain the four reliances: 'Reliance on rag-robes, reliance on begged food, reliance on sitting beneath trees, reliance on discarded medicine. Can you rely on these four things for the rest of your life?' If he says he can, one should confer the precepts. If he says he cannot, one should not confer them."

There was a great elder, a brahmin, who was weary and disenchanted with the world. He thought: "The śramaṇa sons of the Śākyas practice the true Dharma and broadly cultivate the brahma-conduct. By going forth among them, one can reach the end of suffering." Having thought this, he went to the monastery and sought to go forth and receive the complete precepts. The bhikṣus said: "The Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One has established the four reliances. If you can rely on them for the rest of your life, we will grant you the going-forth and confer the complete precepts." The brahmin said: "What are the four reliances?" The bhikṣus explained them. The brahmin said: "These four reliances are despised and disdained by the world. I cannot rely on them!" He further said: "If the venerable ones would first confer the complete precepts on me and explain them afterward, then, having no choice, I might be able to practice them!" Thereupon he returned home. The bhikṣus thought: "If the Buddha would permit us to explain the four reliances after conferring the complete precepts, this person would not have turned away from the Buddha's Dharma." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit the four reliances to be explained after the complete precepts have been conferred."

At that time, the bhikṣus who had conferred the complete precepts walked ahead and returned home first. The newly ordained person was behind them and encountered a woman with whom he had formerly had illicit relations. The woman said: "Could you not make a living, and so you entered the path?" He replied: "I was weary of birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, suffering, and affliction, and wished to exhaust the source of suffering. Therefore, in this Dharma, I practice the true path and broadly cultivate the brahma-conduct." The woman further said: "If what you say is true, we shall have no chance for intimacy. Come now, let us engage in one last act of desire!" He then engaged in it with her, and returned only at dusk. The bhikṣus asked: "Why did you fall behind?" He answered truthfully. The bhikṣus then expelled him, saying: "Get out! Be gone! In the dharma of bhikṣus, if one does such a thing, one is no śramaṇa and no son of the Śākya clan!" When that bhikṣu heard this, he fainted and fell to the ground, saying: "If you had told me at the time of ordination, even at the cost of my life, how could I have committed this!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "After the complete precepts have been conferred, one should immediately explain the twelve matters, the four pārājika offenses, the four analogies, and the four reliances."

At that time, the Buddha had not yet permitted the bhikṣus to have ācāryas. When bhikṣus' preceptors passed away, because they had neither preceptors nor ācāryas, their wearing of upper and lower robes was improper, up through talking in confusion while eating, all as stated above. The elder bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "From now on, for the sake of ten benefits, I permit the bhikṣus to have ācāryas."

An ācārya naturally gives rise to caring for his disciple as for a son. A disciple naturally gives rise to regarding his ācārya as a father. In all matters, it is as stated in the section on preceptors.

Since the Buddha permitted ācāryas, but they did not know how many kinds of ācāryas there were, this was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "There are five kinds of ācāryas: the going-forth ācārya, the instructing ācārya, the karman ācārya, the sūtra-receiving ācārya, and the dependence ācārya."

The bhikṣus did not know what constituted the going-forth ācārya through to the dependence ācārya. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The one who first ordains and confers the śrāmaṇera precepts is called the going-forth ācārya. The one who teaches deportment and procedures at the time of receiving the complete precepts is called the instructing ācārya. The one who performs the karman at the time of receiving the complete precepts is called the karman ācārya. The one from whom one receives sūtras, even for one day's recitation, is called the sūtra-receiving ācārya. The one upon whom one depends for residence, even for one night, is called the dependence ācārya."

Since the Buddha permitted the dependence ācārya, some then took dependence on bhikṣuṇīs, śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, śrāmaṇerīs, mad persons, confused persons, persons with minds impaired by illness, suspended persons, expelled persons, persons dwelling elsewhere, persons in parivāsa, persons undergoing mānāpya, persons returning to the original date, persons who should have their offenses cleared, self-proclaimed offenders, persons under multiple-person censure, and persons under various karmans. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to take dependence on such persons as listed above! One should only take dependence on a properly qualified bhikṣu."

Herein, there are valid requests for dependence and invalid requests for dependence; there are valid grants of dependence and invalid grants of dependence; there are valid receptions of dependence and invalid receptions of dependence.

Invalid request for dependence: If a bhikṣu requests dependence from a bhikṣuṇī, or from a śikṣamāṇā, a śrāmaṇera, a śrāmaṇerī, up through persons under various karmans, this is called an invalid request for dependence. If one requests dependence from a properly qualified bhikṣu but does not say these words: "I, so-and-so, now request the venerable one for dependence. May the venerable one be my dependence. I shall dwell depending on the venerable one. May the venerable one instruct and admonish me; I shall receive the venerable one's instruction and admonition," this is also an invalid request for dependence.

Valid request for dependence: Requesting from a properly qualified bhikṣu using the above words is called a valid request for dependence.

Invalid grant of dependence: If a bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, śrāmaṇerī, up through persons under various karmans, grants dependence to a bhikṣu, this is called an invalid grant of dependence. If, after a proper request has been made to a properly qualified bhikṣu, that bhikṣu does not say: "Do not be heedless!" this is also an invalid grant of dependence. This is called an invalid grant of dependence.

Valid grant of dependence: After a proper request has been made to a properly qualified bhikṣu, and that bhikṣu says: "Do not be heedless!" this is called a valid grant of dependence.

Invalid reception of dependence: If a bhikṣu receives dependence from a bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, śrāmaṇerī, up through persons under various karmans, none of these are called reception of dependence. If, before a properly qualified bhikṣu, one does not say the above words: "I, so-and-so, now request the venerable one for dependence, up through: I shall receive the venerable one's instruction and admonition," this also does not constitute valid reception of dependence. This is called an invalid reception of dependence.

Valid reception of dependence: Before a properly qualified bhikṣu, saying the above words: "I, so-and-so, now request the venerable one for dependence, up through: I shall receive the venerable one's instruction and admonition," this is called a valid reception of dependence.

At that time, some bhikṣus received dependence from behind walls or obstructions, or disrespectfully received dependence with head covered, shoulders covered, wearing leather sandals, or while sitting or lying down. The elder bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should bare the right shoulder, remove one's leather sandals, kneel with palms joined, and face-to-face say the above words: 'I, so-and-so, now request the venerable one for dependence, up through: I shall receive the venerable one's instruction and admonition.'"

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus did not respect their preceptors and ācāryas, and did not respect the precepts. Other bhikṣus also imitated them. The elder bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha asked the group-of-six and the other bhikṣus: "Is this truly so?" They replied: "It is truly so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha criticized them in various ways: "You are foolish! How can you not respect your teachers and not respect the precepts?" After the criticism, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, if any bhikṣu does not respect his preceptor or ācārya, or does not respect the precepts, it is a duṣkṛta."

Some bhikṣus still remained disrespectful. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The method of not speaking with them should be applied!"

The bhikṣus then imposed the non-speaking penalty for the entire lifespan, and also refused to see the offender, or drove him from his dwelling. They also imposed the non-speaking penalty on foolish bhikṣus and bhikṣus without fault, and imposed the non-speaking penalty without informing the person of his offense. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not impose the lifelong non-speaking penalty on those who do not respect their preceptors and ācāryas. One should not impose the non-speaking penalty on foolish persons or those without fault. Nor should one impose the non-speaking penalty without informing the person of his offense."

There are five kinds of non-speaking: first, saying: "Do not speak with me!" Second: "Whatever you do, do not report it to me!" Third: "Do not enter my room!" Fourth: "Do not handle my robe and bowl or assist me in various tasks!" Fifth: "Do not come to see me!"

Some bhikṣus imposed the non-speaking penalty for trivial matters. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not impose the non-speaking penalty for trivial matters. If a disciple possesses five qualities, the teacher should impose the non-speaking penalty: being without shame before the teacher, being without embarrassment before the teacher, being without respect, being without affection, and not making offerings. These are the five qualities. If these five qualities are absent, one should not impose the non-speaking penalty."

Some bhikṣus, having imposed the non-speaking penalty on their disciples, then resumed speaking and living with them. The disciples became even more arrogant. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not, after imposing the non-speaking penalty, resume speaking with them. I do not wish them to lose their dependence; therefore the non-speaking penalty is imposed. It is for the sake of taming and pacifying, directed toward nirvāṇa, that the non-speaking penalty is imposed. If one resumes speaking, it is a duṣkṛta."

Furthermore, some bhikṣus, when a teacher had imposed the non-speaking penalty on his disciple, would go and speak with the disciple themselves. The disciple thereby became even more arrogant toward his teacher. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "When another has imposed the non-speaking penalty on his disciple, one should not speak with that disciple."

Since the Buddha did not permit others to speak with the disciple, some then returned to lay life or became non-Buddhist practitioners because of this. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one wishes to teach the offender to repent before his teacher, then one is permitted to speak with him."

At that time, there was a teacher who imposed the non-speaking penalty on his disciple, but the disciple refused to repent. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not refuse to repent! One should repent in this manner: baring the right shoulder, placing the right knee on the ground, supporting the teacher's feet with both hands, humbling oneself to the utmost, and saying: 'I was petty; I was foolish! I dare not do this again!'"

At that time, there were teachers who would not accept their disciples' repentance. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the disciple has regained shame, embarrassment, respect, affection, and offerings, one should not refuse to accept the repentance. When the repentance is accepted, the offense is eliminated."

At that time, there were also teachers who did not know whether their disciples had violated the precepts or not; did not know whether they had repented or not; and when they saw disciples violating the precepts, did not admonish them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A teacher should know whether his disciple has violated the precepts or not, whether he has repented or not; and when he sees a violation, he should admonish. If he does not know or does not admonish, it is a duṣkṛta."

At that time, resident bhikṣus did not bow to visiting or departing bhikṣus. Visiting and departing bhikṣus also did not bow to resident bhikṣus. Resident bhikṣus also did not bow to one another. There was a bhikṣu who arrived at a dwelling place and did not bow to the bhikṣus there. The bhikṣus asked: "Where do you come from?" He replied: "From such-and-such a place." The bhikṣus said: "We know that the bhikṣus of your dwelling place are all this arrogant. We should not live with them." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should bow to all! If one does not bow, it is a duṣkṛta."

Furthermore, some bhikṣus bowed from behind walls or obstructions, or bowed from afar, or while lying down said "Vandanā!", or merely raised a hand, or slightly lowered the head. The elder bhikṣus criticized them in various ways. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not bow in such manners! One should single-mindedly and respectfully remove one's leather sandals, bare the right shoulder, place both knees on the ground, and bow touching the feet."

There was a bhikṣu who bowed to each bhikṣu one by one and thereby lost his travel companions. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should bow only to one's teacher, and make a general bow to the others before departing."

At that time, the bhikṣu Upasena, who was of two rains, brought a one-rain disciple to the Buddha's place, bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, and sat to one side. Afterward, the disciple b

I notice that the prompt indicates Fascicle 16 (卷第十六) but then references "Fascicle 017: 五分律" with no actual Chinese text provided between the dividers.

Since no Chinese text has been provided for translation, I am unable to produce a translation. The content area between the dividers is empty.

If you could provide the actual Chinese text of the fascicle you would like translated, I would be happy to translate it in full according to the rules specified.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

## Fascicle Seventeen

### Third Division, Beginning of the Ordination Procedures, Continued

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time there was a naked ascetic of the non-Buddhist schools who was extremely intelligent. The people of Magadha called him "the knower" and "the seer." He came to the monastery and said: "Which of you śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, dares to debate with me?" At that time the bhikṣus were absorbed in playing among the various dhyānas and did not debate with him, nor did they speak with him. Śāriputra had this thought: "If he makes such statements and no one debates with him, he will surely humiliate the Buddha's Dharma. I should debate with him." He further thought: "This Nirgrantha is honored and revered by the people of Magadha. If I question him with a single proposition and he cannot answer it, he will certainly lose his reputation and not take refuge in the great Dharma. I should therefore debate with him for seven days." Having thought this, he said to him: "I will debate with you for seven days."

At that time the elders, householders, śramaṇas, and brahmins of Rājagṛha all discussed among themselves: "Śāriputra, the second teacher among the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, has arranged to debate with the Nirgrantha, the foremost teacher, for seven days. We should all go and listen."

When the time arrived, from the first day through the sixth day, they debated on various topics, and in every case he left the Nirgrantha tongue-tied. On the seventh day, Śāriputra said that desire arises from thought and imagination, while the Nirgrantha said that desire arises from contact with objects.

At that time Śāriputra spoke this verse:

> "All the roots of desire in the world
> arise from thought and imagination.
> Abiding in the roots of worldly desire,
> there arises an attached and defiled mind."

The Nirgrantha then challenged with a verse:

> "If desire arises from thought and imagination,
> and there is attachment,
> then when a bhikṣu has unwholesome thoughts and reflections,
> he has already lost the holy life."

Śāriputra replied with a verse:

> "If desire does not arise from thought and imagination,
> but arises from contact with objects,
> then when your teacher sees various forms,
> how is it that he does not experience desire?"

When the Nirgrantha heard this verse, he was unable to respond further. He then gave rise to a wholesome mind and wished to go forth and train in the way within the Buddha's Dharma. At that time Upananda was in that assembly, tall and handsome in appearance, while Śāriputra was short and small in form. The Nirgrantha had this thought: "This short, small bhikṣu possesses such talent and wisdom; how much more so one so stately and impressive!" He then went to Upananda and said: "Give me the going forth and full ordination." Upananda immediately ordained him.

When Śāriputra had finished the debate, he went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and sat to one side. The Buddha asked: "Why did you debate with the Nirgrantha for seven days?" He answered fully, explaining the matter. The Buddha praised him: "Excellent! Excellent, Śāriputra! You have great compassion and bring great benefit to many."

That Nirgrantha bhikṣu asked Upananda about the sūtras and vinaya, but Upananda could not answer any of his questions. The Nirgrantha then despised the Buddha's Dharma, saying that the bhikṣus knew nothing at all, and returned to being a non-Buddhist ascetic. The senior bhikṣus heard of this and rebuked, saying: "How can a bhikṣu of ten years' standing not know the Dharma, unable to resolve his disciple's doubts, causing him to return to non-Buddhist ways?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha asked Upananda: "Is this really so?" He answered: "It is so, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked him as above, he told the bhikṣus: "If one does not know the Dharma oneself, and gives someone the going forth and full ordination, it is a duṣkṛta."

If a bhikṣu is accomplished in ten qualities, he may confer full ordination upon others: he is accomplished in morality; accomplished in dignified deportment; he is cautious and fearful of even minor offenses; he is learned and can uphold what the Buddha has taught; he is skilled in reciting both divisions of the vinaya and can analyze their meaning; he can teach his disciple the training in higher morality, the training in higher mind, and the training in higher wisdom; he can resolve his disciple's doubts and can also have others resolve those doubts; he can treat his disciple's illnesses and can also have others treat those illnesses; if his disciple gives rise to wrong and evil views, he can teach him to abandon them and can also have others teach him to abandon them; if his disciple develops longing for his home country, he can turn his mind back, and can also have others turn it back; and he has completed ten rains or exceeded ten rains.

Again, one accomplished in ten qualities may confer full ordination upon others: knowing heavy offenses, knowing light offenses; knowing coarse offenses, knowing non-coarse offenses; knowing offenses with remainder, knowing offenses without remainder; knowing offenses involving a formal act, knowing offenses not involving a formal act; knowing the causes and conditions of offenses; and having completed ten rains or exceeded ten rains.

Again, one accomplished in five qualities may confer full ordination upon others: being able to teach his disciple the training in higher morality, the training in higher mind, and the training in higher wisdom; conducting himself with careful truthfulness; and keeping mindfulness present before him.

Again, one accomplished in five qualities: three qualities as above, plus intelligence and eloquence.

Again, one accomplished in five qualities: accomplishment in morality, accomplishment in concentration, accomplishment in wisdom, accomplishment in liberation, and accomplishment in the knowledge and vision of liberation.

Again, one accomplished in five qualities: personally abiding in morality and teaching others to abide in morality; personally abiding in concentration and teaching others to abide in concentration; personally abiding in wisdom and teaching others to abide in wisdom; personally abiding in liberation and teaching others to abide in liberation; personally abiding in the knowledge and vision of liberation and teaching others to abide in the knowledge and vision of liberation.

Again, one accomplished in five qualities: being accomplished in the aggregate of the morality of one beyond training, the aggregate of the concentration of one beyond training, the aggregate of the wisdom of one beyond training, the aggregate of the liberation of one beyond training, and the aggregate of the knowledge and vision of liberation of one beyond training.

Again, one accomplished in five qualities: being able to teach his disciple the higher morality and the higher holy life; knowing what constitutes an offense and what does not constitute an offense; knowing what has been confessed and what has not been confessed; and having completed ten rains or exceeded ten rains. Such a one may confer full ordination upon others.

The same applies for ordaining śrāmaṇeras and for serving as a reliance teacher for others.

There was a non-Buddhist ascetic who had previously gone forth and who came to the monastery and said to the bhikṣus: "Venerable ones, give me the going forth and full ordination." The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should first give him a separate probationary period of four months through a motion-and-one-proclamation formal act to test him. If he is agreeable to the bhikṣus, then afterwards give him the going forth and full ordination through the formal act procedure."

One should instruct the non-Buddhist ascetic to remove his leather sandals, bare his right shoulder, bow at the feet of each member of the Sangha, kneel with palms joined, and say: "May the noble Sangha listen! I, so-and-so, was formerly a non-Buddhist ascetic. I now seek the going forth in this Dharma and Vinaya. I request from the Sangha a separate probationary period of four months. May the Sangha, out of compassion, grant me the separate probationary procedure. If I am agreeable to the bhikṣus, then afterwards grant me the going forth and full ordination!" He should make this request three times.

A bhikṣu who knows the procedure should make the motion: "May the noble Sangha listen! This person, so-and-so, was formerly a non-Buddhist ascetic and wishes to go forth and receive full ordination in this Dharma and Vinaya. He now requests from the Sangha a separate probationary period of four months. The Sangha now grants the separate probationary period of four months. If he is agreeable to the Sangha, he shall be given the going forth and full ordination. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

"May the noble Sangha listen! This person, so-and-so, was formerly a non-Buddhist ascetic and wishes to go forth and receive full ordination in this Dharma and Vinaya. He now requests from the Sangha a separate probationary period of four months. The Sangha now grants the separate probationary period of four months. If he is agreeable to the Sangha, he shall be given the going forth and full ordination. Let those elders who consent remain silent; let those who do not consent speak. The Sangha has already granted so-and-so, the former non-Buddhist ascetic, the separate probationary period of four months. The Sangha consents, as shown by its silence. Let the matter be upheld thus."

What constitutes not being agreeable to the Sangha: if he enters the village early and returns only at dusk; if he frequently visits the homes of widows, unchaste women, or older unmarried girls, frequently converses with them, and makes various efforts in pursuit of sensual desires; if, upon hearing his former non-Buddhist teachers disparaged, he harbors anger and resentment; if, upon hearing the Three Treasures praised, he feels no joy or delight; if he does not delight in the deportment of bhikṣus; if he does not delight in studying and reciting the Buddha's sūtras; if he does not delight in receiving instruction and admonition: this is called not being agreeable to the Sangha. If none of these are present, it is called being agreeable, and he should be given the going forth and full ordination.

At that time the bhikṣus ordained a person who was in debt and gave him full ordination. After receiving full ordination, he entered Rājagṛha to beg for food. His creditor saw him and said: "You owe me a debt! Who permitted you to go forth?" Some said: "His robe and bowl should be seized, and he should be taken and handed over to the authorities!" Others said: "He has already entered the city of fearlessness; he should be released and allowed to go. Why? Because King Bimbisāra has decreed that anyone within the kingdom who insults a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī shall receive a heavy punishment." The creditors then criticized, saying: "These śramaṇas make no distinction between those who may be ordained and those who may not. How can they ordain someone who is in debt? They have no śramaṇa conduct and have violated the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, rebuked them in various ways, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "One should not ordain a debtor and give him full ordination. At the time of ordination and conferring full ordination, one should first ask: 'Are you in debt?' If he says: 'I am not in debt,' he may be ordained and accepted. If he says: 'I am in debt,' he should not be ordained or accepted. If one ordains or accepts such a person, it is a duṣkṛta in both cases. The same applies if one fails to ask. Ordaining a slave is likewise."

At that time there was a young boy whose parents had sent him to study writing and various skills under a teacher. That teacher also made him do extra work and frequently beat him with a stick, so the boy ran away from the teacher and returned home. His parents immediately sent him back to the teacher. He then thought: "The teacher torments me, and my parents do not care for me with affection. Where can I be freed from this suffering? Only by going forth and receiving full ordination." Having thought this, he went to the monastery and said to the bhikṣus: "Give me the going forth and full ordination." The bhikṣus immediately ordained him. When the teacher lost him, he asked the boy's parents. The parents said: "We sent him back right away. Why did he not arrive?" Thereupon the parents and the teacher went searching in all four directions and came to the monastery to ask the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus all said they had not seen him, but the teacher alone stood in silence, and not finding the boy, returned home. Later the boy entered Rājagṛha to beg for food. The teacher saw him and criticized, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, always say one should not speak falsely. How can they ordain my apprentice and then say they have not seen him?" The senior bhikṣus heard this, rebuked them in various ways, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not ordain another person's apprentice." This is also as stated above. "From now on, when ordaining someone, he should bow to the Sangha in each room, announcing his own name, so that all the Sangha members know him."

At that time in Śrāvastī, the group of seventeen boys, not yet twenty years old, were given full ordination by Pilindavatsa. They could not endure hunger and cried out begging for food, as described in the precept origins. At the time of conferring full ordination, one should ask: "Are you fully twenty years of age?"

At that time the bhikṣus ordained wilderness bandits and gave them full ordination. Afterwards they entered Rājagṛha to beg for food. The householders saw them and said: "This person previously killed such-and-such of my relatives and robbed me of my property." Some said: "He should be seized and handed over to the authorities!" And so forth, up to the Buddha telling the bhikṣus that they should not ordain such people, as stated above.

There were also bandits who were weary of committing evil deeds and sought to go forth and receive full ordination. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They may be taken to a place where people do not recognize them and given the going forth and full ordination there."

At that time there was a man who was a menace to his village. The villagers petitioned the king: "May Your Majesty command him to cease his evil deeds!" The king said: "Bring him here and I will have him killed for you!" When that man heard this, he immediately fled. They searched everywhere but could not find him. They reported again to the king, and the king then issued a decree: whoever catches him may kill him on the spot. When that man heard this again, he thought: "Where can I now preserve my life? Only among the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, can I find deliverance!" He went to the monastery and sought to go forth. The bhikṣus immediately ordained him. Later he entered Rājagṛha to beg for food. When people saw him, they wanted to seize and kill him. Some people said: "Since he has already gone forth, he is as good as dead; there is no need to kill him again." Others said: "This man has already entered the city of fearlessness." And so forth, up to the Buddha telling the bhikṣus that they should not ordain such people, as stated above.

At that time Upananda had two śrāmaṇeras, one named Kaṇḍa and the second named Māgadhika. They engaged in sexual misconduct with each other. The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha asked Upananda: "Did you really keep two śrāmaṇeras?" He answered: "I did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "One should not keep two śrāmaṇeras. One who does so commits a duṣkṛta."

At that time there was a family that had been harmed by non-human beings, and only the father and one child survived. The father thought: "My family has been destroyed. I fear the calamity is not yet over. Moreover, we are hungry and destitute. Where can I be freed from this suffering?" He further thought: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, receive many offerings and medicine for illness. I should take my child and go forth and receive full ordination." Having thought this, he went to the monastery and said to the bhikṣus: "Give me the going forth and full ordination." The bhikṣus gave him the going forth and full ordination. When he entered the city to beg for food, he held the child in one arm and the bowl in the other hand. The lay people saw this and criticized, saying: "This śramaṇa, son of the Śākyan, does not practice the holy life." Others said: "He probably had this child before he went forth. But why did the bhikṣus not wait until the child was grown before ordaining him? Instead they allow this man to beg for food while carrying a child. Who would not think that he has violated the holy life?" The senior bhikṣus heard this and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not ordain small children!"

At that time the people of Magadha contracted seven kinds of serious illness: whole-body sores, abscesses, white leprosy, half-body paralysis, possession by spirits, red patches, and fatty discharge. The only one who could treat these illnesses was Jīvaka; no one else was able to. King Bimbisāra had issued a decree commanding Jīvaka: "You shall treat only those in my palace and the bhikṣus and bhikṣuṇīs. You are not permitted to treat anyone else!" Because of this, all the sick people sought to go forth and receive full ordination. The bhikṣus gave them all the going forth and full ordination. They then sought medicinal herbs, compounded and cooked and ground them, creating many tasks and duties that hindered and obstructed their practice of the path. The lay people saw this and criticized, saying: "These śramaṇas are like physicians and physicians' apprentices, always compounding medicines and ordaining the seriously ill. They make no distinction between those who may be ordained and those who may not. They have no śramaṇa conduct and have violated the śramaṇa Dharma!"

There was also a wealthy elder who suddenly contracted all seven kinds of serious illness. He went and said to Jīvaka: "Treat me!" Jīvaka answered: "Have you not heard the king's decree?" The elder said again: "Treat me in secret, and I will pay you one hundred thousand gold coins." Jīvaka answered as before. The elder then increased the offer to two hundred, three hundred, four hundred, five hundred, and one thousand gold coins, up to his entire family fortune, and even offered his wife and children as slaves. The answer was the same as before. That elder then had this thought: "Since this is fruitless, I should just go forth and receive full ordination." He went to the monastery and said to the bhikṣus: "Give me the going forth and full ordination." The bhikṣus gave him the going forth and full ordination. Jīvaka treated him, and within seven days he was completely cured. As a result, Jīvaka was no longer available to treat the palace residents, and some of the palace people who were ill died. Once the elder was cured, he immediately returned to lay life. Jīvaka saw him and asked: "You had already gone forth. Why did you leave the path?" He answered: "I originally had no intention of going forth. Because you refused to treat my illness, I temporarily went forth. Now that my illness is cured, I have returned to lay life." Thereupon Jīvaka went to the Buddha, fully explained the matter to him, and said: "If the king learns of this, my punishment will be considerable! I beg the Buddha to instruct the bhikṣus not to ordain the seriously ill!" The Buddha taught Jīvaka various wonderful Dharma and sent him back to his dwelling. The Buddha asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really ordain the seriously ill?" They answered: "We did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "One should not ordain the seriously ill!" This is also as stated above.

At that time the bhikṣus ordained people who were in government service. Afterwards they entered Rājagṛha to beg for food. The householders recognized them and criticized, saying: "How can the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, ordain people who are in government service? These people make no distinction between those who may be ordained and those who may not. They have no śramaṇa conduct and have violated the śramaṇa Dharma!"

Furthermore, King Ajātaśatru had a mighty general whose strength was equal to a thousand men. The people of the time called him "the warrior of a thousand men's strength." He was weary of the suffering of the world and had this thought: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, practice the true Dharma. I should go forth among them to put an end to the source of suffering." He went to the monastery and sought ordination, and the bhikṣus immediately ordained him. Later when the king wished to muster his army, he did not see this man and asked his subordinates. The subordinates reported to the king: "We do not know his whereabouts." The king then issued a command: "If he does not appear at the military assembly, he shall be punished according to military law!" When the day of the military assembly arrived, he asked again: "Has that man come?" The answer was: "He has not come!" The king said: "The infantry without this man is like the elephant corps without the lead elephant." After the military inspection was concluded, he then heard that the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, had ordained the man. The king said angrily: "At this rate, before long the śramaṇas will ordain all my soldiers!" The king then established a strict regulation: "If anyone henceforth ordains a person in government service, the preceptor's ribs shall be broken, the ācārya's tongue shall be cut off, and the remaining monks shall each receive eight lashes with a raw leather whip and be driven out of the country." The senior bhikṣus heard this, rebuked them in various ways, and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "One should not ordain people who are in government service!" This is also as stated above.

At that time the bhikṣus stayed permanently in Rājagṛha. The householders criticized, saying: "Even the non-Buddhist ascetics know to move their dwelling places according to the seasons. The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, are attached to one place and do not move through all four seasons. How are they any different from ordinary worldly people?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha told Ānanda: "You may announce to the bhikṣus that the Tathāgata now intends to travel to the southern region. Those who wish to follow may go along as they please." Ānanda received the instruction and announced this message everywhere. Among the bhikṣus, those of one rain-retreat up to nine rain-retreats who were intelligent, conscientious, and desirous of learning the precepts had this thought: "If my preceptor or ācārya goes, I shall follow. If they do not go, I shall stay. Why? If I request a reliance teacher here, I would have to request one again there, creating many tasks and duties that hinder and obstruct my practice of the path." After the Buddha set out, very few followed. The Buddha traveled to the southern region with only a few bhikṣus, and gradually returned to Rājagṛha. Because of this matter, the Buddha assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Ānanda: "Why were there so few bhikṣus following me on the southern journey?" Ānanda explained the matter fully. The Buddha praised fewness of desires and contentment in various ways, praised morality and the upholding of morality, and then told the bhikṣus: "One accomplished in five qualities may be freed from the obligation of reliance: accomplishment in morality, accomplishment in concentration, accomplishment in wisdom, accomplishment in liberation, and accomplishment in the knowledge and vision of liberation." All of these are as stated in the section on qualifications for conferring ordination.

At that time the World-Honored One was in the Śākya country. The bhikṣus ordained people without the permission of their parents. The householders criticized as above.

Afterwards, the World-Honored One, early in the morning, put on his robes and took his bowl and went to King Śuddhodana's palace. At that time, Rāhula's mother, with Rāhula, was on a high balcony. Seeing the Buddha coming from afar, she said to the child: "Do you see that śramaṇa?" He answered: "I see him!" She further said: "He is your father. You may go and ask for your father's inheritance." After the Buddha entered the palace and sat down on the bare ground in the middle courtyard, Rāhula ran down to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and stood in the Buddha's shadow. He said: "This shadow is most pleasant! May the Buddha give me my father's inheritance!" The Buddha said: "Do you truly wish to have it?" He answered: "I do!" The Buddha then took him back to his dwelling and told Śāriputra: "You may ordain him." Śāriputra said to the Buddha: "The World-Honored One previously established the rule that one may not keep two śrāmaṇeras. I already have Cunda; I cannot ordain another." The Buddha said: "I now permit those who, like you, are able to instruct and admonish, to keep two śrāmaṇeras."

The ordination should be performed as follows: First, confer the upāsaka three refuges, instructing him to say: "I, so-and-so, take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, take refuge in the Bhikṣu Sangha." This is said three times. Then instruct him to say: "I, so-and-so, have taken refuge in the Buddha, have taken refuge in the Dharma, have taken refuge in the Bhikṣu Sangha." This also three times. "I am an upāsaka of the Buddha, the Bhagavat." Then he should also be instructed to say: "I, so-and-so, for my whole life will not kill living beings; for my whole life will not steal; for my whole life will not engage in sexual misconduct; for my whole life will not speak falsely; for my whole life will not drink intoxicants."

Then he should also be instructed to say: "I, so-and-so, take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, take refuge in the Bhikṣu Sangha." This is said three times. "I now, in the presence of the Tathāgata Śākyamuni, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, go forth as a śrāmaṇera. My preceptor is so-and-so." He should then be told: "For your whole life, not killing living beings: this is a śrāmaṇera precept. For your whole life, not stealing: this is a śrāmaṇera precept. For your whole life, not engaging in sexual acts: this is a śrāmaṇera precept. For your whole life, not speaking falsely: this is a śrāmaṇera precept. For your whole life, not drinking intoxicants: this is a śrāmaṇera precept.
