For your whole life, not singing, dancing, performing music or entertainments, and not going to watch or listen: this is a śrāmaṇera precept. For your whole life, not adorning the body with garlands, perfumes, or ointments: this is a śrāmaṇera precept. For your whole life, not sitting or lying on high and large beds: this is a śrāmaṇera precept. For your whole life, not accepting or keeping gold, silver, or money: this is a śrāmaṇera precept. For your whole life, not eating after the proper time: this is a śrāmaṇera precept." These are the ten precepts of a śrāmaṇera.

At that time King Śuddhodana heard that the Buddha had already ordained Rāhula. He was greatly distressed and went to the Buddha. He said to the Buddha: "When the Buddha went forth in the past, Nanda still remained, and that did not cause me such distress as now. Then Nanda also went forth, and all my remaining hopes rested solely on this child. Now he too has gone forth, and the great plan for the family and kingdom is forever severed. Unable to forget my feelings, how can I bear this?" The king further, reflecting on his own feelings, said to the Buddha: "The love for children and grandchildren pierces deeper than the marrow of one's bones. How can the bhikṣus lure away people's children and ordain them for the path? I beg the Buddha, from now on, to command the bhikṣus that without parents' permission, they may not ordain anyone for the path!" The Buddha taught the king various wonderful Dharma, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening him. The king then took his leave and returned to the palace. The Buddha then assembled the bhikṣu Sangha on account of this matter and asked the bhikṣus: "Without parents' permission, did you really ordain people and give them full ordination?" They answered: "We did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, without parents' permission, one may not ordain anyone!" This is also as stated above.

At that time in Rājagṛha there was a very wealthy elder who had faith in and delighted in the Buddha's Dharma and constantly provided food for bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, and upāsikās. Later his family was harmed by non-human beings, and only two small children remained. They then fell into great poverty and constantly scavenged for leftover food. The two children had frequently seen bhikṣus before, so whenever they saw bhikṣus from afar, they would run toward them, grab their robes and bowls, and climb onto their laps. The bhikṣus, fearing their robes and bowls would be soiled, always avoided them and went far away. The householders saw this and criticized, saying: "When this family was previously wealthy, not a day passed without all the śramaṇas visiting. Now seeing them orphaned and destitute, they abandon them and avoid them from afar. They know no gratitude and care only about food. They have no śramaṇa conduct and have violated the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard this, rebuked them in various ways, and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha asked the bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he asked Ānanda: "How old are those two small children? Can they shoo crows away from food?" Ānanda answered: "They can! The older one is eight years old, and the younger is seven." The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "I now permit ordaining children, as young as those able to shoo away crows."

After the bhikṣus had ordained the two small children, they constantly had them shoo away crows from the food, but did not give them proper food. The householders saw this and said: "These śramaṇas constantly praise the giving of equal food, yet now they ordain two small children and only make them shoo away crows without giving them proper food." The senior bhikṣus heard this and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Just as a senior bhikṣu receives his food portion, a śrāmaṇera should also be given the same. Children who shoo away crows should likewise be given an equal share."

At that time there was a young brahmin who had killed his mother. Reflecting on the gravity of the offense, he was constantly filled with remorse and fear. Not knowing how to extinguish this sin, he thought: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, practice the true Dharma and purely cultivate the holy life. If I go forth among them, perhaps my sin can be lightened." He went to the monastery and said to the bhikṣus: "Give me the going forth and full ordination." The bhikṣus asked: "Young brahmin, you are a non-Buddhist who does not have faith in the Buddha's Dharma. Why do you now wish to go forth in it?" He answered truthfully. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One who has killed his father or mother cannot be reborn in my Dharma. He should not be given the going forth and full ordination. If he has already received full ordination, he should be expelled."

At that time there was a wilderness bandit who killed a bhikṣu dwelling in the wilderness. From then on, his mind was constantly tormented, as if hot ashes were scorching his body. Day and night he suffered without a moment's relief. He had this thought: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, practice the true Dharma and purely cultivate the holy life. If I go forth among them, perhaps I can be freed from this torment." Having thought this, he went to the monastery and sought to go forth. The bhikṣus said to him: "You are a wilderness bandit who constantly wished to kill people and rob them of their possessions, without any compassion. Why do you now wish to go forth in the Buddha's Dharma and Vinaya?" He answered truthfully. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "That bhikṣu was an arhat. This person cannot be reborn in my Dharma. He should not be given the going forth and full ordination. If he has already received full ordination, he should be expelled."

At that time Devadatta, with evil intent, drew blood from the Buddha's body. The bhikṣus did not know how to deal with him and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One who, with evil intent, draws blood from the Buddha's body cannot be reborn in my Dharma. He should not be given the going forth and full ordination. If he has already received full ordination, he should be expelled." The same applies to Devadatta's splitting of the Sangha: he should not be given the going forth.

At that time the Buddha was traveling through the country of Kośala with a great bhikṣu Sangha of one thousand two hundred and fifty persons. Gradually traveling, they came to the bank of the Dark River and stopped beneath a śāla grove. A certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, bared his right shoulder, placed his right knee on the ground, joined his palms, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, this śāla grove is the place where many bhikṣuṇīs were violated." The Buddha asked: "How do you know this?" He answered: "I was here at that time!" The Buddha further asked: "Did you violate bhikṣuṇīs?" He answered: "I did!" The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "One who has sexually violated a bhikṣuṇī cannot be reborn in my Dharma. He should not be given the going forth and full ordination. If he has already received full ordination, he should be expelled."

At that time there was a son of an asura who was weary of birth, old age, sickness, and death. He had this thought: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, practice the true Dharma and purely cultivate the holy life. I should go forth among them and put an end to the source of all suffering." Having thought this, he transformed himself into a human form, went to the monastery, and sought to go forth. The bhikṣus gave him full ordination. He ate one person's portion of food, then up to seven persons' portions of food, and still was not satisfied. He also ate the Sangha's leftover food, and still it was not enough. At that time in Rājagṛha there were two householders who, on the same day, each invited five hundred monks. The bhikṣus all went to one household, while only the transformed bhikṣu went to the other place alone, and in a short time consumed all five hundred persons' portions. The householders criticized, saying: "How can the bhikṣus ordain a non-human being?" When that bhikṣu realized that people knew, he suddenly reverted to his original form. The senior bhikṣus heard of this and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Non-human beings cannot be reborn in my Dharma. They should not be given the going forth and full ordination. If they have already received full ordination, they should be expelled. At the time of conferring full ordination, one should ask: 'Are you a non-human being?'"

At that time the Nāga King Svayambhū was weary of birth, old age, sickness, and death, and wished to go forth. He transformed himself into a young brahmin, and so forth, up to the bhikṣus ordaining him and giving him full ordination, as above. According to the nature of nāgas, at two times they cannot maintain their transformed shape: when engaging in sexual desire and when sleeping. Later, while deeply asleep, his body filled an entire room, and the sound of his breathing was like the crash of thunder, disturbing the bhikṣus in their meditation. They all came out to look. When the nāga heard people's voices, he woke up and resumed the form of a bhikṣu, sitting in the cross-legged posture. They called for him to open the door, and he opened it. The bhikṣus asked: "Who are you?" He answered: "I am a śramaṇa, son of the Śākyan." They further said: "Do not speak falsely!" The transformed bhikṣu then answered truthfully. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Animals cannot be reborn in my Dharma. They should not be given the going forth and full ordination. If they have already received full ordination, they should be expelled. From now on, when conferring ordination, those who are not recognized should be tested and observed for seven days."

At that time the bhikṣus ordained a paṇḍaka and gave him full ordination. He then called the śrāmaṇeras and the garden keeper to engage in impure acts, and when he went outside and encountered people, he did the same. The lay people saw this and criticized, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, ordain paṇḍakas. They must certainly engage in impure acts together. These people make no distinction between those who may be ordained and those who may not!" And so forth, up to "If he has already received full ordination, he should be expelled," as stated above. "At the time of conferring full ordination, one should first ask: 'Are you a man?'" The same applies to a person with dual sex organs.

At that time there was a family that had been harmed by non-human beings, and only the head of the household survived alone. He had this thought: "I am now destitute and hungry. What plan can I make to save my life? The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, receive much in the way of robes, food, and medicine for illness. I should shave my head myself, put on a kāṣāya robe, live at home, and constantly go to the monastery to eat in turn." Having thought this, he shaved his own head and made himself a bhikṣu, and went to the bhikṣus' residence to seek food. When the bhikṣus bowed, he accepted their bows, and he also bowed to the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus asked: "Why do you bow to others and also accept others' bows? How many rains do you have? When did you receive ordination? Who is your preceptor and ācārya?" He answered: "I shaved my own head and put on the Dharma robes. I have no preceptor, nor any ordination date." The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one shaves his own head and calls himself a bhikṣu, he cannot be reborn in my Dharma. He should not be given the going forth and full ordination. If he has already received full ordination, he should be expelled."

At that time the Nirgrantha bhikṣu, the disciple of Upananda, who had previously abandoned the path, later came back seeking to go forth again. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One who has abandoned the inner Dharma for non-Buddhist ways cannot be reborn in my Dharma. He should not be given the going forth and full ordination. If he has already received full ordination, he should be expelled."

At that time Sundarananda the Vajjiputta engaged in sexual acts without having renounced his precepts. Afterwards he himself confessed what he had done. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one himself confesses having committed a pārājika offense, he cannot be reborn in my Dharma. He should not be given full ordination. If he has already received full ordination, he should be expelled. At the time of ordination, one should ask: 'Have you previously gone forth and purely cultivated the holy life?'"

At that time the bhikṣus did not take reliance and dwelt without anyone to instruct and admonish them. They were ignorant and lacking in understanding, unable to study the precepts. The senior bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should take reliance. If one does not take reliance for even a single night, one is not even permitted to drink water within the monastery. If one drinks, it is a duṣkṛta."

Since the Buddha did not permit dwelling without taking reliance, they no longer dared to dwell within the monastery. At that time a certain bhikṣu, avoiding his dwelling place, came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and sat to one side. The Buddha asked: "From which direction have you come?" He answered: "From such-and-such a direction." The Buddha further asked: "Who is the first senior, the second, and the third senior at that dwelling place?" He answered: "I do not know them." The Buddha further asked: "Who are the seniors at the neighboring dwelling places?" He answered: "I do not know them either." The Buddha further asked: "Why do you not know them?" He answered: "I have been avoiding that dwelling place and have not entered that community. That is why I do not know them." The Buddha further asked: "Why are you avoiding that dwelling place?" He answered: "The Buddha does not permit dwelling without taking reliance. If one does not take reliance, one is not even permitted to drink water within the monastery. That is why I avoid it." The Buddha rebuked that bhikṣu in various ways: "What you have done is contrary to the Dharma! You should not avoid your dwelling place for the sake of taking reliance!" After rebuking him, the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "If one avoids a dwelling place for the purpose of taking reliance, it is a duṣkṛta."

There were also bhikṣus who, while traveling on the road and seeing a monastery, would enter to take reliance. Sometimes the bhikṣus there were sitting in meditation, or they happened to be angry with each other, and would not grant it, causing the traveler to lose his traveling companions. Or sometimes, after taking reliance, a bhikṣu would immediately depart. The bhikṣus asked: "Why do you take reliance and then immediately leave?" He answered: "The World-Honored One does not permit avoiding a dwelling place for the sake of taking reliance. Now, seeing this Sangha dwelling place, I did not dare not stop to take reliance, but I must also keep up with my companions. That is why I leave." Those who lost their companions encountered bandits on the road. The senior bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now permit not taking reliance for one night."

Although they were allowed one night, there were still various difficulties. They reported this again to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now permit not taking reliance for up to six nights."

There were also bhikṣus who went beyond six nights without taking reliance. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to exceed six nights! Those who exceed it commit a duṣkṛta."

At that time bhikṣus casually served as reliance teachers for others, and also casually took reliance from others. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! One should take reliance from a senior bhikṣu who acts in accordance with the Dharma and is skilled in giving instruction and admonition. If one who has taken reliance wishes to move to another place, he should first ask his preceptor or ācārya and ascertain that there is a suitable reliance teacher there before going."

There were disciples who bade farewell to their preceptor or ācārya only at the time of departure. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to take leave at the time of departure. One must inform one's teacher two or three days in advance. The teacher should consider the place to which the disciple is going, and only if there is a suitable reliance teacher there should he permit the departure. Upon arriving at the new dwelling place, one should first pay respects at the stūpa, then pay respects to the senior bhikṣu, arrange lodging, and then seek reliance." The bhikṣu who serves as reliance teacher should ask: "Who is your preceptor and ācārya? Where did you previously reside? Which sūtras do you recite?" If the answers are in accordance with the Dharma, he should grant the reliance. If they are not in accordance with the Dharma, he should say: "You do not know me, and I do not know you. You should go to a place where they know you and seek reliance there." If there is doubt, he should say: "Stay for a while." The one seeking reliance should stay for a while, up to six nights, while he is observed. If he is found agreeable, he should be granted reliance. If he is not found agreeable, he should be told as above.

There was also a sick bhikṣu who sought reliance. That bhikṣu thought: "The Buddha instructs bhikṣus to care for their disciples in such-and-such a way. This person is sick and I cannot look after him." So he did not grant him reliance. The sick bhikṣu did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now permit that during the time of illness, one need not take reliance. After recovering from illness, one should then take reliance."

There was also a bhikṣu who was caring for a sick person and sought reliance. That bhikṣu said: "The Buddha instructs bhikṣus to attend to their preceptors and ācāryas in such-and-such a way. You are now nursing the sick, so I cannot grant you reliance." The bhikṣu caring for the sick, not having obtained reliance, felt ashamed and abandoned the sick person, going to seek reliance. The sick person had no one to care for him, and in some cases the illness worsened, and in some cases the sick person died. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now permit that a bhikṣu who is caring for the sick need not take reliance. He should wait until the sick person recovers, and then take reliance."

There were also bhikṣus who, at a place that was conducive to their practice and the attainment of fruits of the path, sought reliance, but the bhikṣus there would not grant it, and they lost their attainment of the fruits of the path. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If it is a place conducive to one's practice and the attainment of fruits of the path, and there is no one to serve as a reliance teacher, one is permitted to mentally take reliance upon the senior bhikṣu in that community, or upon one equal to the senior, and dwell there respecting him as one would a teacher."

At that time the ācāryas of bhikṣus died, or left the path, or traveled far away, or joined the non-Buddhist orders, or went beyond the boundary. They did not know whether they had lost their reliance or not. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "There are eight ways of losing reliance: if the reliance teacher travels far away; if he leaves the path; if he dies; if he becomes a non-Buddhist; if one encounters one's original preceptor; if the reliance teacher says: 'You should go and take reliance from so-and-so'; if the reliance teacher goes beyond the boundary and stays overnight; if one has completed five rains and is intelligent and eloquent, at the moment the dawn appears. These are the eight ways in which reliance is lost."

At that time the bhikṣus distributed the rains-retreat offerings in equal shares with the śrāmaṇeras. The śrāmaṇeras then showed no respect to the Sangha. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One bhikṣu's share should be divided among three śrāmaṇeras." The śrāmaṇeras still showed no respect. This was again reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should be punished!"

The bhikṣus punished the śrāmaṇeras without consulting their teachers, and the teachers were displeased. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should inform their teachers."

The teachers then improperly sided with the śrāmaṇeras. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The teachers should not improperly side with the śrāmaṇeras."

There was also a śrāmaṇera whom the Sangha punished by cutting off his food. Later, his patron invited the Sangha for a meal. The bhikṣus went and sat in order. The patron did not serve the food. The bhikṣus said: "The time has come. Why are you not serving the food?" He answered: "I am waiting for the entire Sangha to be assembled!" The bhikṣus said: "The Sangha is assembled!" The patron said: "The śrāmaṇera whom I support has not yet arrived." The bhikṣus said: "He is not allowed to come!" The patron asked: "Why?" They answered: "The Sangha has punished him by withholding food." The patron said: "There are many other punishments. How can you bear to deprive him of food?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Food should not be withheld. He should be punished by being made to sweep the grounds, remove refuse, carry stones, maintain the walking-meditation paths, build steps and walkways, and so forth with various such punishments."

At that time a certain bhikṣu's male organ disappeared and a female organ appeared. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The ordination received should remain as it is, the teachers requested should remain the same, and the rains-count should remain the same. The person should go to the bhikṣuṇī residence and dwell according to the rules for bhikṣuṇīs. If previously she committed offenses that are shared with bhikṣuṇīs, she should confess them among the bhikṣuṇīs. If previously she committed offenses that are not shared, they need not be confessed again. The same applies when a bhikṣuṇī's sex organ changes."

There was a śikṣamāṇā whose sex organ changed. They did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The ordination received should remain as it is. If he is twenty years of age, he should receive full ordination among a quorum of ten bhikṣus. If he is not yet twenty years of age, he is to be regarded as a śrāmaṇera. The same applies for a śrāmaṇerī."

There was a śrāmaṇera whose sex organ changed. They did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The ordination received should remain as it is. If she is of sufficient age, she should be given the two-year training, and she should receive the two-year training precepts among the bhikṣuṇī community. If she is not yet of sufficient age, she should not be given the two-year training, and she is to be regarded as a śrāmaṇerī."

At that time a certain bhikṣu, burned by the fire of desire and unable to endure it, severed his own organ. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha rebuked him, saying: "You foolish person! What should not be cut, you cut. What should be cut, you do not cut!" He told the bhikṣus: "If one cuts the tip or half of it, it is a duṣkṛta. If one cuts it off entirely, it is a sthūlātyaya. If one removes one testicle, it is a sthūlātyaya. If one removes both testicles, one should be expelled. If bitten by a wild animal, or harmed by an enemy, or it decays and falls off on its own, and one can no longer function as a man, all these cases require expulsion."

At that time the bhikṣus ordained people who had had their hands or feet amputated and gave them full ordination. The householders saw them and criticized, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyan, make no distinction between those who may be ordained and those who may not! They have no śramaṇa conduct! They have violated the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard this and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not ordain such people! If one does ordain them, it counts as valid full ordination. The teachers and the Sangha commit a duṣkṛta. From now on, one who has had a hand amputated, a foot amputated, both hands and feet amputated, an ear cut off, a nose cut off, both ears and nose cut off, fingers cut off, the tip of the male organ cut off, eyes gouged out, who has been whipped so as to disfigure their appearance, who has been punished by the government, who is crippled, who is mute, who is deaf, who is blind, who has a wasting disease, who is insane, who is extremely old, who lacks dignified deportment, who is extremely ugly, or who would disgrace the Sangha: all such persons may not be ordained." If they have already been ordained, it counts as valid full ordination, as stated above.

The bhikṣus ordained a person who stutters. The Buddha said: "One should not ordain a person who stutters and give him full ordination."

There were also bhikṣus who, without first conferring the śrāmaṇera precepts, gave full ordination. There were also bhikṣus who, without requesting a preceptor, gave full ordination. There were also bhikṣus who, without a request for full ordination being made, gave full ordination. There were also bhikṣus who gave full ordination to a naked person. There were also bhikṣus who gave full ordination to a person who did not have complete robes and bowl. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "None of these should be done!"

At that time a certain bhikṣu borrowed another's robes and bowl to receive full ordination. After receiving full ordination, the bhikṣus said to him: "Put on your robes, take your bowl, and let us go together to beg for food." He answered: "I have no robes or bowl." The bhikṣus said: "Did the Buddha not establish the rule that without robes and bowl, one may not receive full ordination?" He answered: "The Buddha did! But I borrowed someone else's robes and bowl to receive ordination." The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha assembled the bhikṣu Sangha on account of this matter and told the bhikṣus: "The person about to receive ordination should be placed outside the ordination platform, at a place where he can see but cannot hear what is said. Ten members of the assembly should be requested to sit on the ordination platform. The preceptor should say to the karma master: 'Elder, please now perform the formal act.' He should also say to the examining teacher: 'Elder, please now receive the formal act.' The karma master should make this motion to the Sangha: 'May the noble Sangha listen! So-and-so requests full ordination from so-and-so. So-and-so will serve as the examining teacher. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus.'

"The examining teacher should rise from his seat, go before the preceptor, and ask: 'Have you already ordained this person?' If the answer is 'not yet,' he should say: 'Please ordain him first.' If the answer is 'already ordained,' he should ask: 'Have you already served as his preceptor?' If the answer is 'not yet,' he should say: 'Please first serve as his preceptor.' If the answer is 'I have already served as his preceptor,' he should ask: 'Are the disciple's robes and bowl complete?' If the answer is 'not yet complete,' he should say: 'Please first provide complete robes and bowl.' If the answer is 'already complete,' he should ask: 'Are they his own, or borrowed from someone?' If the answer is 'borrowed from someone,' he should say: 'The owner should relinquish them to him.' If the answer is 'they are his own,' the examining teacher should then go to comfort the person about to receive ordination, saying: 'Do not be afraid. Shortly you will be placed in a high and noble position!'

"If they do not already know each other, one should not confer full ordination during foggy, cloudy, or dark conditions. While instructing him in putting on robes, the examining teacher should discreetly and properly examine whether there are any serious illnesses. He should also ask: 'Of your three robes, which is the saṃghāṭī? Which is the uttarāsaṅga? Which is the antaravāsaka?' If he does not know, he should tell him: 'This is the saṃghāṭī, this is the uttarāsaṅga, this is the antaravāsaka.' He should then formally assign the three robes and bowl.

"He should further say: 'Listen, so-and-so! Now is the time for truthfulness. I am going to question you. If something is true, say it is true. If something is not true, say it is not true. People have illnesses such as these: leprosy, white leprosy, abscesses and boils, wasting disease, insanity, hemorrhoids and fistulas, hot swellings, fatty discharge. Do you have any of these?' If he says 'no,' he should further ask: 'Are you not in debt? Are you not a government servant? Are you not a slave? Are you a man? Are you a human? Are you fully twenty years of age? Are your robes and bowl complete? Have you accepted a preceptor? What is your name? What is your preceptor's name? Have you previously gone forth?' If he says he has previously gone forth, he should ask: 'When you previously went forth, did you keep your precepts completely intact? Do your parents give permission? Do you wish to receive full ordination? You will be asked these same questions again in the assembly, and you should answer truthfully as well.'

"If each question is answered in accordance with the Dharma, the examining teacher should return and stand on the ordination platform, and say to the karma master: 'I have completed the examination and instruction of so-and-so in accordance with the Dharma!'

"The karma master should then make the motion to the Sangha: 'May the noble Sangha listen! So-and-so requests full ordination from so-and-so. So-and-so has completed the examination and instruction in accordance with the Dharma. He should now be brought forward. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus.'

"The examining teacher should bring him forward. He should bow at the feet of each member of the Sangha in order. After bowing at the feet of the Sangha, he should kneel before the karma master, with his right knee on the ground and palms joined, and be taught to request full ordination. He should be taught to say: 'I, so-and-so, request full ordination from the preceptor so-and-so. I now request from the Sangha full ordination. May the Sangha rescue and deliver me, out of compassion!' He should make this request three times. After the examining teacher has completed the instruction, he should return to his original seat.

"The karma master should make the motion to the Sangha: 'May the noble Sangha listen! This person, so-and-so, requests full ordination from so-and-so. He requests full ordination from the Sangha. I shall now question him regarding the obstructive matters and perform the formal act of full ordination for him. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus.'

"He should then say to the ordination candidate: 'Now is the time for truthfulness. I am going to question you. If something is true, say it is true. If something is not true, say it is not true. People have illnesses such as these: leprosy, white leprosy,' and so forth up to 'Do you wish to receive full ordination?' He should ask just as above.

"After all answers are in accordance with the Dharma, the karma master should say: 'May the noble Sangha listen! This person, so-and-so, requests full ordination from so-and-so. So-and-so has declared himself pure, free from all obstructive matters. His three robes and bowl are complete. He has accepted a preceptor. His parents have given permission. He has requested full ordination from the Sangha. The Sangha now confers full ordination upon so-and-so, with so-and-so as preceptor. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus.'

"'May the noble Sangha listen! This person, so-and-so, requests full ordination from so-and-so. So-and-so has declared himself pure, free from all obstructive matters. His three robes and bowl are complete. He has accepted a preceptor. His parents have given permission. He has requested full ordination from the Sangha. The Sangha now confers full ordination upon so-and-so, with so-and-so as preceptor. Let those elders who consent remain silent; let those who do not consent speak.' The second and third proclamations are likewise.

"'The Sangha has conferred full ordination upon so-and-so, with so-and-so as preceptor. The Sangha consents, as shown by its silence. Let the matter be upheld thus.'

"He should then say to the ordination candidate: 'Listen, so-and-so! The World-Honored One, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, has declared these four pārājika offenses. If a bhikṣu commits any one of them, he is no longer a śramaṇa, no longer a son of the Śākyan. You must never, even with a mind stained by desire, gaze at a woman. If a bhikṣu engages in sexual intercourse, even with an animal, he is no longer a śramaṇa, no longer a son of the Śākyan. For the whole of your life, you must not commit this offense!'

"If he is able, he should say: 'I am able!'

"'You must never take so much as a blade of grass without it being given. If a bhikṣu, whether in a village or in an uninhabited place, steals another's guarded property worth five coins or the equivalent of five coins, he is no longer a śramaṇa, no longer a son of the Śākyan. For the whole of your life, you must not commit this offense!'

"If he is able, he should say: 'I am able!'

"'You must never kill so much as an ant. If a bhikṣu kills a person or a being resembling a person, whether with his own hand, or by instructing another to kill, or by providing a weapon, or by urging death, or by praising death, saying: "Bah! Man! What use is this wretched life? Death is better than living!" he is no longer a śramaṇa, no longer a son of the Śākyan. For the whole of your life, you must not commit this offense!'

"If he is able, he should say: 'I am able!'

"'You must never speak even a playful lie. If a bhikṣu, without actually possessing superhuman attainments, claims to have attained superhuman attainments: the dhyānas, the liberations, the samādhis, the samāpattis, and the various fruits of the path, he is no longer a śramaṇa, no longer a son of the Śākyan. For the whole of your life, you must not commit this offense!'

"If he is able, he should say: 'I am able!'

"'All the Buddhas, the World-Honored Ones, in order to illustrate this matter, have skillfully spoken these similes: Just as a dead person can never again come to life in this body; just as when the eye of a needle is broken, it can never again serve as a needle; just as when the heart of a tāla tree is cut, it will never again grow, increase, or expand; just as when a stone is broken, it cannot be put back together. If a bhikṣu commits any one of these pārājika offenses, it is impossible that he

I notice that while the header references Fascicle 17 (卷第十七), the actual text content appears to be missing or empty between the separator lines, and then references Fascicle 18. There is no Chinese text provided for me to translate.

Could you please provide the actual Chinese text content of the fascicle you would like me to translate? I would be happy to translate it in full once the source text is supplied.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Eighteen

### The Third Division, Part Four: The Uposatha Method

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, the non-Buddhist practitioners, śramaṇas, and brāhmaṇas would gather together on the eighth, fourteenth, and fifteenth days of the month, harmoniously performing the uposatha and expounding the Dharma, and many people would come and go making offerings. King Bimbisāra saw this and had this thought: "If the disciples of the True Dharma also did likewise, would that not be excellent? I should lead my officials and retinue to go there to listen to the Dharma, pay reverence, and make offerings, causing all people to obtain peace through the long night."

At that time, the World-Honored One also had this thought: "I have established precepts for the bhikṣus, yet there are bhikṣus who have not heard them, who cannot recite and study them, and who cannot remember and retain them. I should now permit the bhikṣus to perform the uposatha and recite the precepts." After King Bimbisāra had this thought, he went to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and reported his thought to the Buddha. The Buddha expounded various wonderful teachings for the king, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and delighting him, and then the king returned to the palace. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha, and relating both what King Bimbisāra had reported and his own thought, he told the bhikṣus: "Now, for the sake of ten benefits, I permit the bhikṣus to perform the uposatha and recite the precepts."

After the Buddha had permitted the uposatha recitation of precepts, the bhikṣus performed the uposatha every day. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

The bhikṣus then performed the uposatha every two days, every three days, or every five days. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This also should not be done! I permit expounding the Dharma on the eighth and fourteenth days, and performing the uposatha on the fifteenth day."

The bhikṣus did not know what Dharma they should expound. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should praise the Three Treasures, the foundations of mindfulness, the right exertions, the bases of supernatural power, the faculties, the powers, the factors of awakening, and the path. For the donors, you should praise the devas."

The bhikṣus then praised the Three Treasures in unison. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! I permit appointing one person."

The bhikṣus appointed bhikṣus who had broken the precepts or who held wrong views, and these thereby gained influence. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! You should appoint one who studies the precepts."

The bhikṣus then appointed bhikṣus with squinting eyes and various illnesses, who brought disgrace to the Sangha. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! You should appoint one whose faculties are complete, who is accomplished in exposition and discussion, and who upholds the Āgamas."

At that time there were many such persons in the assembly, and the bhikṣus did not know whom to appoint. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should appoint them in sequence."

The appointed bhikṣu became exhausted from expounding the Dharma. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should appoint another to take his place."

The bhikṣus expounded the Dharma in a singing, chanting voice. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

During the Dharma talk, the assembly could not all hear. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A high seat should be prepared, and the Dharma should be expounded from upon it."

Still not everyone could hear. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should stand to listen."

From standing a long time, their feet became swollen. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should walk while listening."

At that time, the bhikṣus performed the uposatha in the open, and were troubled by mosquitoes, gadflies, wind, rain, and dust. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit building an uposatha hall."

That uposatha hall had no floor covering, and the bhikṣus' feet became soiled, and from frequent washing they fell ill. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The floor should be plastered with clay, cleaned, and made smooth. I also permit spreading the ten kinds of cloth and soft grasses such as bābhā and the like."

After the Buddha permitted spreading cloth, they spread brocade on the ground. Lay people saw this and criticized them, saying: "These śramaṇas are like kings and great ministers!" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not walk on brocade!"

At that time, the bhikṣus scattered flowers upon the bhikṣu on the high seat. Lay people criticized this, saying: "Like kings and great ministers!" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also lay people who, wishing to make offerings to the Dharma, wanted to scatter flowers upon the bhikṣu on the high seat. The bhikṣus did not permit it, and the lay people became angry and criticized them, saying: "These bhikṣus are not worthy of receiving offerings!" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If lay people wish to scatter flowers, they may do so as they please. If flowers fall on the bhikṣu's head or robes, they should be brushed off. If they fall on the high seat, there is no problem."

At that time, the lay people, rejoicing at hearing the Dharma, wished to make offerings. The bhikṣus feared this would incur the fault of hired labor and repeatedly dared not accept. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Offerings made for the sake of the Dharma may be accepted."

At that time, the bhikṣus would expound the Dharma for a short while and then stop. The devas and spirits thought it was finished and departed. Shortly afterward, they resumed expounding, and those beings returned again. This happened repeatedly, and the beings became angry, saying: "These bhikṣus do not set a proper limit for their Dharma talks; it is like children playing!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A proper limit should be set for the Dharma talk. When the Dharma talk is finished, a blessing should be recited."

At that time, Kapphinya was dwelling on Mount Iśirasana and had this thought: "Should I now go to the place where the Sangha gathers for the uposatha?" He then had this thought: "I am always pure; what need is there for me to go?" At that time, the World-Honored One knew his thought. He vanished from Rājagṛha and appeared before him, took a seat, and said: "Do not think thus: 'I am always pure; what need is there for me to go to the uposatha?' If you and others do not go, and do not respect the uposatha, who then should respect it?" After the World-Honored One had instructed him thus, he vanished together with him from that place and appeared in Rājagṛha. On account of this matter, he assembled the bhikṣu Sangha, related Kapphinya's thought and his own instruction, and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit the bhikṣus to perform the uposatha in harmony. If one does not go, it is a duṣkṛta."

A bhikṣu who knows the Dharma, whether the senior monk or one equal to the senior monk, should declare: "Venerable Sangha, listen! Today is the fifteenth day, the uposatha for reciting the precepts. The Sangha, with one mind, performs the uposatha and recites the precepts. If the time is appropriate, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

"Venerable ones! Today we perform the uposatha and recite the Prātimokṣa. Let all listen together and reflect well upon it. If anyone has an offense, they should confess it. Those without offenses should remain silent. By your silence, it shall be known that I and all the venerable ones are pure. Just as in response to an individual question one answers, so too do I and all the venerable ones respond in the same way. If a bhikṣu in such an assembly, up to the third proclamation, remembers an offense and does not confess it, they incur the offense of deliberate false speech. Deliberate false speech, the Buddha has declared, is an obstruction to the path. One who confesses obtains peace and happiness."

Herein, "Prātimokṣa" means: because these precepts guard the sense faculties, increase wholesome dharmas, and among all wholesome dharmas serve as the very first gateway, they are called "Prātimokṣa." Furthermore, enumerating the clauses and sections of these precept rules and giving them a collective name, they are called "Prātimokṣa."

The bhikṣus did not know how many kinds of uposatha there should be. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "There are five kinds of uposatha: first, mental recollection with verbal declaration; second, declaring purity to another; third, the full and abbreviated recitation of the precepts; fourth, the pravāraṇā uposatha; fifth, the harmonious uposatha."

The bhikṣus did not know how many kinds of precept recitation there should be. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "There are five kinds of precept recitation: first, after reciting the preface to the precepts, saying, 'The rest is as the Sangha always hears'; second, after reciting the preface and the four pārājika offenses, saying, 'The rest is as the Sangha always hears'; third, after reciting the preface through the thirteen saṅghāvaśeṣa offenses, saying, 'The rest is as the Sangha always hears'; fourth, after reciting the preface through the two aniyata offenses, saying, 'The rest is as the Sangha always hears'; fifth, the full recitation."

The bhikṣus did not know how many kinds of Vinaya-holders there are. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "There are five kinds of Vinaya-holders." As previously stated. "A Vinaya-holding bhikṣu has five kinds of merit." Also as previously stated.

A Vinaya-holding bhikṣu has seven kinds of suitability: first, being learned in the many dharmas; second, being able to deliberate what is Dharma and what is not Dharma; third, being skilled in deliberating the Vinaya; fourth, being skilled in preserving the teacher's instructions; fifth, when going to other places, speaking without fear; sixth, abiding in the Vinaya himself; seventh, knowing shared and unshared precepts.

There are another seven kinds of suitability: first, abiding in the precepts oneself, with deportment accomplished, fearing and being cautious about even minor offenses; second, being learned, able to retain what the Buddha has taught; third, reciting the precepts of both divisions; fourth, knowing what constitutes an offense; fifth, knowing what does not constitute an offense; sixth, knowing how to repent; seventh, knowing what requires no repentance.

There are yet another seven kinds of suitability: the first three are as above; fourth, not following desire; fifth, not following anger; sixth, not following delusion; seventh, not following fear.

At that time, the bhikṣus within a boundary performed the uposatha as a separate assembly and not in accordance with the Dharma; there were also those who performed it in harmony but not in accordance with the Dharma; there were also those who performed it in accordance with the Dharma but as a separate assembly; and there were those who performed it in accordance with the Dharma and in harmony. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The first three uposathas are faulty; the karman is invalid, and they incur a duṣkṛta. The last uposatha is without fault; the karman is valid, and there is no offense."

At that time, King Bimbisāra established an intercalary period of once every five years. The non-Buddhist practitioners, śramaṇas, and brāhmaṇas all followed it, but the bhikṣus alone would not comply. The ministers and the people all criticized them, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākya, dwell within the king's territory but do not follow the king's intercalation." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should follow the king's regulation."

The bhikṣus did not know how to follow the king's regulation. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit shortening by one night for the uposatha."

The bhikṣus then constantly shortened by one night for the uposatha. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should not constantly shorten by one night for the uposatha! I permit, for every three full months, shortening one. In this way, over five years, one month is added, in order to conform with the king's intercalation."

At that time, the bhikṣus on the precept-recitation day went to various places for the uposatha. Some encountered wildfires, some encountered flooding waters, some encountered bandits in the eighth month. There were dangers to their chaste conduct, dangers to their robes and bowls, and dangers to their lives. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "On the precept-recitation day, one should not go to another place for the purpose of reciting the precepts. One who goes incurs a duṣkṛta. I permit that at the place where one is residing, whether there is level ground, or soft grass, or a large tree, or a large flat rock, a jñapti-dvitīya-karman should be performed to designate it as the uposatha place."

One bhikṣu announces: "Venerable Sangha, listen! We now designate this as the uposatha place. If the time is appropriate, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! We now designate this as the uposatha place. Those venerable elders who consent, remain silent. Those who do not consent, speak. The Sangha has designated this as the uposatha place. The Sangha consents, by its silence. Let this matter be held thus."

The bhikṣus performed the uposatha in the open and were troubled by wind, rain, mosquitoes, and gadflies. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit designating, by means of a jñapti-dvitīya-karman as above, a centrally located room that is easy to access for coming and going, as the uposatha hall."

The bhikṣus then wished to designate by karman many rooms as uposatha places. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This is permitted."

The bhikṣus then quarreled over priority. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to designate by karman many rooms as uposatha places!"

There were lay people who came into the monastery and told the bhikṣus: "If you perform the uposatha in the room I had built, I will provide the morning meal, the afternoon meal, and the dessert course, and give oil for anointing the feet, anointing the body, and for lamps." The bhikṣus had this thought: "If the World-Honored One would again permit designating by karman many rooms as uposatha places, we would not lose these offerings." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I again permit designating by karman many rooms as uposatha places, performing the uposatha in them in sequence."

The rooms were small and could not accommodate everyone. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit sitting under the front and rear eaves and in the courtyard." The bhikṣus could hear the sound but could not understand the words, and feared the uposatha would be invalid. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one is present for the purpose of the uposatha, it counts as the uposatha."

The bhikṣus did not assemble promptly for the uposatha, neglecting meditation and practice. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The time should be announced, either by striking the ghaṇṭā, or by beating the drum, or by blowing the conch."

The bhikṣus then made drums of gold and silver. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should be made of copper, iron, clay, or wood, with leather stretched over the top."

They did not know who should strike it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A śrāmaṇera or the garden keeper should be assigned to strike it."

They struck it too many times. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should be struck in three rounds." After striking, they hung it in the central courtyard. Outsiders came and struck it repeatedly, and the bhikṣus, thinking it was a Sangha matter, all came out, disrupting their practice. Or when it rained, it became wet and made no sound. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should be stored under shelter in a secluded place."

A visiting śrāmaṇera whose turn it was to strike it did not know the location and missed the time. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The resident should strike it. I permit keeping a Sangha drum, a private drum, a drum belonging to the Sangha of the four directions, and a spare drum."

The bhikṣus also made conches of gold and silver. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A sea conch should be blown, or one made from horn. A śrāmaṇera or garden keeper should blow it, and likewise up to keeping a spare conch, as stated above."

The bhikṣus did not know from what wood to make the ghaṇṭā. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Except for lacquer trees and poisonous trees, any wood that produces a resonant sound may be used. If there is no śrāmaṇera, a bhikṣu may also strike it." The rest is as above.

The bhikṣus did not know who should make the three announcements that the time has arrived. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit assigning a śrāmaṇera or garden keeper." In places where the Sangha resided in many locations, the announcement could not be heard everywhere. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should go to a high place to make the announcement."

The bhikṣus did not know whether everyone had assembled or not. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Bhikṣus sitting nearby should inform each other."

Later, visiting bhikṣus came and were not accounted for. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should be counted." The bhikṣus counted but then forgot. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Counting sticks should be distributed, collected, and counted."

When one person distributed and also collected, things became confused. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A separate person should be assigned to collect them."

The bhikṣus then made counting sticks of gold and silver. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should be made of copper, iron, ivory, horn, bone, bamboo, or wood, excluding lacquer and poisonous trees." The bhikṣus made some short and some long. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Short ones should be the length of five fingers laid side by side; long ones should be the length of one cubit measured with a fist."

The bhikṣus made some thick and some thin. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The thickest should not exceed the size of the little finger, and the thinnest should not be less than a chopstick. They should be lacquered, stored in a tube, and hung in the uposatha hall."

The bhikṣus did not know who should distribute the counting sticks. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A junior bhikṣu should be assigned to distribute them."

The junior bhikṣu did not know how to distribute them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One who knows how should be selected."

A bhikṣu threw the counting sticks to the Sangha. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should be handed over personally."

After collecting them, they did not count. After counting, they did not announce. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "After collecting, they should be counted. After counting, the number should be announced: 'There are such-and-such number of bhikṣus, such-and-such number of śrāmaṇeras; the total of ordained persons is such-and-such.'"

At that time, lay people attended the uposatha. Afterward, when bhikṣus committed offenses, the lay people raised charges against them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Lay people should not be allowed to attend. Śrāmaṇeras likewise."

Although the bhikṣus sent the śrāmaṇeras to a place where they could not see, they could still hear. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should be placed where they can neither see nor hear."

There were also śrāmaṇeras who, knowing the uposatha was about to take place, hid in advance under the beds and still heard the precepts. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should look under the beds and illuminate thoroughly with a light."

The light smoked up the room or burned the floor coverings. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A lamp cage and lamp stand should be made. Both Sangha-owned and privately owned are permissible." The bhikṣus then made them of gold and silver. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should be made of copper, iron, clay, or wood."

There were lay people who, upon completing the construction of a new house, invited the bhikṣus to first perform the uposatha and expound the Dharma therein, as a housewarming offering. The bhikṣus did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to accept!"

There were also lay people whose homes were troubled by non-human beings, who invited the bhikṣus to perform the uposatha and expound the Dharma in their homes as a blessing and offering. The bhikṣus did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to accept!"

There was a lay person who offered sugarcane. The bhikṣus dared not accept. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to accept!"

There were lay people who asked the bhikṣus: "What day is it today?" The bhikṣus did not know, and the lay people criticized them, saying: "These śramaṇas, sons of the Śākya, do not even know what day it is; how much less do they understand profound principles!" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one intends to visit a lay household, one should first ask the teacher the day count. If the teacher does not know, one should ask others."

At that time, lay people on the uposatha day brought timely food, timely drink, seven-day medicines, and lifetime medicines to the monastery as offerings, wishing to hear the Dharma and receive the eight-part precepts. The bhikṣus paid them no attention at all, and the lay people left in anger, taking their offerings with them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The senior monk should instruct the junior monks to sweep the ground and fetch water, and have the pure attendants prepare vessels to receive the items they have brought."

The bhikṣus ate but gave nothing to the visitors. The visitors criticized them, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākya, always praise generosity, only receiving others' gifts but never giving to others." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Food should be given to the visitors."

They gave it by placing it in the visitors' hands without providing vessels, and the visitors criticized them again, saying: "The bhikṣus treat us like children." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Vessels should be provided and the food served in them. When the meal is finished, the senior monk, or one equal to the senior monk, should expound the Dharma and recite blessings for them."

After the visitors have departed, if there are four or more bhikṣus, the full uposatha should be performed. If there are two or three bhikṣus, they should face each other and declare their purity, saying: "Today, on the fourteenth or fifteenth day, the Sangha performs the uposatha. I, bhikṣu so-and-so, am pure. Venerable one, remember this!" This is said three times. If there is only one bhikṣu, he should wait briefly. If someone comes to perform the uposatha together, that is good. If no one comes, he should bare his right shoulder, kneel on both knees with palms joined, and mentally recollect while verbally declaring: "Today, on the fourteenth or fifteenth day, the Sangha performs the uposatha. I now mentally receive the uposatha." This is said three times. He told the bhikṣus: "This is the uposatha method. From now on, it should be practiced thus for one's entire life. If not, it is a duṣkṛta."

At that time, lay people entered the monastery and asked the bhikṣus: "How many are in the Sangha?" They answered: "The Sangha has such-and-such a number." The lay people said: "We invite the Sangha for a meal tomorrow." Bhikṣus from nearby places heard this and all came the next day. There were not enough seats, and the food was insufficient. The bhikṣus said: "You invited the Sangha; why do you not give us food?" The lay people replied: "Yesterday we asked the Sangha's number and prepared food according to that count. You were not originally invited, yet you forcibly demand food. To eat without being invited is worse than non-Buddhists. These people have no śramaṇa conduct and violate the śramaṇa Dharma!" The elder bhikṣus heard this and reproached them in various ways. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not go without being invited. One who goes incurs a duṣkṛta."

There were also bhikṣus who had business that required them to pass by the host's home on the way, and they were embarrassed and did not dare go. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one is not going for the sake of food, it is permitted to go."

At that time, a lay person invited the Sangha for a meal, and visiting bhikṣus arrived. The bhikṣus did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The host should be told: 'There are visiting bhikṣus; will you allow them to join?' If the host allows it, good. If not, then say: 'Give us our food portions, and we will share equally among ourselves.' If that is obtained, good. If not, each should receive his portion in his bowl and go outside to share the meal. If that is obtained, good. If that too is not obtained, and there is food in the monastery, it should be brought and given to them."

At a time of famine when alms were difficult to obtain, bhikṣus from other places all abandoned their residences and gathered in Rājagṛha. The monasteries were all empty with no one to guard them. The buildings and furnishings were either burned by fire, damaged by flooding, or gnawed by insects. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All the residences near and around Rājagṛha should first have their boundaries dissolved by a jñapti-dvitīya-karman as above, and then by a jñapti-dvitīya-karman as above, they should all be united into one great boundary, so that the bhikṣus can receive their share of offerings without abandoning their original residences."

Later, the other regions became prosperous again. The people there missed the bhikṣus and sent a message saying: "We wish the bhikṣus would travel among us; we will provide robes and food." At that time, King Bimbisāra also wished the bhikṣus to travel and teach, and said: "I wish you would travel about. If there is any need, I will order the local authorities to supply whatever is required." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "First, by a jñapti-dvitīya-karman as above, dissolve the great boundary of Rājagṛha. Then each may, as they wish, declare the boundary markers anew and re-establish smaller boundaries."

There were forest-dwelling bhikṣus who did not know how far their boundary should extend. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The natural boundary extends two krośas from oneself in all directions. If a boundary is formally established, it may be as far or near as desired."

At that time, the bhikṣus established a boundary without limits. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If a boundary without limits is established, the boundary is invalid, and those who do so incur a duṣkṛta!"

The bhikṣus then established boundaries of twelve yojanas or ten yojanas. When it was time to recite the precepts, they had to travel four or five days. They encountered wildfires, flash floods, or bandits, and there were dangers to their chaste conduct, dangers to their robes and bowls, and dangers to their lives. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If a boundary of twelve yojanas or ten yojanas is established, the boundary is invalid, and those who do so incur a duṣkṛta. I now permit a maximum distance of three yojanas."

At that time, the bhikṣus established boundaries without announcing the boundary markers on the four sides. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the boundary markers are not announced, the boundary is invalid, and those who do so incur a duṣkṛta!"

At that time, the bhikṣus used living beings and smoke or fire as boundary markers, or overlapped boundaries, or had two boundaries intersecting. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All of these are invalid for establishing a boundary, and those who do so incur a duṣkṛta!"

There were two residences whose bhikṣus wished to perform the uposatha together and share offerings by establishing a joint boundary. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit each to dissolve their original boundary by a jñapti-dvitīya-karman, and then to assemble together and establish a joint boundary by a jñapti-dvitīya-karman."

There were also bhikṣus who wished to dwell together, perform the uposatha together, but receive offerings separately by establishing a boundary. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit dissolving the original boundary and then assembling to establish a new one."

There were also bhikṣus who wished to dwell together and share offerings, but perform the uposatha separately by establishing a boundary. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. Those who do so incur a sthūlātyaya!"

There was one residence where the bhikṣus wished to dwell separately, perform the uposatha separately, and receive offerings separately by establishing boundaries. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit dissolving the original boundary and then each establishing a new one."

There were also bhikṣus who wished to dwell separately, perform the uposatha separately, but share offerings by establishing a boundary. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This is permitted." He told the bhikṣus: "All rivers, all lakes and ponds, and all seas may not be designated as boundaries. If traveling on water, the natural boundary is the distance that can be reached by the splash of water thrown by the strongest person in the group."

The Buddha was at the Bamboo Grove. Ājñāta Kauṇḍinya was at Mount Laṅkura. On uposatha days, he would manifest a blue rainbow, sit cross-legged within it, and come to the Buddha's place. Many people came to see this, and so afterward he went on foot. His paṃśukūla robes were heavy, and the journey exhausted him. The bhikṣus had this thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit the bhikṣus to establish, within a village or village boundary, a territory where they do not lose their robes, then this elder would not become exhausted like this." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha praised the precepts and praised those who keep the precepts in various ways, and then told the bhikṣus: "I now permit the bhikṣus to establish, within a village or village boundary, by a jñapti-dvitīya-karman, a territory where they do not lose their robes."

One bhikṣu announces: "Venerable Sangha, listen! In this established boundary area, within the village or village boundary, where we dwell together, perform the uposatha together, and share offerings, we now designate this as a territory where one does not lose one's robes. If the time is appropriate, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! In this established boundary area, within the village or village boundary, where we dwell together, perform the uposatha together, and share offerings, we now designate this as a territory where one does not lose one's robes. Those venerable elders who consent, remain silent. Those who do not consent, speak. The Sangha has designated this as a territory where one does not lose one's robes. The Sangha consents, by its silence. Let this matter be held thus."

At that time, the bhikṣus first established the robe-retention territory, then afterward established the great boundary. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The great boundary should be established first, and then the robe-retention territory should be established based on it."

The bhikṣus then kept the robe territory established at all times, wearing only coarse, shabby robes when going out. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! It should be dissolved by a jñapti-dvitīya-karman."

One bhikṣu announces: "Venerable Sangha, listen! In this established boundary area, within the village or village boundary, the robe-retention territory that was previously established, the Sangha now dissolves it. If the time is appropriate, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! In this established boundary area, and so forth up to: the Sangha now dissolves it. Those venerable elders who consent, remain silent. Those who do not consent, speak. The Sangha has dissolved the former robe-retention territory. The Sangha consents, by its silence. Let this matter be held thus."

At that time, the bhikṣus dissolved the great boundary first, and then dissolved the robe territory. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The robe territory should be dissolved first, and then the great boundary should be dissolved."

At that time, the bhikṣus, in trying to protect the rains retreat, lost their robes, and in trying to protect their robes, lost the rains retreat. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit re-establishing the robe-retention territory."

At that time, at one residence, on the uposatha day, a disciple took leave of his preceptor, wishing to travel to a certain place. The preceptor did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The preceptor should consider: if the road is doubtful and frightening, and the preceptor permits him to go, it is a duṣkṛta. If the preceptor does not permit him but the disciple goes by force, it is a pācittika for disrespecting the teacher. If the road has no doubts or fears, but in that region alms are difficult to obtain; or if the traveling companions are ignorant, do not recite the precepts, do not know the uposatha, and do not know the uposatha karman; or if in that region there is no one who upholds the Dharma, upholds the Vinaya, and understands the disciplinary rules; or if in that region there is much quarreling and contention; or if in that region there is a schism in the Sangha; or if in that region, should one fall ill, there is no suitable food, medicine, bedding, or attendant for the sick; or if in that region robes and food are difficult to obtain: if the preceptor permits him to go in any of these cases, it is a duṣkṛta. If none of these conditions exist, it is permitted to go, and there is no offense."

At one residence, on the fifteenth day, the bhikṣus assembled for the uposatha to recite the Prātimokṣa. They asked the first senior monk to recite the precepts. The senior monk said: "I have forgotten the recitation." The second and on down all said they could not recite, and the uposatha could not be performed. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "By a jñapti-dvitīya-karman, one bhikṣu should be appointed to go to another community to learn the precepts, whether in abbreviated or full form, and return the same day. If this cannot be done, one should not remain at this place; one who remains incurs a duṣkṛta."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus committed offenses and performed the uposatha without repenting. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. Those who do so incur a duṣkṛta!"

There were also bhikṣus who confessed their offenses to someone who had themselves committed offenses. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

At one residence, on the uposatha day, the entire Sangha had committed offenses. The bhikṣus did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit, by a jñapti-dvitīya-karman, appointing one bhikṣu to go to another community, confess, and return purified. The remaining bhikṣus should then confess to this bhikṣu. If this can be done, good. If not, all should assemble in the uposatha hall, and one bhikṣu should perform a jñapti-dvitīya-karman:

"'Venerable Sangha, listen! The Sangha presently all has this offense and is unable to confess. We now set it aside, and will confess later. If the time is appropriate, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus.'

"'Venerable Sangha, listen! The Sangha presently all has this offense, and so forth up to: will confess later. Those venerable elders who consent, remain silent. Those who do not consent, speak. The Sangha has set aside this offense. The Sangha consents, by its silence. Let this matter be held thus.'

"Then perform the uposatha. One must not fail to perform the uposatha!"

There was a sick bhikṣu who had committed an offense. He said to another bhikṣu: "Venerable one, I have committed an offense." The other replied: "I too have committed an offense." He could not confess and passed away. The bhikṣus had this thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit confessing to a bhikṣu who has an offense, then this bhikṣu would not have died without confessing." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit confessing to a bhikṣu who has an offense, but one may not confess to someone who has committed the same offense."

At one residence, the entire Sangha had committed the same offense. They did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should also be handled by setting aside the offense as above."

There was a sick bhikṣu who had committed an offense. He said to another bhikṣu: "Venerable one, I have committed this offense." The other said: "I too have committed this offense." He could not confess and passed away. The bhikṣus had this thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit confessing to a bhikṣu who has committed the same offense, then this bhikṣu would not have died without confessing." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now permit confessing to anyone, whether they have committed the same offense or a different offense."

At one residence, the bhikṣus assembled for the uposatha to recite the precepts. One bhikṣu said to the precept-reciting bhikṣu: "Stop! I remember I have an offense. I wish to confess." The bhikṣus reproached him: "How can you create such an obstruction during the precept recitation?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If during the precept recitation one remembers an offense, one is permitted to tell the bhikṣu sitting beside one, either by verbal declaration or by mental recollection: 'I have this offense; when the precept recitation is finished, I will confess.' One should not create an obstruction." The same applies to cases of doubt.

At one residence, the bhikṣus did not know the uposatha or the uposatha karman. A bhikṣu who knew the Dharma, upheld the Vinaya, and understood the disciplinary rules came. The bhikṣus did not attend to him or provide him with lodging, and that bhikṣu departed. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "He should be properly attended to and given lodging. If not, it is a duṣkṛta!"

At one residence, the entire Sangha had committed offenses but did not know under which category the offenses fell. A bhikṣu who knew the Dharma, upheld the Vinaya, and understood the disciplinary rules came. One bhikṣu from among them went and asked: "We have committed such-and-such offenses; under which category do they fall?" The visitor replied: "They fall under such-and-such a category." The questioner then told him: "The entire Sangha at this residence has committed such offenses." He then confessed to that bhikṣu. After confessing, he returned and told the other bhikṣus: "The offenses we previously committed fall under such-and-such a category. We should all confess together and not defile our practice of the holy life, suffering through the long night." The bhikṣus became angry and said: "Why do you say such things to us?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The bhikṣus should confess to that Dharma-knowing bhikṣu. It is not that one should not speak of it. But one should not speak carelessly to people. One who speaks carelessly incurs a duṣkṛta."

At that time, Śāriputra went to the residence of the group-of-six bhikṣus. Seeing their violations of the precepts, he said: "You should not commit these offenses!" The group-of-six bhikṣus then pestered and provoked him, asking: "What should be done? What should not be done?" They harassed him throughout the night. Śāriputra reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha reproached the group-of-six bhikṣus in various ways: "You foolish people! A senior bhikṣu compassionately instructs you, and yet you harass him throughout the night in return?" After the reproach, he told the bhikṣus: "It is not that one should not instruct, but one should not carelessly instruct people. If they have committed an offense, one should ascertain that they themselves recognize their fault, and only then instruct them."

At that time, Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana were traveling among the people. They were honored as teachers by the four assemblies, kings, great ministers, śramaṇas, and brāhmaṇas. They arrived at a bhikṣu's residence. The donors, for the sake of these two, made offerings to the Sangha, distributing robes and items, as well as to the garden keeper. They also collectively received a kambala blanket valued at twelve thousand gold coins. Three times it was announced: "Whoever needs it, take it." No one took it, and it was returned to the donors. The bhikṣus who had been at the gathering gradually traveled and returned to the Buddha's place. The Buddha, according to his custom, consoled and inquired of the bhikṣus, then asked: "Were Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana well provided for during their travels?" They answered: "Very well provided for!" "World-Honored One, there was also one remarkable event: the Sangha collectively received a kambala blanket valued at twelve thousand gold coins. It was offered three times to the bhikṣus, but no one took it, and it was returned to the donors." At that time, there were two mahallaka bhikṣus not far from the Buddha who heard this. One said: "Those senior monks are foolish! They lose offerings for themselves and prevent the donors from gaining great merit. If I had been there, I would have taken it." The other said: "I am the senior; I should take it!" They then fell into dispute. The Buddha, seeing this, then spoke a verse:

> "You two mahallakas
> were not present in that assembly.
> Because of this, there was no dispute,
> and the precious robe was returned to its owner."
