The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upāli and the Vinaya-holders were traveling and arrived at a bhikṣu's residence. For those who should receive the censure karman, the expulsion karman, the dependence karman, the accusation karman, or the submission karman, they performed all of these. For those who should receive probation, mānāpya, return to the beginning, or rehabilitation, they performed all of these. If a boundary should be dissolved, they dissolved it. If one should be established, they established it. If the karman for instructing bhikṣuṇīs should be performed, they performed it. If one was unfit for instruction, and the instruction karman should be dissolved, they dissolved it. The other bhikṣus heard and had this thought: "These Vinaya-holding bhikṣus have come; they will surely cause us much doubt and regret." They then put away the furnishings, closed the doors, and abandoned the residence, departing. Furthermore, the bhikṣuṇīs, upon seeing Upāli, angrily reviled him, saying: "Because of this bhikṣu constantly asking the World-Honored One whether this precept should be upheld by both assemblies or by one assembly, the Buddha then ordered it upheld by both assemblies. Because of this, we suffer much hardship." Among the bhikṣus traveling with Upāli, those who returned first were asked by the Buddha in the customary manner, and then the Buddha asked: "Were Upāli's offerings sufficient during his travels?" They answered: "They were not sufficient." He asked further: "Why?" They answered the Buddha in full, detailing how the bhikṣus abandoned the residences and how the bhikṣuṇīs angrily reviled him. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked those bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways: "You foolish ones! If you do not respect the Vinaya-holding bhikṣus, who should be respected?" After the reproach, he told the bhikṣus: "I now establish for the bhikṣus the first rule of conduct to be learned.

"When a bhikṣu hears that a Vinaya-holding bhikṣu is coming, he should not avoid and depart. He should sweep and clean, arrange the rooms, furnishings, and bedding. When he hears the Vinaya-holder is about to arrive, he should go out half a yojana to greet him. If there are doubtful or difficult matters, he must at least go outside the gate. If the Vinaya-holding bhikṣu has robes or belongings, one should carry them for him; prepare water for washing, a towel for wiping hands and feet, and bathing supplies; provide after-noon drinks; and invite him to expound the Dharma. If one sincerely seeks understanding, the Vinaya-holding bhikṣu should answer according to the Dharma. If one asks in a harassing manner, he should not answer. The next morning, provide the morning meal and dessert, and then the afternoon meal. One should seek a patron to invite and ask the Vinaya-holder to remain for the rains retreat. One should also seek a donor to offer robes. Such offerings should be made. If not, it is a duṣkṛta!"

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, the World-Honored One, on the uposatha day, sat in the open surrounded by bhikṣus before and behind, and told the bhikṣus: "Be silent! Now the uposatha shall be performed, and the Prātimokṣa shall be recited."

A bhikṣu rose from his seat and said to the Buddha: "The bhikṣu Gāgā has recently developed a mental illness. Sometimes he comes, sometimes he does not come. He also does not remember whether he came or did not come, and because of this, Sangha business is obstructed. He has not come now!" The Buddha said: "Send a bhikṣu to call him." The bhikṣu received the instruction, went to call him, searched everywhere but could not find him, and returned to report this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now permit the bhikṣus to perform, at a distance, a jñapti-dvitīya-karman declaring him insane."

One bhikṣu announces: "Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so has a mental illness. Sometimes he comes, sometimes he does not come. He also does not remember whether he came or did not come, and because of this, Sangha business is obstructed. The Sangha now performs, at a distance, the insanity karman for so-and-so, so that whether he is present or not present, Sangha business may proceed. If the time is appropriate, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so has a mental illness, and so forth up to: whether he is not present, Sangha business may proceed. Those venerable elders who consent, remain silent. Those who do not consent, speak. The Sangha has performed the insanity karman for so-and-so. The Sangha consents, by its silence. Let this matter be held thus."

When he later recovered, he sought to have the karman revoked. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit revoking it by a jñapti-dvitīya-karman."

The recovered bhikṣu should come before the Sangha, bare his right shoulder, remove his leather sandals, kneel on both knees with palms joined, and say: "Venerable Sangha, listen! I, bhikṣu so-and-so, previously had a mental illness. Sometimes I came, sometimes I did not come. I also did not remember whether I came or did not come. The Sangha performed the insanity karman for me. I have now recovered and request the Sangha to revoke the insanity karman." He requests thus three times.

One bhikṣu should announce: "Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so was previously insane. Sometimes he came, sometimes he did not come. He also did not remember whether he came or did not come. The Sangha performed the insanity karman for him. He has now recovered and requests the Sangha to revoke the insanity karman. The Sangha now revokes the insanity karman. If the time is appropriate, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so previously had a mental illness, and so forth up to: the Sangha now revokes the insanity karman. Those venerable elders who consent, remain silent. Those who do not consent, speak. The Sangha has revoked the insanity karman for bhikṣu so-and-so. The Sangha consents, by its silence. Let this matter be held thus."

At that time, the World-Honored One, on the precept-recitation day, sat in the open surrounded by bhikṣus before and behind, and told the bhikṣus: "Be silent! The uposatha shall now be performed and the precepts recited."

A bhikṣu rose from his seat and said to the Buddha: "Bhikṣu so-and-so is ill and cannot come." The Buddha said: "One bhikṣu should be sent to bring him." The bhikṣus did not know how to bring him. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Instruct him to use a walking stick, or have someone support him. If he cannot manage, carry him on a cloth." They received the instruction and carried him. From the exertion, his illness worsened, and some even died. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "His declaration of purity should be obtained and brought."

Herein, some may properly be said to give a declaration of purity, and some may not. Some may properly be said to receive a declaration of purity, and some may not. Some may properly be said to bring a declaration of purity, and some may not.

Those who cannot properly be said to give a declaration of purity: if one gives the declaration to a bhikṣuṇī, a śikṣamāṇā, a śrāmaṇera, or a śrāmaṇerī; if one gives it to someone with a deranged mind, a distracted mind, a mind ruined by illness, a person who has been expelled, a person who has been suspended, a person who has confessed an offense, or a person residing in a different boundary; if one does not properly make the three declarations: "I now give you my declaration of purity. You receive my declaration of purity. At the lawful Sangha proceedings, announce my name and cast the counting stick for me." In all these cases, it cannot properly be called giving a declaration of purity. The opposite of the above constitutes properly giving a declaration of purity.

Those who cannot properly be said to receive a declaration of purity: if one is oneself not in accordance with the Dharma, or if one does not know the other's name; the rest is as above. In all these cases, it cannot properly be called receiving a declaration of purity. The opposite constitutes properly receiving a declaration of purity.

Those who can properly be said to bring a declaration of purity: if the bhikṣu carrying the declaration of purity arrives at the uposatha assembly but then falls asleep, or becomes deranged, or mentally distracted, or has his mind ruined by illness; or if the Sangha performs against him the karman for not acknowledging an offense, the karman for not abandoning wrong views, or the karman for not repenting an offense; or if he transforms into a person of dual sex, a eunuch, or a sexless person; or if he forgets to announce it. In all these cases, it can still properly be called bringing a declaration of purity. However, if he falls asleep or forgets to announce it, he incurs a duṣkṛta offense. If along the way he falls asleep, or any of the above occurs up to forgetting to announce it, none of these can properly be called bringing a declaration of purity.

There were also sick bhikṣus who could not verbally declare their purity. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The declaration of purity should be given by bodily gesture." The bhikṣus did not know how to give the declaration of purity by bodily gesture. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "By raising the hand, raising a finger, moving the body, nodding the head, or even raising the eyes, it can properly be called giving the declaration of purity by bodily gesture."

There were also sick bhikṣus who could not give the declaration of purity even by bodily gesture. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The entire assembly should go to where the sick person is. The precept-reciting bhikṣu should sit in the center and recite the precepts, and all the sick bhikṣus should be made to face the precept-reciting bhikṣu."

There were also sick bhikṣus who could not face the precept-reciting bhikṣu and lay or sat with their backs to him. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The uposatha should be performed outside the boundary. One must not perform the uposatha as a separate assembly within the boundary."

At that time, the World-Honored One, on the precept-recitation day, sat in the open surrounded by bhikṣus before and behind, and told the bhikṣus: "Be silent! The uposatha shall now be performed and the precepts recited."

A bhikṣu rose from his seat and said to the Buddha: "Bhikṣu so-and-so has been seized by officials and cannot come." The Buddha said: "A bhikṣu should be sent to speak to the authorities and request that he be released for the uposatha. If they permit it, good. If they do not permit it, the bhikṣu should tell the lay people to step aside briefly and obtain the declaration of purity from him. If this can be done, good. If not, he should say: 'The entire Sangha will come and perform the uposatha with this bhikṣu.' If this is permitted, good. If not, the uposatha should be performed outside the boundary. One must not perform the uposatha as a separate assembly within the boundary."

At a certain forest dwelling, the bhikṣus assembled on the fifteenth to perform the uposatha and recite the precepts. Bandits arrived. The bhikṣus, upon seeing them, stopped and did not recite the precepts. The bandits asked: "Why are you silent?" They answered: "What we recite should not be heard by lay people." The bandits asked again: "Is what you recite not the words of the Buddha?" They answered: "It is." The bandits asked further: "If these are the words of the Buddha, who should not hear them? You have gathered here; you must be plotting something unfavorable against us!" They then beat the bhikṣus and seized their robes and bowls. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "From now on, if bhikṣus see bandits coming, they should immediately recite another sūtra, without allowing there to be a break."

At that time, there was a king named Udayana who was skilled in the science of physiognomy. He had a consort named Candraprabhā, who was beautiful in appearance and excelled in music and dance. Later, upon a high pavilion, she danced before the king. The king perceived the sign of death, that she would not survive beyond one year. He gazed at her intently, his mind reflecting, his expression displeased. The consort noticed and said to the king: "Is my dancing not good? Why are you displeased?" The king said: "Do not ask me!" The consort pressed him up to three times. The king, unable to avoid it any longer, told her everything. The consort said to the king: "If that is truly so, I wish to be permitted to go forth into the homeless life!" The king said: "We are bound by love and respect; even in death we would not part. With so few days of joy remaining, how can we be separated in life?" The consort again said to the king: "Slight attachment to worldly glory obscures the work of the path. In this brief span, there is only the meeting with suffering. I beg you to have compassion and permit me to go forth!" The king said: "You are young and devoted to the path; your insight is keen and clear. You will surely attain rebirth in heaven. If you return to see me, I will fulfill your wish." The consort said: "If this wish is fulfilled, I vow not to break the promise!" He then permitted her to go forth. She departed, and not long after practicing the path, she attained the fruit of anāgāmin. She then passed away and was reborn in the Brahmā heaven. She then had this thought: "I was able to go forth thanks to the king's kindness. Such great kindness should be repaid, and a vow must not be broken." She immediately descended to above the king's palace and stood in the air, saying to the king: "The consort Candraprabhā is this very person. As I previously vowed to the king, I have come to fulfill my promise." The king said: "I do not recognize celestial beings. Please show your former appearance." She then transformed into her previous form and stood before the king. The king, overcome with deep emotion, wished to approach her intimately. She then flew up into the sky and said to the king: "Why does the king still indulge in these sensual desires? Desires are impermanent, suffering, empty, and impure. If you contemplate this truth, you can attain liberation. If not, you will surely fall into the three evil destinies, and self-rescue will be most difficult!" Hearing these words, the king's mind became gentle and pliant. He immediately abdicated the throne and entrusted it to the crown prince, went forth to practice the path, and dwelt among the mountains, forests, and under trees near the city. The crown prince, seeing his father had gone forth but had not gone far, constantly feared he would change his mind and reclaim the throne, always wishing his father would go far away to another country. At that time, the bhikṣu-king had this thought: "I follow the Buddha's teachings but have not yet seen the Buddha. I should now go and pay respect to the World-Honored One." Having resolved this, he set off. At that time, the crown prince was on a high tower with his brāhmaṇa advisors and saw him leaving the forest. They told the new king: "The bhikṣu-king has now departed!" The crown prince was delighted. The bhikṣu-king had forgotten his sitting cloth. Shortly afterward, he remembered and turned back to retrieve it. The brāhmaṇas told the new king: "The bhikṣu-king has returned. Perhaps there is a reason!" The crown prince became terrified and ordered his attendants: "Go quickly and kill him! Kill all śramaṇas, sons of the Śākya, as well!" He further ordered: "They may fear death and use many stratagems. Be sure not to fail to kill him and then come to see me!" The men received the order and went, saying: "The new king has ordered us to kill you, bhikṣu!" The bhikṣu asked: "Why are you killing me?" They answered: "Because the bhikṣu left the forest and returned, and the king fears you will reclaim the throne. That is why you are to be killed." The bhikṣu-king said: "I do not covet the throne. I forgot my sitting cloth and just came back to retrieve it. How can I be killed for this?" The messengers said: "The king gave strict orders: 'They will certainly fear death and use many stratagems. Be sure not to fail to kill him and return to me!' How can we return empty-handed?" The bhikṣu-king said further: "What I sought in going forth I have not yet attained. Give me a brief respite, until the shadow of that tree reaches here." The messengers agreed. He then strenuously contemplated and attained the fruit of srotāpanna. When the tree's shadow reached the spot, the messengers told him the shadow had arrived. The bhikṣu said further: "What I sought in going forth I have not yet fully attained. Please grant me more time, until the next shadow." The messengers again agreed. In this way, four times he attained the four fruits of the śramaṇa path. He then told the messengers: "You may do as you wish. Go tell your king: I do not covet the throne; I merely forgot my sitting cloth and came back for it. By killing me for this, you will have killed your father and killed an arhat. I only grieve that you will suffer greatly through the long night!" Having spoken, he submitted to death. The messengers killed him and returned to the king. The king, seeing them from afar, immediately felt remorse. When they arrived, he asked: "Have you killed him?" They answered: "We have killed him!" The king asked further: "What did my father say before his death?" The messengers, choking with sobs, answered in full as above. Hearing these words, blood gushed from the king's mouth, and he descended alive into the great hell.

At that time, King Bimbisāra and the neighboring kingdoms, having previously heard the order to kill all śramaṇas, sons of the Śākya, feared they would enter his own territory. He ordered the guards to protect the bhikṣus, and the guards, following orders, never left their side.

At that time, at one residence in Rājagṛha, there were five hundred bhikṣus who assembled on the fifteenth and told the guards to step aside briefly: "We wish to perform the uposatha." The guards replied: "We have received orders not to leave even briefly. How could we publicly violate the great king's command?" The bhikṣus did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If they will not leave, just recite the preface to the precepts and conclude by saying: 'The rest is as the Sangha always hears.' The uposatha should be performed in this way. One must not fail to perform the uposatha."

At that time, the bhikṣus assembled on the uposatha day intending to recite the precepts, but the group-of-six bhikṣus quarreled incessantly and the precepts could not be recited. When King Bimbisāra came, they temporarily stopped; after he left, they continued fighting and the precepts could not be recited. Not knowing what to do, this was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If there is someone like King Bimbisāra before whom the bhikṣus feel apprehension and the quarreling temporarily stops, then the preface to the precepts should be recited. When the preface is finished, say: 'The rest is as the Sangha always hears

I notice that the prompt mentions Fascicle 卷第十八 (37 of 61) but then says "Fascicle 019: 五分律" with no Chinese text provided between the delimiter lines. There is no Chinese text to translate.

If you could provide the actual Chinese text of the fascicle, I would be happy to translate it in full. Please paste the Chinese source text and I will render a complete translation following all the specified rules.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421, Fascicle 19

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

---

## Third Division, Part Three: The Rules for the Rains Retreat (Varṣā)

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the bhikṣus wandered about at all times, in spring, summer, and winter, trampling and killing insects and grass. Carrying heavy robes and belongings, they became weary and exhausted on the roads. Laypeople who saw this criticized and reproached them, saying: "Even the various non-Buddhist śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas know the three seasons and settle in retreat during the summer. Even birds make nests and dens and dwell within them. Yet these bhikṣus do not know the three seasons, when they should travel and when they should not. They constantly speak of having few desires, of compassion, sympathy, and care for sentient beings, yet now they trample and crush without any feeling of benevolence or pity. They lack the conduct of śramaṇas and destroy the Dharma of śramaṇas!"

The senior bhikṣus heard this and reproached them in various ways, then reported the matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the bhikṣus: "Is it true that you do this?" They answered: "It is true, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "You should not wander about at all times. Those who violate this commit a duṣkṛta. From now on, I permit the summer rains retreat."

The method for undertaking the rains retreat: one should bare the right shoulder, remove one's leather sandals, kneel with palms joined, and address one bhikṣu, saying: "Venerable one, with single-minded attention! I, bhikṣu so-and-so, at this dwelling place undertake the summer rains retreat, the first three months, depending on such-and-such a village, such-and-such a room. If the room is damaged, I shall repair it." This is said three times. The reply is: "I acknowledge it."

The bhikṣus then undertook the rains retreat day after day, or once every two days up to every five days. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should not be done like that. On the upoṣadha day at the end of spring, rooms and bedding should be distributed, and the rains retreat should be undertaken on the first day of summer."

There were bhikṣus who wished to undertake the rains retreat depending on being under an elephant, or depending on a carriage or palanquin. There were also bhikṣus who wished to undertake the rains retreat depending on an overturned bowl. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "None of this should be done; those who violate this commit a duṣkṛta. I permit undertaking the rains retreat in a place where one can sit in full lotus posture, with one's robes and bowl, in a place not reached by rain leaks. Depend on such a place for the rains retreat."

There were bhikṣus who undertook the rains retreat in an unprotected place and were robbed by bandits. There were also bhikṣus who undertook the rains retreat in a charnel ground and were harassed by non-human beings. There were also bhikṣus who undertook the rains retreat in hollow trees and were afflicted by poisonous creatures. There were also bhikṣus who undertook the rains retreat in a hut covered with hides, and flesh grew inside their noses. There were also bhikṣus who undertook the rains retreat in the open, and their skin peeled off. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "None of this should be done!"

At that time, laypeople invited bhikṣus to undertake the rains retreat in an unprotected place, saying: "Venerable ones! Please undertake the rains retreat there; we will provide protection from afar." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to accept."

There was also a charnel-ground bhikṣu who was troubled that in the midst of people there were no rooms or bedding, and wished to return to the charnel ground for the rains retreat. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If he is able to keep mindfulness at the forefront and has no fear, it is permitted."

There were also bhikṣus who wished to prepare and protect a tree hollow and undertake the rains retreat inside it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted. One should first throw stones at the tree, or strike it with a stick, and listen for what sounds there are and what creatures come out. If there are no unusual sounds and no creatures come out, then one may enter. One should seal up and plaster the top so that one can stand upright, make an earthen platform, plaster the ground on all sides, and install a door with an opening and closing mechanism."

At that time, the Nāga King Anavatapta invited the bhikṣus to undertake the rains retreat in five hundred caverns of gold, silver, and various treasures in his palace. The bhikṣus did not dare go. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit them to go."

The bhikṣus wished to make stairways, place sitting stones, and washing stones for feet, but all of these were of gold and silver, and they were embarrassed and did not dare. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The gold and silver there are like earth and stone here; use them as you wish."

There were also bhikṣus in the rains retreat who encountered danger from bandits, danger from the king, or danger from relatives. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should leave to avoid it and undertake the rains retreat elsewhere. There are two kinds of rains retreat: the first rains retreat and the latter rains retreat. If there is no impediment, one should undertake the first rains retreat. If there is an impediment, it is permitted to undertake the latter rains retreat."

Bhikṣus of the latter rains retreat went to another place, but the bhikṣus there would not give them rooms or bedding. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should give them."

Having given them, the newcomers would not stay there but seized the rooms of other residents. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should stay according to what they receive. When bhikṣus are about to undertake the rains retreat, they should first consider whether there is danger or no danger at that place. If there is no danger, they should stay; if there is danger, they should leave."

At that time in Śrāvastī, there was a householder named Udayana who had faith in and delighted in the Buddha's Dharma and constantly made offerings to the bhikṣus. During the rains retreat, he built a house for the Sangha and prepared a housewarming meal, wishing to donate the house at the same time. He invited the bhikṣus of the surrounding dwelling places. The bhikṣus were embarrassed and did not accept. The householder then criticized and reproached them, saying: "I have spent my wealth and prepared this food and drink, yet the bhikṣus refuse to accept my invitation!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to accept. If one is building a bhikṣuṇī's dwelling or a non-Buddhist's dwelling, or even building steps and stairways, providing meals, and being invited to accept gifts, all are permitted. Whether there is an invitation or no invitation, if one needs to go outside the boundary, in all cases it is permitted to go and return within seven days."

There was a bhikṣu who did not know the Vinaya himself and did not depend on a Vinaya-holder. During the rains retreat he developed doubts and thought: "The World-Honored One does not permit me to travel during the rains retreat. I have nowhere to ask, and I do not know what to do." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to undertake the rains retreat near a place where there is a Vinaya-holding bhikṣu. If the dwelling of the Vinaya-holder has cramped and narrow rooms, it is permitted to stay at a place close to the Vinaya-holder from which one can go and return within seven days, and there, with mental determination, depend on the Vinaya-holder from afar for the rains retreat."

There was a bhikṣu who, after rooms and bedding had been distributed, did not make the thought: "I now undertake the rains retreat," nor did he say it aloud. Afterward, he had doubts and regret: "I did not formally undertake the rains retreat. Does my rains retreat count or not?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Having received a room and prepared bedding for the purpose of the rains retreat, even though one did not form the mental intention or verbally declare the undertaking, it is still called a rains retreat."

At that time, the people of Śrāvastī wished to dig a channel to bring water through the Jetavana. King Prasenajit heard of this and issued an order: "If anyone cuts a water channel through the Jetavana, they shall receive a severe punishment." Later, there was trouble at the border, and the king himself went out on campaign. Afterward, the non-Buddhists wished to join forces to dig the channel. The bhikṣus told the upāsakas about this. The upāsakas said: "This is not something we can control. You should go and inform the king." The bhikṣus said: "The World-Honored One does not permit going beyond seven days' travel and return during the rains retreat. The king is far from here; how could we go?" They then reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told them: "From now on, if there is business of the Buddha, the Dharma, or the Sangha, or personal business, beyond the seven days, I further permit a motion-and-one-proclamation procedure to receive fifteen days or one month to travel outside the boundary."

One bhikṣu proclaims: "May the noble Sangha listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so, for such-and-such business, wishes to travel outside the boundary. Beyond the seven days, he further receives thirty nights, and will return to this rains retreat. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha approve. The motion is thus."

"May the noble Sangha listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so, for such-and-such business, wishes to travel outside the boundary. Beyond the seven days, he further receives thirty nights, and will return to this rains retreat. Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has granted bhikṣu so-and-so the further reception of thirty nights to travel outside the boundary. The Sangha approves, by its silence. The matter is thus held."

There was a bhikṣu in the rains retreat whose coarse food was insufficient. He thought: "I am in the rains retreat here and my coarse food is insufficient. Yet the World-Honored One does not permit breaking the rains retreat. What should I do?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted that for this reason, breaking the rains retreat is without offense."

There was also a bhikṣu in the rains retreat whom a bhikṣuṇī was enticing to commit an impure act. He thought: "The human mind is easily swayed. Later I may lose my resolve. Yet the World-Honored One does not permit breaking the rains retreat. What should I do?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted that for this reason, breaking the rains retreat is without offense." The same applies for a śikṣamāṇā and so on, up to a paṇḍaka. If a king wishes to destroy his celibate practice, the same applies up to parents and relatives.

There was a bhikṣu in the rains retreat who saw a hidden treasure. He thought: "This treasure would be sufficient for my entire lifetime. If I stay here long, I may lose my resolve. Yet the World-Honored One does not permit breaking the rains retreat. What should I do?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted that for this reason, breaking the rains retreat is without offense." If one sees a king or a person of high status, and so on up to seeing the suffering or happiness of one's parents and relatives, and fears losing one's resolve in the path, the same applies.

There was a bhikṣu in the rains retreat who heard that a bhikṣu wished to split the Sangha. He thought: "If there is an event of splitting the Sangha, the Sangha will not be in harmony, and one cannot dwell in peace. Yet the World-Honored One does not permit breaking the rains retreat. What should I do?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted that for this reason, breaking the rains retreat is without offense."

There was also a bhikṣu in the rains retreat who heard that at another dwelling place a bhikṣu wished to split the Sangha, and that bhikṣu was his close friend. He thought: "If I go and admonish him, he will surely accept my words. Yet the World-Honored One does not permit breaking the rains retreat. What should I do?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted that for this reason, breaking the rains retreat is without offense." If one can send someone to admonish, one goes for that purpose. If the Sangha there has already split and one is able to reunify it oneself, or send someone to reunify it, going for these purposes is likewise permissible. If a bhikṣuṇī can reunify the Sangha, the same applies.

At that time, there was a merchant caravan encampment, and bhikṣus wished to depend on it for the rains retreat. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to depend on it."

That merchant caravan suddenly departed during the rains retreat. The bhikṣus did not know what to do, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to follow them."

The merchants split into two groups. The bhikṣus did not know what to do, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one group has faith and joy, and their destination is prosperous and comfortable, follow them. If the bhikṣus have a Vinaya-holder and the other place also has many Vinaya-holders, it is permitted to follow that group." The same applies for depending on cow herders, sheep herders, raft-builders, or boat-travelers for the rains retreat.

There were bhikṣus whose rooms and bedding burned down during the rains retreat, leaving them no place to stay. Not knowing what to do, they reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Whether destroyed by fire, swept away by flood, danger from the king, danger from bandits, danger from non-human beings, danger from lions, tigers, and wolves, danger from various poisonous creatures, and even up to danger from ants or water lice, in all cases it is permitted to break the rains retreat without offense."

At that time, Upananda accepted an invitation for the rains retreat. After the upoṣadha was finished, he set out on the road and saw two dwelling places with abundant offerings of robes and food. He then stayed at each of the two places, half the time at each, wishing to take a share from both. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked Upananda: "Is it true that you did this?" He answered: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached him in various ways, saying: "You foolish man! How could you, having already accepted another's invitation, stay at two places for the rains retreat for the sake of material gain?" He told the bhikṣus:

"From now on, if a bhikṣu accepts another's invitation for the first rains retreat and, after the upoṣadha, sets out on the road and sees two dwelling places with abundant offerings of robes and food and stays there, he has neither a first nor a latter rains retreat, and incurs a duṣkṛta offense for breaking his word. If a bhikṣu accepts another's invitation for the first rains retreat and, after the upoṣadha, sets out but does not arrive, and by the dawn of the seventeenth day he has not arrived, that bhikṣu has no first rains retreat but has a latter rains retreat, his rains retreat is not broken, and he incurs an offense for breaking his word. If a bhikṣu accepts another's invitation for the first rains retreat and, after the upoṣadha, goes and undertakes the rains retreat, but does not receive the seven-day leave and goes outside the boundary, whether he returns within seven days or not, that bhikṣu has no first rains retreat but has a latter rains retreat, his rains retreat is not broken, and he incurs an offense for breaking his word. If a bhikṣu accepts another's invitation for the first rains retreat and, after the upoṣadha, goes and undertakes the rains retreat, receives the seven-day leave and goes outside the boundary but does not return, he has no first rains retreat but has a latter rains retreat, his rains retreat is not broken, and he incurs an offense for breaking his word. If he returns within seven days, his rains retreat is not broken, and he does not incur an offense for breaking his word. If a bhikṣu accepts another's invitation for the first rains retreat and, after the upoṣadha, goes and undertakes the rains retreat, and within the seven days before the pravāraṇā, without the seven-day procedure he goes outside the boundary, he has no first rains retreat but has a latter rains retreat, his rains retreat is not broken, and he incurs an offense for breaking his word. If he has the seven-day procedure and goes outside the boundary, his rains retreat is not broken, and he does not incur an offense for breaking his word. If a bhikṣu accepts another's invitation for the first rains retreat and goes there for the upoṣadha, the same applies.

"If a bhikṣu accepts another's invitation for the latter rains retreat and, after the upoṣadha, sets out on the road and sees two dwelling places with abundant offerings of robes and food and stays there without going, he incurs two duṣkṛta offenses: for breaking the rains retreat and for breaking his word. If he accepts another's invitation for the latter rains retreat and, after the upoṣadha, sets out but does not arrive, and by the dawn of the seventeenth day he has not arrived, that bhikṣu incurs two offenses: for breaking the rains retreat and for breaking his word. If he accepts another's invitation for the latter rains retreat and, after the upoṣadha, goes and undertakes the rains retreat, but does not receive the seven-day leave and goes outside the boundary, whether he returns within seven days or not, or receives the seven-day leave but does not return within seven days, in all cases he incurs two offenses: for breaking the rains retreat and for breaking his word. If he returns within seven days, his rains retreat is not broken, and he does not incur an offense for breaking his word. If he accepts another's invitation for the latter rains retreat and, after the upoṣadha, goes and undertakes the rains retreat, and within the seven days before the pravāraṇā, without the seven-day procedure he goes outside the boundary, he incurs two offenses: for breaking the rains retreat and for breaking his word. If he has the seven-day procedure and goes outside the boundary, his rains retreat is not broken, and he does not incur an offense for breaking his word. If a bhikṣu accepts another's invitation for the latter rains retreat and goes there for the upoṣadha, the same applies."

There was a bhikṣu looking for a place for the rains retreat who saw an empty cave and thought: "I shall undertake the rains retreat here." There were also numerous bhikṣus who saw it and all thought the same, without knowing of each other. On the upoṣadha day before the rains retreat, they all gathered there and each said: "I already claimed this cave first." Not knowing who should get to stay, they reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Whoever arrived first should make a mark: either write one's name on the wall, or tell the people around the cave, so that later there is a witness. That person should get to stay."

There was also a bhikṣu who claimed a dwelling place first but ultimately never came; other bhikṣus did not dare to stay, and the place was left empty. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The mark should be removed, or people should be told so that they know, so that other bhikṣus can stay there."

---

## Third Division, Part Four: The Rules for Pravāraṇā (Invitation to Admonish)

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, many bhikṣus stayed together in one place for the rains retreat and discussed among themselves: "If we speak to one another, it may lead to additions and subtractions. Let us make a rule together that there shall be no more speaking. Whoever returns first from begging food shall sweep and sprinkle the eating area, fill a vessel with water, set out hand-and-foot wiping cloths, spread out the sitting mats, and place containers for surplus food. If there is surplus food measured out, put the remainder in the container. If one obtains little, take enough from it. After eating, take turns clearing and putting away things. If one cannot do it alone, call for a companion to help. By doing this during the rains retreat, we shall dwell in peace and happiness, without troubles of right and wrong or additions and subtractions." Having made this agreement, they immediately put it into practice.

When the rains retreat was finished, as is the constant practice of the Buddhas, there are two great assemblies each year. They went to the Buddha's presence, bowed their heads at his feet, and sat to one side. The Buddha consoled and asked them: "Did you dwell in harmony during the rains retreat? Was begging food not lacking? Were you not weary from the road?" They answered: "We dwelt in harmony during the rains retreat. Begging food was not lacking. We were not weary from the road." He further asked: "How did you dwell in harmony during the rains retreat?" The bhikṣus then answered in full detail. The Buddha reproached them in various ways: "You foolish ones! You dwelt together like enemies! How could you obtain harmony and happiness? With countless skillful means I have taught you that when dwelling together, you should instruct and guide one another, mutually awakening each other, to exhaust the work of the path. How is it that you now practice the mute method? From now on, if anyone again establishes a rule of not speaking to each other, it is a duṣkṛta offense."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus repeatedly committed offenses. The bhikṣus, in accordance with the Buddha's teaching that they should mutually instruct and admonish, spoke to them, saying: "You repeatedly commit offenses. You should see your own faults and reform and repent. Do not defile the pure conduct, bringing great suffering upon yourselves, betraying others' faithful offerings, and gaining nothing." The group-of-six bhikṣus did not reform their faults but instead falsely accused the senior bhikṣus, saying: "The senior bhikṣus commit various offenses." Those seniors, hearing this, were embarrassed and went to the Buddha to report the matter. The Buddha, on account of this, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Is it true that you did this?" They answered: "It is true, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "If there is a bhikṣu who has committed an offense, one should first ask: 'I wish to admonish you; will you listen to me or not?' If he says he will listen, then admonish him. If he does not listen, then stop. If he does not listen, he commits a duṣkṛta offense."

The group-of-six bhikṣus later committed offenses and preemptively asked the senior bhikṣus: "I wish to admonish you; will you listen to me or not?" Those seniors thought: "The Buddha has decreed that one must not refuse to listen." So they answered: "Speak as you wish." The group-of-six bhikṣus then said: "If you follow my wishes, then follow me. What offense shall I speak of? When shall I speak? Where shall I speak?" Hearing these words, the seniors followed after them, not daring to go far away. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha again asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Is it true that you did this?" They answered: "It is true, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha reproached them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus:

"If one possesses five qualities, one should not ask permission to speak of offenses: being without a sense of shame, being foolish, being of little learning, being oneself not in accordance with the Dharma, and being intent on exposing others' faults. If one has a sense of shame, is learned, is wise, is oneself in accordance with the Dharma, and truly wishes to help others abandon evil, then one may ask permission to speak of offenses.

"There are another five qualities for which one should not ask permission to speak of offenses: being partial due to desire, being partial due to anger, being partial due to delusion, being partial due to fear, and not knowing proper and improper timing. The opposites of these are qualities for which one may ask permission to speak of offenses.

"If one possesses the five unwholesome qualities and asks, one should not respectfully listen to the speaking of offenses. If one possesses the five wholesome qualities and asks, one should respectfully listen to the speaking of offenses."

At that time, the bhikṣus discussed: "As the World-Honored One has said: one should ask permission to listen, or should not ask permission to listen; one should respectfully listen, or should not respectfully listen. Only an arhat would be qualified to ask. How can we carry this out?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the bhikṣus: "Is it true that you discussed this?" They answered: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "From now on, for ten benefits, I establish the pravāraṇā procedure for the bhikṣus. One should invite the Sangha to admonish and speak of offenses, saying:

"'Venerable ones! If you see my offense, if you hear of my offense, if you suspect my offense, out of compassion, please freely speak of it. I shall, upon seeing the offense, repent.' This is said three times."

At that time, the bhikṣus thought: "The World-Honored One has taught us the pravāraṇā; we should all practice it together." They then performed the pravāraṇā every day, or once every two or three days up to every five days. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should not be done like that. The pravāraṇā should be performed on the last day of the three months of summer."

The bhikṣus then performed the pravāraṇā in the presence of bhikṣuṇīs, śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, śrāmaṇerīs, laypeople, non-Buddhists, those of deranged mind, those of confused mind, those of mind impaired by illness, those who had been suspended, those who had been expelled, and those of different views. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should not be done like that. The pravāraṇā should be performed within an assembly of bhikṣus who are in accordance with the Dharma."

There were bhikṣus who performed the pravāraṇā sitting on beds. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should not be done like that."

When the bhikṣus got down on the ground for the pravāraṇā, their robes became soiled. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The ground should be well-plastered, and grass should be spread on top for the pravāraṇā."

The group-of-six bhikṣus said: "When it is my turn, only then shall I get down on the ground." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should not be done like that. One bhikṣu should first proclaim:

"'May the noble Sangha listen! Now the time for the Sangha's pravāraṇā has arrived. The Sangha should perform the pravāraṇā in harmony. The motion is thus.'

"Then all should get down on the ground together and kneel for the pravāraṇā."

While the bhikṣus' pravāraṇā was not yet finished, the senior monks, being old and ill, could not endure kneeling for a long time. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted that those who have finished their pravāraṇā may return to their seats."

Bhikṣus who had already finished their pravāraṇā then left. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not leave first; one must wait until everything is completely finished."

The bhikṣus all performed the pravāraṇā toward the senior monk at the same time, so that it was not known who had already performed the pravāraṇā and who had not yet done so. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The pravāraṇā should not be done all at once."

The bhikṣus then performed the pravāraṇā one by one starting from the senior monk. Laypeople who wished to make offerings or hear the Dharma waited a long time and could not obtain it. They then criticized and reproached: "We have many affairs and have left our work to come here, yet the bhikṣus do not accept our offerings in a timely manner or teach us the Dharma." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The pravāraṇā should not be done one by one. It is permitted that the top eight senior monks perform the pravāraṇā one by one. Below them, those of the same ordination year should perform the pravāraṇā together at the same time."

The bhikṣus did not know how far the pravāraṇā had proceeded. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A motion-and-one-proclamation procedure should be used to appoint pravāraṇā attendants, either two or more."

One bhikṣu proclaims: "May the noble Sangha listen! These bhikṣus so-and-so and so-and-so are able to serve as pravāraṇā attendants for the Sangha. The Sangha now appoints so-and-so and so-and-so as pravāraṇā attendants. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha approve. The motion is thus."

"May the noble Sangha listen! These bhikṣus so-and-so and so-and-so are able to serve as pravāraṇā attendants for the Sangha. The Sangha now appoints so-and-so and so-and-so as pravāraṇā attendants. Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has appointed so-and-so and so-and-so as pravāraṇā attendants. The Sangha approves, by its silence. The matter is thus held."

The bhikṣus appointed an unwise bhikṣu as pravāraṇā attendant. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One who possesses five qualities should not be appointed: being partial due to desire, anger, delusion, or fear, or not knowing proper and improper timing. One who possesses the opposite qualities should be appointed."

The appointed bhikṣu should rise and tell the bhikṣus: "Those of the same ordination year, sit together in one place."

The pravāraṇā attendant did not know when he himself should perform the pravāraṇā. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "When the sequence reaches him, he should perform the pravāraṇā."

Even with the bhikṣus performing the pravāraṇā in this manner, it was still slow, and laypeople criticized as before. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The appointed person should further proclaim: 'Each of you, facing one another, perform the pravāraṇā.'"

After the bhikṣus finished the pravāraṇā, they additionally performed the upoṣadha. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The pravāraṇā procedure is also called the upoṣadha."

At that time, the World-Honored One, on the pravāraṇā day, sat in the open surrounded by bhikṣus before and behind him, and told the bhikṣus: "Now the time for the Sangha to perform the pravāraṇā in harmony has arrived. You should together perform the pravāraṇā." A bhikṣu rose and said to the Buddha: "There is a sick bhikṣu who has not come." The Buddha said: "A bhikṣu should be appointed to bring him." And so on up to performing the pravāraṇā outside the boundary, as explained in the section on reciting the precepts.

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus performed the pravāraṇā while having offenses. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should not be done like that; those who violate this commit a duṣkṛta!"

They still performed the pravāraṇā while having offenses. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Their pravāraṇā should be stopped!"

The bhikṣus stopped others' pravāraṇā before the procedural act, and there were also those who stopped it after the pravāraṇā was finished. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should not be done like that. After the procedural act is complete but before the pravāraṇā, that is when it should be stopped."

He told the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of illegitimate stopping of pravāraṇā, and four kinds of legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā. What are the four kinds of illegitimate stopping of pravāraṇā? Stopping based on groundless breach of precepts, groundless breach of views, groundless breach of deportment, and groundless breach of right livelihood. The opposites are the four kinds of legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā.

"There are also seven kinds of illegitimate stopping of pravāraṇā and seven kinds of legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā. What are the seven kinds of illegitimate stopping of pravāraṇā? Stopping based on a groundless pārājika, a groundless saṃghāvaśeṣa, a groundless sthūlātyaya, a groundless pāyattika, a groundless pratideśanīya, a groundless duṣkṛta, and groundless wrong speech. The opposites are the seven kinds of legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā.

"There are also eight kinds of illegitimate stopping of pravāraṇā and eight kinds of legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā. What are the eight kinds of illegitimate stopping of pravāraṇā? Stopping based on a groundless breach of precepts without action, a groundless breach of views without action, a groundless breach of deportment without action, and a groundless breach of right livelihood without action. The opposites are the eight kinds of legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā.

"There are also nine kinds of illegitimate stopping of pravāraṇā and nine kinds of legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā. What are the nine kinds of illegitimate stopping of pravāraṇā? Stopping based on groundless breach of precepts with action or without action, and the same for groundless breach of views and groundless breach of deportment. The opposites are the nine kinds of legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā.

"There are also ten kinds of legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā and ten kinds of illegitimate stopping of pravāraṇā. What are the ten kinds of legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā? There is a bhikṣu who, by this characteristic and by this matter, has received a legitimate disciplinary procedural act. If a bhikṣu sees that by this characteristic and by this matter he has received a legitimate disciplinary procedural act, that bhikṣu later, in another Sangha assembly, states that he has received a legitimate disciplinary procedural act and stops his pravāraṇā: this is called a legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā. If at the time of stopping his pravāraṇā a difficulty arises and all the Sangha disperses, and later, upon seeing him again, one stops his pravāraṇā as before: this is called a legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā.

"If one has abandoned the precepts, if one has committed a pārājika, if one has committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa, if one has committed a sthūlātyaya, if one has committed a pāyattika, if one has committed a pratideśanīya, if one has committed a duṣkṛta, if one has committed wrong speech: if a bhikṣu, by this characteristic and by this matter, states his offense in the Sangha and stops his pravāraṇā, this is called a legitimate stopping of pravāraṇā. The opposites are called illegitimate stopping of pravāraṇā."

At that time, Upāli asked the Buddha: "World-Honored One! With how many qualities should a bhikṣu stop another's pravāraṇā?" The Buddha said: "With five qualities one should stop another's pravāraṇā: based on truth, not on falsehood; at the proper time, not at an improper time; with benefit, not without benefit; with a mind of compassion, not with ill intent; with gentle words, not with harsh words."

He further asked: "World-Honored One! When one wishes to stop another's pravāraṇā, with how many qualities should one examine oneself?" The Buddha said: "One should examine oneself with five qualities: One should consider, 'Is my stopping of that person's pravāraṇā based on truth or on falsehood?' If it is false, one should not stop it. If it is true, one should further verify. 'Is this the proper time or not?' If it is not the proper time, one should not stop it. If it is the proper time, one should further verify. 'Is this beneficial or not?' If it is not beneficial, one should not stop it. If it is beneficial, one should further verify. 'Will this cause a dispute that splits the harmonious Sangha, or not?' If it would cause a split, one should not stop it. If it would not, one should further verify. One should also consider, 'Are there bhikṣus who uphold the Dharma, uphold the Vinaya, understand the disciplinary code, are wise, eloquent, and devoted to the training rules who will properly assist me?' If they will certainly assist, one should still further verify. After verifying, one should stop the pravāraṇā at the proper time."

He further asked: "World-Honored One! When one wishes to stop another's pravāraṇā, with how many qualities should one examine oneself?" The Buddha said: "One should examine oneself with five qualities: Examine whether one's own bodily conduct is pure, whether one's verbal conduct is pure, whether one's mental conduct is pure, whether one has studied the sūtras extensively, and whether one understands the Abhidharma well. If one's bodily, verbal, or mental conduct is not pure, the bhikṣus will say: 'Your bodily, verbal, and mental conduct are not pure; how can you stop another?' If one has not studied the sūtras extensively, the bhikṣus will say: 'From whom did you hear this? In what sūtra is it stated? If you cannot yet be a teacher of people, how can you teach about matters?' If one does not well understand the Abhidharma, the bhikṣus will say: 'What meaning does your statement have? You yourself do not understand the meaning; how can you stop another?'"

He further asked: "World-Honored One! With how many qualities can one stop another's pravāraṇā and afterward have no regret?" The Buddha said: "With five qualities one can stop another's pravāraṇā and afterward have no regret: out of compassion, for the sake of benefit, wishing to rescue and deliver, to cause the person to leave evil conduct, and to establish them in complete precepts."

He further told Upāli: "There are five kinds of stopping another's pravāraṇā after which one will feel regret. The bhikṣus say: 'What you said about another's offense is not true; you should stop!' 'What you said was at an improper time; you should stop!' 'What you said has no benefit; you should stop!' 'You spoke of another's offense with ill intent, not with a compassionate mind; you should stop!' 'What you said was harsh, not gentle words; you should stop!' If the opposites apply, there will be no regret afterward.

"The bhikṣu whose pravāraṇā was stopped has five things about which he should not worry. The bhikṣus say to him: 'He stopped your pravāraṇā without truth; you should not worry!' 'He stopped your pravāraṇā at an improper time; you should not worry!' 'He stopped your pravāraṇā without benefit; you should not worry!' 'He stopped your pravāraṇā with ill intent; you should not worry!' 'He stopped your pravāraṇā not with gentle words; you should not worry!'"

Upāli asked the Buddha: "When a bhikṣu enters the Sangha assembly, with how many qualities should he conduct himself?" The Buddha said: "He should conduct himself with five qualities: first, with humility; second, with a compassionate mind; third, with reverence; fourth, knowing the proper seating order; fifth, not discussing other matters.

"There are another five qualities: one should not tuck one's robe behind; one should not tuck one's robe to the left or right; one should not put one's hands on one's hips; one should not cover one's head; and one should show reverence to the Sangha."

Upāli asked the Buddha: "With how many qualities may a bhikṣu participate in the Sangha's harmonious procedural acts?" The Buddha said: "With five qualities one may participate in the Sangha's harmonious procedural acts: one should share the same view, one should follow the Sangha, one should believe there is a matter at hand, one should go in person, or one may give one's proxy."

He further told Upāli: "If there is Sangha business, one should not fail to attend. If one does not attend, one is at variance with the Sangha. There are five kinds of views that are illegitimate with regard to Sangha business: mentally thinking one thing but saying another, saying one thing but mentally thinking another, assisting the Sangha in what is not Dharma, assisting a person who acts contrary to Dharma, and saying one has not committed an offense when one has. The opposites are legitimate."

There was a bhikṣu who, on the pravāraṇā day, committed a duṣkṛta offense and told other bhikṣus. Half said it was a duṣkṛta; half said it was wrong speech. In each group there were Vinaya-holders, those who were wise and intelligent, those with a sense of shame, and those devoted to learning the rules of training. They disputed without resolution, and the pravāraṇā was stopped. The bhikṣus did not know what to do, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One bhikṣu should take him to a place where he can be seen but not heard, and instruct him to perform repentance for wrong speech. Then return and announce to the Sangha: 'That bhikṣu has already performed the procedure. The Sangha should perform the pravāraṇā.' The bhikṣus must not ask what procedure was performed; one who asks commits a duṣkṛta."

There was also a bhikṣu who, on the pravāraṇā day, committed a duṣkṛta offense and told other bhikṣus. Half said it was a pratideśanīya; half said it was a duṣkṛta. And so on up to: half said it was a pārājika; half said it was a duṣkṛta. In each group there were Vinaya-holders, those who were wise and intelligent, those with a sense of shame, and those devoted to learning the rules of training. They disputed without resolution, and the pravāraṇā was stopped. The bhikṣus did not know what to do, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One bhikṣu should take him to a place where he can be seen but not heard, and instruct him to perform repentance for a duṣkṛta. Then return and announce to the Sangha: 'That bhikṣu has already performed the procedure. The Sangha should perform the pravāraṇā.' The bhikṣus must not ask what procedure was performed; one who asks commits a duṣkṛta. For committing a pratideśanīya, and so on up to a sthūlātyaya, the same applies."

"If one has committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa or a pārājika, a motion-procedural act should be made to table the matter: 'May the noble Sangha listen! This matter is now tabled. After the pravāraṇā, it shall be judged in accordance with the Dharma. The motion is thus.' After this motion is made, the pravāraṇā should be performed. One must not fail to perform the pravāraṇā."

There was a bhikṣu who, on the pravāraṇā day, said to the bhikṣus: "There are things but no persons." Because of this, they engaged in fruitless debate. Half said there are things but no persons; half said there are persons but no things. They disputed in confusion. The bhikṣus did not know what to do, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A motion should be made to table this matter and perform the pravāraṇā. One must not fail to perform the pravāraṇā. If after the tabling motion someone raises this discussion again, they commit a pāyattika."

A sick bhikṣu stopped a sick bhikṣu's pravāraṇā. A sick bhikṣu stopped a healthy bhikṣu's pravāraṇā. A healthy bhikṣu stopped a sick bhikṣu's pravāraṇā. They did not comply with each other. The bhikṣus did not know what to do, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should not be done like that; all who violate this commit a duṣkṛta! If a sick bhikṣu stops a sick bhikṣu's pravāraṇā, the bhikṣus should say to him: 'You are now sick; why are you stopping another?' If a sick bhikṣu stops a healthy bhikṣu's pravāraṇā, they should likewise say this. If a healthy bhikṣu stops a sick bhikṣu's pravāraṇā, the bhikṣus should say to him: 'Please stop for now! This bhikṣu is ill; you may stop his pravāraṇā after he recovers.'"

There were bhikṣus who sent a messenger to stop another's pravāraṇā. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not send a messenger to stop another's pravāraṇā; one who does so commits a duṣkṛta!"

At that time, Upananda still sent a messenger to stop another's pravāraṇā. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The person who accepts the mission commits a duṣkṛta; Upananda commits a pāyattika."

Or a foolish bhikṣu stopped a foolish bhikṣu's pravāraṇā, or a foolish bhikṣu stopped a wise bhikṣu's pravāraṇā, or a wise bhikṣu stopped a foolish bhikṣu's pravāraṇā, and they did not comply with each other. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should not be done like that; those who violate this commit a duṣkṛta!"

If a bhikṣu stops another bhikṣu's pravāraṇā, and the Sangha knows and sees that person's bodily, verbal, and mental conduct is not pure, and that he is of little learning and foolish, they should not accept his words but should simply perform the pravāraṇā. If the Sangha sees that person's bodily, verbal, and mental conduct are partly pure and partly impure, and that he is of little learning and foolish, the same applies. If the Sangha sees that person's bodily, verbal, and mental conduct are pure, and that he is learned and wise, they should accept his words and ask him: "What fault do you see in that person? Is it a breach of precepts? A breach of views? A breach of deportment? A breach of right livelihood?" If he says a breach of precepts, they should ask: "Do you know the characteristics of a breach of precepts?" If he says he does not know, the bhikṣus should reproach him and make him feel shame, saying: "You do not know the characteristics of a breach of precepts, yet in the midst of the Sangha you speak of another's breach of precepts!" If the Sangha does not make this reproach, all commit a duṣkṛta. If he says he knows, the bhikṣus should ask: "What constitutes a breach of precepts?" He answers: "Committing a pārājika or a saṃghāvaśeṣa." If he says a breach of views, they should ask: "Do you know the characteristics of a breach of views?" If he says he does not know, the bhikṣus should reproach him as above; if they do not reproach, all commit a duṣkṛta. If he says he knows, they should ask: "What constitutes a breach of views?" He answers: "There is no present world or future world, no karmic retribution for offenses and merit, no father, no mother, no arhat." If he says a breach of deportment, they should ask: "Do you know the characteristics of a breach of deportment?" If he says he does not know, the bhikṣus should reproach him as above; if they do not reproach, all commit a duṣkṛta. If he says he knows, they should ask: "What constitutes a breach of deportment?" He answers: "Committing a pāyattika, a pratideśanīya, a duṣkṛta, or wrong speech." If he says a breach of right livelihood, the bhikṣus should ask: "Do you know the characteristics of a breach of right livelihood?" If he says he does not know, the bhikṣus should likewise reproach him as above; if they do not reproach, all commit a duṣkṛta. If he says he knows, they should ask: "What constitutes a breach of right livelihood?" If he says: "With a deceitful and crooked mind, seeking material gain." The Sangha should further ask: "Is it based on what you have seen? What you have heard? Or what you suspect?" If he says seen, they should ask: "How did you see it? When did you see it? Where did you see it? Where were you? Where was that person?" If, when asked in this way, he cannot answer, he should be dealt with according to the Dharma, and then the pravāraṇā should be performed. One must not fail to perform the pravāraṇā. The same applies for hearing and suspicion.

There was a dwelling place where the Sangha had practiced the rains retreat for three months and all attained realization of the path. They thought: "If we perform the pravāraṇā at the end of the three months, we would have to move away, and we would lose this happiness." The bhikṣus did not know what to do, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now permit that on the three-month pravāraṇā day, all bhikṣus should gather in one place, and one bhikṣu should proclaim: 'May the noble Sangha listen! We have obtained one-pointed mental happiness while dwelling in the rains retreat here. If we perform the pravāraṇā, we will leave and lose this happiness. Let us now postpone this until the end of the eighth month, and perform a four-month pravāraṇā. The motion is thus.' After this motion is made, if there are bhikṣus who wish to travel far away, they are permitted to perform the pravāraṇā and leave. If there are those who wish to stop another's pravāraṇā, the Sangha should investigate in accordance with the Dharma so that the person can perform the pravāraṇā and depart. If the departing bhikṣus wish to stop the remaining bhikṣus' pravāraṇā, the bhikṣus should say to them: 'We have not yet performed the pravāraṇā; how can you stop it?' If those bhikṣus have departed and later return at the time of the postponed pravāraṇā to stop the bhikṣus' pravāraṇā, the bhikṣus should investigate in accordance with the Dharma, and after it is completed, should perform the pravāraṇā."

There were bhikṣus dwelling together in one place for the rains retreat who heard that certain quarrelsome bhikṣus were coming. They discussed: "When they arrive, they will surely stop our pravāraṇā." The bhikṣus did not know what to do, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Two or three days before the pravāraṇā, they should perform the pravāraṇā and leave. If they hear the others are arriving that day, they should perform the pravāraṇā immediately and leave. If they hear the others have already entered the boundary, they should quickly go outside the boundary, perform the pravāraṇā, and return. If they cannot do so, they should go out to meet them, bow and exchange greetings, carry their robes and bowls, prepare bathing supplies, take them to the bathing room and provide body-anointing oil and honey, and then go outside the boundary to perform the pravāraṇā. If this too is not possible, they should prepare food for them, whether within or outside the boundary. If they eat within the boundary, at mealtime they should go outside the boundary to perform the pravāraṇā. If they eat outside the boundary, at mealtime they should stay within the boundary to perform the pravāraṇā. If even this is not possible, they should all gather together. After the pravāraṇā, one resident bhikṣu should announce to the other resident bhikṣus: 'May the noble Sangha listen! Let us now perform the upoṣadha and recite the precepts. On the fifteenth day of the dark half of the fourth month, we shall perform the pravāraṇā. The motion is thus.' If the visiting bhikṣus ask: 'Why perform the pravāraṇā on the fifteenth of the dark half of the fourth month?' the resident bhikṣus should answer: 'We did not originally undertake the rains retreat together; you should not ask us.' If the visiting bhikṣus are still present on the fifteenth of the dark half, the resident bhikṣus should again announce as above: 'On the fifteenth of the bright half, we shall perform the pravāraṇā,' and so on up to 'you should not ask us,' as above. If the visiting bhikṣus are still present on the fifteenth of the bright half, food should again be prepared for them as above. If this succeeds, good. If not, they should forcefully unite in harmony and perform the pravāraṇā together. One must not fail to perform the pravāraṇā."

I notice that the provided Chinese text for this fascicle is empty (there is no actual Chinese text between the separator lines). Without source text to translate, I cannot produce a translation.

If you could provide the actual Chinese text of Fascicle 19 (卷第十九) of the *Mīśāsaka Vinaya* (彌沙塞部和醯五分律, Taishō T1421), I would be happy to translate it in full according to your specifications.

Please paste the Chinese text and I will translate it completely.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Twenty

### Third Division, Part Five: Rules Concerning Robes (Upper)

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, the wet nurse of Jīvaka was bathing Jīvaka and carefully observed his body, showing a displeased expression. Jīvaka noticed this and asked, "Why do you look at me with displeasure?" The wet nurse said, "I am displeased that your bodily features are so exceptional, yet your mind has not yet drawn close to the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha!" When Jīvaka heard this, he praised her, saying, "Excellent! Excellent! You are able to instruct me in such a matter." He then put on new clothes and went to where the Buddha was. From afar he saw the World-Honored One, whose bearing and appearance were outstanding and distinguished, possessing the thirty-two marks of a great person, with a halo one fathom wide, like a mountain of gold. He immediately gave rise to faith and reverence, went forward and bowed at the Buddha's feet, then sat to one side. The Buddha expounded various wonderful teachings for him, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and delighting him, namely: discourse on giving, discourse on morality, discourse on rebirth in heaven, the defilements and entanglements of household life, and the non-attachment of renunciation. He revealed such teachings that aid the path, and then expounded the Dharma that all Buddhas constantly teach: suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. Right there on his seat, Jīvaka became free from dust and stain and attained the purity of the Dharma-eye. Having seen the Dharma and obtained its fruit, he took refuge in the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha, and then received the five precepts. Jīvaka was skilled in discerning the characteristics and origins of sounds. The Buddha took him to the charnel ground and showed him five skulls. Jīvaka tapped each one and said to the Buddha, "The first one I tapped was reborn in hell. The second was reborn as an animal. The third was reborn as a hungry ghost. The fourth was reborn in the human realm. The fifth was reborn in heaven." The Buddha said, "Excellent! It is all as you have said." He then showed him one more skull. Jīvaka tapped it three times but could not determine the destination. He said to the Buddha, "I do not know where this person was reborn." The Buddha said, "You should not be able to know. Why? This is the skull of an arhat; there is no place of rebirth!"

At that time the World-Honored One had a slight illness. He said to Ānanda, "My illness requires that I take emetic and purgative medicine." Ānanda said to the Buddha, "We should inform Jīvaka!" He then went and told him. Jīvaka said, "I cannot have the Tathāgata take ordinary medicine. I shall compound what a wheel-turning sage-king should take." He then used medicine to fumigate three utpala flowers and brought them to the Buddha's presence, saying to the Buddha, "Please smell these flowers! Smelling one flower should produce ten purgations. Three flowers will produce thirty, and the illness will be completely cured." The World-Honored One then smelled two flowers, and there were twenty purgations. From the remaining one flower there were nine purgations. After a short while, Jīvaka came to the Buddha's presence and asked, "Did the medicine produce purgations? How many?" The Buddha said, "Although the medicine produced purgations, there is still one short." Jīvaka said to the Buddha, "You should drink warm water." He immediately drank it, and there was one more purgation, and the illness was completely cured. Jīvaka further said, "You should now take nourishing food to recuperate. I shall provide appropriate offerings at the proper times." The Buddha silently accepted. Jīvaka then prepared a porridge of sandalwood powder and offered it to the World-Honored One. After the World-Honored One had taken it, Jīvaka again said to the Buddha, "When I treat illnesses for kings, ministers, and common people, sometimes I receive a hundred thousand gold coins, countless jewels of the seven treasures, or I receive villages or towns. I only wish that the World-Honored One would grant me a small wish!" The Buddha said, "All Buddhas and Tathāgatas have gone beyond all wishes!" Jīvaka again said to the Buddha, "May the Buddha grant me an obtainable wish!" The Buddha said, "If it is obtainable, I will not go against your intention." Thereupon Jīvaka took a single robe of great value, worth half a kingdom, and offered it to the Buddha, saying to the Buddha, "This robe is the foremost among all robes. May you compassionately accept it! Also, may you permit all bhikṣus to accept gifts of householder robes!" The Buddha then accepted it and also permitted all bhikṣus to accept gifts of householder robes. He expounded various wonderful teachings for Jīvaka and sent him back to his residence. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus, "Jīvaka cured my illness, offered me a fine robe, and also wished that bhikṣus be permitted to accept gifts of householder robes. I accepted it on their behalf and also permit all bhikṣus to accept gifts of householder robes. From now on, if bhikṣus wish to wear householder robes, they are permitted to accept them. However, those of few desires who are content and wear rag-robes, these I praise."

At that time the householders of Rājagṛha, having heard that the Buddha permitted bhikṣus to accept gifts of householder robes, together brought three thousand sheets of karpāsa cloth in pure colors of blue, yellow, red, and black, and offered them to the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus had doubts about the colors. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to accept them! You should wash them to destroy the fine color, and re-dye them before wearing."

There were bhikṣus who went to the charnel ground to contemplate corpses, practicing the contemplation of impurity from the feet up to the head. A vetāla spirit entered the dead body, opened its eyes, stuck out its tongue, and stomped on the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus were terrified, and non-human beings took advantage of this, seizing their vital energy, and some died from it.

There was also a bhikṣu who went to the charnel ground and contemplated a newly dead woman from the feet up to the head. Sensual desire arose in him and he committed an impure act. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should not first contemplate from the feet."

There was also a bhikṣu who was contemplating a dead person from the side. A vetāla spirit again entered the corpse, opened its eyes, stuck out its tongue, and struck him with its hand. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "Do not contemplate from the side; you should contemplate from in front of the head."

There were also bhikṣus who, for the sake of robes, dug up newly dead people. Householders saw this and reproached them, saying, "These Śākyan śramaṇas are foul and impure! How can they come into our homes in such a state?" The senior bhikṣus heard this and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should not dig up dead people. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta."

There were also bhikṣus who brought dead people's bones into the monastery. Some placed dead people's skulls in the walking meditation area or under beds.
