Householders saw this and reproached them, saying, "The bhikṣus are impure and repulsive! How can they bring dead people's bones into the monastery, making it like a charnel ground, and keep dead people's skulls as if keeping bowls?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "This should not be done! Nor should one handle dead people's bones with one's hands. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who had an eye disease. A physician said, "Grind a human frontal bone and apply it to the eyes." The bhikṣus said, "The Buddha does not permit us to handle dead people's bones. Please suggest another remedy." The physician said, "There is no other treatment." The bhikṣus thought, "If the World-Honored One permits handling dead people's bones when ill, the illness can be cured." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to take a bone in a secluded place, about the size of two fingers, grind it, and apply it to the eyes."

There were bhikṣus who, having eaten sesame, honey, fish, or meat, went to the charnel ground to seek rag-robes. The ghosts and spirits were displeased. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should not eat these things and then go to the charnel ground."

There were bhikṣus who ate sesame, honey, fish, or meat among the Buddha's assembly, in the Sangha, or at the homes of laypeople. When traveling and their path passed through the charnel ground, they would go out of their way to avoid it, and because of this they lost their companions. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "If one is not afraid, one is permitted to pass along the edge."

There were bhikṣus who constantly resided in the charnel ground. When they obtained fish or meat through begging, they did not dare to return. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "If one is not afraid, one is permitted to return."

There were bhikṣus who went to and from the charnel ground on the eighth, fourteenth, and fifteenth days of the month to seek rag-robes. The ghosts and spirits also assembled on those days and said to the bhikṣus, "Today is our day of assembly. Why have you come?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should not go to the charnel ground on those days."

Bhikṣus who constantly resided in the charnel ground and bhikṣus traveling on the road did not dare to go on those days either. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "If one is not afraid, it is permitted."

There were bhikṣus who relieved themselves in the charnel ground. The ghosts and spirits reproached them, saying, "How can you relieve yourselves in our dwelling place?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "This should not be done!"

There were charnel grounds that were vast and remote. Bhikṣus passing through did not dare to stop and relieve themselves, and because of this they became ill. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should first snap one's fingers, and then relieve oneself. If the ghosts and spirits wish to hear sūtras, chants of praise, or Dharma teachings, one should do so for them."

At that time King Kāśī gave a precious kambala robe to Jīvaka. Jīvaka immediately brought it to the monastery and offered it to the Sangha. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You should accept it and use it to adorn a stūpa."

There were bhikṣus who obtained woolen rugs with long hair, short hair, no hair, or of mixed colors, and did not dare to accept them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to accept them. Those of mixed colors should be washed to destroy the color before wearing. If the color cannot be made into a single hue, they are permitted to be worn within the monastery."

There were bhikṣus who obtained felt that was already made and felt not yet finished, and did not dare to accept or make it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to accept and make them."

There were bhikṣus who wished to pick up rag-robes in the streets and alleys. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to pick them up."

At that time there were laypeople who removed their clothes in the streets and alleys to relieve themselves. Bhikṣus, thinking these were rag-robes, picked them up. The layperson said, "Venerable one! Do not take my clothes!" The bhikṣu replied, "I thought they were rag-robes; that is why I took them!" The layperson further said, "You did not look carefully and just took them. This is stealing clothes!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should examine carefully. If it is dust-covered, sun-bleached, and shows signs of having been there a long time, one should look around and ask people, and only then take it."

Bhikṣus who picked up rag-robes brought them into their rooms without washing them first, and they were foul and impure. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should not bring them into one's room without washing them first."

There were bhikṣus who picked up rag-robes and did not wash them promptly, and insects bred in them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should wash them clean immediately."

There were bhikṣus who washed rag-robes in clean ponds or upstream in rivers. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "This should not be done!"

There were bhikṣus who used clean vessels to wash rag-robes. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "This should not be done!"

The Buddha, traveling with a great bhikṣu Sangha of one thousand two hundred and fifty, was wandering through the countryside from Rājagṛha. The bhikṣus were carrying rag-robes. The Buddha saw this and thought, "I should set a limit for the bhikṣus on accepting gifts of householder robes."

At that time King Bimbisāra heard that the Buddha was traveling with one thousand two hundred and fifty through the countryside. He thought, "I should now take the four kinds of troops and escort the World-Honored One as he travels through my territory." Having thought this, he ordered the four kinds of troops to be readied and they followed behind the Buddha. The Buddha gradually arrived at the Ganges River and wished to cross over to the Vṛji country. Mahāmaudgalyāyana thought, "If we use boats to cross, the king may be detained for a long time and neglect affairs. I shall now use my spiritual powers to make this water shallow." Having thought this, he immediately made the water shallow, and the Buddha and the bhikṣus all waded across at once. When the Buddha reached the other bank, he spoke a verse:

> "Diligence is the raft and boat
> That can cross deep and wide rivers.
> Who, seeing something like this,
> Would not give rise to faith and reverence?"

Thereupon King Bimbisāra thought, "The Buddha has now left my territory; I should turn back." He then joined his palms, bowed from afar, and returned.

The Buddha arrived at the village of Kuṭṭha and told the bhikṣus, "There are four things which, when I and you had not yet attained them, we revolved endlessly in birth and death. What are the four? They are: noble morality, noble concentration, noble wisdom, and noble liberation. Now that we have attained them, birth and death is ended, the holy life is established, and what was to be done has been done." He then spoke a verse for the bhikṣus:

> "Morality, concentration, wisdom, and liberation:
> I have now realized them thus.
> Having exhausted all sources of suffering,
> I therefore teach them to you."

At that time the Buddha, together with five hundred bhikṣus, was traveling through the Vṛji country, intending to reach the city of Vaiśālī. There was a courtesan there named Āmrapālī, who had heard that the Buddha, the World-Honored One, possessed great renown and virtue, bearing the titles: Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyak-Saṃbuddha. The Dharma he taught was good in the beginning, middle, and end, complete and pure, with the marks of the holy life. He was traveling through the lands and was about to arrive at this city. She exclaimed, "Excellent! I wish to see him!" She then readied a carriage drawn by four horses, accompanied by five hundred courtesans, and went out to welcome the World-Honored One. The Buddha saw her from afar and told the bhikṣus, "All of you should keep your mindfulness in the forefront and guard your own minds. This is the teaching of all Buddhas. What is mindfulness? It means practicing the four foundations of mindfulness: contemplating the body internally, observing the body closely, removing ignorance and the suffering of the world; and the external body, the internal-and-external body, and feelings, mind, and dharmas are likewise. What is 'in the forefront'? It means whether walking, standing, sitting, lying down, sleeping, waking, going, coming, looking forward or behind, bending or stretching, bowing or raising up, putting on robes or holding the bowl, eating, drinking, or relieving oneself, speaking or remaining silent, one should always unify one's mind. This is my teaching." Āmrapālī saw from afar that the World-Honored One's appearance was exceptional and distinguished, with his faculties peaceful and settled, possessing the thirty-two marks of a great person, with a halo one fathom wide like a mountain of gold. Faith and joy arose in her. She went forward to the Buddha's presence, bowed her head to his feet, then stood to one side. The Buddha expounded various wonderful teachings for her, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and delighting her. She then said to the Buddha, "World-Honored One! I wish that the Buddha and the Sangha would lodge in my garden tonight and accept my invitation for tomorrow's meal." The Buddha silently accepted. Āmrapālī, knowing the Buddha had accepted, bowed, circumambulated him, and withdrew.

At that time five hundred Licchavi princes heard that the Buddha and the bhikṣu Sangha were traveling through the territory and heading toward this city. They made a pact to welcome the Buddha, with a penalty of five hundred gold coins for anyone who did not go out. Having made the pact, they all went out. Some rode blue horses with blue carriages, and all their retinues and garments were entirely blue. Yellow, black, red, and white were likewise. Āmrapālī met them on the road and did not yield the way. The Licchavi princes said, "Why do you not yield, causing our horses and carriages to collide?" Āmrapālī said, "I have invited the Buddha and the Sangha to lodge in my garden, and tomorrow I am preparing a meal. I have no time to yield." The Licchavi princes said, "We also wish to invite the Buddha. Let us go first!" She answered, "He has already accepted my invitation; I cannot yield it." The Licchavi princes said, "We will give you five hundred thousand gold coins; let us go first!" She answered as before. The Licchavi princes then said, "We will give you half the kingdom's wealth. Will that do?" She answered, "Even if it were the entire kingdom, it would not be possible. If you can guarantee me three things without fail, then I will agree!" The Licchavi princes said, "What are these three things?" She answered, "First, guarantee my life will never be cut short. Second, guarantee my wealth will never be diminished. Third, guarantee the Buddha will always remain and never go elsewhere." The Licchavi princes said, "If your wealth is lost, we can replace it. If the Buddha goes elsewhere, we can invite him to stay. But your life is fragile; who can guarantee it?" They departed in anger. The Buddha saw the Licchavi princes coming from afar and told the bhikṣus, "If you wish to know how the gods of Trāyastriṃśa heaven come and go, it is no different from this." The Licchavi princes saw the Buddha's exceptional appearance, and so forth up to "like a mountain of gold." They descended from their carriages, proceeded on foot, went forward to the Buddha's presence, bowed their heads to his feet, then sat to one side. At that time among the assembly there was a young brahmin named Piṅgiya, who rose from his seat, bared his right shoulder, knelt on his right knee, joined his palms, and said to the Buddha, "I wish to praise the World-Honored One with a verse." The Buddha said, "As you wish." He then spoke it:

> "Bimbisāra gained a great blessing,
> Aṅga with its jeweled armor.
> The Buddha once arose from that land,
> His voice like the thunder's roar,
> And like a newly opened flower,
> Its fragrance exceedingly sweet.
> Behold the Buddha's radiant body,
> Like the sun adorning the sky,
> And like the full moon
> Rising in a cloudless sky;
> The World-Honored One's luminous body
> Blazes and surpasses even these.
> The Buddha's wisdom illumines all,
> Dispelling the darkness of scheming thoughts,
> Able to bestow eyes upon the world,
> Resolving all doubts and hesitations."

The Licchavi princes, hearing the verse with delight, immediately gave him five hundred robes. The young brahmin said, "I do not need robes; I only wish to be the first to invite the Buddha!" The Buddha said to the Licchavi princes, "You may allow him to invite first!" They then permitted it, and gave the robes as before. The young brahmin, having received the robes, immediately offered them to the Buddha. The Buddha accepted them for him. The Buddha then told the Licchavi princes, "In the world there are five treasures exceedingly difficult to encounter: first, Buddhas, World-Honored Ones; second, one who skillfully teaches what the Buddha has taught; third, one who, hearing the Dharma, understands it well; fourth, one who, as he has heard, is able to practice; fifth, one who does not forget even small kindnesses." The Licchavi princes, hearing the Dharma with delight, deliberated together, saying, "The Buddha will not stay long. If each person makes a separate offering, it will not go around to all. We should now collect goods and provide offerings each day. Those not of our clan shall not be allowed to participate!"

Āmrapālī prepared various fine foods throughout the night. At dawn she brought them to the garden. When the seating was arranged, she announced that the time had come. The Buddha told the bhikṣus, "You should all keep your mindfulness and together accept that food." They then all took their seats. The mango-grove woman personally served and ladled the food with joy and composure. When the meal was finished and water was passed, she withdrew and stood to one side. She said to the Buddha, "Among all the parks and pleasure gardens in Vaiśālī, this garden is the foremost. I cultivated this garden originally intending to create merit. Now I offer it to the World-Honored One. Please deign to accept it!" The Buddha said, "You may offer it to the Sangha, and you will obtain great merit." The mango-grove woman again offered it to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "Simply offer it to the Sangha; I am counted among the Sangha." The mango-grove woman accepted the instruction and offered it to the Sangha. She then took a small seat and sat in front of the Buddha. The Buddha spoke a verse of appreciation for her, as he had spoken for Verañjā. He further expounded various wonderful teachings for her, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and delighting her. Right there on her seat she attained the purity of the Dharma-eye. She then received the three refuges and the five precepts, rose from her seat, bowed to the Buddha, and departed. The Licchavi princes afterward made offerings as they had deliberated.

The Buddha, departing from Vaiśālī, gradually traveled and arrived at Pañcāla Stūpa. At that time it was the depths of winter, bitterly cold. He wore one robe and slept in the open. When the first watch of the night had passed, he felt cold. He put on another robe. When the middle watch had passed, he felt cold. He put on yet another robe, and no longer suffered from the cold. He thought, "If future bhikṣus cannot endure the cold, wearing these three robes will be sufficient to ward it off. I should now establish a rule for bhikṣus to keep three robes." The next morning, on account of this, he assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus, "Previously, when I was traveling from Rājagṛha, I saw bhikṣus carrying heavy burdens of robes. At that time I wanted to set a limit on the householder robes they receive. Last night the cold was extreme. I first wore one robe. At the beginning of the middle watch I felt cold, and put on another robe. At the beginning of the last watch I felt cold, and put on another robe, and then no longer suffered from the cold. I then thought, 'If future bhikṣus cannot endure the cold, wearing these three robes will be sufficient to ward it off. I should now establish a rule for bhikṣus to keep three robes.' Now, for ten benefits, I establish for bhikṣus the keeping of three robes and do not permit having extra. If robes are worn out and damaged, they are permitted to be mended: patched with overlapping stitches, or permitted to be sewn with straight stitching."

At that time bhikṣus were keeping kuśūla robes. Householders saw them and reproached them, saying, "Bhikṣus wearing kuśūla robes are no different from us wearing pull-over garments!" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "Wearing kuśūla robes is not permitted. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta."

There was a bhikṣu whose antaravāsaka was damaged. He temporarily sewed it together into a kuśūla form and wore it, and later felt doubt and remorse. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "It is permitted to temporarily make and wear it."

There were bhikṣus who kept pull-over garments and garments with sleeves, which they gathered and draped over themselves. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "Keeping pull-over garments or garments with sleeves is not permitted. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta. If such garments are received, they are permitted to be accepted, but should be altered into other robes."

There was an outside-path practitioner who had sewn threads of various colors onto his robe to create the appearance of panels and sections. Later he went forth in the Buddha's teaching and still wore this robe. Householders saw him and reproached him, saying, "The Śākyan śramaṇas wear the robes of outside-path practitioners; they cannot be distinguished!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "Wearing outside-path robes is not permitted. Those who do so commit a sthūlātyaya. If one does not know it is an outside-path robe, yet it is not what the Buddha has permitted, one should destroy it. If one knows it is an outside-path robe, one should pick out the threads, spread it on the ground, and have people tread on it to quickly destroy it completely."

There were bhikṣus sitting in meditation under trees. Flocks of birds made noise disturbing their meditation, and their droppings soiled the bhikṣus' bodies. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to drive away the birds, or to build a meditation hut."

There were bhikṣus who wished to go to the charnel ground to take robes from the dead. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to take them." They then took them. Later another bhikṣu also went to take robes, saw the first bhikṣu, and said, "Share them with me." The bhikṣu would not share. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should be shared." There were bhikṣus who had already obtained robes in the charnel ground and were sharing them with a bhikṣu who arrived later. While they were dividing them, yet another bhikṣu came and asked for a share. The bhikṣus would not give him one. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "He should also be given a share." When they had finished dividing the robes and each wished to leave, yet another bhikṣu came and asked for a share. The bhikṣus would not give him one. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "He should also be given a share." When they had finished dividing and were each returning, and were about to leave the charnel ground boundary, yet another bhikṣu came and asked for a share. The bhikṣus would not give him one. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "He should also be given a share." When they had finished dividing and had each returned, and had already left the charnel ground boundary, yet another bhikṣu came and asked for a share. The bhikṣus would not give him one. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "He should not be given a share."

There was a bhikṣu who, having put on his robes and taken his bowl, entered a village to beg for food. He thought, "It is still too early for me to beg for food." So he went to the charnel ground and obtained many robes. Having obtained them, he further thought, "If I carry these into the village, the heavy burden will be shameful. If I first bring them back, the time for begging may have passed." So he bundled and hid them, and then went begging. Another bhikṣu, after the meal, went to the charnel ground to seek robes and found the robes the first bhikṣu had obtained, and took them back. When the first bhikṣu later went to retrieve the robes, they were nowhere to be found. Returning to the monastery, he saw a bhikṣu washing them and said, "Do not wash my robes!" The other bhikṣu said, "These are not your property!" The first bhikṣu explained the full circumstances. The other bhikṣu said, "Ownerless things in the charnel ground; how can you claim them?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should belong to the first bhikṣu. From now on, if one first obtains robes and places them in the charnel ground, one should make a mark on them."

There was a bhikṣu who used a dead person's bone as a mark. Later bhikṣus thought a bird had dropped it there and took the robes. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should not use a dead person's bone as a mark."

There was also a bhikṣu who used lac dye as a mark. Bhikṣus thought it was blood stains and took the robes. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should not use lac dye as a mark. One should use blue, black, or mūlaka dye, or affix a piece of kāṣāya robe cloth on top."

There were bhikṣus who made a pact: half would enter the village to beg for food, and half would go to the charnel ground to seek robes, and they would share everything when they returned. Having made the pact, they went. Those who went to the charnel ground obtained many robes and regretted the pact, saying, "The robes I obtained belong to me, and the food they obtained belongs to them. I will not share anymore!" When the bhikṣus who had begged for food returned, they offered the food to the others. The bhikṣus who had obtained robes refused and spoke as above. The food-begging bhikṣus said, "We made a clear pact beforehand. How can you go back on it midway?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should share."

The robe-gathering bhikṣus, at the time of obtaining the robes, made a further pact: "If you can carry these robes back to the residence, you shall receive a double share." Having carried them back, they regretted it and would not give the extra share. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should give it." The same applies to washing.

There was a bhikṣu who went to the charnel ground and saw a newly dead person. He wanted to take the person's robe. A vetāla spirit entered the body and it rose up, saying, "Venerable one! Do not take my robe." He replied, "You are already dead; this is not your robe!" He then forcibly seized it. The dead person cried out loudly and chased him to the monastery. The good spirits and deities would not let it enter, so it stood outside the gate. Seeing bhikṣus going in and out, it said, "There is a bhikṣu who seized my robe and came here. Please make him return it." The bhikṣus went inside and asked, "Outside there is a person who says a bhikṣu seized their robe. Who brought it?" The bhikṣu who had obtained the robe said, "This is a corpse, not a living person!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "If newly dead, with the body not yet decomposed, and the vetāla spirit is still attached, one should not take the robe. It should be returned. Taking the robe of an undecomposed dead person is a duṣkṛta." That bhikṣu then returned the robe. The corpse, upon receiving the robe, collapsed to the ground. That bhikṣu reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You may carry it to the charnel ground." The bhikṣu then carried the robe and walked. The corpse rose again and followed. When they reached the charnel ground, he placed the robe on the ground, and the corpse fell down again.

There was a bhikṣu who went to the charnel ground and saw a person wearing a new kambala robe, lying in the shade of a tomb. He thought it was a dead person and reflected, "The Buddha does not permit me to take robes from undecomposed dead people." So he struck the person's head, breaking it open. The person woke up startled and said, "Venerable one! What offense have I committed that you crack my head open?" He answered, "I thought you were a dead person." The person said, "Could you not tell that I was breathing? How can you, for the sake of a robe, try to take my life?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should not strike, nor have others strike, a corpse to cause decomposition. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta."

At that time bhikṣus obtained karpāsa robes and wore them without cutting or trimming. Householders saw them and reproached them, saying, "The Śākyan śramaṇas also wear these robes; how are they different from us?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should not be worn thus. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta."

At that time the World-Honored One, together with a great bhikṣu Sangha of one thousand two hundred and fifty, was traveling through the countryside in the southern region. Coming down from a mountain, he saw rice paddies with well-made ridges and borders. He thought, "My bhikṣus should make robes like this!" He then asked Ānanda, "Do you see these fields?" He answered, "I have seen them!" He further told Ānanda, "Bhikṣus should wear robes like this. Can you make them?" He answered, "I can!" He then received the instruction and made them himself, and also taught the bhikṣus to make them: some with one long strip and one short strip, some with two long strips and one short strip, some with three long strips and one short strip. The panels on the left side slant to the left, the panels on the right side slant to the right, and the panels in the middle slant in both directions. When completed and worn, they were perfectly suitable. The Buddha, upon seeing them, told the bhikṣus, "Ānanda possesses great wisdom! Hearing my brief instruction, he made them exactly according to the Dharma. This is called the cut-and-sewn distinctive robe, different from outside-path practitioners, which enemies and thieves would no longer take. From now on, I permit bhikṣus to cut and sew the three robes. If they are torn, they should be mended!" The Buddha was in the city of Vaiśālī. There was a certain dwelling place where the ground was extremely low and damp, with many mosquitoes and gadflies. Bhikṣus could not stay there and all went to Śrāvastī, Campā, Kapilavastu, or Rājagṛha for the rains retreat. The dwelling place was left empty. Householders said, "Venerable ones! Please stay here for the rains retreat. We will provide food and drink." The bhikṣus said, "There are too many mosquitoes and gadflies here; we cannot stay!" The householders further said, "Venerable ones, just stay. We will supply mosquito nets." The bhikṣus did not know whether they could accept them or not. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to accept them." The bhikṣus did not know what size to make them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should be made to match the size of the bed."

The bhikṣus always wore a single robe when entering the village and returning to the monastery, never changing or removing it, so that it became soiled and impure. Householders saw this and reproached them, saying, "The Śākyan śramaṇas are impure and repulsive, always wearing one robe going in and out of the village!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "Within the monastery one should wear an inner garment and should not wear the robe used for entering the village."

Bhikṣus had no rooms to live in and wished to build new rooms. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to build them within the monastery grounds for the Sangha." Other bhikṣus did not help. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should help." The bhikṣus then helped for extended periods, interfering with their meditation and practice of the path. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should not help for extended periods! Only if the labor force is insufficient should they then help."

The bhikṣus, during the construction, soiled and damaged their robes and frequently mended and washed them, interfering with their meditation and practice of the path. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "The Sangha should make work robes, to be worn during work."

The bhikṣus were embarrassed and did not dare to wear them against the body. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "When working for the Sangha, they are permitted to wear them freely."

The bhikṣus, wearing the Sangha's robes, washed them at the slightest soiling, and because of this the robes quickly deteriorated. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should not be washed repeatedly. When all the work is completely finished, then wash and store them."

The bhikṣus wished to make new walking meditation paths. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They are permitted to make them."

The bhikṣus made them curved. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should be made straight."

The bhikṣus wished to make the walking meditation path raised. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They are permitted to make them raised."

When two people walked side by side, the path frequently crumbled. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to plaster it with white chalk, and also permitted to spread cloth or babbaja grass on top."

At the time of a great assembly, there were many people but few rooms, and bhikṣus had no place to stay. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "If there is enough room in a cell for one's knees, one is permitted to spread a robe on the ground and sit, leaving the center clear."

Since the bhikṣus were sharing rooms, they disturbed each other. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "Robes should be used as partitions. Making enclosed spaces is also permitted."

Bhikṣus leaned against walls while sitting. Householders saw this and reproached them, saying, "Are these Śākyan śramaṇas old when they go forth? Or do they lack proper deportment? Why do they lean against walls while sitting?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should not sit leaning against walls!"

There were elderly and ill bhikṣus who could not support themselves. They took bundles of grass to lean on while sitting, soiling the room. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One should not lean on bundles of grass. They are permitted to make leaning supports and meditation straps."

The bhikṣus made meditation straps too wide. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should not exceed eight finger-widths of a person."

The bhikṣus then made meditation straps too narrow. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should not be less than five finger-widths."

The bhikṣus then made meditation straps of various colors. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should be made of a single color. If of mixed colors, they should be washed to destroy the color, and then they are permitted to be kept."

At that time the elder Kahuṣṭha obtained a piece of cloth. He wanted to make an antaravāsaka but it was too long. He wanted to make a saṃghāṭī or uttarāsaṅga but each was too small. He kept stretching and pulling it. The Buddha, inspecting the rooms, saw him and asked, "What are you doing?" He explained the full circumstances. The Buddha said, "If it is not enough, make it with three long strips and one short strip. If still not enough, make it with two long strips and one short strip. If still not enough, make it with one long strip and one short strip. If still not enough, make the panels by affixing strips."

The elder Kahuṣṭha again obtained a piece of cloth, too small to make a complete set of three cut-and-sewn robes. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to make a cut-and-sewn saṃghāṭī and uttarāsaṅga, and a plain antaravāsaka."

At the time of a great assembly, laypeople offered robes as gifts. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to accept them."

The laypeople wished to receive a benediction. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You should perform a benediction for them!"

The bhikṣus did not know how to perform a benediction. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "The karmadāna should perform the benediction!"

They did not know where to store them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "As before, by a motion with one approval, they should be stored in a central room."

They did not know who should guard them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "As before, by a motion with one approval, one bhikṣu should be appointed to guard them."

The bhikṣus then appointed an ignorant bhikṣu by motion, one who could not distinguish the quality of robes. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "One who possesses five qualities should not be appointed to guard robes: one who acts out of favoritism, anger, delusion, or fear, and one who does not know the quality of robes."

The bhikṣus distributed robes in a noisy place, and robes were lost. The robe-guarding bhikṣu gained a bad reputation. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They should be distributed in a quiet place."

When robes were being distributed, a visiting bhikṣu arrived. The resident bhikṣus asked, "Where were you on such-and-such a day?" He answered, "At such-and-such a place." The bhikṣus said, "On the day we received the robes, this bhikṣu was within our boundary. Now the distribution is invalid." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "When robes are received, if there is a bhikṣu and one has the perception that there is a bhikṣu, the distribution is invalid, and one incurs a duṣkṛta offense. Having doubt about whether there is a bhikṣu is likewise. If there is no bhikṣu but one has the perception that there is a bhikṣu, the distribution is valid, but one incurs a duṣkṛta offense. Having doubt about whether there is no bhikṣu is likewise. If there is no bhikṣu and one has the perception that there is no bhikṣu, the distribution is valid, and there is no offense."

At that time bhikṣus slept without underwear on the Sangha's bedding, soiling it. There was also a bhikṣu who slept without underwear on the Sangha's bedding, stretched out his legs, and tore it with his feet. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "To protect the body, to protect the robes, and to protect the Sangha's bedding, they are permitted to keep a single coverlet, to be spread over the Sangha's bedding."

At that time the group-of-six bhikṣus, because the Buddha had not permitted sleeping without underwear on the Sangha's bedding, spread a piece only a few inches wide on the Sangha's bedding. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "Its width and length should match the bedding."

Bhikṣus who slept without mindfulness emitted impure substances, soiling the single coverlet. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "The sitting cloth should be spread on top of the single coverlet."

There were bhikṣus troubled by bedbugs. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "They are permitted to make a separate large single coverlet to spread underneath, with the edges hanging over the four sides of the bed by one foot each."

At that time Upāli asked the Buddha, "World-Honored One! How many kinds of cloth should be formally held?" The Buddha said, "The three robes should be formally held. An inner garment, a coverlet, a rain-bathing robe, a wound-covering cloth, a mosquito net, a walking meditation path cloth, a bedbug-blocking cloth, a single coverlet, a sitting cloth, a thigh-covering cloth, a shin-covering cloth, a head-covering cloth, a body-wiping towel, a hand-and-face-wiping towel, a needle-and-thread case, a bowl bag, a sandal bag, a water-straining bag: all such cloths and cloth-like items should be formally held."

There was a bhikṣu who said to the Buddha, "World-Honored One! You always praise for us having few desires and being content, and I greatly delight in this. Please permit us to go naked!" The Buddha said, "Foolish person! You wish to adopt the practices of outside-path practitioners. Those who do so commit a sthūlātyaya."

There were bhikṣus who said to the Buddha, wishing to make robes of human hair, deerskin robes, sheepskin robes, bird-feather robes, horse-mane robes, yak-tail robes, or robes of grass, bark, and leaves. The Buddha said, "Foolish people! You wish to adopt the practices of outside-path practitioners. No practices of outside-path practitioners are to be adopted. Those who do so commit a sthūlātyaya."

There was a bhikṣu who said to the Buddha, "Please permit us to wear a pull-over garment as an inner garment, with a paṭṭa robe draped on top!" The Buddha said, "Foolish person! You wish to adopt the manners of laypeople. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who wished to wear a pull-over garment inside with a karpāsa robe draped outside; or wished to make soma cloth robes or patterned karpāsa robes; or wished to wear finger rings, paint their eyebrows and eyes, or wear multi-colored sandals. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "Foolish people! These are the manners of laypeople. No manners of laypeople are to be adopted. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta."

There was a bhikṣu who said to the Buddha, "Please permit us to wear robes of pure blue, yellow, red, white, or black!" The Buddha said, "Pure black robes are worn by women who have just given birth. Those who wear them commit a pāyattika. For the other four colors, it is a duṣkṛta."

At that time bhikṣus had an illness from cold in the head. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "You are permitted to cover it with cloth, and also permitted to make a cap. When the condition improves, cease wearing it."

There were bhikṣus who entered the village without wearing a saṃkakṣikā, exposing their chests. Women saw them and laughed and teased them. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "This should not be done! When entering a village, one should wear a saṃkakṣikā. Those who do not commit a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus whose saṃkakṣikā was blown off by the wind and fell to the ground. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "It should have a tie attached."

There were bhikṣus working at a height. Women below saw their bodies and laughed and teased them. The bhikṣus were embarrassed. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "When working, one is permitted to take the back of the robe, pass it between the legs, and tuck it in the front."

At that time Upananda, knowing the places where rains-retreat offerings had not yet been distributed, would go there uninvited and say, "Why do you not distribute quickly? If you do not, there may be damage from insects, water, fire, or other calamities. If you distribute, each can use his share, give to disciples, or use it for meritorious purposes." The bhikṣus then distributed, and Upananda said, "You cannot distinguish the valuable from the worthless." The bhikṣus said, "If you are skilled at this, distribute for us and also take your share." He then distributed for them. Having received his share and departed, he went to another place, and did this in many places. He obtained a heavy load of robes and returned to his residence. The bhikṣus saw him and praised him, saying, "You are greatly blessed to have obtained such robes!" He answered, "This is not from blessings. I cleverly talked my way into shares at the various rains-retreat locations!" The bhikṣus variously reproached him, saying, "How can you have the rains retreat in one place and receive distribution shares in many places?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Upananda, "Did you truly do this?" He answered, "Truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha variously reproached him, saying, "I always teach having few desires and being content. How can you now be insatiable in receiving?" After reproaching him, he told the bhikṣus, "One should not have the rains retreat in one place and receive rains-retreat distribution shares in other places. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta."

At that time bhikṣus entered the village wearing only their upper and lower robes. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "This should not be done. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta."

There was a bhikṣu who entered the village wearing his robe inside-out. Other bhikṣus saw him and said, "Wearing a robe inside-out is no different from wearing an uncut robe!" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "This should not be done. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who, before entering or after leaving a village, had their robes caught and torn by thorns and branches, with dust entering the folds. They wished to wear them inside-out but did not dare. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "To protect the robe, before entering or after leaving a village, one is permitted to wear it inside-out."

There were bhikṣus who dyed plain robes to create panels, or who sewed strips onto robes, or who gathered the cloth to form robe panels, or made panels half facing up and half facing down. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "This should not be done. Those who do so all commit a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who wore multi-colored robes. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "This should not be done. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who wore their robes upside-down in the rain, so that water entered the folds and caused rot. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "In the rain, one should not wear the robe upside-down. When it is not raining, one may do as one wishes."

At that time bhikṣus had surplus items of robes and bowls and wished to offer them to the Sangha. They asked the Buddha. The Buddha said, "There are nine kinds of offerings that may all be given to the Sangha: first, a boundary-based offering; second, a pact-based offering; third, a designated offering; fourth, a Sangha offering; fifth, a present-Sangha offering; sixth, a rains-retreat Sangha offering; seventh, a two-part Sangha offering; eighth, an instructed offering; ninth, a person-based offering. A boundary-based offering is when a donor says, 'I offer this to the Sangha within this boundary.' This is called a boundary-based offering. A pact-based offering is when, during the rains retreat, Sanghas residing in different boundaries make a pact that if one community receives offerings, they will all share them together. This is called a pact-based offering. A designated offering is when a donor says, 'I offer this to such-and-such persons.' This is called a designated offering. A Sangha offering is when a donor makes an offering to the Sangha, and the Sangha should know the offered items and allocate them as appropriate. This is called a Sangha offering. A present-Sangha offering is when a donor makes an offering to the Sangha face to face. This is called a present-Sangha offering. A rains-retreat Sangha offering is when a donor says, 'I offer this to the Sangha of this rains retreat.' This is called a rains-retreat Sangha offering. A two-part Sangha offering is when a donor makes an offering to the two-part Sangha. If there are many bhikṣus and few bhikṣuṇīs, or many bhikṣuṇīs and few bhikṣus, it should still be divided equally in half. If there are bhikṣus but no bhikṣuṇīs, the bhikṣus should take the entire share. If there are bhikṣuṇīs but no bhikṣus, the bhikṣuṇīs should take the entire share. This is called a two-part Sangha offering. An instructed offering is when a donor instructs the Sangha to use it in such-and-such a way, or to share it. This is called an instructed offering. A person-based offering is when a donor says, 'I offer this to so-and-so.' This is called a person-based offering.

"There are also five kinds of offerings: an offering to the Buddha and the Sangha; an offering to the Buddha and the bhikṣuṇī Sangha; an offering to the Buddha and the two-part Sangha; an offering made for a person; and a Sangha standing-invitation offering."

There was a śrāmaṇera who died. The bhikṣus did not know how to dispose of his belongings. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "If during his lifetime he had already given them to someone, they should be given to that person. If during his lifetime he had not given them to anyone, the Sangha present should distribute them."

There was a bhikṣu with few acquaintances who died. He had upper and lower robes and also non-robe items. The bhikṣus did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "If during his lifetime he had not given them to anyone, the Sangha present should distribute them. If during his lifetime he had given them to someone but they had not yet been taken away, the Sangha should give them by a motion with one approval."

One bhikṣu announces: "May the noble Sangha hear me! The bhikṣu named so-and-so has died in this place. What he possessed during his lifetime, whether robes or non-robe items, the Sangha present should distribute them. They are now given to so-and-so. If the time is right and the Sangha approves and consents, let it be so. This is the motion."

"May the noble Sangha hear me! The bhikṣu named so-and-so has died in this place. What he possessed during his lifetime, whether robes or non-robe items, the Sangha present should distribute them. They are now given to so-and-so. Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has already given the robes to so-and-so. The Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. Let this matter be held thus."

There was a bhikṣu with many acquaintances who was honored and supplied by kings, ministers, and the people. He died and his possessions were very numerous. The bhikṣus did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "If during his lifetime he had already given them to someone, they should be given by a motion with one approval. If during his lifetime he had not given them to anyone, there are divisible and non-divisible items. Divisible items include: paṭṭa cloth, soma cloth, karpāsa cloth, kuśa-hair fabric with hair five finger-widths long, saṃghāṭī, uttarāsaṅga, antaravāsaka, lower garments, inner shirts, single coverlets, inner garments, coverlets, sitting cloths, needle-and-thread cases, water-straining bags, bowl bags, sandal bags, large and small bowls, and door hooks. Such items are divisible, and the Sangha present should divide them all. Non-divisible items include: brocade, silk, woolen blankets, felt, kuśa-hair fabric with hair exceeding five finger-widths, rain-bathing robes, wound-covering cloths, mosquito nets, walking meditation cloths, bedbug-blocking coverlets, sitting and sleeping beds and squatting stools; except for large and small clay bowls and clay water vessels, all other clay vessels; except for large and small iron bowls, door hooks, nail-trimming knives, and needles, all other iron implements; except for copper spoons and copper containers for eye medicine, all other copper vessels; umbrellas, staffs. Such items are non-divisible and should belong to the Sangha for its use."

There were bhikṣus who received rains-retreat offerings that had not yet been distributed when some died, some returned to lay life, some became outside-path practitioners, some traveled far away, some became śrāmaṇeras, some re-received the full precepts, some developed dual sexual characteristics, and some had their faculties disappear. The bhikṣus did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "If rains-retreat offerings have been received but not yet distributed, and someone has died: if during his lifetime he had already given them to someone, they should be given by a motion with one approval. If during his lifetime he had not given them to anyone, the Sangha present should distribute them. For those who returned to lay life, became outside-path practitioners, traveled far away, developed dual sexual characteristics, or whose faculties disappeared, it is likewise. For one who became a śrāmaṇera, he should be given a śrāmaṇera's share. For one who re-received the full precepts, he should be given a full bhikṣu's share."

There were bhikṣus who, during the rains retreat, had not yet received the rains-retreat offerings when some died and so forth up to when their faculties changed. Later when offerings were received, it is likewise. For bhikṣuṇīs it is also likewise.

At that time Devadatta received rains-retreat offerings that had not yet been distributed when he caused a schism in the Sangha. The bhikṣus did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "If offerings were received before the Sangha was split, they should be divided equally. If offerings were received after the split, they should be distributed according to where they were given."

There were bhikṣus in a community that had split within the same boundary. Afterward they wished to perform various saṃghakarmas and give people the full ordination. They did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "If the Sangha has already split, even though within the same boundary, they are permitted to perform saṃghakarmas and conduct Sangha affairs without it constituting a separate assembly."

At a certain dwelling place, one bhikṣu resided alone. Outside of the rains retreat, robes were offered to the Sangha. He thought, "The Buddha says four people and above constitute a Sangha. I am only one person; I do not know what to do." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "He should formally hold them, or make a gift of purification, or give them to someone. If he does not do so, when other bhikṣus come, they should share them together."

If at a dwelling place where bhikṣus reside, outside of the rains retreat, offerings of Sangha cloth are received, and there are no bhikṣus, then bhikṣuṇīs should distribute them. If at a dwelling place where bhikṣuṇīs reside, outside of the rains retreat, offerings of bhikṣuṇī Sangha cloth are received, and there are no bhikṣuṇīs, then bhikṣus should distribute them.

If at a dwelling place where bhikṣus reside, outside of the rains retreat, a bhikṣu dies, and there are no bhikṣus, then bhikṣuṇīs should distribute his belongings. If at a dwelling place where bhikṣuṇīs reside, outside of the rains retreat, a bhikṣuṇī dies, and there are no bhikṣuṇīs, then bhikṣus should distribute her belongings. When offerings are received during the rains retreat, it is all likewise.

There was a disciple of an outside-path teacher who went forth in the Buddha's Dharma and Vinaya. His relatives all said, "How can he abandon our arhat's path and go forth among the Śākyan śramaṇas? We must go and bring him back!" They further said, "If he hears of this, he may flee. The Śākyan śramaṇas do not break the rains retreat. If we go at that time, we will certainly succeed." That bhikṣu heard of it and did not know what to do. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "He is permitted to break the rains retreat and leave." That bhikṣu then went from one dwelling place to another and did not know at which place he should receive his share of the rains-retreat offerings. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said, "At the place where he stayed the most days, he should receive his share there."

There were two bhikṣus traveling together on the road. One bhikṣu fell ill. The other bhikṣu cared for him, but he died. The attending bhikṣu brought his robes and bowl to the Buddha's presence and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus, "Caring for the sick is very difficult. I now permit that the three robes and bowl be given to the caretaker by a motion with one approval."

One bhikṣu announces: "May the noble Sangha hear me! The bhikṣu named so-and-so has died. His three robes and bowl are to be distributed by the Sangha present. They are now given to the person who cared for him during his illness. If the time is right and the Sangha approves and consents, let it be so. This is the motion."

"May the noble Sangha hear me! The bhikṣu named so-and-so has died. His three robes and bowl are to be distributed by the Sangha present. They are now given to the person who cared for him during his illness. Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has already given the robes and bowl to the bhikṣu named so-and-so. The Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. Let this matter be held thus."

There was a lazy bhikṣu who never helped with communal duties and never attended to his preceptor or ācārya. When he fell ill, no one looked after him. He was soiled with excrement and urine, impure and foul-smelling. The Buddha, making his rounds of inspection, saw him. He personally bathed him, washed his robes, removed the filth, laid him on a bed, and sat beside him, comforting him: "Do not be afraid! You will certainly not die from this." The bhikṣu, hearing this, was overjoyed. The Buddha further expounded various wonderful teachings for him, and he became free from dust and stain, attaining the purity of the Dharma-eye among the dharmas. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Ānanda, "Why is no one looking after the bhikṣu in that room?" Ānanda explained the full circumstances. The Buddha said to Ānanda, "What you have done is wrong! Bhikṣus have no fathers or mothers. If they do not look after each other, who will look after you? I now permit all bhikṣus to care

I notice that the prompt indicates Fascicle 卷第二十 (Fascicle 20) but then references "Fascicle 021" at the end. The actual Chinese text content between the delimiter lines is empty; there is no Chinese text provided for me to translate.

Could you please provide the actual Chinese text of the fascicle you would like me to translate? Once you paste the source text, I will translate it in full according to all the specified rules.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Twenty-One

### Third Division, Part Five: Rules on Robes (continued)

At that time, the Śākya country still followed its ancient custom of not intermarrying with any other clan. King Prasenajit, coveting their lineage and relying on his military strength, sent a messenger to declare: "If you do not give me a marriage alliance, I will destroy your country." The Śākyas deliberated together: "What stratagem can we devise to escape his violence without violating our country's ancient custom?" They all said: "We should simply select a fine slave-girl of beautiful appearance, adorn her in the most splendid manner, call her a Śākya maiden, and give her to him." As agreed, they presented her to King Prasenajit with full marriage rites, and he welcomed her. Later she bore a son of extraordinary appearance, and the king ordered his physiognomists to name him according to his characteristics. The physiognomists said: "The king originally obtained his mother through force; according to the meaning, he should be named Virūḍhaka." When he reached the age of eight, the king wished to have him educated and thought: "Among all arts, archery is the most excellent. In all of Jambudvīpa, only the Śākya clan excels at it. When the Buddha was still a bodhisattva, he could shoot one yojana plus one krośa; Śākya Mahānāma could shoot one yojana; and even the least skilled could shoot no less than one krośa. I should send my son to study with his maternal relatives!" He then ordered the sons of his great ministers to attend the prince and go to Śākya Mahānāma to request instruction in archery.

At that time, the Śākyas had newly built a great hall and had made a solemn agreement together: "First we shall make offerings to the Buddha and his disciples, and only afterward shall we ourselves occupy it." Prince Virūḍhaka and his retinue entered and played in it without permission. The Śākyas saw this and angrily rebuked him: "Base son of a slave-woman! We do not regard you as a worthy field of merit! How dare you sit here first, before the World-Honored One has entered and taken his seat?" Prince Virūḍhaka was greatly resentful and ordered one attendant: "Remember this in your heart; when I become king, remind me of it!" He then went out. Afterward, the Śākyas dug up the earth of the hall, laid new ground, and then invited the Buddha and the Sangha to eat within it and expound the wondrous Dharma. After Prince Virūḍhaka had finished learning archery, he returned to Śrāvastī. While still young, he succeeded to the throne, and those who had formerly studied with him all held key positions. The attendant who had been ordered to remember then said to the king: "Does the king recall when the Śākyas reviled you?" The king said: "I remember!" The minister further said: "If you do not take revenge now, when will you?" The king, hearing these words, immediately arrayed his four divisions of troops and marched to attack the Śākyas. The World-Honored One heard of this and sat beside the road under a leafless Śākya tree. The king saw the Buddha from afar, dismounted from his chariot, approached on foot, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and said: "World-Honored One! There are many fine trees; why do you sit under this leafless tree?" The World-Honored One replied: "The shade of one's kinsmen is pleasant." The king understood that the Buddha was thinking compassionately of the Śākyas, and he turned his army back. This happened twice. That minister again spoke to the king as before, and the king then arrayed his forces and marched to attack the Śākyas. The Buddha knew that the Śākyas' past karma was unavoidable, and so he stopped and did not go out. When the Śākyas heard that King Virūḍhaka was coming to attack their country, they also arrayed their four divisions of troops and went out to meet and resist him. From a distance of one yojana, they shot arrows at his forces: some passed through their ears, some cut off their hair, shaving it completely clean, leaving no beard or eyebrows, and all their war equipment was destroyed at once, yet no flesh was harmed. King Virūḍhaka asked his attendants: "How far away are the Śākyas?" They answered: "One yojana away." The king was greatly frightened and said: "Our forces have not even engaged, and already it is like this! If we meet them in battle, my army will surely be defeated! It would be better to return to the kingdom and count ourselves fortunate to be whole!" At that time, a minister said: "The Śākyas all observe the five precepts. They would rather lose their lives than harm any living being. Your Majesty need only advance your troops; do not worry about defeat!" The king followed this advice and ordered his army to advance. The Śākyas retreated into their city, closed the gates, and defended themselves. King Virūḍhaka sent a messenger to say: "If you open the gates immediately, some will be spared; but if I take the city by force, I will not pardon a single person."

At that time, Maudgalyāyana heard that King Virūḍhaka intended to attack the Śākyas and said to the Buddha: "May the Buddha permit me to create an iron cage to enclose that great city!" The Buddha told Maudgalyāyana: "Though you have supernatural powers, how could you alter this fixed retribution of karma?"

The Buddha, because of this matter, spoke these verses:

> "Whether karma is black or white,
> it never decays or rots.
> Though long delayed, it surely comes,
> and one receives it right before one's eyes.
> Not in the sky, not in the sea,
> not entering mountains or rocks,
> is there any place where one can find
> escape from the calamity of past deeds.
> Drawn along by the force of retribution,
> whether near or far, hidden or deep,
> one naturally gravitates into its midst;
> wherever one goes, it is unfailingly determined."

At that time, the Śākyas, seeing the enemy's forces so powerful, some said to open the gates and preserve their lives, others said to defend to the death. Unable to agree, they cast lots, following the majority. At that time, Māra Pāpīyas was among those who favored opening the gates and took a lot seven times. The lots for opening the gates were more numerous, so they opened the gates. After King Virūḍhaka had taken the city, he proclaimed to the three armies: "All Śākyas are to be killed; those who are not Śākyas must not be harmed!" Three hundred million Śākyas, hearing this, all took up reeds and came out saying: "I am a reed-holding Śākya." The gate guards believed them and let them go. Thereupon, Śākya Mahānāma went to King Virūḍhaka. Virūḍhaka regarded him as his maternal grandfather and said: "Grandfather! What wish do you desire?" He answered: "I wish that you would stop killing my kinsmen!" The king said: "This cannot be granted. Ask for another wish!" He then said: "I wish that from the time I submerge myself in the water until I emerge, during that interval you will permit the Śākyas to leave; all who get out shall not be killed." King Virūḍhaka thought: "A moment under water, what harm could it do?" and granted it. Śākya Mahānāma then loosened his hair, washed it, and submerged, tying his hair to a tree root under the water, and never came back up. The king, surprised at how long it took, sent someone into the water to look. They found him already dead, his hair tied to a tree root, and reported this to the king. The king exclaimed with a sigh: "He was able to sacrifice his own life for his kinsmen!" He then proclaimed to the three armies: "If anyone further kills a Śākya, they shall be punished by military law."

At that time, the bhikṣus heard that King Virūḍhaka had slaughtered the people of the Śākya country and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "King Virūḍhaka is foolish! Seven days from now, he shall receive the punishment for having harmed those who have attained the fruits of the path, and his retinue, both great and small, shall likewise perish together." When King Virūḍhaka heard this teaching of the Buddha, he thought: "The Buddha's words are never empty. Other sufferings I can bear; I only fear being burned by fire!" He then took his retinue and boarded boats on the Aciravatī River. When the seventh day arrived, the water suddenly surged and flooded, and they were overturned and submerged, all perishing at once.

Among the surviving remnants of the Śākyas' destruction, those who had been stripped bare to the raw flesh came to the bhikṣus and said: "We are of the Śākya clan, kinsmen of the World-Honored One. We beg for a little clothing!" The bhikṣus did not dare give it, thinking: "The Buddha has not permitted us to give clothing to the Śākyas." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You are permitted to give it."

There were also upāsakas who held the five precepts and had been stripped. They came to the bhikṣus to borrow clothing. The bhikṣus did not dare lend it, saying: "The Buddha has not permitted us to lend clothing to upāsakas." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You are permitted to lend it. If they return it, you should take it back. If they do not return it, then consider it given."

At that time, Upananda, for the sake of rains-retreat offerings, settled in two places for the rains retreat. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "He should receive half a share from each of the two places."

At that time, bhikṣus were walking on the road without gathering up their robes, which dragged on the ground and became soiled and torn. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; offenders commit a duṣkṛta. You should make bags to hold the robes."

The bhikṣus made bags that were too long. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. At maximum length, the front should reach the navel and the back should reach the waist."

The bhikṣus made robe bags from expensive materials. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. You should use coarse material."

There were bhikṣus who, while traveling on the road, casually asked someone to carry their robes, and also casually carried others' robes, resulting in either losing their own robes or losing others' robes. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. If you ask someone to carry your robes, you should first take them out and show them. If you carry robes for someone, you should take them out and check them."

The bhikṣus, after placing robes in bags, sewed the mouths shut, making it difficult to retrieve them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. You should make ties to fasten them. If it is not constantly needed, you are also permitted to sew it shut."

The bhikṣus stored kambala woolen cloth and kārpāsa cotton cloth each in one place, and insects were born and gnawed them to ruin. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should intersperse the kārpāsa among the kambala, and then place among them kuṣṭhīra incense, nābhīra incense, blue sandalwood, and such fragrances that repel insects."

The bhikṣus later could not recognize their robes when retrieving them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should write names and make marks on them. If a bhikṣu stores robes for twelve years and does not come back to retrieve them, the Sangha should be assembled to appraise the value and use them as property of the Sangha of the four directions. If that bhikṣu later returns, he should be reimbursed with property of the Sangha of the four directions. If he does not accept it, that is fine."

At that time, Ānanda received a donated robe that he did not need, and he accepted it on behalf of Śāriputra, then sent a bhikṣu to deliver the robe to him. That bhikṣu thought: "Śāriputra already has plenty of offerings, while I have no robes. I shall now take it by the method of 'consent-taking.'" He then put it on. Later he had doubts and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Although he accepted it on behalf of another, it had not yet been relinquished. If one performs 'consent-taking' with respect to the bhikṣu who is giving, it is not proper consent-taking for wearing. If one performs 'consent-taking' with respect to the bhikṣu who is to receive, it is proper consent-taking for wearing."

Ānanda again received a waist-cord that he did not need and accepted it on behalf of Aniruddha. He also sent a bhikṣu to deliver it, saying: "This cord already belongs to Aniruddha." That bhikṣu also thought as above, took it, and later had doubts. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Since he said it already belongs to Aniruddha, it has been relinquished. If one performs 'consent-taking' with respect to the bhikṣu who is giving, it is proper consent-taking for wearing. If one performs 'consent-taking' with respect to the bhikṣu who commissioned the delivery, it is not proper consent-taking for wearing."

At that time, bhikṣus did not wear undergarments and used Sangha bedding directly, soiling it with filth, and it was gnawed by rats. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus made undergarments as large as a saṃkakṣikā, or as large as an antarvāsa. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. They should be made in three sizes. The upper size: from covering the head down to the ankles, extending to cover the left hand so that it is completely concealed. The middle size: from covering the head down to the mid-shin, extending to cover the left arm down to the wrist. The lower size: from covering the head down to the knees, extending to cover the left arm to the mid-elbow."

The bhikṣus did not use their undergarment to fully cover the right shoulder, but fully draped the Sangha bedding, which became soiled with mud and filth and was gnawed by rats. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done."

There were bhikṣus who, when receiving instruction in the sūtras or when paying respects to their upādhyāya or ācārya, draped the Sangha bedding with one shoulder bare, letting it drag on the ground. Or when rising at night and walking about, unable to gather it properly, it also trailed on the ground, becoming soiled with mud and dirt. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "When receiving instruction or paying respects, one should bare one shoulder but lift the bedding so it does not touch the ground. When rising at night, one should gather it and drape it fully so it does not become soiled with mud."

The bhikṣus wore the Sangha bedding until it was torn, without mending it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should be mended." They did not know who should mend it. The Buddha said: "During the four months of winter and the three months of summer, the one who uses it should mend it."

There were bhikṣus who dwelt in araṇya places and, when leaving, did not put away the Sangha bedding, causing it to rot and decay. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should be entrusted to someone in a village. If there is no one to entrust it to, one should return it to the owner of the dwelling. If there is fear and danger, and the villagers have all moved away, one should also transport it to a safe place."

The bhikṣus did not check behind them for bedding, causing items to be dropped and lost. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should look behind."

After arriving at the safe place, the bhikṣus there would not give them lodging, nor would they provide a room to store the clothing and articles. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All should be given to them. If the original place is no longer established, the items should be used at the current dwelling place. If the original place is later reestablished, the surplus should be brought back. If all has been used up with nothing remaining, the bhikṣus at that place should give them a small portion."

There were bhikṣus who used the bedding of one dwelling in another dwelling. The owners of those rooms criticized them, saying: "How can you use our room's property in another room? This is taking what is not given!" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done."

There were visiting bhikṣus who wished to pay respects to their teacher and receive instruction, and they wore the resident room's bedding to go to another room. The resident bhikṣu of that room would not permit it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should first inform the bhikṣu of the original room. If he permits it, good. If he does not permit it, one should still wear it and go. If one is going far from there, one should send it back to the original room."

There were bhikṣus who wore Sangha robes into the warm room, the cooking area, into the Sangha dining hall, and to the toilet, and the robes were stained by smoke, soiled with mud, and became impure. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done."

There were sick bhikṣus who needed to wear them to various places but did not dare. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Those who are sick are permitted to wear them to other places, but they should take care of them. They must not be worn to the toilet."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus wore their upper and lower garments and carried a cloth strip five fingers wide in place of the three robes when entering a village. The bhikṣus saw them and asked: "Did the World-Honored One not establish the rule that one cannot enter a village without wearing the three robes?" The group-of-six bhikṣus then showed them the cloth strip, saying: "This is my three robes." The senior bhikṣus rebuked them in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the group-of-six bhikṣus: "Did you really do this?" They answered: "We really did, World-Honored One!" After the Buddha rebuked them in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "This should not be done; offenders commit a duṣkṛta. From now on, the upper, middle, and lower three robes should be made according to the measurements of the undergarment."

At a certain dwelling, the Sangha received distributable robes. One bhikṣu took them to the ordination platform and took them for himself alone. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; offenders commit a duṣkṛta. The Sangha present should distribute them."

A certain senior bhikṣu was traveling through the countryside with other bhikṣus. Among them were visiting bhikṣus and resident bhikṣus. They received distributable robes, but there were too few to distribute. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The resident bhikṣus should say to the visiting bhikṣus: 'Venerable ones, these robes are few and cannot be divided.' If the visiting bhikṣus say: 'Take them all for yourselves,' they should accept them. If they say: 'Even a single thread I will not give up,' then they should divide them together." The visiting bhikṣus saying the same to the resident bhikṣus is likewise.

There were bhikṣus who took cloths that covered graves. The grave owners lost their cloths and asked around: "Who took my grave-covering cloth?" Someone answered: "The bhikṣus took it!" They became angry and rebuked: "The bhikṣus have stolen the cloth of my ancestors!" The senior bhikṣus heard this and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not take such cloths. If the village has been abandoned, and afterward there are such cloths, one is permitted to take them with the intention of taking refuse-rags." It was the same for bhikṣus who took banners and canopies from spirit shrines.

There were bhikṣus who went to battlefields and took the clothing of the dead. Military men criticized them, saying: "These bhikṣus want us to kill more people!" The senior bhikṣus heard this and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done."

There were bhikṣus of few acquaintances who wished to take the clothing of the dead from battlefields but did not dare go. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They are permitted to take them after the soldiers have left."

There were laypeople who, after the soldiers left, came to gather the bones, flesh, and corpses. They saw that all the dead bodies had been stripped and could no longer be identified, and they criticized: "How can bhikṣus strip the clothing from our relatives? If they had clothes, we could identify them." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One is permitted to take them when no one is watching." The same applies to taking clothing at execution grounds.

There were bhikṣus who took clothing from the dead and kept it without altering it into bhikṣu robes. Laypeople saw it and said: "This is the clothing of my relative!" and wept. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should quickly alter it into bhikṣu robes and keep it. If it is an iron implement, one should quickly fashion it into large or small bowls, door hooks, and whatever is needed."

There was a wealthy household that was robbed. The thieves could not carry away everything and hid what remained in a rubbish heap. They planned to come back later, but during the day they did not dare approach, so they watched from afar. A bhikṣu who was gathering refuse-rags came to the place where the goods were hidden, saw a corner of cloth protruding, and took it. The thief called out from a distance: "Venerable one! Do not take my things." The household that had been robbed heard this, recognized the thief, bound him, and delivered him to the authorities, who then executed him. When the thief was being bound, he said: "If the bhikṣu had not taken my cloth, how would they have recognized me? It was the bhikṣu who killed me, not those people!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If it feels heavy when you lift it, you should not take it."

There were bhikṣus who walked looking at the ground in the streets and alleys. Laypeople who saw them said some were looking for money, others were looking for refuse-rags. A disciple of an outside teaching wrapped coins in cloth and placed it on the road. A bhikṣu saw it and picked it up. The man then told the crowd: "The bhikṣus are indeed looking for money." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not take wrapped objects from the streets and alleys."

A certain bhikṣu went to a cemetery for the sake of robes and saw a newly deceased woman with a box at her head. The bhikṣu thought it was an empty box, took it and brought it back. When he arrived at his dwelling and opened it, he found various ornaments inside. Not knowing what to do, he reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "When taking something, one should first open and inspect it. If one takes it without inspecting, it is a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who were traveling on the road together with outside practitioners. They were killed by bandits. The bhikṣus then took the clothing of the outside practitioners and did not dye it to make it into bhikṣu robes. Other outside practitioners saw this and said: "That is the clothing of our fellows; the bhikṣus must have killed them and taken it!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not take it. If one has already taken it, one should immediately dye it and make it into bhikṣu robes."

There were bhikṣus of few desires and contentment who did not accept clothing offered by households. The householders discussed: "What stratagem can we use to make those bhikṣus accept our clothing offerings? We should simply tear and burn some cloth and place pieces in various spots in the streets and alleys. When we see them enter the village, we will say: 'Look around you; if you see anything, take it.'" They did as planned.
