Those bhikṣus saw the cloth and thought: "We do not accept household offerings of clothing; this must have been done by the householders for our sake." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should take it with the intention of taking refuse-rags."

A certain bhikṣu died. The bhikṣus laid him face-up in the courtyard without covering him with cloth, exposing his body. His male organ was swollen. Laypeople saw this and criticized: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, do not practice the holy life; that is why his male organ is so large!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not leave the body face-up and exposed. It should be covered with cloth. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

A certain corpulent bhikṣu died. The bhikṣus placed him on green grass. Fat flowed out and killed the living grass. Outside practitioners saw this and criticized: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, claim to be compassionate, yet how do they harm and kill living things?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not place a body on living things. One should bury it, or burn it with fire, or place it on a stone."

A certain bhikṣu was drowned by a flood. His robe and bowl were caught on a tree branch within the Sangha's boundary. The bhikṣus saw this and thought it had entered the Sangha's boundary and should belong to the Sangha, so they did not dare take it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One is permitted to take it with the intention of taking refuse-rags."

The bhikṣus did not know how many kinds of refuse-rags there were. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "There are ten kinds of refuse-rags: cloth discarded by a king at the time of his enthronement, cemetery cloth, grave-covering cloth, cloth from the streets, old cloth discarded by a newly married woman, cloth worn by a woman at marriage to display her chastity, cloth of a woman in childbirth, cloth chewed by cattle, cloth gnawed by rats, and cloth burned by fire."

At that time, bhikṣus wore bright-colored robes. Laypeople criticized them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not wear bright-colored robes; offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

A bhikṣu kept a rain-bathing cloth of less than five cubits. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A rain-bathing cloth should not be less than five cubits; offenders commit a pācittika."

At that time, householders, during the rains retreat, offered robes to the Sangha because of their children's head-shaving ceremony. After the bhikṣus received them, they wished to redirect them as rains-retreat offerings. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. These are called offerings for a specific occasion and should be distributed among the Sangha present. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who, without relinquishing the three robes they had previously received, received other robes. They then gave the previously received robes as "pure donation" or gave them to others. Later they recalled this and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They can be said to have been re-received, and they can also be said to have been given as 'pure donation' or given to others. But for not having relinquished them first, one commits a duṣkṛta."

At that time, bhikṣus had niḥsargika-pācittika robes that had not yet been relinquished and for which they had not yet done the confession, but these were burned by fire, washed away by flood, seized by thieves, or lost and destroyed. Not knowing what to do, they reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This counts as relinquished. One should simply confess the pācittika offense."

At that time, bhikṣus kept robes without performing "pure donation." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

At a certain dwelling, the Sangha received a gift of brocade. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It should be accepted and redirected for adorning a stūpa, or used for stūpa purposes, or for Sangha use."

At a certain dwelling, the Sangha was about to distribute robes. There were bhikṣus of few desires who did not accept their share, but they needed a waist-cord, a meditation belt, a cap, or a water-strainer. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should be given."

Having given these items, those bhikṣus asked for more. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "At the time of distribution, one should first ask: 'Do you accept your share?' If they say they accept, give them an equal share. If they say they need a waist-cord and such, give them one-third of their share. If they ask for more, it should not be given."

A certain bhikṣu had a kuśa-mat. The Sangha of the four directions had a saṃghāṭī. The bhikṣu wished to trade, but the bhikṣus did not dare allow it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Trading is permitted. If the kuśa-mat is of greater value, the Sangha should additionally compensate the bhikṣu. If the saṃghāṭī is of greater value, the bhikṣu should additionally compensate the Sangha. If the bhikṣu is poor and has nothing to compensate the Sangha, but is truly a person of few desires and contentment, he is also permitted to make the exchange." The same applies for other robes.

At that time, the bhikṣu Revata suffered from cold feet. He begged a foot-wrapping kambala cloth from a brahmin. Having begged it, he had doubts: "The World-Honored One established the rule that we are not permitted to beg clothing from those who are not relatives. I do not know what to do." He reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Under such circumstances, begging is permitted, whether it be a robe one should formally receive, or foot-protecting cloth, thigh-protecting cloth, head-protecting cloth, or a towel for wiping the hands, face, and body."

At that time, bhikṣus performed "consent-taking" of robes from the Sangha, the Sangha of the four directions, stūpas, and persons who had not given consent. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. Only from one's upādhyāya, ācārya, fellow students of the same upādhyāya or ācārya, or one's own disciples and those who have given consent, may one perform 'consent-taking.'"

There were bhikṣus who, before death, disposed of their clothing and belongings, saying: "After I die, give this clothing and belongings to so-and-so; use this clothing and belongings in such-and-such a way." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; offenders commit a duṣkṛta. The giving is not valid as giving, and the use is not valid as use."

At that time, Ānanda regularly visited the household of the Malla warriors of Rūyī. On one occasion he went and they were not home. He performed "consent-taking" of a valuable piece of kārpāsa cloth. He immediately had doubts and regrets, thinking: "The Buddha has not permitted us to perform 'consent-taking' of robes from laypeople." He reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is also permitted to perform 'consent-taking' of robes from laypeople."

At that time, the bhikṣus had set aside their rain-bathing cloth and did not know what clothing to wear when bathing. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "There are five circumstances under which one may set aside the rain-bathing cloth: when it is not raining, when rain is not expected, when not crossing water, when eating during illness, and when the cloth is not yet finished being made. There are five circumstances under which one may retain the saṃghāṭī: when it is raining, when rain is expected, when crossing water, when eating during illness, and when the cloth is not yet finished being made."

At a certain dwelling, the Sangha was about to distribute robes. Visiting bhikṣus arrived. The bhikṣus would receive smaller shares and did not wish to distribute. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Even if the value is only that of one waist-cord, it should be distributed. If the amount is too small to divide, a motion-and-resolution karma should be performed to give it to one bhikṣu who has no robes."

One bhikṣu should announce: "May the noble Sangha listen! The Sangha has received robes or non-robe goods, which are now all given to bhikṣu so-and-so. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha approve. This is the motion."

"May the noble Sangha listen! The Sangha has received robes or non-robe goods, and so forth. If anyone does not approve, speak. The Sangha has given the robes to bhikṣu so-and-so. The Sangha approves by its silence. Let this matter be thus upheld."

---

### Third Division, Part Six: Rules on Leather

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Mahākātyāyana was dwelling in the Paluda Mountain in the country of Aśmaka-Avanti. In that country, there was a householder's son named Śramaṇa Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa, who had faith in and delight in the Buddha's Dharma. He constantly provided for the bhikṣus, had seen the Dharma and attained the fruit, received the three refuges and the five precepts, and regularly went to the monastery to listen to and receive instruction in the Dharma.

At that time, Śramaṇa Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa privately thought to himself: "As the Buddha has said, the household life is one of attachment, and one cannot broadly cultivate the holy life. The homeless life is free of attachment, like open space. Why should I not, in this unconditioned Dharma, shave off my hair and beard and leave home to study the Way?" Having thought this, the next morning he went to Kātyāyana's place, bowed his head at his feet, fully expressed his thoughts, and requested to leave home and receive full ordination. Kātyāyana said: "The household life is indeed one of attachment, as you say. But the homeless life is one of austere discipline, purely cultivating the holy life, sitting alone under trees, and always eating only one meal a day. You have been accustomed to wealth and comfort; this would be very difficult for you." Having heard this, Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa returned home. He came three times in this way, and seeing his determined resolve, Kātyāyana gave him ordination as a novice. Since there were not ten monks in that country, he remained a śrāmaṇera for six years. Kātyāyana then used his supernatural powers to gather ten monks from other countries and conferred full ordination upon him. After receiving ordination, Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa thought: "I have heard of the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Samyak-saṃbuddha, but I have not yet seen him in person. I should now go and pay my respects to the World-Honored One." Having thought this, he went to Kātyāyana's place, bowed his head at his feet, and requested permission to visit the Buddha. Kātyāyana said: "Excellent! I am pleased for you. You should know the right time. Please convey my greetings to the World-Honored One on my behalf. Also, present these five matters to the Buddha: First, in the country of Aśmaka-Avanti, there are not ten monks. Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa remained a śrāmaṇera for six years and could not receive full ordination. Kātyāyana used his supernatural powers to gather a Sangha from other countries, and only then could he receive ordination. May the World-Honored One permit that in this country, full ordination may be conferred with fewer than ten monks! Second, this country has much gravel, thorns, and brambles; may he permit bhikṣus of this country to wear thick-soled leather sandals! Third, in this country, people all use leather spread on the ground for sitting and sleeping; may he permit bhikṣus of this country to use leather spread on the ground! Fourth, the people of this country bathe daily; may he permit bhikṣus of this country to bathe daily! Fifth, there are bhikṣus who send robes to bhikṣus in other regions; before the robe arrives, another bhikṣu tells the bhikṣu who is to receive it, and that bhikṣu has doubts, fearing he might be in violation of the rule on surplus robes; may he dispel this doubt!" Thereupon, Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa received the instructions and departed. When he arrived at the Buddha's presence, he prostrated at the Buddha's feet and conveyed his preceptor's greetings. The Buddha said to Ānanda: "Prepare bedding for this visiting bhikṣu." Ānanda thought: "The Buddha wishes to share a room with this bhikṣu; that is why he has me prepare the bedding." He then prepared it in the Buddha's room. The Buddha and Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa shared one room for the night. During the first watch and the middle watch of the night, they remained in silence. In the last watch of the night, the Buddha thought: "This son of good family is well-disciplined in deportment. I should have him expound the Dharma." He then said to him: "You may expound the Dharma." Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa, accepting the instruction, expounded the Sūtra on Sixteen Topics. When he finished, he remained silent. The Buddha said: "Excellent! Do all the people of that country speak like this?" He answered: "There are those superior to me." The Buddha also asked: "Why have you stayed in that country so long without coming to see me?" He answered: "I have long known the faults of desire. There were circumstances that prevented me from coming earlier."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke these verses:

> "Seeing the faults of the world,
> one personally relies on the Dharma in conduct.
> The wise take no delight in evil;
> one who does evil takes no delight in good."

Thereupon, Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa thought: "My preceptor instructed me to present the five matters to the Buddha. Now is the right time." He then presented them to the Buddha. The Buddha, after the night had passed, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and said to Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa: "You may again state the five matters that Kātyāyana asked you to present." Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa stated them again. The Buddha praised at length those of few desires and contentment, praised the precepts, and praised the keeping of precepts, then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit that in the country of Aśmaka-Avanti and in all border regions where there are few bhikṣus, full ordination may be conferred by five monks who hold the Vinaya. I also permit that in places with gravel, thorns, and brambles, thick-soled leather sandals may be worn. I also permit that in places where there is leather, leather may be used for sitting and sleeping mats. I also permit that in places where bathing is needed, daily bathing is allowed. If a bhikṣu sends a robe to a bhikṣu in another place, even if the bhikṣu has heard about it beforehand, as long as the robe has not yet come into his hands, he does not violate the rule on surplus robes."

At that time, bhikṣus made leather sandals of various forms and various colors. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to make sandals of unusual forms or unusual colors; offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who made leather sandals from horse skin, elephant skin, and human skin. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This is not permitted. If one uses human skin, it is a sthūlātyaya. If horse or elephant skin, it is a duṣkṛta."

At that time, Upananda regularly visited a cowherd's household. He put on his robe and took his bowl and went to that house. There was a spotted calf there. Upananda gazed at it intently, desiring to have its skin for a mat. The owner asked: "Why are you staring at this calf?" He answered: "This calf's spotted color is lovely; it would make a nice mat." The owner then said: "Venerable one, you always take care of our household. How could I begrudge one calf and not give it to you?" He immediately slaughtered the calf before its mother and gave it to him. Upananda took it and returned to the monastery. The calf's mother followed behind, lowing mournfully after him. The bhikṣus asked: "Why is this cow lowing mournfully and following you?" He answered: "I don't know." They asked further: "This cow is following you and not following anyone else. How can you not know?" He then told them the whole matter. The bhikṣus rebuked him in various ways and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Upananda: "Did you really do this?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." After the Buddha rebuked him in various ways, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, it is not permitted to keep any leather."

Afterward, the bhikṣus needed small pieces of leather but did not dare use them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Small pieces of leather are permitted for making items."

There were bhikṣus who returned from outside and went barefoot onto the Sangha bedding, soiling it with mud and filth. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Indoor sandals are permitted for going in and out."

There were elderly and sick bhikṣus traveling with companions on a dangerous road who fell behind and could not keep up. Their companions said: "Venerable ones, walk quickly, lest you be robbed!" They answered: "We are old and sick and cannot walk." The companions said: "There are elephants, horses, donkeys, mules, camels, carts, and oxen here; you can ride them." The bhikṣus did not dare. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "From now on, elderly and sick bhikṣus are permitted to ride, but they must not ride female animals."

There were laypeople who offered the Sangha a leather palanquin. The bhikṣus did not dare accept it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to accept it. Remove the leather and replace it with other cloth."

There were laypeople who offered a leather palanquin specifically to a senior monk. He did not dare accept it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is also permitted to accept it privately. Remove the leather as described above."

Senior and elderly sick bhikṣus wished to ride in a palanquin into a village but did not dare. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted."

They did not know who should carry it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Have laypeople carry it."

There were bhikṣus at a dangerous place who wished to cross a river but had no boat. There were cowherds driving cattle across the water who said: "You can hold onto the cow's tail." The bhikṣus did not dare. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to hold on."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus grabbed the tails of female cattle to cross the water and jabbed their hands into the animals' wounds. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to hold onto the tails of female animals to cross water."

There were bhikṣus who wished to cross water but had no animals to hold onto. Not knowing what to do, they reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to bind grass and wood to make a raft. From now on, bhikṣus are permitted to keep floatation bladders, made from either goat skin or cow skin. Both the Sangha and the Sangha of the four directions should keep them."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, King Bimbisāra ruled the two countries of Magadha and Aṅga, which had forty-two thousand villages. Among all the powerful men of those lands, there was none who did not have faith in the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha. Only in the city of Campā was there a householder's son named Śroṇa Koṭikarṇa. This person was extremely wealthy, possessing two billion coins. The people of that time called him "Śroṇa of the Two Billion." This person had experienced comfort from birth; his hands and feet were soft and delicate, and hair grew on the soles of his feet. King Bimbisāra thought: "Within my domain, only Śroṇa of the Two Billion does not yet have faith in the Buddha's Dharma. How can I cause him to have faith and delight? If I go to him myself, it would greatly alarm him. If I summon him, he would surely become suspicious and fearful. I should simply send a general invitation to the sixty powerful households of Campā city to come observe the prince's wedding. Through this meeting, I can then instruct them in the Dharma." Having thought this, he summoned them. At that time, all his relatives said to the king: "Śroṇa of the Two Billion has never walked on the ground. Hair grows on the soles of his feet like hair on a person's head. He cannot endure the journey. May the king specially exempt this one person!" The king said: "He can ride an elephant, horse, carriage, or palanquin." They answered: "His body is extremely soft; he cannot endure even that." The king said: "This is the prince's wedding; it is essential that we meet. I permit your relatives to devise their own methods!" The relatives deliberated together: "The only way is to dig a canal and travel by boat, going several li per day. Only this way can he arrive without fatigue and fulfill the king's command!" They then brought him by this means to Rājagṛha. The relatives reported to the king: "Śroṇa of the Two Billion has just now managed to arrive. May it be permitted that he follow his household customs." The king asked: "What are his household customs?" They answered: "Cloth is spread on the ground for him to walk upon, and he is carried on a litter." The king said: "Very well!" He then ordered cloth to be spread. He also had fine, soft cloth spread as a seat for him to sit upon. The king asked: "Is it true that hair grows on the soles of your feet?" He answered: "It is true, Great King!" The king said: "I wish to see it." He answered: "Please have a trustworthy person look!" The king said: "I wish to see it myself." He answered: "Please allow me to extend my feet." The king said: "Very well." He then extended his feet and showed the king. It was indeed as reported. The radiance dazzled the king's eyes so that he could not look steadily, and a sense of wonder arose. He thought: "My country has produced such a person of great merit!" He looked around at his attendants. There were already three great householders, and with Śroṇa of the Two Billion they made four. He asked: "How much wealth does each of you have to qualify as a householder?" The first person said: "I have one billion three hundred million coins." The second said: "I have one billion four hundred million." The third said: "I have one billion four hundred million, plus one priceless maṇi jewel." Śroṇa of the Two Billion said: "I have two billion, plus five hundred maṇi jewels and one maṇi jewel bed." The king asked Śroṇa of the Two Billion: "Where did you obtain these?" He answered: "These treasures are not inherited wealth from my father, nor were they acquired through business. While I was sleeping on my upper terrace, when I awoke they were before me." Hearing these words, the king's sense of wonder doubled. He further thought: "Only the Buddha could know the merit of this person; no one else can understand." He then prepared his carriage and went to the Buddha's presence, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and said: "World-Honored One, Śroṇa of the Two Billion has five hundred maṇi jewels and one maṇi jewel bed. Where did they come from?" The Buddha said: "In his previous life, this person was in the Trāyastriṃśa Heaven and had five hundred celestial maidens who loved him dearly. When he was reborn here, the celestial maidens all wondered: 'Where has our celestial lord now been born?' Using the divine eye, they saw he had been born here. Each brought one maṇi jewel, along with the jewel bed he had formerly slept on, magically transformed it to fit his present body, placed them before him, and departed." The king, having heard the Buddha's explanation, paid his respects and withdrew. He then assembled the sixty powerful men of Campā city and told them: "I now appoint Śroṇa of the Two Billion as the chief householder of that city. You shall all respect him." He also told Śroṇa of the Two Billion and the sixty men: "I am your king. I govern with Dharma and have already brought you worldly benefit. Now the World-Honored One, the Buddha, is at Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain. Each of you should go there to seek benefit for future lives." They all accepted the command and went.

At that time, the elder Svāgata was doing walking meditation on a flat rock in the mountain. The householders came to his place and said: "Venerable one, please inform the Buddha for us: Śroṇa of the Two Billion from Campā city and the sixty householders wish to pay their respects to the World-Honored One." Svāgata vanished from the flat rock in front of the householders and emerged before the Buddha, reporting the matter in full. The Buddha said: "You may go ahead and spread a seat on the flat rock; I will come shortly after." Accepting the instruction, Svāgata vanished before the Buddha and emerged on the flat rock before the assembled householders. He spread a seat for the Buddha. The Buddha then came walking behind with his usual dignified manner. The householders thought: "This bhikṣu has great supernatural power; he vanishes and emerges from the rock as if entering water." They all revered him greatly while being neglectful of the World-Honored One. When the World-Honored One arrived and took his seat, according to the constant way of all Buddhas, he first caused them to give rise to joyful hearts, and then expounded the Dharma. The Buddha said to Svāgata: "Rise and fan the Buddha." Accepting the instruction, Svāgata rose and fanned, and in a moment manifested various supernatural transformations, just as in the case of Uruvelā Kāśyapa. He then returned before the Buddha, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, and said: "The Buddha is my great teacher; I am a disciple of the Buddha." He said this three times, then returned to his original seat. Thereupon, the householders thought: "If the disciple's supernatural power is already like this, how much more so the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Samyak-saṃbuddha!" They then turned their minds and focused their veneration on the Buddha. The Buddha expounded various wondrous Dharma teachings for them, instructing, benefiting, and delighting them, up to and including suffering, its origination, its cessation, and the path. All of them, while still seated, attained the purity of the Dharma eye and received the three refuges and the five precepts. Śroṇa of the Two Billion rose from his seat, walked barefoot to the Buddha, and prostrated. The Buddha then smiled. Svāgata thought: "What is the cause and condition of the Buddha's smile?" Having thought this, he rose from his seat, bared his right shoulder, knelt, and said to the Buddha: "What is the cause and condition of this smile?" The Buddha said to Svāgata: "For ninety-one kalpas, this is the first time Śroṇa of the Two Billion's feet have touched the ground." He further asked: "What is the cause and condition that for ninety-one kalpas his feet did not touch the ground?" The Buddha said: "In a past age, there was a Buddha, a World-Honored One, who appeared in the world named Vipaśyin. His father, the king, ruled a city twelve yojanas long and seven yojanas wide, with many people living in security and abundance. That Buddha dwelt there together with a great assembly of sixty-eight thousand bhikṣus, all of whom were arhats. The king invited the Buddha and Sangha to eat in the palace every day. At that time, there was a man in the great assembly named Suviśeṣa. Together with the people, he went to the king and said: 'The king is performing many meritorious deeds. May it please you to permit us also to participate!' The king said: 'The Buddha's Sangha numbers sixty-eight thousand. I fear you cannot manage it, or you may disturb the Sangha.' He again petitioned the king: 'I myself am capable of managing it. I beg that you certainly permit it!' The king said: 'Very well!' But still fearing they could not manage, he ordered that food be prepared as usual: 'If their offering falls short, supplement it.' Thereupon, the people's offerings surpassed the king's food. After many days like this, the king's prepared food was no longer needed. When it was Suviśeṣa's turn to make offerings, he had people dig up the road and fill it anew with fine, soft earth, plastered with fragrant clay. On both sides, he erected eighty jeweled pillars, placed multi-colored maṇi jewels atop the pillars, hung multi-colored banners, stretched multi-colored canopies to cover the road everywhere, and placed various beverages along the road. In his home, he spread sixty-eight thousand seats, one seat for each bhikṣu, and offered five hundred cauldrons of broth to each; he gave two sheets of kārpāsa and one pair of leather sandals to each bhikṣu. He also built a room for the Sangha of the four directions, with ground coverings and bedding, all of fine quality. The Suviśeṣa of that time is the present Śroṇa of the Two Billion. From then until now, he has received blessings in heaven and among humans without any difference. If he had not met me now, his feet still would not have touched the ground."

At that time, Śroṇa of the Two Billion knelt and said to the Buddha: "May I be permitted to leave home and receive full ordination!" The Buddha said: "Have your parents given their permission?" He answered: "Not yet." The Buddha said: "Without parental permission, one cannot leave home." He answered: "I shall return home and request my parents' permission." The Buddha said: "Excellent! Now is the right time." Thereupon, Śroṇa of the Two Billion bowed at the Buddha's feet, circumambulated to the right, and returned to Campā city. He said to his mother: "I now wish to leave home and study the Way." His mother said: "Stop, stop! Why would you leave home? I have only you. Even in death I would not wish to be parted from you; how could I bear a living separation? Now my wealth, treasures, slaves, servants, fields, and houses are without limit. You may give charity as you please and enjoy the five sensual pleasures." He earnestly requested three times, and finally she gave her permission. He bowed at his mother's feet, circumambulated her three times to the right, returned to the Buddha's presence, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, knelt, and said: "My mother has given permission. May you now grant me ordination and full ordination!" The Buddha said: "Come, bhikṣu! Leave home and receive full ordination. Broadly cultivate the holy life. I have well expounded the Dharma, which can completely exhaust the source of suffering!" As these words were spoken, Śroṇa of the Two Billion's hair and beard fell away of their own accord, a saṃghāṭī appeared on his body, and a bowl was in his hand. Not long after leaving home, he was diligently practicing walking meditation in the Śītavana cemetery. His feet were wounded and bled, and crows followed, pecking and swallowing the blood. Śroṇa of the Two Billion thought: "Among the Buddha's disciples, none surpasses me in diligence, yet I have not yet exhausted the source of suffering. My household has abundant wealth and treasures. Perhaps I should return to lay life and joyfully perform meritorious deeds." The Buddha, knowing his thoughts, came down from Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain and saw crows pecking and swallowing his blood. He asked Ānanda: "Why is there this blood that crows are vying to peck?" Ānanda answered: "Śroṇa of the Two Billion was doing walking meditation here; his feet were injured and bled." The World-Honored One then went to his place and asked: "Did you really have such thoughts?" He answered: "I really did, World-Honored One." The Buddha further said: "I will now ask you a question; answer as you wish. When you were a layman, were you skilled at playing the lute?" He answered: "I was skilled." The Buddha asked further: "When the strings are too tight, is the tone good?" He answered: "Not good." The Buddha asked: "When the strings are too loose, is the tone good?" He answered: "Not good." The Buddha asked: "How does one achieve a good tone?" He answered: "Neither too tight nor too loose; only then is the tone good." The Buddha said: "In my Dharma it is also like this. If too lax or too intense, how can one attain the Way? If one's diligence is moderate, before long one will exhaust suffering." After Śroṇa of the Two Billion heard the Buddha's teaching, right there at the place of walking meditation, his defilements were completely exhausted without remainder.

Because of the injuries to Śroṇa of the Two Billion's feet, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "I now permit Śroṇa of the Two Billion to wear single-layered leather sandals." Śroṇa of the Two Billion said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have renounced two billion coins, five hundred maṇi jewels, one maṇi jewel bed, twenty ladies-in-waiting, and countless attendant women. If I alone wear single-layered leather sandals, people will criticize me: 'He renounced such treasures, yet still clings to a single pair of leather sandals.' If the World-Honored One permits all bhikṣus to wear them, then I will wear them." The Buddha then praised those of few desires and contentment, praised the precepts, and praised the keeping of precepts, then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, all bhikṣus are permitted to wear single-layered leather sandals."

There were bhikṣus who wore double-layered leather sandals. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This is not permitted; offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus wore leather sandals and did walking meditation in front of and behind their upādhyāya and ācārya. Other bhikṣus also followed their example. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not wear leather sandals in front of one's upādhyāya or ācārya; offenders commit a duṣkṛta. If there are extenuating circumstances, wearing leather sandals in front of one's upādhyāya or ācārya is not an offense: if the ground has thorns, if the ground has foot-pricking grass, if the ground has gravel, if one is ill, or if it is dark."

At that time, bhikṣus wore sandals and clogs made of gold, silver, ivory, and stone. Householders saw this and criticized: "These bhikṣus are like kings and great ministers. They constantly speak of few desires and contentment, yet now they are extravagant without limit! They have no śramaṇa conduct! They violate the śramaṇa Dharma!" The senior bhikṣus heard this and rebuked them in various ways, then reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, it is not permitted to wear such sandals as described above; offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

The Buddha was in Vaiśālī.

At a certain dwelling, the ground was low and damp. When wearing leather sandals, they rotted, became foul, and bred worms. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Bhikṣus are permitted to make sandals from babbaja grass, kaśa grass, muñja grass, kuśa grass, and similar materials."

The bhikṣus wore them, but water seeped up from below and soaked their feet. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You are permitted to use untanned leather for the sole."

There were bhikṣus who kept and wore wooden clogs and wooden sandals. Walking in the monastery, they made noise and disturbed the bhikṣus who were sitting in meditation. Furthermore, a certain bhikṣu wearing wooden clogs with sharp bottoms stepped on and killed a snake at night. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, because of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, it is not permitted to wear wooden clogs or wooden sandals; offenders commit a duṣkṛta. It is permitted to wear non-walking clogs in three places: the latrine, the urinal, and the hand-and-foot-washing area."

There were bhikṣus who wore leather sandals stuffed with tūla cotton. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus whose sandal straps chafed their toes. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You are permitted to pad the straps with soft material."

There were bhikṣus whose heels cracked. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You are permitted to apply bear grease and wrap them with bear skin."

At that time, Pilindavatsa was always walking in one-pointed concentration and did not notice when he stubbed and broke his toe. The Buddha saw this and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, bhikṣus are permitted to wear boots."

The bhikṣus made boots that were very deep and high. Householders criticized, saying: "The boots these bhikṣus wear are like our high boots." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should not be made deep. They are permitted to reach just above the ankles."

There were bhikṣus who made boots shaped like high boots. Householders criticized as above. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They should be open in the front."

There were bhikṣus who wore painted leather sandals. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not wear them; offenders commit a duṣkṛta. If one obtains them, one is permitted to alter the color and then wear them."

At that time, Revata was traveling in the country of Dārvā. He encountered cold and snow, and his feet were frostbitten and damaged. He returned to the Jetavana, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and sat to one side. The Buddha asked: "Why are your feet like that?" He answered with a full account. The Buddha asked: "Do the people of that country have something they wear?" He answered: "The people of that country wear boots and leather sandals." The Buddha praised at length those of few desires and contentment, praised the precepts, and praised the keeping of precepts, then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, in countries of snow and cold, wearing boots and leather sandals is permitted. If the people of that country wear something else, you are permitted to wear

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

I notice that the prompt indicates Fascicle 21 (卷第二十一) but then references "Fascicle 022: 五分律" with no actual Chinese text provided between the delimiter lines. The body of the text to be translated is empty.

There is no Chinese text content provided for translation. If you could supply the actual Chinese text of the fascicle, I would be happy to translate it in full.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Twenty-Two

### Third Division, Part Seven: The Dharma of Medicines

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, the bhikṣus contracted autumn illnesses. The Buddha, walking through the rooms, saw them and thought: "Worldly people use ghee, oil, honey, and rock sugar as medicines. I should now permit the bhikṣus to take these." On this account, he assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and declared: "From now on, I permit ill bhikṣus to take four kinds of medicine: ghee, oil, honey, and rock sugar."

The bhikṣus took ghee, but it was bitter and foul-smelling. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit it to be thoroughly boiled, either boiling it oneself or having someone else boil it. If there is no pure place, I permit boiling it in a non-pure place."

The bhikṣus took ghee but experienced nausea and vomiting. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit using harītakī, āmalaka fruit, honey, garlic, or roasted grain flour, or whatever suitable substance, to rinse the mouth."

A certain bhikṣu contracted a heat illness and should have taken ghee. The bhikṣus begged for it on his behalf but could not obtain it; however, they obtained milk. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should have a lay attendant make ghee from it, boil it thoroughly, make it free of food-smell, receive it, and take it for seven days."

A certain bhikṣu contracted a wind illness and should have taken oil. The bhikṣus begged for it on his behalf but could not obtain it; however, they obtained sesame seeds. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should have a lay attendant make oil from them, make it free of food-smell, receive it, and take it for seven days."

A certain bhikṣu contracted a heat illness and should have taken rock sugar. The bhikṣus begged for it on his behalf but could not obtain it; however, they obtained sugarcane. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should have a lay attendant make rock sugar from it, make it free of food-smell, receive it, and take it for seven days."

The bhikṣus did not know when it should be considered fully cooked. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "When lifted with a ladle and poured out, if it flows in an unbroken stream, it is done."

Certain bhikṣus contracted wind illness and should have taken the fat of cattle, donkeys, camels, and sturgeon. The bhikṣus begged for it on their behalf but could not obtain it; however, they obtained the four kinds of fatty meat. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should have a lay attendant cook it, skim off the grease, and boil it further. If it is cooked at the proper time, boiled at the proper time, strained at the proper time, but received at an improper time, it must not be taken after staying overnight. If it is cooked at the proper time, boiled at the proper time, strained at the proper time, and received at the proper time, it may be taken for seven days."

Certain bhikṣus contracted autumn illnesses and should have taken root medicines. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All root medicines are permitted. Fruit medicines are likewise."

Certain bhikṣus contracted autumn illnesses and should have taken herbal medicines. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All herbal medicines are permitted."

A bhikṣu had a wind illness and needed to induce sweating. He reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted."

A bhikṣu had a wind illness and should have taken red salt, white salt, and various salts. He reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Taking them is permitted."

A bhikṣu had a wind illness and needed to mix urine, oil, ash, and vinegar to rub on his body. He reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Mixing and rubbing with these is permitted."

A bhikṣu had scabies and sores and wished to treat them. He reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Treatment is permitted."

A bhikṣu had a boil that should be lanced with a knife and medicine applied. He reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted."

A bhikṣu had a foot ailment and needed to wear bear-skin boots and apply bear grease; he also needed to use flour, snake skin, bear grease, and ghee, placed in a bitter gourd to soak. He reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All of these are permitted."

A bhikṣu had a boil in his private area, and a physician lanced it with a knife. The Buddha was passing by, and the physician said to the Buddha: "The knife has reached the anus." The World-Honored One looked at it. The Buddha said: "This is a place difficult to protect. If a common person were to die from this, a great benefit would be lost. From now on, lancing the private area with a knife is not permitted. One who violates this commits a sthūlātyaya!"

A bhikṣu contracted an epidemic fever. The Buddha said: "He should take emetic and purgative medicines to regulate himself, eat in measured portions, and eat according to the illness."

A bhikṣu had an eye ailment. The Buddha said: "Making eye medicine is permitted."

At that time, Revata ate rock sugar outside the proper time. Anuruddha said to him: "Do not eat outside the proper time! I have seen that when rock sugar is made, pounded rice is added to it." Revata then had doubts and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on this account, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked Anuruddha: "You say you saw that when rock sugar is made, pounded rice is added. Why do they do this?" He answered: "It is the proper method of making it." The Buddha praised fewness of desires and contentment in various ways, then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, if medicine is compounded in this manner, taking it outside the proper time is permitted."

At that time, the Elder Upāli asked the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if a proper-time medicine and an improper-time medicine are received together, for how long may they be taken?" The Buddha said: "One should follow the rule for proper-time medicine; they may not be taken outside the proper time. The same applies for seven-day medicines and lifetime medicines."

He further asked: "If an improper-time medicine and a seven-day medicine are received together, for how long may they be taken?" He answered: "One should follow the rule for improper-time medicine; they may not be kept overnight. The same applies for lifetime medicines."

He further asked: "If a seven-day medicine and a lifetime medicine are received together, for how long may they be taken?" He answered: "One should follow the rule for seven-day medicine; they may not be kept for a lifetime."

### Third Division, Part Eight: The Dharma of Food

The Buddha was in Vārāṇasī. At that time, the five bhikṣus came to the Buddha's place, bowed their heads at his feet, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, where should we eat?" The Buddha said: "I permit you to beg for food." They further said to the Buddha: "What vessel should we use?" The Buddha said: "I permit you to use a bowl."

At that time, the bhikṣus received non-glutinous rice when begging and did not dare accept it. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You are permitted to accept and eat food as you wish."

At that time, the bhikṣus, when begging, would sometimes receive various kinds of rice, or various kinds of cakes, or various kinds of roasted grain flour, or various kinds of cooked wheat and beans, or various kinds of roasted wheat and glutinous rice, or various kinds of soup, or various kinds of vinegar and sauce, or various kinds of salt, or various kinds of meat, or various kinds of fish, or various kinds of milk and cheese, or various kinds of vegetables; or various kinds of roots, such as lotus roots; or various kinds of stalks, such as sugarcane; or various kinds of fruits, such as āmra and coconut. They did not dare accept any of them. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All are permitted to be accepted and eaten as one wishes."

The Buddha was in Vaiśālī. At that time, the world was suffering famine and begging for food was difficult. The bhikṣus would put food in other places and lose it. They thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us to keep food overnight in the same place where we eat, we would not suffer this hardship." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit keeping food overnight in the same place where you eat."

The bhikṣus were preparing food in other places and losing it. They thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us to prepare food at our dwelling place, we would not suffer this hardship." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit preparing food at the dwelling place."

The bhikṣus hired people to prepare food, paying wages and providing food, but those people also stole. They thought: "If the Buddha would permit us to prepare food ourselves, we could avoid this expense." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit preparing food yourselves."

The bhikṣus, having prepared food themselves, found that hiring someone to hand them the food also required paying wages. They thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us to carry food ourselves, seeking it without hiring people to hand it to us, we could avoid this expense." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit carrying food yourselves, seeking it without hiring people to hand it over."

The bhikṣus obtained fruit from trees but had no one to hand it to them. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit taking and eating it with the perception of it being like wood."

The bhikṣus obtained fruit from pools but had no one to hand it to them. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit receiving it from the pool water."

The bhikṣus wished to eat fruit but had no lay attendant to make it pure. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit first removing the seeds, then eating it."

The Buddha was in Vaiśālī. At that time, the World-Honored One had a wind ailment, and Ānanda himself cooked medicinal porridge and offered it to the Buddha. The Buddha asked Ānanda: "Who cooked this medicine?" He answered: "I cooked it." The Buddha told Ānanda: "I previously permitted the bhikṣus to keep food overnight together, prepare food at the dwelling place, prepare food themselves, and carry food and receive it from others. Do you still employ these practices?" He answered: "We still do." The Buddha said: "What you are doing is not in accord with the Dharma! I previously permitted these during the famine. Why do you still employ these practices? From now on, one who violates this commits a duṣkṛta!"

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī and asked Ānanda: "I previously permitted the bhikṣus to take fruit from trees with the perception of wood, to receive pool fruit from pool water, and when there is no lay attendant to purify fruit, to remove the seeds first and eat. Do you still employ these practices?" He answered: "We still do." The Buddha said: "What you are doing is not in accord with the Dharma! I previously permitted these during the famine. Why do you still employ these practices? From now on, one who violates this commits a duṣkṛta!"

At that time, in the city of Śrāvastī there was an upāsikā named Suppiyā, who had faith and delight in the Buddha's Dharma, had seen the Dharma and attained its fruit, had taken refuge in the Three Treasures, and always invited the entire Sangha and provided medicines and tonics. At a later time, she entered the monastery and saw a bhikṣu who had taken emetic and purgative medicines. She asked: "Venerable, what do you need now?" He answered: "From the emetics and purgatives I am weak and depleted, and I desire to eat meat." She said: "Venerable, I will send it tomorrow. Please accept it!" She then returned home, and at dawn sent someone with money to buy meat. On that day, King Prasenajit had issued a decree: "Anyone who slaughters shall receive a severe punishment." They could not buy any and returned to report this. She gave more money and ordered a thorough search, saying: "Do not worry about the price. Even if for one coin you get only a piece as large as one coin, still buy it." Still they could not obtain any. The upāsikā thought: "I already promised yesterday. If I do not obtain it, he might die." She then took a sharp knife, entered a room, and cut flesh from her own thigh. She gave it to a maidservant to cook and had it delivered to the bhikṣu. The bhikṣu received it and ate it, and his illness was immediately cured.

At that time, her husband returned from traveling and did not see his wife walking about. He asked: "Where is Suppiyā?" They answered: "She is inside, ill." He went in and asked: "What is your affliction?" His wife answered him with a full account of what had happened. Her husband said: "I fear that from this illness there may be no recovery! While you are still alive, you should invite the Buddha and the Sangha to a midday meal tomorrow." His wife said: "Excellent!" She then had her husband invite the Buddha and the Sangha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and said: "May the Buddha and the Sangha grace us with a meal tomorrow!" The Buddha silently accepted. He returned home, and they worked through the night preparing abundant fine food and drink. At dawn, seats were arranged, and a message was sent saying the time had come. The Buddha, surrounded by the assembly of monks, went to their home and took his seat. The husband himself offered water for washing hands, but the Buddha did not accept it, saying: "Call the upāsikā Suppiyā and have her come out!" A person was sent to tell her: "The World-Honored One calls you." She answered: "Please convey my respects to the World-Honored One with my name. I am ill and cannot come out." This was reported to the Buddha, but the Buddha still called for her. This happened up to three times. Then she was carried on a cloth to the Buddha's presence. Upon seeing the World-Honored One, her wound was immediately healed; her flesh returned to its former color. She gave rise to a thought of wonder: "I have such a great teacher and such companions in the holy life!" Overjoyed and elated, she personally served the food. When the meal was finished and water was offered, she took a small seat and sat before the Buddha. The Buddha spoke verses of appreciation, as he had spoken for Verañjā, and further expounded various subtle teachings of the Dharma. Having instructed, taught, benefited, and delighted them, he returned to his dwelling.

The Buddha, on this account, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked that bhikṣu: "What did you eat yesterday?" He answered: "I ate meat." He further asked: "Was the meat delicious?" He answered: "It was delicious!" The Buddha said: "You foolish person! How could you eat human flesh without asking? From now on, if one eats meat without asking, it is a duṣkṛta! If one eats human flesh, it is a sthūlātyaya."

Certain bhikṣus ate elephant meat. When King Prasenajit's elephants died, they were always sent to the spirits and deities. Because the śramaṇas ate elephant meat, people began killing elephants. The bhikṣus had lay attendants take the meat and bring it back. The lay people who saw this criticized and reproached them, saying: "These śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, eat every kind of meat. They are worse than kites and crows! How can they consume such impure and foul things and then enter our homes? They have no śramaṇa conduct and destroy the śramaṇa Dharma!" The elder bhikṣus heard this and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on this account, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked the bhikṣus: "Is this really so?" They answered: "It is so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways and then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, eating elephant meat is a duṣkṛta!" Horse meat is likewise.

The bhikṣus ate lion meat, tiger meat, leopard meat, and bear meat. The animals, smelling the scent, then killed the bhikṣus. The lay people saw this and asked: "Why did this happen?" Some people said: "Because they ate the flesh of their kind." They then criticized and reproached them, and so forth up to the Buddha telling the bhikṣus, as above: "From now on, eating these four kinds of meat is a duṣkṛta!"

The bhikṣus ate dog meat. The dogs, smelling the scent, followed after them barking. The lay people saw this and asked: "Why do the dogs single out the bhikṣus for barking?" Some people said: "Because they ate dog meat." They then criticized and reproached them, and so forth up to the Buddha telling the bhikṣus, as above: "From now on, eating dog meat is a duṣkṛta!"

The bhikṣus ate snake meat. The lay people criticized and reproached them. The nāga king Vasavartī assumed a human form, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head, and said: "We nāgas possess great supernatural power and travel through the world in various forms and appearances. Now the bhikṣus eat snake meat; some of them might be nāgas and could harm the bhikṣus. I beg the Buddha to prohibit the bhikṣus from eating snake meat!" The Buddha expounded various subtle teachings of the Dharma for him, and having instructed, taught, benefited, and delighted him, sent him back to his abode. The Buddha, on this account, assembled the bhikṣus and conveyed the words of the nāga king Vasavartī to the bhikṣus: "From now on, eating snake meat is a duṣkṛta!"

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, a householder invited the Buddha and the Sangha. The elder bhikṣus asked the Buddha: "World-Honored One, when someone invites the Sangha, whom does the invitation cover?" The Buddha said: "All those who are rightly progressing and rightly directed are already included in the invitation." The bhikṣus thought: "Such people exist everywhere in the four directions and even in the heavens. Are we not then in violation of the rule against separate group eating?" So they dared not go. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If within the boundary one separately invites four or more persons, that is called separate group eating. If the invitation is in sequence, there is no violation."

The invited bhikṣus thought: "The bhikṣuṇīs, śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, śrāmaṇerīs, upāsakas, and upāsikās are also within the boundary. Are we not in violation of the rule against separate group eating?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the Sangha is invited, two assemblies should eat: bhikṣus and śrāmaṇeras. If the Sangha of both orders is invited, five assemblies should eat: bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇeras, and śrāmaṇerīs."

Certain ordinary meditation-practicing bhikṣus thought: "As the World-Honored One said, when the Sangha is invited, all those who are rightly progressing and rightly directed are already included. I am now an ordinary person, not yet rightly progressing or rightly directed. Am I not eating food that was not given to me?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha asked those bhikṣus: "Did you not go forth for the sake of liberation?" They answered: "We went forth for liberation." The Buddha said: "When the Sangha is invited, noble ones and meditation practitioners should all eat."

Certain ordinary sūtra-reciting bhikṣus thought: "I am not a meditation practitioner." They likewise had doubts, as above, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Sūtra reciters should also eat."

Certain ordinary bhikṣus who assisted in Sangha affairs thought: "I am neither a meditation practitioner nor a sūtra reciter." They likewise had doubts, as above, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Those who assist in Sangha affairs should also eat." He told the bhikṣus: "When the Sangha is invited, apart from those of evil conduct, all the rest of the Sangha should eat."

The Buddha traveled to the village of Anupiya. In that village there was a great minister named Subhadra, who invited the Buddha and the Sangha and prepared abundant fine food and drink. The next day at mealtime, he arranged seats and announced: "The food is prepared; may the noble ones come at the proper time." At that time, the bhikṣus had accepted other prior meal invitations and were all already full. The Buddha, surrounded by the great assembly, went to his home and took his seat. The minister Subhadra personally served the food, but the bhikṣus were unable to eat. The minister said: "Why do you not eat freely? Do you think the food is too little? Or is it not to your taste?" The bhikṣus answered: "The food is not unpalatable, nor do we think it too little. We already ate to fullness this morning; that is why we cannot eat." The minister then became angry and resentful, saying: "How could you accept my invitation and then eat to fullness elsewhere?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If you have already accepted another invitation, I permit you to sip thin gruel that does not form letters when drawn. If you receive thick gruel or food, you should tell the host: 'I have previously accepted an invitation. Please give this to others.'"

At that time, the Buddha was traveling with a great company of twelve hundred and fifty bhikṣus, going from Rājagṛha toward Vaiśālī. Between the two countries, there was a householder of Rājagṛha named Hastigāmin, who was traveling with five hundred carts from Vaiśālī. From a distance, he saw the World-Honored One, whose countenance was extraordinary, like a golden mountain. Generating a joyful mind, he approached the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and said: "World-Honored One, I have a small amount of rock sugar that I wish to offer to the World-Honored One and the Sangha of bhikṣus." The Buddha silently accepted, and the householder then personally served it. The bhikṣus did not dare accept, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha praised fewness of desires and contentment, and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit the bhikṣus, when hungry, to eat, and when thirsty, to dissolve it in water and drink." The householder distributed one jar of rock sugar throughout the Buddha's great assembly, and still it was not exhausted. He said to the Buddha: "I distributed one jar of rock sugar to the entire great assembly, and there is still some remaining. To whom else should I give it?" The Buddha said: "You may place it on ground where no living grass grows, or in water free of insects." He then, following the instruction, placed it in water free of insects. The water then boiled violently, with steam rising and hissing sounds, like heated iron plunged into water. The householder was frightened and returned to report this to the Buddha. The Buddha expounded various subtle teachings of the Dharma for him, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and delighting him, namely: a discourse on giving, a discourse on morality, a discourse on rebirth in heaven, that desire is perilous, that household life is defiling, that going forth is non-attachment. Next he taught what all Buddhas always teach: suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the path. Immediately upon his seat, far removed from dust and defilement, he obtained the purity of the Dharma-eye regarding all dharmas.

The Buddha continued on his way. There was a craftsman whose daughter was skilled at making soup. She invited the Buddha and the Sangha, offering purely soup, to serve as the afternoon meal. The bhikṣus did not dare eat, saying: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to use soup as a meal." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit eating it with the intention of it being an afternoon meal."

The Buddha gradually traveled onward and arrived at Vaiśālī, where he stayed at the Kūṭāgāraśālā on the bank of the Monkey River. There was a general named Siṃha, a disciple of the Nirgranthas. He heard that the Buddha, the World-Honored One, had come to this city, possessed of great renown, bearing the titles Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha. He exclaimed: "Excellent! I wish to see and invite such a Buddha!" He then prepared his carriage and set out. From a distance, he saw the World-Honored One, whose countenance was extraordinary, like a golden mountain. He approached the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and sat to one side. The Buddha expounded various subtle teachings of the Dharma for him, up to suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the path. Immediately upon his seat, he obtained the purity of the Dharma-eye. He then rose from his seat, knelt, and said to the Buddha: "I beg the Buddha and the Sangha to grace me with a humble meal tomorrow!" The Buddha silently accepted. The general, knowing that the Buddha had accepted, returned home and ordered his purchasing agents: "Buy all the already-dead meat available here, regardless of price." They bought it all as instructed, and throughout the night prepared various kinds of fine food. At dawn, seats were arranged, and he went himself to the Buddha and said: "The food is prepared; may the noble ones come at the proper time." The Buddha, surrounded by the Sangha of bhikṣus, went to his home and took his seat. The general personally served the food, joyful and composed. At that time, the Nirgranthas heard that General Siṃha had invited the Buddha and the Sangha and had prepared an elaborate feast. Generating jealousy and resentment, they went through the streets and alleys, shouting with all their strength: "General Siṃha has betrayed his teacher without honor! Now he has turned to serving the śramaṇa Gautama, slaughtering cattle and sheep to make offerings!" The bhikṣus heard this and did not dare eat. General Siṃha knelt and said to the Buddha: "These Nirgranthas have long slandered the Buddha. I now declare that even at the cost of my life, I would never intentionally kill. I beg the Buddha to instruct the bhikṣus not to harbor doubts, and to eat freely to their fill!" The Buddha then told the bhikṣus: "Eat to your fill as you wish." When the meal was finished and water was offered, the general took a small seat and sat before the Buddha. The Buddha spoke verses of appreciation as before. He then rose from his seat and departed.

The Buddha, on this account, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told them: "There are three kinds of meat that must not be eaten: seen, heard, or suspected. 'Seen' means one has personally seen that it was killed for oneself. 'Heard' means one has heard from a trustworthy person that it was killed for oneself. 'Suspected' means one suspects it was killed for oneself. If it is not seen, not heard, and not suspected, this is called pure meat, and eating it as one wishes is permitted. If an animal is killed for a bhikṣu, bhikṣus and śrāmaṇeras should not eat it; bhikṣuṇīs, śikṣamāṇās, śrāmaṇerīs, upāsakas, and upāsikās are permitted to eat it. If it is killed for a bhikṣuṇī, or for an upāsaka, or for an upāsikā, the same applies."

At that time, the people of the countries of Magadha, Aṅga, Kāśī, Kosala, Vajji, Malla, and Sūma heard that the Buddha had appeared in the world with great power and virtue, and that his disciples were likewise. They all came flocking to Vaiśālī. Every household in the city had carriages adorned with the seven jewels, horses, and retinues, and the city was already packed to overflowing. In addition, there were twelve thousand carriages that the city could not accommodate, and they camped outside the city walls. Everyone competed to bring proper-time food, improper-time food, seven-day food, and lifetime food to offer to the Buddha and the Sangha. These were piled up in the central courtyard, forming great heaps, scattered about in disarray, soiled with dust and mud, with birds and animals gathering to feed on them. The World-Honored One, walking through the quarters, saw this and turned to ask Ānanda: "Why is there food and drink discarded in the central courtyard?" He answered with a full account: "There is no place to store it; that is why it has come to this." The Buddha praised fewness of desires and contentment and told the bhikṣus: "I now permit using a central room, designated by a motion-and-one-resolution act, as a place for storing food."

One bhikṣu should announce: "May the noble Sangha listen! We now designate such-and-such room as the Sangha's food storage place. If the Sangha is ready, may the Sangha approve. The motion is thus."

"May the noble Sangha listen! We now designate such-and-such room as the Sangha's food storage place. Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has designated such-and-such room as the Sangha's food storage place. The Sangha approves by its silence. The matter is to be held thus."

After the Sangha's food was exhausted, the bhikṣus cooked soup and gruel and compounded medicines in it. Before meals, after meals, and during the first, middle, and last watches of the night, there were sounds of knives and chopping blocks, men and women, and dogs barking. The Buddha asked Ānanda: "Why are there these various sounds in the rooms?" He answered with a full account. The Buddha reproached them in various ways, saying: "How can you prepare food and compound medicines in the Sangha's rooms designated as food storage places? From now on, one who violates this commits a duṣkṛta!"

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. The bhikṣus contracted autumn illnesses, and for the sake of compounding medicines and preparing food suited to their illnesses, they entered the village at both proper and improper times. They encountered floods, fires, bandits, and robbers, facing dangers to their robes and bowls, dangers to their pure conduct, and dangers to their lives. There was a weaver who had built a house along the road to weave in. He saw the bhikṣus entering the village at proper and improper times and said to them: "If you have anything to do, you may do it here; if you have anything to store, you may store it here." The bhikṣus dared not, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit using a layperson's house as a pure kitchen."

Subsequently, they disturbed and disrupted the householder, interfering with his weaving. The weaver thought: "I originally built this house for weaving. Now that I can no longer weave, I should just donate it to the Sangha as a pure kitchen." He then donated it to the Sangha. The bhikṣus, since it was now a Sangha building, did not dare prepare food or compound medicines in it. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit preparing food in a donated Sangha pure kitchen."

Certain bhikṣus at a new dwelling place did not yet have a Sangha pure kitchen and did not know what to do. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "When making a new dwelling place, one should first designate a certain area as a pure ground, and then food may be placed there. If the formal act has not yet been performed, bhikṣus must not enter it until after dawn."

At a certain dwelling place, the bhikṣus had long since departed, and later-arriving bhikṣus did not know which room was the pure kitchen. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the place has been empty for twelve years, the bhikṣus are permitted to re-designate a pure kitchen as they wish."

At a certain dwelling place, there was no Sangha pure kitchen, and it had not yet been twelve years. Later-arriving bhikṣus did not know where to establish a pure ground. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If there is a place not used as a walkway or not needed for any purpose, it should provisionally be used as a pure ground."

Certain bhikṣus stored food in the pure kitchen, and it was stolen by people. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should designate a central room by formal act as a pure storage place."

Certain bhikṣus wished to designate by formal act the area within the walls of one room as a pure ground. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted."

Certain bhikṣus wished to designate by formal act the area up to the eaves of one room as a pure ground. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted."

Certain bhikṣus wished to designate the central courtyard by formal act as a pure ground. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted."

Certain bhikṣus wished to designate by formal act one corner of a room, or half a room, as a pure ground. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted."

Certain bhikṣus wished to designate by formal act a table rack as a pure place for storing food. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This is not permitted. It must be based on the ground. One who violates this commits a duṣkṛta!"

Certain bhikṣus wished to designate by formal act the upper story of a multi-story building as a pure place. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This is not permitted. One who violates this commits a duṣkṛta!"

Certain bhikṣus wished to designate by formal act the lower story of a multi-story building, together with the entire structure, as a pure place. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted."

Certain bhikṣus wished to designate a vehicle by formal act as a pure place. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This is not permitted. One who violates this commits a duṣkṛta!"

Certain bhikṣus wished to designate the entire interior of the monastery by formal act as a pure ground. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted. It should be done by a motion-and-one-resolution act."

One bhikṣu should announce: "May the noble Sangha listen! This entire dwelling place, shared in residence, shared in upoṣadha, and shared in receiving offerings, the Sangha now designates as a pure ground, excluding such-and-such place. If the Sangha is ready, may the Sangha approve. The motion is thus."

"May the noble Sangha listen! This entire dwelling place, and so forth, excluding such-and-such place. Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has designated it as a pure ground. The Sangha approves by its silence. The matter is to be held thus."

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, in the city of Bhadrika, there was a householder named Meṇḍaka, possessed of great merit. His wife, son, daughter-in-law, male servant, and female servant all possessed merit. When the householder entered the granary, grain rained down from the sky, ceasing only when he left. When his wife took out a food vessel and distributed it within and without the household, no matter how much was taken, it was always full, without end. When his son held a gold pouch and poured out gold coins, the flow never ceased. When his daughter-in-law measured out one bushel of rice, it provided food for the entire household, within and without, for one month, and still was not exhausted. When his male servant plowed, every furrow became seven furrows. When his female servant ground half a tael of incense paste and applied it within and without the household, it also did not diminish. People from all four directions heard of this and came to see. King Bimbisāra heard and also wished to go see, but without prior notice, he suddenly arrived at the householder's home with his retinue. The householder heard that the king had arrived, immediately went out to greet him, saw the king, and greeted him: "Welcome, great king! Please honor us with your visit!" The king asked: "Did you know in advance that I was coming?" He answered: "I did not know." The king said: "My army is large and cannot be suddenly provisioned!" The householder said: "I myself will supply the king and all the great ministers; my son will supply the crown prince; my wife will supply the inner palace; my servants are sufficient to supply all the soldiers; and there is grain and hay sufficient to supply the army's elephants and horses. Please deign to stay!" When the king arrived at his home and was seated, he said: "I have heard that the householder, his wife, son, daughter-in-law, and servants all possess merit. I now wish to see it all." He answered: "I dare not conceal anything!" He then ordered the granary emptied of rice, swept and cleaned on all sides, and a royal seat laid out. He invited the king to enter and sit, then entered the granary himself. Spontaneously, the five grains rained down from the sky. The king marveled greatly. He then wished to see his wife's meritorious power. He took a single vessel of rice and placed it before his wife. The wife distributed it, and the entire army was fully satisfied, yet it still was not diminished. He then wished to see his son's meritorious power. He ordered his son to take one gold pouch and pour out gold, presenting it to the king and giving it to the multitude. Everyone took as they wished, and still it was not exhausted. He then wished to see his daughter-in-law's meritorious power. He ordered her to measure out one bushel of rice, and it supplied the king and the great assembly for one month without being exhausted. He then wished to see his male servant's meritorious power. He ordered him to plow, and every furrow became seven furrows. He then wished to see his female servant's meritorious power. He ordered her to grind half a tael of incense paste, and its fragrance could be smelled within a half-yojana with no difference. She applied it to the entire assembly, and still it was not exhausted. The king and the great assembly, having witnessed such meritorious power, all marveled and praised it, and then returned to the palace.

At that time, the World-Honored One, together with a great company of twelve hundred and fifty bhikṣus, was traveling through the countryside and arrived at the city of Bhadrika. Householder Meṇḍaka heard that the Buddha, the World-Honored One, had now come and was staying under a tree in such-and-such grove. He wished to go out, pay respects, and make inquiries. The non-Buddhist practitioners heard this and went to tell him: "Do not go out to greet the śramaṇa Gautama. The śramaṇa Gautama should come to see you! Why? Because your merit surpasses all others. All śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, kings, and householders should come to your door." Hearing this, his resolve subsided. Later, he thought again: "The śramaṇa Gautama has been here a long time and has not come to see me. His way must be superior. Why should I remain at ease and not go pay my respects?" He then prepared his carriage and set out from the city. From a distance, he saw the World-Honored One, whose countenance was extraordinary, like a golden mountain. He approached the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and stood to one side. The Buddha expounded various subtle teachings of the Dharma for him, up to suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the path. Immediately upon his seat, he obtained the purity of the Dharma-eye. He then rose from his seat and said to the Buddha: "May the Buddha and the Sangha accept my invitation for a meal tomorrow!" The Buddha silently accepted. The householder returned home and prepared abundant fine food and drink. The next day at mealtime, he himself went to the Buddha and said: "May the noble ones come at the proper time!" The Buddha, surrounded by the Sangha of bhikṣus, went to his home and took his seat. The householder personally served the food. When the meal was finished and water was offered, he and his entire household sat before the Buddha. The Buddha expounded various subtle teachings of the Dharma for them, up to suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the path. They all obtained the purity of the Dharma-eye and received the Three Refuges and Five Precepts.

The householder said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, my wife, son, daughter-in-law, and servants all say: 'This is my own merit.' Whose power is it actually? I beg the Buddha to explain this!" The Buddha said: "You all share this merit!" He further asked: "How is it shared?" He answered: "Long ago in Rājagṛha, there was a weaver. The weaver had a wife, and the wife had a son, and the son had a wife. Their household had just one male servant and one female servant. They were once eating together. A pratyekabuddha came to beg for food. The weaver said: 'You all go ahead and eat; give my portion!' The wife said: 'Give my portion!' The son, and down to the male and female servants, all likewise said the same. The pratyekabuddha said: 'You have all offered your portions to me. Your good intentions are complete. But you may each just give me a small amount, so that your food is not diminished and I too shall have enough.' Each person then removed one spoonful, and his bowl was already full. The pratyekabuddha, having received the food and eaten, manifested various supernatural transformations in the sky, and then departed. Those people, when their lives ended, were reborn in the Heaven of the Four Great Kings.
