When their lifespan was exhausted, they were reborn upward in Trāyastriṃśa Heaven, and so on successively up to the Paranirmitavaśavartin Heaven. They went through this cycle seven times, and their remaining merit brought them to this present birth. The weaver and his household of that time are all of you now!' " Thereupon, the householder, in the Buddha's presence, made a request to the Sangha, saying: "I now invite the entire Sangha to practice unlimited giving. Whatever is needed, whenever and however much, all may be taken from me." The bhikṣus did not dare accept, thinking: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to accept unlimited offerings." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Accept as you wish."

Certain bhikṣus wished to travel far and asked the householder for travel provisions. The householder then had people carry gold, silver, coins, and goods to escort them. When they arrived at their destination, there was a great surplus. The escort returned and reported: "The provisions we carried now have a great excess." The householder said: "I have already given it as a donation and should not take it back. You may take it to the monastery and donate it to the Sangha." He then donated it to the Sangha. The bhikṣus did not know what to do, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A Sangha lay attendant should receive it on behalf of the Sangha and exchange it for things the Sangha needs. The bhikṣus should not be involved in the matter."

Thereupon, the World-Honored One departed from the grove and traveled through the countryside. Householder Meṇḍaka carried food provisions and followed behind, wishing to prepare a meal in the wilderness where there were no people. There were twelve hundred and fifty elephants, twelve hundred and fifty milk cows, twelve hundred and fifty bulls, and people carrying five hundred carts of various fine foods. When they reached the wilderness at the place of rest, they prepared through the night. The next day at dawn, under the shade of one elephant, a seat was arranged for one bhikṣu, and under the largest elephant's shade, a seat was arranged for the World-Honored One. When the time came and preparations were announced, the bhikṣus did not dare sit, thinking: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to sit under the shade of living beings." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You are permitted to sit!" When the assembly was seated, the householder first had one person milk one cow and offer the milk to one bhikṣu. The bhikṣus did not dare accept, thinking: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to drink hot cow's milk." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Drinking is permitted!" After they drank, the householder personally served the food. When the meal was finished and water was offered, he sat before the Buddha. The Buddha spoke verses of appreciation, as he had spoken for Verañjā, and further expounded various subtle teachings of the Dharma. Having instructed, taught, benefited, and delighted him, the householder returned home.

The Buddha, with the great assembly, rose from their seats and departed, gradually traveling northward toward the dwelling place of the matted-hair ascetic Keṇa. Keṇa heard that the Buddha, who had gone forth from the Śākya clan and attained the status of Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha, would arrive that evening. He thought: "The sages of the past who practiced the holy life did not eat after midday, but they drank various non-time beverages, namely: āmra fruit juice, jambū fruit juice, cola fruit juice, pāruṣaka fruit juice, grape juice, kola fruit juice, sugarcane juice, and honey water. The śramaṇa Gautama should also drink these. I should prepare them in advance and serve them when he arrives." Having prepared them, he went out with five hundred disciples to welcome the World-Honored One. From a distance, he saw the World-Honored One, whose countenance was extraordinary, like a golden mountain. His joy increased further. He approached the Buddha and stood to greet the World-Honored One: "Welcome, Gautama! Please honor my dwelling with your presence." The Buddha then went to his home, and together with the bhikṣus they sat in order. The ascetic then served the non-time beverages. The bhikṣus did not dare accept, thinking: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to drink non-time beverages." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Drinking is permitted!" The bhikṣus further asked: "For what reason does the Buddha permit drinking?" The Buddha said: "When thirsty, one may drink." The ascetic then thought: "I should now prepare the food of sages for the śramaṇa Gautama's monks, to serve tomorrow." He then made cooked rice of millet, foxtail millet, barnyard millet, proso millet, and kodo millet. The next day at mealtime, he announced that the food was prepared. The Buddha and the great assembly came and took their seats. The ascetic personally served the food. The bhikṣus did not dare eat, thinking: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to eat the food of sages." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Eating is permitted!" When the meal was finished and water was offered, the ascetic took a small seat and sat before the Buddha. The Buddha spoke verses of appreciation, as he had spoken for Verañjā, and further expounded various subtle teachings of the Dharma. Having instructed, taught, benefited, and delighted him, the Buddha rose from his seat and headed toward the village of Āṭumā.

At that time, there were a father and son who had been barbers and had gone forth. They heard that the World-Honored One was about to arrive and deliberated: "The laypeople here do not revere the Three Treasures. If the Buddha comes here, surely no one will prepare porridge. We should together earn money by shaving heads and use it to make porridge." Having deliberated, they went out and obtained goods to prepare porridge. At dawn, they invited the Buddha and the Sangha. After the Sangha had eaten, the Buddha asked the two bhikṣus: "How did you manage to prepare this porridge?" They gave a full account. The Buddha reproached them in various ways, saying: "What you did is not in accord with the Dharma! How could you hire yourselves out to laypeople for shaving heads? From now on, if a barber goes forth, he is not permitted to keep a razor. One who violates this commits a duṣkṛta!"

The Buddha went to the village of Pāvā. The Malla chiefs of Pāvā heard that the Buddha was about to arrive and together agreed: "Anyone who does not go out to welcome him shall be fined five hundred gold coins." They all went out with their families, great and small, to welcome the World-Honored One, bowed their heads at his feet, and sat to one side. The Buddha expounded various subtle teachings of the Dharma for them, and having instructed, taught, benefited, and delighted them, they then invited the Buddha and the Sangha for the four months of the rains retreat. The Buddha silently accepted. The Malla chiefs, knowing the Buddha had accepted, arranged provisions: some one person prepared food for one day, or for two days, up to ten days; or two persons jointly prepared for one day, up to ten persons jointly preparing for one day; or some provided only the morning meal, or some made only porridge, or some made only refreshments.

At that time, there was a man named Roja, who had been a friend of Ānanda when he was a layman. He asked the bhikṣus: "Where is Ānanda now?" They answered: "Ānanda reveres the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha. He is now behind the Buddha." He then went to Ānanda's place and bowed at his feet, standing aside. Ānanda said to him: "I am very glad to see you coming to welcome the Buddha!" He answered: "I did not come out of reverence for the Buddha. It is only that our kinsmen agreed that anyone who did not come out to welcome the Buddha would be fined five hundred gold coins. That is why I came!" When Ānanda heard this, he was saddened: "How can it be that my friend does not have faith in and reverence for the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha?" He went to the Buddha and said: "World-Honored One, I wish that this person would come to have faith in and reverence for the Buddha and the Dharma!" The Buddha said to Ānanda: "It is not difficult for this person to have faith in the Buddha. Do not worry!" The Buddha then pervaded his body with a mind of loving-kindness, then entered a room and sat with the door closed. Roja afterward thought longingly of the World-Honored One, like a calf yearning for its mother. Seeing many bhikṣus practicing walking meditation in the open, he asked: "Where is the Buddha?" The bhikṣus pointed and said: "He is in that large room with the door closed. You may go quietly, clear your throat, and knock on the door. The World-Honored One, out of compassion for you, will open the door for you." He did as instructed and the door was opened. Roja entered, clasped the Buddha's feet with his hands, announced his name, and bowed in obeisance. The Buddha expounded various subtle teachings of the Dharma for him, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and delighting him, namely a discourse on giving, up to suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the path. He was immediately far removed from dust and defilement and obtained the purity of the Dharma-eye. Having seen the Dharma and attained its fruit, he received the Three Refuges and Five Precepts. He said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I wish that the Buddha and the Sangha of bhikṣus would always accept food from me and not accept other invitations." The Buddha said: "All those who study the path have this wish. I have already accepted the invitation from these people for the four months of the rains retreat; there are no vacancies remaining." He then thought: "What other offering has the Buddha not yet accepted, so that I will not miss such a field of merit? I have not yet seen anyone offering khādanīya." He then prepared it and served it regularly at mealtimes. The bhikṣus did not dare accept, thinking: "The Buddha has not yet permitted us to eat khādanīya at mealtimes." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Eating is permitted!"

At that time, the mother Viśākhā established a limited offering for the Sangha: "At such-and-such time, take such-and-such amount." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Accept it."

Certain laypeople invited the bhikṣus to accept offerings at the donor's discretion. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not accept offerings at the donor's discretion. The donor should not offer gold, silver, jewels, women, or sensual objects to the Sangha. If a bhikṣu approves of such offerings, one who violates this commits a duṣkṛta. If it is accepted, it should be dealt with according to the Dharma!"

Certain laypeople sequentially invited the Sangha. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should appoint someone in sequence to receive invitations." The bhikṣus did not know who should be appointed. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should appoint one bhikṣu by a motion-and-one-resolution act as the person to manage the reception of invitations."

One bhikṣu should announce: "May the noble Sangha listen! The Sangha now appoints bhikṣu so-and-so as the person to manage the reception of invitations. If the Sangha is ready, may the Sangha approve. The motion is thus."

"May the noble Sangha listen! The Sangha now appoints bhikṣu so-and-so as the person to manage the reception of invitations. Those elders who approve, remain silent; those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has appointed bhikṣu so-and-so as the person to manage the reception of invitations. The Sangha approves by its silence. The matter is to be held thus."

The bhikṣus then appointed an unwise bhikṣu who did not know the proper order. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "An unwise bhikṣu should not be appointed. One who has five qualities should not be appointed: one who follows desire, follows anger, follows delusion, follows fear, or does not know who has already been appointed and who has not."

Certain laypeople regularly prepared food and sent it to the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus did not know how to handle it. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Accept it."

Certain laypeople, having newly built rooms, a heated room, or a bathhouse for the Sangha, prepared a meal celebrating the rooms and had the bhikṣus come to collect it. They did not know who should go to collect it. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The bhikṣus who reside in those rooms should go to collect it."

The Buddha was in the city of Vaiśālī. At that time, the world was suffering famine and begging for food was difficult. The brāhmaṇa bhikṣus thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us to plant fruit trees, we could use them to relieve hunger." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Planting is permitted."

When the fruit ripened, the bhikṣus had doubts because they had planted the trees with their own hands, and did not dare eat. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Eat as you wish."

Certain bhikṣus climbed trees and handled fruit, testing whether it was ripe or unripe. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not touch fruit while on the tree."

Certain bhikṣus saw fruit that had fallen on non-pure ground and had someone gather it to one place, where it stayed overnight. They did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one does not know whether the ground is pure or non-pure, eating is permitted. If one knows it is non-pure ground, one should not eat."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus took the good fruit first and ate it, and the other virtuous bhikṣus did not receive any. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should appoint one bhikṣu by a motion-and-one-resolution act as the fruit distributor. If there is much fruit, eat as you wish."

The group-of-six bhikṣus gave Sangha fruit to laypeople, and the laypeople then asked other bhikṣus for more. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Sangha fruit should not be given to laypeople. One who violates this commits a duṣkṛta!"

Certain laypeople who entered the monastery saw fruit and begged the bhikṣus for some. The bhikṣus did not dare give it, and the laypeople then criticized and reproached them. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should give it."

The Buddha was in Vaiśālī. At that time, the world was suffering famine and begging for food was difficult. The brāhmaṇa bhikṣus thought: "If the World-Honored One would permit us to plant vegetables, when hungry we could supplement our food." They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted." All the rules are as stated above in the section on planting fruit.

If laypeople plant vegetables on Sangha land, and the Sangha needs them, they may ask up to three times.

The bhikṣus had a lay attendant wash vegetables in a non-pure place. Before they finished, dawn had appeared. They had doubts and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "There is no offense."

The bhikṣus had no lay attendant and did not know who should distribute Sangha food. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A bhikṣu should receive it and then distribute it."

There were wooden vessels used for distributing food that were greasy and impure. They scrubbed them with tile and stone, and the Sangha's vessels were damaged. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not scrub with tile and stone. One should wash them with boiling water and ash."

There were jars of ghee, oil, and honey that should be covered, but there was no lay attendant. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Cover them with new material, and do not let hands come near."

A jar tipped over suddenly and there was no lay attendant to right it. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should right it oneself, but one must not let the vessel leave the ground."

A certain bhikṣu, out of anger and resentment toward another, placed his ghee jar on non-pure ground overnight, wishing to make it so that he could no longer eat it. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "For that bhikṣu who placed it there, it is impure; but the bhikṣu who owns the ghee may still eat it. The one who placed it on non-pure ground commits a duṣkṛta!"

The bhikṣus transported food and drink by boat and vehicle, but there was no lay attendant to steer the vehicle or row the boat. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If there is no lay attendant, bhikṣus are permitted to steer the vehicle themselves and row the boat themselves."

At that time, the Sangha was transporting rice by cart. A certain brāhmaṇa threw one handful of impure rice belonging to the Sangha into the cart. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If it can be separated, remove it. If it cannot be separated, remove one handful."

A wild fox stole a bhikṣu's ghee jar and placed it on non-pure ground overnight. They did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to eat; there is no offense."

There was a fruit tree whose roots were in non-pure ground, but whose branches extended over pure ground. A bhikṣu, also on non-pure ground, placed food and drink on the tree branches, and it stayed overnight. They did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The branches are attached to the roots, which are in non-pure ground. It may not be eaten."

There was a fruit tree whose roots were in pure ground, but whose branches extended over non-pure ground. A bhikṣu, also on pure ground, placed food and drink on the branches, and it stayed overnight. They did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Eating is permitted; there is no offense."

There was a fruit tree whose roots were in both pure and non-pure ground, and whose branches extended over both pure and non-pure ground. A bhikṣu, whether on pure or non-pure ground, found that fruit had fallen on both pure and non-pure ground and stayed overnight. They did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Since it was not caused by the bhikṣu, all may be eaten without offense."

A bhikṣu took earth from pure ground and built a house on non-pure ground. A bhikṣu placed food inside, thinking it was pure. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is the ground it rests on that determines purity. Since it is non-pure, the food may not be eaten!"

A bhikṣu took earth from non-pure ground and built a house on pure ground. He did not dare place food inside. He reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Placing food there is without offense."

Dead meat of deer and other animals floated on the water. There was no lay attendant to retrieve it, and a bhikṣu himself entered the water to get it. They did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "When it reaches the shore, have a lay attendant cut off the part touched by the bhikṣu's hand. The rest may be eaten without offense."

At a certain dwelling place, the bhikṣus obtained a large quantity of āmra fruit. They ate their fill and gave the rest to a lay attendant. The next day, the lay attendant made it into soup and gave it to the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus did not dare eat. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If originally there was no intention of it being returned for eating, it is all permitted to be eaten without offense."

Certain bhikṣus, at mealtimes, did not share with those who had not received food. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "These śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, eat like cats, not sharing with each other." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should share with each other. Even failing to share with one person is a duṣkṛta."

A certain brāhmaṇa entrusted roasted grain flour to a bhikṣu for safekeeping. The bhikṣu placed it on non-pure ground overnight. The next day, the brāhmaṇa came to retrieve it and distributed it to the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus, knowing it had been placed on non-pure ground, did not dare accept and eat it. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Originally it was the layperson's roasted grain flour. Accepting and eating it is permitted, without offense."

He further told the bhikṣus: "Even though something was established by me, if in another region it is not considered pure, it should not be practiced there. And even though something was not established by me, if in another region it must be practiced, it must not fail to be practiced."

### Third Division, Part Nine: The Dharma of the Kaṭhina Robe

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, if any bhikṣu needed one of the three robes, he would take it from the Sangha. At that time, Anuruddha's robe was worn out. The bhikṣus said to him: "Venerable, you may take material from the Sangha and make one." He answered: "The World-Honored One does not permit keeping extra robes. If I make it, I cannot finish it in one day, and I fear committing the offense of keeping extra robes."

Furthermore, there were bhikṣus from Pāli village, well-known to the community, who came to Śrāvastī. They had intended to arrive for the latter rains retreat but were one night short, and so they observed the rains retreat at Sāketa. After the rains retreat ended, on the sixteenth day, carrying heavy robes through mud and rain, they arrived at the Buddha's place, bowed their heads at his feet, and stood to one side. It was the World-Honored One's regular practice to console visiting bhikṣus, saying: "Was your rains retreat harmonious? Was begging for food easy? Are you not fatigued from the journey?" They answered: "The rains retreat was harmonious, and begging for food was not lacking. But on the road, we encountered mud and rain, and carrying heavy robes, we were extremely exhausted." The bhikṣus also reported Anuruddha's matter to the Buddha.

The Buddha, on account of these two matters, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus, praised fewness of desires and contentment in various ways, praised keeping the precepts, and then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit the bhikṣus to receive the kaṭhina robe. Having received the kaṭhina robe, one may be exempt from five matters without offense: eating in a separate group, eating repeatedly, going into a village

I notice that while the header mentions Fascicle 22, the actual content provided appears to be empty, and then there's a reference to Fascicle 23. There is no Chinese text provided between the separator lines for me to translate.

Could you please provide the actual Chinese text of the fascicle you would like me to translate? I would be happy to translate it in full once the source text is supplied.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Twenty-Three

### Beginning of the Fourth Division: Methods for Settling Disputes

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, the bhikṣus were fond of fighting and quarreling with each other, making mutual accusations and litigation. Bhikṣus quarreled with bhikṣus, bhikṣus quarreled with bhikṣuṇīs, bhikṣuṇīs quarreled with bhikṣuṇīs, and bhikṣuṇīs quarreled with bhikṣus.

At that time, Chanda abandoned the bhikṣus and assisted the bhikṣuṇīs. Disputes that had not yet arisen then arose; those already arisen were increased and expanded; those not yet extinguished were not extinguished; and those already extinguished arose again. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and asked them: "Is it really so?" They answered: "It is really so, World-Honored One!" The Buddha rebuked them in various ways: "What you have done is not Dharma and does not accord with the Way!" After rebuking them, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, when bhikṣus quarrel with bhikṣus, and so on up to abandoning bhikṣus and assisting bhikṣuṇīs, all commit a duṣkṛta offense. There are four kinds of disputes: first, verbal disputes; second, admonition disputes; third, offense disputes; fourth, procedural disputes."

"Because of this matter, I establish for the bhikṣus seven methods for settling disputes, so that when disputes arise they may be eliminated by these means. When the vinaya of presence should be applied, apply the vinaya of presence. When the vinaya of recollection should be applied, apply the vinaya of recollection. When the vinaya of non-insanity should be applied, apply the vinaya of non-insanity. When self-confession should be applied, apply self-confession. When the decision by majority should be applied, apply the decision by majority. When the spreading of grass on the ground should be applied, apply the spreading of grass on the ground. When the original statement procedure should be applied, apply the original statement procedure."

What is a verbal dispute? If bhikṣus quarrel together, and some say "this is Dharma," others say "this is not Dharma"; "this is vinaya," "this is not vinaya"; "this is an offense," "this is not an offense"; "this is grave," "this is not grave"; "this has a remainder," "this has no remainder"; "this is a coarse offense," "this is not a coarse offense"; "this requires a karman to emerge from the offense," "this does not require a karman to emerge from the offense"; "this was spoken by the Buddha," "this was not spoken by the Buddha"; "this was established by the Buddha," "this was not established by the Buddha"; and because of this they become angry and revile each other. This is called a verbal dispute.

What is an admonition dispute? If a bhikṣu admonishes another bhikṣu saying: "Do you recall committing a pārājika offense? Do you recall committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa, sthūlātyaya, pāyattika, pratideśanīya, duṣkṛta, or evil speech?" That bhikṣu is displeased and does not accept it, and because of this a dispute arises. This is called an admonition dispute.

What is an offense dispute? If a bhikṣu commits a pārājika and so on up to evil speech, or if in fighting and quarreling and reviling each other, unwholesome actions of body, speech, and mind arise. This is called an offense dispute.

What is a procedural dispute? The regular affairs conducted by the Sangha, all karmans and all undertakings, when disputes arise because of these. This is called a procedural dispute.

Upāli asked the Buddha: "World-Honored One, by how many methods is a verbal dispute settled?" The Buddha said: "It is settled by the vinaya of presence and by the decision by majority."

He further asked: "How is it settled?" The Buddha answered: "If a bhikṣu quarrels with another bhikṣu about whether it is Dharma or not Dharma, and so on up to whether it was established by the Buddha or not established by the Buddha, and the Sangha settles it in accordance with Dharma, in accordance with vinaya, and in accordance with the Buddha's teaching; if the other side says, 'This is Dharma, this is the Buddha's teaching,' and accepts it and agrees to it. This is called settlement by the vinaya of presence."

What is meant by "presence"? Presence has three kinds: presence of the Sangha, presence of the persons, and presence of the vinaya.

What is presence of the Sangha? The Sangha is assembled in harmony. This is called presence of the Sangha.

What is presence of the persons? The persons involved in the dispute are present. This is called presence of the persons.

What is presence of the vinaya? Whatever Dharma, whatever vinaya, whatever Buddha's teaching can be used for settlement is used for the settlement. This is called presence of the vinaya.

If after being settled in this way, someone again raises the matter, they commit a pāyattika offense.

Furthermore, if in settling a verbal dispute in this way, the bhikṣus involved in the verbal dispute are not satisfied, and they hear that in another dwelling place there is one bhikṣu, or two, or three, or even a whole assembly, who are wise and intelligent and understand the Prātimokṣa, and they think: "If we go there to settle this matter, that would be good," then they should go to settle it. They should first go to the bhikṣu who knows the Dharma in that assembly and explain the matter from beginning to end in full, and then request that the Sangha be assembled. After the Sangha has assembled, they should say to the disputing bhikṣu: "You should go away for now; we will discuss your matter together." After that bhikṣu has gone away, the Sangha should discuss: "If that bhikṣu has spoken truthfully and requests us to settle this matter in accordance with Dharma and vinaya, we should together settle it in accordance with Dharma and vinaya. If that bhikṣu has not spoken truthfully, we cannot settle this matter for him in accordance with Dharma and vinaya." The bhikṣus involved in the verbal dispute should also discuss among themselves: "If the Sangha makes a definite settlement in accordance with Dharma and vinaya, fixing a time limit of today, tomorrow, or the day after to settle our matter, we should present the full account from beginning to end before the Sangha and entrust the settlement to the Sangha." Having come before the Sangha and presented the full account from beginning to end, if the Sangha gives two kinds of opinions, some saying "It should be so!" and others saying "It should not be so!" and the matter cannot be determined, the Sangha should say: "According to whichever of the two opinions you take, each side should select four persons to form an adjudication panel." After the bhikṣus involved in the verbal dispute have each selected four persons, the Sangha should appoint them through a motion-and-one-resolution karman. First a karman should be performed for three persons, then a karman for two persons.

One bhikṣu announces: "Venerable Sangha, listen! The Sangha now appoints such-and-such bhikṣus as adjudicators, to settle that verbal dispute in accordance with Dharma and vinaya. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent and approve. This is the motion."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! The Sangha now appoints such-and-such bhikṣus as adjudicators, to settle that verbal dispute in accordance with Dharma and vinaya. Whichever elders consent, let them remain silent; those who do not consent, speak. The Sangha has appointed such-and-such bhikṣus as adjudicators. The Sangha consents, therefore it is silent. Let the matter be held thus."

At that time, the bhikṣus appointed unwise bhikṣus as adjudicators. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One possessing five qualities should be appointed: receiving others' words without anger; receiving others' words without forgetting; skillfully discerning the meaning of words; when questioned, speaking, and when not questioned, not speaking; and when speaking, not laughing. Those with the opposite of these five qualities should not be appointed. Furthermore, one possessing five qualities should be appointed: not following desire, anger, delusion, or fear, and not speaking in secret. Those with the opposite of these five qualities should not be appointed."

If bhikṣus who were not appointed, whether one, two, or three, even if wise and intelligent, sit among the assembly and wish to interfere with the adjudication, the Sangha should expel them. If furthermore there are bhikṣus who, though they have studied much and recited widely, do not understand the meaning, and interfere with the adjudication, the adjudicators should say to them: "The meaning of the sūtra is not like this!" Settling a verbal dispute in this way is called settlement by the vinaya of presence.

If in settling a verbal dispute in this way, a bhikṣu says: "This matter should be settled by the decision of the majority," the Sangha should say to him: "Your words are good! Do you understand the decision by majority?" If he says: "I do not understand," the Sangha should rebuke each person, saying: "You do not understand the decision by majority; how can you say this matter should be settled by the decision by majority?" If the Sangha does not rebuke him, all commit a duṣkṛta offense. If he says he understands, the Sangha should ask: "What constitutes the decision by majority?" He answers: "It is settled by a karman of majority vote." They further ask: "How does one determine the majority?" He answers: "By distributing voting sticks." The Sangha should further say: "What you say is good! Do you understand how many kinds of distributing and taking voting sticks are in accordance with Dharma, and how many kinds are not in accordance with Dharma?" If he says: "I do not understand," the Sangha should rebuke him as above. If he says: "I understand," the Sangha should have him explain the ten kinds of distributing and taking voting sticks that are not in accordance with Dharma and the ten kinds that are in accordance with Dharma. What are the ten kinds not in accordance with Dharma? Distributing and taking voting sticks for a trivial matter; distributing and taking voting sticks without knowing the root of the matter; distributing and taking voting sticks for a matter whose root should not be investigated; distributing and taking voting sticks in a non-Dharma manner; distributing and taking voting sticks when one desires the majority to be not in accordance with Dharma; distributing and taking voting sticks when one knows the majority is not in accordance with Dharma; distributing and taking voting sticks that would split the Sangha; distributing and taking voting sticks when one knows the Sangha will certainly split; distributing and taking voting sticks without following good teachers; distributing and taking voting sticks when the Sangha is not in harmony. The opposites of these are in accordance with Dharma.

If one possesses fourteen qualities, the Sangha should appoint that person as the one who distributes the voting sticks: knowing the ten that are in accordance with Dharma, and also not following desire, anger, delusion, or fear. These make fourteen.

The Sangha should make two kinds of voting sticks: one called "in accordance with Dharma" and the other called "not in accordance with Dharma." The announcer says: "Those who say it is in accordance with Dharma, take the 'in accordance with Dharma' voting stick; those who say it is not in accordance with Dharma, take the 'not in accordance with Dharma' voting stick." After announcing this, they are distributed. The distributor collects them and counts them in a private place. If the "not in accordance with Dharma" voting sticks are more numerous, they should further have the members get up and sit far apart from each other, and whisper privately to each person, saying: "This is the Dharma statement, the vinaya statement, the Buddha's teaching. Venerable one, you should abandon what is not Dharma, not vinaya, not the Buddha's teaching." After speaking thus, they should distribute the voting sticks again. If those holding the non-Dharma position are still more numerous, they should again announce: "The Sangha has not yet decided this matter. You may disperse as you wish; it will be decided later." In this way, one should not decide a matter by non-Dharma.

If those holding the Dharma position are more numerous, the matter should be settled by a motion-and-one-resolution karman. One bhikṣu announces: "Venerable Sangha, listen! The Sangha now settles this dispute by the decision of the majority. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent and approve. This is the motion."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! The Sangha now settles this dispute by the decision of the majority. Whichever elders consent, let them remain silent; those who do not consent, speak. The Sangha has settled this dispute by the decision of the majority. The Sangha consents, therefore it is silent. Let the matter be held thus." This is called settling a verbal dispute by the decision of the majority.

Upāli further asked the Buddha: "By how many methods is an admonition dispute settled?" The Buddha said: "It is settled by the vinaya of presence, the vinaya of recollection, the vinaya of non-insanity, and the original statement procedure."

He further asked: "How is it settled?" The Buddha answered: "If a bhikṣu goes to another bhikṣu and asks: 'Do you recall committing a grave offense, a pārājika or a borderline pārājika offense?' and he answers: 'I do not recall,' and is asked a second and third time and answers the same as before, then the Sangha should give this bhikṣu the vinaya of recollection through a motion-and-three-resolution karman, and should not punish that bhikṣu for the offense."

The bhikṣu who was questioned should go before the Sangha, bare his right shoulder, remove his leather sandals, bow at the feet of the Sangha, kneel, and say: "I, bhikṣu so-and-so, was approached by bhikṣu so-and-so who came to me two and three times and asked me: 'Do you recall committing a grave offense, a pārājika or a borderline pārājika offense?' I also answered two and three times: 'I do not recall.' Now I request from the Sangha the vinaya of recollection. May the Sangha grant me the vinaya of recollection, so that he will no longer repeatedly question me!" Thus he makes the request a second and third time.

The Sangha should examine this bhikṣu: Has he previously been without deficiency in his precepts? Is his deportment in accordance with Dharma? Are his actions of body, speech, and mind pure? Does he study the precepts well? When he speaks to one bhikṣu, to two, to three, and to the Sangha, is his speech consistent or inconsistent? If the Sangha examines him and finds that this bhikṣu previously had deficiencies in his precepts and possessed various unwholesome qualities, they should not grant it. If they find that he has not committed a pārājika or a borderline pārājika offense, they should grant the vinaya of recollection through a motion-and-three-resolution karman.

One bhikṣu announces: "Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so has requested before the Sangha, saying: 'Bhikṣu so-and-so came to me two and three times and asked me: "Do you recall committing a grave offense, a pārājika or a borderline pārājika offense?" I also answered two and three times: "I do not recall." Now I request from the Sangha the vinaya of recollection. May the Sangha grant me the vinaya of recollection, so that that bhikṣu will no longer repeatedly question me!' The Sangha now grants so-and-so the vinaya of recollection, so that that bhikṣu will no longer repeatedly question him about his offenses. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent and approve. This is the motion."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so has requested before the Sangha, saying: 'Bhikṣu so-and-so came to me two and three times and asked me: "Do you recall committing a grave offense, a pārājika or a borderline pārājika offense?" I also answered two and three times: "I do not recall." Now I request from the Sangha the vinaya of recollection. May the Sangha grant me the vinaya of recollection, so that that bhikṣu will no longer repeatedly question me!' The Sangha now grants so-and-so the vinaya of recollection, so that that bhikṣu will no longer repeatedly question him about his offenses. Whichever elders consent, let them remain silent; those who do not consent, speak." Thus a second and third time.

"The Sangha has granted bhikṣu so-and-so the vinaya of recollection. The Sangha consents, therefore it is silent. Let the matter be held thus." This is called settling an admonition dispute by the vinaya of presence and the vinaya of recollection.

If a bhikṣu goes to another bhikṣu and says: "Do you recall committing a grave offense, a pārājika or a borderline pārājika offense?" that bhikṣu answers: "I do not recall! Previously I was of deranged mind, distracted mind, or mind impaired by illness, and I committed many acts unbecoming to a śramaṇa." He is asked a second and third time and answers the same as before. For such a bhikṣu, the Sangha should grant the vinaya of non-insanity and should not punish that bhikṣu for the offense.

That bhikṣu should go before the Sangha, bare his right shoulder, remove his leather sandals, bow at the feet of the Sangha, kneel, and say: "Venerable Sangha, listen! I, bhikṣu so-and-so, was approached by bhikṣu so-and-so who came to me two and three times and asked me: 'Do you recall committing a grave offense, a pārājika or a borderline pārājika offense?' I also answered two and three times: 'I do not recall. Previously I was of deranged mind, distracted mind, or mind impaired by illness, and I committed many acts unbecoming to a śramaṇa.' Now I request from the Sangha the vinaya of non-insanity. May the Sangha grant me the vinaya of non-insanity, so that that bhikṣu will no longer repeatedly question me!" Thus he requests three times.

The Sangha should examine this bhikṣu: Has he previously been without deficiency in his precepts? Is his deportment in accordance with Dharma? Are his actions of body, speech, and mind pure? Does he study the precepts well? When he speaks to one bhikṣu, to two or three bhikṣus, and to the Sangha, is his speech consistent or inconsistent? If the Sangha finds that he previously had such various evil qualities, they should not grant it. If not, they should grant the vinaya of non-insanity through a motion-and-three-resolution karman.

One bhikṣu announces: "Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so has requested from the Sangha, saying: 'Bhikṣu so-and-so came to me two and three times and asked me: "Do you recall committing a grave offense, a pārājika or a borderline pārājika offense?" I also answered two and three times: "I do not recall. Previously I was of deranged mind, distracted mind, or mind impaired by illness, and I committed many acts unbecoming to a śramaṇa." Now I request from the Sangha the vinaya of non-insanity. May the Sangha grant me the vinaya of non-insanity, so that that bhikṣu will no longer repeatedly question me!' The Sangha now grants the vinaya of non-insanity, so that that bhikṣu will no longer repeatedly question him about his offenses. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent and approve. This is the motion."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so has requested from the Sangha, saying: 'Bhikṣu so-and-so came to me two and three times, and so on up to the Sangha now grants the vinaya of non-insanity, so that that bhikṣu will no longer repeatedly question him about his offenses.' Whichever elders consent, let them remain silent; those who do not consent, speak." Thus a second and third time.

"The Sangha has granted bhikṣu so-and-so the vinaya of non-insanity. The Sangha consents, therefore it is silent. Let the matter be held thus." This is called settling an admonition dispute by the vinaya of presence and the vinaya of non-insanity.

If a bhikṣu goes to another bhikṣu and asks: "Do you recall committing a grave offense, a pārājika or a borderline pārājika offense?" He answers: "I do not recall!" After being asked three times, he then answers: "I recall committing a minor offense." He is asked again: "You did not even confess your minor offenses to others, how much less your grave offenses. Think carefully!" He answers: "I do not recall anything at all." He is questioned further, and then answers: "I recall committing a pārājika or a borderline pārājika offense." After answering thus, he immediately says: "I do not recall committing a grave offense; what I said before was just in jest!" For such a bhikṣu, the Sangha should apply the original statement procedure.

The original statement has two kinds: one is remediable, and the other is irremediable. Since that bhikṣu's original statement was that he committed a grave offense, he should be given the motion-and-three-resolution karman of irremediable status for life.

One bhikṣu should announce: "Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so was approached by bhikṣu so-and-so who came to him and asked: 'Do you recall committing a grave offense, a pārājika or a borderline pārājika offense?' He answered: 'I do not recall.' He was asked a second time and also said he did not recall. When asked a third time, he then said: 'I do not recall committing a grave offense, but I recall committing a minor offense.' He was further asked: 'You did not even disclose your minor offenses, how much less your grave ones! Now think carefully; did you commit a grave offense or not?' He answered: 'I do not recall.' When asked again, he also said he did not recall, until the sixth time he was asked, and then he said: 'I recall committing a grave offense, a pārājika or a borderline pārājika offense.' After answering thus, he immediately said: 'I do not recall committing a grave offense; what I said before was just in jest!' The Sangha now applies the original statement procedure, the karman of irremediable status for life. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent and approve. This is the motion."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so was approached by bhikṣu so-and-so who came to him and asked: 'Do you recall committing a grave offense,' and so on up to the Sangha now applies the original statement procedure, the karman of irremediable status for life. Whichever elders consent, let them remain silent; those who do not consent, speak." Thus a second and third time.

"The Sangha has applied the original statement procedure to bhikṣu so-and-so, the karman of irremediable status for life. The Sangha consents, therefore it is silent. Let the matter be held thus." This is called settling an admonition dispute by the vinaya of presence and the original statement procedure.

Upāli asked the Buddha: "World-Honored One, by how many methods is an offense dispute settled?" The Buddha said: "It is settled by the vinaya of presence, spreading grass on the ground, and self-confession." He further asked: "How is it settled?" The Buddha answered: "If one bhikṣu goes to another bhikṣu, bares his right shoulder, kneels, and with palms joined says: 'Venerable one! I, so-and-so, have committed such-and-such an offense. I now confess and repent before you, Venerable one.' That bhikṣu should ask: 'Do you yourself see the offense?' He answers: 'I myself see the offense.' He should further ask: 'Do you wish to confess and repent?' He answers: 'I wish to confess and repent.' That bhikṣu should then say: 'Do not commit it again hereafter!' This is called settling an offense dispute by the vinaya of presence and self-confession."

If one bhikṣu goes to two bhikṣus, three bhikṣus, or a multitude of bhikṣus; or if two bhikṣus and so on up to a multitude of bhikṣus go to one bhikṣu and so on up to a multitude of bhikṣus, it is likewise.

If bhikṣus fight, quarrel, and revile each other, committing unwholesome actions of body, speech, and mind, and afterward they think: "We have fought, quarreled, and reviled each other, committing unwholesome actions of body, speech, and mind. Would it not be better to remove our offenses before the Sangha and perform the repentance of spreading grass on the ground?" These bhikṣus are permitted to remove their offenses before the Sangha, and the Sangha should grant them the repentance of spreading grass on the ground through a motion-and-one-resolution karman.

All the bhikṣus involved in the dispute should come to the Sangha, bare their right shoulders, remove their leather sandals, kneel, and say: "Venerable Sangha, listen! We fought and reviled each other, committing unwholesome actions of body, speech, and mind. Afterward we thought: 'We fought and reviled each other, committing unwholesome actions of body, speech, and mind. Would it not be better to remove our offenses before the Sangha and perform the repentance of spreading grass on the ground?' We now request from the Sangha the repentance of spreading grass on the ground." After saying this three times, they should all stretch out their hands and feet and prostrate on the ground facing the karman master, single-mindedly listening to and receiving the karman.

The karman master announces: "Venerable Sangha, listen! These bhikṣus fought and reviled each other, committing unwholesome actions of body, speech, and mind. Afterward they thought: 'We fought and reviled each other, committing unwholesome actions of body, speech, and mind. Would it not be better to remove our offenses before the Sangha and perform the repentance of spreading grass on the ground?' They now request from the Sangha the repentance of spreading grass on the ground. The Sangha now grants them the repentance of spreading grass on the ground. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent and approve. This is the motion."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! These bhikṣus fought and reviled each other, and so on up to the Sangha now grants them the repentance of spreading grass on the ground. Whichever elders consent, let them remain silent; those who do not consent, speak. The Sangha has granted these bhikṣus the repentance of spreading grass on the ground. The Sangha consents, therefore it is silent. Let the matter be held thus." This is called settling an offense dispute by the vinaya of presence and spreading grass on the ground.

What is meant by "spreading grass on the ground"? Those bhikṣus no longer discuss the origin of the fight, and the Sangha also does not further investigate the root of the matter.

Upāli asked the Buddha: "World-Honored One, by how many methods is a procedural dispute settled?" The Buddha said: "A procedural dispute is settled by any of the seven methods, as appropriate."

If one bhikṣu goes to another bhikṣu and settles a procedural dispute in a manner that is not Dharma, not vinaya, and not the Buddha's teaching, saying: "This is Dharma, this is vinaya, this is the Buddha's teaching," if the procedural dispute is settled by this means, it is called a non-Dharma settlement. If one bhikṣu goes to two bhikṣus and so on up to the Sangha; or if two bhikṣus and so on up to the Sangha go to one bhikṣu and so on up to the Sangha, it is likewise.

If one bhikṣu goes to another bhikṣu and settles a procedural dispute in a manner that is in accordance with Dharma, in accordance with vinaya, and in accordance with the Buddha's teaching, saying: "This is Dharma, this is vinaya, this is the Buddha's teaching," if the procedural dispute is settled by this means, it is called a Dharma settlement. If one bhikṣu goes to two, three bhikṣus, and so on up to the Sangha; or if two bhikṣus and so on up to the Sangha go to one bhikṣu and so on up to the Sangha, it is likewise.

### Second Section of the Fourth Division: Karman Procedures

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, a certain bhikṣu intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, without concealment. Not knowing what to do, he asked the other bhikṣus. The other bhikṣus also did not know, and they reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told them: "This bhikṣu has committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense without concealment. I now permit the Sangha to give this bhikṣu the motion-and-three-resolution karman for six nights of performing mānāpya."

The offending bhikṣu should bare his right shoulder, remove his leather sandals, bow at the feet of the Sangha, kneel, and say: "Venerable Sangha, listen! I, bhikṣu so-and-so, intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, without concealment. I now request from the Sangha six nights of performing mānāpya. May the Sangha grant me six nights of performing mānāpya!" Thus he requests three times.

One bhikṣu should announce: "Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, without concealment, and requests from the Sangha six nights of performing mānāpya. The Sangha now grants so-and-so six nights of performing mānāpya. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent and approve. This is the motion."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so intentionally emitted impure substances, and so on up to the Sangha now grants so-and-so six nights of performing mānāpya. Whichever elders consent, let them remain silent; those who do not consent, speak." Thus a second and third time.

"The Sangha has granted bhikṣu so-and-so six nights of performing mānāpya. The Sangha consents, therefore it is silent. Let the matter be held thus."

That bhikṣu should go each day to the Sangha, bare his right shoulder, remove his leather sandals, bow at the feet of the Sangha, kneel, and say: "Venerable Sangha, listen! I, bhikṣu so-and-so, intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, without concealment. I requested from the Sangha six nights of performing mānāpya, and the Sangha has granted me six nights of performing mānāpya. I have now performed mānāpya for such-and-such number of days, with such-and-such number of days remaining. May the venerable ones take note of this."

After the six nights have passed, he should request from the Sangha the abhyuddhāraṇa, saying: "Venerable Sangha, listen! I, bhikṣu so-and-so, intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, without concealment. I requested from the Sangha six nights of performing mānāpya, and the Sangha granted me six nights of performing mānāpya. I have completed the six nights of performing mānāpya. I now request from the Sangha the abhyuddhāraṇa. May the Sangha grant me the abhyuddhāraṇa!" Thus he requests three times.

One bhikṣu should announce: "Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, without concealment. He requested from the Sangha six nights of performing mānāpya, and the Sangha granted him six nights of performing mānāpya. That bhikṣu has completed the six nights of performing mānāpya and requests from the Sangha the abhyuddhāraṇa. The Sangha now grants so-and-so the abhyuddhāraṇa. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent and approve. This is the motion."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so intentionally emitted impure substances, and so on up to the Sangha now grants so-and-so the abhyuddhāraṇa. Whichever elders consent, let them remain silent; those who do not consent, speak." Thus a second and third time.

"The Sangha has granted bhikṣu so-and-so the abhyuddhāraṇa. The Sangha consents, therefore it is silent. Let the matter be held thus."

A certain bhikṣu intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, and concealed it for one night. Not knowing what to do, he asked the other bhikṣus. The other bhikṣus also did not know, and they reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told them: "I now permit the Sangha to give this bhikṣu the procedure of one night of separate dwelling through a motion-and-three-resolution karman."

The offending bhikṣu should come before the Sangha and say thus: "Venerable Sangha, listen! I, bhikṣu so-and-so, intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, and concealed it for one night. I now request from the Sangha the procedure of one night of separate dwelling. May the Sangha grant me the procedure of one night of separate dwelling!" Thus he requests three times.

One bhikṣu should announce: "Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, and concealed it for one night. He requests from the Sangha the procedure of one night of separate dwelling. The Sangha now grants so-and-so the procedure of one night of separate dwelling. If the time is right for the Sangha, may the Sangha consent and approve. This is the motion."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so intentionally emitted impure substances, and so on up to the Sangha now grants so-and-so the procedure of one night of separate dwelling. Whichever elders consent, let them remain silent; those who do not consent, speak." Thus a second and third time.

"The Sangha has granted bhikṣu so-and-so the one night of separate dwelling. The Sangha consents, therefore it is silent. Let the matter be held thus."

After that bhikṣu has completed the one night of separate dwelling, he should request from the Sangha the performance of mānāpya, and so on up to the abhyuddhāraṇa, and the Sangha should also grant it as above.

A certain bhikṣu intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, without concealment, and requested from the Sangha the performance of mānāpya. During the six nights he committed the offense again, also without concealment. Not knowing what to do, he asked the other bhikṣus. The other bhikṣus also did not know, and they reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told them: "I now permit that bhikṣu to again request from the Sangha the performance of mānāpya, and the Sangha should also grant that bhikṣu the performance of mānāpya again through a motion-and-three-resolution karman. After that bhikṣu has completed the six nights, he should further request from the Sangha the performance of the original mānāpya again, and the Sangha should also grant it through a motion-and-three-resolution karman."

That bhikṣu should again request from the Sangha, saying: "I, bhikṣu so-and-so, previously intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, without concealment, and requested from the Sangha the performance of mānāpya. The Sangha granted me the performance of mānāpya. During the six nights I committed the offense again, without concealment. I now again request from the Sangha the performance of mānāpya. May the Sangha again grant me the performance of mānāpya!" Thus he requests three times.

One bhikṣu should, according to the words of his request, perform the motion-and-three-resolution karman to grant it.

After he has completed the six nights, he should further request from the Sangha the performance of the original six nights of mānāpya, saying: "I, bhikṣu so-and-so, previously intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, without concealment. I requested from the Sangha the performance of mānāpya, and the Sangha granted me the performance of mānāpya. During the six nights I committed the offense again, without concealment. I further requested from the Sangha the performance of six nights of mānāpya. I have now completed the six nights of performing mānāpya. I now request from the Sangha the performance of the original six nights of mānāpya. May the Sangha grant me the performance of the original six nights of mānāpya!" Thus he requests three times.

One bhikṣu should, according to the words of his request, perform the motion-and-three-resolution karman to grant it.

After that bhikṣu has completed the performance of the original six nights of mānāpya, he should request the abhyuddhāraṇa as above, and the Sangha should also grant it as above.

A certain bhikṣu intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, and concealed it for one night. The Sangha granted him one night of separate dwelling. During the separate dwelling he committed the offense again and also concealed it for one night. Not knowing what to do, he asked the other bhikṣus. The other bhikṣus also did not know, and they reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus and told them: "I now permit that bhikṣu to again request from the Sangha one night of separate dwelling, and the Sangha should also grant him one night of separate dwelling again through a motion-and-three-resolution karman. After he has completed the one night of separate dwelling, he should further request from the Sangha the original one night of separate dwelling again, and the Sangha should also grant it through a motion-and-three-resolution karman."

That bhikṣu should again request from the Sangha for one night of separate dwelling, saying: "I, bhikṣu so-and-so, previously intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, and concealed it for one night. I requested from the Sangha one night of separate dwelling, and the Sangha granted me one night of separate dwelling. During the separate dwelling I committed the offense again and also concealed it for one night. I now again request from the Sangha one night of separate dwelling. May the Sangha again grant me one night of separate dwelling!" Thus he requests three times.

One bhikṣu should, according to the words of his request, perform the motion-and-three-resolution karman to grant it.

After the one night of separate dwelling is completed, he should further request from the Sangha the original one night of separate dwelling, saying: "I, bhikṣu so-and-so, previously intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, and concealed it for one night. I requested from the Sangha one night of separate dwelling, and the Sangha granted me one night of separate dwelling. During the separate dwelling I committed the offense again and also concealed it for one night. I further requested from the Sangha one night of separate dwelling, and the Sangha further granted me one night of separate dwelling. I have now completed the one night of separate dwelling. I now request from the Sangha the original one night of separate dwelling. May the Sangha grant me the original one night of separate dwelling!" Thus he requests three times.

One bhikṣu should, according to the words of his request, perform the motion-and-three-resolution karman to grant it.

After that bhikṣu has completed the original one night of separate dwelling, he should request the performance of the six nights of mānāpya as above. After completing the mānāpya, he should further request the abhyuddhāraṇa as above, and the Sangha should grant all of these through the motion-and-three-resolution karman as above.

A certain bhikṣu intentionally emitted impure substances, committing a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, and concealed it for one night. The Sangha granted him one night of separate dwelling as above. During the separate dwelling he committed the offense again and also concealed it for one night. The Sangha further granted him one night of separate dwelling again as above. After completing it, the Sangha further granted him the original one night of separate dwelling as above. After completing it, the Sangha further granted him six nights of mānāpya as above. During the mānāpya he committed the offense again, without concealment. The Sangha further granted him six nights of mānāpya as above. After he completed the six nights, the Sangha further granted him the performance of the original mānāpya as above. After completing it, then the abhyuddhāraṇa was granted as above.

If a bhikṣu commits one saṃghāvaśeṣa and so on up to many, and conceals it for two nights and so on up to many nights, when the Sangha grants separate dwelling, they should calculate only the longest period of concealment and grant separate dwelling according to that number of days. If the Sangha has granted separate dwelling and during that time the bhikṣu commits the offense again, if he conceals it, the Sangha should grant him separate dwelling again according to the number of days of concealment. If he does not conceal it, the Sangha should grant him six nights of mānāpya as above. After completing the additional separate dwelling and the mānāpya, the Sangha should further grant him the original separate dwelling again as above. After completing the original separate dwelling, grant the six nights of mānāpya. If during the mānāpya he commits the offense again, the Sangha should further grant six nights of mānāpya. After completing it, the Sangha should further grant the original mānāpya as above, and then the abhyuddhāraṇa as above.

A certain bhikṣu committed two saṃghāvaśeṣa offenses, both concealed for the same one night. He came before the Sangha and declared only one offense concealed for one night, and the Sangha granted him one night of separate dwelling. After completing the one night of separate dwelling, he felt remorseful, thinking: "I actually committed two saṃghāvaśeṣa offenses; how could I have declared only one, concealed for one night?" He came before the Sangha again and said: "I actually committed two saṃghāvaśeṣa offenses, both concealed for the same one night." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Permit him to be granted one more night of separate dwelling." That bhikṣu should explain the matter fully as above and request three times, and one bhikṣu should, according to the words of his request, perform the motion-and-three-resolution karman to grant it.

A certain bhikṣu committed one saṃghāvaśeṣa offense and concealed it for two nights. He came before the Sangha and declared that he had concealed it for one night, and the Sangha granted him one night of separate dwelling. After completing the one night of separate dwelling, he felt remorseful, thinking: "I actually concealed it for two nights; how could I have said one night?" He came before the Sangha again and reported the matter. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Permit the Sangha to grant him one more night of separate dwelling." That bhikṣu should explain the matter fully as above and request three times, and the Sangha should also, according to the words of his request, perform the motion-and-three-resolution karman to grant it.

A certain bhikṣu committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense and concealed it. He left the monastic life. Later he went forth again and received full ordination, and on the very same day he declared the offense he had previously committed. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "He should be granted separate dwelling according to the number of days he concealed the offense before he left the monastic life."

A certain bhikṣu committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense without concealment. Before performing the mānāpya, he left the monastic life. Later he returned and received full ordination, and then concealed it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "He should be granted separate dwelling according to the number of days since he received ordination again."

If a bhikṣu committed two saṃghāvaśeṣa offenses, concealed one and did not conceal the other, and left the monastic life; later returned and received full ordination, and then concealed the one he previously did not conceal, and did not conceal the one he previously concealed; then the separate dwelling should be calculated according to the number of days from the earlier concealment until the day he left the monastic life, and for the later concealment, from the day he received ordination again, and the separate dwelling should be granted accordingly.

If a bhikṣu committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, concealed it, and left the monastic life; later returned and received full ordination, and continued to conceal it; he should be granted separate dwelling according to the total number of days of concealment, both before and after.

If a bhikṣu committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, did not conceal it, and left the monastic life; later returned and received full ordination, and also did not conceal it; he should be granted six nights of mānāpya.

If a bhikṣu committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense and became a śrāmaṇera, or had a deranged mind, or a distracted mind, or a mind impaired by illness, or the Sangha imposed a karman for not seeing an offense, or a karman for not confessing, or a karman for not relinquishing wrong evil views, all these cases should be treated as stated for leaving the monastic life.

If a bhikṣu committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, knowing the number of offenses, whether one offense or different offenses, and concealed them and left the monastic life, and after returning and receiving full ordination did not conceal them; or previously did not conceal them and left the monastic life, and after receiving full ordination again concealed them; or previously half concealed and half did not conceal, and after receiving ordination again, those previously concealed are no longer concealed, and those previously not concealed are then concealed; or both before and after concealed them; the procedure for separate dwelling in all these cases is as stated above. If both before and after they were not concealed, the six nights of mānāpya are also as above. If becoming a śrāmaṇera and so on up to the karman for not relinquishing wrong evil views, it is also likewise.

If a bhikṣu left the monastic life during the period of separate dwelling, and later returned and received full ordination, the days of the earlier separate dwelling should be counted, and he only needs to complete the remainder. The same applies to the original separate dwelling. If becoming a śrāmaṇera and so on up to the karman for not relinquishing wrong evil views, it is also likewise.

If a bhikṣu left the monastic life during the performance of mānāpya, and later returned and received full ordination, completing the remaining days is also likewise; the same applies to the original mānāpya. If becoming a śrāmaṇera and so on up to the karman for not relinquishing wrong evil views, it is also likewise.

If a bhikṣu has completed the separate dwelling and the original separate dwelling but has not yet been granted the mānāpya when he leaves the monastic life, and later returns and receives full ordination, he should be made to perform the mānāpya.

If the mānāpya has been completed and the original mānāpya has been completed, but the abhyuddhāraṇa has not yet been granted, and later he returns and receives full ordination, he should be granted the abhyuddhāraṇa. If becoming a śrāmaṇera and so on up to the karman for not relinquishing wrong evil views, it is also likewise.

If a bhikṣu has committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense, knows the number of offenses, and knows the days of concealment; and properly requests from the Sangha separate dwelling, properly requests from the Sangha mānāpya, properly requests from the Sangha the original days, and properly requests from the Sangha the abhyuddhāraṇa; if the Sangha grants all of these properly, that person is called pure. If the Sangha grants even one matter improperly, that person is not called pure.

Two bhikṣus committed saṃghāvaśeṣa offenses, both concealing them. One bhikṣu knew he had committed the offense; the other did not know he had committed the offense. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The one who knew he committed the offense should be granted separate dwelling. The one who did not know he committed the offense should be granted mānāpya." The case of remembering and not remembering is also likewise.

Two bhikṣus committed saṃghāvaśeṣa offenses and concealed them. One bhikṣu perceived it correctly; the other bhikṣu perceived it differently, perhaps thinking it was a pārājika, or a sthūlātyaya, and so on up to evil speech. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The one who perceived it correctly should be granted separate dwelling. The one who perceived it differently should be granted mānāpya."

Certain bhikṣus died while performing separate dwelling, or while performing mānāpya, or while receiving the abhyuddhāraṇa. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha, asking: "Did they die with their precepts intact, or did they die with broken precepts?" The Buddha said: "All of them had their precepts intact."

A certain bhikṣu committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense and did not know the number of offenses, and also forgot how long he had concealed them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Grant him separate dwelling from the time he can recall committing the offense." The case of doubt is also likewise.

A certain bhikṣu concealed from all people. Another bhikṣu concealed from certain people but not from others. Another bhikṣu concealed in one land but not in another land. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Concealing from all people is called concealment. If one conceals only from one's preceptor, ācārya, or persons one respects and fears, it is not called concealment. Concealing from other people is called concealment. If in a certain land one does not wish to be known because many people there recognize and esteem one, and one conceals for this reason, it is not called concealment. Concealing in another land is called concealment."

I notice that the provided Chinese text between the delimiter lines is empty. There is no actual text content to translate for this fascicle.

If you could provide the Chinese text for Fascicle 23 (卷第二十三) of the *Mīśāsākavinaya* (彌沙塞部和醯五分律, Taishō T1421), I would be happy to translate it in full.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Fascicle Twenty-Four

### Fourth Division, Part Two: Karman Procedures, Continued

The Buddha was in the city of Kauśāmbī. At that time, there was a bhikṣu who had violated a precept but did not know what he had violated. He told the other bhikṣus, and among them, some said he had committed an offense and some said he had not. Those who said he had not committed an offense told him: "You have not violated any precept." Hearing this, he developed the perception that he had not violated any precept. Those who said he had committed an offense told him: "You have violated a precept. You should acknowledge your offense and repent. Do not defile the pure conduct, betray the faithful offerings of donors, and suffer through the long night of existence!" That bhikṣu said: "I have committed no offense. Why should I acknowledge an offense and repent?" The bhikṣus who maintained that he had violated a precept then performed against him a karman of non-acknowledgment of offense. After he was thus suspended, he entered the city of Kauśāmbī seeking allies and supporters, saying: "I have committed no offense, yet those bhikṣus have forcibly declared me guilty and performed against me a karman of non-acknowledgment of offense. This karman is invalid. Venerable ones! You should rescue and support me in accordance with the Dharma and Vinaya!" He also went to bhikṣus outside the city, seeking support as above. The bhikṣus who heard all came together to assist him.

At that time, the World-Honored One knew that the Sangha had split. He rose from his seat and went to the group of bhikṣus who were assisting the suspended bhikṣu, and said: "You should not speak in this way, saying that bhikṣu has not committed an offense. Even if that bhikṣu truly has not committed an offense yet was suspended, you should still tell him: 'You should acknowledge your offense and repent.' He should then think: 'If I say I do not acknowledge any offense, the Sangha will perform against me a karman of non-acknowledgment of offense, will not dwell with me, will not perform the poṣadha, pravāraṇā, or other Sangha proceedings with me.' Because of this, you have caused disputes, making the Sangha disharmonious and dwelling separately, giving rise to various defilements. You should fear this matter and should cause that person to acknowledge his offense and repent!"

After the World-Honored One said this, he went to the group of bhikṣus who had raised the charge, and said: "You should not forcibly raise charges against others! If he has truly committed an offense, the Sangha should tell him to acknowledge it. If he says: 'I have no offense to acknowledge,' the Sangha should still deliberate: 'If we perform against him a karman of non-acknowledgment of offense, not dwelling with him, not performing the poṣadha, pravāraṇā, or other Sangha proceedings with him, this will cause disputes, mutual abuse, making the Sangha disharmonious and dwelling separately, giving rise to various defilements.' You should fear this matter and set it aside without raising the charge!"

Although the bhikṣus heard the Buddha's words, the disputes still did not cease, and they raised their voices in abuse during meals, striking one another. The Buddha further declared: "You should not abuse one another. You should not raise your voices during meals. Those who violate this commit duṣkṛta! If they strike one another, it is sthūlātyaya!"

Although the bhikṣus heard the Buddha's words, the disputes still did not cease, and they conducted separate Sangha proceedings within the same boundary. The Buddha further declared: "If the Sangha has already split, and within the boundary separate karman is performed, if it accords with Dharma and Vinaya, it is also called a valid karman. Why? Because the two groups hold different views and do not dwell together. There are two kinds of not dwelling together: not dwelling together by one's own action, and not dwelling together through Sangha karman."

Although the bhikṣus heard the Buddha's words, the disputes still did not cease. The Buddha further told the bhikṣus: "You should not engage in quarrels and disputes, slandering and abusing one another. You should dwell together in harmony, gathered in one place, like water and milk blended together, jointly promoting the Teacher's instruction." The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! May you dwell in peace! Although the Buddha is the Lord of Dharma, we ourselves know our own affairs." The Buddha admonished them three times, and the bhikṣus' answers were the same each time.

The Buddha further told the bhikṣus: "Long ago in the past, in Kośala there was a king named Long Life. He ruled over a small territory with few and weak troops. The neighboring king of Kāśī, named Brahmadatta, ruled over a vast territory with strong and mighty armies, and gradually encroached upon and eventually swallowed his kingdom. King Brahmadatta captured one of King Long Life's ministers, treated him with great favor, and entrusted him with affairs of state.

"At that time, King Long Life stripped himself of his royal garments, took his wife, and disguised himself as a brahmin, going to the city of Vārāṇasī, where he lodged at the house of a potter. His wife suddenly had this thought: 'I wish to see, at the time of sunrise, at the crossroads, four kinds of troops arrayed in battle, and to drink the water from blade-sharpening.' After this thought, she told the king: 'If this wish is not fulfilled, I shall die here.' The king said: 'This cannot be accomplished! With this ailment, you will surely die.' He then told his wife: 'If Brahmadatta hears of this and learns my whereabouts, he will surely bind me with my arms behind my back, beat a donkey-skin drum, and divide my body into five pieces! Wait a moment; I shall secretly go to my former minister and ask him about this matter.' After saying this, he went and told him everything. The former minister replied: 'I need to see the queen, and I shall examine her myself.' He then went to where the queen was. Seeing the queen from a distance, he bared his right shoulder, prostrated with his head at her feet, and three times proclaimed: 'My lady! You now carry in your womb a son of great merit and virtue.
