The succession of the kingdom of Kośala is assured!' He then told the king: 'Tomorrow morning, the queen's wish shall be fulfilled.' After saying this, he returned to Brahmadatta's court and reported: 'Does the great king know? Such-and-such a star has appeared. The four kinds of troops should be assembled, and at sunrise tomorrow, at the crossroads, two battle arrays should clash, and all should sharpen their blades, in order to avert this calamity. If this is not done, there will surely be great misfortune and decline!' King Brahmadatta said: 'Then let it be done.' Thereupon the great minister ordered the four kinds of troops to be prepared. At sunrise the next morning, at the crossroads, two formations engaged in battle, and all sharpened their blades. He secretly had the queen stationed at a certain place and brought her the blade-sharpening water. The queen drank it, and the fetus was nourished. When the months were full, she gave birth to a son of extraordinarily beautiful appearance, named Long Life Prince. When he reached ten years of age, his father told him: 'Brahmadatta invaded and seized our kingdom. Your mother and I fled here, and much time has already passed. Now you have grown. If he should hear of us, father and son would both perish at once. You must go far away; do not cling to your parents!' Long Life Prince wept sorrowfully, prostrated at his parents' feet, circumambulated them three times, and departed. He studied the arts, mathematics, writing, archery, chariot-driving, elephant and horse training, and music, excelling in all beyond others. He especially served an elephant trainer and mastered all the arts of elephant training.

"King Long Life's former barber later became a barber for King Brahmadatta. When Long Life came to him to request a haircut, the barber recognized him. Not daring to conceal this, the barber inquired about his dwelling place and hiding location, and reported it to Brahmadatta. When Brahmadatta heard this, he immediately ordered their arrest. The husband and wife were bound with their arms behind their backs, a donkey-skin drum was beaten, and they were paraded through every lane and alley, then taken to the crossroads to be divided into five pieces. The executioners received the order and seized them. Long Life Prince heard of this and went to the roadside. Upon seeing them, his heart broke within him, and he thought: 'The enmity of my parents' murder is as vast as heaven and earth. How can I endure this in peace? Even a common man's sincerity is enough to move others!' He was about to sacrifice his life to avenge this humiliation. His parents saw him from afar and knew he must harbor thoughts of vengeance. They then spoke aloud like madmen, in a single voice: 'Do not see the long; do not see the short! If you repay enmity with enmity, enmity will never cease. Repay enmity with virtue, and enmity will come to an end! To follow the wishes of your parents is what makes a filial son. To follow one's impulses and indulge one's will is not what we call the Way!' At that time, all the onlookers said King Long Life was raving in fear. Only Long Life Prince heard and deeply understood his father's meaning. He restrained himself and accepted the command, and his emotions subsided for the moment. Though his heart was torn apart within, he did not show it outwardly. He suppressed his feelings and returned to the elephant trainer's place, yet he still did not forget his plan for vengeance. Later, at night in the elephant stable, he played the lute, and its sound was pure and harmonious. Brahmadatta heard it and asked: 'Who in the stable can make such music?' The answer came: 'So-and-so the elephant trainer has a disciple; it is his playing.' He immediately summoned him and had him play again. After hearing it, Brahmadatta thought: 'Since I became king, I have never heard the like of this!' So he came to trust and favor him, keeping him always at his side.

"Later, that king prepared the four kinds of troops, taking along his palace women, ministers, and crown prince, to go hunting in the wilds for sport. The troops scattered in all directions, pursuing deer. Long Life Prince was at that time driving the king's chariot, and galloped ahead of the army by three yojanas, with no one noticing. The king was exhausted and said to Long Life Prince: 'I wish to take a short nap. Can you guard me?' He answered: 'Your Majesty, just sleep peacefully. I can guard you.' The king then rested beneath a tree, pillowing his head on Long Life's lap, and fell asleep. The king's personal sword spontaneously slid out of its sheath and lay before Long Life Prince. Seeing it, Long Life Prince thought: 'This king has done me such a great wrong. Today's opportunity, how can I not seize it!' He rose and grasped the sword, about to cut the king's throat, but then he thought again: 'My parents' grace is weightier than heaven and earth. On their deathbed they commanded me: "Do not see the long; do not see the short! If you repay enmity with enmity, enmity will never cease!" How can I now violate this instruction?' He then replaced the sword and attended the sleeping king as before. The king startled awake, and Long Life Prince asked: 'Why is Your Majesty alarmed?' He answered: 'I dreamed that the son of King Long Life held a sword and was about to take my life!' Long Life Prince said: 'In this empty wilderness, how could the son of King Long Life suddenly appear? It must be a mountain spirit trying to frighten Your Majesty! Please sleep peacefully, and do not worry!' This happened three times. During the king's last sleep, Long Life Prince again thought: 'My parents' dying instruction to me was most earnest: repay enmity with virtue, and enmity will come to an end! How could I three times have nearly violated it? From now on, I shall restrain my mind, serve the king as a parent, and never again give rise to even a hair's breadth of harmful intent.' After thinking this, the king awoke greatly joyful. Long Life Prince asked: 'Why the great joy?' He answered: 'I dreamed that the son of King Long Life wished to serve me as a parent and no longer harbored any harmful intent. Therefore I am greatly joyful.' Thereupon Long Life Prince addressed the king: 'The son of King Long Life is none other than myself. Your Majesty killed my parents, and my intent was to slay you. Three times I recalled their final command, and that is why Your Majesty was spared. Yet this heart is hard to guarantee; in the future, such thoughts may arise again. I wish Your Majesty to take measures, so there will be no future cause for worry!' The king said: 'I acted unrighteously; your father and you have been benevolent. How could I now harbor any plan against you? You have granted me my life; I swear I shall never betray you!' He then returned to the army and gathered his ministers to deliberate: 'If we captured the son of King Long Life, what punishment should be applied?' Some said: 'His hands and feet should be cut off!' Some said: 'His ears and nose should be cut off!' Some said: 'He should be hacked with axes!' Some said: 'He should be skewered on a wooden spit and roasted!' The king then pointed and said: 'This person here is the son of King Long Life. This man has already spared my life, and I now must repay him with my own life. None of you may harbor any ill will toward him!' Thereupon, returning to the palace, the king gave him his daughter in marriage. With his left hand he held a golden water basin, and with his right hand a golden water pitcher, and he poured water over Long Life's hands, restoring to him his original kingdom, making him again king of Kośala. The neighboring kingdoms lived in harmony, and so it continued for generations."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Even kings and worldly people, having built up such great enmity, were still able to let it go and instead became close and affectionate. You have all left the household life, seeking the unconditioned Way. How is it that over small matters you engage in quarrels and disputes, losing the great benefit? You should abandon such thoughts and return to dwelling together in harmony, like water and milk blended together, jointly promoting the Teacher's instruction, and attain peaceful dwelling." The bhikṣus again said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! May you dwell in peace! Although the Buddha is the Lord of Dharma, we ourselves know our own affairs." They still would not desist.

The Buddha then rose into the sky and spoke these verses:

> "Hurling abuse at one another,
> There is ultimately no victorious Dharma.
> The Sangha breaks into two parts,
> And nothing does not arise from this.
>
> Breaking bones and taking lives,
> Plundering cattle, horses, and wealth,
> Destroying kingdoms, annihilating clans in enmity,
> Even these can still reach reconciliation.
>
> Like two sticks rubbed together,
> Both produce fire and burn themselves,
> With nothing untouched by the blaze;
> The fool's anger is also thus.
>
> If you abuse and revile each other,
> Grasping and never letting go,
> Enmity and calamity will never cease,
> Day and night the roots grow deeper.
>
> When reviled with all manner of harsh words,
> If one can refrain from retaliating,
> This forbearance brings no enmity,
> And existing enmity naturally vanishes.
>
> If one tries to end enmity with enmity,
> Enmity can never be ended.
> By not dwelling on enmity, it naturally ceases;
> This is the most courageous and powerful."

After the World-Honored One spoke these verses, he then by his spiritual power flew to the village of Pārileyyaka and stayed beneath a Bhadrasāla tree, without any attendants.

At that time, there was a great elephant there who was harassed by the herd of elephants. Whenever he wished to drink water, the young elephants would go ahead and muddy it. Whenever he wished to eat grass, the young elephants would eat it first, trampling and soiling it. That elephant thought: "I am now troubled by the herd. It would be better for me to go away." After thinking this, he departed. He always found clean water and fine grass, and gradually came to the Bhadrasāla grove. Seeing the Buddha, he was joyful. He fetched water for the Buddha and cleared the grass around him.

Because this elephant had left the herd and was joyful in solitude, and the Buddha also delighted in quietude, the Buddha spoke these verses:

> "Two great beings share the same mind,
> Both troubled by the annoyance of the crowd.
> Both have left to wander alone;
> Now they delight in this empty forest."

After speaking these verses, the Buddha departed from the Bhadrasāla grove and went to the city of Śrāvastī, staying at the Jetavana monastery. At that time, upāsakas, upāsikās, kings, great ministers, elders, householders, adherents of other paths, śramaṇas, and brahmins made offerings, paid respect, honored, and praised him. He received abundant food, drink, clothing, and donations, yet the World-Honored One remained unattached, like a lotus flower.

At that time, the upāsakas in the city of Kauśāmbī all said: "We have lost a great benefit. Because those bhikṣus are fond of quarreling, the World-Honored One has left. We should devise a means to make them go away." They made a joint resolution to no longer speak with them or offer them clothing and food. Those bhikṣus also said: "It is because of our faults that the World-Honored One has abandoned this place and departed. We should now go together to the Buddha's place and bitterly repent." They then put on their robes, took their bowls, and came to the Buddha's place.

At that time, Śāriputra heard that the quarreling bhikṣus were coming. Together with five hundred bhikṣus, he went to the Buddha, prostrated with his head at the Buddha's feet, and said to the Buddha: "The quarreling bhikṣus of Kauśāmbī are now coming. How should we receive them?" The Buddha told Śāriputra: "You should listen to what both groups say. If it accords with the Dharma, accords with the Vinaya, and accords with the Buddha's teaching, treat them well and be their companions." Śāriputra further asked: "World-Honored One! By how many criteria does one know that their words accord with the Dharma, the Vinaya, and the Buddha's teaching? By how many criteria does one know that their words do not accord with the Dharma, the Vinaya, or the Buddha's teaching?" The Buddha said: "If they fulfill fourteen criteria: what is Dharma they call non-Dharma, and so forth up to what is established by the Buddha they say is not established by the Buddha, this is non-Dharma, non-Vinaya, not the Buddha's teaching. If the opposite, it is Dharma, Vinaya, and the Buddha's teaching."

At that time, Mahāprajāpatī Bhikṣuṇī heard that the quarreling bhikṣus were coming. Together with five hundred bhikṣuṇīs, she went to the Buddha, prostrated with her head at the Buddha's feet, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The quarreling bhikṣus of Kauśāmbī are now coming. How should we receive them?" The Buddha said: "You should listen to what both groups say. If it accords with the Dharma, the Vinaya, and the Buddha's teaching, treat them well. You should seek five things from bhikṣus who accord with the Dharma, the Vinaya, and the Buddha's teaching: Bhikṣuṇīs should every half-month request from a Dharma-according bhikṣu a person to give them instruction. Bhikṣuṇīs must take their summer retreat relying upon a place where there are Dharma-according bhikṣus. When the retreat is concluded, they should request from Dharma-according bhikṣus the procedure for offenses seen, heard, or suspected. When a śikṣamāṇā has completed two years of training precepts, she should receive full ordination in a dual Sangha. If a bhikṣuṇī commits a gross offense, she should undergo the mānatva for half a month in a dual Sangha. After completing the mānatva, she should seek exoneration from a group of at least twenty in each of the two assemblies. If the bhikṣuṇī Sangha has any other matters, they should seek Dharma-according bhikṣus."

At that time, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada heard that the quarreling bhikṣus were coming. Together with five hundred upāsakas, he went to the Buddha, prostrated with his head at the Buddha's feet, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The quarreling bhikṣus of Kauśāmbī are now coming. How should we respectfully receive them?" The Buddha said: "You should listen to what both groups say. If it accords with the Dharma, the Vinaya, and the Buddha's teaching, accept their instruction and admonition. As for respectful reception and offerings, all should be equal. Why? It is like genuine gold cut into two pieces; there should be no difference."

Viśākhā the Mother, together with five hundred upāsikās, went to the Buddha's place and asked the Buddha. The Buddha's answer was also the same.

At that time, Ānanda saw those bhikṣus entering the city of Śrāvastī. He went and reported to the Buddha: "Those quarreling bhikṣus have already entered. How should I arrange their lodging?" The Buddha said: "They should be given rooms on the periphery. If there are not enough, give them rooms in the middle. But do not allow senior bhikṣus to be left without a place to stay!" Ānanda received the instruction and arranged their lodging.

At that time, the suspended bhikṣu, in a secluded place, had this thought: "Do I actually have an offense, or do I not? Was the suspension valid, or was it not? Was the karman lawful, or was it not? I should now carefully rely upon the sūtras and Vinaya and reflect upon this." After reflecting, he knew that he had an offense, knew that the suspension was valid, and that the karman was lawful. He then went to the bhikṣus who had been his allies and told them: "I have now acknowledged my offense! Venerable ones! Please seek reconciliation on my behalf and have the previous karman revoked." Those bhikṣus then brought him to the bhikṣus who had performed the karman of non-acknowledgment of offense, and said: "This bhikṣu has now acknowledged his offense. We ask that you revoke the previous karman!" Thereupon, the two groups of the Sangha brought the suspended bhikṣu to the Buddha's place, prostrated with their heads at his feet, and reported this matter to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "This bhikṣu committed an offense; he is not without offense. The suspension was valid; it was not invalid. The karman was accomplished; it was not unaccomplished. The Sangha should now revoke the previous karman and perform a new motion-and-resolution karman to effect reconciliation."

That bhikṣu should go to the midst of the Sangha, prostrate at the feet of the Sangha, and say: "I, bhikṣu so-and-so, had a karman of non-acknowledgment of offense performed against me by the Sangha. I now comply with the Sangha, repent, and request that the karman of non-acknowledgment of offense be revoked. May the Sangha take pity on me and revoke it!" He should speak thus three times.

One bhikṣu should announce: "Venerable Sangha, listen! These bhikṣus previously engaged in quarrels and disputes, abusing one another. Some said there was an offense; some said there was no offense. Some said the suspension was valid; some said it was not valid. Some said the karman was accomplished; some said it was not accomplished. This bhikṣu now acknowledges his offense; he does not fail to acknowledge it. The suspension was valid; it was not invalid. The karman was accomplished; it was not unaccomplished. The Sangha now revokes the karman of non-acknowledgment of offense and effects reconciliation. If the time is right for the Sangha and the Sangha approves, let it be so. The motion is thus."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! These bhikṣus previously engaged in quarrels and disputes, and so forth up to effecting reconciliation. Will the elders who approve remain silent? Those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has revoked the karman for bhikṣu so-and-so and effected reconciliation. The Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. Let the matter be held thus."

The Buddha said: "After the karman is concluded, one should immediately perform the poṣadha together in reconciliation."

At that time, Upāli asked the Buddha: "World-Honored One! How many qualities must a bhikṣu possess to be able to raise charges?" The Buddha said: "As explained in the section on the pravāraṇā."

The Buddha was in the land of Campā, staying by the bank of the Ganges River. Not far from the city of Rājagṛha, at a certain monastery, there was a bhikṣu surnamed Kāśyapa who served as the monastery superintendent. He made this vow: "May many bhikṣus from the four directions come and gather here, so that the upāsakas and upāsikās may gain much merit on this account!" The monastery was spacious, and later his vow was fulfilled. At that time, many bhikṣus who were acquaintances arrived at that monastery. Bhikṣu Kāśyapa went out to greet them, paid his respects with bows and inquiries, carried their robes and bowls, prepared bathing supplies, and served them afternoon beverages. The next day he served them morning and midday meals, and also offered them robes. This went on for many days. The visiting bhikṣus discussed among themselves: "This bhikṣu has a sense of shame, cultivates the pure conduct, and wishes us to stay long. We might as well take our summer retreat here." After this discussion, they stayed together. Later, Bhikṣu Kāśyapa thought: "These visiting bhikṣus have recovered from their fatigue and now know the local village. I cannot continue day after day soliciting offerings and serving them morning and midday meals." After thinking this, he stopped. The visiting bhikṣus resented him and discussed: "This bhikṣu wants us to leave quickly. He is certainly an evil bhikṣu, without shame, not cultivating the pure conduct! We should perform against him a karman of non-acknowledgment of offense and suspension." After their discussion, they performed the suspension against him. Bhikṣu Kāśyapa thought: "Do I have an offense or not? Was the suspension valid or not? Was the karman accomplished or not? The World-Honored One is now by the bank of the Ganges. I should go and ask him. If he gives me instruction, I shall follow it." After thinking this, he put on his robe, took his bowl, and went to the Buddha's place, prostrated with his head at the Buddha's feet, and stood to one side. The Buddha inquired: "Where have you come from? Were alms not lacking? Was the journey not tiring?" He answered: "Alms were not lacking, the journey was not tiring. Not far from the city of Rājagṛha, there is a monastery where I serve as superintendent; I have come from there." He then related to the Buddha in full the above circumstances and their causes. The Buddha said: "You have not committed an offense; there is no offense to be acknowledged. You may return and dwell there at ease." Kāśyapa received the instruction, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, circumambulated to the right, and withdrew. When the visiting bhikṣus saw him return, they discussed: "We have not done well! How could we suspend this pure, guiltless bhikṣu? We should go together to the Buddha's place to repent and remove our offense." When their summer retreat and pravāraṇā were completed, they went to the Buddha's place, prostrated with their heads at the Buddha's feet, and stood to one side. The Buddha inquired: "Were alms not lacking? Was the journey not tiring? Where did you take your summer retreat?" They answered: "Alms were not lacking, the journey was not tiring. Not far from the city of Rājagṛha, there is a monastery where we took our summer retreat." The Buddha asked: "At that monastery, did you perform against that bhikṣu a karman of non-acknowledgment of offense and suspension?" They answered: "We did." He asked further: "On what grounds did you suspend him?" They answered: "Without grounds." The Buddha reproached them in various ways: "What you have done is unlawful! You should not commit such evil acts! How could you perform against a pure, guiltless bhikṣu a karman of non-acknowledgment of offense and suspension?" The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! We are foolish and ignorant. Having done this, we are all filled with remorse. We have now come to repent. May the World-Honored One please accept our repentance!" The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If bhikṣus perform any karman without grounds, the karman is entirely invalid."

There were bhikṣus who performed at a distance a karman of reproof, a karman of banishment, a karman of dependence, a karman of suspension for an offense, and a karman of making amends; who also at a distance performed separate residence, counting from the original day, mānatva, and abbhāna; who also at a distance delimited a boundary and dissolved a boundary; who also at a distance dismissed persons appointed by the Sangha and at a distance appointed persons not yet appointed by the Sangha. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If one performs at a distance a karman of reproof, and so forth up to a karman of appointing persons not yet appointed by the Sangha, all of these are unlawful karman, and the karman is invalid."

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus performed outside the boundary an unlawful karman of reproof, and so forth up to a karman of making amends. After performing them, they came inside the boundary and told the bhikṣus: "We have performed outside the boundary a karman of reproof against such-and-such bhikṣus, and so forth up to a karman of making amends. Venerable ones! You should accept these as lawful karman." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "If bhikṣus perform outside the boundary unlawful five kinds of karman, and so forth up to a karman of appointing persons not yet appointed by the Sangha, even if they return and tell the bhikṣus within the boundary and ask them to accept these as valid karman, all are entirely invalid."

At that time, bhikṣus had one bhikṣu perform karman against one bhikṣu, and so forth up to against many bhikṣus; two bhikṣus and so forth against many bhikṣus, likewise. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All of these karman are invalid, and incur the offense of duṣkṛta." The Buddha further said: "When performing a karman, if one simply performs the karman without the motion, it is not a valid karman. If in the karman what should be stated first is stated last, or what should be stated last is stated first, all are likewise invalid. If at the time of karman there is a person eligible to object who does not agree, it is likewise invalid. All incur the offense of duṣkṛta."

At that time, bhikṣus performed karman according to other methods and other vinaya. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The karman is invalid."

At that time, bhikṣus performed unlawful separate-assembly karman, unlawful harmonious karman, lawful separate-assembly karman, and lawful harmonious karman. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "I do not permit three kinds of karman. I permit only lawful harmonious karman!"

There are five kinds of karman: unlawful karman, separate-assembly karman, quasi-lawful separate-assembly karman, quasi-lawful harmonious karman, and lawful karman.

What is unlawful karman? Those who should come do not come; those who should give their proxy do not give their proxy; there are persons eligible to object who do not agree, yet the karman is forced through. For what should be a motion-and-one-resolution karman, there is only the motion without the resolution; or only the resolution without the motion; or two motions without the resolution; or two resolutions without the motion. For what should be a motion-and-three-resolutions karman, there is only the motion without three resolutions; or only three resolutions without the motion. This is called unlawful karman.

What is separate-assembly karman? Those who should come do not come; those who should give their proxy do not give their proxy; at the time of karman, persons eligible to object do not agree, yet the karman is forced through. This is called separate-assembly karman.

What is quasi-lawful separate-assembly karman? Those who should come do not come; those who should give their proxy do not give their proxy; for motion-and-one-resolution or motion-and-three-resolutions karman, the resolution is stated before the motion; at the time of karman, persons eligible to object do not agree, yet the karman is forced through. This is called quasi-lawful separate-assembly karman.

What is quasi-lawful harmonious karman? Those who should come do come; those who should give their proxy do give their proxy; for motion-and-one-resolution or motion-and-three-resolutions karman, the resolution is stated before the motion; at the time of karman, persons eligible to object do not object. This is called quasi-lawful harmonious karman.

What is lawful karman? Those who should come do come; those who should give their proxy do give their proxy; at the time of karman, persons eligible to object do not object; for motion-and-one-resolution or motion-and-three-resolutions karman, the motion is always stated before the resolution. This is called lawful karman.

When an unlawful karman of reproof is performed against a bhikṣu, if seven persons in the Sangha dispute, one saying it is an unlawful karman, one saying it is a separate-assembly karman, one saying it is a quasi-lawful separate-assembly karman, one saying it is a quasi-lawful harmonious karman, one saying it is a lawful harmonious karman, one saying the karman is accomplished, and one saying the karman is not accomplished, among these seven persons, two speak in accordance with the Dharma: namely, the one who says it is an unlawful karman and the one who says the karman is not accomplished. When a separate-assembly karman of reproof, a quasi-lawful separate-assembly karman of reproof, or a quasi-lawful harmonious karman of reproof is performed against a bhikṣu, it is likewise.

When a lawful karman of reproof is performed against a bhikṣu and there are seven opinions, the two who speak in accordance with the Dharma are the one who says it is a lawful harmonious karman and the one who says the karman is accomplished. The same applies to a karman of banishment, a karman of dependence, a karman of suspension for an offense, and a karman of making amends.

There are bhikṣus who engage in quarrels and disputes. The bhikṣus discussed: "This bhikṣu is fond of quarreling and repeatedly causes trouble. We should come together in harmony and perform against him a lawful karman of reproof." They gathered in harmony, intending to perform a lawful karman of reproof, but instead performed an unlawful karman of reproof. The karman was invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a quasi-lawful harmonious karman of reproof; likewise the karman was invalid.

That bhikṣu then moved to another residence. The bhikṣus at the other residence discussed: "This bhikṣu is fond of quarreling. The other bhikṣus performed against him a quasi-lawful harmonious karman of reproof, but the karman was invalid. We should perform against him a lawful harmonious karman of reproof." They intended to perform a lawful harmonious karman of reproof together, but instead performed an unlawful karman of banishment; and so forth up to instead performing a lawful karman of banishment; all these karman were invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a karman of making amends; likewise.

There is a bhikṣu who commits evil actions and corrupts families. The bhikṣus discussed: "This bhikṣu commits evil actions and corrupts families. We should come together in harmony and perform against him a lawful karman of banishment." They intended to perform a lawful karman of banishment together, but instead performed an unlawful karman of banishment. The karman was invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a quasi-lawful harmonious karman of banishment; likewise.

That bhikṣu then moved to another residence. The bhikṣus at the other residence discussed: "This bhikṣu commits evil actions and corrupts families. The other bhikṣus performed against him a quasi-lawful harmonious karman, but the karman was invalid. We should perform against him a lawful karman of banishment." They intended to perform a lawful karman of banishment together, but instead performed an unlawful karman of dependence. The karman was invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a lawful karman of dependence; all these karman were invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a karman of reproof; likewise.

There is a bhikṣu who is foolish and without wisdom, committing offenses repeatedly. The bhikṣus discussed: "This bhikṣu is foolish and without wisdom, committing offenses repeatedly. We should come together in harmony and perform against him a lawful karman of dependence." They intended to perform a lawful karman of dependence together, but instead performed an unlawful karman of dependence. The karman was invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a quasi-lawful harmonious karman of dependence; likewise.

That bhikṣu then moved to another residence. The bhikṣus at the other residence discussed: "This bhikṣu is foolish and without wisdom, committing offenses repeatedly. The other bhikṣus performed against him a quasi-lawful harmonious karman of dependence, but the karman was invalid. We should perform against him a lawful karman of dependence." They intended to perform a lawful karman of dependence together, but instead performed an unlawful karman of suspension for an offense. The karman was invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a lawful karman of suspension for an offense; all these karman were likewise invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a karman of banishment; likewise.

There is a bhikṣu who commits an offense but does not acknowledge the offense, does not repent, and does not abandon evil wrong views. The bhikṣus discussed: "This bhikṣu commits offenses but does not acknowledge them, does not repent, and does not abandon evil wrong views. We should come together in harmony and perform against him a lawful karman of suspension for an offense." They intended to perform a lawful karman of suspension for an offense together, but instead performed an unlawful karman of suspension for an offense. The karman was invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a quasi-lawful harmonious karman of suspension for an offense; likewise.

That bhikṣu then moved to another residence. The bhikṣus at the other residence discussed: "This bhikṣu commits offenses, does not acknowledge them, does not repent, and does not abandon evil wrong views. The other bhikṣus performed against him a quasi-lawful harmonious karman of suspension for an offense, but the karman was invalid. We should perform against him a lawful karman of suspension for an offense." They intended to perform a lawful karman of suspension for an offense together, but instead performed an unlawful karman of making amends. The karman was invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a lawful karman of making amends; all these karman were likewise invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a karman of dependence; likewise.

There is a bhikṣu who uses coarse and abusive language to revile laypeople. The bhikṣus discussed: "This bhikṣu uses coarse and abusive language to revile laypeople. We should come together in harmony and perform against him a lawful karman of making amends." They intended to perform a lawful karman of making amends together, but instead performed an unlawful karman of making amends. The karman was invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a quasi-lawful harmonious karman of making amends; likewise.

That bhikṣu moved to another residence. The bhikṣus at the other residence discussed: "This bhikṣu uses coarse and abusive language to revile laypeople. The other bhikṣus performed against him a quasi-lawful harmonious karman of making amends, but the karman was invalid. We should perform against him a lawful karman of making amends." They intended to perform a lawful karman of making amends together, but instead performed an unlawful karman of reproof. The karman was invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a lawful karman of reproof; all these karman were likewise invalid. And so forth up to instead performing a karman of suspension for an offense; likewise.

There are five kinds of Sangha: a Sangha of four bhikṣus, a Sangha of five bhikṣus, a Sangha of ten bhikṣus, a Sangha of twenty bhikṣus, and a Sangha of an unlimited number of bhikṣus.

A Sangha of four bhikṣus: except for the ordination karman and the karman of exoneration from an offense, all other karman may be performed together.

A Sangha of five bhikṣus: in the central lands, except for the ordination karman and the karman of exoneration from an offense; in the border regions, except for the karman of exoneration from an offense; all other karman may be performed together.

A Sangha of ten bhikṣus: except for the karman of exoneration from an offense, all other karman may be performed together.

A Sangha of twenty bhikṣus: all karman may be performed together.

When a Sangha of four bhikṣus performs a karman, if the fourth person is unlawful or not in accordance with the Vinaya, the karman is invalid, and the Sangha is at fault.

Upāli asked the Buddha: "World-Honored One! When the Sangha is performing a karman, if someone objects, whose objection is valid and whose is not valid?" The Buddha said: "If the person undergoing the karman objects, it is not a valid objection. If a bhikṣu objects from behind a wall, it is not a valid objection. If a bhikṣuṇī, a śikṣamāṇā, a śrāmaṇera, or a śrāmaṇerī objects, all are not valid objections. If a bhikṣu within the same boundary objects, and at least the person sitting next to him can hear, it is a valid objection."

There are three kinds of persons for whom the karman should not be revoked; if revoked, the revocation is not valid. What are the three? If a bhikṣu has committed an offense but does not acknowledge the offense; if a bhikṣu should repent but does not repent; if a bhikṣu should abandon evil wrong views but does not abandon them. These are the three. If the karman has not yet been performed against them, it should be performed. If the karman has already been performed, it is called a well-performed karman. If the opposite applies, and the karman has not yet been revoked, it should be revoked. If it has already been revoked, it is called a well-revoked karman.

The Buddha was in the city of Śrāvastī. At that time, there were two bhikṣus, one named Pannā and the other named Rohita, who were fond of quarreling and also stirred up quarrels among others. Disputes that had not yet arisen would arise, and those already arisen would grow and expand. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked those two bhikṣus: "Is this true?" They answered: "It is true, World-Honored One!" The Buddha reproached them in various ways: "You foolish people! What you have done is unlawful. You should not commit such evil acts!" After reproaching them, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, if there are such bhikṣus, the Sangha should perform against them a karman of reproof. If they do not desist, a motion-and-three-resolutions karman should be used to impose additional punishment in accordance with their actions."

When three qualities are present, a karman of reproof should be performed: the person himself quarrels, he also stirs up quarrels among others, and this has not been just a single occurrence.

There are also three qualities: associating with evil companions, keeping company with wicked persons, and delighting in doing evil. A karman of reproof should also be performed.

There are also three qualities: violating the higher precepts, violating the higher views, and associating with and following laypeople. A karman of reproof should also be performed.

There are three ways in which a karman of reproof is invalid: the person being reproved should be present, but the reproof is performed at a distance; the Sangha should be asked, "Should a karman of reproof be performed?" but is not asked; the person being reproved should be made to state his own faults, but is not made to do so.

There are also three ways in which a karman is invalid: the karman should be performed with the person present, but is not done so; it is unlawful and with a separate assembly; the person should be made to state his own faults, but is not made to do so.

A bhikṣu who has received a karman of reproof should properly comply with the Sangha. What is proper compliance? He should not ordain others. He should not confer full ordination upon others. He should not serve as preceptor for others. He should not keep śrāmaṇeras. He should not serve as the ballot distributor; if the Sangha appoints him, he should not accept. He should not instruct bhikṣuṇīs; if the Sangha appoints him, he should not accept. He should not accept any appointments from the Sangha. When Sangha business is being conducted, he must not speak. He must not scold other bhikṣus. He must not rely on the power of the king, must not rely on his own power, and must not rely on the power of his kinsmen. He should rely only on the power of the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha. He should repent, reproach himself, not go against the Sangha's wishes, and seek the revocation of the karman.

Those two bhikṣus later properly complied with the Sangha, reformed themselves, reproached themselves, and requested the revocation of the karman of reproof. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The Sangha should revoke it with a motion-and-three-resolutions karman."

Those bhikṣus should go to the midst of the Sangha, prostrate at the feet of the Sangha, and three times request the revocation of the karman of reproof.

One bhikṣu should announce: "Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so was fond of quarreling and stirred up quarrels among others. Disputes that had not yet arisen would arise, and those already arisen would grow and expand. The Sangha previously performed against him a karman of reproof, and if he did not desist, additional punishment was imposed. So-and-so has now properly complied with the Sangha, repented, reproached himself, and requested the revocation of the karman. The Sangha now revokes the karman. If the time is right for the Sangha and the Sangha approves, let it be so. The motion is thus."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so was fond of quarreling, and so forth up to the Sangha now revokes the karman. Will the elders who approve remain silent? Those who do not approve, speak." Thus the second and the third time.

"The Sangha has revoked the karman of reproof for bhikṣu so-and-so. The Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. Let the matter be held thus."

At that time, not far from the city of Śrāvastī, there was an āmalaka grove. Beside that grove lived a householder named Citra, who had faith in and delighted in the Buddha-Dharma and constantly provided for the bhikṣus. In the āmalaka grove there was a bhikṣu named Sudhamma who was the longtime resident superintendent. Whenever the householder Citra invited the Sangha and offered clothing and food, or made gifts to individuals, he would always first inform Sudhamma.

At that time, Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana, together with five hundred bhikṣus, were traveling through that grove. The householder heard of this and went out himself to greet them. Upon arriving, he prostrated with his head at their feet, then stood to one side. They expounded the wonderful Dharma for him, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening him. He then said: "May you accept my invitation to a meal for the visiting bhikṣus tomorrow!" They silently accepted. Knowing they had accepted, he returned home, then went to bhikṣu Sudhamma's place and said: "I have invited Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana for a meal tomorrow. Venerable one! You should also come to eat." Bhikṣu Sudhamma thought: "This wicked householder's mind has already become corrupt. Previously, whenever he invited the Sangha, he would always inform me first. Now he has invited Śāriputra, Maudgalyāyana, and five hundred bhikṣus without letting me know!" After thinking this, he said: "I shall come tomorrow." The householder returned home and throughout the night prepared all sorts of fine food. There were no worldly delicacies that were not provided. At dawn he set out the seats. Bhikṣu Sudhamma had already arrived and saw the extraordinary and complete provisions. Because the householder's family pressed sesame oil, Sudhamma said: "You have every delicacy, but you lack only one thing: sesame cakes." Hearing this, the householder spoke with anger: "Venerable one! You possess an abundance of Dharma treasures, yet you utter such wicked words!" He then offered this parable: "Once there was a merchant who brought a hen from the north to the country of Pāpīyān. In Pāpīyān there were no roosters, so the hen mated with a crow and produced eggs. When the chicks hatched and grew, they tried to crow like a rooster but could not, and tried to caw like a crow but also could not. Venerable one, you are the same: you possess an abundance of Dharma treasures, yet you utter such wicked words." Bhikṣu Sudhamma heard this, became resentful and angry, and said: "The householder heaps scorn and insult upon me! How can I remain here? I shall now depart far away!" The householder replied: "Venerable one! Do not be angry. Please stay here. I shall continue to provide you with clothing and food as before." He said this repeatedly three times, but Sudhamma still refused to stay. The householder asked: "Venerable one! Where do you wish to go?" He answered: "I wish to go to the Buddha's place." The householder said: "If you go to the Buddha's place, please convey my regards to the World-Honored One. Report this matter in full, without adding or omitting anything!" He answered: "Very well!" Thereupon, the householder finished setting out the seats, went and announced that the time had come and the food was ready. Śāriputra, Maudgalyāyana, and the great assembly came in procession to the householder's dwelling and took their seats. The householder personally served the food. After the meal, water was brought around. The householder took a small bench and sat before them. He expounded various wonderful teachings, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening them. Then he rose from his seat and departed.

After the meal, bhikṣu Sudhamma returned to the grove, put on his robe, took his bowl, and went to the Buddha's place. He prostrated with his head at the Buddha's feet, sat to one side, and reported to the World-Honored One in full what the householder Citra had said. The Buddha then reproached him: "You foolish person! How could you address that householder with such base language?" He then, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, for such bhikṣus, the Sangha should perform a motion-and-three-resolutions karman of making amends, requiring an apology to the layperson."

One bhikṣu should announce: "Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so addressed such-and-such a layperson with base language. The Sangha now performs against him a karman of making amends, requiring an apology to the layperson. If the time is right for the Sangha and the Sangha approves, let it be so. The motion is thus."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so addressed such-and-such a layperson with base language. The Sangha now performs against him a karman of making amends, requiring an apology to the layperson. Will the elders who approve remain silent? Those who do not approve, speak." Thus the second and the third time.

"The Sangha has performed the karman of making amends against bhikṣu so-and-so. The Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. Let the matter be held thus."

Furthermore, a motion-and-one-resolution karman should be performed to appoint a bhikṣu to accompany that bhikṣu in apologizing to the layperson. One bhikṣu should announce: "Venerable Sangha, listen! The Sangha now appoints bhikṣu so-and-so to accompany bhikṣu so-and-so in making an apology to the layperson. If the time is right for the Sangha and the Sangha approves, let it be so. The motion is thus."

"Venerable Sangha, listen! The Sangha now appoints bhikṣu so-and-so to accompany bhikṣu so-and-so in making an apology to the layperson. Will the elders who approve remain silent? Those who do not approve, speak. The Sangha has appointed bhikṣu so-and-so to accompany bhikṣu so-and-so in making an apology to the layperson. The Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. Let the matter be held thus."

That bhikṣu should take the bhikṣu appointed by the Sangha and go to grasp the layperson's hand and apologize, saying: "I previously addressed you with base language. I now repent. Please accept my repentance!" If the layperson accepts, that is good. If the layperson does not accept, the bhikṣu appointed by the Sangha should take that bhikṣu to a place within sight but out of hearing, and teach him to perform a duṣkṛta repentance, saying: "I, bhikṣu so-and-so, used coarse and abusive language toward such-and-such layperson, committing an offense of duṣkṛta. I now repent before the Elder!" He should say this a second and a third time. Then the bhikṣu appointed by the Sangha should return alone to the layperson's place and say: "The Sangha has already disciplined that bhikṣu, and I have also further disciplined him. Please accept his repentance." After that, the bhikṣu should come again and apologize to the layperson as described above.

He should properly comply with the Sangha, as stated in the section on the karman of reproof. After he has properly complied with the Sangha, repented, reproached himself, and requested the revocation of the karman, the Sangha should revoke the karman. The karman procedure is also as stated above.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Fascicle Twenty-Five

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

---

## Part Five, First Section: The Method of Schism of the Sangha

At that time, Devadatta thought for a third time: "If I now split the Sangha of the śramaṇa Gautama, I will gain great fame and renown. Everyone will say: 'The śramaṇa Gautama possesses great supernatural power, yet Devadatta was able to split his Sangha.'" Having thought this, he spoke to his retinue: Aśvajit, Vāṣpāyana, Rohitaka, Raudhiśa, Kokālika, Khaṇḍadevīputra, Samudradatta, and others. Among them, Samudradatta was the most clever. He said to Devadatta: "The śramaṇa Gautama possesses great majesty and virtue. How could his Sangha possibly be split?" Devadatta replied: "I shall propose five practices before the Sangha, declaring they should be observed for one's entire life: first, not eating salt; second, not eating ghee or milk; third, not eating fish or meat, for if one eats these, wholesome dharmas do not arise; fourth, begging for alms, for if one accepts invitations from others, wholesome dharmas do not arise; fifth, during the eight months of spring and summer sitting in the open, and during the four months of winter dwelling in a grass hut, for if one accepts another's dwelling, wholesome dharmas do not arise. The people of these two countries, Magadha and Aṅga, all have faith in and delight in ascetic practices. If we practice these five methods, those who follow us will certainly be many, and this will be sufficient to cause a split." When Samudradatta heard this, he too thought that Devadatta could divide the Sangha equally with the Buddha and spread his fame far and near, so he went along with it. At that time Devadatta had a lay disciple named Vasudhara, who regularly made offerings to Devadatta. Devadatta next spoke to him about this, and he too agreed.

Thereupon, on the fifteenth day at the time of the poṣadha, Devadatta proclaimed the above five matters in the midst of the Sangha, distributed voting sticks himself, and declared: "Whoever approves and delights in these five practices, take this stick." At that time five hundred bhikṣus all took sticks; only Ānanda and one bhikṣu who was a stream-enterer did not. At that time Śāriputra, Maudgalyāyana, and the other great arhats were all absent from that poṣadha assembly. When Devadatta had finished distributing the sticks, he performed the poṣadha in harmony with the five hundred bhikṣus. Ānanda and the one stream-enterer bhikṣu, having not accepted the sticks, left and went to the Buddha. They bowed their heads to his feet and reported this matter to the Buddha. The Buddha then spoke a verse:

> "For good people to gather together is easy; for evil people to gather in goodness is hard.
> For evil people to gather together is easy; for good people to gather in evil is hard."

At that time, Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana heard about this matter and went to the Buddha. The Buddha saw them coming from afar and praised them, saying: "Welcome, Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana! You may go to Devadatta's assembly and bring the five hundred bhikṣus back." The two accepted his instruction, bowed at his feet, and departed. At that time, the stream-enterer bhikṣu who had followed Ānanda, seeing Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana depart, began to weep. The Buddha asked the bhikṣu: "Why do you weep?" He replied: "Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana are the Buddha's foremost disciples. Now they go to Devadatta's assembly. I fear they will learn his ways. That is why I weep." The Buddha said to the bhikṣu: "Stop, do not weep! Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana will shortly return with the five hundred bhikṣus."

Thereupon Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana went to that assembly. Samudradatta saw them from afar, ran to tell Devadatta, and said: "Now the foremost disciples of the śramaṇa Gautama, Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana, are coming. They may change the minds of the bhikṣus. You should not speak with them, nor allow them to sit down!" But Devadatta, confident that his five practices constituted the true path, did not accept this advice and paid it no mind. When Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana arrived, Devadatta said: "Welcome, Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana! Please sit here." He said: "If a person has wisdom, upon hearing what he has not heard before, he accepts and practices it. You were previously the foremost disciples of the śramaṇa Gautama. Now you have come to be my foremost disciples. Is that not excellent?" Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana remained silent and did not reply. Devadatta then supposed they had accepted his words and, imitating the Buddha's customary manner, told Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana: "You may expound the Dharma for the assembly. My back is a little sore; I shall rest." He then folded his saṅghāṭī robe four times as a pillow, lay on his right side, stacked his feet, and lay down. Not keeping his mindfulness to the fore, he soon fell deeply asleep and turned onto his left side, snoring so loudly as to startle others. At that time Maudgalyāyana displayed various supernatural powers, as is customarily described. Śāriputra expounded various wonderful teachings, good in the beginning, middle, and end, with good meaning, good flavor, and the marks of the holy life. When the five hundred bhikṣus heard this, right there on their seats they became free from dust and stain, and in regard to all dharmas obtained the purity of the Dharma-eye. Having seen the Dharma and attained its fruit, they said to one another: "We should arise and return to the Buddha." Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana then rose from their seats and, together with the five hundred bhikṣus, returned to the Buddha.

At that time Samudradatta kicked Devadatta with his toe and cursed him, saying: "Śākya slave, get up! Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana have used skillful means to take the bhikṣus away!" Devadatta started up in alarm and cursed: "You evil-desiring bhikṣus! You first had good intentions. How could you suddenly produce evil minds and use skillful means to take my bhikṣus away?" He then became greatly terrified, and hot blood flowed from his nostrils. Thereupon, with his living body, he fell into the great hell. After Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana arrived at the Buddha's place, they bowed their heads to his feet and sat to one side. Maudgalyāyana said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I wish to have these five hundred bhikṣus receive the full ordination again." The Buddha said: "They do not need to receive it again. Why? Because these five hundred bhikṣus, out of foolishness, took the voting sticks with the perception that it was Dharma. Now simply allow the Sangha to have them perform repentance for a sthūlātyaya offense."

Maudgalyāyana said to the Buddha: "How extraordinary, World-Honored One! Devadatta imitated the Buddha and fell into such a place of suffering." The Buddha said: "Devadatta did not only now imitate me and fall into hell. In the past too, he once imitated me and fell into a place of suffering." Maudgalyāyana asked: "How was that?" The Buddha said: "In a past age, in a secluded place there was a pond. A great elephant would enter the pond, take lotus roots, wash them clean and eat them, and was full of vigor and strength. Another elephant also imitated him in taking lotus roots, but ate them without washing. Because of this he became ill and died."

The Buddha then spoke a verse on this matter:

> "Do not imitate the great dragon; the great dragon cannot be imitated.
> Because of imitating the great dragon, one eats mud and suffers death."

The Buddha told Maudgalyāyana: "That great elephant was myself. The other elephant was Devadatta. In the past, because he imitated me, he met with death. Now again he imitates me and receives this great suffering." Maudgalyāyana said to the Buddha: "How extraordinary, World-Honored One! Devadatta heard the Dharma from the Buddha, recited the eighty-four thousand treasure-stores of Dharma, and attained the five supernatural powers. How could he then become arrogant toward the World-Honored One?" The Buddha said: "Not only in this life; in the past too he once learned the Dharma from me and was haughty toward me." Maudgalyāyana said to the Buddha: "How was that?" The Buddha replied: "In a past age, there was an elephant trainer who was extremely skilled in training elephants. The king provided for him generously. At that time a man came to him and said: 'Teach me to train elephants; I will be your disciple.' The elephant trainer then taught him, hiding nothing whatsoever. Once that man had learned, he developed a jealous heart and went to the king, saying: 'That man's knowledge is no greater than mine! Why is the provision given to him so far beyond what I receive?' The king then summoned the elephant trainer and asked: 'Between you and your disciple, who is the better?' He replied: 'May it please you to allow seven days, and I will demonstrate the art of training elephants!' The king consented. During those seven days, the trainer further trained the elephants with reversed commands: he said 'advance' and they retreated; he said 'retreat' and they advanced; he said 'sit' and they stood; he said 'stand' and they sat. In this manner he trained the elephants with reversed commands. When the seven days were complete, he appeared before the king and, together with the disciple, each demonstrated their art of elephant training. At first, there was no difference between the two. The king asked the disciple: 'Do you have any further different techniques?' He answered: 'No.' He then asked the trainer: 'Do you have any further different techniques?' He answered: 'Yes.' The king said: 'Then demonstrate them.' The trainer then gave the reversed commands, and all the elephants obeyed. The king then realized that the disciple's earlier claims were false, and angrily said: 'How dare you deceive me to my face?' The elephant trainer said to the king: 'This man is my disciple. When I first taught him, I hid nothing whatsoever. Before he could fully master it, he already showed contempt. Now I will give an analogy; may the king hear it! Once there was a man who, at the end of spring, wore a single layer of leather sandals. The ground was hot, the leather dried and cracked, and it cut and injured his feet. Originally intending to protect his feet, it instead caused him harm. I am the same: I first taught my disciple, hoping he would benefit, but instead he caused me harm.'"

At that time the World-Honored One spoke a verse:

> "Like a man wearing leather sandals, who originally wished to protect his feet,
> When the heat dries and tightens them, they instead injure him.
> Foolish and evil people in the world do not think of the kindness shown to them;
> They learn skills from their teacher, yet turn around and slander him."

The Buddha said: "That elephant trainer was myself. The disciple was Devadatta. Life after life he has studied under me, yet turns around and shows contempt toward me."

Maudgalyāyana said to the Buddha: "How remarkable, World-Honored One! With a single teaching of the Dharma, Śāriputra broke Devadatta's assembly." The Buddha said: "Not only in this life; in the past too he once taught the Dharma and broke his assembly." Maudgalyāyana asked: "How was that?" The Buddha replied: "In a past age, there was an archery master named Kuhāli. A man came to study the art of archery from him. For six years he taught him, saying: 'You should hold the bow like this, and nock the arrow like this.' But he had not yet taught him the method of releasing. Later, the disciple thought: 'For six years I have studied holding the bow and nocking the arrow, but have never once released. Now I shall try releasing.' He then shot an arrow at a great tree; it passed completely through and entered the ground. His master heard about it and asked: 'Have you already shot an arrow?' He replied: 'I have.' He asked again: 'Where did you shoot?' The disciple showed him the tree he had shot. The master said: 'You have mastered archery! I am the first, and you are the second.' He then told him: 'At a certain place there are five hundred bandits blocking the road, and no one dares pass through. You should go and defeat them, clearing the road. This would be a great achievement!' He then gave him a horse-drawn chariot, one beautiful woman, a golden bowl, and five hundred arrows. Thereupon the disciple rode the chariot carrying the woman, held his magical bow, wore the five hundred arrows, and departed as instructed. He happened upon the bandits as they were dividing their spoils, with scouts posted on the main road. A scout saw him from afar and rushed to report to the bandit chief. The bandit chief told his men: 'We have been bandits for a long time, but never has a man alone traveled this road with a beautiful wife. He must be brave and strong, unafraid of powerful enemies. We should let him pass and not disturb him!' The man then stopped at a certain place and had his wife take the golden bowl to the bandits' camp and, giving his name, beg for food. The bandits all lusted after his wife and coveted the golden bowl, and they deliberated: 'The woman is so beautiful, and the golden bowl is such! How can we let them go?' The bandit chief again said: 'He must have assessed his own strength and has nothing to fear, which is why he dares do this. We should restrain ourselves for now; take care not to invite disaster!' The bandit band, hearing this, gave her a bowlful of fine rice. The wife carried the food back, and the man sent her again with the message: 'You are dividing your spoils; give me a share!' The bandits were greatly enraged: 'Who is this man? With just one person, he dares to make light of our entire band! We should kill him together; let us not bear this humiliation!' The bandit chief spoke to them as before, and they again gave a share. The wife obtained the share and returned, and the man again sent her to tell the bandits: 'Come fight with me; let us not leave each other be.' The bandits again said: 'This man increasingly shows contempt; this cannot be endured any longer!' The bandit chief tried to reason with them but could not make them stop. Enraged and heedless of danger, they gathered their full strength and went to attack the man. The man then shot one arrow and killed one man; with four hundred and ninety-nine arrows, he killed four hundred and ninety-nine men. He had one arrow remaining, kept in reserve for the bandit chief. Each watched for an opening in the other, but neither could find one. The man then had his wife stand naked in front of the bandit chief. The bandit chief's mind became distracted, and seizing this chance, the man released his arrow and killed him too.

"Thereupon his wife spoke a verse:

> "'Though you have a sharp bow and arrows, not a single shot has missed;
> With killing and wounding spread all around, how do you not feel remorse?'

"The man also replied with a verse:

> "'I possess this wondrous skill; bow and arrows respond to my heart and hand.
> With each kill I feel joy; for what reason should I feel remorse?
> Originally I traveled this road to rid others of enmity and harm.
> Not sparing my own body and life, I have established a name for valor.'"

The Buddha said: "That archery master was myself. The archery disciple was Śāriputra. The five hundred bandits are the present five hundred bhikṣus. The bandit chief was Devadatta. Śāriputra in the past, with arrow after arrow, broke the band of bandits; now with a single teaching of the Dharma, he has broken Devadatta's assembly."

Maudgalyāyana again said to the Buddha: "How extraordinary, World-Honored One! Devadatta cursed, saying 'Evil-desiring bhikṣus!' and with his living body fell into the great hell." The Buddha said: "Not only in this life; in the past too he once used evil speech and, with his living body, received great suffering." Maudgalyāyana asked: "How was that?" The Buddha replied: "In a past age, beside a pond in a wilderness, there were two geese and one tortoise who had formed a close friendship. Later, the pond dried up. The two geese deliberated: 'Now this pond has dried up; our dear friend will surely suffer greatly!' After deliberating, they said to the tortoise: 'This pond has dried up; you have no way to survive. You can bite onto a stick, and we will each hold one end in our beaks and carry you to a place with abundant water. While biting the stick, you absolutely must not speak!' They then carried him off. As they passed over a village, the children below all called out: 'Geese are carrying a tortoise! Geese are carrying a tortoise!' The tortoise angrily said: 'What business is it of yours?' He immediately lost his grip on the stick, fell to the ground, and died."

At that time the World-Honored One spoke a verse on this:

> "When a person is born, an axe is in his mouth; that which hews his body
> is due to his evil speech. He should criticize yet instead praises; he should praise yet instead criticizes.
> He receives his own calamity, and in the end has no happiness.
> If one contends over wealth and gain, this evil is not yet great;
> But one who directs an evil mind toward the Buddha, that is truly the greatest evil.
> Arbuda hells number a hundred thousand; Nīraya hells number thirty-six.
> One who directs an evil mind toward a worthy person shall fall into these hells."

The Buddha said: "That tortoise was Devadatta. In the past, because of angry speech, he met with death and suffering; now again with angry cursing, he has fallen into the great hell." He told the bhikṣus: "If I saw in Devadatta even a hair's tip of wholesome dharma, I would never have predicted that he would fall into the great hell and suffer for an entire kalpa. It is like a person submerged in a great cesspool; if someone wished to rescue him, they could not find even a hair's breadth of clean surface to grasp. When I observe Devadatta, it is just like this." He further told the bhikṣus: "I do not see any other dharma that destroys a person's aspiration for the unsurpassed path like fame and material gain. The reason Devadatta split the Sangha was because of material gain. Devadatta, having accomplished eight unwholesome qualities, split the Sangha: gain, non-gain, praise, non-praise, respect, disrespect, delight in evil, and association with evil companions."

Upāli asked the Buddha: "What constitutes splitting the Sangha?" The Buddha said: "There are four conditions for splitting the Sangha: proclaiming the five practices, distributing voting sticks oneself, taking the sticks, and performing separate Sangha acts within the boundary."

He further asked: "What constitutes the Sangha being in disharmony but not split?" The Buddha said: "If a king assists in splitting the Sangha, it causes disharmony but is not a split. If a minister, upāsaka, upāsikā, bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, śrāmaṇerī, or one bhikṣu up to seven bhikṣus assist in splitting the Sangha, it is likewise. If one performs Sangha acts without consulting the senior monk, this constitutes disharmony but is not a Sangha split. If they do not eat together, sit separately at mealtimes, quarrel, and hurl insults, it is likewise. Only when, within a boundary, eight bhikṣus divide into two groups and perform separate Sangha acts is it called a split."

He further asked: "Among them, who splits the Sangha?" The Buddha said: "The one who is the instigator."

He further asked: "Who suffers for one kalpa in the great hell, beyond rescue?" The Buddha said: "The one who is the instigator."

He further asked: "Do all who split the Sangha suffer in the great hell for one kalpa?" The Buddha said: "Not all necessarily suffer in the great hell for one kalpa. There are eight types of people who split the Sangha and suffer in the great hell for one kalpa: if it is Dharma, and with the perception of Dharma, they declare it is not Dharma; if it is non-Dharma, and with the perception of non-Dharma, they declare it is Dharma; if it is Dharma, and with the perception of non-Dharma, they declare it is Dharma; if it is non-Dharma, and with the perception of Dharma, they declare it is not Dharma; if it is both Dharma and non-Dharma, and with the perception of Dharma, they declare it is not Dharma; if it is both Dharma and non-Dharma, and with the perception of non-Dharma, they declare it is Dharma; if it is Dharma, and with doubt about non-Dharma, they declare it is Dharma; if it is Dharma, and with doubt about non-Dharma, they declare it is not Dharma.

"There are six types of people who split the Sangha but do not fall into the great hell and suffer for one kalpa: if it is Dharma, and with the perception of Dharma, they declare it is Dharma; if it is non-Dharma, and with the perception of non-Dharma, they declare it is non-Dharma; if it is Dharma, and with the perception of non-Dharma, they declare it is not Dharma; if it is non-Dharma, and with the perception of Dharma, they declare it is Dharma; if it is both Dharma and non-Dharma, and with the perception of non-Dharma, they declare it is not Dharma; if it is both Dharma and non-Dharma, and with the perception of Dharma, they declare it is Dharma."

---

## Part Five, Section Two: The Rules Concerning Bedding and Lodging

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, the bhikṣu Aśvajit attended the Buddha. Later, he put on his robes, took his bowl, and entered the city to beg for alms. He walked with dignified deportment, his eyes directed downward. A certain householder saw him and thought: "I have never seen anyone like this man." He went and asked: "Who are you? Whose disciple are you? Under whom did you go forth? Whose path and Dharma do you practice?" At that time, the Buddha had just attained the path, and the world all called him the Great Śramaṇa. Aśvajit replied: "My name is Aśvajit. The Great Śramaṇa is my teacher. I went forth under him and practice his path and Dharma." The householder, hearing this, exclaimed: "How extraordinary! Having such dignified deportment, he has gone forth under the Great Śramaṇa and practices his path and Dharma." He further asked: "Where do you presently dwell?" He replied: "Wilderness places, mountain caves, beneath trees, in the open, among tombs: these are my dwelling places." The householder, hearing this, was doubly delighted and exclaimed: "His deportment is dignified and graceful, his teacher is already supreme, and he dwells in such places as these!" He further asked: "What bedding do you use?" He replied: "Kaśa grass, kuśa grass, vāvā grass, mṛdu grass, and tree leaves, down to sand and earth: these are all my bedding." The householder, hearing this, felt yet more joy and reverence, and exclaimed: "He is able to practice such contentment with little!" He further asked: "If I were to build a dwelling for the Venerable One, would you be able to make use of it?" He replied: "The World-Honored One has not yet permitted us to make use of dwellings." The householder said: "Venerable One, please report this to the Buddha; I too shall go myself to inform him." Aśvajit silently accepted his words. After the meal, he returned to the Buddha, bowed his head at his feet, and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus. He praised contentment with little and knowing what is enough, praised the precepts, and praised the upholding of precepts. He then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit bhikṣus to accept the gift of dwellings."

That householder later came to the Buddha. From afar he saw the World-Honored One, his countenance extraordinarily special, like a golden mountain. Inwardly filled with joy and reverence, he bowed at the Buddha's feet and sat to one side. The Buddha taught him various wonderful Dharma teachings, up to suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. Right there on his seat he obtained the purity of the Dharma-eye. Having seen the Dharma and attained its fruit, he took the three refuges and the five precepts, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I wish to build dwellings and offer them to the bhikṣus. May you permit them to accept." The Buddha silently consented. The householder, knowing the Buddha had consented, rose from his seat, bowed at the Buddha's feet, circumambulated three times to the right, and departed. On that very day he built sixty dwellings. He further prepared food for the offering of the dwellings, and all his household worked together to prepare it. Every worldly delicacy was fully provided. Some split firewood, some fetched water, some cooked food, some swept the ground, some sprinkled the ground with fragrant water, some laid out seats, some scattered flowers, and some prepared a high seat.

At that time in the city of Śrāvastī there was a householder named Sudatta, who had lent out three hundred thousand gold coins to people in Rājagṛha, coming yearly to collect the debt. The householder would always come out one yojana to welcome him and set out a great feast, but this time he did not come out. Sudatta thought: "Perhaps he has encountered trouble from the king, or from water, fire, thieves, or non-human beings, and that is why he did not come to welcome me?" When he arrived, he first went to the householder's residence and saw him preparing all manner of delicacies. He asked: "Is this for a wedding, a festival, or are you inviting the king?" The householder replied: "It is neither a wedding nor a festival, nor am I inviting the king." He asked again: "Then why are you preparing such marvelous food and drink?" The householder replied: "A Buddha has appeared in the world, possessing great majesty and virtue, and all his disciples are likewise. I have invited them, and so I have prepared this offering. That is why I was unable to come out to welcome you." Sudatta said: "I too have heard that a Buddha would appear in the world, called the Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha, Vidyācaraṇasaṃpanna, Sugata, Lokavidū, Anuttara, Puruṣadamyasārathi, Śāstā Devānāṃ ca Manuṣyāṇāṃ ca, Buddha, Bhagavat. Is the one you have invited this Buddha, or not?" He replied: "He is the Buddha!" He asked again: "Where is he now?" The householder then bared his right shoulder, placed his right knee on the ground, and with his right hand pointed toward where the Buddha was, saying: "The Buddha is over there." Sudatta, upon hearing this, was overjoyed. He bared his right shoulder, bowed toward the Buddha from afar, and three times uttered "Namo Buddhāya." Throughout the night he contemplated the Buddha, and exhausted from fatigue, he fell asleep. His good friend from past lives, serving as a guardian spirit, protected him. The spirit thought: "I shall cause this householder to see the Buddha without waiting through the night." He then made the night appear bright. Sudatta thought the sun had risen, got up, and headed for the city gate. The gate opened of itself. After he had passed through, the gate closed by itself, and suddenly it became dark again. Sudatta was frightened and thought: "Was I just now perhaps mad?"

The spirit, knowing his thought, spoke a verse:

> "Now is the time to go to the Buddha. If one takes but a single step,
> The benefit outweighs a thousand gold offerings; elephants and horses cannot compare."

He further said: "Do not be afraid, do not be frightened! Go forward, go forward! You will see the Buddha in a moment." Sudatta, hearing this, was freed from fear. He went forward and from afar saw the World-Honored One, whose appearance and deportment were extraordinarily special, like a golden mountain. The World-Honored One, seeing him, praised: "Welcome, Sudatta!" Sudatta, hearing this, was overjoyed: "The Buddha even knows the name given to me by my parents!" He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and sat to one side. The Buddha taught him various wonderful Dharma teachings, up to suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. Right there on his seat he obtained the purity of the Dharma-eye. Having seen the Dharma and attained its fruit, he took the three refuges and the five precepts, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, may the Buddha and the Sangha accept my invitation for the summer retreat in Śrāvastī!" He made this request three times, and each time the Buddha remained silent. At the fourth request, the Buddha told him: "If a dwelling place is free from clamor and noise, silent and still, Buddhas will consent to take up their rainy-season retreat there." The householder said to the Buddha: "I understand, World-Honored One! Please assign a bhikṣu to oversee the arrangements." The Buddha asked: "Whom do you wish?" He replied: "I wish to have Śāriputra." The Buddha then said to Śāriputra: "You may go and oversee the arrangements." Śāriputra accepted the instruction and departed.

At that time, the previous householder came early in the morning to the Buddha and said: "The food is ready. May the Holy One come at the appropriate time." The Buddha, together with the Sangha of bhikṣus, put on their robes, took their bowls, surrounded fore and aft, and went to the householder's residence, where they sat down in order. The householder personally served the food. When the meal was finished and water had been passed, the householder said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I offer this park and these dwellings to the Sangha of the four directions." The Buddha silently accepted. Knowing the Buddha had accepted, the householder took a small seat and sat before the Buddha. The Buddha then spoke verses of joyful dedication and blessing:

> "To shelter from wind, cold, and heat, and to ward off harmful creatures;
> To protect from rain, dew, and dust, and also to relieve the affliction of mosquitoes and gadflies,
> Given to those who uphold the precepts, who sit in meditation, recite, and teach the Dharma.
> If they hear and understand its meaning, they will exhaust the source of all suffering."

After the Buddha spoke these verses, he further taught various wonderful Dharma teachings. Having instructed, benefited, and delighted them, he returned to his dwelling.

Thereupon, the householder Sudatta took Śāriputra and returned to the city of Śrāvastī. At every village they passed through, he proclaimed: "A Buddha has appeared in the world, possessing great majesty and virtue, and all his disciples are likewise. I have already invited them to take up the rainy-season retreat in Śrāvastī. You should all together arrange dwellings, repair roads and bridges, and prepare offerings in advance to await the World-Honored One." The people of those places, hearing his proclamation and knowing that the Buddha, the World-Honored One, would pass that way, were all greatly delighted and respectfully followed his words. When the householder Sudatta arrived in Śrāvastī, he thought: "Where is the very best place, suitable for building a monastery? Only the Jeta grove of Prince Jeta in this city, with its beautiful gardens and fruit trees, its clear waters, its flowing springs and bathing pools, its fragrant flowers all complete, would be suitable." Having thought this, he went to Prince Jeta and said: "I wish to buy your garden. Would you be willing to sell it?"
