Prince Jeta replied: "If you can cover the ground with gold coins leaving no gap, then I will give it to you." Sudatta thereupon covered the ground with gold coins. Prince Jeta said: "I spoke that as a figure of speech; I did not intend to sell." Sudatta replied: "Having stated this as the price, how can you go back on it?" They argued back and forth and eventually brought the matter before the officials. The officials judged according to the law and awarded it to Sudatta. Prince Jeta asked Sudatta: "Why do you not begrudge your gold and treasure, buying this garden?" Sudatta replied: "A Buddha has appeared in the world, possessing great majesty and virtue, and all his disciples are likewise. I have already invited them to take up the rainy-season retreat here. Therefore I exhaust everything and begrudge nothing." Prince Jeta then said: "If you allow me to add my name to the garden, calling it the Jetavana Monastery, I will give it to you." Sudatta said: "Excellent!" He then had people bring out gold coins and cover the ground. Where trees stood, the measurements were supplemented to fill in completely. Śāriputra then used cords to measure and lay out walking paths, lecture halls, heated rooms, kitchens, bathing houses, and all the various dwellings, ensuring everything was suitably arranged.

At that time, the various dwellings had walls that were not plastered tightly, allowing wind, dust, snakes, and mice to damage the Sangha's bedding and disturb the bhikṣus. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit plastering the walls inside and out, as well as the ceiling. The Sangha may keep axes, chisels, knives, saws, plowshares, spades, ladders, trowels, and all manner of tools for building dwellings. I also permit whitewashing, painting, making windows, doors, shutters, hooks, and locks, and making door-hooks. They should not be worn at the waist like a knife handle; one who does so commits a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who did construction work and got their bodies dirty with dust and earth, needing to bathe. The Buddha said: "I permit bathing."

There was mud in the bathing area. The Buddha said: "I permit paving the ground with bricks and installing bed-boards."

There were bhikṣus in the charnel ground who found bedding and rope beds but did not dare take them. The Buddha said: "I permit taking them. If they are too large, they should be cut down."

At that time, between the two cities of Rājagṛha and Śrāvastī, there was a dwelling place that lay donors had offered to the bhikṣus, but no one lived there. The Buddha said: "I permit the lay people to invite an ārāmika to stay and watch over it, providing for their needs."

At that time, dwelling places had no fences or barriers, and cattle and horses intruded, damaging the walking paths. The Buddha said: "I permit building fences around the perimeter and digging ditches." Cattle and horses still got in. The Buddha said: "I permit planting thorny hedges as barriers." Cattle and horses still intruded. The Buddha said: "I permit building walls, or stacking bricks, and covering the top with thatch or tiles. I permit building a gate structure, I also permit building it in multiple levels, and I permit making double doors."

There were bhikṣus who chewed tooth-sticks, washed their hands and faces, and washed their feet inside rooms, wetting the floor and damaging the Sangha's bedding. The Buddha said: "This should not be done."

There were old and sick bhikṣus who in cold weather could not go out to wash. The Buddha said: "I permit using a washbasin and a jar for holding water."

In dwellings where dust arose, the Buddha said: "The floor should be plastered with mud, and one of the ten types of cloth should be used to spread over it."

There were junior bhikṣus who washed their feet first. When senior bhikṣus arrived later and the junior bhikṣus had not finished washing, they were driven away. The Buddha said: "If junior bhikṣus have already begun washing, they should be allowed to finish."

There were bhikṣus who practiced walking meditation in the open. When it rained, their robes got wet, and they abandoned their walking meditation. The Buddha said: "I permit building covered walkways."

There were bhikṣus who walked in the courtyard. When it rained, the ground was damaged and their feet became dirty. The Buddha said: "I permit stacking bricks and stones to make paved paths."

In some dwelling places there was no water. The Buddha said: "I permit digging wells or making clean pools."

Bhikṣus wished to make mattresses to spread on their beds. The Buddha said: "I permit making them from one of the ten types of cloth, stuffed with sheep's wool, camel hair, kapok, or even soft grass."

Bhikṣus made their mattresses too thick. The Buddha said: "The maximum thickness permitted is eight finger-breadths. I permit them to be made by the Sangha, made for the Sangha of the four directions, or made privately."

When the Sangha's bedding was damaged, they did not know what to do. The Buddha said: "A person should be appointed to mend and wash it."

At that time, bhikṣus distributed the Sangha's bedding every day. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. The bedding should be distributed at the end of spring."

At the beginning of summer, when the rainy-season retreat was determined, the group-of-six bhikṣus chose the best rooms and best bedding for themselves. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. A bhikṣu should be appointed through a motion-and-one-proclamation karman to serve as the bedding distributor." The appointed bhikṣu should label the bedding to indicate which room it belongs to, and distribute it in order of seniority. If there is extra good bedding, it should be given to the senior monk if he needs it. If he does not need it, it goes down in order, assigned to whichever seat the bhikṣu occupies. If bhikṣus arrive later, they are assigned in order of seniority. Those below shift progressively to the lower rooms. If a junior bhikṣu has no room, then so be it.

Bhikṣus wished to make new rope beds and wooden beds. The Buddha said: "I permit making them. If there is no skilled craftsman and a bhikṣu can make one himself, that is also permitted. I permit ten types of cord, each type to be used for making rope."

There were bhikṣus who wished to pad their rope beds. The Buddha said: "I permit padding."

There were bhikṣus who stood and walked on rope beds, breaking the ropes. The Buddha said: "One should not stand or walk on rope beds."

There were bhikṣus who were short and, wishing to take robes from or place robes on the shelf, could not reach. The Buddha said: "I permit, while keeping mindfulness to the fore, standing on the frame of the rope bed to reach them."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who sat on padded rope beds, and menstrual blood soiled and ruined them through impure decay. The Buddha said: "Bhikṣuṇīs should not sit on padded rope beds."

There were small śrāmaṇeras who stayed on padded rope beds and urinated on them, causing impure decay. The Buddha said: "Small śrāmaṇeras also should not stay on padded rope beds."

There were bhikṣus who sat on high beds to receive instruction in the sūtras or ask about meaning. The Buddha said: "When receiving instruction in the sūtras or asking about meaning, one should sit in a lower place."

In the dwelling places of bhikṣus, grass grew in the courtyard. The Buddha said: "I permit having a lay attendant take care of it."

Dust and dirt in the rooms soiled the beds and bedding. The Buddha said: "I permit making a whisk of any appropriate kind and using it to clean."

At times of great assemblies, many bhikṣus came, and the rooms were large but few, with no place to stay. The Buddha said: "In the rooms, bedding should be laid out in order, just enough to contain their bodies, until full. If they wish to hang cloth for privacy, each is permitted to do so. If there is enough space, good. If not, and there is open ground outside, I permit building temporary shelters. The resident bhikṣus should build these for them."

After temporary shelters were built and the great assembly had passed, they were burned by fire, which spread to the dwelling places. The Buddha said: "After the great assembly has passed, they should be dismantled and removed. If the resident bhikṣus are unwilling and refuse to let them be dismantled, the visiting bhikṣus should simply entrust the matter to the resident bhikṣus and depart."

During heavy rains, bhikṣus had no place to gather. The Buddha said: "I permit building a large hall."

During cold weather, bhikṣus gathered together and suffered from the cold. The Buddha said: "I permit building a heated room."

At that time, Śāriputra was overseeing construction for Viśākhā's mother, building a new large hall. She brought grain and rice to offer to the Sangha of the four directions. The bhikṣus did not dare eat it. The Buddha said: "If it is made for the Sangha of the four directions, they are permitted to eat freely."

There were bhikṣus who begged for food and offered it to the Sangha of the four directions. It was not known who should eat it. The Buddha said: "Those who are working for the Sangha of the four directions may eat it."

There were dwellings that had fallen into disrepair, and the bhikṣus did not repair them. The Buddha said: "They should encourage and solicit donations through a motion-and-one-proclamation karman, and allow monastics and lay people who wish to repair them to do so."

One bhikṣu proclaims: "May the Sangha of great virtue listen! Such-and-such room has deteriorated and is in disrepair, with no one to repair it. Such-and-such person wishes to repair it. The Sangha now grants them permission to repair it. If the time is appropriate for the Sangha and the Sangha consents, this is the motion."

"May the Sangha of great virtue listen! Such-and-such room has deteriorated and is in disrepair, and so forth, and the Sangha now grants them permission to repair it. Those elders who consent, remain silent. Those who do not consent, speak. The Sangha has granted such-and-such person permission to repair the old deteriorated room. The Sangha consents, as indicated by its silence. The matter is held thus."

If one wishes to label it as such-and-such donor's room, it is permitted to label it so. The same applies to bedding.

At one dwelling place, there was a great flood. Each bhikṣu moved the bedding of his own room. In rooms where no bhikṣus were staying, no one moved the bedding, and the water swept it away and ruined it. Later, the donor of those rooms saw this and angrily scolded the bhikṣus, saying: "How could you let only my room's bedding be ruined by the flood?" The Buddha said: "When there is water or fire, one should call out loudly, strike the gong, and have the entire Sangha help together to move everything. If even one person does not help, he commits a duṣkṛta offense!"

At that time, Rāhula went to the village of Nālā. There was a certain upāsaka who had deep faith in him and had a room built for him. When the room was completed, Rāhula had minor business and went traveling. At that time Ānanda went to that village, and the upāsaka then offered the room to Ānanda. When Rāhula returned, he told Ānanda to leave. Ānanda said: "Although it was first given to you, after you left, it was given to me. Therefore it is my room." Thereupon both went to the upāsaka and asked: "Whose room is this really?" The upāsaka replied: "Although I first offered it to Rāhula, Rāhula left on a journey. Afterward I offered it to Ānanda; it should be Ānanda's room." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus on account of this matter and told the bhikṣus: "This upāsaka did not behave this way only in this life; in the past too he once did the same." The bhikṣus asked: "How was that?" The Buddha said: "In a past age, there was a king named Bāru. In his kingdom there were two sages; one was named Rāhula, who always delighted in sitting meditation, and one was named Ānanda, who was learned and fearless. That king first met Rāhula, revered him greatly, and had a room built for him. When the room was completed, Rāhula went traveling. Ānanda came later, and the king also revered him and offered him the room that had been previously built. Rāhula returned from his journey and told Ānanda to leave, saying: 'This is my room!' Ānanda also said, as above: 'This is my room!' They went together to the king and asked: 'Whose room is this really?' The king replied: 'Although I first offered it to Rāhula, Rāhula left on a journey. Afterward I offered it to Ānanda; it should be Ānanda's room.' At that time, the devas, nāgas, ghosts, and spirits all said: 'This king is unjust! How can he first offer a room to Rāhula and then take it away to offer to Ānanda? We shall now destroy his household!' They went together to the king's palace and pelted it with stones, killing the king's household members."

The Buddha, on account of this matter, spoke a verse:

> "The king's gift made no distinction of before and after; the sages contended over it.
> This caused the ghosts and spirits to become wrathful, and he himself brought about the destruction of his household.
> If one acts according to favoritism, the wise will not praise it.
> Therefore one should abandon favoritism and speak joyfully according to what is right."

The Buddha said: "The sage Rāhula of that time is the present Rāhula. The sage Ānanda of that time is the present Ānanda. The king of that time is the present upāsaka." He told the bhikṣus: "From now on, it is not permitted to accept a dwelling that has previously been offered to another. One who does so commits a duṣkṛta!"

At that time, the Buddha, together with a great Sangha of one thousand two hundred and fifty bhikṣus, was traveling through the Kośala country toward the town of Kirākirā. There were five resident bhikṣus there who heard that the Buddha and a great assembly were coming. They deliberated together: "Among that assembly are Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana, who will certainly trouble us. We had better divide up this dwelling place, its rooms, bedding, gardens, and fruit trees into five portions, each to become private property." Having deliberated, they divided it. When the Buddha's assembly arrived, the bhikṣus went and said to them: "Open the rooms and lay out bedding; we need to stay." The five bhikṣus said: "The Buddha is the Lord of the Dharma; we shall open the first room for him to stay in. As for the rest, we have already divided everything. These are private possessions and no longer belong to the Sangha. You may seek lodging from acquaintances in the village."

At that time Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana had no place to stay, so they spent the night under the eaves of the Buddha's dwelling. The next day, the Buddha assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus on account of this matter and told the bhikṣus: "The Sangha of the four directions has five kinds of property that may not be restricted, may not be sold, and may not be divided. What are the five? First, the land of the dwelling place; second, the buildings; third, articles for communal use; fourth, fruit trees; fifth, flowers and fruit. All bhikṣus who are śramaṇas and sons of the Śākya have their share in these. If one restricts, sells, or divides them, one commits a sthūlātyaya offense."

At the place those five bhikṣus had divided, afterward bhikṣus from the four directions gathered and again divided it among themselves. Later, more visiting bhikṣus came and said: "Open a room for us; we wish to stay in it." The earlier bhikṣus said: "We have come from the four directions; this is our share. We have already divided it, and it no longer belongs to you. You may go to the village and seek lodging elsewhere." The later bhikṣus then went to the village to seek lodging. The lay people said: "Venerable Ones, there are Sangha dwellings over there. Why do you not stay there, but come here instead?" The bhikṣus then turned back toward the Sangha dwellings, but by then it had already become dark, and on the road they were killed by a tiger. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha assembled the Sangha of bhikṣus on account of this matter and told the bhikṣus: "Did I not previously say that the Sangha of the four directions has five kinds of property that may not be restricted, may not be sold, and may not be divided? How could you restrict the Sangha dwelling and not give it to the later-arriving bhikṣus, causing them to be killed by a tiger!" Having rebuked them in various ways, he further said: "If one restricts, sells, or divides the property of the Sangha of the four directions, it is all a sthūlātyaya!"

There were bhikṣus living by the seashore where timber was difficult to obtain and there was nothing to build with. They had many large fish bones and wished to use them for construction. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit it."

The bhikṣus were troubled by the smell of the fish bones. The Buddha said: "I permit plastering them with fragrant mud."

There were bhikṣus who had overseen the construction of a Sangha dwelling. When it was completed, a visiting bhikṣu who was senior came and drove the builder out, staying in that room himself. The builder, angry, said: "I labored hard in the construction, yet I am not permitted to stay there in peace." This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit the one who oversaw the construction to stay in whichever room he pleases."

The bhikṣus then gave the room to the builder for an indefinite period. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The duration should be measured according to the amount of his labor. The maximum permitted is twelve years of residence. It should be granted through a motion-and-one-proclamation karman."

One bhikṣu proclaims: "May the Sangha of great virtue listen! This bhikṣu, such-and-such, served as the construction overseer. The Sangha now grants him residence in the room of his choosing for such-and-such number of years. If the time is appropriate for the Sangha and the Sangha consents, this is the motion."

"May the Sangha of great virtue listen! This bhikṣu, such-and-such, served as the construction overseer. The Sangha now grants him residence in the room of his choosing for such-and-such number of years. Those elders who consent, remain silent. Those who do not consent, speak. The Sangha has granted the bhikṣu, such-and-such, residence in the room of his choosing for such-and-such number of years. The Sangha consents, as indicated by its silence. The matter is held thus."

There were bhikṣus who made wooden beds or rope beds, placed them in a room, or plastered the floor, or did minor repairs, and then sought permission to stay as they pleased. The Buddha said: "This should not be sought. Only if the repair work amounts to at least one third of the cost of building the room may one request from the Sangha to stay as one pleases."

I notice that while the header mentions Fascicle 25 (卷第二十五), the actual content section indicates "Fascicle 026: 五分律" but no Chinese text has been provided between the separator lines. The text body appears to be empty.

Without any Chinese source text to translate, I cannot produce a translation. Could you please provide the actual Chinese text content of the fascicle you would like translated? I would be happy to translate it in full once the source text is supplied.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Fascicle Twenty-Six

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

---

## Fifth Division: Miscellaneous Rules

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha. At that time, bhikṣus were eating from shared vessels with laypeople, their hands touching each other, and they had to wash repeatedly. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not eat from shared vessels with laypeople."

There was a bhikṣu who went to the home of his relatives. The relatives said: "We are not strangers, nor are we impure. Why not eat together?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to eat together with mindful attention, but do not let hands touch each other."

There was a bhikṣu eating with a layperson, but the vessel was small and their hands touched. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to hold the vessel with the left hand while eating."

Bhikṣus were bowing to each other during meals, during Sangha meals, while drinking gruel, while eating fruit, while walking in meditation, when not wearing the three robes, in darkness, and when not speaking to each other; those who were angry with each other bowed in secluded places. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "At all these times one should not bow. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

There are also five kinds of persons one should not bow to: those under a censure sanghakamma, those under an expulsion sanghakamma, those under a dependence sanghakamma, those under a charge-laying sanghakamma, and those under a submission sanghakamma.

There are again five kinds of persons one should not bow to: those who are suspended, those placed under non-communication, those given the original-speech discipline, bhikṣuṇīs, and śrāmaṇeras.

There are again five kinds of persons one should not bow to: those of deranged mind, those of distracted mind, those mentally impaired by illness, laypeople, and non-Buddhist ascetics.

There are again five kinds of persons one should not bow to: those in separate dwelling, those who should undergo mānāpya, those undergoing mānāpya, those starting over from the beginning, and those awaiting rehabilitation.

There are five kinds of persons one should bow to: the Buddha, pratyekabuddhas, senior monks who accord with the Dharma, one's upādhyāya, and one's ācārya.

At that time, bhikṣus were growing their hair long. Their minds were not delighted in the Way, and some returned to lay life or became non-Buddhist ascetics. Laypeople criticized and reproached them, saying: "We laypeople grow our hair, and the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, do the same. What difference is there? They merely wear dyed and cut robes, that is all!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not grow one's hair. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

Bhikṣus were shaving their hair in the kitchen, the lecture hall, and the warming room. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not do so. If one is old or sick and cannot endure the cold, it is permitted in the warming room."

At that time, bhikṣus were shaving hair in strict order of seniority. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "There is no need to follow strict order. If someone has urgent business, he may shave first. If there is no urgent business, whoever has washed first may shave first."

There were bhikṣus who shaved their hair everywhere in the courtyard without sweeping up. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should shave in one place. After shaving, sweep up and put the hair in water, in fire, or bury it. If there is no barber, a bhikṣu who is able to shave is also permitted to do so. It is permitted to keep a razor."

There were bhikṣus whose nasal hair grew long. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep tweezers to pull them out."

Bhikṣus then made tweezers of gold and silver. The Buddha said: "That is not permitted. It is permitted to use copper, iron, ivory, horn, bamboo, or wood, excluding lacquer wood."

There were bhikṣus whose ears were blocked with matter. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep an ear-cleaning implement." The rest is as above.

There were bhikṣus who had food stuck between their teeth, causing bad breath. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep a tooth-picking implement." The rest is as above.

At that time, King Bimbisāra had this thought: "What have I not yet given to the Sangha?" Thinking it over completely, he had given everything except tooth-picking implements. He then made a full cart of them and offered them to the bhikṣus, also providing a meal. The bhikṣus did not dare accept. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All may be accepted."

The Buddha was in the country of Sumā. He personally made a bowl form as a model for the future and had a potter fire it. The potter made many and fired them together. When he opened the kiln mouth to look, they had all become gold bowls. Frightened, he said: "This is the great śramaṇa's spiritual power! If the king hears of this, he will surely think I possess much gold treasure." He then took them and buried them. The Buddha made more and had them fired, and they all became silver bowls. He was also frightened and buried them. The Buddha made more and had them fired, and they finally became copper bowls, of a fine blue-green color, like the Jambu tree. He gave them to the bhikṣus, but the bhikṣus did not dare accept. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep them."

There was a bhikṣu whose bowl broke, and he traveled without a bowl. The Buddha said: "He should seek another bowl. If he can make one himself, it is permitted to make one."

There were bhikṣus whose bowls turned red after firing. The Buddha said: "They should be smoked."

There were bhikṣus who kept bowls of gold, silver, the seven precious substances, ivory, copper, stone, and wood. Householders criticized and reproached them, saying: "These bhikṣus are like kings and great ministers. They always speak of having few desires and knowing contentment, yet now they keep such fine bowls." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, it is not permitted to keep the above bowls. If one keeps gold or silver, down to stone bowls, it is a duṣkṛta in each case. If one keeps a wooden bowl, it is a sthūlātyaya."

At that time there was a brahmin named Ukkala who had a daughter who always ate from a white-copper bowl. After that woman went forth, she still used her former vessel to beg for food. Householders criticized and reproached, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, use copper bowls and cannot be distinguished from non-Buddhist ascetics." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to use non-Buddhist copper bowls. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta. It is permitted to use three kinds of bowls: iron bowls, earthenware bowls, and Sumā bowls."

At that time in Vaiśālī, the Licchavi princes obtained a sandalwood bowl. They deliberated: "To whom should this bowl be given?" Some said: "It should be given to the World-Honored One." Others said: "It should be given to Saccaka the Nirgrantha." The majority wished to give it to the World-Honored One, so following the majority, they filled the bowl with white rock-sugar and joy-balls, and presented it to the World-Honored One, saying: "We have jointly obtained this bowl and offer it to the World-Honored One. We humbly request that you compassionately accept it." The Buddha accepted the joy-balls but returned the bowl, saying: "This is a non-Buddhist bowl; the Buddha does not keep such things." They deliberated again: "We offered the bowl to the World-Honored One, but the World-Honored One did not accept it. We should now offer it to the Sangha." Having decided, they then took the bowl and went to the monastery to offer it to the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus did not dare accept. This matter was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to accept it. Break it up and use it as incense."

Later the Licchavi princes again obtained a bull's-head sandalwood bowl. They placed it on top of a high pole and announced: "Whoever has the spiritual power to take it down may have it." At that time, Piṇḍola said to Maudgalyāyana: "The World-Honored One has declared you foremost in supernatural powers. Why do you not take it?" He replied: "You also have supernatural powers; you may go and take it." Piṇḍola then took it and offered it to the Sangha. The bhikṣus did not know what to do and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to accept it. Break it up and use it as incense."

At that time, four great śrāvaka elders, Kāśyapa, Maudgalyāyana, Aniruddha, and Piṇḍola, deliberated: "Now in Rājagṛha there are those who do not have faith in or delight in the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha. We should together cause them to have faith and delight." Having deliberated thus, they surveyed far and near and saw only the elder Bhadrika and his sister who did not have faith in or delight in the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha. Three of the śrāvakas said: "Can you convert Bhadrika?" Piṇḍola said: "I can convert his sister."

That elder had seven layers of gates and three troupes of performers. When he wished to eat, all seven gates were closed, and at each meal one troupe performed. Aniruddha, at mealtime, appeared before him begging for food. The elder asked: "How did you enter?" He answered: "Through the gate." The elder then asked the gatekeeper: "Why did you let a beggar in?" The gatekeeper replied: "The gates were shut as usual; I did not see anyone enter." The elder then placed a piece of hemp cake in his bowl, saying: "Go away! If you have ingredients, make this kind of food yourself." Aniruddha took it and left. At the next mealtime, Kāśyapa again appeared before him begging. The questions and answers were as before. Afterward the elder asked the gatekeeper: "Why did you twice allow beggars to burst through my gates?" The gatekeeper replied: "The gates were shut as usual; I did not see anyone enter." The elder again placed a piece of fish in his bowl, saying: "Go away! If you have ingredients, make this kind of food yourself." After Kāśyapa left, the elder's wife asked: "What do you think? Do you suppose these bhikṣus are unable to obtain food and come to beg from you?" He answered: "Indeed!" His wife said: "Do you know who the first bhikṣu was?" He answered: "I do not." His wife said: "That was Aniruddha, a son of the Śākya clan, who renounced his three-season palaces and the pleasures of the five desires to go forth and study the Way." She asked again: "Do you know who the second bhikṣu was?" He answered: "I do not." His wife said: "That was the son of the great Pippalāyana brahmin lineage, who renounced nine hundred and ninety fields, residences, and plow-oxen to go forth and study the Way. Out of compassion for you, he came to beg for food!" After hearing his wife's words, the elder felt inward respect and submission. Thereupon Maudgalyāyana flew through the air and expounded the Dharma for him, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening him, until right there on his seat he became free from dust and stain and attained the purity of the Dharma-eye. Having seen the Dharma and attained the fruit, he then took the Three Refuges and the Five Precepts. From then on, he constantly provided for bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, upāsikās, and also non-Buddhist ascetics.

At that time, the three śrāvakas said to Piṇḍola: "We have already converted Bhadrika and caused him to have faith and delight. You should now go and next convert his sister." Thereupon Piṇḍola, in the early morning, put on his robes, took his bowl, and entered the city to beg for food. He arrived at her house in turn. At that time, the elder's sister was making cakes with her own hands. When she suddenly saw Piṇḍola, she lowered her head and closed her eyes. Piṇḍola also gazed single-mindedly at his bowl. She said: "I absolutely will not give to you! You gaze single-mindedly at your bowl; what do you intend?" Piṇḍola then emitted smoke from his body. She said again: "Even with smoke coming from your whole body, I will not give to you!" Piṇḍola then burst into flames all over his body. She said again: "Even with your whole body ablaze, I will not give to you!" Piṇḍola then flew up into the sky. She said again: "Even flying up into the sky, I will not give to you!" Piṇḍola then hung upside down in midair. She said again: "Even hanging upside down in midair, I will not give to you!" Piṇḍola thought: "The World-Honored One does not permit us to forcibly beg from people." So he left. Not far from Rājagṛha there was a great boulder. Piṇḍola sat upon it, and together with the boulder flew into Rājagṛha. The people in the city saw it and were all greatly frightened, fearing the boulder would fall to the ground. Everyone fled. When it reached the space above the elder's sister's house, it stopped and did not move. When she saw this, she was greatly terrified; her heart trembled and her hair stood on end. She pressed her palms together and said: "Please spare my life! Put the boulder back in its original place, and I will give you food!" Piṇḍola then carried the boulder and returned it to its original place, then came and stood before her. The elder's sister then thought: "I cannot give a large cake. I will make a small one to give him." She made a small ball, but it kept turning out large. This happened three times, each time becoming larger than before. She then thought: "I want to make them small, but they all turn out large. I might as well just give one cake." She then handed over one cake, but all the cakes stuck together in a chain, and even the cake vessel stuck to them. When she grasped the vessel with her hand, her hand also stuck to it. She said to Piṇḍola: "If you want cakes, I will give you all of them; I do not begrudge the vessel either. Why do you need me? Why do you make my hand stick to the vessel and not come free?" He answered: "I do not need the cakes or the vessel, nor do I need you. The four of us deliberated together to convert you and your brother. Three have already converted your brother. It falls to me to convert you; that is why I have done this." She asked: "What do you want me to do now?" He answered: "Sister, you may carry these cakes on your head and follow me to make an offering to the Buddha and the Sangha." She then carried the cakes on her head and followed Piṇḍola. Piṇḍola guided her along, passing through the gates of others so that people would see her. When they arrived at the Buddha's place, she personally served the Buddha and one thousand two hundred and fifty bhikṣus, and all were satisfied, yet the cakes were still not exhausted. She brought the remainder and said to the Buddha: "With these few cakes of mine, I have served the Buddha and one thousand two hundred and fifty bhikṣus, and all were satisfied, yet they are still not exhausted. Where should I put the remainder?" The Buddha said: "You may place them on barren ground where no grass grows, or in water that contains no insects." The woman then placed them in water with no insects. The water bubbled and hissed, as if hot iron had been thrown into a small amount of water. She became frightened, and her body-hair all stood on end. She returned to the Buddha's place, bowed her head at his feet, and sat to one side. The Buddha expounded various wonderful teachings for her, until she attained the purity of the Dharma-eye, took the Three Refuges and the Five Precepts, and provided for the four assemblies and sought the Way just like her brother, without difference.

The senior bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked Piṇḍola: "Did you truly do this?" He answered: "Truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha reproached him in various ways and then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, it is not permitted to display supernatural powers. If one displays them, it is a duṣkṛta."

At that time, King Bimbisāra had an āmra fruit garden that was flourishing and beautiful in all three seasons. He had long offered its flowers and fruits to the bhikṣus, for them to use as they wished. The bhikṣus then ate the fruits, at the morning meal, at the afternoon meal, eating at all times without cease. Some carried away full bowls; some ate half and threw the rest on the ground. Later, a neighboring country sent an envoy. The envoy said to the king: "I have heard that the king has an āmra fruit garden that flourishes in all three seasons. I wish to see its fruits." The king then ordered that fruits be gathered. But the bhikṣus had eaten them all up, and this was reported to the king. The ministers and attendants around the king all criticized and reproached, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, are insatiable. Although the king does not begrudge them, the recipients should exercise moderation. How could they eat all the fruits of an entire garden?" The senior bhikṣus heard this and reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you truly do this?" They answered: "Truly so, World-Honored One." The Buddha reproached them in various ways and then told the bhikṣus: "From now on, it is not permitted to eat unpurified fruit. If one eats it, it is a duṣkṛta."

There was a householder who invited the Sangha to eat fruit. The bhikṣus had someone purify each fruit one by one. The day passed midday and they could no longer eat. The householder criticized and reproached, saying: "These śramaṇas are like little children, having someone purify each fruit one by one, so that it did not finish before midday. What am I to do with all these fruits now?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "There are five kinds of seeds: root-seeds, graft-seeds, node-seeds, fruit-seeds, and kernel-seeds. If one eats fruit, one should perform the five kinds of śramaṇa purification: purification by fire, purification by knife, purification by bird, purification by damage, and purification of immature seeds. If one eats roots, one should also perform the five kinds of śramaṇa purification: purification by peeling, purification by cutting, purification by splitting, purification by washing, and purification by fire. If one eats stems and leaves, one should perform three kinds of śramaṇa purification: purification by knife, purification by fire, and purification by washing. When performing purification, one should perform it collectively: in one pile or one vessel, if one item is purified, the whole is considered collectively purified."

There was a bhikṣu who wished to light the fire in the bathing room. When splitting firewood, a snake emerged from a hole in the wood and bit his foot, and he died immediately. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "That bhikṣu did not know the names of the eight kinds of snakes, did not direct lovingkindness toward them, and did not recite the spell, and so he was harmed by the snake. The eight kinds of snakes are: the Dhṛtarāṣṭra snake, the Takṣa snake, the Airāvaṇa snake, the Śabara-putra snake, the Kamala-Aśvapāraka snake, the Virūḍhaka-Akṣa snake, the Gautama snake, and the Nanda-Upananda snake. The spell for snakes is:

> "I show lovingkindness to all nāga kings,
> Those in the heavens and those in the world.
> By this, my lovingkindness,
> May all venomous anger be extinguished.
> By the power of my wisdom,
> I use it to destroy this poison.
> Flavored poison and flavorless poison,
> May they be destroyed and go into the earth."

The Buddha said: "If that bhikṣu had protected himself with this spell, he would not have been harmed and killed by the venomous snake."

Again there was a bhikṣu who was bitten by a snake. The bhikṣus reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "You should recite this spell over him and cause him to be safe and well." They went as instructed and recited the spell, and he was healed.

Again there were bhikṣus who were bitten by snakes in various places. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to perform healing spells and treat them as appropriate."

At that time, the bhikṣus were eating much rich food, which increased their illnesses. Jīvaka went at dawn to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the bhikṣus are now eating much rich food, which increases their illnesses. Please permit bathing in a bathing room to remove these ailments." The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha, and using Jīvaka's words, told the bhikṣus: "From now on, it is permitted for bhikṣus to build a bathing room and bathe therein for the sake of eliminating illness."

There were bhikṣus who bathed naked, rubbing each other, and also went out of the bathing area naked. Laypeople criticized and reproached, saying: "These śramaṇas are all like Nirgranthas, without any decorum." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha, on account of this matter, assembled the bhikṣu Sangha, reproached them, and told the bhikṣus: "From now on, it is permitted to wear a bathing garment. Bathing naked is not permitted. Bathing naked and rubbing each other are each a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who, while bathing, went outside and rubbed their backs against walls and trees, then returned to pour water over themselves, injuring and breaking their skin. The Buddha said: "One should not do so. It is permitted to use grape bark, māru bark, bath-powder, and other dirt-removing substances."

Bhikṣus gave bath-powder and such only to their acquaintances. The Buddha said: "It should be distributed equally."

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, Upananda wore his outer robe inside-out and crawled on all fours in the dark, frightening the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not do so. Wearing the outer robe inside-out or crawling on all fours are each a duṣkṛta."

Bhikṣus suffered from heat in their rooms and wished to wear their outer robes inside-out. The Buddha said: "Inside one's room, it is permitted."

There were bhikṣus who had no pillow when sleeping. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to make a pillow."

There were bhikṣus afflicted by lice. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to pick them off."

Bhikṣus picked them off and placed them inside the room, and they crawled back into their clothes. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to place them outside the room."

Bhikṣus during rain placed them in water. The Buddha said: "One should place them on a discarded cloth and set them aside with a compassionate mind."

There were bhikṣus afflicted by fleas. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to spread cloth on the ground and sweep them away. If they are in mats, it is permitted to expose the mats to the sun to remove them."

There were bhikṣus afflicted by bedbugs. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to remove them and seal the cracks with clay."

There were old and sick bhikṣus who suffered from cold and wished to light a fire inside their rooms. The Buddha said: "When it is cold, it is permitted to light a fire."

Bhikṣus lit fires that burned and damaged floor coverings and stained the room with smoke. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to make a brazier and light it outside, and bring it in after the smoke has cleared."

The bhikṣus did not know what material to use. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to make them of copper, iron, clay, or stone. The Sangha, Sangha of the four directions, and individuals are all permitted to keep them. It is also permitted to make a fire pit in the ground."

Bhikṣus drank from their bowls after midday. The Buddha said: "One should not drink from the bowl after midday. It is permitted to make a separate drinking vessel, made of copper, iron, or earthenware."

There was a bhikṣu who spent the rains retreat in the country of Takṣaśilā. When it ended, he went to the Jetavana in Śrāvastī. He went to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and said to the Buddha: "In this country, people drink gruel, but in that country they drink barley drink. Please permit the bhikṣus to drink barley drink in the morning." The Buddha said: "It is permitted to drink it."

Bhikṣus needed salt while drinking. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep salt. The Sangha, Sangha of the four directions, and individuals are all permitted to keep it. It is also permitted to make a salt spoon."

Bhikṣus then made salt spoons in the shape of living beings, or in the shape of a human hand holding a salt scoop. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to make such shapes."

At that time, laypeople offered food to the bhikṣus on platters and dishes. The bhikṣus did not dare accept. The laypeople criticized and reproached, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, are not worthy to receive offerings!" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to accept."

There were bhikṣus who drank gruel from their bowls; it was painfully hot and they could not hold it. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to make a separate vessel for drinking gruel."

Bhikṣus found the food heavy to hold up. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to set it on a small table."

Bhikṣus then made small tables with legs of various shapes. The Buddha said: "That is not permitted."

When bhikṣus went to laypersons' homes, the laypeople served food on small tables with legs of various shapes. The bhikṣus did not dare eat. The Buddha said: "At laypersons' homes, it is permitted to accept, but one is not permitted to keep such things oneself."

Bhikṣus needed ladles. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep them, made of copper, iron, earthenware, or stone."

There were laypeople who brought gruel for the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus did not know where to put it to distribute. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to make basins and tubs, with ring-handles attached. When distributing gruel, one should ask: 'Is there separate gruel for the sick?' If there is none, one should give first to the sick."

At that time, Mother Viśākhā wished to have gruel cooked at the monastery. The Buddha said: "It is permitted."

The bhikṣus did not know where to put the rice. The Buddha said: "It should be placed on a bamboo mat."

There was unhusked grain in the rice, and they did not know what to do. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep a mortar and pestle and have attendants winnow it."

They did not know what to use for winnowing the rice. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep a winnowing basket."

Bhikṣus needed a cooking pot. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep one, made of copper, iron, earthenware, or stone."

Bhikṣus needed a ladle. The Buddha said: "It is also permitted to make one. Except for lacquer wood, all other woods may be used."

There was a sick bhikṣu who wished to have fine gruel. The Buddha said: "It is permitted for an attendant to make it. If there is no attendant, a bhikṣu is permitted to thoroughly wash the cooking vessel, add water, have an attendant wash the beans or rice and put them in, and then the bhikṣu may light the fire. When the gruel is cooked, he should again receive it from the attendant and bring it to the sick person."

Bhikṣus had much rice and nowhere to store it. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to plaster a room with fine clay, sweep the floor clean, and store it there."

After the rice was used up and the bhikṣus stayed there, they smelled a stench. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to coat the floor with fragrant clay."

The Buddha was in the country of Kośala, traveling among the people, together with a great assembly of one thousand two hundred and fifty bhikṣus. They arrived at the brahmin village of Dvija, and beside the road, under a śāla tree, he spread a seat and sat down to rest. The Buddha then smiled slightly. Ānanda thought: "Buddhas do not smile without reason. Now that the Buddha has smiled slightly, there must be a cause." He then bared his right shoulder, knelt, and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Ānanda, in a past age there was a king named Jitasvapna. He had a daughter who, at birth, was naturally adorned with a gold flower garland. He assembled his ministers to discuss giving her a name. All said: 'One should ask the brahmin soothsayers.' He then ordered the soothsayers to all gather and name her. The soothsayers said: 'This girl was born wearing a natural gold flower garland; she should be named Mālinī.' And so she was named. The king was very fond of her and inquired throughout the country for girls born on the same day to attend her. At that time, in the country, there were five hundred women born on the same day. Their names were all recorded and they were assigned to serve her. When she grew up, the king had her provide for five hundred brahmins whom he supported, and he instructed her: 'You should do as I do, daily making five hundred pots of broth and providing for them according to their wishes.' The girl accordingly provided for the brahmins as instructed. After the meal, she would always go with the five hundred women in four-horse chariots to visit pleasure gardens and scenic spots, from garden to garden, from park to park, every day the same.

"At that time, Kāśyapa Buddha was residing in one of those gardens. The charioteer, whenever he reached the garden where the Buddha was staying, would turn the chariot around and not enter. The girl asked the charioteer: 'In this entire kingdom there is no garden I have not entered. Why do you always avoid this garden?' He answered: 'In this garden there is a bald-headed śramaṇa named Kāśyapa. It would not be fitting to see him; therefore I do not enter.' The girl said: 'What does the śramaṇa Kāśyapa have to do with our affairs? Turn the chariot around and enter this garden.' He then turned the chariot and entered. Going as far as the chariot could go, they proceeded on foot into the garden. From a distance she saw Kāśyapa Buddha, whose countenance was extraordinary, like a golden mountain. Upon seeing him, she gave rise to a joyful mind. She went before the Buddha, bowed her head at his feet, and stood to one side. The Buddha expounded various wonderful teachings for her, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening her, until she saw the Dharma and attained the fruit, and received the Three Refuges and the Five Precepts. She rose from her seat, bowed at the Buddha's feet, circumambulated him to the right, and departed. Not long after leaving, she thought: 'I have constantly been making five hundred pots of broth, twice daily, to provide for five hundred brahmins. They are not a field of merit and should not receive offerings! It would be better to prepare the most excellent food and drink to provide for Kāśyapa, the World-Honored One.' Having thought this, she ordered the preparation and had it sent daily as an offering.

"At that time, the brahmins heard that Mālinī had become a disciple of Kāśyapa Buddha and was now offering superior provisions to Kāśyapa. They became jealous and resentful, and deliberated: 'We should devise a stratagem to kill this girl together.' At that time, King Jitasvapna had eleven dreams in the night. He dreamed of trees four inches tall already bearing flowers. He dreamed of flowers immediately becoming fruit. He dreamed of calves plowing while grown oxen stood watching. He dreamed of three pots cooking rice side by side, and the rice in the two outer pots kept jumping into each other, not falling into the middle pot. He dreamed of a camel eating grass with two heads. He dreamed of a mother horse drinking the milk of her own foal. He dreamed of a golden bowl traveling through the sky. He dreamed of a jackal urinating into a golden bowl. He dreamed of a monkey sitting on a golden throne. He dreamed of ox-head sandalwood being sold as if it were rotten grass. He dreamed of water that was turbid in the middle but clear at the four edges. At dawn, he assembled all the ministers and described the above dreams in full, asking: 'What do these dreams mean?' The ministers said: 'One should ask the brahmin soothsayers.' He then summoned and questioned them. The brahmins thought: 'We intended to kill this girl, and now we have our opportunity!' They said to the king: 'These dreams are inauspicious. You may either lose your kingdom or lose your life.' The king asked: 'Is there any means to escape this calamity?' They answered: 'There is, but it involves what the king holds dear, and he will surely not be able to follow through.' The king said: 'Just tell me!' The soothsayers said: 'The king's such-and-such elephant, such-and-such horse, such-and-such great minister, such-and-such great brahmin, along with five hundred bulls, five hundred water buffaloes, five hundred heifers, five hundred bull calves, five hundred billy goats, five hundred wethers, the princess Mālinī and her five hundred attendants: on the seventh day from now, at a crossroads, they should all be slaughtered as a sacrifice to heaven. Then this calamity can be averted. If this is not done, the disaster cannot be avoided!' The king heard this and believed it. He ordered preparations to be made and then summoned his daughter and told her everything, allowing her six days to do whatever she wished.

"The girl said to the king: 'I do not at all begrudge my life. I wish that on the first day, together with the people of the city, men and women, old and young, I may go to Kāśyapa Buddha's place.' The king granted this. Thereupon she summoned everyone in the city, and surrounded on all sides, they went to Kāśyapa's place. The Buddha expounded various wonderful teachings for them, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening them, until they saw the Dharma and attained the fruit, and received the Three Refuges and the Five Precepts. 'I wish that on the second day, together with the king's ministers, I may go to the Buddha's place. I wish that on the third day, together with the princes, I may go to the Buddha's place. I wish that on the fourth day, together with the princesses, I may go to the Buddha's place. I wish that on the fifth day, together with the queen and the court ladies, I may go to the Buddha's place. I wish that on the sixth day, together with the king, I may go to the Buddha's place.' The king granted all of these. All of them saw the Dharma, attained the fruit, and received the Three Refuges and the Five Precepts, just as described above. After the king had attained the fruit, he asked Kāśyapa Buddha about the eleven dreams: 'What is the consequence of these dreams?' The Buddha said: 'These eleven dreams pertain to the future, not to the present. The dream of small trees bearing flowers means: in the future there will be a Buddha who appears in a time when humans live to one hundred years, named Śākyamuni, Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha. At that time, people will already have white hair at thirty. The dream of flowers immediately becoming fruit means: at that time, people of twenty years will already have children. The dream of calves plowing while grown oxen stand watching means: at that time, people's children will manage household affairs, and the parents will have no autonomy. The dream of three pots cooking rice side by side, with the rice of the two outer pots jumping into each other but not falling into the middle pot, means: at that time, the wealthy will give gifts to each other, but the poor will receive nothing. The dream of a camel eating grass with two heads means: at that time, the king's ministers, while receiving the king's salary, will also take from the people's property. The dream of a mother horse drinking her foal's milk means: at that time, after a mother has married off her daughter, she will go back to her for food. The dream of a golden bowl traveling through the sky means: at that time, rain will come at the wrong seasons and will not be widespread. The dream of a jackal urinating in a golden bowl means: at that time, the people will marry only for wealth, without regard to family lineage. The dream of a monkey sitting on a golden throne means: at that time, the king will govern with unrighteous methods, tyrannical and lacking the Way. The dream of ox-head sandalwood being sold at the same price as rotten grass means: at that time, the śramaṇas of the Śākya clan, out of greed for offerings, will preach the Dharma to laypeople. The dream of water turbid in the middle but clear at the four edges means: at that time, the Buddha's Dharma will first decline in the central lands, while the border lands will flourish instead.' The Buddha said: 'The meaning of the king's eleven dreams is as stated. There is nothing inauspicious for the great king himself.' The king then, right on his seat, ordered all ministers that the items prepared for the sacrifice to heaven should all be given the gift of fearlessness. 'From now on, I would rather lose my own life than intentionally kill a living being; how much less would I kill humans! I will not intentionally harm even insects and ants; how much less would I harm this girl and these people!'"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "After the parinirvāṇa of Kāśyapa Buddha, that king built a stūpa of gold and silver for the Buddha, half a yojana in length and breadth and one yojana in height, with gold and silver bricks alternating one after another. It still remains underground." The Buddha then brought forth the stūpa and showed the four assemblies. The complete body-relics of Kāśyapa Buddha were majestic and intact, just as originally.

The Buddha, on account of this matter, took a lump of clay and spoke this verse:

> "Though one might obtain Jambudvīpa gold,
> Hundreds of thousands in golden treasures and profit,
> It does not compare to a single lump of clay
> Used to build a stūpa for the Buddha."

After showing it, he restored it to its original place. The Buddha then used four lumps of clay to seal the place where the stūpa had sunk. One thousand two hundred and fifty bhikṣus also each applied four lumps of clay. Thereupon, the bhikṣus wished to build a stūpa at the place they had applied clay, for Kāśyapa Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to build one." They then built it together. This was the very first stūpa built upon the earth of Jambudvīpa.

Afterward, bhikṣus wished to build stūpas for arhats, śrāvakas, and pratyekabuddhas. The Buddha said: "It is permitted. There are four kinds of persons for whom stūpas should be built: Tathāgatas, noble disciples, pratyekabuddhas, and cakravartin kings."

Bhikṣus wished to make open stūpas, roofed stūpas, stūpas without walls, to make niches and images within, to make railings on the outside, to make canopy-discs, to make pillars of copper, iron, stone, or wood in front of the stūpa, with elephants, lions, and various beast-forms on top, and to plant trees on either side of the stūpa. The Buddha permitted all of these.

At that time, non-Buddhist ascetics also built stūpas for themselves and made various offerings, and people who saw them developed faith and delight. The bhikṣus thought: "If the Buddha permits us to make various offerings to stūpas, people will also develop faith and delight." The Buddha also permitted this.

Bhikṣus then personally sang and danced as offerings to the stūpas. Laypeople criticized and reproached, saying: "Laypeople sing and dance, and the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, do the same. How are they different from us?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Bhikṣus should not personally sing and dance as offerings to stūpas. It is permitted to have others do so. It is permitted for bhikṣus personally to praise the Buddha and make offerings to the stūpa with flowers, incense, banners, and canopies."

When bhikṣus received flowers while begging for food, they did not dare accept them. Laypeople criticized and reproached, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, are not worthy to receive offerings, and moreover do not wish to make offerings to the stūpa." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to accept." They did not know where to put them. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to make three kinds of bags: a flower bag, a food bag, and a water-straining bag."

There were bhikṣus who personally went out to gather flowers, going from one village to another. Outside a village, they were robbed by bandits. Laypeople criticized and reproached, saying: "These śramaṇas are just like garland-makers or garland-makers' apprentices." The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to go outside the village to gather flowers."

Bhikṣus made offerings with flowers strung together in continuous garlands. Laypeople reproached them as above. The Buddha said: "One should not string them together; just scatter them as offerings. If there are withered leaves, those that are still green on the outside may all be picked off. Rub the stem three times with the hands and the flower will open nicely by itself."

The Buddha was in Śrāvastī. At that time, bhikṣus were growing their nails long. They developed attachment, were not delighted in practicing the holy life, and some returned to lay life or became non-Buddhist ascetics. Laypeople criticized and reproached: "These śramaṇas are like people who indulge in sensual pleasures, adorning their nails, without any mind of dispassion."

There was a bhikṣu with long nails who entered a village to beg for food. A woman saw him and called him to have sexual intercourse. The bhikṣu said: "I am a renunciant; I do not do such things!" The woman said: "If you will not comply, I will give you a bad reputation." She then scratched herself with her own nails, tearing her clothes and injuring her flesh. She cried out loudly: "A bhikṣu forcibly grabbed and pulled me! When I refused him, he scratched me, tearing my clothes and injuring my flesh!" People came to look; some believed and some did not. Those who believed said: "This bhikṣu has long nails; he must have done this." Those who did not believe said: "This woman has always been of bad character; she is slandering the bhikṣu." All criticized and reproached: "Why does a bhikṣu keep such long nails?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to grow nails long. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta. It is permitted to keep a nail-clipping knife, with an ear-cleaning implement at one end."

There were bhikṣus who dyed their nails red. Laypeople criticized and reproached. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "That is not permitted. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

At that time, bhikṣus made robes for the purpose of adornment. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to make robes for the purpose of adornment. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

There was a bhikṣu guarding the monastery. The bhikṣu who was to bring food was late, so he climbed a tree to look for him and fell from the tree, breaking his ribs. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to climb trees. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who had minor reasons for needing to climb trees. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to climb, but one must not climb higher than a person's height."

There were bhikṣus who wished to take dead branches from the tops of tall trees for firewood. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to use a ladder to reach them, but not to climb the tree."

Again there were bhikṣus who encountered floods, fires, wild beasts, or bandit dangers and wished to climb trees but did not dare, and were consequently harmed. The Buddha said: "In such dangers, it is permitted to climb as one wishes."

There were two bhikṣus traveling together on the road without a water-straining bag. They were thirsty and wanted to drink water but saw insects in it. One bhikṣu drank; the other did not drink and died. The bhikṣu who drank went to the Buddha's place and reported the matter. The Buddha said: "That bhikṣu had a sense of shame and conscience, and was able to keep the precepts even unto death. From now on, it is not permitted to travel without a water-straining bag. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who wished to go nearby but had no water-straining bag and did not dare go. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to travel without a water-straining bag within half a yojana."

Again there were two bhikṣus traveling together. One had a water-straining bag and one did not. The one would not lend it, and the other was extremely thirsty and exhausted. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Did I not previously rule that one may travel without a water-straining bag as long as it does not exceed half a yojana? If one has no water-straining bag but has a corner of a robe that can strain water, it is permitted. When setting out, one should mentally resolve to use it for straining water. It is also permitted to keep a water-straining tube."

Bhikṣus then made them of gold, silver, and precious substances. The Buddha said: "That is not permitted. It is permitted to make them of copper, iron, bamboo, wood, earthenware, or stone. Cover the mouth with the finest of the ten kinds of allowable cloth materials. It is not permitted to use rag-robes. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

At that time, Śāriputra suffered from wind-illness. There was a harītakī fruit placed beside the foot of his bed. Kokālika came, and because he was senior, he displaced Śāriputra. Śāriputra moved away and forgot the harītakī. Kokālika saw it and said to the bhikṣus: "The World-Honored One praises Śāriputra for having few desires and knowing contentment, yet now he hoards things that we do not have." When Śāriputra heard this, he thought: "How is it that because of this small matter I have fallen into reproach?" He then took it and threw it away. The bhikṣus said to him: "Venerable one, this medicine is needed for your wind illness. Do not discard it; you may take it back." He answered: "Because of this small thing, it has caused fellow practitioners of the holy life to harbor such suspicion. I have discarded it and will never take it back." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Śāriputra not only now discards this medicine and refuses to take it back; in past lives he was also like this. Long ago in a past life, there was a black snake that bit a calf, then returned into its hole. There was a snake charmer who used a billy goat and spells to try to draw the snake out of its hole, but he could not get it out. The snake charmer then lit a fire before the calf and performed his spells, transforming it into a fire-bee, which entered the snake's hole and burned and stung the black snake. The snake could not endure the pain and finally came out of the hole. The billy goat tossed it with its horns and threw it before the snake charmer. The snake charmer said: 'Lick back your venom, or else I will throw you into this fire!' The black snake then spoke this verse:

> "'Having once ejected this poison,
> I will never take it back.
> Even if death should come upon me,
> I will never turn back, unto the end of my life.'

"Thereupon, he would not retract the poison and threw himself into the fire." The Buddha said: "The black snake at that time was Śāriputra. In the past, even under such mortal suffering, he would not retract the poison; how much less would he now take back the medicine he has discarded. From now on, it is permitted for bhikṣus to keep medicine."

The group-of-six bhikṣus then accumulated much medicine. Laypeople criticized and reproached, saying: "Do these śramaṇas intend to become physicians? Or do they intend to sell it? They always speak of having few desires and knowing contentment, yet they are insatiable!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not keep much medicine. It is permitted to keep various mixed medicines, one āḍhaka of each. If one has a chronic illness, it is permitted to keep additional amounts as appropriate."

There were bhikṣus without knives who used bamboo or reed strips to cut cloth, and the cloth was ruined. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep a cloth-cutting knife."

Bhikṣus then made large knives, and when bandits came they were able to use them to harm bhikṣus. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to make large knives. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta. It is permitted to make one finger-length in size, with a blade on one side and a wooden handle, excluding lacquer wood."

Bhikṣus received needles but did not dare accept them. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to accept and keep them." Bhikṣus then kept many. The Buddha said: "One should not keep many. It is permitted to keep three needles; the rest should be given away as pure offerings."

At that time, in Vaiśālī, at the dwelling place of Ānūpya, the ground was low and damp, with many mosquitoes and gadflies, and it was steamy and hot. The bhikṣus suffered from this. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep fans and whisks."

Bhikṣus made whisks from horse-tail hair, killing insects. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to make whisks from horse-tail hair. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus who placed their bowls full of food on the ground, and the bowls tipped over. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to make a bowl-stand, of copper, iron, ivory, horn, earthenware, stone, bamboo, or wood, excluding lacquer wood. Even binding grass and placing it underneath is permitted."

There were bhikṣus whose bowls lost their smoking-finish and it peeled off. The Buddha said: "They should be re-smoked."

Bhikṣus gave food to workers without measuring the amounts. Even when given much, the workers still grumbled. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep measures: pecks, bushels, pints, and cups. The Sangha, Sangha of the four directions, and individuals are all permitted to keep them. There should also be one extra set in reserve."

Bhikṣus hired workers and gave them ghee, oil, honey, and rock-sugar without weighing it. Even when given much, the workers still grumbled. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to keep scales, also as above."

At that time, bhikṣus were learning calligraphy. Laypeople criticized and reproached, saying: "Why don't the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, diligently recite scriptures? What is the use of learning calligraphy?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to learn calligraphy."

Later, bhikṣus were assigned turns for assemblies but could not keep written records and forgot. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to learn calligraphy, but one must not neglect one's practice for the sake of fine penmanship."

Bhikṣus wished to learn spells to counter the poison of bees, snakes, and other venomous creatures. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to learn."

Bhikṣus kept fields, houses, and shops. Laypeople criticized and reproached: "We laypeople keep fields, houses, and shops because we have wives and children to support. These bhikṣus do the same. How are they different from us?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "None of these are permitted to be kept. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

There were laypeople who donated fields, houses, and shops to the Sangha. The bhikṣus did not dare accept, and the laypeople again criticized and reproached, saying: "These bhikṣus are not worthy to receive offerings!" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted for the Sangha to accept and have attendants manage them."

There were bhikṣus who went to pay respects to Pūraṇa Kāśyapa, Maskarin Gośālīputra, and other non-Buddhist teachers. The Buddha said: "One should not pay respects to them. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

Bhikṣus learned various forms of divination. Laypeople criticized and reproached, saying: "The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, cannot even purify their own views; how can they know future events?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

Bhikṣus learned spells to enchant people. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted. Offenders commit a sthūlātyaya."

Bhikṣus learned spells to raise the dead. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted. Offenders commit a sthūlātyaya."

Bhikṣus consulted fortune-tellers and diviners, wishing to know their own good or bad fortune. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

Bhikṣus read and recited non-Buddhist texts. Laypeople saw this and criticized and reproached, saying: "These śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, have no faith in or delight in the holy life. They abandon the Buddha's scriptures and precepts to read and recite non-Buddhist texts!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted."

There were bhikṣus who, when debating with non-Buddhists, were ignorant and felt ashamed, thinking: "If the Buddha permitted us to read non-Buddhist texts, we would not suffer this shame." The Buddha said: "For the purpose of overcoming non-Buddhists, it is permitted to read non-Buddhist texts. But one must not develop views in accordance with those texts."

There were bhikṣus who kept many small copper vessels. Laypeople criticized and reproached, saying: "These śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, keep so many vessels. How are they different from us?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to keep many small copper vessels. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta."

There were two brahmin brothers who recited the Chandas and Veda texts. They later went forth in the true Dharma. Hearing that the bhikṣus recited the scriptures incorrectly, they criticized and reproached, saying: "Venerable ones, you have been ordained for a long time, yet you do not know masculine and feminine forms, singular and plural, present, past, and future tenses, long and short vowels, or heavy and light accents. You recite the Buddha's scriptures in such a manner!" The bhikṣus who heard this felt ashamed. The two bhikṣus went to the Buddha's place and reported everything to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to recite according to the local language, but one must not distort the Buddha's meaning. It is not permitted to render the Buddha's words in non-Buddhist literary forms. Offenders commit a sthūlātyaya."

There were bhikṣus who entered villages without tying their lower garment. It fell to the ground, exposing their bodies. Women laughed at them, and they were ashamed. The Buddha said: "It is not permitted to enter a village without tying one's lower garment. Offenders commit a duṣkṛta. It is permitted to keep a waist-cord."

Bhikṣus made waist-cords that were too long, wrapping around the waist four or five times. The Buddha said: "They should not be long. At most, two or three wraps."

Bhikṣus made waist-cords that were too wide. The Buddha said: "The width should be at most four finger-breadths and no less than one finger-breadth."

Bhikṣus made them with threads of mixed colors. The Buddha said: "That is not permitted. It is permitted to use only one color."

There were bhikṣus who wore light robes when entering a village. The wind blew and exposed their bodies. Women laughed, and they were ashamed. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to make robe hooks and clasps and fasten the robe. They should be made of copper, iron, ivory, horn, bamboo, or wood, excluding lacquer wood. It is even permitted to make a belt and fasten it."

Bhikṣus always wore robes the same way up, and the lower part wore out easily. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to wear the robe alternating top and bottom. Both top and bottom should have hooks, clasps, and a belt."

There were bhikṣus who, when reciting spells, abstained from eating salt, did not sleep on beds, and proclaimed: "Namo Bhagavat." They had doubts, thinking: "Am I perhaps following heterodox views and accepting the methods of another teacher?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This is the way of divine spells. Just do not follow their views."

At that time, the bhikṣu Cīṭika said to the layman Lūhitakṣa: "The bhikṣu Dharmapāla has had sexual relations with your wife." When Lūhitakṣa heard this, he immediately asked Dharmapāla: "Did you truly do this?" Dharmapāla did not answer. Lūhitakṣa then said: "Dharmapāla has committed a sexual offense and is ashamed; he has no words to answer me." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to perform a bowl-turning formal act by a motion-and-one-proclamation sanghakamma against Lūhitakṣa the strongman's son, so that none may enter his home."

One bhikṣu should announce: "May the venerable Sangha listen! Lūhitakṣa the strongman's son has falsely accused Dharmapāla of having sexual relations with his wife. The Sangha now performs a bowl-turning formal act against him, so that none may go and enter his home. If the Sangha finds it timely, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

"May the venerable Sangha listen! Lūhitakṣa the strongman's son has falsely accused Dharmapāla of having sexual relations with his wife. The Sangha now performs a bowl-turning formal act against him, so that none may go and enter his home. Will those elders who consent remain silent; those who do not consent, speak. The Sangha has completed the bowl-turning formal act against Lūhitakṣa the strongman's son. The Sangha consents, as shown by its silence. This matter is thus held."

If the Sangha has performed a bowl-turning formal act, all of the four assemblies are not permitted to associate with or speak to that person.

There were bhikṣus who, over minor disputes with upāsakas, would perform a bowl-turning formal act against them. The Buddha said: "One should not perform a bowl-turning formal act against a layperson over a minor matter. Only when eight conditions are fulfilled should it be done: if an upāsaka, in the presence of bhikṣus, reviles the Three Treasures and the precepts, if he wishes to cause harm to the bhikṣus, if he creates a bad reputation for the bhikṣus, if he wishes to seize the bhikṣus' dwelling place, or if he violates a bhikṣuṇī. These are the eight."

Ānanda, when he was a layperson, had been close friends with Lūhitakṣa. The Buddha told Ānanda: "You should go to Lūhitakṣa's place and tell him: 'The Sangha has performed a bowl-turning formal act against you.'" Ānanda received the instruction and went to his gate. The gatekeeper reported: "Ānanda is outside." Lūhitakṣa said: "Let him in." Ānanda said: "I can no longer enter your gate." Lūhitakṣa, hearing this, quickly came out and asked: "Why suddenly can you not enter my gate?" He answered: "The Sangha has performed a bowl-turning formal act against you. All four assemblies are not permitted to associate with or speak to you." Lūhitakṣa said: "If what you say is true, this is as good as killing me!" He fainted and collapsed on the ground. Ānanda said: "Get up! Go and apologize to Dharmapāla, and the Sangha will lift the bowl-turning formal act for you." Lūhitakṣa then rose from the ground, went to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and said to the Buddha: "I truly did not see Dharmapāla having relations with my wife. Cīṭika told me, and I believed his words." The Buddha said: "You should go and apologize to Dharmapāla, and the Sangha will lift the bowl-turning formal act for you." Receiving the instruction, he went to Dharmapāla's place, bowed his head at his feet, grasped his feet with his hands, and said: "Because of my foolishness, I believed another's words and slandered the venerable one. Please accept my repentance!" The bhikṣu Dharmapāla then accepted it. After Lūhitakṣa had apologized, he returned to the Buddha's place and said: "I have already apologized to the bhikṣu Dharmapāla." The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "It is permitted for the Sangha, by a motion-and-one-proclamation sanghakamma, to lift it for him."
