Lūhitakṣa the strongman's son should come to the midst of the Sangha, bare his right shoulder, remove his sandals, bow at the feet of each member of the Sangha one by one, kneel with palms joined, and make the following statement: "May the venerable Sangha listen! I, Lūhitakṣa the strongman's son, falsely accused Dharmapāla of having sexual relations with my wife. The Sangha performed a bowl-turning formal act against me, not permitting any of the four assemblies to associate with my household. I have already apologized to Dharmapāla. I now obey the Sangha and request the Sangha to lift the bowl-turning formal act. May the Sangha, out of compassion,

I notice that the prompt indicates Fascicle 卷第二十六 (26) but then at the bottom references "Fascicle 027." The actual text content between the separator lines appears to be empty. There is no Chinese text provided for me to translate.

If you could provide the actual Chinese text of the fascicle, I would be happy to translate it in full. Please paste the content and I will produce a complete translation following all the specified rules.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

## Fascicle Twenty-Seven

### Part Five, Section Four: Rules of Deportment

The Buddha was in Śrāvatī. At that time there was a brahmin who had gone forth, who was excessively fastidious about cleanliness beyond the norm. He was disgusted by his own excrement and urine, and used sharp latrine-grass to wipe himself, cutting and injuring his flesh, so that blood stained his robes and the Sangha's bedding. The bhikṣus reproached him in various ways: "You are even disgusted by your own excrement and urine; how could you ever attend to sick bhikṣus?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The use of latrine-grass is not permitted."

At that time, bhikṣus went up to the latrine naked. Laypeople criticized them, saying: "These bhikṣus are just like Nirgrantha ascetics!" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not go up to the latrine naked! Going up to the latrine naked is a duṣkṛta."

There were bhikṣus dwelling in a forest hermitage whose latrine was far away. When they had urgent need for defecation, they could not endure until reaching the latrine. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one truly cannot endure, one is permitted, before reaching the latrine, to look around in all four directions, and if there is no one, then to proceed."

There was a bhikṣu already in the latrine. Afterward, another bhikṣu went up to the latrine without keeping his mind attentive, and without snapping his fingers or coughing, he went straight in and bumped into the first bhikṣu. The first bhikṣu was ashamed and angrily reproached him. The second bhikṣu apologized in remorse. Also, a bhikṣu going up to the latrine did snap his fingers, but the bhikṣu inside the latrine did not make any sound, so he entered and caused resentment. Both matters were reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not go up to the latrine with a distracted mind! Going up to the latrine with a distracted mind is a duṣkṛta. I now establish for the bhikṣus the preliminary training rules beginning with going to the latrine. In these, bhikṣus should train for their entire lives. If one does not train, it is a duṣkṛta!"

"When a bhikṣu goes up to the latrine, he should be single-minded. He should look in front, behind, left, and right. Upon reaching the latrine, he should cough and snap his fingers to let persons or non-human beings inside the latrine know. The person inside the latrine should also snap his fingers and cough. Having entered the latrine, he should again look in front, behind, left, and right, and look upward between the rafters to see whether there are snakes, lizards, or venomous creatures. He should not brush the sides of the doorway with his robes, but should carefully gather them up. He should single-mindedly place his feet, not letting them go too far forward or backward so as to soil the latrine surface. If there was prior soiling or if he himself soiled it, he should clean it, washing what needs washing, wiping what needs wiping, and removing any grass or filth that needs removing, and only then depart. He should slowly lower his robes, taking care not to let them get soiled. When going to the place for urinating and the place for washing after defecation or urination, one should also act in this manner.

"When using water, one should first check whether the water contains insects or not. One should not use excessive water, but should use enough to accomplish the task. When scooping water with a vessel, one should do so very slowly and gently. One must not let the vessels knock against each other, causing breakage. If the water is used up, one should fetch more to fill it. If there is an urgent matter, one must at least fetch enough for one person's use, then cover the head and depart."

There were bhikṣus who sat in meditation, slept, dyed and sewed robes, studied sūtras, and walked in meditation beside the latrine, obstructing other bhikṣus from using the latrine. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not do this!"

There were bhikṣus whose dwelling place was narrow and small, and they could not avoid the latrine area. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the dwelling place is narrow and small, they are permitted to screen it off with cloth or other materials so that they do not obstruct one another."

There were bhikṣus who chewed tooth-cleaning sticks on the latrine. The bhikṣus found this disgusting, and it also obstructed other bhikṣus from using the latrine. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "One should not do this!"

There were bhikṣus who chewed tooth-cleaning sticks beside the latrine. The Buddha said: "One should also not do this!"

There were bhikṣus who, after chewing tooth-cleaning sticks, stuck them on hooks on the latrine wall, tearing other bhikṣus' robes or injuring their flesh. The Buddha said: "One should not do this!"

Bhikṣus, after chewing tooth-cleaning sticks, placed them at the base of trees, and the tree deities became angry. The Buddha said: "One should not do this!"

There were bhikṣus who, after defecating, had nothing to clean and wipe with, and soiled their bodies and robes. The Buddha said: "The use of latrine-grass is permitted."

The bhikṣus then used bamboo strips and reed strips, which cut and injured their flesh. The Buddha said: "One should not use sharp objects as latrine-grass. The edges should be shaved smooth. Except for lacquer trees, all other woods are permitted for use."

The bhikṣus made latrine-grass that was too long, or too short, or too coarse, or too fine. The Buddha said: "It should be made to a moderate size."

The bhikṣus, after using latrine-grass, dropped it into the latrine hole. The Buddha said: "One should not do this!"

The bhikṣus scattered used latrine-grass by tossing it on the ground. The Buddha said: "A container is permitted to hold them. When it is full, whoever sees it should remove the contents and place them in a pit, or have them burned."

Bhikṣus washed after defecation and urination and soiled their hands. The Buddha said: "The use of ash, earth, and cow dung for thorough washing is permitted."

Bhikṣus, after washing from defecation and urination, rubbed their hands against the walls to clean them, damaging the walls. The Buddha said: "One should not rub the walls. One should scrub and wash using bricks or stones."

Bhikṣus placed the ash, earth, and cow dung on the ground, creating filth. The Buddha said: "They should be stored in a container."

There were bhikṣus who made tooth-cleaning sticks too long. The Buddha said: "One should not do this! The maximum permitted length is one span of the hand."

There was a bhikṣu chewing a short tooth-cleaning stick who saw the Buddha and, out of reverence, swallowed it. Through the Buddha's spiritual power, he suffered no harm. The Buddha said: "One should not do this! The minimum permitted length is the width of five fingers side by side. Also, they should not be too thick or too thin."

The bhikṣus did not stay in one place when chewing tooth-cleaning sticks, soiling the ground everywhere. The Buddha said: "One should not do this!"

There were bhikṣus dwelling in a forest hermitage who, if they stayed in one place to chew tooth-cleaning sticks, found the path long, which interfered with begging for food and also caused them to miss distributions given to the Sangha. The Buddha said: "Bhikṣus dwelling in a forest hermitage are permitted to chew tooth-cleaning sticks single-mindedly while walking."

There were bhikṣus who chewed tooth-cleaning sticks at the edge of a well. The Buddha said: "One should not do this!"

There were bhikṣus who, after using tooth-cleaning sticks, did not wash them; insects ate them and died. The Buddha said: "One should not do this! After use, one should wash them clean before discarding."

There were bhikṣus who lacked tooth-cleaning sticks. The Buddha said: "One is permitted to cut off the used portion and keep and use the remainder."

There was a bhikṣu who stored his tooth-cleaning sticks in his leather sandal bag, and the sandals' filth soiled them. The Buddha said: "One should use a separate container to store them."

There were bhikṣus who chewed tooth-cleaning sticks in the warm room, the lecture hall, the dining hall, or the cooking area, or in front of their preceptor, ācārya, or senior monks. The Buddha said: "One should not do this!"

There were bhikṣus who were sick, and their preceptor, ācārya, or senior monks came to see them, but they did not dare to chew tooth-cleaning sticks in their presence. The Buddha said: "When sick, it is permitted!"

Bhikṣus chewed tooth-cleaning sticks in front of laypeople. The laypeople criticized them, saying: "These śramaṇa sons of the Śākya clan do nothing but diligently clean their teeth!" The Buddha said: "One should not do this!"

The bhikṣus then did not dare to chew tooth-cleaning sticks in front of any laypeople at all. The Buddha said: "One should not do this either! One should not chew in front of respected laypeople."

There were bhikṣus who chewed tooth-cleaning sticks in front of non-Buddhist practitioners, who also criticized them as above. The Buddha said: "One should also not do this!"

The bhikṣus then did not dare to chew tooth-cleaning sticks in front of any non-Buddhist practitioners at all. Those practitioners then said: "The śramaṇa sons of the Śākya clan revere us so much that they dare not chew tooth-cleaning sticks in our presence." The Buddha said: "One should not do this either! One should not chew in front of those who are able to cause harm or benefit to the Buddha's Dharma."

The Buddha said: "I have established for the bhikṣus the preliminary training rules beginning with going to the latrine. They should be upheld for one's entire life."

At that time, there was an alms-begging bhikṣu who, without keeping his mind attentive to what was before him, entered another person's household and did not remember the way out. He went out through a different exit and saw a woman lying naked on her back in a room. Upon seeing this, he was frightened and ran out in great haste. The householder returned and saw the bhikṣu running out in fright. He thought: "This bhikṣu must have done something wrong in my house!" He immediately checked inside the house and saw his wife lying naked on her back. He then assumed the bhikṣu had committed adultery with his wife. He urgently chased after the bhikṣu and said to him: "Stop! You did such and such things in my house." The bhikṣu replied: "Do not say such things! It is the way of a bhikṣu not to commit such evil." The man did not believe him, beat him nearly to death, seized his robe and bowl, and let him go. After that bhikṣu returned to the Sangha's quarters, he told the whole matter to the other bhikṣus. The bhikṣus reproached him in various ways: "How could you not keep your mind attentive to what was before you, entering another's household without remembering the way out?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now establish for alms-begging bhikṣus the preliminary training rules. They should be trained in for one's entire life. If one does not train, it is a duṣkṛta!"

An alms-begging bhikṣu should single-mindedly rise early, get down from his bed, put on his leather sandals, take his inner garment and put it on, shaking it to remove dust. The waist cord should be treated likewise. He should neatly put on his lower garment, measuring one span of the hand up from the heel, folding the left side over on top, making two folds on each side, and two folds at the back, then pulling through and tying the cord. He should slowly take the walking sandals, and should not put them on the wrong feet. He should single-mindedly take his saṅghāṭī and bowl. When washing the bowl, he should kneel on one knee; he must not stand. If washing an iron bowl, he is permitted to hold it one foot above the ground; for a saumya bowl, four finger-widths above the ground; for a clay bowl, between the two measures. After washing, he should not place it in a precarious spot, nor should he place it where something above might fall on it. He must not leave it unwashed and expose it to the sun. He should place drinking water in the shade and place the bowl-washing water in the sun.

When going out, he should be single-minded. If he needs to close the door, he should set the bowl down between his feet, then close the door. The lock and key should be hidden so people cannot see them. Not far from the village, where the ground is level and there is good soft grass, he should set the bowl on it and then shake out his saṅghāṭī and his middle and lower garments, putting them on neatly. With his left hand gathering his robe and his right hand holding the bowl, he should lower his head, look forward, and go. He should carefully note the appearance of the streets and alleys, and carefully distinguish the appearance of others' doorways and gates. Upon reaching a doorway, he should snap his fingers, cough, and knock to make a sound, so that if there are persons or non-human beings inside, they will know. After entering the gate, he should consider where to stand. If someone says, "Venerable one, please come in!" he should single-mindedly enter. If someone offers food, he should not receive it leaning over the food. If a woman offers food, he should not converse with her, should not stare at her, and should not note whether she is beautiful or ugly. If one household provides enough, that is good. If not enough, he should go to another household, and stop when he has enough. Having obtained food and left the village, when far from people, he should set the bowl on the ground, take off his saṅghāṭī, shake it to remove dust, and if there is mud, clean and wipe it off, then put it back over his shoulder. Returning to his dwelling, he should open the door, enter, and put his robe and bowl in their usual place. He should shake out his sandals and scrub them clean. Then, single-mindedly, he should wash his feet, wipe them dry, put his sandals back on, and go to his room. He should open the room door, enter, and put his robe and bowl in their original places. If he wishes to apply oil to his feet, he is permitted to oil the soles of his feet. He should sweep the eating area clean, spread out his sitting cloth, fetch clean water, and prepare cloths for wiping hands and feet. If there are already fresh or cooked vegetables, vinegar, salt, or sauce at the dwelling, he should receive them beforehand and place them in one spot. He should wash the containers for leftover food and, estimating that he has surplus food, first set aside the surplus in a container. If he sees a senior monk returning late with food, he should rise to greet him, take his robe and bowl and place them in the senior monk's usual spot, and say: "Your robe and bowl are here!" He should remove the senior monk's sandals; if they are dirty, place them outside the door. When the time comes, he should strike the ghaṇṭā or announce the gathering for sitting together. After all have gathered and sat down, he should first pass around the containers of surplus food. If there is surplus, he should reduce and place it in a container. If there is not enough, he should go and take enough from them. Afterward, he should distribute vegetables and sauce. If a bhikṣu arrives late while eating is in progress, he should offer water to that one, or offer water along with food from the surplus food containers. If that person does not accept water, it means he has already eaten. After the assembly has finished eating, he should collect the sitting cloths, sweep the floor, remove refuse and discard it far away, thoroughly wash the surplus food containers, cover them, and put them back in their original places, and put away the water pitcher.

He should first go to the room of his preceptor or ācārya and perform whatever tasks need doing. Then he should return to his room for reciting, sitting in meditation, or walking meditation, purifying his mind and removing the hindrances and fetters. The preceptor or ācārya also must not detain the disciple for trivial matters. If the preceptor or ācārya is to teach the Dharma to the fourfold assembly, the disciple should sweep the Dharma-teaching area, spread out sitting cloths, prepare water pitchers, and cloths for wiping hands and feet. If there is allowable between-meals drink, he should strain it clean and place it in one spot. After the Dharma teaching is finished, he should put away the sitting cloths and all the items.

If the preceptor or ācārya needs to bathe, the disciple should prepare the bathing supplies. If cold water is needed, fetch cold water; if warm water is needed, heat it. When the teacher has entered the bathing room, the disciple should ask: "Do you need me to come in?" If the teacher needs him, he should enter; if he enters, he should stay behind the teacher's back. When the teacher comes out, the disciple should support and attend him. If the teacher needs to return to his room but cannot walk, the disciple should carry him on his back, or carry his robes. If the teacher needs between-meals drink, the disciple should provide it. If the teacher says, "Call such-and-such bhikṣu," the disciple should call for him. If a lamp is needed, he should light it, if there is no lamp. When offering a drink, the disciple should say: "The drink is here; it has already been strained clean." At night, the disciple should ask the teacher: "Do you need me to stay here tonight?" If the teacher says he does, then the disciple should stay. If the teacher says he does not, the disciple should return to his room and practice as described above. In the early morning, he should go and inquire: "Did you sleep well?" He should seek the morning meal, the afternoon meal, and gruel and dumplings for the teacher. If the Sangha has them, he should request a share for the teacher. If there is a place with a fixed invitation, he should also request a share.

If the teacher is about to enter the village, the disciple should ask and, depending on whether light or heavy robes are needed, hand them to the teacher. If the teacher says, "Come along with me," the disciple should follow. At another household, if the disciple is not allowed to enter the gate, he should not be resentful. If allowed to enter the gate but not given a seat, he should also not be resentful, and should stand behind the teacher. If the donor offers food, he should accept; if he does not receive any, he should also not be resentful. If the teacher gives him leftover food to eat, he should also not be resentful. If the Dharma is being taught at a layperson's house, the disciple should not speak random words. If the teacher utters crude or clumsy words, the disciple should make the teacher aware. When the teacher returns home, the disciple should follow and act as described above.

These are the preliminary training rules for bhikṣus dwelling in forest hermitages who beg for alms. They should be upheld for one's entire life.

At that time, many bhikṣus were spending the rains retreat at one dwelling place. After the retreat had begun, there was no one to help prepare the morning meal, the afternoon meal, or the gruel and dumplings. During the rains retreat, the bhikṣus were extremely short of food. After the pravāraṇā was completed, they left. After they left, the resident bhikṣus said to various laypeople: "You should rejoice, for such and such excellent bhikṣus stayed here for the rains retreat!" The laypeople said: "If so, then we did not give well! You are all our acquaintances; how is it that when such excellent bhikṣus came, you did not tell us? We have always made invitations from afar and exhausted ourselves without succeeding. Yet now they came on their own, and we did not get to make offerings!"

At that time, the group-of-six bhikṣus arrived at another dwelling and said to the resident bhikṣus: "Open the rooms for us and spread out bedding!" The resident bhikṣus arranged lodgings for them. One of the group-of-six bhikṣus entered first without keeping his mind attentive. A snake fell from above and bit him, killing him. The remaining bhikṣus wept and grieved. The elder bhikṣus quickly came out and asked them, and they answered by recounting the matter. The elder bhikṣus reproached them, saying: "How could he enter an empty room without keeping his mind attentive?" The bhikṣus reported all the above matters to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now establish for visiting and resident bhikṣus the preliminary training rules. They should be trained in for one's entire life. If one does not train, it is a duṣkṛta!"

If resident bhikṣus hear that a senior visiting bhikṣu is traveling through the area and will come to this place, they should repair and tidy the rooms, shake out the beds and mats, air out the bedding, sweep the rooms inside, level the ground in front of the rooms, fetch clean water, cover it and place it in one spot, and prepare cloths for wiping hands and feet. When they hear the visitor has arrived, they should go out the gate to greet him and have a junior bhikṣu take his robe and bowl. After the visitor has entered, they should spread a seat for him, provide foot-washing water, wash his feet, and offer cloths for wiping hands and feet as well as a sandal cloth. If the visiting senior monk has many belongings and attendants, he should be given two rooms. They should ask: "How are your undergarments?" and provide bedding appropriate to his status, whether upper, middle, or lower. If the visiting bhikṣu is sick, he should be given a room near the latrine. If he needs to bathe, bathing supplies should be prepared. If he needs between-meals drink, it should also be provided. Throughout the night, they should gather together and teach the Dharma. The next morning, they should prepare the morning meal, the afternoon meal, and gruel and dumplings. They should invite the visitor to stay for the rains retreat and encourage everyone to set up offerings.

When the visiting bhikṣu is about to arrive at the monastery, if he has previously been carrying his robe slung back over his shoulder, he should lower it. If he has previously had his hands on his hips, he should stop doing so. If he has previously been carrying his robe on his head, he should lower it and place it on his shoulder. He should remove his sandals, shake them out and wipe them clean, wrap them in grass or leaves, and carry them in. After entering, he should sit in one place and rest briefly. He should ask the resident bhikṣus: "Which is the senior monk's room?" Once he knows the location, he should go and pay respects, offer greetings, and converse. If the day is still early, he should pay respects at the stūpa. After paying respects at the stūpa, he should in order pay respects to the various senior monks. Then he should wash his hands and feet. After washing his hands and feet, he should ask: "In this dwelling, who is the person who distributes the Sangha's bedding?" Once he knows, he should go and ask: "I have such-and-such number of rains years. Is there a room allocation for me?" If told there is, he should say: "Please give it to me." If given one, he should further ask: "Has anyone been living in this room previously?" If told no one has, he should go to the room's doorway and first toss a piece of tile or stone inside and listen for sounds. If there is a sound, he should not enter. If there is no sound, he should open the door and stand aside in front of the doorway. If nothing comes out, then he should enter, wait a moment for his eyes to adjust, look around the entire room, press the bed with a staff, look under the bed and on the ground for any venomous creatures. He should slowly open the windows. If the day is still early, he should take the bedding out, shake it, and air it in the sun. If there was no bedding there previously, he should go to the bhikṣu who distributes bedding and request some. If he obtains some and spreads it out, he should further ask: "In that room, during the first watch of the night, what is there to fear? During the middle watch and the last watch, what is there to fear?" If told: "During the first watch, there is fear of forest bandits," he should ask: "What should I do?" If told: "You should protect yourself in such and such a way," he should follow that advice. The middle and last watches should be handled likewise. He should further ask: "Is there food in this room?" "Do the people in this village prepare food early or late?" "In which alley has the Sangha performed the karma of designating a training household?" "In which alley has the Sangha performed the karma of overturning the bowl?" "In which alley are there fierce dogs?" "In which alley are there prostitutes, older unmarried women, or widows?" "Where is the place for the poṣadha here? When is the poṣadha held? Where is the place for drinking gruel? Where is the place for eating?" If there are any Sangha duties there, he should promptly attend to them without delay.

These are the preliminary training rules for visiting and resident bhikṣus. They should be upheld for one's entire life.

The Buddha was in Rājagṛha.

At that time, many laypeople invited the Sangha for a meal. Some bhikṣus were just putting on their robes and about to go to the meal; some had already returned and were taking off their robes; some were taking their bowls and about to go; some had already returned and were washing their bowls; some were just leaving the monastery; some had already returned and were just entering; some had just finished eating; some were just about to eat. The laypeople criticized them, saying: "Even other non-Buddhist practitioners know to go together for invitations and eat at the same time, yet the śramaṇa sons of the Śākya clan have no order whatsoever! We cannot tell who has already eaten and who has not." The elder bhikṣus heard these various criticisms and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now establish for the senior monks and all bhikṣus the preliminary training rules for mealtime. They should be trained in for one's entire life. If one does not train, it is a duṣkṛta!"

If laypeople invite the Sangha, the bhikṣu who regularly comes and goes from that layperson's household should report to the senior monk. The senior monk should have word spread to all the bhikṣus: "Today we have accepted the invitation of such-and-such donor." Everyone should assemble together and maintain proper deportment. They should also send the bhikṣu who regularly deals with the host ahead to let the host know they are coming. If the day is still early and the meal is not yet ready, the senior monk may go elsewhere if needed, and is permitted to take one bhikṣu with him, but he must return quickly and not delay past the assembly time. When entering the door of the host's home, everyone should keep their minds attentive, know their seat order, and leave space for those who have not yet arrived.

There was a rope bed that had no ropes on it, covered with a cloth on top. A bhikṣu did not realize this; when he sat down, he fell through, exposing his body, and was ashamed. The Buddha said: "Before sitting, one should first press with one's hand, and then sit."

There were bhikṣus who sat on a rope bed's tight covering, which tore and broke. The Buddha said: "One should first lift the covering to loosen it, and then sit."

When the donor serves water, one should ask: "Is there a vessel to catch the water?" If there is, one should not let water fall on the ground. If there is not, one should not let the water pool in one spot and form mud.

If one receives roots, stems, leaves, or fruits that one does not know how to eat, one should wait for the person next to one to eat, and then eat likewise.

There were bhikṣus who ate before the food had been distributed to everyone. The laypeople criticized them, saying: "These bhikṣus don't wait for everyone; the moment they get food they eat, worse than little children!" The Buddha said: "One should not do this! One must wait until everyone has received food, and only then is one permitted to eat."

In some places where the Sangha was large, the senior monk did not know when everyone had received their food. The Buddha said: "One should loudly call out 'saṃbhoga.'"

The bhikṣus, after finishing eating, left in silence. The laypeople criticized them, saying: "Other non-Buddhist practitioners, after eating someone's food, all recite blessings before leaving. The śramaṇa sons of the Śākya clan leave in silence, and the donors do not know whether they are satisfied or not." The Buddha said: "The senior monk should recite blessings before leaving."

The bhikṣus left without waiting for the senior monk. The Buddha said: "The first eight senior monks are permitted to wait for each other; the rest may go as they wish."

At one dwelling, Śāriputra was the most senior, and Rāhula was the most junior. The host of the invitation gave butter broth to the senior monk, oil broth to the middle-ranked monks, and dregs broth to the junior monks. After eating, Rāhula went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and sat to one side. The Buddha asked Rāhula: "What did you eat today?"

Rāhula answered in verse:

> "Those who eat oil have strength; those who eat butter have beauty.
> If one eats dregs broth, one has no strength, let alone beauty!"

He said to the Buddha: "Today I ate with dregs broth."

After eating, Śāriputra went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and sat to one side. The Buddha asked Śāriputra: "What did you eat today?" He answered: "I ate with butter broth." The Buddha rebuked him: "Today you did not eat well! How is it, bhikṣu, that the senior monk eats with butter, the middle-ranked monks eat with oil, and the junior monks eat with dregs?" Śāriputra was silent and did not answer, and then went to a secluded place and vomited until all the food was gone. The Buddha said: "From now on, when accepting an invitation, the senior monk should tell the host: 'Give to all equally; give impartially.'"

If the donor sends food, the senior monk should tell the junior bhikṣus to sweep the eating area, spread out seats, fetch clean water, and bring out the surplus food containers. All that is needed should be prepared. When the time comes, he should announce it or strike the ghaṇṭā to have everyone assemble together to receive the food. If the host's preparation of food is delayed, they should urge haste so that the proper time is not missed.

These are the preliminary training rules for the senior monks at mealtime. They should be upheld for one's entire life.

At that time, there were bhikṣus dwelling in a forest hermitage who were lazy and negligent. They did not fetch drinking water, did not fetch water for washing hands and feet, and did not fetch water for the latrine area. At that time, a group of forest bandits came seeking drinking water. The bhikṣu answered: "There is none." They then sought water for washing hands and feet, and water by the latrine. The answer was: "There is also none." The bandits said to the bhikṣu: "You śramaṇa sons of the Śākya clan always keep three kinds of water ready. Why is there none today?" The bhikṣu answered: "I did not fetch it, therefore there is none." The bandits said again: "Just give us water now, and we will not come again!" The answer was the same as before. The bandits asked again: "Why is there no water?" The bhikṣu answered: "Because I am lazy and negligent, therefore I do not store water." The bandits then seized his robe and bowl, beat him nearly to death, and left.

There was also a bhikṣu dwelling in a forest hermitage who could not distinguish the stars. Bandits stayed overnight and said to the bhikṣu: "We will sleep a little; tell us when dawn approaches." After the bandits had slept a while, they asked the bhikṣu about the time. The bhikṣu said: "It is still early!" They asked three times in this manner. The night was already near dawn, but he still said it was early. The people pursuing the bandits arrived, seized the bandits, and took them away. The bandits angrily said: "If the bhikṣu had told us the correct time, we would not have slept until dawn. Now we have been caught, and it is the bhikṣu's fault!" The bhikṣus reported all this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now establish for bhikṣus dwelling in forest hermitages the preliminary training rules. They should be trained in for one's entire life. If one does not train, it is a duṣkṛta!"

A bhikṣu dwelling in a forest hermitage should know well the characteristics of the four directions. He should know well how to assess situations. He should know well how to distinguish the stars and know the time, whether early or late. He should keep track of the month, the half-month, and the count of days. He should also keep track of the year, month, and count of days.

For what benefit should he know the characteristics of the four directions? If he knows where bandits come from, he can thereby avoid them.

For what benefit should he know well how to assess situations? When bandits come, he should consider: "Should I flee? Should I rise and greet them? Should I teach them the Dharma? Should I provide for them?" Once he knows the appropriate response, he should act accordingly.

For what benefit should he know well the stars? He should know the star patterns of the first watch, the middle watch, and the last watch, so as to know for himself: "Now is the time for sleep; now is the time for practice." If bandits ask, he can tell them the time, whether early or late. If bandits take him away and later release him, he can observe the stars and know his way back.

For what benefit should he know well the months, half-months, and count of days? By this, he knows when the poṣadha day arrives and goes to the village to seek forgiveness and perform the poṣadha in purity.

For what benefit should he know well the years, months, and count of days? When spring arrives, he knows after how many days he should begin the rains retreat. During the retreat, he knows how many days have passed. When the time for pravāraṇā arrives, he goes to the village to seek forgiveness and perform the pravāraṇā in purity.

A bhikṣu dwelling in a forest hermitage should, on level ground or under a tree, make a foot-washing place, set up foot-washing items, keep foot-washing water, and sit at his regular sitting place. If someone comes, he should greet them joyfully. If bandits ask for water, he should remove his sandals, thoroughly wash his hands, and hold the water out to them with both hands. If they ask whether the water is cold or warm, he should answer as appropriate. If an upāsaka asks for water, he should do the same. If a non-Buddhist practitioner comes asking for water, one who has the ability to cause harm or benefit to the Buddha's Dharma should be treated as above. If the person has no such ability, the bhikṣu should put on his sandals and hold the water out with both hands, so that the person does not say: "The bhikṣu reveres me."

At that time, there was a bhikṣu returning from begging for alms in a village who did not cover the top of his bowl. A kite's droppings fell into the bowl. The bhikṣu did not notice and ate, and contracted a wasting disease.

There was also another bhikṣu returning from begging for alms in a village who did not cover the top of his bowl. A garuḍa bird carrying a snake flew over the bowl and dropped the snake into it. Although the bhikṣu removed the rice on top and ate the rice below, he died. The bhikṣus reported all this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one wishes to eat in the village, one is permitted to stay and eat there. If one wishes to bring it back, one should cover the top of the bowl."

There were old and sick bhikṣus who, when begging for alms, had tired hands from carrying their bowls back. The Buddha said: "They are permitted to make a net bag to hold the bowl, hanging it under the arm."

There were bhikṣus who hung their bowls under their arms, and sweat flowed and soiled them. The Buddha said: "They are permitted to wrap the bowl in a hand cloth and carry it back."

At that time, there was a bhikṣu returning from begging for alms. A forest hermitage bandit followed behind him, thinking: "If this bhikṣu does not even give me a single morsel of food, I will kill him!" When the bhikṣu reached his dwelling, he saw the bandit approaching from afar and invited him to eat. After the bandit had eaten, he said: "How wonderful! Today you have gained great merit! You have given your own life and also given me mine." He then revealed his earlier intention. The bhikṣu reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If a bhikṣu carries food to a forest hermitage, should someone come, he should offer food. If no one comes, he should wait a little. If after waiting a long time no one has come, the bhikṣu, if hungry, should first eat half. If still no one comes, he should eat another half. At the very end, he should set aside one morsel."

There were bhikṣus dwelling in a forest hermitage who, after eating, had consumed everything. When bandits came and asked the bhikṣus for food, they had no food to give. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Bhikṣus dwelling in forest hermitages are permitted to store food. If food is difficult to obtain, village bhikṣus should provide some. If there is no attendant to carry it, the bhikṣu is permitted to carry it back himself, and may place it on either clean or unclean ground as he wishes."

There were bhikṣus who carried food back to their forest hermitage themselves and placed it on unclean ground. When bandits did not come to ask for it, they did not know what to do. The Buddha said: "It should be given to workers, garden keepers, or śrāmaṇeras."

"If bhikṣus dwelling in a forest hermitage need jars, basins, or other utensils, village bhikṣus should provide them. If they need bedding, they should also provide it."

Along the path there were trees or grass that obstructed the bhikṣus' passage or caught and tore their robes. The Buddha said: "They are permitted to braid the grass and push it aside to the edge of the path, or to cover it with something. They are permitted to bend back tree branches and tie them to the tree." When braiding and tying grass and branches, twigs broke and leaves fell. The Buddha said: "If unintentional, there is no offense."

There were bhikṣus dwelling in a forest hermitage who went out early in the morning and the dew dampened their robes and ruined the color. The Buddha said: "They are permitted to raise the robes up high, and are also permitted to use a stick to knock off the dew. Near the village, they should put on their robes properly as prescribed. The dew-knocking stick should be hidden in one place, and retrieved on the return trip."

There were bhikṣus dwelling in a forest hermitage who needed earth to make mud. The Buddha said: "If there is no attendant, they are permitted to take earth from a collapsed riverbank. If there is no collapsed riverbank earth, they are permitted to pour water on the ground, cut grass and spread it on top, stamp it into mud, and use it."

There were bhikṣus dwelling in a forest hermitage who, when leaving, did not put away the Sangha's rope beds, wooden beds, and bedding, which rotted or were burned by fire. The Buddha said: "They should put them away in one place, lock the door, hide the door key in a place protected from rain, mark it, and then leave. If other bhikṣus go there, they should be told where the key is hidden."

At that time, bhikṣus were making robes without stretching and measuring the cloth, cutting directly. The results were either too long, or too short, or crooked, and they could not produce a proper robe. When they sought more cloth, they could not obtain it.

Also, there were bhikṣus who always wore the same single robe, wearing it at the dwelling and also when entering the village.

Also, there were bhikṣus who used their three robes to wrap fruits, gourds, grass, wood, leaves, and cow dung.

There were bhikṣus who used their eating bowls to remove refuse, hold leftovers, hold drinks past the midday hour, hold incense and medicine; or who did not wash and put away the bowl; or who placed it in the sun; or who placed it on the ground; or who placed it in a precarious spot. The bhikṣus reported all these matters to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I now establish for the bhikṣus the preliminary training rules for robes and bowls. They should be trained in for one's entire life. If one does not train, it is a duṣkṛta!"

When a bhikṣu obtains a new robe, he should first wash it, stretch it out and measure it, and then cut it. After cutting, he should sew it. After sewing, he should dye it and hang it upside down to dry. After dyeing, he should spread it on the ground. If he wishes to hang it, he should fasten ties at the top and bottom. The saṅghāṭī should be kept according to the rules for the saṅghāṭī, and must not be used to wrap various objects. The uttarāsaṅga, the antaravāsaka, and all other formally held robes should likewise be treated the same way. They should be carefully guarded like the thin skin of one's own body. The bowl should be maintained according to the rules for bowls, and must not be treated carelessly as described above. It should be carefully guarded as one would guard one's own eyes.

I notice that while the header mentions Fascicle 27 (卷第二十七), the actual content indicator says "Fascicle 028: 五分律" but no Chinese text has been provided for translation.

There is no Chinese text content between the delimiter lines to translate. The space between the two `========================================` markers is empty.

Could you please provide the actual Chinese text of the fascicle you would like me to translate? I am ready to translate it in full once the source text is supplied.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421, Fascicle 28

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

---

## Fifth Division, Part Five: The Method of Obstructing the Poṣadha

The Buddha was in the country of Campā, beside the Ganges River. At that time, on the fifteenth day, the time of the poṣadha, the World-Honored One sat in the open air, surrounded by the assembly of bhikṣus in front and behind. He surveyed the entire Sangha and remained silent. When the first watch of the night had passed, Ānanda rose from his seat, went forward, bowed at the Buddha's feet, knelt with palms joined, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The first watch of the night has passed, and the assembly has been sitting for a long time. We wish that you would recite the precepts for the bhikṣus!" The World-Honored One remained silent, and Ānanda returned to his seat. When the middle watch of the night had passed, he again made the same request, and the Buddha was again silent. At the last watch he again said: "The dawn is about to appear, and the assembly has been sitting for a long time. We wish that you would recite the precepts for the bhikṣus!" The Buddha said to Ānanda: "The assembly is not pure. The Tathāgata will not recite the precepts for them."

At that time Maudgalyāyana had this thought: "Who in this assembly is impure, such that the World-Honored One would say such a thing?" He then surveyed the entire assembly and saw one bhikṣu sitting near the Buddha who was not a bhikṣu yet claimed to be a bhikṣu, was not a śramaṇa yet claimed to be a śramaṇa, did not cultivate the holy life yet claimed to cultivate the holy life, had accomplished evil dharmas, concealed his offenses, and had not abandoned wrong views. He rose from his seat, went before that person, and said: "The Tathāgata has already seen you! Leave! Be gone! Do not remain here!" He then seized his arm, led him out, and placed him outside the door, then returned to his own seat. The Buddha said to Maudgalyāyana: "How extraordinary, Maudgalyāyana! How unprecedented! This foolish person did not himself acknowledge his offenses, so that another person had to seize his arm and put him out." Thereupon Ānanda again rose from his seat and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The assembly is now pure. We wish that you would recite the precepts for the bhikṣus!" The Buddha told Ānanda: "From now on, you shall recite the precepts among yourselves. I shall no longer recite the precepts for bhikṣus. Why is this? If the assembly is impure and the Tathāgata recites the precepts, that person who has violated the precepts will have his head split into seven pieces." He further told Ānanda: "The great ocean has eight unprecedented qualities, and the asuras delight in dwelling therein. What are the eight? The great ocean is gradually deep; its tides do not exceed their limits; it does not harbor dead corpses overnight; the hundred rivers converge into it, yet lose their separate names; the myriad streams all return to it, yet it neither increases nor decreases; it produces pearls, maṇi gems, coral, lapis lazuli, conch-shell jade, gold, silver, crystal, and various other treasures; great-bodied beings all dwell within it; it has a single salty taste. These are the eight. My true Dharma is likewise, having eight unprecedented qualities in which all bhikṣus take delight. What are the eight? Gradual establishment of rules, gradual teaching, gradual learning; my disciples never dare transgress the precepts that have been established; those who transgress are certainly expelled, without being harbored overnight; those of mixed classes who go forth all abandon their original surnames and are called 'śramaṇas of the Śākya clan'; the good men and good women who go forth mostly attain nirvāṇa without remainder, and this neither increases nor decreases; there are various Dharma treasures, namely the four foundations of mindfulness, up to the eightfold noble path and all factors aiding the Way; there are great persons: arhats and those progressing toward arhatship, down to stream-enterers and those progressing toward stream-entry, dwelling in the true Dharma; whoever enters it partakes of the single flavor of liberation. These are the eight."

At that time the group-of-six bhikṣus committed offenses without repenting, yet performed the poṣadha, and some bhikṣus imitated them. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. Those who transgress commit a duṣkṛta!"

The bhikṣus still continued to commit offenses without repenting and yet performed the poṣadha. The Buddha said: "Their poṣadha should be obstructed."

There were bhikṣus who obstructed before the poṣadha was performed, or who obstructed after the poṣadha was already completed. All such cases of obstructing the poṣadha are as explained in the section on obstructing the pravāraṇā.

---

## Fifth Division, Part Six: The Method of Separate Dwelling (Parivāsa)

At that time, bhikṣus dwelling in separate residence (parivāsa) ordained śrāmaṇeras, conferred full ordination, served as reliance teachers, and kept śrāmaṇeras. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who accepted reverence from other pure bhikṣus, and had them carry their robes, bowls, and leather sandals. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who, upon seeing a bhikṣu of good standing coming, would hide and conceal themselves, fearing he would learn of their parivāsa. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who, when the Sangha was invited to a meal, went back to eat in their private rooms. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! They should eat after the senior bhikṣus, going last in the serving line."

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who walked ahead of bhikṣus of good standing. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who wished to go to laypeople's homes and traveled together with bhikṣus of good standing. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who either committed the same original offense again or committed other grave offenses. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who, in the presence of bhikṣus of good standing, did not wear their robes properly draped. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who always wore their three robes while working, getting them dirty with mud. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who sat on the same bed with bhikṣus of good standing, or who themselves sat on fine beds. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who walked in meditation alongside bhikṣus of good standing, or who themselves used the superior walking meditation paths. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who accepted the Sangha's assignment to recite the precepts, or to chant sūtras and hymns. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who performed various karmas: the karma of censure, the karma of expulsion, the karma of requiring reliance, the karma of bringing charges, the karma of requiring submission. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who conversed with bhikṣus of good standing. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who wished to teach the Dharma to the four assemblies. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who did not keep track of the number of days of their parivāsa, whether months, half-months, or a full year. The Buddha said: "They should know."

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who, in the presence of bhikṣus of good standing, tucked their robes behind them, stood with hands on hips, wore leather sandals, covered their heads, wore their robes covering both shoulders, or sat down, or lay down. The Buddha said: "None of this should be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who did not follow the rules for bhikṣus in parivāsa. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who did not inform visiting bhikṣus who arrived, and did not inform bhikṣus who were departing. The Buddha said: "They should inform them."

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who informed the Sangha every day. The Buddha said: "They should inform at the time of poṣadha. However, if performing mānāpya, they should inform daily."

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who wished to travel far, and asked the Buddha about it. The Buddha said: "They should relinquish it before going. They should relinquish in this manner:"

Facing one bhikṣu of good standing, say: "Venerable, please listen! I now relinquish the parivāsa practice, and will resume it later." Say this three times.

If one departs without relinquishing, and encounters bhikṣus on the road, one should declare one's parivāsa to them.

Bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa made lengthy declarations of their parivāsa on the road. Laypeople who saw them said: "What offense has this bhikṣu committed that he is repenting?" The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "On the road one should not make a lengthy declaration, but simply say: 'Venerable! I, the bhikṣu so-and-so, am practicing parivāsa. I have completed such-and-such number of days, with such-and-such number of days remaining. Venerable, please keep this in mind!'"

When that bhikṣu has relinquished the parivāsa and gone to another place, he should request that Sangha to resume the parivāsa practice. That Sangha should permit the parivāsa practice; if they do not permit it, they commit a duṣkṛta.

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who, after accepting the parivāsa practice, went to dwell in a place without any bhikṣus, and during the parivāsa committed further grave offenses. The Buddha said: "A bhikṣu in parivāsa must not dwell alone in a place by himself, and should not commit further grave offenses. Those who transgress all commit duṣkṛta!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who, after accepting parivāsa, went to dwell with bhikṣus who were not of good standing. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! If there is even one bhikṣu of good standing there who permits their stay, those who transgress commit a duṣkṛta!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who shared a room with bhikṣus of good standing. The Buddha said: "This should not be done!"

There were also bhikṣus dwelling in parivāsa who bathed together with bhikṣus of good standing in the same bathhouse. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! They are permitted to carry firewood into the bathhouse, wash and sweep the bathhouse, prepare ash, earth, and bean-paste soap, lay out seats, help the bhikṣus remove their robes and leather sandals, oil and massage their bodies, and do whatever those bhikṣus will accept."

There were also bhikṣus under the same parivāsa who bathed together in the bathhouse. The Buddha said: "This is permitted! But they should take turns attending to each other's needs."

A bhikṣu in parivāsa has three lowest positions: last among the senior bhikṣus in the serving line, given the lowest bedding, and given the lowest quarters.

A bhikṣu in parivāsa has three matters that follow his original seniority: when the Sangha receives offerings, at the time of pravāraṇā, and when distributing bowls.

A bhikṣu in parivāsa has eight things that cause the loss of the parivāsa practice: going to another place without informing; not informing visiting bhikṣus from outside; going out oneself without informing; not informing when others depart; dwelling alone in one place; during the parivāsa, committing further grave offenses; sharing a room overnight with a bhikṣu of good standing; departing on a long journey without relinquishing the parivāsa, and on the road encountering bhikṣus without informing them.

The practice of mānāpya is likewise lost by eight things: except for "dwelling alone in one place," replace it with "performing it in a Sangha of fewer than twenty," with the remaining seven as above.

---

## Fifth Division, Part Seven: The Method of Disciplinary Adjudication

At that time, the elder Upāli asked the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Did Sudinna Kalandakaputta commit a pārājika?" The Buddha said: "For the first occurrence, none are found guilty."

He also asked: "Did the bhikṣu in the forest hermitage commit an offense?" The Buddha said: "He committed an offense!"

There was a bhikṣu who had a mental illness and engaged in sexual intercourse. When he recovered from his madness, he had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Those who are insane are all without offense. Those with scattered minds or minds ruined by illness are likewise."

Sundarananda the Vajjiputtaka, without having renounced the precepts, engaged in sexual acts. Later he had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "He committed an offense!"

There was a bhikṣu who engaged in sexual intercourse with a person of dual sex organs. There was a bhikṣu who engaged in sexual intercourse with a woman whose two orifices were joined. There was a bhikṣu who engaged in sexual intercourse with a paṇḍaka. There was a bhikṣu who engaged in sexual intercourse with a man. There was a bhikṣu who engaged in sexual intercourse with a young boy. All later had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All committed offenses!"

There was a bhikṣu who engaged in sexual intercourse with a young girl, and the girl then died. Having doubts about committing two pārājikas, he asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "For the sexual act, he committed a pārājika. For the girl's death, he committed a sthūlātyaya."

There was a bhikṣu who made a wooden female figure and engaged in sexual intercourse with it. Later he had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If semen was emitted, it is a saṃghāvaśeṣa. If not emitted, it is a sthūlātyaya." The same applies to figures made of clay or painted images of women.

There was a bhikṣu who engaged in sexual intercourse with an elephant. Later he had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "He committed an offense!" The same applies to all animals.

At that time in Rājagṛha there was a foolish and devoted upāsikā who held this view: "To give the gift of sexual intercourse is the foremost gift." She invited bhikṣus and offered herself to them. The bhikṣus said: "Sister! This should not be done. It is prohibited by the Buddha!" The woman further said: "Doing it lying down is an offense, but doing it standing is not called an offense." The bhikṣu then followed her words. Later he had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It is an offense!" Doing it sitting, with the woman on top; the woman moving while the bhikṣu does not move: all are likewise.

At that time in Śrāvastī there was a foolish and devoted upāsikā named Sulaghu, who held this view: "To give the gift of sexual acts is the foremost gift." She invited bhikṣus and offered herself to them. The bhikṣus said: "Sister! This should not be done. It is prohibited by the Buddha!" The woman further said: "The three orifices are offenses, but between the thighs, in the navel, and in all other places, it is not called an offense." The bhikṣu then followed her words. Later he had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If semen was emitted, all are saṃghāvaśeṣa. If not emitted, all are sthūlātyaya."

At that time, in a forest hermitage, a bhikṣu was sleeping soundly in the open air. A woman saw him and performed sexual intercourse upon him. When the bhikṣu awoke, he saw impure substance on his body, and also saw the woman leaving from there. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha asked: "Did you experience pleasure?" He answered: "I did not." The Buddha said: "No offense. However, sleeping soundly in the open air is a duṣkṛta." Sleeping with the room door open is likewise.

There was another bhikṣu sleeping soundly in the open air, and a woman performed sexual intercourse upon him. The bhikṣu awoke and experienced pleasure upon withdrawal. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "He committed an offense!"

In Vaiśālī there was an arhat bhikṣu who suffered from a wind disease, and his entire body was rigid and stiff. His attendant placed him in the open air and went into the village to beg for food. A woman came and performed sexual intercourse upon him. After the act, the bhikṣu's male organ remained stiff and erect. The women said: "This is a virile man." They then anointed him with perfume, adorned his head with flower garlands, paid respects, and departed. When the attendant returned, he saw impure substance on the bhikṣu's body. He thought: "This bhikṣu does not cultivate the holy life. He has broken the pure precepts. I should obstruct his poṣadha!" Then he thought again: "The World-Honored One does not permit obstructing the poṣadha of a sick bhikṣu. I should wait until he recovers." After the bhikṣu recovered, the attendant obstructed his poṣadha, saying: "When you were sick before, you broke the precepts!" The bhikṣu answered: "At that time I was ill, my body was rigid and stiff, and I could not control myself. It was not breaking the precepts." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This bhikṣu has already attained arhatship. Due to wind disease his body was rigid, and he could not control himself. Because he did not experience pleasure, there is no offense. The attending bhikṣu who left him in the open air and departed commits a duṣkṛta."

At that time, a bhikṣu inserted his male organ into another bhikṣu's mouth. Later, both had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the one who inserted did it in jest, it is a sthūlātyaya. If the one who received it was not in jest, it is a pārājika. If the receiver was in jest, it is a sthūlātyaya. If the inserter was not in jest, it is a pārājika. If both were in jest, both commit sthūlātyaya. If neither was in jest, both commit pārājika."

At that time, bhikṣus bathed together with laypeople in a layperson's bathhouse. The laypeople observed their physical features and described them to women. Also, physical contact gave rise to minds of attachment, and consequently some returned to lay life or became non-Buddhist practitioners. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! If one bathes in a layperson's bathhouse together with laypeople, it is a sthūlātyaya."

There was an elderly bhikṣu who, in a dream, engaged in sexual intercourse with his former wife. Upon awakening, he came out of his room crying aloud: "I am not a śramaṇa! I am not a son of the Śākya clan!" The bhikṣus asked the reason. He answered: "I engaged in sexual intercourse with my former wife." They asked: "Where is your former wife now?" He answered: "In my native village." They asked: "Did she come here? Did you go there?" He answered: "She did not come, and I did not go." They asked: "Then how did you engage in sexual intercourse?" He answered: "In a dream I engaged in sexual intercourse." The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense. However, not maintaining mindfulness before falling asleep incurs a duṣkṛta."

There was a bhikṣu who was standing to urinate when a dog came and seized his male organ. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense. Standing to urinate incurs a duṣkṛta. In a bhikṣu's dwelling place, such animals should not be kept. Those who keep them commit a duṣkṛta!"

There was a bhikṣu whose male organ was long, and with a lustful mind he inserted it into his own anus. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "He committed an offense!"

There was a bhikṣu whose body was flexible, and he inserted his male organ into his own mouth. It is likewise.

There was a bhikṣu who was sitting in meditation when a Māra-woman appeared before him. The bhikṣu, upon seeing her, gave rise to a mind of attachment, and without realizing it rose and seized the woman. The woman fled, and the bhikṣu also ran after her. The woman vanished into a dead horse. The bhikṣu then performed sexual intercourse upon the horse. He immediately felt remorse and told the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The Sangha is permitted to perform a pārājika motion-and-three-announcements karma for that bhikṣu."

That bhikṣu should go before the Sangha, remove his leather sandals, bare his right shoulder, bow at the Sangha's feet, kneel with palms joined, and make this declaration: "I, the bhikṣu so-and-so, committed sexual intercourse and immediately felt remorse, without concealment. I now request the Sangha to perform the pārājika karma for me. May the Sangha perform the pārājika karma for me!" Request thus three times.

A bhikṣu learned in procedure should announce: "Venerable Sangha, please listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so committed sexual intercourse and immediately felt remorse, without concealment. He requests the Sangha to perform the pārājika karma. The Sangha now performs the pārājika karma. If the time is appropriate and the Sangha approves, please listen. The motion is thus."

"Venerable Sangha, please listen! This bhikṣu so-and-so committed sexual intercourse, and so forth, the Sangha now performs the pārājika karma. Will the elders who approve remain silent; those who do not approve, speak." Thus the second and third announcements.

"The Sangha has performed the pārājika karma for the bhikṣu so-and-so. The Sangha approves, as shown by its silence. The matter is thus held."

The Buddha said: "That bhikṣu, for the rest of his life, may not serve food to senior bhikṣus, but shall himself receive food from a lay attendant. At the time of poṣadha, pravāraṇā, or the performance of various karmas, if he comes, that is good. If he does not come, neither party is at fault."

There were bhikṣus who engaged in sexual intercourse with celestial women, nāga women, or asura women. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All committed offenses."

There was a bhikṣu who went to a place where others were preparing food and took a bowl of soup. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "With what intention did you take it?" He answered: "With the intention to steal." The Buddha said: "If its value is five coins, it is a pārājika. If less than five coins, it is a sthūlātyaya." Entering another's garden and taking a fruit or vegetable with intent to steal is likewise.

There was a bhikṣu who was being robbed by bandits, and he struggled to keep his robes and possessions. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense."

There was another bhikṣu who was being robbed by bandits. After the goods had already entered the bandits' hands, or had already been carried away, the bhikṣu later pursued and seized them back. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Had you mentally relinquished the robes?" He answered: "I had not relinquished them." The Buddha said: "If not yet relinquished, no offense. If already relinquished, then it is an offense."

There were bhikṣus in a forest hermitage who saw a wild boar running toward them with an arrow in it. They said to each other: "Let us not say we saw it!" The hunter came along and asked the bhikṣus: "Did you see the boar I shot?" They answered: "Where is any boar? Whose boar? There is no boar." Later they thought: "By saying there is no boar, could we have incurred the offense of concealing a boar?" They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense. If there is such a circumstance and one uses other words to deflect the question, there is no guilt in any case."

There were bhikṣus in a forest hermitage who saw a hunter catch a live deer and tie it up, then leave. The bhikṣus, out of compassion, untied and released it. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense. However, one should not contrive to release another's property. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta." Releasing any living being belonging to another out of compassion is likewise.

There was a bhikṣu who saw a hawk (kuruḍa) carrying meat in flight. He playfully chased it and made it drop the meat. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense. However, one should not, for no beneficial purpose, contrive to cause another to lose something. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta!"

There was a bhikṣu who saw another's ox walking along the road and, with intent to steal, drove it along. He then immediately repented and let it go. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense; he incurs a sthūlātyaya."

There were bhikṣus who saw objects floating in water and, with intent to steal, caught them. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the value is five coins, it is a pārājika."

There were bhikṣus who, not far from the Jetavana, saw someone plowing a field and said: "This is Sangha land. Do not plow it!" The plowman thought: "The bhikṣus are powerful. If they bring a lawsuit against me, I might lose my property." So he stopped plowing. The bhikṣu returned to the Jetavana and asked the other bhikṣus: "Whose field is that?" The bhikṣus said: "It is the field of such-and-such a householder." He then had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense. However, if one contrives without beneficial purpose, intending to cause another loss, it is all duṣkṛta."

At that time, the group of seventeen boys were kidnapped by bandits. Their parents wept and wailed in anguish. Pilindavatsa, while on his alms round, saw this and asked the reason. They told him the full story. Pilindavatsa then entered concentration and observed that the bandits had taken them to an island in the Aciravatī River. He then used his supernatural power to retrieve them and placed each one on the upper story of their respective homes, and told the parents: "Do not weep anymore! Your children have all returned home and are playing on the upper stories." Later he had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense. He incurs a duṣkṛta."

There was a bhikṣu who, for the sake of material gain, exaggerated his own age to make himself senior to others. Later he had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense of theft; for deliberate false speech he incurs a pāyattika."

There was a dwelling place where bhikṣus shared a well with laypeople. The laypeople, with intent to steal, took an excessive amount of water. A bhikṣu also, with intent to steal, thought: "Why shouldn't I also take more?" But he had not yet taken any. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If one takes extra water from another's share, and the value reaches five coins, it is a pārājika."

There was a bhikṣu who stole another's robe at one place, where its value was five coins, and then came to another place to sell it where it was not worth five coins. He believed he had not committed a pārājika. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "At the original place, if it was worth five coins, it is a pārājika. If at the original place it was not worth five coins, but at the place of sale it was worth five coins, it is a sthūlātyaya."

There was a bhikṣu who stole another's Buddhist scripture, thinking that since it was the Buddha's words, there was no offense. Later he had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Calculating the value of the paper, ink, and scribing labor, if it amounts to five coins, he has committed an offense."

At that time in Kauśāmbī there was a householder who had seen the Dharma and attained its fruit, and who regularly provided for the bhikṣus. The householder and his elder sister each had a son. The two of them regularly made offerings to the elder Ācū bhikṣu. When the householder was about to die, he showed the bhikṣu Ācū the location of a hidden treasure and told him: "After my death, whichever of the two boys has faith and delight in the Buddha-Dharma and is always generous toward the bhikṣus, you may show him this treasure." Having spoken, he breathed his last. The bhikṣu Ācū subsequently observed the two youths. The householder's son turned away from the true path, while the sister's son had faith and delight. He therefore told the sister's son and enabled him to obtain the treasure. The householder's son, upon hearing of this, went to Ānanda and asked: "To whom should a father's bequeathed wealth belong?" Ānanda answered: "It should belong to the son." The son then told Ānanda: "The bhikṣu Ācū gave my father's treasure to my cousin." Ānanda then went and told Ācū: "You are not a śramaṇa! You are not a son of the Śākya clan!" Ācū replied: "I am a śramaṇa! I am a son of the Śākya clan! We may judge this matter according to the sūtras and vinaya." Ānanda said: "This matter is self-evident. What need is there for sūtras and vinaya?" At that time, the senior bhikṣus all took Ācū's side and supported him. The followers of Ānanda and the followers of Ācū thus separated into two factions and were no longer in harmony. For six years they conducted the rains retreat separately, and in their respective dwelling places they did not perform poṣadha or pravāraṇā together. The news spread far and wide, reaching even the Brahma heavens.

At that time, Rāhula was traveling in Kapilavastu. The Śākya women all came out to greet him and told him the full story: "How is it that, not long after the World-Honored One's parinirvāṇa, the Sangha has been in disharmony, and this has lasted six years? We wish to bring about reconciliation but do not know the means." Rāhula said: "I will teach you a way to bring about their reconciliation. Ānanda will be coming soon. All who have infants, carry them out to greet him. Then place the children on the ground; the children will certainly cry. Ānanda will surely ask: 'Why don't you pick them up?' You should answer: 'If the elder reconciles with Ācū, only then will we pick up our children!' By this means, reconciliation can be achieved."

Shortly after, Ānanda arrived. Five hundred Śākya women carried their children out to greet him and placed them all on the ground before Ānanda. The children cried loudly. Ānanda, as expected, said: "Why don't you pick them up?" They answered in unison: "If the elder reconciles with Ācū, only then will we pick up our children!" Ānanda was moved by their sentiment and also felt compassion for the infants. He said: "Sisters, pick up your children. I will reconcile with him." He then returned and assembled the Sangha. Upāli asked Ānanda: "In how many ways does a bhikṣu take what is not given, such that he is not a śramaṇa, not a son of the Śākya clan?" Ānanda answered: "There are three ways: taking oneself, instructing someone to take, or sending someone to take." He then asked Ācū: "Did you take it yourself, instruct someone to take it, or send someone to take it?" He answered: "No!" He then asked Ānanda: "What offense has the bhikṣu Ācū committed?" Ānanda said: "Ācū has no fault; the fault is mine!" Thereupon Ānanda proclaimed throughout the Sangha: "The fault is mine; Ācū has no fault." He then went to Ācū, made confession, and they were reconciled.

There was a bhikṣu who had formerly been a thief. He told the other bhikṣus: "Let us go together to that village and take things." The bhikṣus followed his words and went first to take them. That bhikṣu later had doubts and asked the Buddha: "I instructed others to steal in this way. Did I commit a pārājika?" The Buddha said: "No offense; you incur a sthūlātyaya."

There were bhikṣus who took cloths covering a tomb, banners on a tomb, cloth placed among tombs, and objects in a spirit shrine, all of which were under someone's protection. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the owner's mind has not relinquished them, and the value taken is five coins, it is all pārājika. Objects in a shrine, even if ownerless, if under government protection, are likewise."

There were bhikṣus who, out of anger toward another, set fire to his house, or burned his fields, grain, and possessions. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Not an offense of theft; they incur a sthūlātyaya. Destroying another's property out of anger is likewise."

There was a bhikṣu who found a pouch containing a thousand pieces of gold in a rat's hole. He took it with intent to steal. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "It belongs to the rat; no offense of theft. He incurs a sthūlātyaya. If one takes the property of birds or beasts with intent to steal, it is likewise."

There was a bhikṣu who, during a meal, ate another's portion of food without permission. The other asked: "Who ate my portion?" He answered: "I ate it!" The other became angry. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Without intent to steal, there is no offense. One should not presumptously eat another's portion assuming mutual consent. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta!"

There were bhikṣus who gambled and won other people's property. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense of theft; they incur a duṣkṛta."

There were two bhikṣus who, by mutual consent, exchanged and wore each other's robes. Later they quarreled and accused each other of stealing. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense. One should not presumptously wear another's robes by assumed mutual consent. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta!"

At that time Upananda was traveling on the road together with merchants. When they reached the customs checkpoint, a merchant borrowed Upananda's bag and secretly placed a jewel of great value inside it, then returned it. Upananda did not notice. After passing through the checkpoint, the merchant asked for the jewel from the bag. Upananda said: "I did not take your jewel." The merchant said: "You truly did not take it. Earlier when I borrowed your bag, I placed the jewel inside it!" He then returned the jewel to the merchant. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense. If, when about to pass through a checkpoint, someone asks to borrow one's belongings, after they are returned, one should shake them out and inspect them. Those who fail to do so commit a duṣkṛta!"

At that time, a disciple of the bhikṣuṇī Caṇḍā Sumanā went to her teacher's patron's home and falsely said: "My teacher is ill and requests three kinds of medicinal porridge." Having received it, she ate it herself outside. The women of that household later went to pay a visit and asked: "Has the ācāryā recovered from her illness?" She answered: "I have not been ill at all. Why do you ask?" They then told her the full story. The teacher said to her disciple: "You stole my porridge!" The disciple answered: "I truly did not steal it. I took it from the preceptor assuming mutual consent!" She had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Without intent to steal, no offense. She incurs the pāyattika for deliberate false speech."

There was a bhikṣu who saw that his patron's field had no water, and diverted water belonging to another to irrigate it. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the value is five coins, it is an offense."

There was a bhikṣu at a high place who threw another's robe down to a bhikṣu at a lower place. Both had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the thrower had intent to steal, it is a pārājika. If the receiver had no intent to steal, it is a sthūlātyaya. If the receiver had no intent to steal in throwing, it is a sthūlātyaya. If the taker had intent to steal, it is a pārājika. If both had intent to steal, both commit pārājika. If neither had intent to steal, both commit sthūlātyaya."

There were bhikṣus who, with intent to steal, bartered for good items belonging to the Sangha. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the value of the bartered difference is five coins, it is an offense."
