At that time, a bhikṣu threw a stone at a snake, but accidentally hit and killed a person. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "With what intention?" He answered: "I intended to kill the snake." The Buddha said: "For the person's death, no offense. For throwing at the snake, a duṣkṛta."

There was a bhikṣu who killed a monkey, because it resembled a human. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Taking the life of an animal is a pāyattika."

There was a woman whose husband was away traveling, and she had an illicit affair and became pregnant. She requested an abortifacient medicine from a bhikṣu who regularly provided for her. He gave it to her; the fetus died but the mother did not die. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "He committed an offense. If one intends to abort the fetus and the mother dies but the child does not die, it is a sthūlātyaya. If both die, it is a pārājika. If neither dies, it is a sthūlātyaya. Pressing the abdomen to cause an abortion is likewise."

There were bhikṣus who took no joy in cultivating the holy life, yet would not disrobe, and returned to base conduct. From a high place they threw themselves down to kill themselves, but fell upon a person below. The person below died, but they themselves did not die. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "With what intention?" They answered: "We intended to kill ourselves by falling." The Buddha said: "For the other person's death, no offense. Contriving to kill oneself is always a sthūlātyaya."

There were two bhikṣus who had previously quarreled with each other. Later they traveled together on the road. On the way they fought, and one of them died. The survivor had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "With what intention?" He answered: "With an angry mind." The Buddha said: "Without intent to kill, no offense. Being angry at another bhikṣu incurs a pāyattika. From now on, those who are angry with each other and have not yet reconciled are not permitted to travel together on the road. Those who do so commit a duṣkṛta!"

There was a bhikṣu who struck and killed a ghost. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "He committed a sthūlātyaya."

There was a bhikṣu who intended to kill one person but accidentally killed another. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense for the intended target; he incurs a sthūlātyaya."

At that time, bhikṣus, for the sake of material gain, offered various praises of others' accomplishment of precepts, accomplishment of concentration, accomplishment of wisdom, accomplishment of liberation, and accomplishment of the knowledge and vision of liberation, while covertly glorifying themselves. Later they had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Such indirect self-praise, where one does not explicitly declare it, all incurs a sthūlātyaya."

In Vaiśālī there was a great tree called a nyagrodha, whose shade covered five hundred chariots. The bhikṣuṇī Utpalavarṇā saw it and told the other bhikṣuṇīs: "When I was in heaven, the flower I wore by my ear was as large as this tree." The bhikṣuṇīs considered this impossible and censured her in various ways: "How can a bhikṣuṇī claim to have attained superhuman dharma?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "In heaven there truly are such flowers. The bhikṣuṇī Utpalavarṇā spoke the truth; there is no offense."

The bhikṣu Pilindavatsa's private servant had five hundred boils on his body. The boils were festering and horrible to look at. The bhikṣuṇī Utpalavarṇā, together with five hundred bhikṣuṇīs, went to pay a visit. Upon seeing him, she laughed. The bhikṣuṇīs censured her: "How can you have no compassion? Seeing a bhikṣu in such condition, you laugh at him?" Utpalavarṇā replied: "In a past life, this bhikṣu was a great king named Vīḍagarbha. At that time I was his chief queen. That king forcibly seized five hundred maidens and violated their virginity. Due to this cause, for countless hundreds of thousands of myriads of years he fell into the great hell, where he was burned and boiled with bitter torments. The residual retribution is these five hundred boils." The bhikṣuṇīs considered this impossible and censured her in various ways: "How can a bhikṣuṇī claim to have attained superhuman dharma?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "That bhikṣu's story is indeed so! Utpalavarṇā spoke the truth; there is no offense."

At that time, the elder Maudgalyāyana told the bhikṣus: "I have seen lotus flowers in Lake Anavatapta, as large as chariot wheels." The bhikṣus did not believe him and considered this a false claim of superhuman dharma. They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Such flowers truly exist. Maudgalyāyana spoke the truth; there is no offense."

There were bhikṣus who falsely claimed to have attained superhuman dharma, saying: "I have karmic causes and conditions for the divine eye, divine ear, and knowledge of others' minds." Later they had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "No offense of pārājika; they incur a sthūlātyaya."

There were bhikṣus who falsely claimed to have attained superhuman dharma, saying: "I have attained the divine eye, the divine ear, knowledge of others' minds, and all my outflows are exhausted." Later they had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They committed an offense."

There was a brahmin who invited the Sangha for a meal, saying: "May the venerable arhats come and sit to eat." After the meal he said: "May the arhats return." The bhikṣus had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "When others make such praises on their own, there is no offense in any case."

There were bhikṣus who, for the sake of material gain, sat, rose, walked, stood, and spoke with composure, displaying the appearance of one who has attained the Way, wishing to make others think so. Later they had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All such displays of extraordinary conduct incur a sthūlātyaya."

There were bhikṣus who, on the verge of death, were destined to fall into hell. They saw all the signs of hell, with hell-wardens before them. There were also bhikṣus destined to be reborn in heaven, who saw all the celestial palaces, heard the sound of music, and had celestial sons and celestial maidens speaking to them before them. They all told others of this. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "These are the auspicious signs of where one is to be reborn. It is not false speech; there is no offense."

There were bhikṣus who had originally intended to speak of other matters, but inadvertently spoke of superhuman dharma. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Since it was not the original intention, there is no offense; they incur a sthūlātyaya."

There were bhikṣus who told laypeople: "Whoever stays in your house will all accomplish such-and-such attainments of the Way." Then they themselves went to stay there. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Making such contrivances all incurs a sthūlātyaya."

There were bhikṣus who declared that they had attained superhuman dharma, intending for humans to hear, but non-humans heard instead. Others intended for non-humans to hear, but humans heard instead. Others intended for both humans and non-humans to hear, but no one heard. All had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All incur a sthūlātyaya."

At that time, Maudgalyāyana told the bhikṣus: "The wife of such-and-such a householder will bear a boy." But when she was about to give birth, the child turned out to be a girl. King Prasenajit went to war with King Ajātaśatru. Maudgalyāyana said: "King Prasenajit will be victorious." But the opposite happened. They assembled for battle again, and he said: "King Ajātaśatru will be victorious." Again the opposite happened. The bhikṣus censured him, saying: "How can you falsely claim superhuman dharma?" They reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "What Maudgalyāyana said was correct; he only observed the earlier conditions, not the later changes!"

At that time, a bhikṣu was scratching his private area, and semen came out. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "With what intention?" He answered: "From beginning to end I had no intention to emit." The Buddha said: "No offense. If one intends to emit and it comes out, it is a saṃghāvaśeṣa. If one intends to emit but it does not come out, it is a sthūlātyaya." When semen comes out due to bathing in warm water or warming by fire, all are likewise.

There was a bhikṣu who recalled engaging in sexual activity, and semen came out. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "With what intention?" He answered: "I recalled engaging in sexual activity, and semen came out by itself." The Buddha said: "No offense. Recalling sexual activity is a duṣkṛta."

There was a bhikṣu who deliberately rubbed his organ against his robe and emitted semen. He believed it was not a saṃghāvaśeṣa. He asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "For a bhikṣu who emits semen in this way, it is a saṃghāvaśeṣa. If semen is not emitted, it is a sthūlātyaya."

There was a bhikṣu who emitted semen near a female image. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If semen is emitted, it is a saṃghāvaśeṣa. If not emitted, it is a sthūlātyaya."

At that time, there was a bhikṣu who jabbed a woman's body with his elbow. There was also a bhikṣu who used a bowl-hook to pull a woman. They had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All committed a sthūlātyaya. If one seizes her clothing, or pulls her by a rope or staff, it is likewise."

There was a bhikṣu who, when a woman was on a bed, in a boat, on a cart, or in a tree, rocked it with a lustful mind. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "In all such cases, the bhikṣu committed a sthūlātyaya."

At that time, there was a woman wearing a blue garment. A bhikṣu, upon seeing her, said: "Sister! Your 'blue' is splendid." He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "He committed a sthūlātyaya. If one uses such euphemisms based on physical form to make lewd speech, all are likewise."

At that time, there was a married couple who quarreled and separated. A bhikṣu went and reconciled them. He had doubts and asked the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If the marital bond had already been dissolved and one reconciles them, it is a saṃghāvaśeṣa. If it had not yet been dissolved, there is no offense."

There is no Chinese text between the dividers to translate. Could you please provide the actual Chinese text of Fascicle 28 (卷第二十八) of the 彌沙塞部和醯五分律 (Taishō T1421) so that I can translate it in full?

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Twenty-Nine

### Part Five, Section Eight: Rules for Bhikṣuṇīs

At that time, the World-Honored One returned to the Śākya land. Before reaching the city of Kapilavastu, he stopped beneath a nyagrodha tree. King Śuddhodana came out to welcome him. Seeing from afar the World-Honored One's extraordinary countenance, like a golden mountain, he prostrated at the Buddha's feet and spoke this verse:

> "At birth, the physiognomists foretold; hearing this, I offered my first reverence.
> When the tree inclined, I bowed my head; now that the Way is accomplished, I prostrate thrice."

Having spoken this verse, he withdrew and sat to one side. The Buddha expounded various subtle teachings for him, until he saw the Dharma and attained its fruit. He rose from his seat, bared his right shoulder, knelt on both knees with palms joined, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I wish to go forth from the household life and receive the full precepts of ordination." The Buddha thereupon observed him and saw that should the king go forth, he would gain nothing further. He then said to the king: "Do not be heedless! In due course you will naturally attain this wondrous Dharma." Thereupon the king requested to take the Three Refuges and Five Precepts. After receiving the Five Precepts, the Buddha further expounded various subtle teachings for him, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and delighting him, and the king returned to his residence. After the king returned to the palace, he had it proclaimed three times in the courtyard: "Whoever wishes to go forth in the Tathāgata's True Dharma and Vinaya is permitted to do so."

At that time, Mahāprajāpatī Gautamī heard the king's proclamation. Together with five hundred Śākya women surrounding her, she took two new garments and went to where the Buddha was. She prostrated with her head at his feet and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I personally wove these garments and now offer them to you. I pray you will accept them!" The Buddha said: "You may offer them to the Sangha; you will obtain great merit." She again made the same request as above. The Buddha said: "You may offer them to the Sangha; I am counted among the Sangha." She again made the same request as above. The Buddha said: "I will accept one; offer the other to the Sangha." She then followed his instruction and made offerings to both the Buddha and the Sangha. Gautamī further said to the Buddha: "I pray that you permit women to go forth in the Buddha's True Dharma and receive the full precepts of ordination!" The Buddha said: "Stop, stop! Do not speak thus! Why? All the Buddhas of the past never permitted women to go forth. Such women relied on the Buddha while remaining at home, shaving their heads and wearing kāṣāya robes, diligently practicing with vigor, and attained the fruit of the Way. The Buddhas of the future will likewise be the same. I now permit you to take this as your practice!" Gautamī made this request three times as above, and the Buddha likewise three times refused as above. Thereupon Gautamī wept loudly, prostrated at his feet, and withdrew.

The Buddha departed from Kapilavastu together with a great assembly of twelve hundred and fifty bhikṣus, traveling through the land. Gautamī and the five hundred Śākya women themselves shaved their heads, put on kāṣāya robes, and followed behind weeping, always staying wherever the World-Honored One stayed for the night.

The Buddha gradually traveled until he reached Śrāvastī and stayed in the Jetavana. Gautamī and the five hundred Śākya women stood weeping at the gate. Ānanda, going out in the morning, saw them in this state and asked the reason. They answered: "Venerable one! The World-Honored One does not permit women to go forth and receive the full precepts of ordination. We therefore grieve and lament! We pray that you will speak on our behalf so that we may fulfill our aspiration!" Ānanda then returned, prostrated with his head at the Buddha's feet, and fully reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha stopped Ānanda and spoke as above. Ānanda further said to the Buddha: "When the Buddha was born, after only a few days his mother passed away. Gautamī nursed and raised the World-Honored One until he was grown. How can such great kindness not be repaid?" The Buddha said: "I too have shown great kindness to Gautamī. Through reliance on me, she came to know the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha, and gave rise to reverence and faith. If a person relies on a good teacher and comes to know the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha, giving rise to faith and reverence, then even if one were to attend that person with robes, food, and medicine for one's entire life, one could never repay the debt!" Ānanda further said to the Buddha: "If women go forth and receive the full precepts of ordination, can they attain the four fruits of the śramaṇa path?" The Buddha said: "They can attain them!" Ānanda said: "If they can attain the four fruits, World-Honored One, why do you not permit them to go forth and receive the full precepts of ordination?" The Buddha said: "I now permit Gautamī to accept the eight rules that cannot be transgressed; this itself constitutes going forth and receiving the full precepts of ordination. What are the eight? A bhikṣuṇī should every half-month request from the assembly of bhikṣus a person to give instruction. A bhikṣuṇī should not spend the summer retreat in a place where there are no bhikṣus. At the time of pravāraṇā, a bhikṣuṇī should request from the assembly of bhikṣus the three matters of offenses seen, heard, or suspected. A śikṣamāṇā, after completing two years of training in the precepts, should receive the full precepts of ordination in the assembly of both orders. A bhikṣuṇī must not revile a bhikṣu, and must not speak of a bhikṣu's violations of precepts, violations of deportment, or violations of right view before laypeople. A bhikṣuṇī must not charge a bhikṣu with an offense, but a bhikṣu may reprove a bhikṣuṇī. When a bhikṣuṇī commits a grave offense, she should perform mānatva for a half-month in the assembly of both orders; after performing mānatva for a half-month, she should seek rehabilitation in an assembly of twenty members of each order. Even if a bhikṣuṇī has been ordained for a hundred years, she should still prostrate to, rise for, and greet a newly ordained bhikṣu." Ānanda received this instruction and went out to tell Gautamī: "Listen carefully! I will speak what the Buddha has taught." Gautamī straightened her robes, prostrated from afar at the Buddha's feet, knelt upright with palms joined, and listened single-mindedly. Ānanda recounted everything in full as above. Gautamī said: "Just as a young man or woman, clean and fond of bathing their body, puts on fresh, clean garments, and if someone bestows upon them a garland of campaka flowers, a garland of vārṣika flowers, a garland of utpala flowers, or a garland of atimuktaka flowers, that person would joyfully receive it with both hands and place it upon their head, so too I now receive upon my head the World-Honored One's Dharma instruction." She further said to Ānanda: "I pray that you will again go in and report to the World-Honored One that I have received the eight rules upon my head! Among the eight rules, I wish to make one request: please permit bhikṣuṇīs to pay respects to bhikṣus according to seniority! How can a bhikṣuṇī of a hundred years' standing prostrate to a newly ordained bhikṣu?"

Ānanda again reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha told Ānanda: "If I were to permit bhikṣuṇīs to pay respects to bhikṣus according to seniority, there would be no grounds for that. Women have five obstacles: they cannot become Śakra, Lord of the Devas; Māra, the Heavenly King; Brahmā, the Heavenly King; a wheel-turning sage king; or the Dharma King of the three realms. If women were not permitted to go forth and receive the full precepts of ordination, the Buddha's True Dharma would abide in the world for a thousand years. Now that they are permitted to go forth, it is diminished by five hundred years. Just as in a household with many daughters and few sons, one should know that household will decline before long." He further told Ānanda: "If women had not gone forth in my Dharma to receive the full precepts of ordination, then after my parinirvāṇa, upāsakas and upāsikās would hold the four offerings, follow behind bhikṣus, and say: 'Venerable one! Out of compassion for us, please accept our offerings.' If they saw one upon going outside, they would take him by the arm and say: 'Venerable one, you have shown kindness to us. Please come sit for a while and bring us peace!' If they met on the road, they would all undo their hair, brush the dust from the bhikṣu's feet, and spread it out for him to tread upon. Now that women are permitted to go forth, such things will nearly cease!" Ānanda, hearing this, shed tears of grief and regret, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Had I known this before, had I understood this Dharma, I would not have requested that women be permitted to go forth and receive the full precepts of ordination. If I had known beforehand, how could I have made the request three times?" The Buddha told Ānanda: "Do not weep anymore! Māra obscured your mind; that is why it happened. Now that women are permitted to go forth and receive the full precepts of ordination, they must follow and comply with what I have established and must not violate it. What I have not established, they must not arbitrarily establish!" Ānanda went out and fully conveyed the Buddha's teaching to Gautamī. Gautamī joyfully accepted and practiced it, and thereby accomplished going forth and receiving the full precepts of ordination. She further said to Ānanda: "These five hundred Śākya women, how should they now receive the full precepts of ordination? Please report this on our behalf." Ānanda reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit Bhikṣuṇī Prajāpatī to serve as preceptor for them, and in the presence of a quorum of ten bhikṣus, they shall receive the full precepts of ordination through a motion and three proclamations. I permit that the karma procedure be performed for three persons at a time, but not four."

After they had received the precepts, Bhikṣuṇī Mahāprajāpatī, together with the five hundred bhikṣuṇīs, went to the Buddha. She prostrated with her head at his feet and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! How should we wear our robes?" The Buddha said: "According to the rules for bhikṣus." She further asked: "How should we eat?" He answered: "You are permitted to beg for food." She further asked: "How should we perform the poṣadha?" He answered: "You are permitted to perform the poṣadha separately; every half-month you should request from the bhikṣu Sangha a person to give instruction." She further asked: "How should we observe the summer retreat?" He answered: "You are permitted to observe the three-month summer retreat under a roof." She further asked: "How should we perform the pravāraṇā?" He answered: "You are permitted to perform the pravāraṇā separately. You should go to the bhikṣu Sangha and request regarding offenses seen, heard, or suspected." She further asked: "How should we accept the kaṭhina robe?" He answered: "You are permitted to accept it on the fourth month day through a motion and one proclamation." She further asked: "How should we keep leather goods?" He answered: "You are permitted to make leather sandals for traveling." She further asked: "How should we settle disputes?" The Buddha said: "You are permitted to use the seven methods for settling disputes to settle the four kinds of disputes."

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs were conferring the full precepts without first giving their disciples the two-year training precepts. These women, being ignorant and foolish, were unable to study the precepts. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a duṣkṛta! It should be done as follows: first give the two-year precept of not killing, two years of not stealing, two years of not engaging in sexual conduct, two years of not speaking falsely, two years of not drinking alcohol, two years of not eating at improper times."

At that time, a bhikṣuṇī acted as a matchmaker and committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense. Not knowing what to do, this was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha, on this account, assembled both orders and told the bhikṣus: "I permit the assembly of both orders to give that bhikṣuṇī a half-month mānatva through a motion and three proclamations."

That bhikṣuṇī should go to the Sangha, bare her right shoulder, remove her leather sandals, prostrate at the feet of both orders, and say: "May the venerable Sangha hear me! I, bhikṣuṇī so-and-so, acted as a matchmaker and committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense. I now request from the Sangha a half-month mānatva. May the Sangha grant me a half-month mānatva!" She should make this request three times.

One bhikṣu should proclaim: "May the venerable Sangha hear me! This bhikṣuṇī so-and-so acted as a matchmaker and committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense. She requests from the Sangha a half-month mānatva. The Sangha now grants her a half-month mānatva. If the time is appropriate and the Sangha consents, may it approve. This is the motion."

"May the venerable Sangha hear me! This bhikṣuṇī so-and-so acted as a matchmaker and committed a saṃghāvaśeṣa offense. She requests from the Sangha a half-month mānatva. The Sangha now grants her a half-month mānatva. Whichever elder consents, let him be silent; whoever does not consent, let him speak." This is proclaimed three times.

"The Sangha has granted bhikṣuṇī so-and-so a half-month mānatva; the Sangha consents, as shown by its silence. Let this matter be thus upheld."

After the Sangha has granted her the mānatva to be performed, she should rise early and sweep and clean the rooms at the bhikṣuṇī residence, plaster the walls and floors, fill all water vessels wherever there is a place for water, and do whatever other work there is to be done. If guest bhikṣuṇīs arrive or bhikṣuṇīs depart, she should also report this. She should also take one bhikṣuṇī as companion and go to the bhikṣu residence. If there is work to be done, she should do it all as above. If guest bhikṣus arrive or bhikṣus depart, she should also report this. As the sun sets, she should return to the bhikṣuṇī residence. After performing this for a half-month, she should seek the rehabilitation karma procedure in an assembly of twenty members of each of the two orders, following the same procedure as for bhikṣus.

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs did not prostrate to bhikṣus, and no one instructed them. Being ignorant and foolish, they were unable to study the precepts. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha reproved them, saying: "I previously stated in the eight rules that cannot be transgressed that a bhikṣuṇī of a hundred years' standing should prostrate to a newly ordained bhikṣu. How is it that they now fail to show respect?" After his reproof, he told the bhikṣus: "From now on, I permit bhikṣus to pay respects to senior monks in order of seniority. Bhikṣuṇīs should prostrate to all bhikṣus and also pay respects to one another in order of seniority. A śikṣamāṇā should prostrate to all bhikṣus and bhikṣuṇīs, and also pay respects to one another in order of seniority. Śrāmaṇeras likewise. A śrāmaṇerī should prostrate to all bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, śikṣamāṇās, and śrāmaṇeras, and also pay respects to one another in order of seniority."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who prostrated to bhikṣus from an elevated place while the bhikṣu was below, or who prostrated from behind the bhikṣu, or who prostrated from the side, or who grasped the bhikṣu's foot, or who prostrated with only their knees touching the ground. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "None of these are proper! I permit bhikṣuṇīs, standing neither too close nor too far from the bhikṣu, to join their palms, bow their heads, and say: 'Vandanā.'"

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs' hair grew long. The Buddha said: "They should seek a woman to shave it. If there is no woman available, a man is permitted; but she must not be alone, and he must not hold her. Another bhikṣuṇī as companion should hold her, and then have him shave."

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs studied sūtras under bhikṣuṇīs but could not learn them. There was also a bhikṣuṇī who studied the Prātimokṣa under a bhikṣuṇī and after many days could not learn it. Afterward she studied under a bhikṣu and learned it. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit bhikṣuṇīs to study sūtras under bhikṣus. If there are coarse or offensive words in the sūtra, they should be taught by writing. If she does not know writing, they should be taught from behind a barrier. If there is no barrier, they should be taught sitting back to back."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who performed the poṣadha together with bhikṣus. They saw bhikṣus commit offenses and wished to charge them. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Bhikṣuṇīs must not perform the poṣadha together with bhikṣus. Every half-month, one bhikṣuṇī should be appointed to go to the bhikṣu Sangha and request a person to give instruction."

The bhikṣus were unwilling to make the request on their behalf. The Buddha said: "I permit bhikṣuṇīs to make offerings to them: a bowl bag, a water-straining bag, a waist cord, scented oil, morning food, and afternoon food."

Some made the announcement before the poṣadha, and some made it after the poṣadha was already finished. The Buddha said: "This should not be done. When the announcement is being made of the wishes and purity declarations of those bhikṣus who are absent, she should rise from her seat, stand before the Sangha, and say: 'May the venerable Sangha hear me! The harmonious bhikṣuṇī Sangha of such-and-such a monastery prostrates at the feet of the harmonious bhikṣu Sangha and requests a person to give instruction.'"

If the Sangha has already appointed an instructor, the senior monk should reply: "Receive instruction from bhikṣu so-and-so." If the Sangha has not appointed anyone, but there is someone who can teach the Dharma, he should reply: "Go to bhikṣu so-and-so and receive instruction." If there is no such person either, he should reply: "There is no appointed instructor here, nor anyone able to teach the Dharma. Do not be heedless!"

The bhikṣuṇīs should come the next day and ask: "Has the request for an instructing bhikṣu been completed? Has it been reported to the Sangha?" This bhikṣu should convey the senior monk's words and tell them.

There were bhikṣuṇīs who performed the pravāraṇā together with bhikṣus. Bhikṣuṇīs wished to go to a forest dwelling to perform the pravāraṇā with the bhikṣus. On the way they encountered bandits, floods, or fire, facing danger to their lives, danger to their chastity, or danger to their robes and bowls. Furthermore, waiting for each other delayed the pravāraṇā. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "Bhikṣuṇīs must not perform the pravāraṇā together with bhikṣus. They should perform the pravāraṇā separately and then go to the bhikṣu Sangha to request regarding offenses seen, heard, or suspected."

At that time, there were no bhikṣus in a village. The bhikṣuṇīs went to a forest dwelling to request regarding offenses seen, heard, or suspected, but either the road was too far and they could not arrive, or the bhikṣus there were not in harmony, and so they could not make the request. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit bhikṣus in forest dwellings to come to the village for the bhikṣuṇīs' pravāraṇā and to assemble in harmony for them. The bhikṣuṇīs should first gather and perform the pravāraṇā, and then appoint bhikṣuṇīs to go to the bhikṣu Sangha and request regarding offenses seen, heard, or suspected."

When they have arrived, they should bare the right shoulder, remove their leather sandals, and prostrate from afar at the Sangha's feet. They should then enter the Sangha, join their palms, and bow, saying: "The harmonious bhikṣuṇī Sangha of such-and-such a monastery prostrates at the feet of the harmonious bhikṣu Sangha. We bhikṣuṇīs, the Sangha in harmony, request the venerable Sangha at the pravāraṇā to speak of offenses seen, heard, or suspected." This request is made three times.

At that time, bhikṣus said to Bhikṣuṇī Prajāpatī: "You have no preceptor; your going forth and ordination are not valid." She then had doubts and reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "When you accepted the eight rules that cannot be transgressed, that itself was your going forth and receiving the full precepts of ordination."

There was a bhikṣuṇī who enticed a bhikṣu, saying: "I am of noble family, accomplished in propriety and etiquette, skilled in womanly arts." Her intention was to serve that bhikṣu. The bhikṣu then gave rise to thoughts of attachment, no longer found joy in the Way, and consequently returned to lay life. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "A bhikṣuṇī must not entice a bhikṣu; one who does so commits a duṣkṛta!"

There were bhikṣuṇīs who came to the bhikṣu residence with exposed chests and sides, or exposed thighs and shins. The bhikṣus who saw them gave rise to thoughts of attachment, no longer found joy in the Way, and consequently returned to lay life. The bhikṣus reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! From now on, bhikṣuṇīs are not permitted to enter the bhikṣu residence!"

Since they could not enter, they received no instruction. Being ignorant and foolish, they were unable to study the precepts. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "If they are proper bhikṣuṇīs, they are permitted to enter and should also be summoned."

When summoned, they would not come. The Buddha said: "If they do not come when summoned, it is a duṣkṛta!"

At that time, bhikṣuṇīs did not speak with bhikṣus, and so no one instructed them. Being ignorant and foolish, they were unable to study the precepts. The bhikṣuṇīs reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a duṣkṛta!"

At that time, Bhikṣuṇī Uccā repeatedly committed offenses. The bhikṣuṇī Sangha imposed on her the karma of not acknowledging her offense. She wept and said: "I am foolish. The Sangha has imposed on me the karma of not acknowledging my offense. I may commit further grave offenses on account of it! May the Sangha lift this karma for me!" The bhikṣuṇīs reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The karma should not be lifted. A motion and one proclamation should be used to appoint a bhikṣuṇī companion for her, to converse with her and share the same dwelling."

One bhikṣuṇī should proclaim: "May the elder sisters of the Sangha hear me! We now appoint bhikṣuṇī so-and-so to be the companion of Bhikṣuṇī Uccā, to converse with her and share the same dwelling. If the time is appropriate and the Sangha consents, may it approve. This is the motion."

"May the elder sisters of the Sangha hear me! We now appoint bhikṣuṇī so-and-so to be the companion of Bhikṣuṇī Uccā, to converse with her and share the same dwelling. Whichever elder sister consents, let her be silent; whoever does not consent, let her speak. The Sangha has appointed bhikṣuṇī so-and-so to be the companion of Bhikṣuṇī Uccā; the Sangha consents, as shown by its silence. Let this matter be thus upheld."

There was a bhikṣuṇī whose menstrual blood flowed and stained her feet and robes. When she entered the village to beg for food, laypeople who saw this mocked and criticized her. The Buddha said: "When a bhikṣuṇī's menstrual blood is flowing, she is not permitted to enter the village to beg for food. She is permitted to store food in advance. She is also permitted to have her disciple beg on her behalf. If she has no disciple, she is permitted to put on a menstrual cloth and beg for food."

There were bhikṣuṇīs of noble family who went forth but did not wear a shoulder-covering garment. Laypeople who saw their shoulders and arms teased and flirted with them, and they were all mortified. The bhikṣuṇīs reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "They are permitted to wear a shoulder-covering garment!"

At that time, when a bhikṣuṇī's disciple completed the two-year training precepts but not to the satisfaction of the preceptor, the preceptor would still give her the full precepts of ordination. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a duṣkṛta! From now on, the preceptor and ācārya must be in agreement before assembling the quorum of ten."

Upon reaching the place of ordination, the candidate for ordination should be placed where she can be seen but cannot hear. The preceptor should find a karma master and an instructor. Once obtained, the karma master should perform the karma to appoint the instructor and send her out to instruct, proclaiming: "May the elder sisters of the Sangha hear me! So-and-so requests so-and-so to receive the full precepts of ordination. So-and-so is appointed as the instructor. If the time is appropriate and the Sangha consents, may it approve. This is the motion."

That instructor should carry out the preliminary procedures. She should first ask the preceptor: "Has this candidate completed the full term of the two-year training precepts? Are her robes and bowl complete?" If she says they are not complete, she should tell her to make them complete. If she says they are complete, she should further ask: "Are they her own, or are they borrowed?" If she says they are borrowed, she should tell the owner to give them to her. Then she should go to the candidate for ordination and say: "Do not be afraid. In a moment you will be placed in a high and excellent position!"

If she is not already known, she should slightly open the robe to examine whether there are any disqualifying conditions for ordination. She should ask: "Which is your saṃghāṭī? Which is your uttarāsaṅga? Which is your antaravāsaka? Which is your shoulder-covering garment? Which is your bathing robe?" If she does not know them, she should tell her so she can identify them. Next, she should confer the robes and bowl, as explained in the section for bhikṣus.

She should further say: "Listen, so-and-so! This is the time of truthful speech. I will now ask you; if it is so, you should say it is so; if it is not, you should say it is not. Women have illnesses such as these: leprosy, white leprosy, wasting disease, madness, abscesses and sores, fistulas, and fatty growths. Do you have any such serious illnesses? Are you free from debt? Are you not another's wife? Has your husband consented? Are you not bound to government service? Are you not a slave? Are you a human being? Are you a woman? Are your female organs intact? Are you not a hermaphrodite? Are you not barren? Do you not have a joined passage? Does your menstrual blood flow? Does it not flow constantly? Have you completed the full term of the two-year training precepts? Have you already requested a preceptor? Have your parents consented? Do you wish to receive the full precepts of ordination? As I have now asked you, you will be asked in the same way before the Sangha, and you should answer them in the same way."

The instructor should return and stand in the Sangha, saying: "I have completed the questioning!"

The karma master should address the Sangha: "May the elder sisters of the Sangha hear me! So-and-so requests so-and-so to receive the full precepts of ordination. So-and-so has completed the questioning. We now permit her to be brought in. If the time is appropriate and the Sangha consents, may it approve. This is the motion."

The instructor should go and bring her in, teaching her to prostrate at the Sangha's feet. After prostrating, she should be brought before the karma master and taught to kneel with palms joined, facing the karma master, and request from the Sangha the full precepts of ordination. She should be taught to say: "I, so-and-so, request so-and-so as preceptor to receive the full precepts of ordination. I now request from the Sangha the full precepts of ordination; my preceptor is so-and-so. May the Sangha rescue and help me, out of compassion!" This request is made three times.

The instructor should then return to her seat. The karma master should address the Sangha: "May the elder sisters of the Sangha hear me! This so-and-so requests so-and-so to receive the full precepts of ordination. She requests from the Sangha the full precepts of ordination; her preceptor is so-and-so. I will now question her about the disqualifying matters before the Sangha. If the time is appropriate and the Sangha consents, may it approve. This is the motion."

She should say to the candidate: "Listen! This is the time of truthful speech. I will now ask you; if it is so, say it is so; if it is not, say it is not." And so forth, down to "Do you wish to receive the precepts?" All questions are as above.

After questioning as above, the karma master should again proclaim: "May the elder sisters of the Sangha hear me! This so-and-so requests so-and-so to receive the full precepts of ordination. She requests from the Sangha the full precepts of ordination. She declares herself free from all disqualifying conditions, has completed the two-year training precepts, and her five robes and bowl are complete. She has already requested a preceptor, and her parents have consented. She wishes to receive the full precepts of ordination. The Sangha now confers upon so-and-so the full precepts of ordination; her preceptor is so-and-so. If the time is appropriate and the Sangha consents, may it approve. This is the motion."

"May the elder sisters of the Sangha hear me! So-and-so requests so-and-so to receive the full precepts of ordination." And so forth, down to "Her preceptor is so-and-so. Whichever elder sister consents, let her be silent; whoever does not consent, let her speak." This is proclaimed a second and a third time.

"The Sangha has conferred upon so-and-so the full precepts of ordination; her preceptor is so-and-so. The Sangha consents, as shown by its silence. Let this matter be thus upheld."

The preceptor and ācārya should further assemble a quorum of ten bhikṣuṇīs and take the candidate to the bhikṣu Sangha. She should kneel on both knees at a slight distance before the bhikṣu karma master and request the full precepts of ordination. The karma master should instruct her to say: "I, so-and-so, request so-and-so as preceptor to receive the full precepts of ordination. I have already received the full precepts of ordination in one assembly. I am pure, free from all disqualifying conditions. I have completed the two-year training precepts. My robes and bowl are complete. I have already requested a preceptor. My parents have consented. I have not committed any grave offenses. I wish to receive the full precepts of ordination. I now request from the Sangha the full precepts of ordination; my preceptor is so-and-so. May the Sangha rescue and help me, out of compassion!" This request is made three times.

After the three requests, the karma master should announce: "May the venerable Sangha hear me! This so-and-so requests so-and-so to receive the full precepts of ordination. She has already received the full precepts of ordination in one assembly. She is pure, free from all disqualifying conditions. She has completed the two-year training precepts. What was previously required has been completed. Her robes and bowl are complete. She has already requested a preceptor. Her parents have consented. She has not committed any grave offenses. She wishes to receive the full precepts of ordination. She now requests from the Sangha the full precepts of ordination; her preceptor is so-and-so. The Sangha now confers upon so-and-so the full precepts of ordination; her preceptor is so-and-so. If the time is appropriate and the Sangha consents, may it approve. This is the motion."

"May the venerable Sangha hear me! This so-and-so requests so-and-so to receive the full precepts of ordination." And so forth, down to "The Sangha now confers upon so-and-so the full precepts of ordination; her preceptor is so-and-so. Whichever elder consents, let him be silent; whoever does not consent, let him speak." This is proclaimed a second and a third time.

"The Sangha has conferred upon so-and-so the full precepts of ordination; her preceptor is so-and-so. The Sangha consents, as shown by its silence. Let this matter be thus upheld."

She should further be told: "Listen, so-and-so! The Tathāgata, the Worthy of Offerings, the Perfectly Enlightened One has taught eight pārājika offenses. If a bhikṣuṇī commits any one of these, she is no longer a bhikṣuṇī, no longer a daughter of the Śākya clan. One must not engage in any sexual conduct, even to the point of looking at another man with lustful intent. If a bhikṣuṇī engages in sexual conduct, even with an animal, she is no longer a bhikṣuṇī, no longer a daughter of the Śākya clan. For your entire life you must not commit this offense!" If she can uphold it, she should say: "I can!"

"One must not steal anything at all, even a blade of grass. If a bhikṣuṇī, whether in a village or in an uninhabited place, steals property guarded by another, worth five coins or more, she is no longer a bhikṣuṇī, no longer a daughter of the Śākya clan. For your entire life you must not commit this offense!" If she can uphold it, she should say: "I can!"

"One must not kill any living being, even an ant. If a bhikṣuṇī, whether it is a human being or a human-like being, cuts short its life with her own hand, provides a weapon, instructs another to kill, instructs to die, or praises death, she is no longer a bhikṣuṇī, no longer a daughter of the Śākya clan. For your entire life you must not commit this offense!" If she can uphold it, she should say: "I can!"

"One must not speak any false words, even in jest. If a bhikṣuṇī, not herself having attained superhuman states, claims to have attained the various dhyānas, liberations, samādhis, samāpattis, or the fruits of the path, she is no longer a bhikṣuṇī, no longer a daughter of the Śākya clan. For your entire life you must not commit this offense!" If she can uphold it, she should say: "I can!"

"One must not be intimate with any man. If a bhikṣuṇī, with passion aroused and mind disturbed, touches a man's body from below the hair to above the knees; and if a man performs such touching, she must not accept it, whether pressing, pinching, lifting, lowering, grasping, or pulling, she is no longer a bhikṣuṇī, no longer a daughter of the Śākya clan. For your entire life you must not commit this offense!" If she can uphold it, she should say: "I can!"

"One must not dwell together with, or converse alone with, any man. If a bhikṣuṇī, with passion aroused and mind disturbed, receives from a man any of these: holding hands, holding her garments, arranging to travel together, traveling alone together, dwelling alone together, conversing alone together, sitting alone together, or drawing physically close, and all eight of these conditions are met, she is no longer a bhikṣuṇī, no longer a daughter of the Śākya clan. For your entire life you must not commit this offense!" If she can uphold it, she should say: "I can!"

"One must not follow the words of a bhikṣu who acts contrary to the Dharma. If a bhikṣuṇī knows that the harmonious bhikṣu Sangha has lawfully charged a bhikṣu, yet follows this bhikṣu, and other bhikṣuṇīs say to her: 'Sister! This bhikṣu has been lawfully charged by the harmonious bhikṣu Sangha. You should not follow him!' and she is thus admonished but firmly persists and does not give up, she should be admonished a second and a third time. If at the second and third admonition she gives it up, that is good. If she does not give it up, she is no longer a bhikṣuṇī, no longer a daughter of the Śākya clan. For your entire life you must not commit this offense!" If she can uphold it, she should say: "I can!"

"One must not conceal the grave offenses of others. If a bhikṣuṇī knows that another bhikṣuṇī has committed a pārājika offense, and that bhikṣuṇī later either leaves the order, or dies, or goes far away, or is expelled, or her sex changes, and she then tells the bhikṣuṇīs, saying: 'I previously knew that this bhikṣuṇī committed a pārājika offense,' but she did not report it to the Sangha or tell anyone, she is no longer a bhikṣuṇī, no longer a daughter of the Śākya clan. For your entire life you must not commit this offense!" If she can uphold it, she should say: "I can!"

"The Buddhas, the World-Honored Ones, are skillful at using similes to illustrate present matters. Just as when the eye of a needle is broken, it is no longer fit for use as a needle. Just as when a person has died, they can never be reborn with this body. Just as when the heart of a tāla palm is cut, it neither grows nor flourishes. Just as when a stone is broken, it cannot be made whole again. If a bhikṣuṇī commits any one of these eight offenses, to be restored as a bhikṣuṇī is an impossibility."

She should further be told: "Listen, so-and-so! The Tathāgata, the Worthy of Offerings, the Perfectly Enlightened One has taught these eight rules that cannot be transgressed. For your entire life you must not transgress them. A bhikṣuṇī should every half-month request from the assembly of bhikṣus a person to give instruction. A bhikṣuṇī should not spend the summer retreat in a place where there are no bhikṣus. At the time of pravāraṇā, a bhikṣuṇī should request from the assembly of bhikṣus the three matters of offenses seen, heard, or suspected. A śikṣamāṇā, after completing two years of training in the precepts, should receive the full precepts of ordination in the assembly of both orders. A bhikṣuṇī must not revile a bhikṣu, and must not speak of a bhikṣu's violations of deportment, violations of precepts, or violations of right view before laypeople. A bhikṣuṇī must not charge a bhikṣu with an offense, but a bhikṣu may reprove a bhikṣuṇī. When a bhikṣuṇī commits a grave offense, she should perform mānatva for a half-month in the assembly of both orders; after performing mānatva for a half-month, she should seek rehabilitation in an assembly of twenty members of each order. Even if a bhikṣuṇī was ordained a hundred years prior, she should still prostrate to, rise for, and greet a newly ordained bhikṣu."

She should further be told: "Listen, so-and-so! The Tathāgata, the Worthy of Offerings, the Perfectly Enlightened One has taught these four reliances. For your entire life, rely on these to go forth and receive the full precepts of ordination. Relying on rag-robes, go forth and receive the full precepts of ordination." If she can, she should say: "I can!"

"If she receives extra robes: cotton robes, woolen robes, kauśeya robes, kṣauma robes, kṣaumī robes, vāsana robes, āhana robes, kuṭaka robes, or hemp robes, she may accept them."

"Relying on the practice of begging for food, go forth and receive the full precepts of ordination." If she can, she should say: "I can!"

"If she receives extra food: Sangha food, morning food, afternoon food, or invitation food, she may accept it."

"Relying on coarse and simple bedding, go forth and receive the full precepts of ordination." If she can, she should say: "I can!"

"If she additionally receives a hut, a multi-storied dwelling, a large or small room, or a square or round building, she may accept it."

"Relying on inferior medicine, go forth and receive the full precepts of ordination." If she can, she should say: "I can!"

"If she additionally receives ghee, oil, honey, or sugar, she may accept it."

She should further be told: "Listen, so-and-so! You have now received the full precepts of ordination through a motion and three proclamations. The karma procedure was lawful. Devas, nāgas, ghosts, spirits, and gandharvas constantly make this vow: 'When will we obtain a human body and go forth to receive the full precepts of ordination?' You have now obtained this. Just as a person who receives a royal throne, so too is your receiving of the bhikṣuṇī Dharma. You should be patient and easy to speak with, easy to instruct! You should study the three trainings, extinguish the three poisons, transcend the three realms, and accomplish the fruit of arhatship. Whatever else you do not know, your preceptor and ācārya will explain it to you."

At that time, there was a courtesan named Ardhakaśī who wished to go forth in the True Dharma and Vinaya and go to a forest dwelling to receive the full precepts of ordination. Bandits heard of this and planned to waylay her on the road. That woman also heard of this and did not dare to go. The bhikṣuṇīs reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit the full precepts of ordination to be conferred at a distance through a motion and three proclamations."

The preceptor and ācārya should first assemble a quorum of ten bhikṣuṇīs and confer the precepts upon her. Then, leaving the candidate for ordination at one place, they should take the ten bhikṣuṇīs to the forest dwelling and prostrate at the feet of the bhikṣu Sangha.

The karma master should request the precepts from the Sangha on her behalf, saying: "May the venerable Sangha hear me! So-and-so requests so-and-so to receive the full precepts of ordination. She has already received the full precepts of ordination in one assembly. She is pure, free from all disqualifying conditions. She has completed the two-year training precepts. What was previously required has been completed. Her robes and bowl are complete. She has already requested a preceptor. Her parents have consented. She has not committed any grave offenses. She wishes to receive the full precepts of ordination. She now requests from the Sangha the full precepts of ordination; her preceptor is so-and-so. May the Sangha rescue and help her, out of compassion!" This request is made three times.

The bhikṣu karma master should take her words of request and perform the motion and three proclamations as above. After the karma procedure is completed, the preceptor and ācārya should take the ten bhikṣuṇīs and return to the original place of ordination, summon the newly ordained person, have her prostrate at the Sangha's feet, and kneel with palms joined before the karma master. The karma master should inform her of the Sangha's motion and three proclamations, and after she has heard them, should then explain in full as above the eight pārājika offenses, the four similes, the eight rules that cannot be transgressed, and the four reliances, down to "Whatever else you do not know, your preceptor and ācārya will explain it to you."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who wore brightly colored garments, considering them decorative. Laypeople mocked and criticized them, saying: "These bhikṣuṇīs are just like courtesans, seeking to attract men!" The bhikṣuṇīs reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a duṣkṛta!"

There were bhikṣuṇīs who painted their eyes. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a duṣkṛta!"

There were bhikṣuṇīs who had eye ailments and needed to apply eye paint. The Buddha said: "If ill, they are permitted to apply it."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who walked in front of bhikṣus. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a duṣkṛta!"

There were bhikṣuṇīs who, seeing a bhikṣu approaching from afar, stopped and did not dare to go forward, thus hindering their begging for food. The Buddha said: "If the distance is far, they are permitted to walk in front."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who, in front of bhikṣus, sniffed noisily or spat on the ground. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a duṣkṛta!"

There were bhikṣuṇīs who sat cross-legged, and their menstrual blood stained their heels. People who saw this mocked and criticized them. There was also a bhikṣuṇī who sat cross-legged, and a dung beetle entered her vagina, causing her to become ill. This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "All bhikṣuṇīs should sit with their legs folded to one side. If they sit cross-legged, they should extend one leg alternately. Those who violate this commit a duṣkṛta!"

A householder wished to exchange a dwelling with the bhikṣuṇīs, but the bhikṣuṇīs did not dare. The Buddha said: "They are permitted to make the exchange."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who stretched out cloth and stitched it in the manner of thorn-boots. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! If the cloth is curled or shrunken, they are permitted to attach ties."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who urinated and defecated everywhere in the dwelling, making it foul-smelling. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a duṣkṛta! A latrine should be built."

Bhikṣuṇīs dug the latrine pit too deep, and miscarried fetuses fell into it. Nightsoil removers saw this and mocked and criticized them, saying: "These women always praise detachment from desire, from lustful thoughts, from the heat of desire; yet they do such things and, fearing people will find out, drop fetuses into the latrine. Why not leave the religious life and enjoy the five sensual pleasures?" The bhikṣuṇīs reported this to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "The latrine pit should not be dug deep. At most, it may be one cubit deep with the fist clenched, and the opening should be made small."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who placed fetuses in their bowls or bags and discarded them in the morning. At that time, King Prasenajit had a border incident and dispatched troops to deal with it. Among them was one who had faith in the Buddha's Dharma, and he thought: "I should first offer food to renunciants, and then go forth to battle." He sent a messenger to look for them and happened upon that bhikṣuṇī, and invited her back to offer food. The bhikṣuṇī said: "You go on ahead; I will follow later." But he pressed her insistently and forcibly brought her along. He took out a bowl and placed food in it, but saw a baby's fetus. He then variously mocked and criticized her, saying: "These women always speak of compassion and mindful protection for all sentient beings, yet they themselves kill their own children. They have no conduct of śramaṇas and violate the śramaṇa Dharma!" The bhikṣuṇīs reported this to the bhikṣus, and the bhikṣus reported it to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "When a bhikṣuṇī goes to beg for food, if she sees a bhikṣu, she should show her bowl."

The bhikṣuṇīs then all brought out their bowls and tipped them to one side to show them, and this hindered their begging for food. The Buddha said: "Just roughly show it, enough for them to know it is empty."

There was a bhikṣuṇī who gave birth to a boy. Not knowing what to do, this was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "I permit a motion and one proclamation to appoint a bhikṣuṇī companion for her."

One bhikṣuṇī should proclaim in the Sangha: "May the elder sisters of the Sangha hear me! This bhikṣuṇī so-and-so has given birth to a boy. We now appoint bhikṣuṇī so-and-so as her companion. If the time is appropriate and the Sangha consents, may it approve. This is the motion."

"May the elder sisters of the Sangha hear me! This bhikṣuṇī so-and-so has given birth to a boy. We now appoint bhikṣuṇī so-and-so as her companion. Whichever elder sister consents, let her be silent; whoever does not consent, let her speak. The Sangha has appointed bhikṣuṇī so-and-so as the companion of bhikṣuṇī so-and-so; the Sangha consents, as shown by its silence. Let this matter be thus upheld."

The two bhikṣuṇīs held the child and had doubts. The Buddha said: "There is no offense!"

The two bhikṣuṇīs slept together with the child and had doubts. The Buddha said: "There is also no offense!"

They adorned the child and played with it, cooing. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! They are permitted to bathe it and nurse it. Once it is weaned, it should be given to a bhikṣu to have him go forth. If they do not wish him to go forth, he should be given to relatives, who will raise him to maturity."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who, when invited by the bhikṣu Sangha, did not go in order. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! They should be appointed to go in order."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who entered the village without tying their lower garment, which fell to the ground, exposing their bodies. The Buddha said: "They should tie it with a waist cord."

They made the waist cord too long. The Buddha said: "It is permitted to be just enough to go around the waist once."

They made the waist cord too wide. The Buddha said: "At widest, it is permitted to be one finger's breadth."

They made waist cords of mixed colors. The Buddha said: "This should not be done! A single plain color is permitted."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who wore light garments when entering the village, and the wind blew them, exposing their bodies. The Buddha said: "Hooks, buttons, and ties are permitted to be attached at the top and bottom to secure them."

There were women of noble families who had gone forth but found it difficult to hold the bowl while begging for food, as their hands grew weary. The Buddha said: "They are permitted to make a net bag to hold the bowl while begging for food."

When hung under the arm, sweat stained it and dust got in. The Buddha said: "They are permitted to make a bowl-cover cloth."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who practiced poisonous arts, intending to kill living beings. The Buddha said: "If one practices poisonous arts with the intention of killing living beings, it is a sthūlātyaya. If one uses spells to raise the dead with the intention of killing living beings, it is likewise."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who kept various multi-colored garments. Laypeople mocked and criticized them, saying: "These bhikṣuṇīs are just like courtesans!" The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a duṣkṛta!"

Bhikṣus had leftover food, and bhikṣuṇīs had no food, but they did not dare to give it. The Buddha said: "They are permitted to give it; there is no offense. When bhikṣuṇīs have leftover food and give it to bhikṣus, it is likewise."

There were bhikṣus who went to the bhikṣuṇī residence, but there was no attendant to hand them food. The Buddha said: "When there is no attendant, bhikṣuṇīs are permitted to hand food to bhikṣus; there is no offense. When bhikṣus hand food to bhikṣuṇīs, it is likewise."

There were bhikṣuṇīs who brewed and sold alcohol. Many people mocked and criticized them. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a sthūlātyaya!"

There were bhikṣuṇīs who kept fields, plows, oxen, and servants, and personally oversaw the plowing and planting. Laypeople mocked and criticized them: "These bhikṣuṇīs also personally oversee plowing the fields. How are they different from us?" The Buddha said: "They should not personally oversee it; they should have attendants manage it. Those who violate this commit a duṣkṛta!"

There were bhikṣuṇīs who lent money at interest. Many people mocked and criticized them. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a sthūlātyaya!"

There were bhikṣuṇīs who kept courtesans, setting up stalls and renting them out. Many people mocked and criticized them. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a sthūlātyaya!"

There were bhikṣuṇīs who pressed oil and sold it. Many people mocked and criticized them. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a sthūlātyaya!"

There were bhikṣuṇīs who played a stomping game. Many people mocked and criticized them. The Buddha said: "This should not be done; those who do so commit a duṣkṛta! Hanging from ropes and swinging for play is likewise."

There was a fire at the bhikṣuṇī residence. The Buddha said: "They should strike the gong. Or they should call out to assemble, and all should help put out the fire, with earth thrown on it, water poured on it, and cloth soaked in water used to beat it out."

At that time, many householders invited the bhikṣuṇī Sangha for a meal. The bhikṣuṇīs, in the morning, put on their robes and took their bowls, and upon arriving at the host's house began questioning each other about seniority. The day wore on, and the householders mocked and criticized them: "These bhikṣuṇīs are just like brāhmaṇa women! They question each other about how many scriptures they know, and whoever knows more is senior. We have prepared this offering, but the day has already passed. What shall we do?" This was reported to the Buddha. The Buddha said: "When there is a large assembly gathering, the eight senior nuns are permitted to inquire about seniority among themselves and be seated in order. The rest may take whatever seat is available and sit."

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese during the Liu Song dynasty by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Thirty

*Note: The source text provided for this fascicle is empty. There is no Chinese text to translate.*

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Buddhajīva and Zhu Daosheng.

## Fascicle Thirty

### Part Five, Section Nine: The Council of Five Hundred for Collecting the Dharma

At that time, not long after the World-Honored One's nirvāṇa, Mahākāśyapa was at the Kuṭāgāraśālā (Gabled Hall) beside the Monkey Pool in Vaiśālī, together with a great assembly of five hundred bhikṣus, all of whom were arhats, with the sole exception of Ānanda. He addressed the bhikṣus: "Formerly, when I was traveling from the country of Pāvā toward the city of Kuśinagara, between the two countries I heard that the Buddha, the World-Honored One, had already entered parinirvāṇa. At that time, my mind was confused and distraught, and I could not compose myself. The bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, and upāsikās of the villages were either falling down or leaping about, rolling on the ground, and there was none who did not cry out in grief, exclaiming how sudden, how swift it was: 'The world is empty! The Eye of the World is extinguished!' At that time, Upananda had previously been traveling in that area, and he stopped the people, saying: 'That elder always used to say: "You should practice this; you should not practice that. You should learn this; you should not learn that." Now we are finally freed from this suffering! We may act as we please, without any more hindrance. Why should we all weep and cry together?' When I heard his words, my grief and distress doubled. Although the Buddha has entered nirvāṇa, the Vinaya still exists. We should jointly encourage each other and collect it together. Do not let Upananda and others establish separate factions and thereby destroy the True Dharma." All the bhikṣus agreed that this was good, and said to Kāśyapa: "Ānanda always attended the World-Honored One, is wise and learned of much learning, and fully retains the treasury of the Dharma. He should now be permitted to be included in the assembly for collecting the Vinaya." Kāśyapa said: "Ānanda is still on the path of training. He may follow attachment, anger, delusion, or fear. He should not be admitted."

At that time, Ānanda was in Vaiśālī, constantly teaching the Dharma to the fourfold assembly day and night. People came and went almost as if the Buddha were still present. There was a Vṛji bhikṣu who was sitting in meditation on the upper story of that hall, and because of this commotion he was unable to roam through the various liberations and samādhis. He thought: "Ānanda is now on the path of training. Does he still have work to do, or does he not? Yet he is constantly in the midst of noise and bustle, speaking much." He then entered concentration and observed, and saw that Ānanda still had work to do. He further thought: "I should now teach him the Dharma of disenchantment, so that he may thereby awaken." He then went to Ānanda's place and spoke a verse:

> "Sitting quietly beneath a tree in a secluded place,
> With mind directed toward nirvāṇa;
> Meditate and do not be heedless,
> What is the use of much speaking?"

The bhikṣus also told Ānanda: "You should quickly accomplish your task! Mahākāśyapa now wishes to collect the Vinaya, yet he does not permit you to be included in this assembly." Ānanda, having heard the verse spoken by the Vṛji bhikṣu, and also having heard that Kāśyapa did not permit him to be included in the assembly for collecting the Vinaya, diligently practiced walking meditation and contemplation through the first, middle, and last watches of the night, hoping to attain liberation, but was unable to attain it. As the last watch was about to end, his body was extremely weary, and he wished to lie down briefly. Before his head reached the pillow, his defilements were suddenly and completely exhausted. The bhikṣus knew this and reported it to Kāśyapa: "Ānanda attained liberation last night. He should now be permitted to be included in the assembly for collecting the Vinaya!" Kāśyapa then permitted it. Thereupon Kāśyapa thought: "Where is there an abundance of food and drink, beds, seats, and bedding that can be used to supply the assembly for collecting the Vinaya?" He saw that only Rājagṛha was sufficient to supply them. He then proclaimed in the Sangha: "The five hundred arhats in this assembly should go to Rājagṛha for the rains retreat. No one else may go!"

Having made this ruling, the five hundred arhats went to Rājagṛha. During the first month of the summer, they repaired the dwellings and bedding. During the second month, they enjoyed roaming through the various dhyānas and liberations. In the third month, they then gathered together in one place. Thereupon Kāśyapa addressed the Sangha: "May the venerable Sangha listen! I now wish, in the midst of the Sangha, to question Upāli on the meaning of the Vinaya. If the time is right, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

At that time, Upāli also addressed the Sangha: "May the venerable Sangha listen! I now shall answer Kāśyapa on the meaning of the Vinaya. If the time is right, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

Kāśyapa then asked Upāli: "In what place did the Buddha lay down the first precept?" Upāli answered: "In Vaiśālī." He further asked: "On account of whom was it laid down?" He answered: "On account of Sudinna Kalandakaputta." He further asked: "On account of what matter was it laid down?" He answered: "Having sexual intercourse with his former wife." He further asked: "Was there a second enactment?" He answered: "There was! There was a bhikṣu who had sexual intercourse with a monkey."

Kāśyapa further asked: "In what place was the second precept laid down?" He answered: "In Rājagṛha." He further asked: "On account of whom was it laid down?" He answered: "On account of Dhaniya." He further asked: "On account of what matter was it laid down?" He answered: "Stealing King Bimbisāra's timber."

Kāśyapa further asked: "In what place was the third precept laid down?" He answered: "In Vaiśālī." He further asked: "On account of whom was it laid down?" He answered: "On account of a number of bhikṣus." He further asked: "On account of what matter was it laid down?" He answered: "Killing each other."

Kāśyapa further asked: "In what place was the fourth precept laid down?" He answered: "In Vaiśālī." He further asked: "On account of whom was it laid down?" He answered: "On account of the bhikṣus by the Vagumukhā River." He further asked: "On account of what matter was it laid down?" He answered: "Falsely claiming to have attained superhuman states."

After Kāśyapa had asked such questions about the entire Vinaya, he proclaimed in the Sangha: "This is the bhikṣu Vinaya; this is the bhikṣuṇī Vinaya. Together they are called the Vinaya Piṭaka."

Kāśyapa further addressed the Sangha: "May the venerable Sangha listen! I now wish, in the midst of the Sangha, to question Ānanda on the meaning of the sūtras. If the time is right, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

Ānanda also addressed the Sangha: "May the venerable Sangha listen! I now shall answer Kāśyapa on the meaning of the sūtras. If the time is right, may the Sangha consent. The motion is thus."

Kāśyapa then asked Ānanda: "In what place did the Buddha speak the Ekottara Sūtra? In what place did he speak the Daśottara Sūtra, the Mahānidāna Sūtra, the Saṅgīti Sūtra, the Sāmaññaphala Sūtra, and the Brahmajāla Sūtra? Which sūtras were spoken on account of bhikṣus? Which sūtras were spoken on account of bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, upāsikās, devaputras, or devakanyās?" Ānanda answered each in accordance with what the Buddha had spoken.

After Kāśyapa had asked about the entire collection of sūtras in this manner, he proclaimed in the Sangha: "These are the long discourses, now collected into one division, called the Dīrgha Āgama. These are neither long nor short, now collected into one division, called the Madhyama Āgama. These are miscellaneous discourses spoken to bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, upāsikās, devaputras, and devakanyās, now collected into one division, called the Saṃyukta Āgama. These are discourses arranged from one dharma increasing up to eleven dharmas, now collected into one division, called the Ekottara Āgama. The remaining miscellaneous discourses are now collected into one division, called the Kṣudraka Piṭaka. Together they are called the Sūtra Piṭaka. We have now finished collecting the Dharma. From now on, what the Buddha did not lay down should not be recklessly laid down; what has already been laid down must not be violated. As the Buddha taught, one should diligently study it."

Ānanda further said to Kāśyapa: "I personally heard from the Buddha: 'After my parinirvāṇa, if you wish to abolish the minor and lesser precepts, you are permitted to abolish them.'" Kāśyapa then asked: "Which ones do you consider to be the minor and lesser precepts?" He answered: "I do not know!" He further asked: "Why do you not know?" He answered: "I did not ask the World-Honored One." He further asked: "Why did you not ask?" He answered: "At the time the Buddha was in physical pain, and I feared it would cause him distress." Kāśyapa reproached him: "By not asking about this matter, you committed a duṣkṛta. You should recognize this offense and repent!" Ānanda said: "Venerable one! It was not out of disrespect for the precepts that I did not ask about this matter. I feared causing distress to the World-Honored One, and therefore did not dare. I do not see any offense in this matter, but out of reverent faith in the venerable one, I shall now repent!"

Kāśyapa further reproached Ānanda: "When you were sewing the saṅghāṭī for the World-Honored One, you pressed it with your foot. You committed a duṣkṛta. You should also recognize this offense and repent!" Ānanda said: "It was not out of disrespect for the Buddha. There was no one to hold the cloth taut, and therefore I pressed it with my foot. I also do not see any offense in this matter, but out of reverent faith in the venerable one, I shall now repent!"

Kāśyapa further reproached Ānanda: "You made three requests to the World-Honored One, seeking permission for women to go forth into the True Dharma. You committed a duṣkṛta. You should also recognize this offense and repent!" Ānanda said: "It was not out of disrespect for the Dharma. But Mahāprajāpatī Gautamī nurtured the World-Honored One, raising him until he went forth and attained the Great Path. This merit should be repaid, and therefore I made the three requests. I also do not see any offense in this matter, but out of reverent faith in the venerable one, I shall now repent!"

Kāśyapa further reproached Ānanda: "When the Buddha was about to enter nirvāṇa, he displayed a sign and said to you: 'If one who has attained the four bases of supernatural power wishes to remain for a lifespan of one kalpa, or beyond one kalpa, one can do so.' The Tathāgata had accomplished immeasurable states of concentration. Three times he displayed this sign and spoke to you, yet you did not request the Buddha to remain in the world for one kalpa or beyond one kalpa. You committed a duṣkṛta. You should also recognize this offense and repent!" Ānanda said: "It was not that I did not wish to request the Buddha to remain long. The evil Māra Pāpīyas clouded my mind, and therefore this happened! I also do not see any offense in this matter, but out of reverent faith in the venerable one, I shall now repent!"

Kāśyapa further reproached Ānanda: "Formerly, the Buddha asked you three times for water, and you never offered it. You committed a duṣkṛta. You should also recognize this offense and repent!" Ānanda said: "It was not that I did not wish to offer it. At the time, five hundred carts were fording upstream, and the water was turbid and not yet clear. I feared it would cause illness, and therefore I did not offer it. I also do not see any offense in this matter, but out of reverent faith in the venerable one, I shall now repent!"

Kāśyapa further reproached Ānanda: "You allowed women to pay homage to the relics first. You committed a duṣkṛta. You should also recognize this offense and repent!" Ānanda said: "It was not that I intended for women to pay homage to the relics first. I feared that if it grew late, they would not be able to enter the city, and therefore I permitted it. I also do not see any offense in this matter, but out of reverent faith in the venerable one, I shall now repent!"

Out of reverent faith in Mahākāśyapa, Ānanda then, in the midst of the assembled Sangha, performed repentance for six duṣkṛta offenses.

Kāśyapa further questioned Ānanda: "If we take the śaikṣa rules as the minor and lesser precepts, other bhikṣus will then say that the four prātideśanīya rules are also minor and lesser precepts. If we take up through the four prātideśanīya rules as the minor and lesser precepts, other bhikṣus will then say that the pāyattika rules are also minor and lesser precepts. If we take up through the pāyattika rules as the minor and lesser precepts, other bhikṣus will then say that the niḥsargika-pāyattika rules are also minor and lesser precepts. This would quickly result in four different interpretations; how could any definitive boundary be established?" Kāśyapa further said: "If we do not know the characteristics of the minor and lesser precepts and recklessly abolish them, the non-Buddhist ascetics will say: 'The dharma of the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, is like smoke. When their teacher was alive, they practiced everything he laid down; after his parinirvāṇa, they refused to study it further.'"

Kāśyapa further proclaimed in the Sangha: "We have now finished collecting the Dharma. What the Buddha did not lay down should not be recklessly laid down; what has already been laid down must not be violated. As the Buddha taught, one should diligently study it."

At that time, the Elder Purāṇa was in the south. He heard that the Buddha had entered parinirvāṇa in the city of Kuśinagara, and that the elder bhikṣus had gathered together in Rājagṛha to discuss the Vinaya. He came with his retinue, as quickly as the bending and stretching of an arm, arriving in the midst of the assembly. He said to Kāśyapa: "I heard that the Buddha entered nirvāṇa, and that the senior bhikṣus all gathered here to discuss the Vinaya. Is this truly so?" Kāśyapa answered: "Venerable one! It is truly so." Purāṇa said: "Please discuss it again!" Kāśyapa then discussed it again as above. When the discussion was finished, Purāṇa said to Kāśyapa: "I personally heard from the Buddha that the following are pure: storing overnight food cooked indoors; cooking indoors; cooking oneself; carrying food oneself; receiving food from others; picking and eating fruit oneself; accepting water from a pool; and eating fruit cleaned without a净人 by removing the seeds." Kāśyapa answered: "Venerable one! These seven items were permitted by the Buddha when he was in Vaiśālī during a time of famine when alms were difficult to obtain. They were temporarily permitted. Afterwards, while still in that place, he re-established four of them, and upon reaching Śrāvastī, he re-established the remaining three." Purāṇa said: "The World-Honored One should not lay down rules and then permit them again, or permit them and then lay down rules again." Kāśyapa answered: "The Buddha is the Lord of the Dharma, sovereign over the Dharma. To lay down rules and then permit them again, or to permit them and then lay down rules again, what fault is there in this?" Purāṇa said: "I accept the other matters, but regarding these seven items, I cannot practice accordingly!"
