Kāśyapa again proclaimed in the Sangha: "What the Buddha did not lay down should not be recklessly laid down; what has already been laid down must not be violated. As the Buddha taught, one should diligently study it."

At that time, the bhikṣu Channa in Kauśāmbī was offending and annoying the Sangha and was not in harmony with them. A certain bhikṣu, having completed the rains retreat, went to Kāśyapa's place and fully reported the matter. Kāśyapa said to Ānanda: "Go to Kauśāmbī and, with the words of the Buddha and the words of the Sangha, impose the brahmadaṇḍa penalty on him." Ānanda accepted the commission and went together with five hundred bhikṣus. Channa heard that Ānanda was coming with five hundred bhikṣus, and he went out to meet him. He asked Ānanda: "Why have you come here? Surely you do not intend to do something that is not for my benefit?" He answered: "Rather, it is to benefit you!" Channa said: "How will you benefit me?" He answered: "We shall now, with the words of the Buddha and the words of the Sangha, impose the brahmadaṇḍa penalty on you!" Channa then asked: "What is the brahmadaṇḍa?" He answered: "The brahmadaṇḍa means that all bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, and upāsikās are not permitted to come and go with you or exchange words with you." When Channa heard this, he fainted and fell to the ground, and said to Ānanda: "Is this not the same as killing me?" Ānanda said: "I personally heard from the Buddha: 'You shall attain the path through me.' Rise, and I will teach you the Dharma." Channa then rose and listened. Ānanda taught him various wonderful Dharma, instructing, teaching, benefiting, and gladdening him. Channa immediately became free from dust and defilement, and in all dharmas attained the purity of the Dharma Eye.

At the time of the collection of the Vinaya, the Elder Ājñāta Kauṇḍinya was the first senior seat; Purāṇa was the second senior seat; Dhammiya was the third senior seat; Dāsaka Kāśyapa was the fourth senior seat; Bhadra Kāśyapa was the fifth senior seat; Mahākāśyapa was the sixth senior seat; Upāli was the seventh senior seat; Aniruddha was the eighth senior seat. There were in all five hundred arhats, neither more nor less. Therefore it is called the Council of Five Hundred for Collecting the Dharma.

### Part Five, Section Ten: The Council of Seven Hundred for Collecting the Dharma

One hundred years after the Buddha's nirvāṇa, the Vṛji bhikṣus of Vaiśālī first started ten unlawful practices: first, storing salt mixed with ginger overnight is pure; second, eating food picked up with two fingers is pure; third, eating again after sitting down is pure; fourth, eating after going beyond the village boundary is pure; fifth, mixing ghee, oil, honey, or rock sugar with yogurt is pure; sixth, drinking jālogia liquor is pure; seventh, making a sitting mat of any size one wishes is pure; eighth, practicing what one formerly practiced is pure; ninth, seeking approval afterwards is pure; tenth, accepting and keeping gold, silver, and coins is pure. Those bhikṣus, on the eighth, fourteenth, and fifteenth days of each month, would fill a bowl with water, sit in a place with a large crowd of people, place the bowl before them as an auspicious sign, and solicit donations from people. When lay men and women, young and old, passed before them, they would point to the bowl of water and say: "This is auspicious! You may donate the price of robes, bowls, leather sandals, or medicine." Those who wished to give would give; those who did not wish to give would criticize and scold them, saying: "Śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, should not accept and keep gold, silver, or coins! Even if someone offers them, they should not look at them. Yet how can you now solicit donations in this way?"

At that time, the Elder Yaśas Kālandakaputta was at the Kuṭāgāraśālā beside the Monkey Pool in that place, and he said to the bhikṣus: "You must not solicit donations in this way! I personally heard from the Buddha that if someone makes an unlawful solicitation, and another gives to an unlawful solicitation, both incur offense!" Having spoken to the bhikṣus, he further told the lay men and women, young and old: "You must not make such donations! I personally heard from the Buddha that if someone makes an unlawful solicitation, and another gives to an unlawful solicitation, both incur offense!"

After those bhikṣus had obtained the gold, silver, and coins, they said to Yaśas: "Venerable one! Please accept this share." He answered: "I will not accept a share obtained through unlawful solicitation!" They further said: "If you will not accept it yourself, then donate it to the Sangha." He answered: "Since I myself do not accept it, how can I donate it to the Sangha?" Thereupon the bhikṣus, because Yaśas had earlier instructed the laity, charged him with reviling the laity, and imposed on him a patisāraṇīya-kamma (act of reconciliation). After the act, Yaśas said: "I personally heard from the Buddha that if the Sangha imposes a patisāraṇīya-kamma, they should appoint one bhikṣu as a companion to go and apologize to the laity." The bhikṣus then, by a jñaptidvitīya-karman (motion followed by one proclamation), appointed one bhikṣu to accompany him. Yaśas then took him to the laity. It happened that five hundred upāsakas were gathered together in one place. He said to them: "May the gentlemen know: what is Dharma, I declared to be Dharma; what is not Dharma, I declared to be not Dharma. What is Vinaya, I declared to be Vinaya; what is not Vinaya, I declared to be not Vinaya. What is the Buddha's teaching, I declared to be the Buddha's teaching; what is not the Buddha's teaching, I declared to be not the Buddha's teaching. What I previously said caused the upāsakas to be angry, and I have now come to apologize!" All the upāsakas were greatly astonished and said: "Venerable one! When did you ever tell us: 'This is Dharma, this is Vinaya, this is the Buddha's teaching,' and cause us to be angry, that you now come to apologize?" Yaśas further told the people: "Once, the World-Honored One was in Rājagṛha at the Jīvaka Āmrapālī Garden. At that time, the great ministers of King Bimbisāra gathered at the king's gate and discussed as follows: 'The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, should be allowed to accept and keep gold, silver, jewels, and to use them for trade.' At that time, in that assembly there was a great minister named Maṇicūḷaka, who said to the people: 'Do not make such a proposal. The śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, should not accept and keep gold, silver, jewels, or use them for trade.' He then went and reported this matter to the World-Honored One: 'Was what I said perhaps erroneous?' The Buddha said: 'What you said was exactly right! Why? Because I always say this: the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, should not accept and keep gold, silver, jewels, or use them for trade.' He further said to the Buddha: 'May the World-Honored One please send word to inform the people, so they know it is not erroneous!' The Buddha said: 'Excellent!' He further told Maṇicūḷaka: 'Just as the sun and moon are obscured by four eclipsing factors: smoke, clouds, dust, and asuras, and become neither bright nor pure, so too, śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas have four kinds of eclipsing factors: some do not cut off desire and lust, and engage in sexual acts; some indulge in wine and food and are unable to cut them off; some exclusively engage in wrong livelihood to sustain themselves; some accept and keep gold, silver, jewels, and use them for trade. If a person regards the five sense-pleasures as pure, that person then regards accepting and keeping gold, silver, jewels, and using them for trade as pure. If a person regards accepting and keeping gold, silver, jewels, and using them for trade as pure, that person then regards the five sense-pleasures as pure. If a person has gone forth and received full ordination in reliance upon me, yet regards accepting and keeping gold, silver, jewels, and using them for trade as pure, you should know that this person certainly does not have faith in my Dharma and Vinaya. Although I always say that when one needs a cart one should seek a cart, when one needs a person one should seek a person, and one is permitted to seek whatever supplies one needs, one must never accept and keep gold, silver, jewels, or use them for trade.'" After Yaśas had said this, he further said: "I previously said what is Dharma and what is not Dharma, what is Vinaya and what is not Vinaya, what is the Buddha's teaching and what is not the Buddha's teaching." The upāsakas said: "We have no disbelief or displeasure regarding these words. Now in Vaiśālī, only the venerable one is truly a śramaṇa, a son of the Śākyas. We wish to offer you the four requisites for as long as you live, and ask you to remain here!"

After Yaśas had apologized to the upāsakas, he returned together with the Sangha's messenger bhikṣu to the monastery. The Vṛji bhikṣus asked the messenger bhikṣu: "Has the bhikṣu Yaśas already apologized to the upāsakas?" He answered: "He has apologized! But all the laity believed his words and all said: 'Now in Vaiśālī, only the venerable one is truly worthy!' They have already invited him for a lifetime of the four requisites. As for us, there is no longer any benefit." The Vṛji bhikṣus then, again because Yaśas had earlier instructed the bhikṣus, charged him with reviling the Sangha, a pāyattika offense. They said: "You should recognize this offense and repent!" Yaśas answered: "I have no offense to recognize; how can I repent?" The Vṛji bhikṣus then gathered together, intending to impose on him an act of suspension for not recognizing the offense. Thereupon Yaśas flew away by his supernatural powers to the country of Pāvā.

At that time, in the town of Pāri, there were sixty bhikṣus who were all forest-dwellers, who kept the three robes, ate only alms food, wore rag-robes, practiced constant sitting, sat in the open, were endowed with the three knowledges and six supernatural powers, and were all disciples of Ānanda. They all flew together toward Vaiśālī. Yaśas saw them, and he set his robe and bowl in mid-air as if resting them on the ground, greeted those bhikṣus, and fully described the ten unlawful practices of the Vṛji bhikṣus, saying: "Venerable ones! We should together discuss the Vinaya to put an end to these matters. Do not let the Vṛji bhikṣus destroy the True Dharma!" Those bhikṣus were in complete agreement and wished to join in putting an end to them.

There were also thirty bhikṣus from the town of Pāri, whose virtues were all as described above, also disciples of Ānanda, who were in the country of Mathurā. Yaśas and the sixty bhikṣus deliberated, saying: "If we can get those thirty bhikṣus to join us, we will certainly be able to lawfully put an end to those evil practices." After deliberating, they flew together to the place of those bhikṣus and explained everything as above. Those bhikṣus were also in complete agreement and wished to join in putting an end to them.

There were also thirty bhikṣus from the town of Pāri, whose virtues were all as described above, also disciples of Ānanda, who were in the town of Aḷavī. Yaśas again deliberated with the ninety people as above, went to their place, and explained everything as above. Those bhikṣus were also of one mind and wished to join in putting an end to them.

At that time, the Elder Sambhūta was on Mount Ahogaṅga. Yaśas again deliberated with the one hundred and twenty people as above, went to his place, and explained everything as above. He was also of one mind and wished to join in putting an end to them.

At that time, the Elder Revata was in the city of Kauśāmbī. He had attained the samādhi of loving-kindness and had a large retinue. Yaśas again deliberated with the one hundred and twenty-one people as above, went to his place, and explained everything as above. He was also of one mind and wished to join in putting an end to them.

At that time, the Vṛji bhikṣus heard that Yaśas had gone to Kauśāmbī to see Revata. They loaded a full boat with śramaṇa robes, bowls, and various requisites, also intending to go there to trade for support. Among the companions on the boat, there was a Vinaya-holding bhikṣu named Śāla, who privately reflected: "Are the Vṛji bhikṣus acting in accordance with the Dharma or not?" He then examined their conduct in light of the various sūtras and Vinaya, and found it to be not in accordance with the Dharma. At that time, a spirit in the sky proclaimed three times: "So it is! So it is! The practices of the Vṛji bhikṣus are not in accordance with the Dharma, just as you perceive!" The Vṛji bhikṣus arrived at Kauśāmbī, and they all went ashore to the Elder Revata's place, and said: "We have brought many loads of requisites needed by śramaṇas, which we offer to the venerable one. Please accept them!" He answered: "My robes and bowl are complete; I have no further need of them!" They further said: "If you do not need much, please accept a little!" He answered: "My robes and bowl are already sufficient. I cannot, for your sake, compromise the Dharma by accepting!" Revata had a disciple named Dharma, who always attended at his side. The Vṛji bhikṣus then went to him and said: "We have requisites needed by śramaṇas. If you are lacking anything, please take them!" He answered: "I have everything I need; I lack nothing." The Vṛji bhikṣus further said: "When the Buddha was in the world, people would offer gifts to the Buddha. When the Buddha would not accept, they would give them to Ānanda, and Ānanda always accepted. Since Ānanda accepted, it was as if the Buddha accepted." Dharma, hearing this, accepted one item. After accepting it, he asked: "What is your intention in pressing this gift upon me?" They answered: "We wish you to speak to your preceptor on our behalf, to lend his support and prevent Yaśas from ruining our Dharma and Vinaya." Dharma then went to his preceptor's place and said: "Preceptor, please support the Vṛji bhikṣus." He answered: "I do not support those who act unlawfully!" Dharma further said: "Please reconsider!" He answered: "You now urge me to support those who act unlawfully. You are not my disciple! From now on, do not remain at my side. I will no longer speak with you!" Dharma, ashamed and fearful, went out to the Vṛji bhikṣus' place. They all asked him: "Does your preceptor have any intention of supporting us?" He answered: "He does not! I have only brought upon myself your reproach and received the punishment of being forbidden to speak with him." The Vṛji bhikṣus asked: "How many rains retreats have you completed?" He answered: "Twenty." They said: "With your years and virtue, how could he bear to impose such a ban on speech?"

Thereupon the Elder Revata thought: "If I try to put an end to those matters here, the perpetrators will certainly raise them again. I should go there with them and put an end to them on the spot." Having thought this, he went together with the great assembly to the city of Vaiśālī.

In that city, there was already a bhikṣu named Sarvagāmin, who was the most senior elder among all the śramaṇas, sons of the Śākyas, in Jambudvīpa. He had attained arhatship, the three knowledges, and the six supernatural powers, and was also Ānanda's most senior disciple. Yaśas, outside the monastery, said to Revata: "You may go to the senior elder's room to lay out bedding and spend the night." He also fully reported the above matters. "I too will go to greet the senior elder in the morning." After the group had entered the monastery, the senior elder prepared bathing supplies for them and offered post-noon beverages. Revata alone went to the senior elder's room to lay out bedding and spend the night. During the night, Revata thought: "This Sarvagāmin is a frail and aged senior elder, yet he still exerts himself, sitting in meditation throughout the night. How could it be proper for me to sleep at ease?" Sarvagāmin also thought: "This visiting bhikṣu is exhausted from travel and has also bathed, yet he still sits in meditation and practices the path throughout the night. How could I lie down to rest?" The two urged each other on, and in the end they sat in meditation throughout the night. Toward the last watch of the night, Sarvagāmin asked Revata: "In which concentration did you most frequently abide tonight?" He answered: "My disposition inclines toward loving-kindness, and tonight I most frequently abided in this concentration." Sarvagāmin said: "This is a coarse concentration." He further asked: "Are you an arhat?" He answered: "I am!"

Revata then asked Sarvagāmin: "Senior elder, in which concentration did you most frequently abide tonight?" He answered: "My disposition inclines toward the contemplation of emptiness, and tonight I most frequently abided in this concentration." Revata said: "This is the practice of a great person. Why? Because the emptiness samādhi is the dharma of great persons." He further asked: "Senior elder, are you an arhat?" He answered: "I am!"

After the last watch had ended, the bhikṣu Yaśas came to the front of the room and snapped his fingers. The senior elder opened the door, and Yaśas entered and greeted him. After the greeting, Revata asked Sarvagāmin: "Is storing salt mixed with ginger overnight pure or not?" He answered: "This matter should be asked in the midst of the Sangha. If you ask me alone, I fear that the unlawful ones will consider me partial, and will not admit me into the assembly for discussing the Vinaya." Thereupon Revata assembled the Sangha, wishing to discuss the Vinaya; but there was much confused talk. He then addressed the Sangha: "Today we wish to discuss the Vinaya together, but there is much confused talk and no conclusion can be reached. Each side should select four persons, and the Sangha shall appoint them by jñaptidvitīya-karman to serve as arbiters." The Vṛji bhikṣus first selected four persons: first, Sarvagāmin; second, Revata; third, Pūjyasoma; fourth, Sumana. The Pāri bhikṣus also selected four persons: first, Sambhūta; second, Śāla; third, Keśakambala; fourth, Vāsabhagāmika. After the senior elders had been appointed by the Sangha, they deliberated together: "Where is there a quiet, level, and spacious place where we can discuss the Vinaya?" After examining everywhere, only the garden donated by the woman Virāya was suitable. Revata then sent his disciple Dharma to prepare seats there: "When the senior elders arrive, you should withdraw!" Having received the instruction, he prepared the seats. The senior elders arrived and sat in order. Thereupon Revata asked the senior elder Sarvagāmin: "Is storing salt mixed with ginger overnight pure or not?" He answered: "It is not pure!" He further asked: "Where was this rule established?" He answered: "In Rājagṛha." He further asked: "On account of whom was it established?" He answered: "On account of an āraṇyaka bhikṣu." He further asked: "What offense was committed?" He answered: "The offense of eating food stored overnight, a pāyattika." Revata said: "This is Dharma, this is Vinaya, this is the Buddha's teaching! What the Vṛji bhikṣus practice is not Dharma, not Vinaya, not the Buddha's teaching! Now cast the first voting stick."

Revata further asked: "Is eating food picked up with two fingers pure or not?" The senior elder asked: "What is meant by 'eating food picked up with two fingers is pure'?" Revata said: "When a bhikṣu has finished eating a full meal and receives more food, he picks it up with two fingers and eats it." He answered: "It is not pure!" He further asked: "Where was this rule established?" He answered: "In Rājagṛha." He further asked: "On account of whom was it established?" He answered: "On account of Upananda." He further asked: "What offense was committed?" He answered: "The offense of eating without performing the procedure for leftover food, a pāyattika." Revata said: "This is Dharma, and so forth down to: not the Buddha's teaching! Now cast the second voting stick."

Eating again after sitting down is pure, and eating after going beyond the village boundary is pure were likewise. He cast the third and fourth voting sticks.

Revata further asked: "Is mixing ghee, oil, honey, or rock sugar with yogurt pure or not?" The senior elder asked: "What is meant by 'mixing ghee, oil, honey, or rock sugar with yogurt is pure'?" Revata said: "Drinking it at an improper time." He answered: "It is not pure!" He further asked: "Where was this rule established?" He answered: "In Śrāvastī." He further asked: "On account of whom was it established?" He answered: "On account of Kālodāyin." He further asked: "What offense was committed?" He answered: "The offense of eating at an improper time, a pāyattika." Revata said: "This is Dharma, and so forth down to: not the Buddha's teaching! Now cast the fifth voting stick."

Revata further asked: "Is drinking jālogia liquor pure or not?" The senior elder asked: "What is jālogia liquor?" Revata said: "Liquor that has been brewed but is not yet fully fermented." He answered: "It is not pure!" He further asked: "Where was this rule established?" He answered: "In Kauśāmbī." He further asked: "On account of whom was it established?" He answered: "On account of Sāgata." He further asked: "What offense was committed?" He answered: "Drinking liquor, a pāyattika." Revata said: "This is Dharma, and so forth down to: not the Buddha's teaching! Now cast the sixth voting stick."

Revata further asked: "Is making a sitting mat of any size one wishes pure or not?" He answered: "It is not pure!" He further asked: "Where was this rule established?" He answered: "In Śrāvastī." He further asked: "On account of whom was it established?" He answered: "On account of Kālodāyin." He further asked: "What offense was committed?" He answered: "A pāyattika." Revata said: "This is Dharma, and so forth down to: not the Buddha's teaching! Now cast the seventh voting stick."

Revata further asked: "Is practicing what one formerly practiced pure or not?" The senior elder asked: "What is meant by 'practicing what one formerly practiced'?" Revata said: "Practicing what one did as a layperson." The senior elder said: "Some things may be practiced, and some may not." Revata said: "This is Dharma, and so forth down to: not the Buddha's teaching! Now cast the eighth voting stick."

Revata further asked: "Is seeking approval afterwards pure or not?" The senior elder asked: "What is meant by 'seeking approval'?" Revata said: "Performing an act separately, and then afterwards seeking the consent of the remaining persons." He answered: "It is not pure!" He further asked: "Where was this rule established?" He answered: "In the country of Campā." He further asked: "On account of whom was it established?" He answered: "On account of the group-of-six bhikṣus." He further asked: "What offense was committed?" He answered: "According to the matter of the act."

Revata said: "This is Dharma, and so forth down to: not the Buddha's teaching! Now cast the ninth voting stick."

Revata further asked: "Is accepting and keeping gold, silver, and coins pure or not?" He answered: "It is not pure!" He further asked: "Where was this rule established?" He answered: "In Rājagṛha." He further asked: "On account of whom was it established?" He answered: "On account of Nanda and Upananda." He further asked: "What offense was committed?" He answered: "The offense of accepting and keeping gold, silver, and coins, a niḥsargika-pāyattika." Revata said: "This is Dharma, and so forth down to: not the Buddha's teaching! Now cast the tenth voting stick."

When the questioning was finished, they returned together, and again assembled the entire Sangha. Revata, in the great assembly, again asked Sarvagāmin one by one as above, casting the first voting stick through the tenth voting stick. Thereupon Revata proclaimed: "We have now finished discussing the Vinaya. What the Buddha did not lay down should not be recklessly laid down; what has already been laid down must not be violated. As the Buddha taught, one should diligently study it."

At that time, in the assembly that discussed the Vinaya, the first senior seat was named Sarvagāmin, with one hundred and thirty-six rains retreats. The second senior seat was named Revata, with one hundred and twenty rains retreats. The third senior seat was named Sambhūta. The fourth senior seat was named Yaśas. Both had one hundred and ten rains retreats. In all there were seven hundred arhats, neither more nor less. Therefore it is called the Council of Seven Hundred for Collecting the Dharma.

# 彌沙塞部和醯五分律
## Five-Part Vinaya of the Mahīśāsaka School

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 1421

## Fascicle the Thirtieth

The Kaśmīra Vinaya master Buddhajīva is a monk of the Mahīśāsaka school.

In the autumn, the seventh month of the first year of the Jingping era of the Great Song dynasty, he arrived at Yangzhou. In the winter, the eleventh month, the Jin Attendant-in-Ordinary Wang Lian of Langye, along with the bhikṣus Shì Huìyán and Zhú Dàoshēng, invited him to bring forth this work. Buddhajīva carefully held to the Sanskrit text, and the Khotanese śramaṇa Zhìshèng served as translator. By the twelfth month of the following year, the entire work was completed. In examining and correcting it, the principles were preserved and the text was kept concise yet thorough. Although it may not have exhausted the original in full, we hope there are no grave errors. May this work, like dust and dew adding to the vastness of mountains and seas, be bequeathed to ten thousand generations, as fellow passengers in the same boat. So it is said!
